# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Q: Define a kinematic pair. Differentiate between a lower pair and a higher pair with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A kinematic pair is a joint between two links that allows constrained relative motion.

| **Lower Pair** | **Higher Pair** |
|---|---|
| Surface / area contact | Point / line contact |
| Example: Shaft in journal bearing | Example: Cam and follower |
| Low contact pressure | High contact pressure |
| Removes 2 DOF per joint (planar) | Removes 1 DOF per joint (planar) |

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for the definition of kinematic pair, 1 mark for lower vs higher distinction, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State Kutzbach’s equation for the degree of freedom of a planar mechanism. Define each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of links in the mechanism (including the fixed frame)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (single DOF joints with surface contact)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (two DOF joints with point/line contact)

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for $n$ and $j_1$ definitions, 1 mark for $j_2$ definition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]` (14 marks)

**Q: (a)** Classify kinematic pairs in detail based on (i) nature of contact and (ii) type of relative motion. Give two examples for each sub-class. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of Kutzbach’s equation, determine the degrees of freedom of a slider-crank mechanism and a four-bar chain. Comment on whether each is a determinate mechanism. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Classification by nature of contact (3 marks):**

1. **Lower Pair — Surface / Area Contact:** Two links are in contact over a finite surface area. Bearing pressure is low, life is high.
2. **Higher Pair — Point / Line Contact:** Two links are in contact at a point or along a line. Bearing pressure is high.

**Classification by type of relative motion (3 marks):**

1. **Sliding Pair:** Relative translation only. Examples: piston in cylinder, slider of a shaper.
2. **Turning / Revolute Pair:** Relative rotation only. Examples: shaft in journal bearing, door hinge.
3. **Screw Pair:** Combined translation and rotation along a helix. Examples: lathe lead screw, bench vice.
4. **Spherical Pair:** Rotation in any direction about a common point. Examples: ball-and-socket joint of a car steering, human shoulder.

**Examples and contact-area justification (1 mark):**

In every example above, the contact between the moving and the fixed link is either a continuous surface (lower) or a discrete point/line (higher). This matches the KTU definition.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Slider-crank mechanism (3 marks):**

- Links: $n = 4$ (crank, connecting rod, slider, frame)
- Lower pairs: $j_1 = 4$ (crank-frame revolute, crank-rod revolute, rod-slider revolute, slider-frame prismatic)
- Higher pairs: $j_2 = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
    &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
    &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since DOF = 1, the slider-crank is a **determinate mechanism**: one input (crank rotation) deterministically produces one output (slider position).

**(ii) Four-bar chain (3 marks):**

- Links: $n = 4$ (input link, coupler, output link, frame)
- Lower pairs: $j_1 = 4$ (4 revolute pairs)
- Higher pairs: $j_2 = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
    &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since DOF = 1, the four-bar chain is also a **determinate mechanism**: one input link rotation produces a fully defined motion of the output link (Grashof / non-Grashof case depends on link length proportions, but the DOF is 1 regardless).

**Comment (1 mark):**

Both mechanisms are determinate with DOF = 1. This means neither can move without a defined input, and for every input there is one and only one output configuration. This validates their use as basic building blocks in machine design.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]` (14 marks)

**Q: (a)** Define and explain with neat sketches: (i) Screw pair, (ii) Spherical pair, (iii) Cam-follower pair, (iv) Ball bearing pair. For each, state whether it is a lower or higher pair and justify. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A planar mechanism consists of **6 links** and **7 lower pairs**, and **1 higher pair**. Determine the DOF of the mechanism. Is the mechanism a structure, mechanism, or preloaded structure? Justify with Kutzbach’s equation. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Screw Pair — Lower Pair (2 marks):**

A screw pair is formed when a helical thread on one link engages with a matching thread on another link, producing a *constrained* motion that is a geometric combination of one rotation and one translation. The contact is along the entire helical surface — full surface contact — therefore it is a **lower pair**. DOF = 1.
*Example: lead screw of a lathe, screw jack, micrometer.*

**(ii) Spherical Pair — Lower Pair (2 marks):**

A spherical pair is formed when a spherical (ball-shaped) end of one link fits inside a matching spherical socket of another link, sharing a common centre. The contact is over the entire spherical surface — therefore it is a **lower pair**. DOF = 3 (rotation about any of three orthogonal axes through the centre).
*Example: ball-and-socket joint of a car steering system, human shoulder joint.*

**(iii) Cam-Follower Pair — Higher Pair (1.5 marks):**

The cam is a rotating link with a contoured profile; the follower is a link that stays in contact with the cam profile, translating or oscillating. The contact is at a *single point* (knife-edge follower) or *narrow line* (roller/flat follower) — therefore it is a **higher pair**. DOF = 2.
*Example: cam shaft and tappet of an IC engine.*

**(iv) Ball-Bearing Pair — Higher Pair (1.5 marks):**

A ball bearing is a system where hardened steel balls run in raceways. Each ball touches the inner race at a single point and the outer race at a single point. The contact is theoretically *point contact* — therefore a **higher pair**. DOF = 2 per ball-race interface.
*Example: deep groove ball bearing of an electric motor shaft.*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $n = 6$ links
- $j_1 = 7$ lower pairs
- $j_2 = 1$ higher pair

**Step 1 — Apply Kutzbach equation for planar mechanism:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
    &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 1 \\
    &= 3(5) - 14 - 1 \\
    &= 15 - 14 - 1 \\
    &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for justification):**

Since DOF = 0, the mechanism has **zero independent inputs**. This means the system **cannot move at all** under the given constraints. It behaves as a **structure** (or more precisely, a preloaded / locked structure, also called a **redundant structure** if you remove one pair it becomes determinate).

> A "preloaded structure" occurs when removing any single pair would give DOF = 1, but the current configuration with all pairs is over-constrained. The fact that DOF = 0 with 1 higher pair and 7 lower pairs indicates that one of the pairs is *redundant* — the mechanism is over-constrained by exactly one constraint.

**Step 3 — Verification by removing one pair (1 mark):**

If we remove the higher pair ($j_2$ becomes 0):
$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 = 15 - 14 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Removing the higher pair gives DOF = 1, confirming that the system as given is a *preloaded structure* with **one redundant pair**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Lower and Higher Pairs (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the classification basis:** Students often write "lower pair = sliding" and "higher pair = rolling". This is **wrong** because the classification is based on **nature of contact**, not on the *type of motion*. A ball bearing has *rolling* motion, but it is a *higher pair* (point contact). Conversely, a piston in a cylinder has *sliding* motion and is a *lower pair* (surface contact).
>
> 2. **Confusing screw pair with helical motion:** A screw pair is **not** a higher pair. The helical surface is a full continuous surface, so it is a **lower pair** with DOF = 1.
>
> 3. **Wrong count of links in Kutzbach:** Forgetting to include the *frame* as a link is the **single most common** error. Always count $n$ = moving links + 1 (for the frame).
>
> 4. **Stating DOF without units:** DOF is dimensionless. KTU examiners explicitly want the *interpretation*: "DOF = 1 means a determinate mechanism, requiring one input to produce one output."
>
> 5. **Forgetting the difference between 3D Kutzbach and Planar Kutzbach:** In PCRUT502, mechanisms are *planar* unless specified otherwise, so use $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. The 3D form $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2$ is **NOT** required in this course.
>
> 6. **Mixing up "self-closed" and "force-closed" with lower/higher pair:** Closure is an *orthogonal* classification. A higher pair can be self-closed (e.g., two meshing gears trap each other) or force-closed (e.g., a cam-follower held down by a spring). They are independent concepts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **kinematic pair** is a constrained joint between two links permitting relative motion.
- [x] **Lower pair** = **surface/area contact** (e.g., shaft in journal bearing, piston in cylinder, screw and nut, ball and socket).
- [x] **Higher pair** = **point/line contact** (e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh, ball bearing, wheel on rail).
- [x] Lower pair sub-types: **sliding, turning (revolute), screw, spherical** — all have surface contact.
- [x] Higher pair examples: cam-follower, gear teeth, ball bearing ball-race, wheel-rail.
- [x] The classification is by **contact**, not by the **type of motion** (sliding vs. rolling).
- [x] **Kutzbach planar equation (the only one needed):** $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [x] **Always include the frame** in the link count $n$.
- [x] Each **lower pair removes 2 DOF**, each **higher pair removes 1 DOF** (in planar analysis).
- [x] DOF = 1 ⟹ **determinate mechanism**; DOF = 0 ⟹ **structure**; DOF > 1 ⟹ **indeterminate** (not a useful mechanism).
- [x] **Screw pair** is a **lower pair** (helical surface contact), DOF = 1.
- [x] **Spherical pair** is a **lower pair** (spherical surface contact), DOF = 3.
- [x] **Cam-follower** is a **higher pair** (point/line contact), DOF = 2.
- [x] **Ball bearing** is a **higher pair** (point contact), DOF = 2 per ball-race.
- [x] Lower pairs have **lower contact stress, longer life, easier lubrication**; higher pairs have **high Hertzian contact stress, need EP lubrication and hardened materials**.
- [x] Real-world analogy: **book on table (lower, area)** vs. **marble on table (higher, point)**.
- [x] Closure is **orthogonal** to lower/higher: a pair can be **self-closed** (form-closed) or **force-closed** (energy-closed) regardless of whether it is lower or higher.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Chain (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **kinematic chain** is an assembly of rigid bodies (called *links* or *kinematic links*) connected together by *joints* (called *kinematic pairs*) in such a way that each link moves relative to the others, and the motion of any one link determines the motion of all other links by a deterministic, geometrically constrained relationship.

In KTU terminology, a kinematic chain becomes:
- A **mechanism** when at least one link is fixed (the *frame* or *ground* link).
- A **machine** when the mechanism is designed to transmit or modify force/energy to perform useful *work*.
- A **structure** when no relative motion is possible (it is essentially a locked chain, e.g., a bridge truss).

The hierarchy of these three terms is the **single most repeated 3-mark question** in KTU Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the bones of your **human arm**:
- The upper arm (humerus), forearm (radius/ulna), and hand are the **links**.
- The shoulder, elbow, and wrist are the **kinematic pairs (joints)**.
- The bones are not welded; they can rotate/translate relative to each other, but their motions are *coupled* — the position of the hand is fully determined once you specify the angles at the shoulder and elbow.

This coupled, multi-link, multi-joint system is exactly what we call a **kinematic chain**. A bicycle, sewing machine, car wiper, robot arm, and the human leg are all real-world kinematic chains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - **Pair** = one joint (between two links).
> - **Chain** = assembly of links + pairs.
> - **Mechanism** = chain with one link fixed.
> - **Machine** = mechanism that transmits/modifies energy.

## 1.3 Fundamental Components of a Kinematic Chain

A kinematic chain is built from three primitive elements:

| S.No. | Element | Definition | Symbol Convention |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Link (Element)** | A rigid body, normally assumed to be infinitely rigid and massless for kinematic analysis. | Numbered **1, 2, 3 … n** |
| 2 | **Kinematic Pair (Joint)** | A connection between two links that permits a definite relative motion. | Lower pair → **L**, Higher pair → **H** |
| 3 | **Frame (Ground)** | The fixed link of reference; it does not move. | Link **1** by convention |

## 1.4 Types of Kinematic Pairs (Foundation of Module 1)

Pairs are classified by the **type of contact** and the **relative motion** allowed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Based on Nature of Contact:**
> - **Lower Pair (1 DOF between the two links):** Surface contact. Eg. revolute, prismatic, screw, cylindrical, spherical, planar.
> - **Higher Pair (2 DOF between the two links):** Point or line contact. Eg. cam-follower, gear meshing, ball-bearing rolling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Based on Type of Motion (Planar pairs only):**
> - **Sliding Pair** → Pure translation (prismatic).
> - **Turning / Revolute Pair** → Pure rotation.
> - **Rolling Pair** → Pure rolling.
> - **Screw Pair** → Helical motion.
> - **Spherical Pair** → Ball-and-socket motion.

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a pair = the minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely specify the relative motion of the two links it joins.

## 1.5 Why Kinematic Chains Matter in Engineering

Every machine you can name is a kinematic chain:
- **Internal Combustion Engine:** Slider-crank chain converts reciprocating to rotary motion.
- **Planter / Tractor Hitch:** Pantograph chain amplifies small motions.
- **Quick-Return Mechanism (Shaper):** Inverted four-bar chain.
- **Watt's Steam Governor:** Two-bar + sliding chain.
- **Robot Manipulator:** Spatial chain (6-DOF) for 3D positioning.

Understanding kinematic chains is therefore the *first step* in design synthesis, motion planning, and dynamic analysis of any machine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Chains

Kinematic chains are broadly classified into two categories based on the relative motion of their links in three-dimensional space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Planar Kinematic Chain:**
> All links move in *parallel planes*. They are simpler, constrained to 3 degrees of freedom per rigid body (2 translations + 1 rotation). Governed by **Gruebler's Equation** (a special case of Kutzbach for planar systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Kinematic Chain:**
> Links move in *non-parallel planes*. Each rigid body has 6 DOF. Governed by **Kutzbach's Criterion**.

## 2.2 Mobility / Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

The **DOF** (or **Mobility**, denoted **M** or **F**) of a kinematic chain is the *number of independent inputs* (driver links) required to bring the mechanism to a definite, predictable configuration. This is the single most important analysis tool of the entire subject.

### 2.2.1 Kutzbach's Criterion (Spatial / General Mechanism)

For a spatial mechanism with *n* links (including the frame) and *j* pairs of various types:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $M$ = mobility (DOF) of the mechanism
- $n$ = total number of links
- $j_1$ = number of full joints (5 DOF removed; e.g., universal joint)
- $j_2$ = number of joints removing 4 DOF (e.g., spherical pair)
- $j_3$ = number of joints removing 3 DOF (e.g., cylindrical pair)
- $j_4$ = number of joints removing 2 DOF (e.g., revolute + prismatic combined)
- $j_5$ = number of full joints removing 1 DOF (e.g., revolute, prismatic, screw)

### 2.2.2 Gruebler's Equation (Planar Mechanism)

For a *planar* mechanism, since each free link has 3 DOF in a plane and the frame is fixed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $j_1$ = number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints, e.g., revolute, prismatic in 2D)
- $j_2$ = number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints, e.g., cam-follower in 2D)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** For Module 1 calculations, almost every problem is *planar*. Default to **Gruebler's Equation** unless the question explicitly mentions a spatial mechanism (like a Hooke's joint).

### 2.2.3 Condition for Deterministic Motion

A mechanism will have a **definite, deterministic motion** only if $M \geq 1$.
- $M = 1$ → *Simple Mechanism* (one input drives the whole chain deterministically).
- $M = 0$ → *Structure* (no motion possible; rigid frame).
- $M = 2$ → *Compound / Multi-DOF Mechanism* (needs two independent inputs).
- $M < 0$ → *Pre-loaded / Interlocked Structure*.

## 2.3 Grashof's Law (Foundation of Four-Bar Synthesis)

Grashof's Law applies to **four-bar planar linkages** and dictates whether at least one link can complete a full rotation (Grashof chain) or whether all links oscillate (non-Grashof chain).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S + L \leq P + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S, L, P, Q$ are the lengths of the **shortest**, **longest**, and the other two links respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grashof Classification Table:**

| Condition | Chain Type | Behaviour |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $S + L < P + Q$ | **Grashof Class I** | At least one link can rotate fully. |
| $S + L = P + Q$ | **Change-Point (Grashof Class II)** | Two positions of double-crank/double-rocker. |
| $S + L > P + Q$ | **Non-Grashof (Class III)** | No link can rotate fully; all oscillate. |

### 2.3.1 Grashof Inversions of the Four-Bar Chain

When $S$ is the *ground* link, the *output* link (opposite the shortest) is fully rotatable. The four inversions are:

| Ground Link | Output Link | Mechanism Name |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Shortest | Longest | **Double-Crank** (Drag-link) |
| Any adjacent to shortest | Longest | **Crank-and-Rocker** |
| Longest | Shortest | **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** |
| Adjacent to longest | Shortest | **Rocker-and-Rocker (non-Grashof)** |

## 2.4 Inversions of a Four-Bar Chain (The Famous KTU 14-Mark Topic)

An **inversion** is obtained by fixing a different link of the kinematic chain as the ground. The *relative* motion of the chain remains the same, but the *absolute* motion of the input/output changes — yielding entirely different machines from the same topology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Inversions & Their Applications:**
> 1. **Slider-Crank Mechanism** (fixing the connecting rod's link as the ground of a different topology) → IC engine, reciprocating compressor, pump.
> 2. **Whitworth Quick-Return** (fixing an offset slider) → Shaper, planer.
> 3. **Crank-and-Slotted Lever** (oscillating cylinder) → Slotter machine.
> 4. **Pantograph** (similarity-of-figure mechanism) → Copying/drafting, scale magnification.
> 5. **Straight-Line Mechanisms** — *Peaucellier*, *Hart*, *Watt*, *Chebyshev* → Approximate / exact straight-line tracing for pistons.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Module 1 Kinematic Chains**

| # | Formula / Rule | Use Case | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Planar DOF (Gruebler) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Spatial DOF (Kutzbach) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Grashof's Law (four-bar) | length |
| 4 | $S + L < P + Q$ | Grashof Class I (full rotation) | length |
| 5 | $S + L = P + Q$ | Change-point (boundary) | length |
| 6 | $S + L > P + Q$ | Non-Grashof (no full rotation) | length |
| 7 | $n = 2P - 4$ (skeleton) | Number of links in basic chain | count |
| 8 | $P_L + 2P_H = 3n - 2$ | Kutzbach equality for $M=1$ | count |
| 9 | $\theta_2 + \theta_3 + \theta_4 = 2\pi$ | Loop-closure (four-bar) | radians |
| 10 | $R = a + b - c$ | Pantograph (copy ratio) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $l \cdot l' = $ constant | Peaucellier exact straight line | m² |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kinematic-chain analysis is the **backbone of every machine design**:
- **Automotive:** Engine valve trains, suspension linkages (4-bar Watt steering), windshield wipers (4-bar).
- **Robotics:** Serial chains = robot arms; parallel chains = flight simulators, Stewart platforms.
- **Agricultural Machinery:** Tractor linkages, planter markers.
- **Biomechanics:** Human gait, prosthetics, exoskeletons.
- **Manufacturing:** Press tools, indexing mechanisms, packaging lines.
- **Textile / Printing:** Pantographs for engraving, printing-press feed chains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Gruebler's Equation (Planar DOF)

We now derive the *mobility equation* used in almost every KTU problem.

### Step 1 — Start with the Unconstrained System

A free rigid body in 2D plane has **3 degrees of freedom**: 2 translations (in X and Y) and 1 rotation (about Z). If the mechanism has $n$ links, and we have *not* introduced any joint, the total DOF of the assembly (unconstrained) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total unconstrained DOF} &= 3n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Account for the Fixed Frame

One of the $n$ links is the **ground/frame** and is held fixed. This removes its 3 DOF from the system, so the *effective* free DOF of the assembly becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unconstrained active DOF} &= 3n - 3 \\
&= 3(n - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Each Joint Removes Some DOF

A **lower pair** (revolute or prismatic) between two links in 2D allows only **1 relative motion**. Hence it removes $3 - 1 = 2$ DOF from the system.

A **higher pair** (cam-follower rolling, gear pair) allows **2 relative motions**. Hence it removes $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF from the system.

### Step 4 — Sum the Removed DOFs

If there are $j_1$ lower pairs and $j_2$ higher pairs, the total DOF removed is $2j_1 + 1 \cdot j_2$. Subtract this from the unconstrained active DOF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Gruebler Mobility Equation** for planar mechanisms. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Kutzbach's Criterion (Spatial DOF)

### Step 1 — Free Rigid Body in 3D

A free rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). For $n$ links:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total DOF} &= 6n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract Frame Constraint

The fixed ground link removes 6 DOF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unconstrained active DOF} &= 6n - 6 = 6(n-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Joint Constraints

A joint that allows $k$ relative DOF between its two links removes $6 - k$ DOF from the system.

- Joint allowing 1 DOF (revolute, prismatic, screw) → removes 5 DOF → counted as $j_1$.
- Joint allowing 2 DOF (cylindrical) → removes 4 DOF → counted as $j_2$.
- Joint allowing 3 DOF (planar) → removes 3 DOF → counted as $j_3$.
- Joint allowing 4 DOF (spherical / ball) → removes 2 DOF → counted as $j_4$.
- Joint allowing 5 DOF (universal / Hooke) → removes 1 DOF → counted as $j_5$.

### Step 4 — Final Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

∎

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: DOF of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

A standard slider-crank has **4 links** and **4 joints**:
- Frame, crank, connecting rod, slider.
- 3 revolute pairs (lower, $j_1 = 3$) and 1 prismatic pair (lower, $j_1 = 1$).
- No higher pairs.

So $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 3 \times 3 - 8 \\
&= 9 - 8 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore$ A slider-crank is a **1-DOF mechanism** — one input (crank rotation) drives the whole system deterministically. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Grashof's Law Check (KTU Style)

Given a four-bar chain with link lengths $a = 40$ mm, $b = 80$ mm, $c = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Identify the lengths:**
> - Shortest: $S = 40$ mm
> - Longest: $L = 100$ mm
> - Other two: $P, Q = 60, 80$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
S + L &= 40 + 100 = 140 \text{ mm} \\
P + Q &= 60 + 80 = 140 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S + L = P + Q$ exactly, the chain lies on the **Grashof change-point boundary**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The mechanism is a **change-point four-bar**; depending on the choice of the ground link, it may behave as a double-crank or double-rocker.

---

## 3.5 Pantograph Ratio Derivation

A pantograph is a 4-bar chain (or 5-bar chain) used to enlarge or reduce a drawing.

Let the parallelogram have sides of length $a$ and $b$, and let $R$ be the distance from the *fixed pivot* to the *tracing point* and $C$ the distance to the *copying point*. By similar triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R}{a + b} &= \frac{C}{a} \\
\Rightarrow \quad C &= R \cdot \frac{a}{a + b}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the *magnification ratio* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnification} = \frac{R}{C} = \frac{a + b}{a}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 1:2 scale enlarger, take $a = 1$ unit, $b = 1$ unit → ratio = 2.

---

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — Inversion Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inversion of Four-Bar Chain — Synthesis Matrix**

| # | Link Fixed (Ground) | Resulting Mechanism | Input Motion | Output Motion | Industrial Application |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Crank | **Slider-Crank** | Rotation of crank | Translation of slider | IC engine, pump |
| 2 | Coupler | **Double-Crank (Drag-link)** | Rotation of one crank | Rotation of other crank | Drag conveyors |
| 3 | Rocker | **Crank-and-Rocker** | Rotation of crank | Oscillation of rocker | Windshield wiper, sewing machine |
| 4 | Frame stays frame | **Double-Rocker** | Rocker 1 oscillation | Rocker 2 oscillation | Wagon steering (Watt linkage) |
| 5 | Coupler (long) | **Pantograph** | Tracing point path | Scaled copy at other end | Drafting, engraving |
| 6 | Coupler (special geometry) | **Straight-line mechanism** | Link rotation | Approx/exact straight line | Watt, Peaucellier, Hart |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Kinematic Chain

The block-level flow below maps the conceptual hierarchy that the KTU board expects when students describe a kinematic chain. It connects the **kinematic pair** to the **degrees of freedom** and the **mechanism classes**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Link 1] -->|joins via| P1[Kinematic Pair Type]
    A -->|joins via| P2[Kinematic Pair Type]
    P1 --> B[Rigid Link 2]
    P2 --> C[Rigid Link 3]
    B -->|joins via| P3[Kinematic Pair Type]
    C -->|joins via| P4[Kinematic Pair Type]
    P3 --> D[Rigid Link n Frame Fixed]
    P4 --> D
    D --> Q{Apply Mobility Criterion}
    Q -->|Gruebler Planar| R1[Use M equals 3 of n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    Q -->|Kutzbach Spatial| R2[Use M equals 6 of n minus 1 minus 5 j1 minus 4 j2 minus 3 j3 minus 2 j4 minus j5]
    R1 --> S{Result of M}
    R2 --> S
    S -->|M equals 0| T1[Structure No Motion]
    S -->|M equals 1| T2[Simple Mechanism]
    S -->|M equals 2| T3[Compound Mechanism Two Inputs]
    S -->|M greater than 0| T4[Pre loaded Structure Locked]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Four-Bar Chain (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FR[Frame Link 1] -->|Revolute Joint A| CR[Crank Link 2]
    CR -->|Revolute Joint B| CO[Coupler Link 3]
    CO -->|Revolute Joint C| RO[Rocker Link 4]
    RO -->|Revolute Joint D| FR
    CO -.->|Loop Closure| CO2[Vector Loop r2 + r3 equals r1 + r4]
    CR -->|Apply Grashof| GR{Grashof Check}
    GR -->|S plus L less than P plus Q| G1[Grashof Class I Full Rotation]
    GR -->|S plus L equals P plus Q| G2[Change Point Boundary]
    GR -->|S plus L greater than P plus Q| G3[Non Grashof Class III]
```

## 4.3 Slider-Crank Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frame] -->|Revolute Joint O2| B[Crank]
    B -->|Revolute Joint A| C[Connecting Rod]
    C -->|Revolute Joint B| D[Slider Piston]
    D -->|Prismatic Joint| A
    C -->|Translates Rotation to Reciprocation| E[Linear Piston Stroke]
    B -->|Input Rotates| E
```

## 4.4 Inversion Tree (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FB[Four Bar Chain Original] --> INV1[Fix Link 2 Crank]
    FB --> INV2[Fix Link 3 Coupler]
    FB --> INV3[Fix Link 4 Rocker]
    INV1 --> R1[Double Crank or Drag Link]
    INV2 --> R2[Pantograph or Straight Line Mechanism]
    INV3 --> R3[Crank and Rocker]
    FB --> SCD[Slider Crank Derived from Four Bar by Slider replacing Rocker]
    SCD --> SC1[Inversion 1 Crank Fixed]
    SCD --> SC2[Inversion 2 Slider Fixed]
    SCD --> SC3[Inversion 3 Connecting Rod Fixed]
    SC1 --> SC1A[Rotary IC Engine]
    SC2 --> SC2A[Whitworth Quick Return]
    SC3 --> SC3A[Crank and Slotted Lever Oscillating Cylinder]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:**
> Each box is a *kinematic link* or *functional state*, each connecting arrow is a *joint* or *logical transition*. The architecture is intentionally abstract because physical linkages cannot be rendered in pure markdown — but the **functional hierarchy** matches the KTU board's standard classification.

## 4.5 Pantograph Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIX[Fixed Pivot O] -->|Link of Length a| T[Tracing Point T]
    FIX -->|Link of Length a plus b| C[Copying Point C]
    T -->|Parallelogram| A[Coupler Link of Length a]
    A -->|Parallelogram| C
    C -->|Magnified Path| OUT[Output Drawing at Scale R over C]
```

The trace at $T$ produces a scaled-up or scaled-down copy at $C$ depending on which point is the input.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> **Q1 (3 marks).** *Differentiate between a kinematic chain, a mechanism, and a machine. Give one engineering example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- A **kinematic chain** is a system of rigid links connected by joints permitting relative motion. Example: the four-link chain of a sewing-machine needle drive *before* any link is fixed.
- A **mechanism** is a kinematic chain in which *one link is fixed (the frame)*, so that definite relative motion occurs among the moving links. Example: the slider-crank of an IC engine with the cylinder block fixed.
- A **machine** is a mechanism (or combination of mechanisms) that *transmits or modifies force/energy* to perform useful work. Example: a lathe, where the spindle-rotation mechanism drives a cutting tool to remove metal.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **Q2 (3 marks).** *State and explain Grashof's Law. What is the significance of the change-point condition?*

**Model Answer:**
Grashof's Law for a four-bar planar linkage states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S + L \leq P + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S, L$ are the shortest and longest link lengths, and $P, Q$ are the other two.
- If $S + L < P + Q$ → **Grashof Class I** (at least one link can complete a full rotation).
- If $S + L = P + Q$ → **Change-point** (boundary case, behaviour is *transition* — two possible outputs).
- If $S + L > P + Q$ → **Non-Grashof (Class III)** (no link can rotate fully).

**Significance of change-point:** At the boundary, the chain can behave as a *double-crank* or *double-rocker* depending on the *ground link selection*. Designers exploit this for special-purpose machines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the two following questions.**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Slider-Crank DOF & Inversions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q.A (a) (7 marks).** *For the slider-crank mechanism shown in a standard IC engine configuration, calculate the degrees of freedom using Gruebler's criterion. List the lower and higher pairs.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the links.**
- Link 1 → Frame (cylinder block).
- Link 2 → Crank.
- Link 3 → Connecting rod.
- Link 4 → Slider / Piston.

Total $n = 4$ links.

**Step 2 — Identify the pairs.**
- Revolute joint between crank and frame (O₂): 1 lower pair.
- Revolute joint between crank and connecting rod (A): 1 lower pair.
- Revolute joint between connecting rod and slider (B): 1 lower pair.
- Prismatic joint between slider and frame: 1 lower pair.

So $j_1 = 4$ lower pairs, $j_2 = 0$ higher pairs.

**Step 3 — Apply Gruebler's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
&= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 3 \times 3 - 8 \\
&= 9 - 8 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The slider-crank has **1 DOF**, confirming it is a deterministic mechanism driven by a single input (crank rotation). ∎

---

> **Q.A (b) (7 marks).** *Explain the inversions of the slider-crank mechanism. List the names of any two industrial applications for each inversion.*

**Model Solution:**

The slider-crank is obtained by evolving a four-bar chain where one revolute pair is replaced by a prismatic pair. By *fixing a different link* as the frame, we obtain three major inversions:

**Inversion 1 — Crank fixed (Frame is the crank).**
- The connecting rod swings and oscillates around a fixed crank pivot.
- The slider traces a curved path; this is the basis of the **oscillating-cylinder engine** and the **rotary internal combustion engine** (e.g., the Wankel, conceptually similar).
- **Application 1:** Old rotary printing presses. **Application 2:** Gnome rotary aero engine.

**Inversion 2 — Connecting rod fixed (Frame is the connecting rod).**
- The crank rotates fully around the fixed connecting-rod centre.
- The slider motion is also along a defined line.
- This is the **Crank-and-Slotted Lever** quick-return mechanism.
- **Application 1:** Slotter machines. **Application 2:** Planer and shaping machine tool drives.

**Inversion 3 — Slider fixed (Frame is the slider).**
- The crank and connecting rod rotate around the fixed slider axis.
- The crank-rotation axis translates.
- This is the **Whitworth Quick-Return** mechanism.
- **Application 1:** Shaper machines. **Application 2:** Power-hack-saw and slotters. ∎

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Grashof's Law & Synthesis

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q.B (a) (7 marks).** *A four-bar linkage has the following link lengths: $a = 50$ mm, $b = 80$ mm, $c = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm. Apply Grashof's Law and identify the type of mechanism when each link is fixed in turn.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify S, L, P, Q.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 50 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{shortest}) \\
L &= 100 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{longest}) \\
P &= 60 \text{ mm} \\
Q &= 80 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check Grashof's condition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S + L &= 50 + 100 = 150 \text{ mm} \\
P + Q &= 60 + 80 = 140 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S + L = 150 \text{ mm} > P + Q = 140 \text{ mm}$, this is a **Non-Grashof (Class III) chain**.

**Step 3 — Behaviour with each link fixed:**

In a *non-Grashof chain*, **no link can complete a full 360° rotation** regardless of which link is the ground. All four possible inversions will be **double-rocker mechanisms** (or their equivalent), in which both the input and output links merely oscillate.

| Fixed Link | Resulting Mechanism |
| :---: | :--- |
| $a = 50$ mm | Double-Rocker (coupler non-Grashof) |
| $b = 80$ mm | Double-Rocker |
| $c = 100$ mm | Double-Rocker |
| $d = 60$ mm | Double-Rocker |

**Conclusion:** The linkage is **non-Grashof** and behaves as a **double-rocker in every inversion**. ∎

---

> **Q.B (b) (7 marks).** *With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Pantograph. Derive the magnification ratio for a copying-enlarging pantograph.*

**Model Solution:**

**Working:**
A pantograph is a five-bar (or four-bar parallelogram) mechanism with three parallel links of lengths $a, a$ and one diagonal link of length $b$. It has:
- One *fixed pivot* $O$.
- A *tracing point* $T$.
- A *copying point* $C$.
- Three parallel links forming a *parallelogram*.

When the operator moves the tracing point $T$ along an original drawing, the parallelogram constraints force the copying point $C$ to traverse a *geometrically similar path* — either an enlargement or a reduction, depending on the geometry.

**Derivation of the Magnification Ratio:**

Let $OT = a$ and $OC = a + b$ (with $a, b$ being the link lengths along the parallelogram diagonal). By similar triangles formed in the parallelogram:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{OT}{OC} &= \frac{a}{a+b} \\
\text{Path at } T : \text{Path at } C &= a : (a + b)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the **magnification** of the copy relative to the original is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnification} = \frac{\text{Path at } C}{\text{Path at } T} = \frac{a+b}{a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $a = 100$ mm and $b = 100$ mm, the magnification = $200/100 = 2$. So a 1 mm movement at the tracing point produces a 2 mm movement at the copying point — the figure is enlarged by a factor of **2**.

**Industrial Applications:**
- 1. **Engraving machines** for mass-producing identical engravings.
- 2. **Drafting / photocopying** of large-scale drawings.
- 3. **Profile milling machines** for cutting identical contours.
- 4. **Pantograph oxy-fuel cutting torches** in ship-building and plate-metal workshops. ∎

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in Kinematic-Chain questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the DOF formula:** Students write Kutzbach's equation for *planar* problems. Always default to **Gruebler ($M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$)** unless spatial motion is explicitly stated. (-2 marks typical loss)
> 2. **Miscounting the links:** Forgetting to include the *fixed frame* as one of the $n$ links is the most common error. The frame counts as **Link 1** in KTU notation. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Misclassifying pairs:** Treating a *prismatic pair* as a higher pair. **Both revolute and prismatic are LOWER pairs** in 2D. Higher pairs in 2D = cam-follower contact, gear teeth contact. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the Grashof classification:** The question says "apply Grashof's Law" — students often just check the inequality but *forget to state the class* (I, II, or III) and the resulting mechanism name. (-2 marks)
> 5. **No diagram or wrongly-labelled diagram:** Inversions and pantograph questions always require a *labelled sketch*. KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** for the diagram quality alone.
> 6. **Units missing:** Always write lengths in **mm** (or m) and DOF as a **dimensionless integer** with the word "DOF" or "M =". Many students leave out units and lose half-marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kinematic Chains**

- A **kinematic chain** = assembly of rigid links connected by kinematic pairs, with at least one degree of relative freedom.
- **Chain → Mechanism** (one link fixed) → **Machine** (does useful work).
- **Lower pair** = surface contact, removes 2 DOF in 2D; **Higher pair** = point/line contact, removes 1 DOF in 2D.
- **Gruebler's Equation (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. **Always use this** for Module 1 unless told otherwise.
- **Kutzbach's Equation (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **Deterministic motion** requires $M \geq 1$. A well-designed mechanism is *always* $M = 1$.
- **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ for at least one link to rotate fully. Below the boundary, the chain is non-Grashof and all links oscillate.
- **Inversions** of a chain yield entirely different machines from the same topology — *fixing different links* is the key.
- **Slider-Crank** has 4 links, 4 joints, $M = 1$. It is the **most-frequently tested mechanism** in KTU Module 1.
- **Pantograph** magnification = $(a + b)/a$. Used for engraving, drafting, oxy-fuel cutting.
- **Straight-line mechanisms** — Peaucellier (exact), Hart, Watt, Chebyshev (approximate) — are tested as 7-mark sub-questions.
- **Default units** in KTU answers: lengths in **mm**, angles in **radians** (or degrees — be consistent), DOF in pure **integer**.
- **Always draw and label** the diagram — 1 free mark is reserved for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Inversions of Four Bar and Single Slider Crank Chains, Mobility/Degrees of Freedom (DOF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Inversions of Four Bar and Single Slider Crank Chains, Mobility/Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Kinematic Chain?

A **kinematic chain** is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints (kinematic pairs) that permits relative motion between the links. When one link is fixed to the ground (frame), the chain becomes a **mechanism**, which is the foundation of all machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The focus in Module 1 is on the *basic chains* and their **inversions** — the systematic way of fixing different links of a chain to obtain useful mechanisms.

## 1.2 The Four Bar Chain — Formal Definition

The **Four Bar Chain (Quadric Cycle Chain)** is the simplest closed kinematic chain consisting of **four rigid links** connected by **four revolute (turning) pairs**. It has:
- **4 links** (one is the fixed frame)
- **4 lower pairs** (all revolute, 1 DOF each)

The four links are typically named:
1. **Frame (Fixed link)** — the link that is stationary
2. **Crank** — the input link that rotates fully (shortest)
3. **Coupler (Connecting rod)** — the floating link
4. **Rocker (Follower)** — the oscillating output link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a window-opening mechanism in a car door. The door frame is fixed, the small arm rotates, a connecting piece transmits motion, and the wiper oscillates. That is essentially a four-bar chain in disguise!

## 1.3 The Single Slider Crank Chain — Formal Definition

The **Single Slider Crank Chain** consists of **four links** connected by **three revolute pairs and one prismatic (sliding) pair**. The links are:
1. **Frame (Fixed link)**
2. **Crank** — rotates fully
3. **Connecting rod (Coupler)**
4. **Slider (Piston)** — reciprocates linearly

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** This is the heart of every petrol/diesel engine. The piston slides up and down in a cylinder, the connecting rod transmits force, and the crank converts this reciprocating motion into rotation. Same chain, different "inversions" give us different machines.

## 1.4 What is a Kinematic Inversion?

A **kinematic inversion** is obtained by fixing different links of a kinematic chain, one at a time, while keeping the relative lengths of the links unchanged. The chain itself is the same; only the choice of the **fixed link (frame)** changes, which dramatically alters the motion of the remaining links.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** A kinematic chain of *n* links can have a maximum of *(n − 1)* inversions because the last link to be fixed simply reverses the first inversion (the chain returns to its original configuration).

For a 4-bar chain → **3 inversions** are possible.
For a slider-crank chain → **3 inversions** are possible (one is degenerate — slider fixed gives the original).

## 1.5 Mobility / Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** or **Mobility (F)** of a mechanism is the **number of independent parameters (inputs)** required to completely specify the configuration (position) of every link of the mechanism at any instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a robotic arm in 3D space. If you can move it in 3 independent directions (say, X, Y, Z), its mobility is 3. Similarly, each kinematic pair removes certain freedoms, and DOF counts how many remain.

The standard unit of DOF is **dimensionless** (a pure number).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inversions of the Four Bar Chain

Since the four-bar chain has 4 links, by fixing each link (except the original fixed one) we get **3 distinct inversions**. Conventionally, in textbooks, the **fourth inversion is also presented** (with the coupler fixed), making it a 4-link chain with 4 inversions presented in classic texts.

### Inversion 1: Beam Engine (Crank and Lever Mechanism)
- **Link fixed:** Frame (link 1)
- **Application:** Used in early steam engines (Beam pumping engines)
- **Working:** Crank rotates → Coupler transmits → Rocker (lever arm) oscillates about its fixed pivot → Piston at the end of the rocker pumps water.

### Inversion 2: Coupling Rod of Locomotive (Double Crank Mechanism)
- **Link fixed:** Coupler (link 3)
- **Application:** Drive coupling between the two wheels of a steam locomotive
- **Working:** When the fixed link is the coupler, the two side links (which were crank and follower) both rotate fully. They act as **cranks** that drive the locomotive wheels.

### Inversion 3: Watt's Indicator Diagram Mechanism
- **Link fixed:** Rocker / Follower (link 4)
- **Application:** James Watt's steam engine indicator — plots pressure vs. volume
- **Working:** The original rocker is now fixed. The small link (originally crank) acts as a **short stroke slider**, and the long link (originally coupler) connects it to the indicator cylinder piston.

### Inversion 4: Drag Link Mechanism (Rotary Internal Combustion Engine — Gupta Mechanism)
- **Link fixed:** Smallest link (crank, link 2)
- **Application:** Used in rotary IC engines (Gupta's mechanism), quick-return mechanisms
- **Working:** Both side links (originally coupler and follower) rotate fully about the fixed crank. The mechanism is therefore a **rotating pair of levers** used for high-speed indexing.

## 2.2 Inversions of the Single Slider Crank Chain

The single slider-crank chain has 4 links. By fixing each link in turn, we obtain **3 distinct inversions**.

### Inversion 1: Reciprocating Engine (Oscillating Cylinder Engine)
- **Link fixed:** Frame (link 1)
- **Application:** Internal combustion engines (car engines)
- **Working:** Crank rotates fully → Connecting rod pushes slider (piston) → Linear reciprocation in cylinder.

### Inversion 2: Rotary Internal Combustion Engine (Rotary I.C. Engine / Wankel-type)
- **Link fixed:** Connecting rod (link 3)
- **Application:** Rotary piston engines, rotary compressors
- **Working:** With the connecting rod fixed, the crank and slider BOTH rotate fully about their pivots. Continuous rotary motion without dead centres.

### Inversion 3: Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism
- **Link fixed:** Slider (link 4)
- **Application:** Shaping machines, planers, slotters
- **Working:** The crank (with its off-centre pivot) rotates, and the connecting rod (now attached to a slotted lever) drives the cutting tool. The forward and return strokes have different speeds → **quick return**.

### Inversion 4 (Extension): Crank and Slotted Lever Quick Return Mechanism
- **Link fixed:** Small crank (link 2)
- **Application:** Shaping machines, slotters
- **Working:** The slotted lever oscillates about a fixed pivot; the crank pin slides in the slot. The forward and return times differ → quick return.

### Inversion 5 (Extension): Bull Engine (Pendulum Pump / Tumbler Gear)
- **Link fixed:** Connecting rod (link 3) — alternative inversion
- **Application:** Mine pumping, chain pumps
- **Working:** The cylinder oscillates about a pivot, and the slider (piston rod) reciprocates. The pendulum-like oscillation gives the name "Bull Engine."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The standard three inversions of slider-crank and four-bar chains are mandatory. The Whitworth and Crank-Slotted Lever are the most important quick-return variants asked in exams.

## 2.3 Mobility / Degrees of Freedom — The Kutzbach Criterion

For a planar mechanism, the **Kutzbach equation** (also called Grübler's equation for 2D) gives the mobility:

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Where:
- $F$ = Degrees of freedom (mobility) of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of links (including the fixed link/frame)
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints) — revolute, prismatic, screw, etc.
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints) — cam-follower, gear pair, ball bearing

### Conditions for a Useful Mechanism
- **F = 1:** Mechanism is **completely constrained** (deterministic motion) — desired for most machines.
- **F = 0:** Structure — no motion possible.
- **F < 0:** Pre-loaded structure — statically indeterminate.
- **F > 1:** Mechanism is **under-constrained** — needs multiple inputs.

## 2.4 Grashof's Law (For Four Bar Chains)

For a four-bar chain, the **type of motion** (whether the shortest link can rotate fully) is governed by **Grashof's Law**:

$$
S + L \leq P + Q
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Length of the **shortest** link
- $L$ = Length of the **longest** link
- $P, Q$ = Lengths of the other two links

**Classifications:**
| Condition | Classification | Motion Type |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| S + L < P + Q | Grashof Chain (Class I) | At least one link rotates fully |
| S + L = P + Q | Grashof Chain (Class II) | Special case — change point |
| S + L > P + Q | Non-Grashof Chain | No link rotates fully — only oscillation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a Grashof chain, which link rotates fully depends on **which link is fixed**:
> - Shortest link fixed → **Double Crank (Drag Link)**
> - Any link adjacent to shortest fixed → **Crank-Rocker**
> - Link opposite to shortest fixed → **Double Rocker**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Used For | Notes / Units |
|---|---------------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Kutzbach (Planar DOF) | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | DOF of planar mechanism | Pure number (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Grübler (Spatial DOF) | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | DOF of spatial mechanism | 6 DOF per unconstrained link |
| 3 | Grashof's Law | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Type of four-bar mechanism | All lengths in mm or m |
| 4 | Inversions of 4-link chain | $n_{inv} = n - 1 = 3$ | Max possible inversions | For 4-bar & slider-crank |
| 5 | Number of links (4-bar) | $n = 4$ | Basic structure | Includes fixed link |
| 6 | Number of links (slider-crank) | $n = 4$ | Basic structure | Includes fixed link |
| 7 | Lower pairs in 4-bar | $j_1 = 4$ | All revolute joints | 1 DOF each |
| 8 | Lower pairs in slider-crank | $j_1 = 4$ | 3 revolute + 1 prismatic | 1 DOF each |
| 9 | Number of higher pairs (basic) | $j_2 = 0$ | Basic 4-bar & slider-crank | Pure rotary/sliding chains |
| 10 | Quick Return Ratio (QRR) | $QRR = \dfrac{T_f}{T_r}$ | Time ratio of strokes | $T_f$ = forward, $T_r$ = return |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DOF analysis** is the first step in designing ANY machine — without it, we cannot verify that the mechanism will move as intended.
- **Inversions** allow engineers to reuse the same chain geometry for many applications, reducing design cost and time.
- **Quick-return mechanisms** are critical in shaping and planing machines where reducing idle (return) time boosts productivity by 20–40%.
- **Grashof's Law** dictates whether a four-bar can serve as a crank-rocker (pendulum clocks) or a drag link (rotary indexing tables).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kutzbach Criterion (Planar Mobility)

### Step 1: Free Body Counting
Consider a kinematic chain with $n$ links in a plane. **In a plane, every rigid body has 3 degrees of freedom** (translation along X, translation along Y, rotation about Z).

**Total DOF without any constraints:**

$$
F_{total} = 3n
$$

> This is the DOF if all links are floating freely in the plane.

### Step 2: Effect of Fixing the Frame
One link is designated as the **fixed frame (ground)**. Fixing a link removes all 3 of its DOF.

$$
F_{free} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)
$$

> After grounding one link, the remaining $(n-1)$ links can move freely.

### Step 3: Effect of Adding a Lower Pair (1-DOF Joint)
A **lower pair** (revolute, prismatic, screw, etc.) allows 1 relative DOF between two links. Therefore, it constrains the system by $(3 - 1) = 2$ DOF.

> **Why 2?** Because a pair between two links replaces 6 DOF (3+3) with 1 allowed DOF, so the net reduction is 5 for spatial, but for planar (3+3) → 1 allowed, so reduction is 2 per pair.

If there are $j_1$ lower pairs, the constraints imposed are:

$$
C_{lower} = 2 j_1
$$

### Step 4: Effect of Adding a Higher Pair (2-DOF Joint)
A **higher pair** (e.g., cam on follower, gear pair) allows 2 relative DOF. It constrains the system by $(3 - 2) = 1$ DOF.

If there are $j_2$ higher pairs:

$$
C_{higher} = 1 \cdot j_2 = j_2
$$

### Step 5: Final Kutzbach Equation
Subtracting all constraints from the free DOF:

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2}
$$

> This is the **Kutzbach criterion for planar mechanisms**.

### Step 6: Verification with the Four Bar Chain
- $n = 4$ links
- $j_1 = 4$ lower pairs (all revolute)
- $j_2 = 0$ higher pairs

$$
F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
F = 3(3) - 8
$$

$$
F = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

> **Result:** Mobility = 1 → the four-bar mechanism has **one degree of freedom** (deterministic), so one input (crank rotation) fully defines the motion of all links. ✓

### Step 7: Verification with the Single Slider Crank Chain
- $n = 4$ links
- $j_1 = 4$ lower pairs (3 revolute + 1 prismatic)
- $j_2 = 0$ higher pairs

$$
F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

> **Result:** Mobility = 1 → one input (crank rotation) fully controls the piston motion. ✓

### Step 8: Verification — Engine with Cam-Follower (Mixed Pairs)
- $n = 4$ links (frame, cam, follower, valve)
- $j_1 = 3$ lower pairs (2 revolute at pivots + 1 prismatic at valve guide)
- $j_2 = 1$ higher pair (cam-follower contact)

$$
F = 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 1
$$

$$
F = 9 - 6 - 1 = 2
$$

> **Result:** Mobility = 2 → This means we need **two inputs** (e.g., cam rotation AND an external force) to control it. In a real valve train, the spring force provides the second constraint.

## 3.2 Derivation of Grashof's Law (Geometric Condition for Full Rotation)

### Step 1: Setup
Consider a four-bar chain with link lengths $a$ (shortest), $b$, $c$, $d$ (longest). Without loss of generality, let $a \leq b \leq c \leq d$.

### Step 2: Condition for the Shortest Link to Rotate Fully
For the shortest link to complete a full 360° rotation, it must be able to align with the other links. The geometric condition derived by Grashof (1843) is:

$$
S + L \leq P + Q
$$

Where $S$ and $L$ are the shortest and longest links.

### Step 3: Mathematical Derivation (Qualitative)
The shortest link can rotate fully if and only if the sum of its length and the longest link is at most equal to the sum of the other two links. This ensures that the chain **can fold and unfold completely** without jamming.

> If $S + L > P + Q$: the chain cannot close at certain configurations, locking the mechanism.

### Step 4: Class of Four-Bar Chain
Let the **shortest link be fixed** as the frame.

**Case A: $S + L < P + Q$ (Grashof Class I)**
- Shortest link fixed → **Double Crank (Drag Link)** — both side links rotate fully.
- Any link adjacent to shortest fixed → **Crank-Rocker** — only one link rotates fully.
- Link opposite to shortest fixed → **Double Rocker (Grashof)** — neither link rotates fully, but the coupler has full rotation potential.

**Case B: $S + L > P + Q$ (Non-Grashof)**
- All four-bar chains are **double rockers** — no link can complete a full rotation.

**Case C: $S + L = P + Q$ (Change Point / Grashof Class II)**
- Special borderline case where the chain changes from one class to another.

## 3.3 Quick Return Ratio (QRR) — Analytical Setup

For a crank and slotted-lever quick return mechanism:
- The crank rotates with **uniform angular velocity** $\omega$.
- Forward stroke angle: $\alpha$ (slower cutting stroke)
- Return stroke angle: $\beta$ (faster idle stroke)
- Total angle: $\alpha + \beta = 2\pi$ rad = 360°

### Time for Forward Stroke
$$
T_f = \dfrac{\alpha}{\omega}
$$

### Time for Return Stroke
$$
T_r = \dfrac{\beta}{\omega}
$$

### Quick Return Ratio
$$
QRR = \dfrac{T_f}{T_r} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a crank-rocker shaping machine (Whitworth):** $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the angles through which the crank rotates to complete forward and return strokes respectively. Higher QRR means greater productivity.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — DOF Calculation

**Problem:** Determine the DOF of a mechanism consisting of a 4-bar chain with a slider attached to the coupler.

**Given:**
- Total links: $n = 5$ (frame, crank, coupler, rocker, slider)
- Revolute pairs: 5 (frame-crank, crank-coupler, coupler-rocker, rocker-frame, coupler-slider)
- Prismatic pair: 1 (slider in its guide)
- Higher pairs: 0

**Step 1:** Apply Kutzbach:

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
F = 3(5-1) - 2(6) - 0
$$

$$
F = 12 - 12 = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** Mobility = 0 → This is a **structure** (no motion possible). To make it a mechanism, we must either add a DOF joint or remove a link.

**Step 2:** Solution: Replace one revolute with a higher pair (cam), giving $j_2 = 1$:

$$
F = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 1 = 12 - 10 - 1 = 1
$$

> Now the mobility is 1 → a proper mechanism.

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Grashof's Law Classification

**Given four-bar links:** $a = 20$ mm, $b = 40$ mm, $c = 60$ mm, $d = 100$ mm.

**Step 1:** Identify $S$ and $L$:
- $S = 20$ mm (shortest)
- $L = 100$ mm (longest)
- $P = 40$ mm, $Q = 60$ mm

**Step 2:** Compute both sides:

$$
S + L = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
P + Q = 40 + 60 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare:

$$
S + L = 120 > P + Q = 100
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion:

> Since $S + L > P + Q$, this is a **Non-Grashof chain** → **Double Rocker** mechanism (no link can rotate fully).

**If we modify:** Set $a = 50, b = 60, c = 80, d = 100$ mm:
- $S + L = 50 + 100 = 150$
- $P + Q = 60 + 80 = 140$
- $150 > 140$ → Still non-Grashof.

**Another case:** $a = 30, b = 50, c = 60, d = 70$ mm:
- $S + L = 30 + 70 = 100$
- $P + Q = 50 + 60 = 110$
- $100 \leq 110$ → **Grashof chain** (Class I).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Basic Four Bar Chain — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the four-bar chain with node labels describing each link and the connections (joints) between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FRAME LINK 1 - FIXED] --- nodeB[CRANK LINK 2 - ROTATES]
    nodeB --- nodeC[COUPLER LINK 3 - FLOATS]
    nodeC --- nodeD[ROCKER LINK 4 - OSCILLATES]
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeA -. PIVOT .-> nodeB
    nodeB -. PIVOT .-> nodeC
    nodeC -. PIVOT .-> nodeD
    nodeD -. PIVOT .-> nodeA
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid lines (`---`) represent physical links (rigid members).
- Dotted lines (`-.->`) represent revolute (pivot) joints — all four are **lower pairs**.
- The frame is the stationary link; the crank is the input.

## 4.2 Inversions of Four Bar Chain — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[FOUR BAR CHAIN - 4 LINKS] --> invA1[INVERSION 1 - FRAME FIXED]
    rootA --> invA2[INVERSION 2 - COUPLER FIXED]
    rootA --> invA3[INVERSION 3 - ROCKER FIXED]
    rootA --> invA4[INVERSION 4 - CRANK FIXED]

    invA1 --> appA1[BEAM ENGINE CRANK AND LEVER]
    invA2 --> appA2[LOCOMOTIVE COUPLING ROD DOUBLE CRANK]
    invA3 --> appA3[WATTS INDICATOR DIAGRAM]
    invA4 --> appA4[DRAG LINK MECHANISM ROTARY IC ENGINE]

    appA1 --> dofA1[DOF = 1]
    appA2 --> dofA2[DOF = 1]
    appA3 --> dofA3[DOF = 1]
    appA4 --> dofA4[DOF = 1]
```

**Key Insight:** All four inversions have the **same DOF (= 1)** because the number of links and joint types is preserved. Only the **choice of frame** changes.

## 4.3 Single Slider Crank Chain — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    linkF[FRAME 1 - FIXED] --- linkC[CRANK 2 - ROTATES]
    linkC --- linkR[CONNECTING ROD 3 - OSCILLATES]
    linkR --- linkS[SLIDER 4 - RECIPROCATES]
    linkF --- linkS

    linkF -. R PIVOT .-> linkC
    linkC -. R PIVOT .-> linkR
    linkR -. R PIVOT .-> linkS
    linkS -. P SLIDER .-> linkF
```

**Joint Legend:**
- **R Pivot** = Revolute pair (1 DOF, lower pair)
- **P Slider** = Prismatic pair (1 DOF, lower pair)

## 4.4 Inversions of Single Slider Crank Chain — Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootB[SINGLE SLIDER CRANK CHAIN - 4 LINKS] --> invB1[INVERSION 1 - FRAME FIXED]
    rootB --> invB2[INVERSION 2 - CONNECTING ROD FIXED]
    rootB --> invB3[INVERSION 3 - SLIDER FIXED]
    rootB --> invB4[INVERSION 4 - CRANK FIXED]

    invB1 --> appB1[RECIPROCATING IC ENGINE OSCILLATING CYLINDER]
    invB2 --> appB2[ROTARY IC ENGINE WANKEL TYPE]
    invB3 --> appB3[WHITWORTH QUICK RETURN MECHANISM]
    invB4 --> appB4[CRANK AND SLOTTED LEVER QUICK RETURN]
    invB2 --> appB5[BULL ENGINE PENDULUM PUMP]

    appB1 --> noteB1[CRANK ROTATES FULLY PISTON RECIPROCATES]
    appB2 --> noteB2[BOTH CRANK AND SLIDER ROTATE CONTINUOUSLY]
    appB3 --> noteB3[OFF CENTRE SLOTTED LEVER QUICK RETURN]
    appB4 --> noteB4[SLOTTED LEVER OSCILLATES CRANK PIN SLIDES]
```

## 4.5 DOF Calculation Flowchart (Kutzbach Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START - GIVEN MECHANISM] --> step1[STEP 1 - COUNT LINKS N]
    step1 --> step2[STEP 2 - COUNT LOWER PAIRS J1]
    step2 --> step3[STEP 3 - COUNT HIGHER PAIRS J2]
    step3 --> step4[STEP 4 - APPLY F EQUALS 3 OPEN PAREN N MINUS 1 CLOSE PAREN MINUS 2J1 MINUS J2]
    step4 --> step5[STEP 5 - INTERPRET RESULT]

    step5 --> out1[F EQUALS 1 - GOOD MECHANISM]
    step5 --> out2[F EQUALS 0 - STRUCTURE]
    step5 --> out3[F LESS THAN 0 - PRE LOADED STRUCTURE]
    step5 --> out4[F GREATER THAN 1 - UNDER CONSTRAINED]

    out1 --> check[VERIFY INDEPENDENT INPUTS MATCH F]
    check --> end[END - REPORT DOF]
```

## 4.6 Grashof's Law — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startG[START - GIVEN 4 LINK LENGTHS A B C D] --> sort[STEP 1 - SORT A LEQ B LEQ C LEQ D]
    sort --> identify[STEP 2 - IDENTIFY S AND L]
    identify --> calc[STEP 3 - COMPUTE S PLUS L AND P PLUS Q]
    calc --> decide{COMPARE S PLUS L WITH P PLUS Q}

    decide --> case1[S PLUS L LESS THAN P PLUS Q - GRASHOF CLASS I]
    decide --> case2[S PLUS L EQUALS P PLUS Q - GRASHOF CLASS II CHANGE POINT]
    decide --> case3[S PLUS L GREATER THAN P PLUS Q - NON GRASHOF]

    case1 --> fixq{WHICH LINK IS FIXED}
    fixq --> fx1[SHORTEST FIXED - DOUBLE CRANK DRAG LINK]
    fixq --> fx2[LINK ADJACENT TO SHORTEST - CRANK ROCKER]
    fixq --> fx3[LINK OPPOSITE SHORTEST - DOUBLE ROCKER GRASHOF]

    case3 --> nong[ALL CONFIGURATIONS GIVE DOUBLE ROCKER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Notes:** All node IDs (e.g., `nodeA`, `invA1`, `step1`, `fx1`) are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are NOT used as node names. Node labels use clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside double quotes.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> **Q:** Define *kinematic inversion*. List the inversions of a four-bar chain and identify the mechanism obtained when the **coupler link is fixed**.

**Model Answer:**

A **kinematic inversion** of a kinematic chain is obtained by fixing different links of the chain, one at a time, while keeping the relative lengths of the links unchanged.

The four inversions of a four-bar chain are:

| Inversion | Link Fixed | Mechanism Obtained |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Frame (Link 1) | Beam Engine (Crank and Lever) |
| 2 | Coupler (Link 3) | **Coupling Rod of Locomotive (Double Crank)** |
| 3 | Rocker (Link 4) | Watt's Indicator Diagram |
| 4 | Crank (Link 2) | Drag Link Mechanism (Rotary I.C. Engine) |

When the **coupler is fixed**, the two side links (originally crank and follower) both rotate fully → mechanism is called the **Coupling Rod of Locomotive** or **Double Crank Mechanism**. Used to drive the wheels of steam locomotives from a single source. (3 marks)

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **Q:** What is the **mobility** of a mechanism? State the Kutzbach criterion for a planar mechanism and explain each term.

**Model Answer:**

**Mobility (F)**, also called **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, is the number of independent inputs required to completely define the configuration of all moving links of a mechanism at a given instant.

**Kutzbach Criterion (Planar):**

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Terms:**
- $F$ = Mobility / Degrees of Freedom (dimensionless)
- $n$ = Total number of links in the mechanism (including the fixed link/frame)
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints: revolute, prismatic, screw, helical)
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints: cam-follower contact, gear pair, ball-bearing)

The factor **3** represents the 3 planar DOF (X, Y translation + Z rotation) of each free link. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks).

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Style
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

> **Q (A):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the **inversions of a single slider crank chain**. Mention at least three mechanisms obtained.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A four-bar mechanism has link lengths of 20 mm, 40 mm, 60 mm and 100 mm. Determine the **type of mechanism** using Grashof's Law when:
> (i) the shortest link is fixed,
> (ii) the link opposite to the shortest is fixed.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Inversions of Single Slider Crank Chain [7 Marks]

A single slider crank chain has **4 links**:
1. Frame (Link 1)
2. Crank (Link 2)
3. Connecting Rod (Link 3)
4. Slider / Piston (Link 4)

By fixing each link in turn (keeping relative lengths constant), we get **3 main inversions**:

**Inversion 1: Reciprocating Engine (Frame Fixed)** *(2 marks)*
- The frame is held stationary.
- The crank rotates fully; the connecting rod transmits motion; the slider reciprocates inside the cylinder.
- **Application:** All standard internal combustion engines (petrol, diesel, steam).

**Inversion 2: Rotary Internal Combustion Engine (Connecting Rod Fixed)** *(2 marks)*
- The connecting rod is fixed.
- Both the crank and the slider (now a rotor) **rotate continuously** about their respective pivots.
- **Application:** Rotary engines, rotary compressors (Wankel-type design).

**Inversion 3: Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism (Slider Fixed)** *(2 marks)*
- The slider is fixed; the crank rotates about an off-centre pivot.
- The connecting rod drives a slotted lever that oscillates, producing **quick-return motion** (different forward and return speeds).
- **Application:** Shaping machines, planers, slotters — reduces idle time.

**Inversion 4 (Extension): Crank and Slotted Lever (Crank Fixed)** *(1 mark)*
- The smallest link (crank) is fixed.
- The slotted lever oscillates about a pivot; the crank pin slides within the slot.
- The cutting tool at the end of the slotted lever has **quick-return motion**.

> **Note:** All inversions retain **DOF = 1** since the number of links (n = 4) and joint types are unchanged.

---

#### Part (b) — Grashof's Law Application [7 Marks]

**Given:** Link lengths = 20 mm, 40 mm, 60 mm, 100 mm

**Step 1: Sort the link lengths**

After sorting (ascending): $S = 20$, $a = 40$, $b = 60$, $L = 100$ mm.

**Step 2: Apply Grashof's Law**

$$
S + L = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
P + Q = 40 + 60 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Compare**

$$
S + L = 120 \text{ mm} > P + Q = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This is a **Non-Grashof Chain** (Class III). No link can complete a full rotation. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Mechanism Type Based on Fixed Link**

**(i) When the shortest link (20 mm) is fixed:** *(2.5 marks)*

Since the chain is **Non-Grashof**, **no link can rotate fully**, regardless of which is fixed. The mechanism will be a **Double Rocker** — both the input (originally crank) and output (originally rocker) will oscillate. There is **no full-rotation capability**.

> ⚠ If this were a Grashof chain with the shortest fixed, it would be a **Double Crank (Drag Link)** — but since S+L > P+Q, this is not possible here.

**(ii) When the link opposite to the shortest (100 mm) is fixed:** *(2.5 marks)*

The link opposite to the shortest is the longest link (100 mm). Fixing it:
- Since the chain is Non-Grashof → again a **Double Rocker**.
- Both side links oscillate; no full rotation.

> **Final Answer for both cases:** The mechanism is a **Double Rocker** because the chain fails Grashof's condition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

> **Q (B):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **kinematic pair**. Classify kinematic pairs with examples. Apply the Kutzbach criterion to find the **mobility** of a slider-crank mechanism used in an IC engine.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With sketches, describe the **inversions of a four-bar chain**. State one application of each inversion. Determine the **DOF** of a mechanism having 6 links, 7 lower pairs and 1 higher pair.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Kinematic Pairs and Slider-Crank DOF [7 Marks]

**Definition:** A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two links that permits relative motion in a constrained manner. *(1 mark)*

**Classification of Kinematic Pairs:** *(3 marks)*

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|------|------------|----------|
| Lower Pair | Surface contact; 1 DOF; relatively enclosed | Revolute, Prismatic, Screw, Spherical (partial) |
| Higher Pair | Point/line contact; 2 DOF; not enclosed | Cam-follower, Gear pair, Ball bearing |
| Sliding Pair | Pure relative translation | Piston in cylinder, ram in guide |
| Turning Pair | Pure relative rotation | Shaft in bearing, crank pivot |
| Rolling Pair | Pure relative rolling | Ball on flat surface, rollers |
| Spherical Pair | 3 rotational DOF | Ball-and-socket joint |
| Screw Pair | Helical motion | Lead screw, nut on bolt |

**DOF of Slider-Crank Mechanism (IC Engine):** *(3 marks)*

- $n = 4$ links (frame, crank, connecting rod, slider)
- $j_1 = 4$ lower pairs (3 revolute + 1 prismatic)
- $j_2 = 0$ higher pairs

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
F = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

> **Mobility = 1.** The mechanism is completely constrained and deterministic — one input (crank rotation) defines all motion.

---

#### Part (b) — Four Bar Inversions and DOF of a Complex Mechanism [7 Marks]

**Four Bar Inversions:** *(4 marks)*

| Inversion | Link Fixed | Mechanism | Application |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Frame | Beam Engine | Steam pumping engines |
| 2 | Coupler | Coupling Rod of Locomotive | Drive coupling in steam engines |
| 3 | Rocker | Watt's Indicator Diagram | Pressure-volume indicator in steam engines |
| 4 | Crank (Shortest) | Drag Link Mechanism | Rotary indexing tables, rotary IC engines |

**DOF of Mechanism with 6 Links, 7 Lower Pairs, 1 Higher Pair:** *(3 marks)*

Given:
- $n = 6$
- $j_1 = 7$
- $j_2 = 1$

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
F = 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 1
$$

$$
F = 15 - 14 - 1 = 0
$$

> **Mobility = 0.** The mechanism is a **structure** (no relative motion possible). To convert it to a working mechanism, either:
> - Add a DOF to one of the joints (e.g., change a higher pair to a lower pair), or
> - Add an extra link with a pair.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the fixed link in inversions:** Many students mix up which link is fixed in each inversion. Memorize the table — examiners deduct 1–2 marks per wrong attribution.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 3 in Kutzbach:** A common mistake is writing $F = 3n - 2j_1 - j_2$ (forgetting $(n-1)$). This is wrong; always subtract 1 from $n$ first.
> 3. **Mis-counting $n$:** Some students exclude the frame from $n$. WRONG — the **fixed link IS counted** in $n$ for Kutzbach.
> 4. **Grashof sign confusion:** Students often write $S + L \geq P + Q$ (reversed inequality). The correct condition for Grashof (full rotation possible) is $S + L \leq P + Q$.
> 5. **Not specifying which link is fixed in Grashof problem:** Just saying "Grashof chain" is incomplete — examiners require the **type of mechanism** based on the fixed link.
> 6. **Unit-less answers for DOF:** DOF is a pure number. Writing "1 rad" or "1 mm" loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)**

### Key Definitions
- **Kinematic Chain:** Series of rigid links connected by joints allowing relative motion.
- **Mechanism:** A kinematic chain with one link fixed (frame).
- **Kinematic Inversion:** Different mechanisms obtained by fixing different links of the same chain.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) / Mobility (F):** Number of independent inputs to fully define the mechanism's configuration.

### Core Formulas (Memorize Exactly)
- **Kutzbach (Planar):** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ (for Grashof chain)
- **QRR (Quick Return Ratio):** $QRR = \alpha / \beta$ where $\alpha + \beta = 2\pi$

### Four Bar Chain Inversions (Memorize the Table)
| Inversion | Fixed Link | Mechanism | Application |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Frame | Beam Engine | Steam pumping |
| 2 | Coupler | Coupling Rod | Locomotive drive |
| 3 | Rocker | Watt Indicator | PV diagram |
| 4 | Crank | Drag Link | Rotary IC engine |

### Single Slider Crank Inversions (Memorize)
| Inversion | Fixed Link | Mechanism | Application |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Frame | Reciprocating Engine | Car IC engine |
| 2 | Connecting Rod | Rotary IC Engine | Wankel-type |
| 3 | Slider | Whitworth QRR | Shaping machine |
| 4 | Crank | Crank-Slotted Lever | Planer, slotter |

### DOF Interpretation (Memorize)
- $F = 1$ → Mechanism (deterministic) ✓
- $F = 0$ → Structure (no motion) ✗
- $F < 0$ → Pre-loaded structure ✗
- $F > 1$ → Under-constrained (need multiple inputs) ✗

### Grashof's Law — Mechanism Type vs Fixed Link
| Fixed Link | Grashof Chain | Non-Grashof |
|------------|---------------|-------------|
| Shortest | Double Crank (Drag Link) | Double Rocker |
| Adjacent to shortest | Crank-Rocker | Double Rocker |
| Opposite to shortest | Double Rocker (Grashof) | Double Rocker |

### Higher-Yield Numerical Traps
- **Always count the frame in $n$.**
- **Prismatic pairs count as $j_1$ (lower pairs).**
- **For cam-follower: contact is $j_2$ (higher pair).**
- **Ball bearing: usually $j_2 = 0$ (treated as multiple revolute pairs in planar analysis).**

### Quick Sanity Checks
- Four bar chain → always $F = 1$ (n=4, j₁=4, j₂=0).
- Slider-crank → always $F = 1$ (n=4, j₁=4, j₂=0).
- Adding a cam with follower to a 4-bar → $F = 1$ (n=5, j₁=4, j₂=1) → $F = 3(4) - 8 - 1 = 3$? **No!** Recount carefully: 5 links, 4 lower pairs, 1 higher → $F = 12 - 8 - 1 = 3$ — actually this is over-constrained unless the cam's pivot is part of the frame. The standard textbook result for cam-follower added to a 4-bar is **F = 1** (only if the cam rotates about a fixed pivot shared with the frame, keeping the effective link count at 4 with 3 lower + 1 higher pair).

### One-Line Summary for the Examiner
> "Inversions give multiple mechanisms from one chain; Kutzbach verifies mobility; Grashof classifies four-bar motion types — these three concepts together form the foundation of kinematic design."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
