topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kutzbach's Formula, Velocity Analysis: Definition of the Velocity Analysis Problem, Graphical Method of Velocity Analysis - Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Kutzbach's Formula — The Mathematical Foundation of Mobility

**Kutzbach's Formula** is a fundamental kinematic identity used in the analysis of planar mechanisms to determine the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — also called the *mobility* — of a kinematic chain. It expresses the number of independent inputs a mechanism requires to produce a deterministic, fully-constrained motion.

For a **planar (2D) mechanism** with rigid links connected by lower and higher pairs, Kutzbach's formula is stated as:

$$F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $F$ = Degrees of freedom of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of links (including the fixed frame/ground)
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints) such as revolute and prismatic
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints) such as cam-follower and gear mesh (line contact)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Kutzbach's formula is a **determinacy criterion**. A mechanism is:
> - **Gruebler-Kutzbach criterion compliant** when $F \geq 1$ (mechanism can move)
> - **Deterministic (Solvable)** when $F = 1$ (one input = one output motion)
> - **Locked / Structure** when $F = 0$ (no relative motion possible)
> - **Over-constrained / Redundant** when $F < 0$ (no physical motion, but redundancy in design)

### Intuitive Analogy — The Mechanical Puppet

Imagine a wooden puppet on a stage. Each limb (arm, leg, head) is a *link*. The strings connecting limbs to the wooden control bar (cross) are like *joints*. Kutzbach's formula tells you exactly **how many strings the puppeteer must pull** to make the puppet produce a unique, predictable dance movement. If there are too few strings ($F=0$), the puppet is a stiff statue. If there is one perfect string setup ($F=1$), one pull produces one dance. If there are extra strings ($F<0$), some are redundant — the puppet has been over-engineered.

In a **four-bar mechanism** (the most common planar linkage): $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute), $j_2 = 0$:
$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

One input crank rotation produces one deterministic output coupler-rotation. This is the KTU textbook example.

## 1.2 Velocity Analysis — Formal Definition

**Velocity Analysis** is the kinematic procedure of determining the **linear and angular velocities** of all moving links in a mechanism, given the input motion (typically the angular velocity of the driver crank) and the instantaneous geometry (link lengths and crank angle) of the mechanism at a particular instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Velocity analysis is the determination of the velocities of various points on the links of a mechanism for a given configuration and a given input angular velocity of the driver link.*

The objective is to find:
- **Magnitude** of velocity of any point
- **Direction** of velocity of any point
- **Angular velocity** magnitude and sense (CW/CCW) of every moving link

### Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle Pedal

Think of a bicycle being ridden at a steady pace. The pedal arm (crank) rotates with a known angular velocity. The chain pulls the rear wheel sprocket. At any instant, you can ask: *"What is the velocity of the toe of my shoe at the very bottom of the pedal stroke?"* The shoe traces a circular path — its velocity is tangential to that circle. Velocity analysis gives you the answer mathematically or graphically, just as a cyclist intuitively feels the speed of their foot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Velocity Analysis Matters in Engineering:**
> - **Design of gear teeth** (tooth profile interference)
> - **Cam profile synthesis** (follower velocity dictates lift curve)
> - **Force and dynamic analysis** (acceleration follows from velocity gradient)
> - **Balancing of linkages** (velocity of mass centers is the first step)
> - **Animation and simulation** of mechanisms before prototyping

## 1.3 The Velocity Analysis Problem — Statement

For a planar mechanism with $n$ links, the input angular velocity $\omega_2$ of the driver is given. The instantaneous configuration is defined by the geometry (link lengths and crank angle). The **velocity analysis problem** is to determine:

1. The **angular velocities** $\omega_3, \omega_4, \ldots$ of all driven links.
2. The **linear velocities** $\vec{V}_A, \vec{V}_B, \vec{V}_C, \ldots$ of all significant points.

Two classical KTU-board-tested approaches exist:
- **Graphical Method** (Instantaneous Centers / Velocity Polygons) — focus of this module
- **Analytical Method** (Vector Loop / Loop-Closure Equations)

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 restricts the graphical method to **Four-Bar** and **Slider-Crank** mechanisms only.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kutzbach's Formula — Extended Grashof Logic

### 2.1.1 Historical Origin and Grashof Connection

Kutzbach's equation is a planar specialization of the more general **Grübler criterion** (1902). The relationship is:

| Criterion | Application | DOF |
|-----------|-------------|-----|
| Grübler's Equation | Spatial (3D) mechanisms | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ |
| Kutzbach's Formula | Planar (2D) mechanisms | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kutzbach's criterion is a necessary but not sufficient condition for mobility.** It correctly identifies *Grashof and non-Grashof* four-bars (e.g., Grashof, Non-Grashof Class I, II, III), but for complex linkages with multiple loops, *special cases* (passive DOF, redundant constraints) may exist. The KTU board only tests single-loop planar mechanisms where Kutzbach is fully sufficient.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Application Logic

To apply Kutzbach's formula correctly:

1. **Count all links** including the ground link. The ground is link 1.
2. **Count lower pairs** $j_1$: revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints.
3. **Count higher pairs** $j_2$: cam-follower, gear mesh, point/line contacts.
4. **Substitute into** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
5. **Interpret** the result:
   - $F = 1$ → determinate mechanism (good design)
   - $F = 0$ → structure (no motion)
   - $F < 0$ → statically indeterminate / over-constrained
   - $F > 1$ → multiple inputs needed

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic arm design**: A 6-DOF robot arm has $n = 7$ links, $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints, hence $F = 3(7-1) - 2(6) = 18 - 12 = 6$ (matches the requirement for full 3D positioning).
- **Suspension system analysis**: A double-wishbone suspension in automobiles is analyzed using Kutzbach to confirm mobility constraints.
- **Foldable furniture**: Designers use it to verify folding chairs and tables produce intended motion.

## 2.2 Velocity Analysis — Fundamental Principles

### 2.2.1 The Relative Velocity Equation (Foundation of All Graphical Solutions)

For any two points $A$ and $B$ on a rigid body, the velocities are related by:

$$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$$

Where:
- $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is the velocity of $B$ **relative to** $A$
- $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is **perpendicular to** the line joining $A$ and $B$ (since the body is rigid and the distance $AB$ is invariant)
- Magnitude: $\vert V_{BA} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$ (where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the link and $L_{AB}$ is the link length)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule (Velocity Direction):** For a **rotating link**, the velocity of any point on the link is **perpendicular to the line joining the point to the instantaneous center of rotation** (which, for pure rotation, is the fixed pivot). For a **coupler** in general plane motion, every point velocity is perpendicular to the line joining that point to the *instantaneous center* of the link.

### 2.2.2 The Velocity Polygon — Graphical Representation

A **velocity polygon** (or **velocity diagram**) is a vector diagram in which each vector represents the velocity of a labeled point. KTU convention uses:

- **Subscript notation** for points (e.g., $B$ means point on a link, often $B_2$ means point $B$ on link 2)
- **Lowercase** letters in the polygon: point $a$ in polygon = point $A$ in mechanism
- **Lowercase** letters also represent velocity *vectors*: e.g., vector $\vec{ab}$ in the polygon represents $\vec{V}_B - \vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_{BA}$
- **Letters start from origin 'o'** = absolute velocity pole (zero velocity reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** The velocity polygon is always drawn *separately* from the configuration diagram, and labeled with **lowercase letters** corresponding to the **uppercase letters** of points in the mechanism. The pole (origin) is labeled **'o'**.

### 2.2.3 Scaling Rule for Velocity Polygon

When drawn to scale, the velocity polygon follows a strict scale conversion:

$$\text{Velocity scale} = \frac{\text{Vector length on paper (mm)}}{\text{Actual velocity (m/s)}}$$

Common KTU convention: denote velocity scale as $v_s = X \text{ m/s per mm}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Kutzbach's formula (planar) | Dimensionless DOF |
| 2 | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Grübler's formula (spatial) | Dimensionless DOF |
| 3 | $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$ | Relative velocity equation | m/s |
| 4 | $\vert V_{BA} \vert = \omega_3 \cdot L_{AB}$ | Magnitude of relative velocity | $\omega$ in rad/s, $L$ in m |
| 5 | $\vec{V}_B \perp AB$ (for a rigid body in pure rotation) | Direction rule | Geometric |
| 6 | $\vec{V}_A = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_A$ | Velocity of point on rotating link | m/s (vector form) |
| 7 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{V_{BA}}{L_{AB}}$ | Angular velocity of link 3 | rad/s |
| 8 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{V_{CB}}{L_{BC}}$ | Angular velocity (alternative) | rad/s |
| 9 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{\text{velocity of any point on link}}{\text{distance from IC of link}}$ | IC-based angular velocity | rad/s |
| 10 | $V_{slider} = \omega_2 \cdot (r \sin\theta + \dfrac{r^2 \sin 2\theta}{2\sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}})$ | Slider velocity (analytical) | m/s — for reference only |
| 11 | $n_c = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | Number of instantaneous centers (N = number of links) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $n_c^{total} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Total ICs in a mechanism | For $n$ links |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When you see a vector equation like $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$, *always draw the polygon*. The polygon pictorially enforces the vector addition. Each letter in the polygon corresponds to a *point* in the mechanism, and the *line* between two letters represents a *velocity difference vector*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's Formula (Planar)

The Kutzbach equation emerges from the count of independent rigid-body motions in a planar kinematic chain.

### Step 1: Free Mobility of Unconnected Links

If a planar mechanism has $n$ unconnected links, each link in 2D space has **3 DOF** (translation along $x$, translation along $y$, and rotation about an axis perpendicular to the plane). Therefore:

$$\text{Total free DOF} = 3n$$

### Step 2: Subtract Ground (Fixed Link)

The ground link is *immovable*. It cannot translate or rotate. So we subtract 3 DOF (one link's worth of freedom):

$$\text{Remaining DOF} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$$

### Step 3: Subtract Constraints Imposed by Joints

A **lower pair** (revolute or prismatic) in 2D removes **2 DOF** (the joint constrains the relative motion to a single rotational or translational degree, removing 2 of the original 3 possible relative motions).

A **higher pair** in 2D removes **1 DOF** (e.g., a cam-follower allows the cam to rotate and the follower to translate independently along the contact normal — only the perpendicular-to-surface motion is constrained).

Each lower pair $j_1$ removes $2 j_1$ DOF; each higher pair $j_2$ removes $j_2$ DOF.

### Step 4: Final Equation

Combining the above:

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

This is **Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms**.

### Worked Example: Four-Bar Linkage

A four-bar linkage has 4 links (1 ground + 3 moving) and 4 revolute (lower) joints.

$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

The mechanism has **1 DOF**, so one input (crank rotation) drives a deterministic output (rocker rotation). ✓

### Worked Example: Slider-Crank Mechanism

A slider-crank has 4 links (ground, crank, connecting rod, slider) and joints: 3 revolute + 1 prismatic = 4 lower pairs.

$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

The slider-crank has **1 DOF**. ✓

### Worked Example: Five-Bar Linkage (Two Loops)

A five-bar has 5 links and 5 revolute joints (single-loop chain):

$$F = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0 = 12 - 10 = 2$$

This is a **two-DOF** mechanism. Two inputs (e.g., two crank rotations) are required. This is *not* determinate from a single input.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Relative Velocity Equation

Consider a rigid link $AB$ in pure plane motion. Let $A$ and $B$ be two points on this link.

### Step 1: Position Vectors

The position of $B$ relative to $A$ in a fixed frame is $\vec{r}_{BA}$. Since the body is rigid, the magnitude $\vert \vec{r}_{BA} \vert$ is **constant** (only the direction can change with rotation).

### Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to Time

Differentiating the position equation $\vec{r}_B = \vec{r}_A + \vec{r}_{BA}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{r}_{BA}}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Identify Velocity Terms

By definition, $\vec{V}_B = \dfrac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt}$ and $\vec{V}_A = \dfrac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt}$. The term $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_{BA}}{dt}$ is the velocity of $B$ as seen from $A$ (rotating with the body):

$$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$$

### Step 4: Show the Perpendicularity

For a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$:

$$\vec{V}_{BA} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA}$$

The cross product of any two vectors is always **perpendicular** to both. Therefore $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp \vec{r}_{BA}$. In other words, $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is perpendicular to the line $AB$.

### Step 5: Magnitude

Since $\vec{\omega} \perp \vec{r}_{BA}$ in 2D (angular velocity vector is out-of-plane), the cross product magnitude reduces to:

$$\vert \vec{V}_{BA} \vert = \omega \cdot \vert \vec{r}_{BA} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$$

This completes the derivation of the relative velocity equation and the two key rules (direction and magnitude) used in all graphical velocity analysis.

## 3.3 Graphical Velocity Analysis — Four-Bar Mechanism

### Setup

Consider a four-bar linkage $O_2 A B O_4$ where:
- $O_2 A$ is the **input crank** (link 2) of length $r$, rotating with angular velocity $\omega_2$
- $A B$ is the **coupler** (link 3) of length $L$
- $B O_4$ is the **output rocker** (link 4) of length $R$
- $O_2 O_4$ is the **fixed link** (link 1) of length $D$

Given: link lengths $r, L, R, D$ and crank angle $\theta_2$, and input angular velocity $\omega_2$.

To find: $\omega_3, \omega_4$ and linear velocities $V_A, V_B$.

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1: Draw the Configuration Diagram**

Draw the four-bar mechanism to scale using the given dimensions and crank angle $\theta_2$. Mark all points: $O_2, A, B, O_4$.

**Step 2: Compute $V_A$**

Point $A$ rotates about $O_2$ with angular velocity $\omega_2$. The direction of $\vec{V}_A$ is **perpendicular to $O_2 A$** (in the sense of $\omega_2$, i.e., if $\omega_2$ is CCW, $V_A$ is rotated $90°$ CCW from $O_2 A$).

Magnitude:

$$V_A = \omega_2 \cdot r$$

On the velocity polygon, lay off the vector $\vec{oa}$ of length:

$$\vert oa \vert = \frac{V_A}{v_s}$$

where $v_s$ is the velocity scale.

**Step 3: Identify the Direction of $\vec{V}_B$**

Point $B$ rotates about the fixed pivot $O_4$. Therefore $\vec{V}_B$ is **perpendicular to $O_4 B$.** Draw a line through 'a' on the velocity polygon in the direction perpendicular to $AB$ (this represents the line of action of $\vec{V}_{BA}$).

**Step 4: Locate Point $b$ on the Velocity Polygon**

Draw another line on the velocity polygon representing the line of action of $\vec{V}_B$, which is perpendicular to $O_4 B$ in the mechanism. The intersection of these two lines gives the point $b$.

**Step 5: Read $\vec{V}_B$ from the Polygon**

The vector $\vec{ob}$ represents $\vec{V}_B$. Its length gives the magnitude:

$$V_B = \vert ob \vert \times v_s$$

The line $\vec{ob}$ gives the direction of $\vec{V}_B$ (which should be perpendicular to $O_4 B$ as a verification).

**Step 6: Determine the Angular Velocities**

$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_{BA}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{\vert ab \vert \times v_s}{L_{AB}}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{V_B}{L_{O_4 B}} = \frac{\vert ob \vert \times v_s}{L_{O_4 B}}$$

The **sense** (CW or CCW) of $\omega_3$ is found by observing whether the vector $\vec{ab}$ is rotated $90°$ CW or CCW from the line $AB$ in the configuration diagram (with the same scale rotated vector).

## 3.4 Graphical Velocity Analysis — Slider-Crank Mechanism

### Setup

Consider a slider-crank with:
- $O_2 A$ is the **crank** of length $r$, rotating CCW at $\omega_2$
- $A B$ is the **connecting rod** of length $L$
- $B$ is the **slider** moving along a horizontal line passing through $O_2$
- Slider position given by crank angle $\theta_2$

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1: Configuration Diagram**

Draw the slider-crank to scale with the crank at angle $\theta_2$ and the slider at the correct position (found using the displacement equation).

**Step 2: Compute $V_A$**

$$V_A = \omega_2 \cdot r$$

Direction: $\vec{V}_A$ is perpendicular to $O_2 A$. Draw $\vec{oa}$ on the velocity polygon.

**Step 3: Direction of $\vec{V}_{BA}$**

Since $A$ and $B$ are on the rigid connecting rod, $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$. Draw a line through 'a' in this direction.

**Step 4: Direction of $\vec{V}_B$**

The slider moves along a fixed straight-line path. Therefore $\vec{V}_B$ is **along the slider's line of motion** (horizontal, in this case). Draw a horizontal line from 'o' (or set the direction of $\vec{ob}$ to be horizontal).

**Step 5: Locate Point $b$**

The intersection of the line from Step 3 (perpendicular to $AB$) and the horizontal line from Step 4 gives the point $b$.

**Step 6: Read Quantities**

$$V_B = \vert ob \vert \times v_s \quad \text{(slider velocity, horizontal)}$$

$$V_{BA} = \vert ab \vert \times v_s$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_{BA}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{\vert ab \vert \times v_s}{L_{AB}}$$

The **direction of slider motion** is given by the sign of $\vec{ob}$ relative to the chosen positive horizontal axis.

## 3.5 Analytical Verification — Vector Loop for Four-Bar

The vector loop equation for the four-bar is:

$$\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_4 - \vec{r}_1 = 0$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\omega_2 \vec{r}_2 + \omega_3 \vec{r}_3 - \omega_4 \vec{r}_4 = 0$$

Resolving into $x$ and $y$ components:

$$-r\omega_2 \sin\theta_2 - L\omega_3 \sin\theta_3 + R\omega_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

$$r\omega_2 \cos\theta_2 + L\omega_3 \cos\theta_3 - R\omega_4 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

These are two linear equations in the two unknowns $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$, which can be solved algebraically. The graphical method gives the *same* results to within drawing accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Practice:** Always cross-verify the graphical result by checking that the *direction* of $\vec{V}_{BA}$ on the polygon is exactly perpendicular to $AB$ in the configuration diagram (after rotating to the same orientation). Drawing errors commonly arise from this.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Kutzbach's Formula Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Start: Kinematic Chain"] --> node2["Count Links n (include ground)"]
    node2 --> node3["Count Lower Pairs j1 (R, P joints)"]
    node3 --> node4["Count Higher Pairs j2 (cams, gears)"]
    node4 --> node5["Apply F = 3(n-1) - 2j1 - j2"]
    node5 --> node6{"Check F Value"}
    node6 --> node7["F = 1 : Determinate Mechanism"]
    node6 --> node8["F = 0 : Structure (no motion)"]
    node6 --> node9["F < 0 : Over-constrained / Locked"]
    node6 --> node10["F > 1 : Multi-input Mechanism"]
    node7 --> node11["Proceed to Velocity Analysis"]
    node8 --> node12["Redesign with fewer constraints"]
    node9 --> node13["Add passive DOF or passive link"]
    node10 --> node14["Apply multiple drivers"]
    node11 --> node15["Graphical Velocity Polygon Method"]
    node15 --> node16["Draw configuration to scale"]
    node16 --> node17["Compute VA = omega2 x r"]
    node17 --> node18["Draw velocity vectors using relative velocity"]
    node18 --> node19["Read omega3, omega4 from polygon"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Graphical Velocity Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT: Geometry and omega2"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Draw Configuration"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Compute VA perpendicular to O2A"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Draw oa on polygon"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Four Bar or Slider Crank"}
    nodeE --> nodeF["Four Bar: VB perpendicular to O4B"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Slider Crank: VB along slider path"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Step 4: Draw ab perpendicular to AB"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Step 5: Locate point b at intersection"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Step 6: Measure ob and ab"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Step 7: Compute omega3 = VAB / LAB"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Step 8: Compute omega4 = VB / LO4B"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["OUTPUT: omega3 and omega4"]
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Four-Bar Mechanism Velocity Polygon Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1["Origin o (zero velocity)"] --> nodeP2["Lay off vector oa representing VA"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["From a: draw line perpendicular to AB"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["From o: draw line perpendicular to O4B"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Intersection gives point b"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["Vector ob = VB (magnitude and direction)"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP7["Vector ab = VBA (magnitude and direction)"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP8["Scale by velocity scale vs to find actual VB"]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP9["omega3 = VBA / LAB"]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP10["omega4 = VB / LO4B"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Slider-Crank Velocity Polygon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["Origin o"] --> nodeS2["Lay off oa perpendicular to crank O2A"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["From a: line perpendicular to connecting rod AB"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["From o: horizontal line representing slider path"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Intersection is point b"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Vector ob = VB (slider velocity, horizontal)"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7["Vector ab = VBA (perpendicular to AB)"]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS8["VB = length ob times velocity scale"]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9["omega3 of connecting rod = VBA / LAB"]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description — Configuration and Polygon Layout

Since the configuration diagram and the velocity polygon are *physical* drawings (not symbolic), the KTU board expects them to be hand-drawn on graph paper to scale. The proper layout is:

- **Top half of sheet**: Configuration diagram with all four-bar or slider-crank links drawn to a chosen **length scale** (e.g., $10 \text{ mm} = 50 \text{ mm}$).
- **Bottom half of sheet**: Velocity polygon drawn with all velocity vectors using a **velocity scale** (e.g., $10 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$).
- **Both diagrams** must have clearly labeled points (uppercase on top, lowercase on bottom), link lengths, and arrow indicators for direction of motion.
- **Sense of rotation** indicated by curved arrows ($\omega_2$ CCW or CW).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:** When you draw a velocity polygon, always start at 'o' (pole of zero velocity). Then use the relative velocity equation $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$ as a *path*: from 'o' go to 'a' (vector $\vec{V}_A$), then from 'a' go to 'b' (vector $\vec{V}_{BA}$). The straight line from 'o' to 'b' is the resultant $\vec{V}_B$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Kutzbach's Formula Application `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms. A four-bar mechanism has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Determine its degrees of freedom.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms gives the number of degrees of freedom (mobility) of a kinematic chain as
> $$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
> where $n$ is the total number of links, $j_1$ the number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints), and $j_2$ the number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints).
>
> For the given four-bar mechanism: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (four revolute joints), $j_2 = 0$.
>
> Substituting:
> $$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$
>
> The mechanism has **one degree of freedom**, meaning a single input (crank rotation) is sufficient to produce deterministic motion of all other links. **(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for interpretation)**

### Question 2: Velocity Analysis Concepts `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define velocity analysis. State the relative velocity equation for two points on a rigid body in plane motion and explain the significance of the direction of $\vec{V}_{BA}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Velocity analysis** is the determination of the linear and angular velocities of all moving links in a mechanism for a given configuration and a given input angular velocity of the driver link.
>
> The relative velocity equation for any two points $A$ and $B$ on a rigid body is
> $$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$$
> where $\vec{V}_B$ and $\vec{V}_A$ are the absolute velocities of points $B$ and $A$, and $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is the velocity of $B$ relative to $A$.
>
> The relative velocity $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is **always perpendicular to the line $AB$**, because the distance $AB$ is constant in a rigid body, so the relative motion of $B$ w.r.t. $A$ is purely rotational about $A$. The magnitude is $\vert V_{BA} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$, where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the link and $L_{AB}$ is the link length. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for direction rule and significance)**

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1 Choice 1]`

In a four-bar mechanism $O_2 A B O_4$, the crank $O_2 A = 100 \text{ mm}$ rotates uniformly at $120 \text{ rpm}$ in the clockwise direction. The coupler $AB = 300 \text{ mm}$, the rocker $O_4 B = 250 \text{ mm}$, and the fixed link $O_2 O_4 = 350 \text{ mm}$. The angle of the crank with the fixed link is $60°$ (CCW from $O_2 O_4$).

**Find using the graphical (velocity polygon) method:**
- **(a) Angular velocities of the coupler and the rocker** (7 marks)
- **(b) Velocities of the midpoint of the coupler and the pin at $A$** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Angular Velocities (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $r = O_2 A = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $L = AB = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $R = O_4 B = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $D = O_2 O_4 = 350 \text{ mm} = 0.35 \text{ m}$
- $\theta_2 = 60°$ CCW
- $N_2 = 120 \text{ rpm} \Rightarrow \omega_2 = \dfrac{2 \pi N_2}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi (120)}{60} = 4\pi \text{ rad/s} = 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$ (CW)

**Step 1:** Velocity of point $A$:
$$V_A = \omega_2 \cdot r = 12.566 \times 0.1 = 1.2566 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to $O_2 A$. Since $\omega_2$ is CW, $V_A$ points in the direction obtained by rotating $O_2 A$ vector by $90°$ CW.

**Step 2:** Draw the configuration diagram to scale. Choose length scale, e.g., $1 \text{ cm} = 50 \text{ mm}$. (1 mark)

**Step 3:** On the velocity polygon, choose a velocity scale, e.g., $1 \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$, so:
$$\vert oa \vert = \frac{V_A}{v_s} = \frac{1.2566}{0.5 \times 100} = 0.0251 \text{ m} = 2.51 \text{ cm}$$

Draw $\vec{oa}$ of length $2.51 \text{ cm}$ in the direction perpendicular to $O_2 A$ (with CW sense). (1 mark)

**Step 4:** From point 'a', draw a line perpendicular to $AB$ (this is the locus of $\vec{ab} = \vec{V}_{BA}$). (1 mark)

**Step 5:** From origin 'o', draw a line perpendicular to $O_4 B$ (this is the locus of $\vec{ob} = \vec{V}_B$). (1 mark)

**Step 6:** The intersection of these two lines gives point 'b'. Measure $\vert ob \vert$ and $\vert ab \vert$ from the polygon. Suppose measurements yield $\vert ob \vert = 2.0 \text{ cm}$ and $\vert ab \vert = 1.8 \text{ cm}$. (1 mark)

**Step 7:** Calculate the angular velocities:

$$V_B = \vert ob \vert \times v_s = 2.0 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{BA} = \vert ab \vert \times v_s = 1.8 \times 0.5 = 0.9 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_{BA}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{0.9}{0.3} = 3.0 \text{ rad/s (CCW)}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{V_B}{L_{O_4 B}} = \frac{1.0}{0.25} = 4.0 \text{ rad/s (CCW)}$$

**(2 marks — 1 for $V_B$ & $V_{BA}$, 1 for $\omega_3$ & $\omega_4$ with units and sense)**

**Part (b) — Velocities of Midpoint and Point $A$ (7 marks)**

**Velocity of $A$** is already computed in part (a):

$$V_A = 1.2566 \text{ m/s, perpendicular to } O_2 A \text{ (CW sense)}$$

(1 mark)

**Velocity of midpoint $M$ of coupler $AB$:**

The midpoint $M$ lies on the rigid coupler link 3. The relative velocity of $M$ w.r.t. $A$ is:

$$\vec{V}_{MA} = \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{MA}, \quad \vert V_{MA} \vert = \omega_3 \cdot \frac{L_{AB}}{2} = 3.0 \times 0.15 = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to $AM$ (which is along the same line as $AB$).

Therefore, on the velocity polygon, the point $m$ lies at the **midpoint** of the segment $ab$ (since $M$ is the geometric midpoint of $AB$ on a rigid body, and the velocity polygon maps points affinely). (2 marks)

**Reading from polygon:** Measure $\vert om \vert$. Suppose $\vert om \vert = 1.6 \text{ cm}$.

$$V_M = \vert om \vert \times v_s = 1.6 \times 0.5 = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$$

(2 marks)

**Direction** of $\vec{V}_M$ is given by the line $\vec{om}$ on the polygon. (1 mark)

**Verification of $V_A$:** $\vec{V}_A$ corresponds to vector $\vec{oa}$ which has length $2.51 \text{ cm}$ giving $1.2566 \text{ m/s}$, matching the calculation in part (a). (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently make these mistakes on four-bar velocity polygon problems:
> 1. **Wrong direction of $\vec{V}_A$**: For CW input, $V_A$ is obtained by rotating $O_2 A$ vector 90° CW (not CCW). Use the **right-hand rule** carefully.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{BA}$ and $V_B$**: $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is *relative* velocity (drawn from 'a' to 'b'), while $\vec{V}_B$ is *absolute* (drawn from 'o' to 'b').
> 3. **Wrong sense for $\omega_3, \omega_4$**: To find the sense, transfer the vector $\vec{ab}$ parallel to itself from point $A$ in the configuration diagram; if it is rotated 90° CCW from $AB$, then $\omega_3$ is CCW.
> 4. **Forgetting units**: Always state the angular velocity in rad/s, not rpm, in the final answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1 Choice 2]`

A slider-crank mechanism has a crank $O_2 A = 150 \text{ mm}$ and a connecting rod $AB = 600 \text{ mm}$. The crank rotates at $300 \text{ rpm}$ CCW. The crank angle is $45°$ measured from the line of stroke (the slider moves along the horizontal line passing through $O_2$).

**Using the graphical (velocity polygon) method, determine:**
- **(a) The velocity of the slider and the angular velocity of the connecting rod** (7 marks)
- **(b) The velocity of point $C$ which is the midpoint of the connecting rod, and the velocity of point $A$** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Slider Velocity and Coupler Angular Velocity (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $r = O_2 A = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $L = AB = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $N_2 = 300 \text{ rpm} \Rightarrow \omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi (300)}{60} = 10\pi \text{ rad/s} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$ (CCW)
- $\theta_2 = 45°$ from the line of stroke

**Step 1: Compute slider position** (for configuration diagram):

The horizontal distance from $O_2$ to slider $B$ is:
$$x_B = r \cos\theta_2 + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta_2}$$

$$x_B = 0.15 \cos 45° + \sqrt{0.6^2 - 0.15^2 \sin^2 45°}$$

$$x_B = 0.15 \times 0.7071 + \sqrt{0.36 - 0.01125}$$

$$x_B = 0.1061 + \sqrt{0.34875} = 0.1061 + 0.5906 = 0.6967 \text{ m}$$

So $B$ is at $0.6967 \text{ m}$ to the right of $O_2$ on the horizontal line.

**Step 2: Configuration diagram** drawn to scale with $O_2$, $A$ at angle $45°$ from $O_2$, and $B$ at the calculated position. (1 mark)

**Step 3: Compute $V_A$**:

$$V_A = \omega_2 \cdot r = 31.416 \times 0.15 = 4.7124 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to $O_2 A$. For CCW rotation, $V_A$ is obtained by rotating $O_2 A$ by 90° CCW.

**Step 4: Choose velocity scale**, e.g., $1 \text{ cm} = 2 \text{ m/s}$. Then:
$$\vert oa \vert = \frac{4.7124}{2 \times 100} = 0.02356 \text{ m} = 2.356 \text{ cm}$$

Draw $\vec{oa}$ on the velocity polygon. (1 mark)

**Step 5:** From 'a', draw a line **perpendicular to $AB$**. (1 mark)

**Step 6:** From 'o', draw a **horizontal line** (along the slider's path of motion). (1 mark)

**Step 7:** The intersection of these two lines gives point 'b'. Measure $\vert ob \vert$ and $\vert ab \vert$. (1 mark)

Suppose $\vert ob \vert = 2.20 \text{ cm}$ and $\vert ab \vert = 0.85 \text{ cm}$.

**Step 8: Calculate the slider velocity and angular velocity of connecting rod:**

$$V_B = \vert ob \vert \times v_s = 2.20 \times 2 = 4.40 \text{ m/s (horizontal, towards right)}$$

$$V_{BA} = \vert ab \vert \times v_s = 0.85 \times 2 = 1.70 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_{BA}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{1.70}{0.6} = 2.833 \text{ rad/s (CW)}$$

(The sense is determined by the direction of $\vec{V}_{BA}$ — if rotating the vector $AB$ 90° gives $\vec{V}_{BA}$, then the rotation is CCW; here, due to geometry, it is CW.) (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Velocity of Midpoint $C$ and Point $A$ (7 marks)**

**Velocity of $A$:** Already computed in part (a):

$$V_A = 4.7124 \text{ m/s, perpendicular to } O_2 A \text{ (CCW sense)}$$

(1 mark)

**Velocity of midpoint $C$:**

The point $C$ is the geometric midpoint of $AB$. The relative velocity of $C$ w.r.t. $A$ is:

$$\vec{V}_{CA} = \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{CA}, \quad \vert V_{CA} \vert = \omega_3 \cdot \frac{L_{AB}}{2} = 2.833 \times 0.3 = 0.85 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to $AC$ (i.e., perpendicular to $AB$). (2 marks)

**Graphical construction:** On the velocity polygon, the point $c$ lies at the **midpoint of $ab$** (because of the affine nature of the velocity polygon for rigid bodies). Measure $\vert oc \vert$. Suppose $\vert oc \vert = 1.65 \text{ cm}$. (2 marks)

**Reading the velocity:**

$$V_C = \vert oc \vert \times v_s = 1.65 \times 2 = 3.30 \text{ m/s}$$

(1 mark)

**Direction:** Given by the line $\vec{oc}$ on the polygon (inclined, not horizontal — confirming that $C$ has both horizontal and vertical velocity components). (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Slider-Crank Specific):**
> 1. **Wrong direction of slider velocity**: The slider velocity $\vec{V}_B$ is always **along the line of stroke** (typically horizontal). The line of action is drawn from 'o' along the slider direction.
> 2. **Misidentifying "crank angle"**: The KTU convention measures the crank angle from the **line of stroke**, not from the fixed link as in a four-bar. Check the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Position calculation error**: If the problem gives the slider position, you don't need to compute $x_B$. If only crank angle is given, the displacement equation $x_B = r \cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}$ must be used.
> 4. **Sense of slider motion**: The direction of $\vec{V}_B$ on the polygon (left or right of 'o' along the horizontal line) tells whether the slider is moving **towards** or **away from** $O_2$. The KTU board requires the explicit statement "slider moves towards $O_2$" or "away from $O_2$".
> 5. **Midpoint construction**: The midpoint $C$ of the connecting rod corresponds to the midpoint of the vector $\vec{ab}$ in the velocity polygon, *not* the midpoint of $\vec{ob}$. This is a frequent error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 Topic:**
>
> **A. Kutzbach's Formula Essentials**
> - **Planar formula**: $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
> - **Spatial formula** (Grübler): $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
> - **Always include the ground link** in the count of $n$.
> - **Lower pairs**: revolute (R), prismatic (P) — contribute $-2$ each.
> - **Higher pairs**: cam-follower, gear mesh — contribute $-1$ each.
> - **Interpretation**: $F=1$ (determinate), $F=0$ (structure), $F<0$ (locked), $F>1$ (multi-input).
>
> **B. Velocity Analysis — Core Vector Identity**
> - **Relative velocity equation**: $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$
> - **Direction rule**: $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$ (perpendicular to the line connecting the two points on the rigid body).
> - **Magnitude rule**: $\vert V_{BA} \vert = \omega_3 \cdot L_{AB}$
> - **Subscript convention**: $\vec{V}_{BA}$ = velocity of $B$ *with respect to* $A$.
>
> **C. Velocity Polygon Conventions (KTU Board Standard)**
> - **Origin 'o'** is the pole of zero velocity.
> - **Uppercase letters in mechanism = lowercase in polygon**: $A \leftrightarrow a$, $B \leftrightarrow b$, etc.
> - **Vector from o to x** = absolute velocity $\vec{V}_X$.
> - **Vector from x to y** = relative velocity $\vec{V}_{YX}$ (note the order reversal).
> - **Choose a velocity scale** $v_s$ (e.g., $1 \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$).
> - **Always state the scale** explicitly on the answer sheet.
>
> **D. Four-Bar Mechanism — Quick Reference**
> - $\vec{V}_A \perp O_2 A$, magnitude $\omega_2 \cdot O_2 A$
> - $\vec{V}_B \perp O_4 B$, magnitude $\omega_4 \cdot O_4 B$
> - $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$, magnitude $\omega_3 \cdot AB$
> - **Polygon construction**: From 'o' draw $\vec{oa}$; from 'a' draw $\perp$ to $AB$; from 'o' draw $\perp$ to $O_4 B$; intersection is 'b'.
> - Angular velocities: $\omega_3 = V_{BA}/L_{AB}$, $\omega_4 = V_B/L_{O_4 B}$
>
> **E. Slider-Crank Mechanism — Quick Reference**
> - $\vec{V}_A \perp O_2 A$, magnitude $\omega_2 \cdot r$
> - $\vec{V}_B$ along the **line of stroke** (horizontal in standard convention)
> - $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$, magnitude $\omega_3 \cdot L$
> - **Polygon construction**: From 'o' draw $\vec{oa}$; from 'a' draw $\perp$ to $AB$; from 'o' draw line of stroke direction; intersection is 'b'.
> - Angular velocity of connecting rod: $\omega_3 = V_{BA}/L_{AB}$
>
> **F. Sense of Angular Velocity — Key Trick**
> - Transfer the relative velocity vector $\vec{V}_{BA}$ (i.e., vector $\vec{ab}$) **parallel to itself** to point $A$ in the configuration diagram.
> - If you must rotate $\vec{V}_{BA}$ by 90° **CCW** to align with $\vec{AB}$, then $\omega_3$ is **CCW**.
> - If you must rotate $\vec{V}_{BA}$ by 90° **CW** to align with $\vec{AB}$, then $\omega_3$ is **CW**.
>
> **G. Common KTU Board Traps**
> - Forgetting to label the velocity scale on the diagram.
> - Drawing $\vec{V}_{BA}$ along $AB$ (must be perpendicular).
> - Forgetting to specify CW/CCW sense of $\omega_3, \omega_4$.
> - Mislabeling vectors in the polygon (e.g., writing 'ab' when meaning 'ba').
> - Not verifying the perpendicularity conditions after construction.
> - Using the wrong sign convention for the crank angle (especially in slider-crank).
> - Drawing the velocity polygon too small, leading to measurement errors.
>
> **H. Suggested Drawing Scales (KTU Board Exam)**
> - Configuration diagram: $1 \text{ cm} = 50 \text{ mm}$ or $1 \text{ cm} = 100 \text{ mm}$
> - Velocity polygon: $1 \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$ or $1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ m/s}$
> - Always use **graph paper** with fine grid for accurate measurement.
> - Use a **sharp pencil** and a **30°-60° set square** to draw perpendiculars accurately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of the Acceleration Analysis Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition of the Acceleration Analysis Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Acceleration Analysis Problem** in planar mechanism kinematics is formally defined as:

> *Given the geometry (link lengths and configuration) of a planar linkage and the input motion (input angular velocity $\omega_2$ and input angular acceleration $\alpha_2$ of the driver link), determine the angular accelerations of all other moving links ($\alpha_3, \alpha_4, ...$) and the linear accelerations of all points of interest in the mechanism.*

This is the **third and most computationally intensive stage** of complete kinematic analysis, performed sequentially after **position analysis** and **velocity analysis**. It represents the highest-order derivative analysis in classical machine kinematics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** Acceleration analysis is a direct continuation of velocity analysis. In every problem, the inputs are: (1) link lengths, (2) $\omega_2$ and $\alpha_2$. The unknowns are angular accelerations of remaining links. All vectors are **2D planar vectors**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Bicycle Wheel

Imagine you are cycling on a bicycle. You can easily **see** where the pedals are (position), you can roughly estimate **how fast** they are moving (velocity), but imagine trying to figure out how *quickly the speed itself is changing* — that is acceleration. Now, suppose one pedal is connected by a chain to the rear wheel, and the rear wheel is connected to a small gear that turns another linkage. If you know how fast *you* are pedaling and how quickly you are speeding up, can you figure out how the connected wheel is *also* speeding up? That is exactly the **acceleration analysis problem** — tracing the second derivative of motion through an interconnected mechanism.

- **Driver link** (you/pedals) = known $\omega_2$ and $\alpha_2$
- **Driven links** (rear wheel, gears, linkages) = unknown $\alpha_3$, $\alpha_4$, ...

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Levels of Kinematic Analysis:**
> | Level | What we know | What we find |
> |---|---|---|
> | Position | Geometry, $\theta_2$ | $\theta_3$, $\theta_4$, coordinates |
> | Velocity | Position, $\omega_2$ | $\omega_3$, $\omega_4$, $\vec{v}$ of points |
> | Acceleration | Position, velocity, $\omega_2$, $\alpha_2$ | $\alpha_3$, $\alpha_4$, $\vec{a}$ of points |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Input angular velocity:** $\omega_2$ in **rad/s** (positive for CCW)
- **Input angular acceleration:** $\alpha_2$ in **rad/s²** (positive for CCW)
- **All link lengths:** in **m** or **mm** (be consistent!)
- **Default reference frame:** Ground (inertial), fixed pivot coordinates.
- **Sign convention:** Standard right-handed coordinate system; CCW = positive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Acceleration Analysis Exists

The acceleration analysis is essential for:

1. **Dynamic Force Analysis** — Newton's second law $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ requires accelerations of all mass centers.
2. **Dynamic Balancing** — Inertial forces in reciprocating machinery depend on $\vec{a}$.
3. **Cam Design** — Cam profile curvature relates to follower acceleration.
4. **Vibration & Resonance Avoidance** — Rapid acceleration changes can excite natural frequencies.
5. **Motor/Torque Sizing** — The torque required to drive a mechanism is proportional to angular accelerations of links.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Problem" in the title?** Because in real mechanisms, the system of equations for unknown accelerations is **coupled and non-linear in position** (because the loop-closure equations are trigonometric). This makes it a *solved problem*, not a closed-form problem, except in the simplest cases.

### 2.2 The Two Principal Methodologies

There are two broad families of solution methods:

1. **Vector / Polygon (Loop-Closure) Method** — uses relative acceleration equations graphically or in vector algebra; excellent for understanding direction of components.
2. **Analytical (Loop-Closure Equation) Method** — differentiates the position vector loop twice; yields a **linear system** in $\alpha_i$ and $\ddot{r}_i$ once velocities are known.

For KTU 2024, **both methods are required**, with the analytical method being dominant in numerical problems.

### 2.3 The Master Equation — Relative Acceleration

For any two points $A$ and $B$ on a rigid body, the acceleration of $B$ relative to $A$ has two components:

$$\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$$

Where:

- $\vec{a}_{B/A}^n$ = **Normal (centripetal) acceleration**, magnitude $\omega^2 \cdot L_{AB}$, directed from $B$ **toward** $A$ along the line $AB$.
- $\vec{a}_{B/A}^t$ = **Tangential acceleration**, magnitude $\alpha \cdot L_{AB}$, directed **perpendicular** to $AB$, in the sense of $\alpha$.
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the link carrying $A$ and $B$.
- $\alpha$ = angular acceleration of the link carrying $A$ and $B$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The *normal* component direction is **always from B to A** (centripetal). The *tangential* component direction is **perpendicular to AB** and follows the sense of $\alpha$. This is the most-frequently-mis-drawn vector in KTU scripts.

### 2.4 The Vector Loop Closure Equation (Position Level)

For a four-bar linkage $ABCD$ (A and D are fixed pivots, link 2 = AB, link 3 = BC, link 4 = CD, link 1 = ground AD):

$$\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_1$$

In scalar form:

$$r_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \cos\theta_3 = r_4 \cos\theta_4 + r_1 \cos\theta_1$$
$$r_2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \sin\theta_3 = r_4 \sin\theta_4 + r_1 \sin\theta_1$$

Differentiating **twice** with respect to time gives the acceleration loop equations.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **complete, high-yield formula set** for KTU Module 2 acceleration analysis. Memorize these — they appear in **every** numerical.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Direction |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$ | Master relative-acceleration equation |
| 2 | $\vert a_{B/A}^n \vert = \omega_{AB}^2 \cdot L_{AB}$ | Centripetal magnitude; directed $B \to A$ |
| 3 | $\vert a_{B/A}^t \vert = \alpha_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}$ | Tangential magnitude; $\perp AB$, sense of $\alpha$ |
| 4 | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} - \omega^2 \vec{r}_{B/A}$ | Compact vector form (with cross product) |
| 5 | $a_x = a_{Ax} + \alpha L \sin\theta - \omega^2 L \cos\theta$ | x-component of B w.r.t. A (if $\theta$ measured from x) |
| 6 | $a_y = a_{Ay} - \alpha L \cos\theta - \omega^2 L \sin\theta$ | y-component of B w.r.t. A |
| 7 | $\sum F_x = m a_{Gx}$ | Newton-Euler link (used in dynamics later) |
| 8 | $\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$ | Moment equation about mass center |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Engine Piston Acceleration:** Determining the inertia force on the piston requires $\alpha_2$ of the crank.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Joint accelerations determine actuator torques.
- **Quick-Return Mechanisms (Whitworth, Crank-Shaper):** Acceleration profile affects cutting forces and chip quality.
- **Walking Robots / Bipedal Locomotion:** Smooth foot acceleration is critical to avoid falling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Position Loop to the Acceleration Loop (Four-Bar Linkage)

We start with the closed-loop vector equation for a four-bar linkage $ABCD$:

$$\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_4 - \vec{r}_1 = 0$$

Writing in complex exponential / scalar form:

$$r_2 e^{i\theta_2} + r_3 e^{i\theta_3} = r_1 + r_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

**Step 1 — First derivative (velocity loop):**

Differentiate with respect to time, treating $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ as constants and $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ as functions of time:

$$i r_2 \omega_2 e^{i\theta_2} + i r_3 \omega_3 e^{i\theta_3} = i r_4 \omega_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

Dividing by $i$:

$$r_2 \omega_2 e^{i\theta_2} + r_3 \omega_3 e^{i\theta_3} = r_4 \omega_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

**Step 2 — Second derivative (acceleration loop):**

Differentiate again, applying the product rule. Note that $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\omega_k e^{i\theta_k}\right] = \alpha_k e^{i\theta_2} + i \omega_k^2 e^{i\theta_k}$:

$$r_2 \alpha_2 e^{i\theta_2} + i r_2 \omega_2^2 e^{i\theta_2} + r_3 \alpha_3 e^{i\theta_3} + i r_3 \omega_3^2 e^{i\theta_3} = r_4 \alpha_4 e^{i\theta_4} + i r_4 \omega_4^2 e^{i\theta_4}$$

**Step 3 — Separate into Real and Imaginary (x and y) components:**

Using $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$, and separating into real and imaginary parts:

$$\left[r_2 \alpha_2 \cos\theta_2 - r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \alpha_3 \cos\theta_3 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3\right] = r_4 \alpha_4 \cos\theta_4 - r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4$$

$$\left[r_2 \alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 + r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 + r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3\right] = r_4 \alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 + r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **These two scalar equations are the KTU acceleration loop equations.** They contain two unknowns: $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ (assuming $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ and $\omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$ are already known from prior position + velocity analysis).

**Step 4 — Matrix form (the "Analytical Matrix"):**

Rearranging the two scalar equations with $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ as unknowns:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[r_3 \cos\theta_3\right] \alpha_3 - \left[r_4 \cos\theta_4\right] \alpha_4 &= -r_2 \alpha_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 - r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4 \\
\left[r_3 \sin\theta_3\right] \alpha_3 - \left[r_4 \sin\theta_4\right] \alpha_4 &= -r_2 \alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 + r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r_3 \cos\theta_3 & -r_4 \cos\theta_4 \\ r_3 \sin\theta_3 & -r_4 \sin\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_3 \\ \alpha_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} A_x \\ A_y \end{Bmatrix}$$

Where $A_x$ and $A_y$ are the **known RHS** quantities containing $\omega$'s and $\alpha_2$. This is a $2 \times 2$ linear system solved using **Cramer's rule** in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relative Acceleration Vector Components (x and y)

For a rigid link $AB$ with $A$ at the origin, $B$ at distance $L$ at angle $\theta$:

Position of B: $\quad x_B = L\cos\theta, \quad y_B = L\sin\theta$

First derivative (velocity):

$$\dot{x}_B = -L\omega\sin\theta, \quad \dot{y}_B = L\omega\cos\theta$$

Second derivative (acceleration):

$$\ddot{x}_B = -L\alpha\sin\theta - L\omega^2\cos\theta$$
$$\ddot{y}_B = L\alpha\cos\theta - L\omega^2\sin\theta$$

**Compact form of acceleration of B:**

$$\vec{a}_B = -L\omega^2\left(\cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j}\right) + L\alpha\left(-\sin\theta \hat{i} + \cos\theta \hat{j}\right)$$

The first term is the **normal (centripetal) component** — directed from B toward A (i.e., along $-\hat{r}_{AB}$). The second term is the **tangential component** — perpendicular to $AB$, with direction determined by the sign of $\alpha$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Four-Bar Linkage

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A four-bar linkage $ABCD$ has $r_1 = 100$ mm (ground), $r_2 = 50$ mm (input crank AB), $r_3 = 150$ mm (coupler BC), $r_4 = 180$ mm (output CD). At the instant of interest, $\theta_2 = 60°$, $\theta_3 = 100.4°$, $\theta_4 = 250°$. The input crank has $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s (CCW) and $\alpha_2 = 5$ rad/s² (CCW). From velocity analysis, $\omega_3 = -5.79$ rad/s, $\omega_4 = -3.55$ rad/s. **Find $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$.**

**Step 1 — Compute trig values:**

$\cos 60° = 0.500$, $\sin 60° = 0.866$
$\cos 100.4° = -0.181$, $\sin 100.4° = 0.983$
$\cos 250° = -0.342$, $\sin 250° = -0.940$

**Step 2 — Set up the matrix equation:**

Coefficient matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 150 \cdot (-0.181) & -180 \cdot (-0.342) \\ 150 \cdot 0.983 & -180 \cdot (-0.940) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -27.15 & 61.56 \\ 147.45 & 169.20 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the right-hand-side terms:**

Term A (in $r_2 \alpha_2 \cos\theta_2$): $50 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.500 = 125$
Term B (in $r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2$): $50 \cdot 400 \cdot 0.866 = 17320$
Term C (in $r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3$): $150 \cdot 33.52 \cdot 0.983 = 4943.4$
Term D (in $r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4$): $180 \cdot 12.60 \cdot (-0.940) = -2131.3$

Term E (in $r_2 \alpha_2 \sin\theta_2$): $50 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.866 = 216.5$
Term F (in $r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2$): $50 \cdot 400 \cdot 0.500 = 10000$
Term G (in $r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3$): $150 \cdot 33.52 \cdot (-0.181) = -910.1$
Term H (in $r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4$): $180 \cdot 12.60 \cdot (-0.342) = -775.6$

$$A_x = -125 + 17320 + 4943.4 - (-2131.3) = 24269.7$$
$$A_y = -216.5 - 10000 - (-910.1) + (-775.6) = -10082.0$$

**Step 4 — Solve using Cramer's rule:**

Determinant of coefficient matrix:
$$\Delta = (-27.15)(169.20) - (61.56)(147.45) = -4593.78 - 9078.22 = -13672.0$$

Determinant for $\alpha_3$:
$$\Delta_3 = (24269.7)(169.20) - (61.56)(-10082.0) = 4106433.2 + 620549.0 = 4726982.2$$

Determinant for $\alpha_4$:
$$\Delta_4 = (-27.15)(-10082.0) - (24269.7)(147.45) = 273725.3 - 3578668.0 = -3304942.7$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha_3 = \dfrac{\Delta_3}{\Delta} = \dfrac{4726982.2}{-13672.0} \approx -345.7 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

$$\alpha_4 = \dfrac{\Delta_4}{\Delta} = \dfrac{-3304942.7}{-13672.0} \approx 241.7 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Result:** $\alpha_3 \approx -345.7$ rad/s² (CW) and $\alpha_4 \approx 241.7$ rad/s² (CCW).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this decision matrix to choose the right approach in your KTU exam:**

| Problem Type | Recommended Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Slider-crank (single slider) | Analytical loop or vector polygon | Geometry is simpler; one link translates |
| Four-bar (general position) | Loop-closure matrix | Robust, programmable |
| Four-bar (special position: $\theta_2 = 0, 90, 180, 270°$) | Vector polygon / direct substitution | Trigonometry simplifies dramatically |
| Inversions of four-bar (drag-link, crank-rocker, double-rocker) | Vector polygon | Conceptually clearest for understanding inversion behavior |
| Quick-return / Whitworth | Analytical | Need general expression over full cycle |
| Instantaneous center-based problems | Vector polygon w/ IC | ICs give velocities; accelerations still need full vector treatment |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Four-Bar Linkage Geometry and Reference Frames

The acceleration analysis problem is defined over the standard four-bar geometry. The following Mermaid block depicts the **functional/architectural flow of the acceleration analysis workflow**, which is more useful for understanding the procedure than trying to draw a physical sketch in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Set] --> B[Link Lengths r1, r2, r3, r4]
    A --> C[Input Motion omega2, alpha2]
    A --> D[Configuration theta2, theta3, theta4]
    A --> E[Velocities omega3, omega4 from prior step]

    F[Stage 1 Position Analysis] --> D
    G[Stage 2 Velocity Analysis] --> E

    E --> H[Compute Normal Accelerations]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    B --> H

    H --> I[Stage 3 ACCELERATION ANALYSIS]
    I --> J[Build 2x2 Linear Matrix]
    J --> K[Compute RHS Terms]
    K --> L[Solve using Cramer's Rule]
    L --> M[Output: alpha3, alpha4]
    L --> N[Output: Linear Acceleration of Points]

    M --> O[Dynamic Force Analysis - Newton-Euler]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Motor Torque Sizing]
    O --> Q[Dynamic Balancing]
    O --> R[Stress Analysis via F=ma]
```

### 4.2 Vector Decomposition of Relative Acceleration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LINK_AB [RIGID LINK AB]
        A[Point A] -->|Vector rAB, angle theta| B[Point B]
    end

    subgraph ACC_B [Acceleration of Point B]
        A1[a_n = omega squared times L, directed B to A]:::normal
        A2[a_t = alpha times L, perpendicular to AB, sense of alpha]:::tangential
        A3[a_B = a_A + a_n + a_t]:::result
    end

    B -.-> ACC_B
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3

    classDef normal fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tangential fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef result fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Acceleration Polygon Construction Order

For the **vector polygon method** (commonly asked in 3-mark and 7-mark KTU problems), the standard construction order is:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Compute aB from known motion of link 2] --> S2[Step 2: From B, draw aB/C normal, magnitude omega3 squared times r3, direction B to C]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: From tip of aB/C normal, draw unknown aB/C tangential, magnitude alpha3 times r3, perpendicular to BC]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: From B, draw aC known from link 4]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: From tip of aC, draw aC/B normal, magnitude omega3 squared times r3, direction C to B]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: From tip of aC/B normal, the vector that closes the polygon is aC/B tangential, magnitude alpha3 times r3]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Solve geometry for alpha3 and alpha4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this construction works:** It exploits the fact that link 3 is rigid, so the *relative* acceleration between B and C has the same tangential component magnitude whether measured from B or from C. The polygon closes the kinematic loop.
