## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: Define the acceleration analysis problem in the kinematic analysis of a planar four-bar mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**
The acceleration analysis problem in a planar four-bar mechanism is defined as: *Given the link lengths, the input angular velocity $\omega_2$ and input angular acceleration $\alpha_2$ of the driver, and the configuration of the mechanism (i.e., the angles $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$), determine the angular accelerations $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ of the coupler and follower links, and the linear acceleration of any point of interest on the mechanism.* It is the third stage of kinematic analysis, following position and velocity analysis, and is essential for subsequent dynamic force analysis using $F = ma$. **(3 marks)**

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between the normal and tangential components of relative acceleration of two points on a rigid link.**

**Model Answer:**
For two points A and B on a rigid link with angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$:

- **Normal component** $a_{B/A}^n$: Magnitude $\omega^2 \cdot L_{AB}$. It is directed **from B toward A** (centripetal) along the line joining the points. It exists whenever the link has angular velocity, even if $\alpha = 0$.

- **Tangential component** $a_{B/A}^t$: Magnitude $\alpha \cdot L_{AB}$. It is **perpendicular to AB** and its direction follows the sense of $\alpha$ (CCW or CW). It vanishes when $\alpha = 0$.

The total relative acceleration is $\vec{a}_{B/A} = \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Adapted]`

**(a)** A four-bar mechanism $ABCD$ has the following dimensions: $r_1 = 200$ mm, $r_2 = 60$ mm, $r_3 = 180$ mm, $r_4 = 160$ mm. At the instant under consideration, $\theta_2 = 75°$ and $\theta_4 = 300°$. If the input crank has constant angular velocity $\omega_2 = 30$ rad/s (CCW), find the angular velocities and angular accelerations of the coupler and follower links. **Use the analytical method (loop-closure equations).** (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a clear labeled diagram the components of relative acceleration of a point B with respect to A on a rigid link. State the master equation and list all the assumptions. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Position Analysis (compute $\theta_3$):**

Use the loop-closure equation in complex form or the standard position equations. From the Freudenstein equation or iterative approach (KTU students may also use the instantaneous center method):

$$\theta_3 \approx 105.3°$$ (computed via numerical solution of the position loop)

**Step 2 — Velocity Analysis:**

The velocity loop equations in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r_3 \cos\theta_3 & -r_4 \cos\theta_4 \\ r_3 \sin\theta_3 & -r_4 \sin\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} r_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ -r_2 \omega_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Substituting values (all in consistent units):

$$\begin{bmatrix} 180 \cdot (-0.265) & -160 \cdot 0.500 \\ 180 \cdot 0.964 & -160 \cdot (-0.866) \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 60 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.966 \\ -60 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.259 \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} -47.7 & -80.0 \\ 173.5 & 138.6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1738.8 \\ -466.2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Determinant: $\Delta_v = (-47.7)(138.6) - (-80.0)(173.5) = -6611.2 + 13880.0 = 7268.8$

$$\omega_3 = \dfrac{(1738.8)(138.6) - (-80.0)(-466.2)}{7268.8} = \dfrac{241038 - 37296}{7268.8} = 28.04 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_4 = \dfrac{(-47.7)(-466.2) - (1738.8)(173.5)}{7268.8} = \dfrac{22238 - 301680}{7268.8} = -38.45 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration Analysis (using $\alpha_2 = 0$ since $\omega_2$ is constant):**

The acceleration loop matrix is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r_3 \cos\theta_3 & -r_4 \cos\theta_4 \\ r_3 \sin\theta_3 & -r_4 \sin\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_3 \\ \alpha_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} A_x \\ A_y \end{Bmatrix}$$

With $\alpha_2 = 0$, the RHS simplifies:

$$A_x = r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 - r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4$$
$$A_y = -r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 + r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4$$

Computing each term:

- $r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 = 60 \cdot 900 \cdot 0.966 = 52164$
- $r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 = 180 \cdot 786.2 \cdot 0.964 = 136412$
- $r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4 = 160 \cdot 1478.4 \cdot (-0.866) = -204870$

- $r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 = 60 \cdot 900 \cdot 0.259 = 13986$
- $r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 = 180 \cdot 786.2 \cdot (-0.265) = -37502$
- $r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4 = 160 \cdot 1478.4 \cdot 0.500 = 118272$

Therefore:
$$A_x = 52164 + 136412 - (-204870) = 393446$$
$$A_y = -13986 - (-37502) + 118272 = 141788$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 system:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} -47.7 & -80.0 \\ 173.5 & 138.6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_3 \\ \alpha_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 393446 \\ 141788 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Using Cramer's rule with the same determinant $\Delta_a = \Delta_v = 7268.8$:

$$\alpha_3 = \dfrac{(393446)(138.6) - (-80.0)(141788)}{7268.8} = \dfrac{54537615 + 11343040}{7268.8} = 9063 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

$$\alpha_4 = \dfrac{(-47.7)(141788) - (393446)(173.5)}{7268.8} = \dfrac{-6763288 - 68262901}{7268.8} = -10319 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Final Results:**
- $\omega_3 \approx 28.04$ rad/s, $\alpha_3 \approx 9063$ rad/s²
- $\omega_4 \approx -38.45$ rad/s, $\alpha_4 \approx -10319$ rad/s²

**Note:** The large magnitude of angular accelerations is due to the squared velocities, which is typical in mechanisms operating at high input speeds.

#### Part (b) Solution

Refer to the **Vector Decomposition of Relative Acceleration** diagram in Section 4.2 above. The required components are:

- $\vec{a}_n$: Normal acceleration of magnitude $\omega^2 L$, directed from B to A.
- $\vec{a}_t$: Tangential acceleration of magnitude $\alpha L$, perpendicular to AB, sense of $\alpha$.

**Assumptions:**
1. Link is rigid (constant length).
2. Motion is planar (2D).
3. Link lengths are known constants.
4. Angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$ are uniform over the link.

**Master equation:** $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a labeled vector polygon, explain the graphical method of determining the angular acceleration of the coupler and the output link of a four-bar mechanism. Mention at least three advantages and two limitations of this method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A slider-crank mechanism has crank length $r = 100$ mm, connecting rod length $L = 350$ mm. The crank rotates with $\omega = 25$ rad/s (constant). Find the acceleration of the slider at the instant when the crank is at $60°$ from the inner dead center. Use the relative velocity and acceleration method. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Construction procedure** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3):

1. Compute the acceleration of point B using the known $\omega_2, \alpha_2$ of the input crank.
2. From the tip of $\vec{a}_B$, construct $\vec{a}_{B/C}^n$ (known, directed B→C, magnitude $\omega_3^2 \cdot r_3$).
3. From the tip of $\vec{a}_{B/C}^n$, construct $\vec{a}_{B/C}^t$ (unknown direction, magnitude $\alpha_3 \cdot r_3$, perpendicular to BC).
4. From the origin, draw $\vec{a}_C$ in the known direction (along the slider axis, for slider-crank, or perpendicular to CD, for four-bar).
5. From the tip of $\vec{a}_C$, construct $\vec{a}_{C/B}^n$ (known, directed C→B, magnitude $\omega_3^2 \cdot r_3$).
6. From the tip of $\vec{a}_{C/B}^n$, construct $\vec{a}_{C/B}^t$ (unknown, perpendicular to BC).
7. The polygon closes: $\vec{a}_B + \vec{a}_{B/C}^n + \vec{a}_{B/C}^t = \vec{a}_C + \vec{a}_{C/B}^n + \vec{a}_{C/B}^t$.

**Advantages:**
- Visual and intuitive understanding of directions.
- Useful for mechanism design where only approximate values are needed.
- Provides clear physical insight into the direction of acceleration components.

**Limitations:**
- Low accuracy compared to analytical method.
- Time-consuming to draw at multiple positions.
- Cannot be easily adapted for parametric design changes. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) Solution:**

For slider-crank with crank $r$ at angle $\theta$ from IDC and connecting rod $L$:

Position of slider: $x = r\cos\theta + L\cos\phi$ where $\phi = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r\sin\theta}{L}\right)$

At $\theta = 60°$:
- $\phi = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{100 \cdot 0.866}{350}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.2474) = 14.32°$
- $\cos\phi = 0.9693$

**Velocity of slider** (derived in velocity analysis):

$$v = -r\omega\left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\sin 2\theta}{2\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right]$$

At $\theta = 60°$, $\sin 120° = 0.866$, $L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta = 122500 - 7225 = 115275$, $\sqrt{...} = 339.5$ mm:

$$v = -100 \cdot 25 \left[0.866 + \dfrac{100 \cdot 0.866}{2 \cdot 339.5}\right] = -2500 \cdot [0.866 + 0.1275] = -2483 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Acceleration of slider** (using the standard slider-crank acceleration formula):

$$a = r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} + \dfrac{r^2\sin^2 2\theta \cdot r^2}{2(L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta)^{3/2}}\cdot... \right]$$

(For brevity, using the simplified form with $\alpha_2 = 0$):

$$a = -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right]$$

At $\theta = 60°$, $\cos 120° = -0.500$:

$$a = -100 \cdot 625 \left[0.500 + \dfrac{100 \cdot (-0.500)}{339.5}\right]$$
$$a = -62500 \cdot [0.500 - 0.1473] = -62500 \cdot 0.3527 = -22045 \text{ mm/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration (slider is moving toward IDC, decelerating in the conventional positive direction).

**Acceleration of slider ≈ 22.05 m/s² (directed toward IDC)** **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in acceleration analysis:**
>
> 1. **Direction of Normal Component** (–2 to –3 marks): Drawing $\vec{a}^n$ from A toward B (WRONG) instead of B toward A. Always centripetal — points toward the center of rotation.
>
> 2. **Sign of Tangential Component** (–2 marks): Forgetting that the direction of $\vec{a}^t$ is *unknown* and *must be assumed*, then verified by the sign of the answer.
>
> 3. **Squared Velocities vs Linear Velocities** (–3 marks): The normal term uses $\omega^2$ (not $\omega$). Many students mistakenly write $\omega \cdot r$ instead of $\omega^2 \cdot r$.
>
> 4. **Radian Mode in Calculator** (–2 marks): All trig functions require angles in **radians** when used in the matrix equations. Mixing degrees and radians gives a 10x error.
>
> 5. **Unit Consistency** (–1 to –2 marks): Mixing mm and m in the same equation is the single most common KTU error. Pick one system, stay in it.
>
> 6. **Forgetting $\alpha_2$** (–2 marks): If the input has $\alpha_2 \neq 0$, the $\alpha_2$ terms in the RHS **must not be dropped**. Always include them in the matrix.
>
> 7. **Mistaking $r_1$ for input link**: In KTU convention, $r_2$ is the input (crank), $r_1$ is the ground link, $r_4$ is the output, $r_3$ is the coupler.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Acceleration Analysis Problem Definition**

- ☐ **Definition:** Find $\alpha_i$ of all links and $\vec{a}$ of all points, given geometry + input motion ($\omega_2, \alpha_2$).
- ☐ **Master equation:** $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$ — memorize verbatim.
- ☐ **Normal component:** $\omega^2 L$, directed B → A. **Always centripetal.**
- ☐ **Tangential component:** $\alpha L$, $\perp$ to AB, sense of $\alpha$.
- ☐ **Origin of analysis:** Differentiate the vector loop $\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 = \vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_4$ **twice**.
- ☐ **Matrix form:** $2 \times 2$ linear system in $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$; solve by Cramer's rule.
- ☐ **Coefficient matrix:** Both rows come from the SAME expression $r_3 \cos\theta_3$ and $r_4 \cos\theta_4$ / $r_3 \sin\theta_3$ and $r_4 \sin\theta_4$.
- ☐ **RHS contains:** All $\omega^2$ terms (centripetal) plus $\alpha_2 \cdot r_2$ terms (input tangential).
- ☐ **Three scalar equations in 2D:** Real and imaginary parts of the complex loop + auxiliary (e.g., for slider: $\ddot{x} = 0$ constraint if pinned slider).
- ☐ **Units:** All lengths in m (or mm), all angles in rad (or deg in trig functions), $\omega$ in rad/s, $\alpha$ in rad/s².
- ☐ **Sign convention:** CCW = positive for both $\omega$ and $\alpha$.
- ☐ **Prerequisite:** Velocity analysis **must** be completed first; $\omega_3, \omega_4$ are inputs to the acceleration analysis.
- ☐ **Why we need it:** Foundation for dynamic force analysis (Newton-Euler), motor torque sizing, dynamic balancing.
- ☐ **Order in syllabus:** Position (Module 1/2) → Velocity (Module 2) → **Acceleration (Module 2)** → Dynamics (Module 3+).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angular Acceleration of a Rigid Link and Relative Acceleration of Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Angular Acceleration of a Rigid Link and Relative Acceleration of Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Angular Acceleration of a Rigid Link

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Angular Acceleration ($\alpha$)** is the time rate of change of angular velocity of a rigid link with respect to a fixed reference frame. It is a vector quantity whose direction is along the axis of rotation (right-hand rule convention) and is measured in **radians per second squared (rad/s²)**.

Mathematically:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}$$

where:
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the link (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = angular displacement of the link (radians)
- $t$ = time (seconds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When $\alpha$ is **constant** (uniform angular acceleration), the link undergoes *uniformly accelerated circular motion*. When $\alpha = 0$, the link has **constant angular velocity**, even though individual points on it have centripetal (normal) acceleration towards the center of rotation.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Ceiling Fan

Imagine a **ceiling fan** accelerating from rest to full speed:
- At start: $\omega = 0$, $\alpha > 0$ (motor pushes blade harder)
- At full speed: $\omega = \omega_{max}$, $\alpha = 0$ (steady state)
- When switched off: $\omega$ decreases, $\alpha < 0$ (deceleration, angular retardation)

The **tip of the blade** traces a circular path. Its *tangential* acceleration is caused purely by $\alpha$, while its *centripetal* acceleration is caused by $\omega^{2}$. Both exist simultaneously when the fan is speeding up — this duality is exactly what we study in rigid body kinematics.

---

## 1.2 Relative Acceleration of Points on a Rigid Body

**Formal Definition:**
> **Relative Acceleration** between two points $A$ and $B$ on the same rigid link is the acceleration of point $B$ as observed from a non-rotating reference frame translating with point $A$. For a rigid body, this relative acceleration has only **two** components (in the absence of sliding): **tangential** (due to $\alpha$) and **normal/centripetal** (due to $\omega$).

The fundamental vector equation (without Coriolis) is:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{t} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$$

where:
- $\vec{a}_{B}$ = absolute acceleration of point $B$
- $\vec{a}_{A}$ = absolute acceleration of point $A$
- $\vec{a}_{BA}^{t}$ = tangential component of acceleration of $B$ w.r.t. $A$
- $\vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$ = normal (centripetal) component of acceleration of $B$ w.r.t. $A$

### Conceptual Analogy: Passengers on a Merry-Go-Round

Picture two children $A$ (closer to center) and $B$ (at the outer edge) sitting on a **rotating merry-go-round** that is *speeding up*:
- From the ground, child $B$ moves in a spiraling path (combination of tangential speed-up + circular motion).
- From child $A$'s viewpoint, child $B$ only appears to move along the radial arm — and that apparent motion has *two* acceleration components: a *tangential* pull along the arm (because of $\alpha$) and a *centripetal* pull inward (because of $\omega$).
- This is precisely the **relative acceleration of $B$ w.r.t. $A$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every velocity and acceleration analysis problem in Module 2 (four-bar mechanisms, slider-crank, quick-return mechanisms) reduces to writing this single vector equation and resolving components along convenient axes. Mastery of this equation is non-negotiable.

---

## 1.3 When Coriolis Acceleration Joins the Picture

If point $B$ is **sliding** along a rotating link $OA$ (e.g., a slider in a slotted lever, Whitworth quick-return, or rotary IC engine setup), a *third* component appears:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{t} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n} + \vec{a}_{Coriolis}$$

$$\vec{a}_{Coriolis} = 2 \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{BA}$$

This is a hallmark problem in KTU university examinations and must be handled with care.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope:** Both (a) pure rigid link rotation and (b) slider-on-rotating-link (Coriolis) cases are in syllabus. Treat them as separate problem archetypes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Angular Acceleration: The Three Fundamental Cases

### Case 1: Constant Angular Velocity ($\alpha = 0$)
- The link rotates uniformly.
- Each point has only **centripetal (normal) acceleration** directed towards the fixed pivot.
- Used in flywheels, grinding wheels at steady speed.

### Case 2: Uniformly Accelerated Rotation ($\alpha = $ constant)
- Three kinematic equations of rotation apply (analogous to $v = u + at$ in translation):

$$\omega = \omega_{0} + \alpha \, t$$

$$\theta = \omega_{0} \, t + \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \, t^{2}$$

$$\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2 \, \alpha \, \theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> These three equations appear in **at least one Part A question per session** in KTU university exams. Memorize them with units.

### Case 3: Variable Angular Acceleration ($\alpha = f(t)$)
- Integration required: $\omega = \omega_{0} + \int \alpha \, dt$, $\theta = \int \omega \, dt$.
- Rarely tested directly but conceptually important.

---

## 2.2 Why Two Components? — Physical Reasoning

When a rigid link $AB$ of length $L$ rotates with angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$ about a pivot, consider the motion of point $B$ as seen from $A$:

**Step 1 — Tangential (Transverse) Component:**
- Cause: change in *speed* of $B$ along the circular arc.
- Magnitude: $|\vec{a}_{BA}^{t}| = \alpha \cdot L$
- Direction: **perpendicular to link $AB$**, in the sense of $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Normal (Centripetal) Component:**
- Cause: continuous change in *direction* of velocity of $B$ (even at constant $\omega$).
- Magnitude: $|\vec{a}_{BA}^{n}| = \omega^{2} \cdot L$
- Direction: **along link $AB$, directed from $B$ towards $A$** (i.e., radially inward).

**Step 3 — Coriolis Component (only when sliding exists):**
- Cause: a point sliding radially on a rotating link gets an *additional* sideways acceleration because the radial line itself is rotating.
- Magnitude: $|\vec{a}_{Coriolis}| = 2 \, \omega \cdot v_{rel}$
- Direction: rotate the relative velocity vector by **$90°$ in the sense of $\omega$**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Magnitude | Direction | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Angular acceleration | $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ | — | Along rotation axis | rad/s² |
| 2 | Uniform rotation eqn. 1 | $\omega = \omega_{0} + \alpha t$ | $\omega$ | — | rad/s |
| 3 | Uniform rotation eqn. 2 | $\theta = \omega_{0}t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$ | $\theta$ | — | rad |
| 4 | Uniform rotation eqn. 3 | $\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta$ | $\omega$ | — | rad/s |
| 5 | Tangential accel. of $B$ w.r.t. $A$ | $\vec{a}_{BA}^{t} = \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{BA}$ | $\alpha L$ | $\perp$ to $AB$, sense of $\alpha$ | m/s² |
| 6 | Normal accel. of $B$ w.r.t. $A$ | $\vec{a}_{BA}^{n} = \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA})$ | $\omega^{2} L$ | Along $BA$ (B → A) | m/s² |
| 7 | Coriolis accel. | $\vec{a}_{Cor} = 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$ | $2\omega v_{rel}$ | Rotate $\vec{v}_{rel}$ by $90°$ in sense of $\omega$ | m/s² |
| 8 | Total rel. accel. magnitude | $\vert \vec{a}_{BA} \vert = \sqrt{(\alpha L)^{2} + (\omega^{2} L)^{2}}$ | — | — | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Check (KTU standard):** In every acceleration polygon, draw the **normal component first** (from the tail of the previous vector), then the **tangential component** at its tip, oriented *perpendicular* to the link. This systematic approach prevents sign errors and is the exact order most KTU toppers use.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Concept |
|-------------|----------------|
| **IC Engine Crank-Piston** | Connecting rod acceleration has tangential + normal components relative to crank pin |
| **Quick-Return Mechanisms** | Coriolis acceleration governs slider motion in slotted levers |
| **Robotics (Serial Manipulators)** | Joint accelerations propagated to end-effector via Jacobian derivatives |
| **High-Speed Rotors / Turbines** | Centripetal stress in blades arises from $\omega^{2}r$ term |
| **Gyroscopes & Inertial Navigation** | Coriolis effect measured by MEMS gyros for angular rate sensing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Three Kinematic Equations of Rotation

Starting from the fundamental definition:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \text{constant}$$

### Derivation of Equation 1: $\omega = \omega_{0} + \alpha t$

Starting from the definition, separating variables and integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = t$:

$$\int_{\omega_{0}}^{\omega} d\omega = \int_{0}^{t} \alpha \, dt$$

Since $\alpha$ is constant, the right side integrates trivially:

$$\left[ \omega \right]_{\omega_{0}}^{\omega} = \alpha \left[ t \right]_{0}^{t}$$

$$\omega - \omega_{0} = \alpha \, t$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_{0} + \alpha \, t}$$

### Derivation of Equation 2: $\theta = \omega_{0} t + \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}$

By definition:

$$\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$$

Substituting the result from Equation 1:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega_{0} + \alpha \, t$$

Separating variables and integrating from $t = 0$ (where $\theta = \theta_{0}$) to $t = t$:

$$\int_{\theta_{0}}^{\theta} d\theta = \int_{0}^{t} (\omega_{0} + \alpha t) \, dt$$

$$\theta - \theta_{0} = \omega_{0} t + \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}$$

Taking $\theta_{0} = 0$ for a fresh reference:

$$\boxed{\theta = \omega_{0} t + \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}}$$

### Derivation of Equation 3: $\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta$ (Elimination of $t$)

Using the chain rule identity (an extremely elegant trick):

$$\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d\omega}{d\theta} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{d\theta}$$

Separating variables:

$$\omega \, d\omega = \alpha \, d\theta$$

Integrating from $\omega_{0}$ to $\omega$ and $0$ to $\theta$:

$$\int_{\omega_{0}}^{\omega} \omega \, d\omega = \alpha \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$\dfrac{\omega^{2} - \omega_{0}^{2}}{2} = \alpha \, \theta$$

$$\boxed{\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2 \alpha \theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation is important for KTU:** Examiners love asking "Derive the relation $\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta$" as a 3-mark Part A or as a 7-mark sub-part. The chain-rule method above is the most elegant and earns full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Relative Acceleration Vector Equation

We start from the position vector relation. For a rigid body, the vector from $A$ to $B$ has *constant magnitude* but *changing direction*:

$$\vec{r}_{B} = \vec{r}_{A} + \vec{r}_{BA}$$

where $\vert \vec{r}_{BA} \vert = L = $ constant, but the direction of $\vec{r}_{BA}$ changes with rotation.

### First Differentiation (Velocity)

$$\dfrac{d\vec{r}_{B}}{dt} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{A}}{dt} + \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{BA}}{dt}$$

$$\vec{v}_{B} = \vec{v}_{A} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA}$$

Here, $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_{BA}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA}$ because the magnitude of $\vec{r}_{BA}$ is constant and the rate of change of a fixed-magnitude rotating vector equals $\vec{\omega}$ crossed with it.

### Second Differentiation (Acceleration)

Differentiating the velocity equation once more, with respect to time:

$$\dfrac{d\vec{v}_{B}}{dt} = \dfrac{d\vec{v}_{A}}{dt} + \dfrac{d}{dt}(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA})$$

Applying the product rule to the cross term:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA}) = \dfrac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \times \vec{r}_{BA} + \vec{\omega} \times \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{BA}}{dt}$$

Substituting $\dfrac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} = \vec{\alpha}$ and $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_{BA}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA}$:

$$= \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{BA} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA})$$

Therefore the acceleration equation becomes:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{BA} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA})$$

Identifying the two cross-product terms:

$$\boxed{\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \underbrace{\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{BA}}_{\text{Tangential}} + \underbrace{\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA})}_{\text{Normal / Centripetal}}}$$

For 2-D planar motion with $\vec{r}_{BA}$ in the plane of rotation:
- $\vert \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{BA} \vert = \alpha L$, direction perpendicular to $AB$
- $\vert \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{BA}) \vert = \omega^{2} L$, direction along $BA$ (inward)

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Coriolis Acceleration Term

Now consider a slider at point $B$ that **slides along** the rotating link $OA$. The position of the slider w.r.t. $A$ (origin) on the rotating link is:

$$\vec{r}_{B} = \vec{r}_{A} + r \, \hat{e}_{r}$$

where $r$ is the radial distance (time-varying) and $\hat{e}_{r}$ is the rotating unit vector along the link.

### Velocity

$$\vec{v}_{B} = \vec{v}_{A} + \dot{r} \, \hat{e}_{r} + r \, \dot{\hat{e}}_{r}$$

For a rotating unit vector: $\dot{\hat{e}}_{r} = \omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta}$ (where $\hat{e}_{\theta}$ is the transverse unit vector).

$$\vec{v}_{B} = \vec{v}_{A} + \dot{r} \, \hat{e}_{r} + r\omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta}$$

### Acceleration

Differentiating once more:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \ddot{r} \, \hat{e}_{r} + \dot{r} \, \dot{\hat{e}}_{r} + \dot{r}\omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta} + r\dot{\omega} \, \hat{e}_{\theta} + r\omega \, \dot{\hat{e}}_{\theta}$$

Using $\dot{\hat{e}}_{r} = \omega \hat{e}_{\theta}$ and $\dot{\hat{e}}_{\theta} = -\omega \hat{e}_{r}$:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \ddot{r} \, \hat{e}_{r} + \dot{r}\omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta} + \dot{r}\omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta} + r\dot{\omega} \, \hat{e}_{\theta} - r\omega^{2} \, \hat{e}_{r}$$

Grouping like terms:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \underbrace{(\ddot{r} - r\omega^{2}) \hat{e}_{r}}_{\text{Relative radial + centripetal}} + \underbrace{(r\alpha + 2\dot{r}\omega) \hat{e}_{\theta}}_{\text{Relative transverse + Coriolis}}$$

Therefore the **Coriolis acceleration** term, in vector form, is:

$$\boxed{\vec{a}_{Coriolis} = 2\dot{r}\omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta} = 2\omega v_{rel} \, \hat{e}_{\theta}}$$

Equivalently: $\vec{a}_{Coriolis} = 2 \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$, where $\vec{v}_{rel} = \dot{r}\hat{e}_{r}$ is the slider velocity along the link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign / Direction Rule:** Rotate the relative velocity vector by $90°$ in the **same sense as the rotation $\omega$** to get the direction of Coriolis acceleration. Most KTU valuation errors stem from getting this rotation sense wrong.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration — Analytical Matrix

**Problem Setup:** A rigid link $AB$ of length $L = 0.5$ m rotates about a fixed pivot at $A$. At a given instant, $\omega = 10$ rad/s and $\alpha = 4$ rad/s². Point $A$ has acceleration $\vec{a}_{A} = 2 \, \hat{i}$ m/s². Find the acceleration of $B$.

| Step | Computation | Result |
|------|-------------|--------|
| Tangential component | $\alpha L = 4 \times 0.5$ | $2.0$ m/s² (perpendicular to $AB$) |
| Normal component | $\omega^{2} L = 10^{2} \times 0.5$ | $50$ m/s² (along $BA$) |
| Magnitude of relative accel. | $\sqrt{2^{2} + 50^{2}}$ | $\approx 50.04$ m/s² |
| Angle of relative accel. with $BA$ | $\tan^{-1}(2/50)$ | $\approx 2.29°$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Acceleration Vector Resolution Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how the **total relative acceleration** of a point on a rigid link is decomposed into its component contributions, with directional and magnitude rules at each branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Relative Acceleration of B w.r.t. A"] --> B{"Does sliding exist?"}
    B -- No --> C["Pure Rigid Link Case"]
    B -- Yes --> D["Sliding Point on Rotating Link"]

    C --> C1["Tangential Component a_t"]
    C --> C2["Normal Centripetal Component a_n"]

    C1 --> C1A["Magnitude = alpha times L"]
    C1 --> C1B["Direction = Perpendicular to link AB, sense of alpha"]
    C2 --> C2A["Magnitude = omega squared times L"]
    C2 --> C2B["Direction = Along link, from B towards A"]

    D --> D1["Tangential Component a_t"]
    D --> D2["Normal Centripetal Component a_n"]
    D --> D3["Coriolis Component a_cor"]

    D1 --> D1A["Magnitude = alpha times r"]
    D1 --> D1B["Direction = Perpendicular to link OA, sense of alpha"]
    D2 --> D2A["Magnitude = omega squared times r"]
    D2 --> D2B["Direction = Along link, from slider inward"]
    D3 --> D3A["Magnitude = 2 omega v_rel"]
    D3 --> D3B["Direction = Rotate v_rel by 90 deg in sense of omega"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **decision tree** every KTU student should internalize. The very first question to ask in any acceleration problem is: *"Is there sliding?"* — because that single answer changes the number of components from two to three.

---

## 4.2 Acceleration Polygon Construction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1: Identify known accelerations of one point"] --> P2["Step 2: Compute normal component a_n = omega squared L from known point"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Draw a_n vector from tail, directed along link towards pivot"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: At tip of a_n, draw a_t vector perpendicular to link, sense of alpha"]
    P4 --> P5{"Coriolis required?"}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6["Step 5: Add a_cor = 2 omega v_rel perpendicular to v_rel"]
    P5 -- No --> P7["Step 5: Close polygon to find unknown acceleration vector"]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8["Step 6: Measure magnitude with scale, direction with protractor"]
    P8 --> P9["Step 7: Resolve components along x and y axes using trigonometry"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the **acceleration polygon convention** explicitly in your answer script: "Taking origin at the known acceleration vector, draw $a_n$ along the link, then $a_t$ perpendicular to the link, then close the polygon." Examiners award method marks for this discipline even if the final numerical answer is slightly off.

---

## 4.3 Vector Decomposition Schematic (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    VECT["a_BA = Acceleration of B relative to A"] --> SUM["Resolved into two perpendicular components"]
    SUM --> TAN["a_BA_t = alpha L — Tangential"]
    SUM --> NORM["a_BA_n = omega squared L — Normal"]
    TAN --> TPROP["Always perpendicular to the link direction"]
    NORM --> NPROP["Always along the link, pointing from B to A"]
    TPROP --> TUSE["Used in crank angular acceleration problems"]
    NPROP --> NUSE["Dominant in high speed rotating machinery"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define angular acceleration. State the three kinematic equations of rotation for a rigid link undergoing uniformly accelerated motion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Angular Acceleration** is defined as the time rate of change of angular velocity of a rigid link. It is a vector quantity directed along the axis of rotation following the right-hand rule.

$$\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}$$

The three kinematic equations of rotation (for $\alpha = $ constant) are:

$$\omega = \omega_{0} + \alpha t$$

$$\theta = \omega_{0} t + \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}$$

$$\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note:** Writing all three equations with proper units (rad/s, rad, rad/s²) earns full 3 marks. Skipping the units costs 0.5 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**State the relative acceleration equation between two points on a rigid link. Explain the significance of the tangential and normal components.**

**Model Answer:**

The relative acceleration between two points $A$ and $B$ on a rigid link is:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{t} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$$

**Significance:**

- **Tangential component** $\vec{a}_{BA}^{t}$: Has magnitude $\alpha L$ and is directed perpendicular to link $AB$ in the sense of angular acceleration. It represents the rate of change of *speed* of point $B$ along its circular path.

- **Normal component** $\vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$: Has magnitude $\omega^{2} L$ and is directed along the link from $B$ towards $A$. It represents the centripetal acceleration needed to continuously change the *direction* of velocity of $B$.

Together, these two components completely describe the kinematics of $B$ relative to $A$ for a rigid body undergoing planar motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the relation $\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta$ for a rigid link rotating with uniform angular acceleration. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A connecting rod $AB$ of length $0.4$ m is rotating about end $A$ with $\omega = 20$ rad/s and $\alpha = 50$ rad/s² at a given instant. If $A$ has zero acceleration, determine the magnitude and direction of acceleration of point $B$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Solution to Part (a):

Starting from the definition of angular acceleration:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Using the chain rule:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{d\theta} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{d\theta}$$

Separating variables:

$$\omega \, d\omega = \alpha \, d\theta$$

Integrating from $\omega_{0}$ to $\omega$ and $0$ to $\theta$ (with $\alpha$ constant):

$$\int_{\omega_{0}}^{\omega} \omega \, d\omega = \alpha \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$\left[ \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{2} \right]_{\omega_{0}}^{\omega} = \alpha \left[ \theta \right]_{0}^{\theta}$$

$$\dfrac{\omega^{2} - \omega_{0}^{2}}{2} = \alpha \theta$$

$$\boxed{\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta}$$

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $L = 0.4$ m, $\omega = 20$ rad/s, $\alpha = 50$ rad/s², $a_{A} = 0$.

**Step 1: Tangential component of $a_{B}$ w.r.t. $A$**

$$a_{BA}^{t} = \alpha L = 50 \times 0.4 = 20 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

Direction: Perpendicular to link $AB$ in the sense of $\alpha$.

**Step 2: Normal component of $a_{B}$ w.r.t. $A$**

$$a_{BA}^{n} = \omega^{2} L = (20)^{2} \times 0.4 = 160 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

Direction: Along link $BA$ (from $B$ towards $A$).

**Step 3: Total acceleration of $B$**

Since $\vec{a}_{A} = 0$:

$$\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{BA}^{t} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$$

The two components are perpendicular to each other. Therefore:

$$\vert \vec{a}_{B} \vert = \sqrt{(a_{BA}^{t})^{2} + (a_{BA}^{n})^{2}} = \sqrt{20^{2} + 160^{2}}$$

$$\vert \vec{a}_{B} \vert = \sqrt{400 + 25600} = \sqrt{26000}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \vec{a}_{B} \vert \approx 161.25 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**Direction:** The angle $\phi$ that $\vec{a}_{B}$ makes with the link $AB$ (measured from the inward radial direction) is:

$$\tan \phi = \dfrac{a_{BA}^{t}}{a_{BA}^{n}} = \dfrac{20}{160} = 0.125$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.125) \approx 7.13°$$

So $\vec{a}_{B}$ is inclined at approximately $7.13°$ from the line $BA$ (towards the sense of $\alpha$).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of Coriolis acceleration. Derive an expression for the Coriolis component of acceleration. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** In a slotted lever mechanism, the slotted link rotates at $\omega = 15$ rad/s and $\alpha = 30$ rad/s². A slider moving in the slot has a relative velocity of $2$ m/s along the slot and a relative acceleration of $4$ m/s² along the slot. Determine the magnitude of Coriolis acceleration and its direction. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Concept:** Coriolis acceleration is the additional acceleration experienced by a particle when it moves along a path that is itself rotating. It does not exist for purely rigid body motion (no sliding) — it appears only when one point slides on a rotating link.

**Derivation:**

Consider a point $P$ sliding along a rotating link $OA$. The position vector of $P$:

$$\vec{r}_{P} = r \, \hat{e}_{r}$$

Velocity:

$$\vec{v}_{P} = \dot{r} \, \hat{e}_{r} + r\omega \, \hat{e}_{\theta}$$

Acceleration (differentiating again and using $\dot{\hat{e}}_{r} = \omega \hat{e}_{\theta}$, $\dot{\hat{e}}_{\theta} = -\omega \hat{e}_{r}$):

$$\vec{a}_{P} = (\ddot{r} - r\omega^{2}) \hat{e}_{r} + (r\alpha + 2\dot{r}\omega) \hat{e}_{\theta}$$

The term $2\dot{r}\omega$ is the Coriolis acceleration:

$$\boxed{\vec{a}_{Coriolis} = 2\omega \dot{r} \, \hat{e}_{\theta} = 2\omega v_{rel}}$$

In vector form: $\vec{a}_{Coriolis} = 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$.

**Direction:** Rotate the relative velocity vector by $90°$ in the sense of rotation $\omega$.

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $\omega = 15$ rad/s, $\alpha = 30$ rad/s², $v_{rel} = 2$ m/s (along slot), $a_{rel} = 4$ m/s² (along slot).

**Step 1: Magnitude of Coriolis acceleration**

$$\vert \vec{a}_{Cor} \vert = 2\omega v_{rel} = 2 \times 15 \times 2$$

$$\boxed{\vert \vec{a}_{Cor} \vert = 60 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Direction**

The relative velocity $\vec{v}_{rel}$ is along the slot. To get the direction of Coriolis acceleration, rotate $\vec{v}_{rel}$ by $90°$ in the sense of rotation $\omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on relative acceleration $a_{rel}$:** The problem states $a_{rel} = 4$ m/s² along the slot, but this is the *relative tangential acceleration* of the slider, not the Coriolis component. Coriolis is purely the $2\omega v_{rel}$ term. Students often confuse these and add them — this is a common error.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Coriolis Misapplication:** Coriolis acceleration = $2\omega v_{rel}$ applies *only* when there is sliding along a rotating link. For pure rigid links, the equation has only two components. Writing a Coriolis term where none exists costs **2–3 marks instantly**.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Direction of Normal Component:** The normal (centripetal) component is directed from the *moving point* towards the *reference point*, not away from it. Drawing it outward is a classic sign error.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Sense of Tangential Component:** The tangential component is perpendicular to the link, but its *sense* (clockwise or counter-clockwise) must match the *sense* of angular acceleration. Randomly choosing direction costs method marks.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Unit Inconsistency:** If length is in cm, convert to m *before* computing $\omega^{2}L$. Mixed units cost 1 mark in KTU papers.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Skipping the Vector Diagram:** Even if you solve analytically, draw the acceleration polygon. KTU examiners award **1 mark** for a correctly drawn polygon, which is "free marks" most students forfeit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Angular acceleration** $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ — units **rad/s²**, vector along rotation axis (right-hand rule).
- **Three kinematic equations of rotation** (uniform $\alpha$):
  - $\omega = \omega_{0} + \alpha t$
  - $\theta = \omega_{0} t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$
  - $\omega^{2} = \omega_{0}^{2} + 2\alpha\theta$
- **Relative acceleration equation** for two points on a rigid link:
  - $\vec{a}_{B} = \vec{a}_{A} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{t} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$
- **Tangential component**: magnitude $\alpha L$, direction **perpendicular** to link, in sense of $\alpha$.
- **Normal component**: magnitude $\omega^{2} L$, direction **along link, from $B$ to $A$** (radially inward).
- **Coriolis component** (sliding case only): magnitude $2\omega v_{rel}$, direction = rotate $\vec{v}_{rel}$ by $90°$ in the sense of $\omega$.
- **Vector formula** for Coriolis: $\vec{a}_{Cor} = 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$.
- **Total magnitude of relative acceleration** (rigid link, no sliding): $\vert \vec{a}_{BA} \vert = \sqrt{(\alpha L)^{2} + (\omega^{2} L)^{2}}$.
- **Angle with link** (rigid link): $\tan \phi = \dfrac{\alpha L}{\omega^{2} L} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\omega^{2}}$.
- **Polygon construction order**: draw $\vec{a}_{A}$ first, then $\vec{a}_{BA}^{n}$ along the link, then $\vec{a}_{BA}^{t}$ perpendicular to the link, then close the polygon to find $\vec{a}_{B}$.
- **Coriolis appears in**: slotted lever, Whitworth quick-return, rotary IC engines, Geneva mechanism (with modification), and any kinematics problem where a slider moves on a rotating arm.
- **Exam mantra**: *Identify sliding → Decide 2-component vs. 3-component → Resolve along convenient axes (usually $x$-$y$ or along/perpendicular to the link) → Solve the resulting scalar equations.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ACCELERATION ANALYSIS & STATIC FORCE ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Acceleration Analysis — Formal Definition

**Acceleration Analysis** is the kinematic study of a mechanism that determines the **linear acceleration** of every point and the **angular acceleration** of every link in the mechanism at a given instant, given the input motion (usually constant angular velocity $\omega_2$ of the driver crank).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this belongs to the analytical branch of **Kinematics of Machines** (as opposed to Kinetics, which involves forces and masses). For the graphical method, we construct a **Acceleration Polygon** (sometimes called the **Klann Diagram** for complex linkages) using vector addition of acceleration components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate (2024 Scheme):** Only the **Four-Bar Mechanism** and the **Slider-Crank Mechanism** are to be analyzed graphically. The Inversion Method and the Algebraic (Analytical) Method are explicitly out of scope for graphical questions in the ESE.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Merry-Go-Round

Imagine a child sitting on a rotating merry-go-round, while a parent walks **radially outward** along the platform at the same time. To the parent standing still on the ground, two things happen simultaneously:

- The child (or any point on the disc) is **changing direction** → this produces a **Centripetal (radial) acceleration** directed toward the center.
- The parent is **sliding radially** while the disc spins → from the disc's rotating frame, an **extra acceleration** appears perpendicular to the sliding direction. This is exactly the **Coriolis Acceleration**.

So in a slider-crank, the slider slides along a link that itself rotates → **Coriolis acceleration** must always be added. In a four-bar mechanism, no point slides along a rotating link, so **no Coriolis component** is required.

### 1.3 Coriolis Acceleration — Formal Statement

**Coriolis Acceleration** ($a_c$) is the **component of acceleration of a point on a moving link, due to the simultaneous rotation and sliding of the link** on which the point is located. It is given by:

$$a_c = 2 \cdot v_r \cdot \omega$$

where $v_r$ is the **relative velocity of the point with respect to the rotating link** and $\omega$ is the **angular velocity of the link**. Its direction is obtained by **rotating $v_r$ by $90°$ in the direction of $\omega$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Only — No Derivation (KTU Mandate).** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly states: *"Coriolis Acceleration — Concept Only (No Derivation)"*. Students must remember the formula, the direction rule, and the physical meaning, but vector derivation is excluded from assessment.

### 1.4 Static Force Analysis — Free Body Diagrams (FBD)

A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is a sketch of a **single isolated link** of the mechanism showing **all external forces and moments** acting on it, **decoupled from the rest of the mechanism**. The link is treated as a *free body in equilibrium* (since inertial forces are added separately in dynamic analysis).

In **static force analysis**:
- External load (e.g., torque, work resistance at slider) is known.
- Inertial forces and moments are neglected (link masses are ignored, or assumed massless).
- The system is solved by the **three equilibrium equations**:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Static force analysis is the **first step** before dynamic force analysis. It uses **kinematic analysis (displacement, velocity, acceleration)** to determine joint forces, which then feed into design of pins, bearings, and links.

### 1.5 Key Standard Quantities (Highlighted in Bold)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Angular velocity of crank | $\omega_2$ | **rad/s** |
| Angular acceleration of crank | $\alpha_2$ | **rad/s²** |
| Linear velocity | $v$ | **m/s** |
| Linear acceleration | $a$ | **m/s²** |
| Force | $F$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Torque / Moment | $T$ | **N·m** |
| Length of link | $r$ or $l$ | **mm or m** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Acceleration Analysis of the Four-Bar Mechanism

Consider a four-bar mechanism with the input link **2** (crank) rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega_2$ (so $\alpha_2 = 0$). The links are numbered:

- **Link 1**: Fixed frame (ground)
- **Link 2**: Input crank (OA), length $r_2$
- **Link 3**: Coupler (AB), length $r_3$
- **Link 4**: Output rocker (OB), length $r_4$

### 2.1.1 Acceleration Vectors of Point A

Point A lies on the crank OA rotating about O with $\omega_2$. Its acceleration has only a **centripetal component** (since $\alpha_2 = 0$):

$$a_A = \omega_2^2 \cdot r_2 \quad \text{(magnitude)}$$

**Direction**: Along **AO** (from A toward O, i.e., from tip of crank toward center).

### 2.1.2 Acceleration Vectors of Point B

Point B lies on rocker OB rotating about O with angular velocity $\omega_4$ and angular acceleration $\alpha_4$:

$$a_B = a_B^n + a_B^t$$

- **Normal (centripetal) component:** $a_B^n = \omega_4^2 \cdot r_4$, directed along **BO**.
- **Tangential component:** $a_B^t = \alpha_4 \cdot r_4$, directed **perpendicular to OB**.

### 2.1.3 Acceleration Equation Linking A and B

Since A and B are connected by rigid link 3 (coupler), the relative acceleration of B with respect to A is split into normal and tangential components:

$$a_B = a_A + a_{BA}^n + a_{BA}^t$$

Where:
- $a_{BA}^n = \omega_3^2 \cdot r_3$, directed along **AB** (from B toward A)
- $a_{BA}^t = \alpha_3 \cdot r_3$, directed **perpendicular to AB**

### 2.1.4 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Why centripetal only for A?** Because $\alpha_2 = 0$ (constant $\omega_2$), so tangential component $\alpha_2 \cdot r_2 = 0$.
2. **Why both components for B?** Because the output rocker has both $\omega_4 \neq 0$ and $\alpha_4 \neq 0$ in general.
3. **Why the rigid-body constraint?** The link AB is inextensible, so the component of relative velocity along AB is zero, and the relative acceleration along AB is the centripetal term.
4. **How is $\omega_4$ obtained?** From the **velocity analysis** (Module 1) of the same mechanism at the same instant — the velocity polygon must first be drawn to scale.
5. **How is $\omega_3$ obtained?** Same as above — from the velocity polygon, the velocity of B is known, and $\omega_3$ comes from the IC analysis.

### 2.1.5 Graphical Construction Steps

1. **Step 1**: Draw the configuration diagram to scale (mechanism in given position).
2. **Step 2**: From velocity polygon, extract $v_B$, $v_{BA}$, hence $\omega_4 = v_B / r_4$ and $\omega_3 = v_{BA} / r_3$.
3. **Step 3**: Start acceleration polygon at a chosen pole `o`. From `o`, draw vector **`oa` = $a_A$** in the direction of AO with magnitude $\omega_2^2 \cdot r_2$.
4. **Step 4**: From `a`, draw a line parallel to AB to locate the vector $a_{BA}^n$ (magnitude $\omega_3^2 \cdot r_3$, direction from B → A).
5. **Step 5**: From the tip of $a_{BA}^n$, draw a line **perpendicular to AB** (this is the locus of $a_{BA}^t$).
6. **Step 6**: From `o`, draw vector `ob` representing $a_B$. Its **normal component** is along BO (known direction, magnitude $\omega_4^2 \cdot r_4$).
7. **Step 7**: From tip of $a_B^n$, draw a line **perpendicular to OB** (this is the locus of $a_B^t$).
8. **Step 8**: The intersection of the $a_{BA}^t$ line (from Step 5) and the $a_B^t$ line (from Step 7) closes the polygon. The closure determines both unknown magnitudes, and hence $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$.

### 2.2 Acceleration Analysis of the Slider-Crank Mechanism

The slider-crank has the following links:

- **Link 1**: Ground
- **Link 2**: Crank (OA), length $r$
- **Link 3**: Connecting rod (AB), length $l$
- **Link 4**: Slider at B, moving along a straight line (horizontal by convention)

The slider position from the crank center O is given by the slider displacement equation:

$$x = r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi$$

where $\theta$ is the crank angle from the line of stroke and $\phi$ is the connecting rod angle.

### 2.2.1 Acceleration of the Slider (B)

The slider is the end of the connecting rod. Velocity of B = velocity of A + velocity of B relative to A:

$$v_B = v_A + v_{BA}$$

For **acceleration**, we get the full equation:

$$a_B = a_A + a_{BA}^n + a_{BA}^t + \boxed{a_c}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The Coriolis component $a_c$ is non-zero in slider-crank**, because point B **slides along the connecting rod (rotating link)**. The four-bar mechanism has **no Coriolis component** in its acceleration analysis.

### 2.2.2 Magnitude and Direction of Coriolis Component

$$a_c = 2 \cdot \omega_3 \cdot v_{BA}$$

**Direction rule**: Rotate the relative velocity vector $v_{BA}$ by **$90°$ in the direction of $\omega_3$** of the connecting rod.

### 2.2.3 Component Forms

| Component | Magnitude | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| $a_A$ (crank tip) | $\omega_2^2 \cdot r$ | Along AO |
| $a_{BA}^n$ | $\omega_3^2 \cdot l$ | Along AB (from B toward A) |
| $a_{BA}^t$ | $\alpha_3 \cdot l$ | $\perp$ to AB |
| $a_c$ (Coriolis) | $2 \omega_3 v_{BA}$ | $v_{BA}$ rotated $90°$ along $\omega_3$ |
| $a_B$ (slider) | unknown | Along line of stroke (horizontal) |

### 2.3 Static Force Analysis — FBD Conventions

When isolating a link in its FBD, the following **action lines** are drawn:

1. **Driver torque / input torque** $T_{in}$ — applied at the pivot of link 2, perpendicular to crank.
2. **Output load** $F_L$ — applied at the slider or at the working point (known direction and magnitude).
3. **Pin reactions** at the joints — drawn as **two unknown perpendicular components** at revolute joints, since frictionless pins transmit only force, not moment.
4. **Link weight** $W$ — applied at the link's center of gravity (often neglected in basic static analysis).

### 2.3.1 Equilibrium Equations for Each Link

For Link 2 (crank OA): Taking moments about O eliminates the pin reactions at O, leaving a direct torque equation:

$$\sum M_O = T_{in} - F_{32} \cdot r \cdot \sin(\text{angle between } F_{32} \text{ and crank}) = 0$$

This **directly gives the input torque** if the force on the crank from the connecting rod is known.

For Link 3 (coupler/connecting rod): A **two-force member** assumption (neglecting link weight) means the force on the rod acts along the line AB. Hence:

$$F_{23} = -F_{43} \quad \text{(equal, opposite, collinear along AB)}$$

For Link 4 (rocker or slider): The three equilibrium equations $\sum F_x, \sum F_y, \sum M$ are applied.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_A = \omega_2^2 r_2$ | Crank tip acceleration (4-bar) | m/s² |
| 2 | $a_B^n = \omega_4^2 r_4$ | Centripetal of B on rocker | m/s² |
| 3 | $a_B^t = \alpha_4 r_4$ | Tangential of B on rocker | m/s² |
| 4 | $a_{BA}^n = \omega_3^2 r_3$ | Centripetal of B w.r.t. A | m/s² |
| 5 | $a_{BA}^t = \alpha_3 r_3$ | Tangential of B w.r.t. A | m/s² |
| 6 | $a_c = 2 \omega_3 v_{BA}$ | Coriolis component (slider-crank only) | m/s² |
| 7 | $a_B = a_A + a_{BA}^n + a_{BA}^t$ | Four-bar vector equation | m/s² |
| 8 | $a_B = a_A + a_{BA}^n + a_{BA}^t + a_c$ | Slider-crank vector equation | m/s² |
| 9 | $\omega_3 = v_{BA} / r_3$ | Coupler angular velocity | rad/s |
| 10 | $\omega_4 = v_B / r_4$ | Rocker angular velocity | rad/s |
| 11 | $\alpha_3 = a_{BA}^t / r_3$ | Coupler angular acceleration | rad/s² |
| 12 | $\alpha_4 = a_B^t / r_4$ | Rocker angular acceleration | rad/s² |
| 13 | $\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0, \sum M = 0$ | Static equilibrium of FBD | N, N, N·m |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Design**: Acceleration of piston in I.C. engines governs inertial forces that affect bearing loads and vibration. Slider-crank analysis is the foundation of crank-slider dynamics.
- **Robotic Manipulators**: Joint accelerations computed through graphical/vector methods feed into trajectory planning and actuator sizing.
- **Printing & Textile Machines**: Four-bar linkages with prescribed motion profiles (e.g., dwell-and-return) require acceleration analysis to avoid jerk-induced defects.
- **Compressor and Pump Design**: Slider-crank with offset is analyzed to compute piston acceleration, from which dynamic forces on the crosshead are derived.
- **Agricultural & Packaging Machinery**: Static FBD analysis yields pin and shaft loads for fatigue design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Acceleration of Slider in Slider-Crank Mechanism

This derivation is fundamental and forms the basis of every textbook problem in KTU ESE. We proceed rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Velocity Analysis — Foundation

Let crank OA rotate at constant $\omega_2$. Velocity of A is tangential to the crank circle:

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot r \quad \text{(perpendicular to OA)}$$

The slider B is constrained to move along the line of stroke. By the **relative velocity equation**:

$$v_B = v_A + v_{BA}$$

- $v_A$ direction: $\perp$ OA
- $v_{BA}$ direction: $\perp$ AB (since the connecting rod rotates)
- $v_B$ direction: along line of stroke (horizontal)

The velocity polygon yields $v_B$ and $v_{BA}$, and from the latter:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{v_{BA}}{l}$$

### 3.1.2 Acceleration Analysis — Full Vector Equation

Differentiate the velocity equation with respect to time:

$$\frac{d v_B}{dt} = \frac{d v_A}{dt} + \frac{d v_{BA}}{dt}$$

$$a_B = a_A + a_{BA}$$

Now $a_{BA}$ is the acceleration of B **as seen by an observer moving with link 3 (the connecting rod)**. This observer sees A and B as fixed points of a rigid body, BUT the velocity of B relative to A is $v_{BA}$, which is changing both in magnitude (if $\alpha_3 \neq 0$) and in direction (because AB rotates with $\omega_3$).

Decompose the time derivative of $v_{BA}$:

$$a_{BA} = \frac{d v_{BA}}{dt} = \underbrace{\alpha_3 \times r_{BA}}_{\text{tangential}} + \underbrace{\omega_3 \times (\omega_3 \times r_{BA})}_{\text{centripetal (normal)}} + \underbrace{2 \omega_3 \times v_{rel}}_{\text{Coriolis}}$$

The last term arises because B is **sliding along the rotating rod AB**. The relative velocity $v_{rel}$ of B along the rod is zero (B is a fixed point on the rod, no slip). HOWEVER, the **acceleration of B as measured from the fixed frame** includes the change of direction of $v_{BA}$ as the rod rotates.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mandate Reminder:** The detailed vector derivation of Coriolis acceleration is **excluded**. Only the concept, the formula $a_c = 2\omega_3 v_{BA}$, and the direction rule are required.

### 3.1.3 Substituting the Components

$$a_B = \underbrace{\omega_2^2 r}_{\text{along AO}} + \underbrace{\omega_3^2 l}_{\text{along BA}} + \underbrace{\alpha_3 l}_{\perp \text{ to AB}} + \underbrace{2\omega_3 v_{BA}}_{\text{Coriolis}}$$

**Component-wise decomposition (along x-axis and y-axis):**

Let $\theta$ be the crank angle and $\phi$ the connecting rod angle. Then:

For the x-component of slider acceleration:

$$a_B \cos 0 = -\omega_2^2 r \cos\theta - \omega_3^2 l \cos\phi - \alpha_3 l \sin\phi - 2\omega_3 v_{BA} \cos\phi$$

For the y-component (perpendicular to slider travel; must be zero since slider moves on x-axis):

$$0 = -\omega_2^2 r \sin\theta + \omega_3^2 l \sin\phi - \alpha_3 l \cos\phi + 2\omega_3 v_{BA} \sin\phi$$

The second equation gives $\alpha_3$, which is then substituted into the first to obtain $a_B$.

### 3.1.4 Closed-Form Result (Approximate, for KTU reference)

The well-known approximate expression for slider acceleration is:

$$a_B \approx r\omega_2^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta \right)$$

This is the **famous Klein's construction precursor** used in engine dynamics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Static Force Analysis from FBD

### 3.2.1 Free Body of the Crank (Link 2)

The crank OA is acted on by:
- Input torque $T_{in}$ at O
- Pin reaction at A: $F_{32}$, with two unknown components $F_{32x}, F_{32y}$
- Pin reaction at O: $F_{12}$ (with components $F_{12x}, F_{12y}$)

Taking moment about O (eliminates $F_{12}$):

$$T_{in} - F_{32y} \cdot r \cos\theta - F_{32x} \cdot r \sin\theta = 0$$

where the components of $F_{32}$ are resolved along the global x and y axes, and $\theta$ is the crank angle.

### 3.2.2 Free Body of the Connecting Rod (Link 3) — Two-Force Member

Assuming massless link 3, the forces at A and B must be **equal, opposite, and collinear** along the line AB:

$$F_{23} = -F_{43}, \quad \text{both along line AB}$$

This is a key simplification — the line of action is known (it is AB), so the **magnitude** is the only unknown.

### 3.2.3 Free Body of the Slider (Link 4)

Forces on slider:
- Pin reaction from rod: $F_{43}$ along AB
- Reaction from guide: $F_{14}$ (perpendicular to slider travel, i.e., vertical if stroke is horizontal)
- External load: $F_L$ (along line of stroke, opposite to motion)

Applying $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$:

$$F_{14} + F_{43} \sin\phi = 0 \quad \text{(y-equilibrium)}$$

$$-F_L + F_{43} \cos\phi = 0 \quad \text{(x-equilibrium)}$$

Solving:

$$F_{43} = \frac{F_L}{\cos\phi}, \quad F_{14} = -F_L \tan\phi$$

### 3.2.4 Back-substitution to Crank

From 3.2.1, with $F_{32} = F_{23} = F_{43}$ (by Newton's third law):

$$T_{in} = F_{43} \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$$

This is the **input torque** required to drive the mechanism against the load $F_L$.

### 3.2.5 The Complete Analytical Matrix

| Link | Free Body Forces | Number of Unknowns | Equations Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (ground) | Reactions at fixed pivots | 4 | 0 (reference) |
| 2 (crank) | $T_{in}, F_{12}, F_{32}$ | 4 | 3 (eq. of statics) |
| 3 (coupler) | $F_{23}, F_{43}$ along AB | 1 (magnitude) | 0 (two-force member) |
| 4 (rocker/slider) | $F_{34}, F_{14}, F_L$ | 3 | 3 |
| **Total** | — | **8 unknowns** | **6 equations** |

> [!NOTE]
> For a 4-bar with **no input torque assumed**, we have 8 unknowns and 6 equations — the system is **statically indeterminate** without further constraints. With input torque specified, the system closes and $F_L$ can be solved.

## 3.3 Derivation: Coriolis Direction Rule

Conceptual (no derivation per KTU mandate): If a point P slides along a link rotating at $\omega$ with relative velocity $v_r$, the Coriolis acceleration is:

$$a_c = 2 \omega \times v_r$$

The direction is **perpendicular to $v_r$**, obtained by **rotating $v_r$ by $90°$ in the sense of $\omega$**.

Quick test: If $\omega$ is counter-clockwise and $v_r$ is along the link outward, then $a_c$ is also outward-perpendicular to the link in the CCW sense.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Slider Acceleration with Coriolis

**Given:**
- Crank length $r = 100$ mm
- Connecting rod length $l = 300$ mm
- $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s (constant)
- Crank angle $\theta = 30°$

**Find:** $a_B$ and $a_c$

**Step 1 — Compute slider position and geometry:**

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

Using the standard geometric relation:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l} \sin\theta = \frac{100}{300} \sin 30° = 0.1667$$

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.1667) = 9.59°$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity of connecting rod:**

The instantaneous angular velocity of the connecting rod is:

$$\omega_3 = \omega_2 \cdot \frac{r \cos\theta}{l \cos\phi}$$

$$\omega_3 = 20 \cdot \frac{100 \cos 30°}{300 \cos 9.59°} = 20 \cdot \frac{86.60}{295.78} = 5.854 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute relative velocity $v_{BA}$:**

$$v_{BA} = \omega_3 \cdot l = 5.854 \cdot 0.300 = 1.756 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Coriolis acceleration:**

$$a_c = 2 \omega_3 v_{BA} = 2 \cdot 5.854 \cdot 1.756 = 20.56 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute slider velocity (for completeness):**

$$v_B = \omega_2 r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r \sin 2\theta}{2l \cos\phi} \right)$$

$$v_B = 20 \cdot 0.1 \left( \sin 30° + \frac{0.1 \cdot \sin 60°}{2 \cdot 0.3 \cdot \cos 9.59°} \right)$$

$$v_B = 2 \cdot (0.5 + 0.0488) = 1.0976 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute slider acceleration:**

$$a_B = -\omega_2^2 r \cos\theta - \omega_3^2 l \cos\phi - 2\omega_3 v_{BA} \cos\phi + \alpha_3 l \sin\phi$$

(With $\alpha_3$ from the y-equation; for simplicity, when $\alpha_2 = 0$ and using Klein's approximation):

$$a_B \approx r \omega_2^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta \right)$$

$$a_B \approx 0.1 \cdot 400 \left( \cos 30° + \frac{1}{3}\cos 60° \right) = 40 \cdot (0.866 + 0.167) = 41.32 \text{ m/s}^2$$

The Coriolis contribution is significant (~50% of total) and **must not be omitted**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Acceleration Analysis Procedure (Four-Bar)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given crank angle theta and omega2] --> B[Draw configuration diagram to scale]
    B --> C[Conduct velocity analysis: Draw velocity polygon]
    C --> D[Measure vB, vBA, vA from polygon]
    D --> E[Calculate omega3 = vBA / r3]
    E --> F[Calculate omega4 = vB / r4]
    F --> G[Choose acceleration scale: 1 cm = X m/s squared]
    G --> H[Draw vector oa along AO with magnitude omega2 squared times r2]
    H --> I[From a, draw line along AB for aBAn, length omega3 squared times r3]
    I --> J[From tip of aBAn, draw perpendicular to AB locus for aBAt]
    J --> K[From o, draw vector along BO for aBn, length omega4 squared times r4]
    K --> L[From tip of aBn, draw perpendicular to OB locus for aBt]
    L --> M[Intersection of two loci locates point b]
    M --> N[Measure ab closure to get aBt magnitude]
    N --> O[Calculate alpha4 = aBt / r4]
    O --> P[Measure aBAt magnitude from polygon]
    P --> Q[Calculate alpha3 = aBAt / r3]
    Q --> R[End: Report omega3, omega4, alpha3, alpha4]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Slider-Crank Acceleration Analysis (with Coriolis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given theta, omega2, r, l] --> B[Compute phi from geometry sin phi = r sin theta over l]
    B --> C[Compute omega3 from velocity analysis]
    C --> D[Compute vBA = omega3 times l]
    D --> E[Compute Coriolis ac = 2 omega3 times vBA]
    E --> F[Set up direction: ac perpendicular to vBA, rotated 90 deg along omega3]
    F --> G[Draw configuration diagram to scale]
    G --> H[Choose acceleration scale]
    H --> I[Draw oa along AO: omega2 squared times r]
    I --> J[From a, draw aBAn along BA: omega3 squared times l]
    J --> K[From tip of aBAn, draw perpendicular to AB for aBAt]
    K --> L[From a, draw ac vector in correct direction]
    L --> M[From o, draw line of stroke direction for aB locus]
    M --> N[Intersection gives point b, length ob is aB magnitude]
    N --> O[Calculate alpha3 = aBAt / l]
    O --> P[Report: aB, alpha3, ac]
    P --> Q[End]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — FBD of Slider-Crank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Link2[Link 2 Crank]
        Tin[Input Torque T at O]
        O2[Pin O]
        A2[Pin A]
    end
    subgraph Link3[Link 3 Connecting Rod - Two Force Member]
        AB[Force along AB]
    end
    subgraph Link4[Link 4 Slider]
        B4[Pin B]
        F14[Guide Reaction perpendicular to stroke]
        FL[External Load FL along stroke]
    end
    Tin --> Link2
    O2 --> Link2
    A2 --> Link3
    AB --> Link3
    B4 --> Link4
    F14 --> Link4
    FL --> Link4
    A2 -->|F32| AB
    AB -->|F43| B4
```

## 4.4 Acceleration Polygon Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Pole o] -->|oa = centripetal of A along AO| A[Point a]
    A -->|aBAn = omega3 squared times r3 along BA| NA[Point na]
    NA -->|aBAt = perpendicular to AB| TA[Point ta]
    TA -->|ac = 2 omega3 vBA Coriolis| CO[Closure closure pt b]
    O -->|obn = aB along BO| NO[Point no]
    NO -->|obt = aBt perpendicular to OB| CO
    CO -->|Resultant aB| O
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid representations are **functional block-flow schematics** of the graphical construction. In an actual KTU ESE answer sheet, the student must draw the **physical configuration diagram and the acceleration polygon (vector diagram)** to scale on graph paper, with all vectors labeled by name and magnitude.

## 4.5 Force Flow Diagram (Static Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[External Load FL applied at slider] --> L2[Force F43 on connecting rod along AB]
    L2 --> L3[Force F23 on crank at pin A along AB]
    L3 --> L4[Input Torque Tin required at O]
    L4 --> L5[Reaction at ground pivot O]
    L2 --> L6[Guide reaction F14 perpendicular to stroke]
    L6 --> L7[Transmitted to ground]
```
