# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define Coriolis acceleration. State its magnitude and direction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Coriolis acceleration is the component of acceleration of a point on a moving link arising from the simultaneous rotation of the link and the sliding of the point along the link.

**Magnitude (1 mark):**

$$a_c = 2 \, \omega \, v_r$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the rotating link and $v_r$ is the relative velocity of the point along the link.

**Direction (1 mark):** The direction of $a_c$ is obtained by rotating the relative velocity vector $v_r$ through $90°$ in the direction of rotation $\omega$ of the link.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is Coriolis component absent in the acceleration analysis of a four-bar mechanism but present in a slider-crank mechanism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a four-bar mechanism, **no link has a point sliding along it** while the link itself rotates. All revolute joints transmit only rotation, no sliding. Hence the relative velocity of a pin with respect to its connecting link is zero, and the Coriolis term $2 \omega v_r = 0$. **(1.5 marks)**

In a slider-crank mechanism, the slider B is a **pin joint that slides along the connecting rod AB** while the rod rotates at $\omega_3$. The slider has a non-zero relative velocity $v_{BA}$ along the rod. Therefore, the Coriolis component $a_c = 2 \omega_3 v_{BA}$ is non-zero and must be added in the acceleration equation. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers **internal choice** (either-or). Below are two fully-worked independent choices, each divided into 7-mark and 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**A four-bar mechanism has the following dimensions:**
- Crank OA = 100 mm, rotating at constant $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s CCW
- Coupler AB = 250 mm
- Rocker OB = 200 mm
- Fixed pivots O₁ and O₂ separated by 280 mm
- Crank angle from horizontal = 60°

#### Part (a) — Draw the configuration diagram and find $\omega_3$, $\omega_4$ using velocity analysis. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Configuration Diagram**
- Draw O₁A = 100 mm at 60° from horizontal.
- With O₂ as center, draw an arc of radius 200 mm.
- With A as center, draw an arc of radius 250 mm.
- Intersection gives point B.

**Step 2: Velocity of A**

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot r_2 = 20 \cdot 0.1 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to OA (i.e., at 150° from horizontal, since crank is at 60°).

**Step 3: Velocity Polygon**
- Draw vector `oa` ⊥ to OA, length 2 m/s (use scale: 1 cm = 0.4 m/s → `oa` = 5 cm).
- From `o`, draw line ⊥ to OB (this is the locus of $v_B$).
- From `a`, draw line ⊥ to AB (this is the locus of $v_{BA}$).
- Intersection → point `b`.

**Step 4: Measure from Polygon**

Assume (from a typical construction):
- $v_B = 1.6$ m/s → $\omega_4 = v_B / r_4 = 1.6 / 0.2 = 8.0$ rad/s
- $v_{BA} = 1.5$ m/s → $\omega_3 = v_{BA} / r_3 = 1.5 / 0.25 = 6.0$ rad/s

**Mark distribution:** Diagram (2), $v_A$ calc (1), Polygon construction (2), Final $\omega$ values (2). Total = 7.

#### Part (b) — Using the velocity results, draw the acceleration polygon and find $\alpha_3$, $\alpha_4$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute known acceleration magnitudes**
- $a_A = \omega_2^2 r_2 = (20)^2 \cdot 0.1 = 40$ m/s², along AO
- $a_{BA}^n = \omega_3^2 r_3 = (6)^2 \cdot 0.25 = 9$ m/s², along BA
- $a_B^n = \omega_4^2 r_4 = (8)^2 \cdot 0.2 = 12.8$ m/s², along BO

**Step 2: Acceleration Polygon**
- Choose scale: 1 cm = 4 m/s²
- From pole `o`, draw `oa` = 10 cm along AO (40 m/s²)
- From `a`, draw aBAn = 2.25 cm along BA (9 m/s²), mark point `n3`
- From `n3`, draw line ⊥ to AB → locus of $a_{BA}^t$
- From `o`, draw `obn` = 3.2 cm along BO (12.8 m/s²), mark point `n4`
- From `n4`, draw line ⊥ to OB → locus of $a_B^t$
- Intersection of the two perpendiculars → point `b`
- Vector `ob` gives $a_B$; components give $a_{BA}^t$ and $a_B^t$

**Step 3: Measure and Compute**

From the polygon (assumed measured values):
- $a_{BA}^t = 6$ m/s² → $\alpha_3 = 6 / 0.25 = 24$ rad/s²
- $a_B^t = 8$ m/s² → $\alpha_4 = 8 / 0.2 = 40$ rad/s²

**Mark distribution:** Acceleration magnitudes (2), Polygon construction (3), Final $\alpha$ values (2). Total = 7.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**A slider-crank mechanism has:**
- Crank length = 150 mm
- Connecting rod length = 450 mm
- Crank rotating at constant $\omega_2 = 30$ rad/s CCW
- Crank angle from inner dead center = 45°
- External load on slider = 2000 N (resisting motion)

#### Part (a) — Determine the angular velocity of the connecting rod, the slider velocity, the Coriolis acceleration component, and the slider acceleration. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Geometry**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \frac{150}{450}\sin 45° = 0.333 \cdot 0.707 = 0.2357$$

$$\phi = 13.64°$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.9718$$

**Step 2: Angular velocity of connecting rod**

$$\omega_3 = \omega_2 \cdot \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\phi} \cdot \frac{r}{l}$$

$$\omega_3 = 30 \cdot \frac{\cos 45°}{\cos 13.64°} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 30 \cdot \frac{0.707}{0.9718} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 7.28 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Relative velocity $v_{BA}$**

$$v_{BA} = \omega_3 \cdot l = 7.28 \cdot 0.45 = 3.276 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Coriolis acceleration**

$$a_c = 2 \omega_3 v_{BA} = 2 \cdot 7.28 \cdot 3.276 = 47.7 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5: Slider acceleration (Klein's approximation)**

$$a_B \approx r\omega_2^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta \right)$$

$$a_B \approx 0.15 \cdot (30)^2 \left( \cos 45° + \frac{1}{3}\cos 90° \right)$$

$$a_B \approx 0.15 \cdot 900 \cdot (0.707 + 0) = 95.4 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6: Slider velocity**

$$v_B = \omega_2 r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r\sin 2\theta}{2l \cos\phi} \right)$$

$$v_B = 30 \cdot 0.15 \left( \sin 45° + \frac{0.15 \cdot \sin 90°}{2 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 0.9718} \right)$$

$$v_B = 4.5 \cdot (0.707 + 0.1714) = 3.95 \text{ m/s}$$

**Mark distribution:** Geometry (1), $\omega_3$ (1.5), $v_{BA}, a_c$ (1.5), $a_B$ (2), $v_B$ (1). Total = 7.

#### Part (b) — Draw the FBD of the mechanism and determine the input torque required at the crank. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: FBD of Slider (Link 4)**
- Load $F_L = 2000$ N along negative x (resisting motion).
- Force from connecting rod $F_{43}$ along BA (known direction, $\phi = 13.64°$).
- Guide reaction $F_{14}$ perpendicular to stroke (vertical).

**Step 2: FBD of Connecting Rod (Link 3)**
- Two-force member: forces at A and B are equal, opposite, along AB.
- $F_{23} = F_{43} = F$ (magnitude to be determined).

**Step 3: Equilibrium of Slider**

$$F \cos\phi = F_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{2000}{\cos 13.64°} = \frac{2000}{0.9718} = 2058 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: FBD of Crank (Link 2)**
- Input torque $T_{in}$ at O (CCW assumed).
- Force at A from rod: $F_{32} = F_{23} = 2058$ N along AB.
- Reaction at O.

**Step 5: Moment about O**

$$T_{in} = F_{32} \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$$

where $(\theta + \phi) = 45° + 13.64° = 58.64°$.

$$T_{in} = 2058 \cdot 0.15 \cdot \sin 58.64° = 2058 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 0.854 = 263.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Mark distribution:** Slider FBD + eq. (2), Rod two-force member (1), Crank FBD + moment eq. (2), Final $T_{in}$ (2). Total = 7.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **forget the Coriolis component** in slider-crank acceleration, costing 2-3 marks. Others **assume the connecting rod is not a two-force member**, leading to wrong FBD. Always check whether link mass/weight is given — if not, **assume massless** and use the two-force rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Acceleration analysis** of a mechanism gives angular accelerations $\alpha$ of each link and linear accelerations of every point.
- The **fundamental vector equation** is $a_B = a_A + a_{BA}$ (or $+ a_c$ for slider-crank).
- In a **four-bar**, NO Coriolis term. In a **slider-crank**, the Coriolis term **$a_c = 2\omega_3 v_{BA}$** is **mandatory**.
- **Coriolis direction** = $v_{BA}$ rotated by $90°$ in the sense of $\omega_3$.
- The **acceleration polygon** is constructed graphically by chaining known and unknown vectors, with the closing side giving the unknown magnitude(s).
- For **static FBD analysis**, the **three equilibrium equations** $\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0, \sum M = 0$ are applied to each link.
- The **connecting rod in a slider-crank is a two-force member** (if massless), simplifying analysis dramatically.
- The **input torque** is found by taking moments about the fixed pivot of the crank, eliminating the ground reaction.
- **KTU 2024 restriction**: Only four-bar and slider-crank are in scope for graphical acceleration analysis.
- **KTU 2024 restriction**: Coriolis acceleration — **concept only**, no derivation.
- **Velocity analysis must precede acceleration analysis** — you cannot find $\omega_3, \omega_4$ without first solving the velocity polygon.
- For crank with $\omega_2 =$ const, $a_A$ has only a **centripetal** component; for crank with $\alpha_2 \neq 0$, a tangential component $\alpha_2 r_2$ must also be added.
- **Negative sign** in acceleration result implies opposite to assumed direction; this is a common error zone in KTU valuations.
- **Always draw to scale** and **label every vector** in the polygon — missing labels cost 1-2 marks even if the construction is correct.
- The **slider acceleration is along the line of stroke** only — the perpendicular component must equal zero; this is the check equation that yields $\alpha_3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Force Analysis of Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Force Analysis of Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Static Force Analysis** of a mechanism is the determination of the unknown forces and couples acting at the various joints of a mechanism when the system is in **static (or dynamic) equilibrium**, with the magnitudes and directions of all externally applied loads (including inertia forces and inertia couples) being known. The analysis assumes the mechanism is moving at a known constant velocity (or momentarily at a known configuration), allowing inertia terms to be evaluated and treated as quasi-static loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope (PCRUT502, Module 2):**
> The scope is restricted to **two canonical planar mechanisms** — the *Four-Bar Chain* and the *Slider-Crank Chain* — analyzed using the **principle of free body diagrams (FBD)** and the **equations of static equilibrium** $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are pushing a heavy office table across the floor at a slow, constant pace. The friction is **200 N**. If you want to know the force you must apply at the handle, and also the load carried by each leg of the table, you draw a free body diagram of the table and apply Newton's equilibrium equations. **Static force analysis of a mechanism is the exact same idea — except the "table" is a chain of rigid links connected by pins and sliders.** Each link is treated as a free body, and the unknown pin reactions and joint forces are found using equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this analysis important for a Mechanical Engineer?**
> - To size **bearings, pins, and links** against failure (stress, buckling, wear).
> - To determine the **driving torque** required at the input shaft (e.g., IC engine crankshaft, robotic arm actuator).
> - To determine the **holding torque** needed to keep the mechanism stationary against applied loads.
> - To identify the **critical pin** that carries the maximum load — essential for fatigue design.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Two-Force Member (Binary Link)** | A link on which forces act at **exactly two points only** (and no couple). The two forces must be **equal, opposite, and collinear** (along the line joining the two points). |
| **Three-Force Member (Ternary Link)** | A link with forces acting at **three points**; must satisfy three equilibrium equations. |
| **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** | A sketch of a link (or part of it), isolated from the rest of the mechanism, showing **all** forces and couples acting on it. |
| **Driving Torque ($T_{dr}$)** | The torque that must be applied at the input crank to overcome the external loads. |
| **Holding / Holding-back Torque** | The torque that must be applied at the input to keep the mechanism stationary against a load that tends to drive it (e.g., gas pressure helping the crank). |
| **Inertia Force ($F_I$)** | The d'Alembert force $\mathbf{F}_I = -m\,\mathbf{a}_G$ acting at the **center of gravity** of a moving link, opposing its linear acceleration. |
| **Inertia Couple ($M_I$)** | The d'Alembert couple $M_I = -I_G\,\alpha$ acting on a moving link, opposing its angular acceleration. |

### 1.4 Static vs. Dynamic Force Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 module 2 is officially titled *"Static Force Analysis"*, but in practice the inertia forces ($F_I$, $M_I$) of the moving links are computed from the **kinematic analysis** (velocities and accelerations) and **superimposed** on the static external loads. The mechanism is then analyzed as if it were in static equilibrium under the combined loading. This is the **d'Alembert principle** of dynamic equilibrium, but the *analysis technique* used is **static equilibrium of free bodies**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Procedure (Board-Examiner Blueprint)

The method is **always** the same five-step ritual. Memorize it — it is the heart of the module.

**Step 1 – Identify the links and degrees of freedom.**
- For a four-bar: 4 links, 4 revolute joints. **DOF = 1** (Gruebler's equation: $3(n-1) - 2j_1$).
- For a slider-crank: 4 links, 3 revolute + 1 prismatic. **DOF = 1**.

**Step 2 – Identify binary (two-force) links.**
- Any link having **only two pin forces** (and no external load or inertia couple/force that is not at the joints) is a two-force member.
- In a four-bar with no external load on the coupler, the **coupler (link 3)** is also a binary member.
- In a slider-crank, the **connecting rod (link 3)** is virtually always a binary member.
- The force in a binary link is directed **along the line joining its two pin joints**.

**Step 3 – Draw free body diagrams.**
- One FBD for each moving link.
- Show the **known external loads** ($W$, gas pressure, spring force, etc.).
- Show the **known inertia loads** ($F_I = m\,a_G$, $M_I = I_G\,\alpha$).
- Show the **unknown joint reactions** (each pin joint supplies two unknown force components; a slider supplies one normal reaction and a pin force).

**Step 4 – Write equilibrium equations.**
- For each FBD: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$.
- For two-force members, the equilibrium is automatically satisfied — you only need the geometry (line of action) to find the unknown magnitude.

**Step 5 – Solve the linear system.**
- Start from the link with the most unknowns eliminated by binary-link assumptions, and propagate.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Shortcut:** Most KTU problems are constructed so that **exactly one link is a two-force member**. This collapses the 9-equations, 8-unknowns indeterminate system into a determinate one. Always *hunt* for the binary link first.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law (static equilibrium) | $\sum F_x = 0,\;\; \sum F_y = 0$ | Used on every FBD. |
| 2 | Moment equilibrium | $\sum M_O = 0$ | About any point $O$ on the FBD. |
| 3 | Two-force member condition | $F_{12}$ is directed along the line connecting pins 1 and 2. | Magnitude unknown; direction known. |
| 4 | Inertia force (d'Alembert) | $\mathbf{F}_I = -m\,\mathbf{a}_G$ | $m$ in kg, $a_G$ in m/s², $F_I$ in N. |
| 5 | Inertia couple | $M_I = -I_G\,\alpha$ | $I_G$ in kg·m², $\alpha$ in rad/s², $M_I$ in N·m. |
| 6 | Slider-crank: angle of connecting rod | $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l}\,\sin\theta$ | $r$ = crank, $l$ = rod, $\theta$ from line of stroke. |
| 7 | Slider-crank: force in connecting rod | $F_{rod} \approx F_{slider}$ | If rod is binary. |
| 8 | Slider-crank: tangential force on crank pin | $F_t = F_{rod}\,\cos\phi$ (approx.) | More precisely $F_t = F_{rod}\,\sin(\theta+\phi)$ from geometry. |
| 9 | Driving torque | $T_{dr} = F_t \cdot r$ | $r$ = crank radius. |
| 10 | Four-bar: torque on input crank | $T_{dr} = F_t \cdot \vert O_2A \vert$ | $F_t$ is component of $F_{32}$ perpendicular to $O_2A$. |
| 11 | Vector cross product (moment) | $M_O(\mathbf{F}) = \mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{F} = x\,F_y - y\,F_x$ | Sign follows right-hand rule. |
| 12 | Force decomposition along / perpendicular to a link | $F_{\parallel} = F\cos\beta,\;\; F_{\perp} = F\sin\beta$ | $\beta$ is the angle between the line of action and the link. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the working backbone for designing **IC engine crankshafts, four-bar steering mechanisms, suspension systems, robotic arms, walking machines, and printing presses**. The very same analysis, repeated at 720 discrete crank angles per minute in a running engine, gives the cyclic load spectrum for fatigue life prediction.

## 2.3 Application of d'Alembert's Principle (Why "Static" includes "Dynamic" Loads)

The inertia force $\mathbf{F}_I$ and inertia couple $M_I$ of every moving link are computed from the **kinematic analysis** of the same mechanism:

$$\mathbf{F}_I \;=\; -m\,\mathbf{a}_G, \qquad M_I \;=\; -I_G\,\alpha$$

These inertia terms are then **superimposed** on the external loads (force $F$, moment $M$, etc.) on the FBD. The mechanism is now treated as if it were in **static equilibrium** under the **net effective load**. The procedure is therefore called *static force analysis* (of a dynamic system).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Forgetting to add $F_I$ and $M_I$ when the question says "the crank rotates at $\omega$ rad/s". If the question says "the mechanism is at rest" or "negligible inertia", inertia loads are zero.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Force-Equilibrium of a Single Link

Consider an arbitrary link $i$ of mass $m_i$, with center of gravity $G_i$ having linear acceleration $\mathbf{a}_{G_i}$ and angular acceleration $\alpha_i$. The link is subjected to:
- An externally applied force $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ at a known point,
- An externally applied couple $M_{ext}$,
- Joint reaction forces $\mathbf{R}_{ij}$ from neighboring links $j$,
- Inertia force $\mathbf{F}_{I,i} = -m_i\mathbf{a}_{G_i}$ at $G_i$,
- Inertia couple $M_{I,i} = -I_{G_i}\alpha_i$.

Applying d'Alembert's principle of dynamic equilibrium:

$$\sum F_x:\quad \sum R_{ij,x} + F_{ext,x} + F_{I,i,x} \;=\; 0$$

$$\sum F_y:\quad \sum R_{ij,y} + F_{ext,y} + F_{I,i,y} \;=\; 0$$

$$\sum M_{G_i}:\quad \sum (R_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}) + M_{ext} + M_{I,i} \;=\; 0$$

For a **two-force member** with forces only at pins $A$ and $B$:

$$\mathbf{R}_{AB} \;=\; -\mathbf{R}_{BA}, \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{R}_{AB} \parallel \overline{AB}$$

The line of action is **along the line $AB$**, and the magnitude is found by applying equilibrium to a neighbouring link.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Slider-Crank Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most repeated problem type in KTU exams**. Master this derivation completely.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A slider-crank mechanism has:
- Crank $OA = r$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (constant, so $\alpha = 0$),
- Connecting rod $AB = l$,
- Slider $B$ moving along the horizontal line of stroke,
- External **resistance force** $W$ acting on the slider horizontally, opposing the motion.

Find the **driving torque $T$** required at the crank and the **reactions at all joints**.

### 3.2.2 Geometry (Loop-Closure)

Place $O$ at origin, line of stroke along the $+x$ axis. Let crank angle $\theta$ be measured from the $x$-axis (i.e., from TDC). Let $\phi$ be the angle of the connecting rod with the $x$-axis.

The slider position constraint (slider on $x$-axis) gives the **loop-closure equation**:

$$r\sin\theta + l\sin\phi \;=\; 0$$

Solving for $\phi$:

$$\sin\phi \;=\; -\frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$

For $0 < \theta < 180^\circ$ (slider to the right of $O$ if TDC is the right-most position), $\phi$ is a small negative angle whose magnitude is given by:

$$\sin\phi \;\approx\; -\frac{r}{l}\sin\theta \quad (\phi \text{ small, hence }\cos\phi \approx 1)$$

### 3.2.3 Free Body Diagrams

**FBD of the Slider (Link 4):**
Forces acting:
- External resistance $W$ acting along $-x$ (opposing motion to the right),
- Connecting rod force $F_{34}$ acting on pin $B$ along the rod (rod in compression, so pushes slider in $+x$),
- Normal reaction $N$ from the guide (vertical, no friction assumed).

**FBD of the Connecting Rod (Link 3):**
Two-force member: forces at $A$ and $B$ only.

$$\mathbf{F}_{34} \;=\; -\mathbf{F}_{23} \;=\; F_{rod}\;\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{AB}$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{AB}$ is the unit vector from $A$ to $B$, and $F_{rod}$ is the magnitude (positive in compression).

**FBD of the Crank (Link 2):**
Forces acting:
- Pin reaction at $O$ from ground ($\mathbf{R}_{12}$),
- Connecting rod force at $A$ from rod ($-\mathbf{F}_{23}$),
- Driving torque $T$.

### 3.2.4 Solving the System

**Step 1: Slider equilibrium along the line of stroke.**

From slider FBD, $\sum F_x = 0$:

$$F_{rod}\cos\phi - W \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F_{rod} \;=\; \frac{W}{\cos\phi}$$

> For $r/l$ small, $\cos\phi \approx 1$ and $F_{rod} \approx W$.

**Step 2: Crank equilibrium — sum of moments about $O$.**

The force $-\mathbf{F}_{23}$ at $A$ has components:

$$F_{Ax} \;=\; -F_{rod}\cos(\theta+\phi), \qquad F_{Ay} \;=\; -F_{rod}\sin(\theta+\phi)$$

Position of $A$: $\mathbf{r}_A = (r\cos\theta,\; r\sin\theta)$.

Moment of this force about $O$:

$$M_O \;=\; x_A F_{Ay} - y_A F_{Ax}$$

Substituting:

$$M_O \;=\; r\cos\theta\cdot\bigl(-F_{rod}\sin(\theta+\phi)\bigr) - r\sin\theta\cdot\bigl(-F_{rod}\cos(\theta+\phi)\bigr)$$

$$M_O \;=\; -rF_{rod}\bigl[\cos\theta\sin(\theta+\phi) - \sin\theta\cos(\theta+\phi)\bigr]$$

Using the identity $\sin(A-B) = \sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B$:

$$M_O \;=\; -rF_{rod}\sin\bigl((\theta+\phi)-\theta\bigr) \;=\; -rF_{rod}\sin\phi$$

The driving torque $T$ must balance this moment:

$$T \;=\; r\,F_{rod}\sin\phi$$

**Step 3: Combine with $F_{rod} = W/\cos\phi$:**

$$T \;=\; \frac{r\,W\,\sin\phi}{\cos\phi} \;=\; r\,W\,\tan\phi$$

Since $\sin\phi = (r/l)\sin\theta$ (from loop closure), and $\cos\phi \approx 1$:

$$\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; \frac{r\,W\,\sin\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta\right)^{2}}}\,}$$

> The **approximate** form for $r/l < 1/4$ (the common KTU assumption):

$$\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; \frac{r^2\,W\,\sin\theta\cos\theta}{l\,} \;=\; \frac{r^2 W \sin 2\theta}{2l}\,}$$

This is the **approximate driving torque formula** for a slider-crank with horizontal resistance $W$.

### 3.2.5 Reactions at the Crank Pin $O$

From crank FBD, $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$:

$$R_{12,x} \;=\; F_{rod}\cos(\theta+\phi)$$

$$R_{12,y} \;=\; F_{rod}\sin(\theta+\phi)$$

Magnitude:

$$R_{12} \;=\; \sqrt{R_{12,x}^2 + R_{12,y}^2} \;=\; F_{rod} \;=\; \frac{W}{\cos\phi}$$

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Four-Bar Mechanism

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

Four-bar $O_2 A B O_4$ with ground link $O_2 O_4$ horizontal. Crank $O_2 A$ of length $a$ at angle $\theta_2$. Coupler $AB$ of length $b$ at angle $\theta_3$. Rocker $O_4 B$ of length $c$ at angle $\theta_4$. An external force $\mathbf{F}$ is applied at a known point $P$ on the coupler at known angle $\beta$ (relative to coupler). Find the **driving torque $T$ on the input crank** and the **reactions at the pins**.

### 3.3.2 Free Body Diagrams

**FBD of Input Crank (Link 2):** Binary link — forces at $O_2$ and $A$.

$$\mathbf{F}_{12} = -\mathbf{F}_{32}, \quad \text{both along line } O_2A$$

**FBD of Output Rocker (Link 4):** Binary link — forces at $O_4$ and $B$.

$$\mathbf{F}_{14} = -\mathbf{F}_{34}, \quad \text{both along line } O_4B$$

**FBD of Coupler (Link 3):** Ternary link — forces at $A$, $B$, and the applied force $\mathbf{F}$ at $P$.

### 3.3.3 Equilibrium of the Coupler

Let $\mathbf{F}_{32}$ (force on coupler from crank) act at $A$ along direction $(\cos\theta_2, \sin\theta_2)$. Let $\mathbf{F}_{34}$ (force on coupler from rocker) act at $B$ along direction $(\cos\theta_4, \sin\theta_4)$ *as seen from the rocker* — so on the coupler, the force from rocker is in the **opposite** direction:

$$\mathbf{F}_{34,\text{on coupler}} = -F_{34}(\cos\theta_4,\;\sin\theta_4)$$

Equilibrium of coupler ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$):

$$F_{32}\cos\theta_2 - F_{34}\cos\theta_4 + F\cos\beta \;=\; 0$$

$$F_{32}\sin\theta_2 - F_{34}\sin\theta_4 + F\sin\beta \;=\; 0$$

Solving this $2\times 2$ system for $F_{32}$ and $F_{34}$ using Cramer's rule:

$$F_{32} \;=\; \frac{F\,(\sin\beta\cos\theta_4 - \cos\beta\sin\theta_4)}{\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)} \;=\; \frac{F\sin(\beta - \theta_4)}{\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}$$

$$F_{34} \;=\; \frac{F\,(\sin\beta\cos\theta_2 - \cos\beta\sin\theta_2)}{\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)} \;=\; \frac{F\sin(\beta - \theta_2)}{\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}$$

### 3.3.4 Driving Torque on the Crank

Take moments about $O_2$ for the input crank. The only force creating moment about $O_2$ is the force at $A$ from the coupler (which is $-\mathbf{F}_{32}$, with magnitude $F_{32}$):

$$T \;=\; F_{32} \cdot a \cdot \sin(0) \;?$$

No — the moment about $O_2$ is the cross product $\mathbf{r}_{O_2 A} \times \mathbf{F}_{A,\text{from coupler}}$. Since both $\mathbf{r}_{O_2 A}$ and $\mathbf{F}_{32}$ are along the *same line* (binary-member condition), their cross product is **zero**.

This is a paradox — the moment is zero! The resolution: the input crank, being a binary member, **cannot be in equilibrium under just a moment**. We must include the **driving torque $T$** explicitly. The driving torque equals the moment of the force on the *coupler* side at $A$, which equals the moment of $\mathbf{F}_{32}$ about $A$ of the *coupler side* (which is $-\mathbf{F}_{32}$ — but applied at the same point, same line of action, so the moment about the joint is the same as the moment of $\mathbf{F}_{32}$ on the **coupler** about a point on the crank.

The correct statement: compute the moment of the **net force on the coupler at $A$** about $O_2$. But that is exactly equal to the moment of the **applied force $\mathbf{F}$ at $P$** about $O_2$, because the crank is a two-force member and the couple at $A$ is internal.

$$T \;=\; F_{32} \cdot a \cdot \sin(\angle(\mathbf{F}_{32},\, \mathbf{r}_{O_2A}))$$

But $\mathbf{F}_{32}$ is along $\mathbf{r}_{O_2A}$ (binary member), so the angle is 0 and $T = 0$?

**Resolution using Virtual Work:** The driving torque on the crank equals the **moment of the applied force on the coupler about $O_2$**, divided by nothing (because the crank rotates):

$$\boxed{\,T \;=\; F \cdot d\,}$$

where $d$ is the **perpendicular distance from $O_2$ to the line of action of the applied force $\mathbf{F}$ on the coupler**.

This is the **principle of virtual work**: $T\,d\theta_2 = \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}_P$, where $d\mathbf{r}_P$ is the virtual displacement of $P$ when the crank rotates by $d\theta_2$.

## 3.4 The Principle of Virtual Work (Elegant Alternative)

For a one-DOF mechanism with input $\theta_2$ and applied loads on any link:

$$T_{in}\,d\theta_2 \;=\; \sum_i \mathbf{F}_i \cdot d\mathbf{r}_{P_i} \;+\; \sum_j M_j\,d\theta_j$$

In a **static** problem with no inertia and no input motion (mechanism held at rest), we compute the *virtual* (infinitesimal) displacements consistent with the constraints, and write the equilibrium of virtual work. For the four-bar with $\mathbf{F}$ on the coupler:

$$T_{in}\,d\theta_2 \;=\; \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{r}_P \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T_{in} \;=\; \frac{\mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}_P}{d\theta_2}$$

where $d\mathbf{r}_P / d\theta_2$ is the **velocity of $P$** when the crank rotates at unit angular velocity.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid cannot draw literal machine geometries (sketches of links, pins, etc.). Instead, the diagrams below show the **block-level functional architecture flow** of the static force analysis procedure and the **kinematic topology** of the two mechanisms. Pair these with the textual FBD descriptions.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Methodology of Static Force Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mechanism and Loads Given] --> B[Step 1: Identify all links and DOF]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Kinematics - positions, velocities, accelerations]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Inertia Loads - m*aG and IG*alpha for each moving link]
    D --> E[Step 4: Identify Two-Force Members - binary links]
    E --> F{Is there a binary link?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Force in binary link is along line of action]
    F -->|No| H[Use full 3-equation equilibrium per link]
    G --> I[Step 5: Draw FBD of each link - mark forces, couples, inertia loads]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step 6: Write equilibrium equations - sum Fx, sum Fy, sum M]
    J --> K[Step 7: Solve linear system for unknown pin forces]
    K --> L[Step 8: Compute required input torque - T = F_perp * crank length]
    L --> M[Step 9: Verify by virtual work or alternate link analysis]
    M --> N[End: Joint forces and driving torque obtained]
```

```mermaid
    Coupler ---|Pin B| Rocker[Rocker Link 4<br/>Binary Member]
    Rocker ---|Pin O4| Ground2[Ground Link 1<br/>Fixed Frame]
    Coupler -.External Load F.-> Coupler
    CrinkTorque[Input Torque T] -.Applied at O2.-> Crank
    ForceF32[Force F32 along O2A] -.Internal.-> Crank
    ForceF34[Force F34 along O4B] -.Internal.-> Rocker
    classDef ground fill:#1f1f1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

```

```mermaid
    Link --> RRight[Joint Reaction R_right from neighbouring link]
```
