<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: List any six steps involved in the design process of a machine element.**

**Model Answer (Verbatim-Worthy):**
1. **Recognition of need** — identifying the problem or requirement.
2. **Problem definition** — preparing a quantitative design specification.
3. **Conceptual design / Synthesis** — generating alternative solutions.
4. **Analysis and preliminary calculations** — applying strength and kinematic equations.
5. **Detailed design** — final sizing, material selection, and tolerance specification.
6. **Prototype testing and iteration** — building, testing, and refining the design.
7. **Production and communication** — releasing the design for manufacture.

> (6 points × 0.5 marks each = 3 marks)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: Differentiate between adaptive design and developmental design.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected):**

| Adaptive Design | Developmental Design |
|------------------|----------------------|
| Minor modifications of an existing product | Substantial changes via new ideas and methods |
| Very low risk and cost | Moderate risk and cost |
| Short duration (days to weeks) | Longer duration (months) |
| Example: New colour variant of a fan | Example: Introduction of ball bearings replacing bush bearings |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives below.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **eight steps of the iterative design process** for a machine element. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A shaft is subjected to a **bending moment of 250 N·m** and a **torque of 150 N·m**. The material is **40C8 steel** with $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa. Take $K_m = 1.5$, $K_f = 2.0$, $K_t = 1.3$, and **factor of safety = 3**. Calculate the shaft diameter using **ASME code** and round to the nearest preferred diameter. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a):**

The eight steps with brief one-line descriptions:

1. **Recognition of Need:** Identifying the gap or demand.
2. **Definition of Problem:** Quantitative design specification.
3. **Conceptual Design:** Generation of alternate concepts.
4. **Analysis and Preliminary Calculations:** Mathematical verification.
5. **Detailed Design and Optimization:** Final sizing and standardization.
6. **Prototype and Testing:** Physical verification.
7. **Iteration / Redesign:** Feedback-driven refinement.
8. **Production:** Documentation and manufacturing release.

*(Draw the flowchart from Section 4.1 — full marks.)*

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b):**

**Given:** $M_a = 250 \times 10^{3}$ N·mm, $T_a = 150 \times 10^{3}$ N·mm, $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa, $N = 3$, $K_m = 1.5$, $K_f = 2.0$, $K_t = 1.3$.

**Step 1 — Working Stress:**
$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{380}{3} = 126.67 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Stress Concentration Products:**
$$K_m K_f = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0$$
$$K_m K_t = 1.5 \times 1.3 = 1.95$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Bending Moment (ASME):**
$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[K_m K_f M_a + \sqrt{(K_m K_f M_a)^2 + (K_m K_t T_a)^2}\right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[3.0 \times 250{,}000 + \sqrt{(3.0 \times 250{,}000)^2 + (1.95 \times 150{,}000)^2}\right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[750{,}000 + \sqrt{(750{,}000)^2 + (292{,}500)^2}\right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[750{,}000 + 805{,}105.6\right]$$

$$\boxed{M_e \approx 777{,}553 \; \text{N·mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Required Shaft Diameter:**
$$d \geq \left(\frac{32 \times M_e}{\pi \times \sigma_{working}}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$d \geq \left(\frac{32 \times 777{,}553}{\pi \times 126.67}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$d \geq \left(\frac{24{,}881{,}696}{397.91}\right)^{1/3} = (62{,}532.6)^{1/3}$$

$$d \geq 39.68 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Standardize:**
$$\boxed{d_{standard} = 40 \; \text{mm (preferred IS series)}}$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the significance of **factor of safety** in the design process. Derive the formula for shaft diameter under combined bending and torsion using **ASME code**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A simply supported shaft carries a **pulley at the centre**. The pulley diameter is **300 mm**, belt tensions are $T_1 = 1500$ N and $T_2 = 500$ N, and the shaft span is **500 mm**. Using allowable bending stress = **80 MPa**, determine the **shaft diameter**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a):**

**Significance of Factor of Safety:**

- Factor of safety (FoS) compensates for **uncertainties** in load estimation, material properties, manufacturing defects, and operating environment.
- It is the ratio of the **failure strength** (yield or ultimate) to the **working stress**.
- Higher FoS is used for **critical applications** (aircraft, pressure vessels); lower FoS is used for **non-critical applications**.

**Formula:**
$$N = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

**Derivation:** (Reproduce the derivation from Section 3.2 of these notes — the full five-step derivation.)

**Final result:**
$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[K_m K_f M_a + \sqrt{(K_m K_f M_a)^2 + (K_m K_t T_a)^2}\right]$$

$$d \geq \left(\frac{32 \, M_e}{\pi \, \sigma_{working}}\right)^{1/3}$$

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Net Belt Pull (Effective Torque):**
$$T_{net} = (T_1 - T_2) \times \frac{D}{2}$$

$$T_{net} = (1500 - 500) \times \frac{300}{2} = 1000 \times 150 = 150{,}000 \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment on Shaft:**

The belt pulls the shaft downward with a net force of 1000 N at the centre of a simply supported beam of span 500 mm:

$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{1000 \times 500}{4} = 125{,}000 \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply ASME Equation (with $K_m = K_f = K_t = 1.0$ for simplicity):**

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[M_a + \sqrt{M_a^2 + T_a^2}\right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[125{,}000 + \sqrt{(125{,}000)^2 + (150{,}000)^2}\right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[125{,}000 + 195{,}256\right] = 160{,}128 \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Diameter:**
$$d \geq \left(\frac{32 \times 160{,}128}{\pi \times 80}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$d \geq \left(\frac{5{,}124{,}096}{251.33}\right)^{1/3} = (20{,}386.4)^{1/3} \approx 27.36 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Standardize:**
$$\boxed{d_{standard} = 28 \; \text{mm or 32 \; \text{mm (next preferred IS value)}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Steps in Design Process" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting iteration arrows:** Students draw the eight steps as a *straight line*. The KTU board **specifically awards 1 mark** for indicating the **iterative (feedback) loops** between analysis, testing, and redesign. **Always show curved feedback arrows.**
>
> 2. **Missing Factor of Safety rationale:** When asked "what is the significance of FoS?", students often write just the formula. Examiners expect **3 distinct reasons** (load uncertainty, material variability, consequences of failure). Listing only one costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Using $\sigma_y$ in denominator instead of $\sigma_u$:** For brittle materials, the FoS is based on ultimate stress; for ductile, on yield. Examiners will deduct **0.5 mark** if you confuse the two without stating the basis.
>
> 4. **Skipping the "standard preferred diameter" step:** Even if the calculated $d = 27.36$ mm is mathematically correct, KTU expects you to round up to a **standard IS preferred diameter** (e.g., 28 mm or 32 mm). Skipping this loses **1 full mark** in Part B sub-questions.
>
> 5. **Not stating units in intermediate steps:** Every numerical sub-step (e.g., $T = 95{,}490$ N·mm) **must carry units** until the final answer. Examiners routinely deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
>
> 6. **Using ASME $M_e$ formula without defining $K_m, K_f, K_t$:** Always write one line stating what these factors represent (stress concentration, dynamic loading, reliability). Examiners treat undefined factors as a **conceptual gap**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Pin to Your Wall:**

- [ ] The design process is **iterative**, not linear — always draw feedback loops.
- [ ] **Eight canonical steps:** Need → Definition → Synthesis → Analysis → Detailed Design → Prototype → Iteration → Production.
- [ ] **Factor of Safety $N$** is dimensionless; always state whether it is based on $\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_u$.
- [ ] Working stress formula: $\sigma_{working} = \sigma_y / N$.
- [ ] ASME Equivalent Bending Moment: $M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[K_m K_f M_a + \sqrt{(K_m K_f M_a)^2 + (K_m K_t T_a)^2}\right]$.
- [ ] ASME Equivalent Torque: $T_e = \sqrt{(K_m K_t T_a)^2 + 0.75(K_m K_f M_a)^2}$.
- [ ] Shaft diameter (bending only): $d \geq \left(\frac{32 M}{\pi \sigma_{working}}\right)^{1/3}$.
- [ ] Shaft diameter (torsion only): $d \geq \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{working}}\right)^{1/3}$.
- [ ] Power-torque-speed: $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ (kW), with $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m.
- [ ] Always use **standard preferred diameters** (10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50 mm) per IS 919.
- [ ] Three design types: **Adaptive** (minor), **Developmental** (major), **New/Original** (novel).
- [ ] Maximum shear stress theory (Guest) is the basis of ASME shaft design.
- [ ] Endurance limit for steels: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_u$ under reversed bending.
- [ ] Always include **units** in intermediate calculations.
- [ ] The design process is **decision-making under constraints** — engineering judgment is just as critical as mathematics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preferred Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preferred Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preferred Numbers** are a standardized series of numbers, arranged in a **geometric progression**, that are used as a basis for selecting standard sizes, dimensions, ratings, and capacities in engineering design. They form the rational foundation of **standardization** and **simplification** in manufacturing, ensuring that components produced in one plant fit and function in assemblies made anywhere else in the world.

According to **ISO 3:1973** and **IS 1076 (Part 2)**, the internationally accepted preferred number systems are based on the work of **Colonel Charles Renard** (French military engineer, 1877), and are therefore commonly called **Renard Series** (R-series).

The general mathematical form of a Renard series is:

$$
N_n = 10^{k/n}
$$

where:
- $N_n$ = the $n^{\text{th}}$ preferred number
- $n$ = the number of steps in a decade (5, 10, 20, or 40)
- $k$ = the integer index (0, 1, 2, ..., $n-1$)
- The common ratio (step factor) is $r = 10^{1/n}$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Ladder" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a carpenter building ladders for sale. If you make ladders of random heights (1.87 m, 2.43 m, 3.15 m, 3.78 m...), customers will be confused, retailers cannot stock them efficiently, and you will waste wood. So, you decide to make ladders only in certain "round" sizes: 1.5 m, 2 m, 2.5 m, 3 m, 4 m, 5 m, etc.
> 
> These "round" sizes must be spaced **intelligently** — not by adding a constant (like 0.5 m each time, which would make the jump feel the same in small ladders but huge in large ladders). Instead, each step should feel like the **same percentage jump** (a "geometric" jump). This way, whether you are in the small range or the large range, the spacing feels perceptually uniform.
> 
> **Preferred numbers are exactly this rational ladder system for engineering dimensions.**

### 1.3 Why Engineers Need Preferred Numbers

- **Rationalization**: Reduces an infinite variety of sizes to a manageable, logical set.
- **Standardization**: Ensures interchangeability of parts across manufacturers and countries.
- **Economy**: Reduces tooling costs, inventory, and production complexity.
- **Design Simplicity**: Designers can select from a limited, well-known palette.
- **Quality Control**: Tolerances and grading are easier to define and inspect.

### 1.4 Physical Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **ISO 3:1973** — International Standard governing preferred numbers.
- **IS 1076 (Part 2):1985** — Indian Standard adopting the R-series.
- **Renard Series Designations**: **R5, R10, R20, R40** (5, 10, 20, 40 steps per decade).
- **Step Factor Range**: From **R5 = 1.5849** (≈ **60 % step**) to **R40 = 1.0593** (≈ **6 % step**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars: Standardization & Simplification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardization** is the process of establishing rules or criteria for repetitive application. It reduces the variety of products, materials, and procedures to a logical, manageable set.
>
> **Simplification** is the process of reducing the number of types within a product group to the minimum necessary to meet the user's needs. Preferred numbers are the **mathematical backbone** of simplification.

### 2.2 Geometric Progression — The Heart of Preferred Numbers

A geometric progression (G.P.) is a sequence where **each term is obtained by multiplying the previous term by a constant ratio**. In a preferred number system:

- The terms range from **1 to 10** within one decade.
- The common ratio is $r = 10^{1/n}$, where $n$ is the number of terms per decade.
- The first term is conventionally $a = 1$, the last is $a \cdot r^{n-1} = 10^{(n-1)/n}$.

**Why G.P. and not A.P.?**
- In a **Geometric Progression**, the **relative (percentage) error** is constant. Whether a part is 1 mm or 1000 mm, the tolerance feels proportionally the same.
- In an **Arithmetic Progression**, the absolute jump is fixed, making small sizes too widely spaced and large sizes too crowded.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Common Ratio (R-n series) | $r = 10^{1/n}$ | Unitless | Step factor per decade |
| 2 | $k^{\text{th}}$ preferred number | $N_k = 10^{k/n}$ | $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$ | Individual preferred value |
| 3 | Step percentage | $\Delta = (r - 1) \times 100$ | Percent | Approximate spacing in % |
| 4 | Multi-decade expansion | $N_{k,m} = N_k \times 10^m$ | $m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$ | Extends range across decades |
| 5 | R5 ratio | $r_5 = 10^{1/5}$ | **1.5849** | ≈ 58.49 % step |
| 6 | R10 ratio | $r_{10} = 10^{1/10}$ | **1.2589** | ≈ 25.89 % step |
| 7 | R20 ratio | $r_{20} = 10^{1/20}$ | **1.1220** | ≈ 12.20 % step |
| 8 | R40 ratio | $r_{40} = 10^{1/40}$ | **1.0593** | ≈ 5.93 % step |
| 9 | R80 ratio (extended) | $r_{80} = 10^{1/80}$ | **1.0294** | ≈ 2.94 % step |
| 10 | Rounded preferred number | $N_k^{\text{rnd}} = \text{round}(10^{k/n}, \text{sig. figs.})$ | Up to 3 significant digits | Rounded practical value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note on Symbols:** All absolute value bars in mathematical expressions within this table are rendered using the word "approx." or the symbol "≈" to maintain Markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 The Four Basic Renard Series — A Summary

| Series | $n$ | Step Factor $r$ | Step % | No. of terms/decade | Typical Use |
|--------|-----|------------------|--------|----------------------|-------------|
| **R5** | 5 | 1.5849 | 58.49 % | 5 | Coarse grading — wire rope, ropes, large bearings |
| **R10** | 10 | 1.2589 | 25.89 % | 10 | General engineering — shaft diameters, sheet metal |
| **R20** | 20 | 1.1220 | 12.20 % | 20 | Precision fits — fasteners, tolerances |
| **R40** | 40 | 1.0593 | 5.93 % | 40 | Fine grading — high-precision components |

### 2.5 R5, R10, R20, R40 — Complete Tabulated Series (Rounded)

**R5 (n = 5):** 1.00, 1.60, 2.50, 4.00, 6.30
**R10 (n = 10):** 1.00, 1.25, 1.60, 2.00, 2.50, 3.15, 4.00, 5.00, 6.30, 8.00
**R20 (n = 20):** 1.00, 1.12, 1.25, 1.40, 1.60, 1.80, 2.00, 2.24, 2.50, 2.80, 3.15, 3.55, 4.00, 4.50, 5.00, 5.60, 6.30, 7.10, 8.00, 9.00
**R40 (n = 40):** 1.00, 1.06, 1.12, 1.18, 1.25, 1.32, 1.40, 1.50, 1.60, 1.70, 1.80, 1.90, 2.00, 2.12, 2.24, 2.36, 2.50, 2.65, 2.80, 3.00, 3.15, 3.35, 3.55, 3.75, 4.00, 4.25, 4.50, 4.75, 5.00, 5.30, 5.60, 6.00, 6.30, 6.70, 7.10, 7.50, 8.00, 8.50, 9.00, 9.50

### 2.6 Selection Logic for Designers

- **Choose R5** when tolerances are wide and the product family is limited.
- **Choose R10** as the most common default for general engineering.
- **Choose R20** when the application demands finer dimensional granularity.
- **Choose R40** only for very high-precision assemblies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Laws of Preferred Numbers (IS 1076):**
> 1. The numbers in a series follow a geometric progression with ratio $10^{1/n}$.
> 2. Any preferred number series is divisible by 10 (e.g., R5 ⊂ R10 ⊂ R20 ⊂ R40).
> 3. Every preferred number is rounded off to 2 or 3 significant figures.
> 4. Preferred numbers extend across all decades by multiplying or dividing by powers of 10.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Wire, sheet metal, and rope** diameters (R5/R10).
- **Shaft diameters, key dimensions** in machine design (R10/R20).
- **Capacitors, resistors, standard fasteners** (R20/R40).
- **Bearings, pipes, electric cables** — all international standard sizes.
- **Tolerances on machined parts** in fits and allowances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Common Ratio

**Step 1: Define the decade.**
A "decade" spans a factor of 10 — for example, from 1 mm to 10 mm, or 10 kW to 100 kW. So the **first term is 1** and the **last term is 10**.

$$
a_1 = 1, \quad a_{n+1} = 10
$$

**Step 2: Use the standard G.P. formula.**
For a G.P. with $n$ terms:

$$
a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{n-1}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the boundary conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{n+1} &= a_1 \cdot r^{n} = 10 \\
1 \cdot r^{n} &= 10 \\
r^{n} &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Take the $n^{\text{th}}$ root to isolate $r$.**

$$
r = 10^{1/n}
$$

This is the **universal Renard common ratio formula**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the $k^{\text{th}}$ Preferred Number

**Step 1: General term of a G.P.**

$$
a_k = a_1 \cdot r^{k-1} = 1 \cdot (10^{1/n})^{k-1}
$$

**Step 2: Simplify using exponent laws.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_k &= 10^{(k-1)/n} \\
a_k &= 10^{k/n} \cdot 10^{-1/n} \\
a_k &= \frac{10^{k/n}}{10^{1/n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Use the more standard convention.**
In practice, Renard series are indexed from $k = 0$ (giving the first term = 1) to $k = n$ (giving the last term = 10):

$$
\boxed{N_k = 10^{k/n}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n}
$$

**Step 4: Verification.**
For $n = 10$ (R10), $k = 0$: $N_0 = 10^{0} = 1$ ✓
For $n = 10$, $k = 10$: $N_{10} = 10^{10/10} = 10$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Step Percentage

**Step 1: Step factor definition.**

$$
r = 10^{1/n}
$$

**Step 2: Percentage step formula.**

$$
\Delta = (r - 1) \times 100 \%
$$

**Step 3: Apply for each series.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R5:} \quad \Delta_5 &= (10^{1/5} - 1) \times 100 \\
&= (1.5849 - 1) \times 100 \\
&= 58.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R10:} \quad \Delta_{10} &= (10^{1/10} - 1) \times 100 \\
&= (1.2589 - 1) \times 100 \\
&= 25.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R20:} \quad \Delta_{20} &= (10^{1/20} - 1) \times 100 \\
&= (1.1220 - 1) \times 100 \\
&= 12.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R40:} \quad \Delta_{40} &= (10^{1/40} - 1) \times 100 \\
&= (1.0593 - 1) \times 100 \\
&= 5.93\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Constructing a Renard Series

**Problem:** Construct the R5 series from 1 mm to 10 mm. Round each value to 2 significant digits.

**Solution:**

The common ratio is $r_5 = 10^{1/5} = 1.5849$.

The general term is $N_k = 10^{k/5}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = 0: \quad N_0 &= 10^{0/5} = 10^0 = 1.0000 \rightarrow \mathbf{1.00} \text{ mm} \\
k = 1: \quad N_1 &= 10^{1/5} = 1.5849 \rightarrow \mathbf{1.60} \text{ mm} \\
k = 2: \quad N_2 &= 10^{2/5} = 2.5119 \rightarrow \mathbf{2.50} \text{ mm} \\
k = 3: \quad N_3 &= 10^{3/5} = 3.9811 \rightarrow \mathbf{4.00} \text{ mm} \\
k = 4: \quad N_4 &= 10^{4/5} = 6.3096 \rightarrow \mathbf{6.30} \text{ mm} \\
k = 5: \quad N_5 &= 10^{5/5} = 10.0000 \rightarrow \mathbf{10.00} \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**R5 Series (1–10 mm):** **1.00, 1.60, 2.50, 4.00, 6.30, 10.00 mm**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Series Comparison

| Parameter | R5 | R10 | R20 | R40 |
|-----------|----|----|----|----|
| Terms per decade | 5 | 10 | 20 | 40 |
| Common ratio $r$ | 1.5849 | 1.2589 | 1.1220 | 1.0593 |
| Step % | 58.49 % | 25.89 % | 12.20 % | 5.93 % |
| Contains lower series? | — | R5 ⊂ R10 | R10 ⊂ R20 | R20 ⊂ R40 |
| Rounding digits | 2 | 2 | 2 or 3 | 3 |
| Selection principle | Coarsest | General | Fine | Finest |

---

### 3.6 Multi-Decade Expansion Formula

A single Renard series only covers one decade (e.g., 1 to 10). To cover multiple decades:

$$
N_{k,m} = N_k \times 10^m
$$

where $m = -2, -1, 0, +1, +2, \dots$ is the decade shift.

**Example:** R10 starting from 0.01:
0.01, 0.0125, 0.016, 0.02, 0.025, 0.0315, 0.04, 0.05, 0.063, 0.08, 0.1, 0.125, ..., 1000.

This allows the same series to scale infinitely up or down.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Logic of Preferred Number Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Need a Standard Dimension] --> nodeB[Identify Application Type]
    nodeB --> nodeC{What is the Tolerance Class?}
    nodeC -- Wide Tolerance / Coarse Step --> nodeD[Choose R5 Series]
    nodeC -- General Engineering --> nodeE[Choose R10 Series]
    nodeC -- Precision Fits --> nodeF[Choose R20 Series]
    nodeC -- Ultra Precision --> nodeG[Choose R40 Series]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Apply Ratio r = 10 power 1 by 5]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Apply Ratio r = 10 power 1 by 10]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Apply Ratio r = 10 power 1 by 20]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Apply Ratio r = 10 power 1 by 40]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Generate Geometric Progression]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Round to 2 or 3 Sig Figs]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Extend Across Decades using 10 power m]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Final Standardized Dimension Set]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Preferred Number System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Input: Required Range 1 to 10] --> blockB[Decade Span]
    blockA --> blockC[Number of Steps n]
    blockB --> blockD[Geometric Progression Engine]
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE[Step Factor r = 10 power 1 by n]
    blockD --> blockF[Series Generator N k = 10 power k by n]
    blockE --> blockG[Rounding Module]
    blockF --> blockG
    blockG --> blockH[Rounded Preferred Series]
    blockH --> blockI[Multi Decade Expander x 10 power m]
    blockI --> blockJ[Output: Complete Standardized Family]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of the Renard Series (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[ISO 3 Standard Preferred Numbers] --> nodeB[Basic Series R5 R10 R20 R40]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Derived Series]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Coarse 58 percent Step]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Medium 26 percent Step]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Fine 12 percent Step]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Very Fine 6 percent Step]
    nodeC --> nodeH[R80 and Special Sub Series]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Wire and Rope Diameters]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Shafts and General Machine Elements]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Fasteners and Precision Fits]
    nodeG --> nodeL[High Precision Assemblies]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparative Graph — Step Percentage by Series

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step Percentage Comparison] --> stepB[R5: 58.49 percent]
    stepA --> stepC[R10: 25.89 percent]
    stepA --> stepD[R20: 12.20 percent]
    stepA --> stepE[R40: 5.93 percent]
    stepB --> trend[Trend: Step Halves as n Doubles]
    stepC --> trend
    stepD --> trend
    stepE --> trend
    trend --> insight[Result: Perceptually Uniform Spacing Across All Magnitudes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define preferred numbers. Why is a geometric progression preferred over an arithmetic progression in engineering standardization?**

**Model Answer:**

Preferred numbers are standardized numerical values arranged in a geometric progression, used as a basis for selecting standard sizes, dimensions, and ratings in engineering design. They are governed by **ISO 3:1973** and **IS 1076 (Part 2)**.

A **geometric progression** is preferred over an **arithmetic progression** because:
1. In G.P., the **percentage step is constant** ($r = 10^{1/n}$), so the spacing feels proportionally uniform across all magnitudes.
2. In A.P., the absolute step is constant, causing small sizes to be over-spaced and large sizes to be under-spaced.
3. G.P. is **dimensionally scalable** across decades by multiplying by powers of 10.
4. It **minimizes the number of sizes** needed to cover a wide range with reasonable tolerances.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for G.P. vs A.P. justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State the common ratios of the four basic Renard series. What is the step percentage for R20?**

**Model Answer:**

The common ratios are:
- **R5:** $r = 10^{1/5} = 1.5849$
- **R10:** $r = 10^{1/10} = 1.2589$
- **R20:** $r = 10^{1/20} = 1.1220$
- **R40:** $r = 10^{1/40} = 1.0593$

Step percentage for R20:

$$
\Delta_{20} = (1.1220 - 1) \times 100 = 12.20\%
$$

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for ratios list, 1 mark for step calculation)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the common ratio formula $r = 10^{1/n}$ for a Renard preferred number series. Show the full algebraic steps. (7 marks)**

**(b) Construct the complete R5 preferred number series for the range 1 mm to 10 mm. Show every calculation. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

A preferred number series spans a **decade**, which means it begins at $a_1 = 1$ and ends at $a_{n+1} = 10$.

For a geometric progression with first term $a_1 = 1$ and common ratio $r$, the $(n+1)^{\text{th}}$ term is:

$$
a_{n+1} = a_1 \cdot r^n
$$

Substituting the boundary condition $a_{n+1} = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 &= 1 \cdot r^n \\
r^n &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the $n^{\text{th}}$ root of both sides:

$$
r = (10)^{1/n} = 10^{1/n}
$$

Hence, the **universal Renard common ratio** is:

$$
\boxed{r = 10^{1/n}}
$$

For $n = 5$: $r = 1.5849$ (R5).
For $n = 10$: $r = 1.2589$ (R10).
For $n = 20$: $r = 1.1220$ (R20).
For $n = 40$: $r = 1.0593$ (R40).

*(7 marks — 2 for boundary setup, 3 for algebraic derivation, 2 for numerical evaluation)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

For R5, $n = 5$, so $r = 10^{1/5} = 1.5849$.

The general term is $N_k = 10^{k/5}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= 10^{0/5} = 1.0000 \rightarrow \mathbf{1.00 \text{ mm}} \\
N_1 &= 10^{1/5} = 1.5849 \rightarrow \mathbf{1.60 \text{ mm}} \\
N_2 &= 10^{2/5} = 2.5119 \rightarrow \mathbf{2.50 \text{ mm}} \\
N_3 &= 10^{3/5} = 3.9811 \rightarrow \mathbf{4.00 \text{ mm}} \\
N_4 &= 10^{4/5} = 6.3096 \rightarrow \mathbf{6.30 \text{ mm}} \\
N_5 &= 10^{5/5} = 10.0000 \rightarrow \mathbf{10.00 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final R5 series (1–10 mm): 1.00, 1.60, 2.50, 4.00, 6.30, 10.00 mm**

*(7 marks — 1 for stating r, 4 for computing 5 intermediate values, 2 for final tabulation and rounding)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) What is the difference between standardisation and simplification? How do preferred numbers contribute to both? (7 marks)**

**(b) The diameter of a shaft is to be selected from the R10 preferred number series. If the minimum required diameter is 32 mm, find the next three preferred diameters greater than 32 mm. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Aspect | Standardisation | Simplification |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Establishing uniform rules, specifications, and criteria for repetitive use. | Reducing the variety of sizes/types within a product group to the minimum necessary. |
| **Scope** | Covers materials, processes, testing methods, and dimensions. | Focuses on the **range and variety** of a specific product class. |
| **Goal** | Interchangeability, safety, quality assurance. | Minimum variety for maximum coverage. |
| **Tool** | Standards like ISO, BIS, ASTM. | Preferred number series. |

**How preferred numbers contribute to both:**

1. They provide a **rational mathematical base** for standardisation, ensuring that standard sizes across the world follow the same geometric ladder.
2. They **simplify production** by reducing the infinite number of possible sizes to a small, manageable set (e.g., 10 sizes per decade in R10).
3. They **lower manufacturing cost** by limiting the number of tools, gauges, and inventory items.
4. They ensure **interchangeability** — a bolt made in one factory fits a nut made anywhere, because both follow the same R-series.

*(7 marks — 3 for definitions with comparison, 4 for preferred numbers' role)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

The R10 series between 1 and 10 (rounded) is:
**1.00, 1.25, 1.60, 2.00, 2.50, 3.15, 4.00, 5.00, 6.30, 8.00, 10.00**

To extend this to 32 mm, we use the **multi-decade expansion** with $m = 1$ (i.e., multiply by 10):

**R10 (10 to 100): 10.0, 12.5, 16.0, 20.0, 25.0, 31.5, 40.0, 50.0, 63.0, 80.0, 100.0**

The next three preferred diameters greater than 32 mm are:

$$
\boxed{D_1 = 40.0 \text{ mm}, \quad D_2 = 50.0 \text{ mm}, \quad D_3 = 63.0 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Verification using the formula $N_{k,m} = N_k \times 10^m$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_5 \times 10^1 &= 3.15 \times 10 = 31.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(just below 32)} \\
N_6 \times 10^1 &= 4.00 \times 10 = 40.0 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(first above 32)} \\
N_7 \times 10^1 &= 5.00 \times 10 = 50.0 \text{ mm} \\
N_8 \times 10^1 &= 6.30 \times 10 = 63.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for stating R10 series, 2 for decade expansion, 2 for correct identification, 1 for verification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the index convention**: Some students use $k = 1$ to $n$ (giving $n$ terms) instead of $k = 0$ to $n$ (giving $n+1$ terms, including the boundary values 1 and 10). The KTU expected answer is **$n+1$ terms per decade** (inclusive).
> 2. **Wrong rounding**: Preferred numbers must be **rounded to 2 significant digits** (R5, R10, R20) or **3 significant digits** (R40). Never use full unrounded values in the final table.
> 3. **Mixing up series**: A very common error is using **R10 values inside an R20 question** or vice versa. Always restate $r$ at the start of every calculation.
> 4. **Skipping decade shift**: For values >10 or <1, students often write the single-decade series and stop. You must apply $N_{k,m} = N_k \times 10^m$.
> 5. **Confusing step factor with step percentage**: Step factor is a ratio (1.5849 for R5), while step percentage is the relative change (58.49 % for R5). The conversion is $\Delta = (r-1) \times 100$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: Preferred numbers are standardized values in geometric progression, used for rationalization of engineering sizes.
- **Origin**: Developed by **Colonel Charles Renard** (1877); codified by **ISO 3** and **IS 1076**.
- **Universal formula**: $N_k = 10^{k/n}$, common ratio $r = 10^{1/n}$, $k = 0, 1, \dots, n$.
- **Four basic series** (per decade inclusive of 1 and 10):
  * **R5** → 6 terms, ratio 1.5849, step ≈ 58.49 %
  * **R10** → 11 terms, ratio 1.2589, step ≈ 25.89 %
  * **R20** → 21 terms, ratio 1.1220, step ≈ 12.20 %
  * **R40** → 41 terms, ratio 1.0593, step ≈ 5.93 %
- **Multi-decade expansion**: Multiply any preferred number by $10^m$ to cover $m$ decades above or below.
- **Hierarchy**: R5 ⊂ R10 ⊂ R20 ⊂ R40 (each higher series contains all lower-series values).
- **G.P. > A.P.**: Geometric progression gives constant **percentage** error, ensuring uniform feel across all magnitudes.
- **Rounding rule**: 2 sig figs for R5/R10/R20, 3 sig figs for R40.
- **Selection guideline**: 
  * Coarse → R5 (ropes, large bearings)
  * General → R10 (shafts, sheet metal)
  * Fine → R20 (fasteners)
  * Ultra-fine → R40 (precision assemblies)
- **Engineering benefits**: Standardization, simplification, interchangeability, lower inventory, reduced tooling cost.
- **KTU key fact**: Examiners expect you to derive $r = 10^{1/n}$ and apply $N_k = 10^{k/n}$ — these are **favourite 7-mark derivation questions**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards and Codes in Design, Shock and Impact Loads
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Standards and Codes in Design, Shock and Impact Loads

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Standards and Codes in Design — The Engineering "Rulebook"

### Formal Definition
**Standards** are documented technical specifications, criteria, or procedures designed to ensure that materials, products, processes, and services are fit for their intended purpose and perform consistently. **Codes** are sets of rules and regulations that govern the design, fabrication, construction, and inspection of engineering components and systems to ensure safety, reliability, and interchangeability.

In KTU 2024 terminology, machine design is never a "free-hand" activity. Every shaft, gear, spring, and bolt must be dimensioned, toleranced, and selected based on **standardized references** published by national and international bodies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standardizing Bodies (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **BIS** – Bureau of Indian Standards (India) – e.g., IS 2062 for steel
> - **ISO** – International Organization for Standardization – e.g., ISO 9001, ISO 6336 (gears)
> - **AGMA** – American Gear Manufacturers Association – e.g., AGMA 2001, AGMA 908
> - **ASME** – American Society of Mechanical Engineers – e.g., ASME Y14.5 (GD&T), ASME BPVC (Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code)
> - **DIN** – Deutsches Institut für Normung (Germany)
> - **SAE** – Society of Automotive Engineers

### Conceptual Analogy — Why Standards Matter
Think of standards like the **rules of cricket** (or football): the pitch size, the ball weight, the player's uniform, and the umpire's signals are all standardized worldwide. Without these, two teams from different countries could never play a fair game. Similarly, a **bolt manufactured in India (BIS)** must fit into a **nut manufactured in Germany (DIN)** because both follow the same ISO metric thread standards. Engineering components are the "players," and standards are the "rulebook" that lets them cooperate globally.

### Preferred Numbers (Renault Series)
Designers often need to select from a logical sequence of sizes. KTU syllabus emphasizes the **Renault series (R5, R10, R20, R40)**, which gives preferred numbers based on geometric progression. The general formula is:

$$\text{Nth term} = a \cdot r^{N-1}$$

where $r$ is the common ratio. For R5: $r = \sqrt[5]{10} \approx 1.5849$. For R10: $r = \sqrt[10]{10} \approx 1.2589$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Factor of Safety vs. Service Factor — A Critical Distinction**
> - **Factor of Safety (FoS or N)** = Ultimate Stress / Working Stress (a *material/design* concept).
> - **Service Factor (Cs)** = Empirical multiplier based on the *type of loading and power source* (uniform, light shock, heavy shock). It is applied to the *load*, not the stress.
> - **Design Equation:** $T_{design} = T_{applied} \times C_s$

## 1.2 Shock and Impact Loads — The "Sudden Surprise" on a Component

### Formal Definition
A **shock load** is a force applied suddenly to a component, with a very short time duration (often microseconds to milliseconds), causing dynamic stresses that are far higher than the static stress calculated by simple force/area. **Impact loading** is the limiting case where the load is applied in essentially *zero time* (a hammer blow, a sudden drop, a collision).

KTU categorizes loads into three principal types based on how rapidly the force reaches its maximum value:
1. **Gradual (Static) Load** — applied slowly (e.g., weight of a building settling).
2. **Sudden Load** — applied from zero to full magnitude in a finite but very short time (e.g., tightening a bolt to full preload in one jerk).
3. **Impact Load** — applied in effectively zero time, transferring kinetic energy (e.g., a hammer striking a nail).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine pulling a dog's leash:
- **Gradual load:** You slowly tighten the leash (the dog eases forward).
- **Sudden load:** A squirrel darts across — you yank the leash instantly to the maximum.
- **Impact load:** The dog sees the squirrel and *lunges* — the leash snaps taut with kinetic energy (the dog was moving!).

The same leash experiences dramatically different forces in each case, even though the *final* tension might be identical. This is the essence of dynamic loading.

### Why the Stress Magnification Happens — A First Look

For a bar of cross-section $A$ and length $L$ under axial force $P$:
- **Static stress:** $\sigma_{static} = \dfrac{P}{A}$
- **Sudden stress:** $\sigma_{sudden} = \dfrac{2P}{A}$ — *exactly double!*
- **Impact stress:** $\sigma_{impact} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{PL^2}}\right)$ — can be 10× to 100× higher!

The number **2** in sudden loading comes purely from energy conservation (no height drop, all strain energy must equal twice the static strain energy), and the expression under the square root in impact comes from the additional term due to the falling height $h$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** A component designed safely for a static load of 50 kN may fail catastrophically under the same 50 kN applied suddenly or as an impact. Always compute **dynamic stress** for shock/impact cases and use a higher service factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engineering Loads

The first step in any KTU design problem is to **classify the load**. The applied force, time history, and energy content dictate which formula to use.

### 2.1.1 Gradual (Static) Loading
The load is applied slowly so that at every instant the system is in quasi-static equilibrium. The strain energy stored in the elastic member is:

$$U_{static} = \frac{P^2 L}{2AE}$$

The stress is $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ and the elongation is $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$.

### 2.1.2 Sudden Loading
A load of magnitude $P$ is applied **abruptly** at the free end of a bar — for example, a weight is hung suddenly rather than lowered gently. The weight performs no work as it is "placed" (height of fall $h = 0$), but the bar deforms. The energy balance gives:

$$P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{P^2 \delta_{max}^2}{2AE} \cdot \frac{1}{\delta_{static}}$$

Solving yields $\delta_{sudden} = 2\,\delta_{static}$, hence:

$$\sigma_{sudden} = 2\,\sigma_{static}$$

The load $P$ does work $P \cdot \delta_{max}$ as it descends by $\delta_{max}$ (since gravity still acts even though the load was "dropped from zero height"). This work equals the strain energy in the bar.

### 2.1.3 Impact Loading
A weight $W$ falls from a height $h$ onto a stop at the end of a bar. The kinetic energy of the weight (loss of potential energy) is converted into the strain energy of the bar:

$$W\left(h + \delta_{max}\right) = \frac{AE\,\delta_{max}^2}{2L}$$

This is a quadratic in $\delta_{max}$:

$$\delta_{max}^2 - \frac{2WL}{AE}\delta_{max} - \frac{2WhL}{AE} = 0$$

Solving by the quadratic formula and noting $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{E\,\delta_{max}}{L}$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{impact} = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2AhE}{WL}}\right]}$$

The term $W/A$ is the static stress, and the bracketed expression is the **stress magnification factor**.

### 2.1.4 Instantaneous Stress
For a suddenly applied *load* (not a falling weight), one can also define an instantaneous stress. The maximum stress reached is twice the static value. This is sometimes called the "factor of 2 effect" and is a classic KTU problem.

## 2.2 Stress Concentration — The Hidden Multiplier

At any geometric discontinuity (a hole, a notch, a shoulder fillet, a keyway), the local stress is far higher than the nominal stress computed from $P/A$ or $Mc/I$. The ratio is called the **Stress Concentration Factor** ($K_t$):

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$$

For ductile materials under static loads, local yielding redistributes stress, so a *notch sensitivity factor* $q$ (between 0 and 1) is used:

$$K_{f} = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

where $K_f$ is the **fatigue stress concentration factor**. KTU problems on shafts and stepped bars frequently ask for $K_t$ from standard charts (Peterson, Pilkey, or Shigley's textbook charts).

## 2.3 Design Philosophy and Material Standards

### 2.3.1 Selection of Factor of Safety
KTU 2024 emphasizes the **deterministic** and **probabilistic** approaches:
- **Deterministic:** $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ (or yield/working).
- **Probabilistic (Reliability-based):** $N$ depends on the standard deviation of stress and strength distributions, leading to a target *reliability index* $\beta$ (typically 3 to 4 for machine components).

### 2.3.2 Service Factor (Cs) Tables
A standard KTU table for service factors is:

| Power Source | Uniform Load | Light Shock | Medium Shock | Heavy Shock |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform (Electric motor, turbine) | 1.00 | 1.25 | 1.50 | 1.75 |
| Light Shock (Multi-cyl. engine) | 1.25 | 1.50 | 1.75 | 2.00 |
| Medium Shock (Single-cyl. engine) | 1.50 | 1.75 | 2.00 | 2.25 |
| Heavy Shock (Crank, hammer) | 1.75 | 2.00 | 2.25 | 2.50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** When designing a belt or gear drive, multiply the *design power* by $C_s$ before consulting the standard tables.

### 2.3.3 BIS Material Designation System (India)
- **IS 2062** – Hot rolled medium and high tensile structural steel.
- **IS 210** – Grey cast iron grades FG 150, FG 200, ..., FG 350 (the number is the minimum tensile strength in MPa).
- **IS 1570** – Carbon and alloy steel designation (e.g., 40C8 = 0.4% C, 0.8% Mn).
- **IS 319** – Free-cutting brass.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Module 3 problem!**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static stress | $\sigma = P/A$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | Static elongation | $\delta = PL/(AE)$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | Static strain energy | $U = P^2L/(2AE)$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | Sudden load elongation | $\delta_{sudden} = 2\delta_{static}$ | m |
| 5 | Sudden load stress | $\sigma_{sudden} = 2\sigma_{static}$ | Pa |
| 6 | Impact load stress | $\sigma_i = (W/A)\left[1+\sqrt{1+2AhE/(WL)}\right]$ | Pa |
| 7 | Stress concentration | $K_t = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{nom}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Notch sensitivity | $K_f = 1+q(K_t-1)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Design power | $P_d = P \times C_s$ | W or kW |
| 10 | FoS (static) | $N = \sigma_u / \sigma_w$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Preferred number (R-series) | $N_k = a \cdot (\sqrt[r]{10})^{k-1}$ | – |
| 12 | Impact factor (general) | $IF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Resilience | $U/V = \sigma^2/(2E)$ | J/m³ |
| 14 | Modulus of resilience | $\sigma_y^2/(2E)$ | J/m³ |

> **Note on units:** The values of $E$ are typically $E_{steel} = \mathbf{2 \times 10^5}$ MPa, $E_{cast\,iron} = \mathbf{1.2 \times 10^5}$ MPa, and $E_{aluminium} = \mathbf{0.7 \times 10^5}$ MPa.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Standards (BIS/ISO/AGMA)** ensure a KTU-graduate design engineer in Kerala can design a gear that is interchangeable with a German or American product, enabling global supply chains.
- **Shock/Impact analysis** is critical in the design of **crash-resistant automotive frames**, **helmet liners**, **drop-tested consumer electronics**, **railway buffers**, **bridge bearings**, and **rock-drill bits**.
- The **factor of 2 rule** (sudden loading) explains why cable connections, anchor bolts, and chain links are rated for *twice* the expected static load — a fact standardized in IS 2266 and IS 3053.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress in a Bar Under Sudden Load (Factor of 2)

### Problem Setup
Consider a vertical bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ fixed at the top. A weight $W$ is suddenly applied (no fall, no impact — just hung instantly) at the free end.

### Free Body and Energy Principle
At the instant of release, the weight $W$ has zero velocity. As the weight descends, gravity does positive work. The bar elongates and stores elastic strain energy. At maximum elongation, the weight is momentarily at rest (velocity = 0). Beyond this point, the bar would rebound.

**Work done by gravity as the bar elongates by $\delta_{max}$:**

$$W_{gravity} = W \cdot \delta_{max}$$

**Strain energy stored in the bar (from $\delta = \dfrac{WL}{AE}$ at any intermediate deflection):**

$$U_{strain} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \delta_{max}^2 = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \delta_{max}^2$$

where $k = AE/L$ is the axial stiffness of the bar.

### Energy Balance
By conservation of energy, the work done by gravity equals the strain energy stored (no losses assumed):

$$W \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \delta_{max}^2$$

Solving for $\delta_{max}$:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2WL}{AE}$$

### Comparison with Static Deflection
The static deflection under load $W$ alone is:

$$\delta_{static} = \frac{WL}{AE}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_{max} = 2\,\delta_{static}$$

Since stress is proportional to deflection ($\sigma = E \cdot \delta / L$):

$$\sigma_{sudden} = 2\,\sigma_{static} = \frac{2W}{A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A load applied suddenly produces exactly **twice** the static stress. This is why hoists, lifts, and pulley supports always carry a built-in safety margin for snap-loading.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress in a Bar Under Impact Load (Falling Weight)

### Problem Setup
A weight $W$ falls from a height $h$ onto a collar (stop) at the end of a vertical bar of length $L$, area $A$, and modulus $E$. The bar's top is rigidly fixed.

### Energy Balance
Three forms of energy are involved:
1. **Initial potential energy of the weight** = $W \cdot h$ (relative to the top of the undeformed bar).
2. **Additional work done by gravity** during deformation = $W \cdot \delta_{max}$ (the weight descends by $\delta_{max}$ as the bar compresses).
3. **Strain energy stored in the bar** = $\dfrac{AE\,\delta_{max}^2}{2L}$.

### Conservation Equation
Total energy input = Strain energy stored:

$$W(h + \delta_{max}) = \frac{AE\,\delta_{max}^2}{2L}$$

### Solving the Quadratic
Rearranging:

$$\frac{AE}{2L}\delta_{max}^2 - W\,\delta_{max} - Wh = 0$$

Multiplying by $2L/(AE)$:

$$\delta_{max}^2 - \frac{2WL}{AE}\delta_{max} - \frac{2WhL}{AE} = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}$:

- Here, $A=1$, $B = -\dfrac{2WL}{AE}$, $C = -\dfrac{2WhL}{AE}$.

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2WL}{2AE} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{2WL}{2AE}\right)^2 + \frac{2WhL}{AE}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{WL}{AE} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{WL}{AE}\right)^2 + \frac{2WhL}{AE}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{WL}{AE}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2AhE}{WL}}\right]$$

### Converting to Stress
Using $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{E\,\delta_{max}}{L}$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{E}{L} \cdot \frac{WL}{AE}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2AhE}{WL}}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{impact} = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2AhE}{WL}}\right]}$$

### Limiting Case Check
If $h = 0$ (sudden load):

$$\sigma = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 0}\right] = \frac{W}{A}[1 + 1] = \frac{2W}{A} \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the **factor of 2** result from Section 3.1 — a perfect internal consistency check.

If $h \to \infty$:

$$\sigma \approx \frac{W}{A} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2AhE}{WL}} = \sqrt{\frac{2WhE}{L}}$$

which scales as $\sqrt{h}$ — the impact stress grows as the *square root* of the fall height.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stress in a Beam Under Impact (Transverse Case)

### Problem Setup
A simply supported beam of length $L$, second moment of area $I$, and modulus $E$ is struck at mid-span by a falling weight $W$ from height $h$.

### Static Deflection at Mid-Span
For a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$\delta_{static} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI}$$

### Strain Energy Stored
The total strain energy in a beam under a central load $W$ is:

$$U_{strain} = \frac{W^2 L^3}{96EI} = \frac{1}{2} W \delta_{static}$$

### Energy Balance
Same logic as the axial case:

$$W(h + \delta_{max}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{eq}\,\delta_{max}^2$$

where $k_{eq} = W/\delta_{static} = 48EI/L^3$.

Solving identically:

$$\delta_{max} = \delta_{static}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{static}}}\right]$$

### Stress Expression
For a rectangular cross-section ($b \times d$), maximum bending stress is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{(W_{eq}) \cdot L/4 \cdot (d/2)}{bd^3/12} = \frac{3WL}{2bd^2}$$

Substituting $W$ with the equivalent dynamic load and using $\delta_{max}/\delta_{static}$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{impact,beam} = \sigma_{static,beam}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{static}}}\right]}$$

The bracketed term is called the **impact factor**:

$$IF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{static}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** A high static deflection $\delta_{static}$ *reduces* the impact factor. This is why springs, rubber mounts, and shock absorbers are deliberately designed to be compliant (high $\delta_{static}$) — they "absorb" the impact by increasing static compliance, lowering the peak stress.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Loading Types

| Loading Type | Energy Source | Stress Magnification | Typical Real Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gradual (static) | Work done by slowly increasing force | 1× (reference) | Building weight, hydrostatic pressure |
| Sudden | Work done by weight as it descends by $\delta_{max}$ | **2×** | Cable taut, hand-brake applied, dock mooring |
| Impact (low $h$) | PE + work by gravity | ~2 to 5× | Bolt tightened, rivet gun |
| Impact (high $h$) | Large PE dominates | Scales as $\sqrt{h}$ | Drop test, hammer blow, crash |
| Impact on compliant structure | PE + work by gravity, large $\delta_{st}$ | Reduced (lower $IF$) | Spring buffer, vehicle bumper |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Loads — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the taxonomy of loads from the perspective of a KTU design engineer and indicates which formula branch to use for stress calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[APPLIED LOAD ON COMPONENT] --> B{How is the load applied?}
    B -- Slowly over time --> C[GRADUAL STATIC LOAD]
    B -- Instantaneously P at once --> D[SUDDEN LOAD]
    B -- Falling Weight W from height h --> E[IMPACT LOAD]

    C --> F[sigma equals P over A]
    F --> F1[delta equals PL over AE]
    F1 --> F2[Use BIS Material Grade]

    D --> G[Work by gravity equals Strain Energy]
    G --> G1[sigma sudden equals 2 times sigma static]
    G1 --> G2[Apply service factor Cs from AGMA table]

    E --> H[PE plus Work equals Strain Energy]
    H --> H1[Quadratic in delta max]
    H1 --> H2[sigma impact equals W over A times one plus square root of one plus two AhE over WL]
    H2 --> H3[Check factor of safety with material yield]

    F2 --> Z[DESIGN OUTPUT: Dimensional Drawing and Material Callout]
    G2 --> Z
    H3 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Design Process Using Standards — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[STEP 01: Identify Functional Requirements] --> A2[STEP 02: Classify Load Type]
    A2 --> A3[STEP 03: Select Material from BIS Catalog]
    A3 --> A4[STEP 04: Compute Static Stress]
    A4 --> A5{Is load dynamic?}
    A5 -- No --> A6[Apply FoS, get working stress]
    A5 -- Yes --> A7[Apply Impact Formula with h and W]
    A7 --> A8[Apply Service Factor Cs]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Stress Concentration Kt from charts]
    A9 --> A10[Final Geometry with Preferred Numbers R series]
    A6 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[STEP 11: Verify against ISO or AGMA Code]
    A11 --> A12[STEP 12: Drawing and Documentation per ASME Y14.5]
```

## 4.3 Impact Loading Physical Setup — Schematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE[BEFORE IMPACT]
        nodeA1[Weight W at height h above collar] --> nodeA2[Bar of length L cross section A modulus E]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Fixed support at top]
    end

    nodeA1 -- PE equals Wh --> nodeB1
    nodeA1 -- Work by gravity equals W times delta max --> nodeB1

    subgraph DURING[DURING DEFORMATION]
        nodeB1[Energy Conversion: PE plus Work goes into Elastic Strain Energy] --> nodeB2[Bar compresses by delta max]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Max stress equals E delta max over L]
    end

    nodeB3 -- continues until --> nodeC1[Velocity of W equals zero at peak compression]

    subgraph AFTER[AFTER PEAK]
        nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Elastic rebound: W oscillates up and down]
        nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Energy dissipated by damping over time]
    end
```

    L5 --> L5a[AGMA 2001 Spur]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Explain the terms "Standard" and "Code" as used in machine design. Give two examples of each from BIS and ISO standards.**

**Model Answer:**

A **standard** is a documented technical specification that establishes uniform engineering or technical criteria, methods, processes, and practices. It primarily concerns *materials, products, and procedures* (e.g., **IS 2062** for structural steel, **ISO 261** for metric screw threads).

A **code** is a set of rules governing the *design, fabrication, and inspection* of components to ensure safety and reliability (e.g., **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code**, **IS 800** for general construction in steel).

The distinction is subtle: standards *specify*; codes *regulate* and *prescribe* minimum safety requirements, often legally enforceable.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU valuation):**
- Definition of Standard with example → 1 mark
- Definition of Code with example → 1 mark
- Clear distinction → 1 mark

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**A weight of 20 kN is applied suddenly to a steel bar. Determine the stress induced if the bar cross-section is 500 mm². Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $W = 20\,\text{kN} = 20{,}000$ N, $A = 500\,\text{mm}^2$.

For **sudden loading**, the stress is double the static stress:

$$\sigma_{sudden} = 2 \times \frac{W}{A} = 2 \times \frac{20{,}000}{500} = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

**Answer:** The induced stress is **80 MPa**.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Formula selection (sudden vs static) → 1 mark
- Substitution → 1 mark
- Final answer with units → 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Modified for 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a)** A weight of 5 kN falls through a height of 40 mm onto a collar attached to the lower end of a vertical steel rod of 30 mm diameter and 2 m long. The upper end of the rod is fixed. Calculate the maximum stress and elongation of the rod.  
Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the various types of engineering standards. Discuss the significance of preferred numbers (Renault series) in design.  
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Step by Step

**Given Data:**
- Falling weight $W = 5\,\text{kN} = 5000$ N
- Height of fall $h = 40$ mm
- Diameter $d = 30$ mm $\Rightarrow A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 30^2}{4} = 706.86$ mm²
- Length $L = 2$ m = 2000 mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Compute the static stress and deflection**

$$\sigma_{static} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{5000}{706.86} = 7.074 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\delta_{static} = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{5000 \times 2000}{706.86 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{10^7}{1.4137 \times 10^8} = 0.07074 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Apply the impact formula**

$$\sigma_{impact} = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2AhE}{WL}}\right]$$

Compute the term $\dfrac{2AhE}{WL}$:

$$\frac{2 \times 706.86 \times 40 \times 2 \times 10^5}{5000 \times 2000} = \frac{1.131 \times 10^{10}}{10^7} = 1131.0$$

$$\sigma_{impact} = 7.074 \times \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 1131.0}\right]$$

$$= 7.074 \times \left[1 + \sqrt{1132.0}\right] = 7.074 \times [1 + 33.645]$$

$$= 7.074 \times 34.645 = 245.10 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 245 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum elongation**

Since $\delta_{max}/\delta_{static} = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{static}$:

$$\delta_{max} = 0.07074 \times 34.645 = 2.450 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Maximum stress ≈ **245 MPa**
- Maximum elongation ≈ **2.45 mm**

> **Verification:** Static stress is only 7.07 MPa, but the impact stress is 245 MPa — a magnification of about **34.6×**. This dramatic increase justifies KTU's emphasis on dynamic analysis.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Formula selection → 2 marks
- Correct substitution of $A, h, E, L$ → 2 marks
- Correct numerical result → 2 marks
- Final answers with units → 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Step by Step

**Engineering Standards — Types:**

1. **Material Standards (e.g., IS 2062, IS 210)** — Define chemical composition, mechanical properties, and designation systems for raw materials.

2. **Dimensional Standards (e.g., IS 919, ISO 261)** — Specify preferred sizes, fits, and tolerances to ensure interchangeability.

3. **Process/Performance Standards (e.g., IS 2825, ASME BPVC)** — Define procedures for manufacturing, testing, inspection, and quality control.

4. **Code Standards (e.g., ASME BPVC, IS 800)** — Set mandatory rules for design and construction of critical components to safeguard life and property.

5. **Safety Standards (e.g., IS 1239, OSHA norms)** — Specify protective measures, ergonomic limits, and operational safety.

**Preferred Numbers — Renault Series:**

Preferred numbers are geometrically progressing series used to select standardized values of dimensions, capacities, speeds, and loads. The basic series are R5, R10, R20, R40, with common ratios $r = \sqrt[5]{10}$, $\sqrt[10]{10}$, $\sqrt[20]{10}$, $\sqrt[40]{10}$ respectively.

**Significance in Design:**
- Reduces the number of sizes a manufacturer must produce.
- Ensures interchangeability and rational progression of product range.
- Avoids unnecessary duplication of sizes.
- Facilitates standardization of components across industries.
- Example: A shaft diameter series of 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80 mm follows R10 (ratio 1.26), making the range compact yet comprehensive.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 5 types of standards with at least 1 example each → 4 marks
- Renault series formula + significance → 3 marks

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 – Modified for 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a)** A steel bar of 20 mm diameter and 1.5 m long is fixed at its upper end. A weight of 1.5 kN is applied suddenly at the lower end. Determine (i) the instantaneous stress, (ii) the instantaneous elongation, and (iii) the impact factor. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the concept of "Factor of Safety" and "Service Factor" in machine design. How are they different? Give a numerical example where a service factor must be applied in design.  
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Step by Step

**Given:**
- $d = 20$ mm $\Rightarrow A = \dfrac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} = 314.16$ mm²
- $L = 1.5$ m = 1500 mm
- $W = 1.5$ kN = 1500 N
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Compute static stress and deflection**

$$\sigma_{static} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{1500}{314.16} = 4.775 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\delta_{static} = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{1500 \times 1500}{314.16 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^6}{6.283 \times 10^7} = 0.03581 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Apply the sudden-load relations**

$$\sigma_{instant} = 2\,\sigma_{static} = 2 \times 4.775 = 9.55 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\delta_{instant} = 2\,\delta_{static} = 2 \times 0.03581 = 0.0716 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Impact Factor (IF)**

$$IF = \frac{\sigma_{dynamic}}{\sigma_{static}} = \frac{9.55}{4.775} = 2.0$$

**Final Answers:**
- Instantaneous stress = **9.55 MPa**
- Instantaneous elongation = **0.0716 mm**
- Impact factor = **2.0**

> **Insight:** Even though the weight is "just placed" with no fall, the stress is *exactly double* the static value. This factor of 2 is a cornerstone of KTU's Machine Design II module.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Area calculation → 1 mark
- Static stress and elongation → 2 marks
- Factor of 2 application → 2 marks
- Impact factor computation → 1 mark
- Final answers with units → 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Step by Step

**Factor of Safety (FoS, N):**
- Definition: The ratio of the *strength* of a material (ultimate or yield stress) to the *working (allowable) stress* in the component.
- Formula: $N = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_w}$ (or $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_w}$).
- It is a *material/design* concept, indicating how much margin exists between failure and operation.
- Typical values: $N = 1.5$ to $3.0$ for static loads, $N = 3$ to $8$ for dynamic/shock loads.

**Service Factor (Cs):**
- Definition: An empirical multiplier applied to the *applied load* (or transmitted power) to account for the type of power source and the nature of the driven machine.
- It is a *system/operating condition* concept, indicating how "rough" the duty cycle is.
- Selected from standard tables (AGMA, BIS, or textbook charts).
- Typical values: 1.0 (uniform) to 2.5 (heavy shock).

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Factor of Safety | Service Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Applied to | Stress (or material strength) | Load or power |
| Reflects | Material variability, failure mode uncertainty | Operating environment, shock severity |
| Source | Material property + analysis type | Empirical tables (AGMA/BIS) |
| Magnitude | Typically 1.5 to 8 | Typically 1.0 to 2.5 |

**Numerical Example:**

A belt drive transmits 10 kW from a 3-cylinder internal combustion engine (light shock power source) to a conveyor (uniform load). From the service factor table (Section 2.3.2), the combined $C_s = 1.25$.

**Design power for belt selection:**

$$P_{design} = P \times C_s = 10 \times 1.25 = 12.5 \text{ kW}$$

The belt must be selected from the manufacturer's catalog based on 12.5 kW, not 10 kW. Then, after selecting the belt, the actual working stress in the belt is compared to the material strength, divided by the material factor of safety (e.g., $N = 7$ for fiber-reinforced belts).

**Mark Distribution:**
- FoS definition + formula → 1.5 marks
- Service factor definition + table reference → 1.5 marks
- Clear tabulated difference → 2 marks
- Numerical example with correct use of $C_s$ → 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3:**
> 1. **Mixing the "factor of 2" with the impact formula** — students often write $\sigma_{sudden} = (W/A)[1+\sqrt{1+2AhE/(WL)}]$ and substitute $h = 0$, getting **1.414 W/A instead of 2W/A**. The *correct* sudden-load derivation gives the factor of 2 directly; the impact formula also gives 2W/A in the limit $h=0$ (as proven in Section 3.2), so do not confuse the two. KTU examiners expect the **direct 2×** for sudden load.
> 2. **Forgetting units consistency** — $E$ in N/mm², $A$ in mm², $L$ in mm, $W$ in N, $h$ in mm. A common error is using $L$ in metres without converting.
> 3. **Confusing Stress Concentration Factor $K_t$ (geometric, theoretical) with Fatigue Notch Factor $K_f$ (effective, notch sensitivity included).** KTU expects $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$.
> 4. **Omitting the impact factor in spring and shock-absorber design problems** — a classic Module 3 trap.
> 5. **Using $E$ for cast iron as $2\times 10^5$ N/mm²** — incorrect. Cast iron: $E \approx 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Wrong $E$ = wrong answer = **full mark deduction**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Standards and Codes in Design, Shock and Impact Loads):**

- ☐ **Standards vs. Codes:** Standards *specify*, codes *regulate*. BIS, ISO, AGMA, ASME are the major bodies. KTU expects 2 examples of each.
- ☐ **Preferred Numbers (Renault series):** R5, R10, R20, R40 with ratios $\sqrt[5]{10}$, $\sqrt[10]{10}$, $\sqrt[20]{10}$, $\sqrt[40]{10}$. Used to minimize the number of manufactured sizes.
- ☐ **FoS** ($N$) is applied to **stress**; **Service Factor** ($C_s$) is applied to **load/power**. Never confuse the two.
- ☐ **Three load categories:** Gradual (1×), Sudden (2×), Impact ($1 + \sqrt{1+2h/\delta_{st}}$).
- ☐ **Factor of 2 for sudden load** is a *direct consequence* of energy balance, not a coincidence. Derive it in your answer for full marks.
- ☐ **Impact stress** scales as $\sqrt{h}$ for large $h$ and as $\sqrt{1/\delta_{st}}$ for compliant structures.
- ☐ **Stress concentration** at notches/holes/keyways: $K_t$ from charts, $K_f = 1+q(K_t-1)$ for ductile materials.
- ☐ **BIS material grades** to remember: IS 2062 (steel), IS 210 (cast iron, FG 200 = 200 MPa tensile), IS 1570 (carbon/alloy steel designation).
- ☐ **Standard $E$ values:** Steel $\mathbf{2\times 10^5}$ MPa, Cast Iron $\mathbf{1.2\times 10^5}$ MPa, Aluminium $\mathbf{0.7\times 10^5}$ MPa, Brass $\mathbf{1.0\times 10^5}$ MPa.
- ☐ **Always use consistent units** (N, mm, N/mm² = MPa) in KTU numerical answers. Mismatch = full mark loss.
- ☐ **Conclusion line** in every design answer should state the *standard reference* and the *final FoS* used (e.g., "Designed per IS 2062, FoS = 3, suitable for medium-shock service").

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fatigue Loading** is a form of *time-dependent, cyclic, and fluctuating* mechanical loading under which a structural member or machine element experiences failure at stress levels significantly **below** its static yield or ultimate tensile strength. According to the KTU 2024 scheme (PCRUT502 Module 3), fatigue is the progressive and localized structural damage caused by *repeated* or *fluctuating stresses* (cyclic stresses) at points where strain localization occurs. Failure occurs due to the gradual initiation and propagation of microscopic cracks through the material bulk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT502 - Module 3):** Fatigue loading is treated as a *deterministic* time-domain phenomenon using stress-life (S-N) and strain-life (ε-N) approaches, along with the influence of mean stress, stress concentration, surface finish, and size effects on the endurance limit.

### 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU 2024 Standardized)

- **Cycle (N):** A complete sequence of stress (or strain) values that is repeated periodically.
- **Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$):** The algebraically largest value of stress in a cycle.
- **Minimum Stress ($\sigma_{min}$):** The algebraically smallest value of stress in a cycle.
- **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** The arithmetic average of maximum and minimum stresses.
- **Stress Amplitude / Alternating Stress ($\sigma_a$):** Half the algebraic range of stress.
- **Stress Range ($\Delta\sigma$):** The algebraic difference between $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$.
- **Stress Ratio (R):** The algebraic ratio of minimum to maximum stress.
- **Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$ or $S_e$):** The maximum completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an infinite number of cycles (typically $10^6$ to $10^8$ for steels) without failure.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Paper Clip

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation:** Take a standard paper clip and bend it once — nothing visible happens. Bend it 10 times — still fine. But bend it 50–80 times, and *snap!* — it suddenly breaks. The stress required to break it in a single bend is enormous, yet it failed under tiny repeated loads. This is **fatigue failure**. The same principle applies to aircraft wings, rotating shafts, springs, connecting rods, and bridges. The damage accumulates *one microscopic crack at a time*, until finally the remaining cross-section can no longer support the load, and catastrophic fracture occurs — often with no warning.

### 1.4 Why Fatigue is Critical in Machine Design

In real machines, almost no load is truly *static*. A rotating shaft experiences fully reversed bending every revolution. A spring on a car suspension experiences thousands of cycles per trip. A connecting rod undergoes tension–compression in every engine cycle. Therefore, **fatigue is the single most common cause (~80–90%) of mechanical failures** in engineered components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact for KTU Board Exams:** Even though the applied stress may be well below the yield strength ($\sigma_y$), the part will fail if the number of cycles is high enough. Hence, machine elements must be designed using *fatigue strength* criteria — not static strength criteria.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Fluctuating Stresses (KTU 2024 Classification)

1. **Fully Reversed Stress Cycle** — $R = -1$
   * $\sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min}$
   * $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max}$
   * Example: Rotating shaft under pure bending.

2. **Repeated Stress Cycle (Tension–Tension or Compression–Compression)** — $0 < R < 1$
   * Same sign, different magnitudes.
   * Example: Tension spring during operation.

3. **Pulsating Stress Cycle (Zero-to-Maximum)** — $R = 0$
   * $\sigma_{min} = 0$, $\sigma_{max} > 0$
   * Example: Leaf spring of a vehicle under one-way loading.

4. **Random / Irregular Stress Cycle** — $R$ varies
   * Non-periodic; analyzed using statistical methods (rainflow counting).

### 2.2 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — The Backbone of Fatigue Analysis

The **S-N Curve** (Stress–Number of cycles) is the experimentally obtained graph of stress amplitude (S) versus the number of cycles to failure (N) on a semi-log plot.

- **Ferrous metals (Steel):** Exhibit a clear **endurance limit** $\sigma_e$ below which the material has *infinite* life.
- **Non-ferrous metals (Aluminum, Copper):** No true endurance limit; design life is specified at $N = 5 \times 10^8$ or $10^9$ cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> The "knee" of the S-N curve for steels typically occurs at $N = 10^6$ cycles. For design purposes, KTU uses the **modified endurance limit** accounting for real-world factors.

### 2.3 Mean Stress Correction Theories (High-Yield for KTU)

When a stress cycle is *not* fully reversed (i.e., $\sigma_m \neq 0$), the allowable alternating stress decreases. The three classical theories are:

- **Soderberg Criterion** (most conservative; uses yield as failure criterion)
- **Goodman Criterion** (uses ultimate strength; widely used in KTU problems)
- **Gerber Criterion** (parabolic; less conservative)

### 2.4 Modifying the Endurance Limit — Marin Factors

The *real* endurance limit of a finished machine element is **not** equal to the lab-tested $\sigma_e$. Marin (1953) proposed a series of multiplicative correction factors:

$$\sigma_e' = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot \sigma_e$$

Where each factor accounts for real-world conditions (surface, size, reliability, temperature, stress concentration, etc.).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | MPa | Average of extremes |
| 2 | Alternating Stress | $\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | MPa | Half the range |
| 3 | Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | MPa | Full range |
| 4 | Stress Ratio | $R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Dimensionless | Classifies cycle type |
| 5 | Amplitude Ratio | $A = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m}$ | Dimensionless | Used in some derivations |
| 6 | Soderberg | $\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{n}$ | — | Yield-based, conservative |
| 7 | Goodman | $\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$ | — | Most common in KTU |
| 8 | Gerber (parabolic) | $\frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | — | Less conservative |
| 9 | ASME-Elliptic | $\left(\frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_y}\right)^2 = 1$ | — | Used in pressure vessels |
| 10 | Stress Concentration | $K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max,actual}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$ | Dimensionless | From charts (Peterson) |
| 11 | Notch Sensitivity | $q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \le q \le 1$ |
| 12 | Fatigue Stress Concentration | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Dimensionless | Used with $k_f$ in Marin |
| 13 | Marin Surface Factor (machined) | $k_a = a \sigma_{ut}^b$ | — | $a, b$ from tables |
| 14 | Marin Size Factor | $k_b = 1.24 \, d^{-0.107}$ (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm) | — | Axial loading: $k_b = 1$ |
| 15 | Miner's Rule | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$ | — | Cumulative damage |
| 16 | Goodman straight line | $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)$ | MPa | For $n = 1$ design |
| 17 | Fatigue stress for $n$ (Goodman) | $\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{n}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^{-1}$ | MPa | Working stress form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** *Always* specify which mean stress theory (Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber) is being used. Skipping this step is a guaranteed mark-deduction.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Crankshaft, connecting rod, valve spring design.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars, turbine blades (with superimposed mean stress from centrifugal loads).
- **Civil:** Bridges, railway axles.
- **Power Generation:** Steam/gas turbine blades, generator shafts.
- **Marine:** Propeller shafts (combined bending + torsion = principal stress fatigue).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Mean and Alternating Stress Components

We start with a generic cyclic stress waveform. Let the stress vary sinusoidally (or piecewise linearly) between $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Define the constant (mean) and oscillating (alternating) parts:**

A cyclic stress $\sigma(t)$ can always be decomposed as the sum of a *steady* (mean) component and a *fluctuating* (alternating) component.

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \cdot f(t)$$

where $f(t)$ is a periodic function (sine, square, etc.) that oscillates between $+1$ and $-1$.

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**

At the maximum of the cycle: $f(t) = +1$

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$

At the minimum of the cycle: $f(t) = -1$

$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system for $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$:**

Add the two equations:

$$\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

Subtract the two equations:

$$\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Express stress ratio $R$:**

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{\sigma_m - \sigma_a}{\sigma_m + \sigma_a}$$

Solving for the ratio of alternating to mean stress:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m} = \frac{1 - R}{1 + R}$$

This is a useful transformation for direct use in fatigue design charts.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Goodman Line

The Goodman line is a *straight-line* approximation in the Haigh diagram (plot of $\sigma_a$ vs. $\sigma_m$).

**Step 1 — Identify the two anchor points of the line:**

- Point 1: Pure reversed loading: $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a = \sigma_e$ (endurance limit).
- Point 2: Static failure: $\sigma_a = 0$, $\sigma_m = \sigma_{ut}$ (ultimate tensile strength, conservatively taken as the fracture point).

**Step 2 — Equation of a straight line passing through $(0, \sigma_e)$ and $(\sigma_{ut}, 0)$:**

Using the two-point form of a line:

$$\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{0 - \sigma_e} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{\sigma_{ut} - 0}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{-\sigma_e} = \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}$$

Multiply both sides by $-\sigma_e$:

$$\sigma_a - \sigma_e = -\frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}$$

Rearrange:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e - \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the factor of safety $n$:**

For a design with factor of safety $n$, the allowable stress pair $(\sigma_a, \sigma_m)$ must lie *below* the Goodman line. The design condition is:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

For a *fully reversed* stress of amplitude $\sigma_{ar}$ applied on top of a mean stress $\sigma_m$, the equivalent fully-reversed stress on the Goodman line is obtained by setting $\sigma_a = \sigma_{ar}/n$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{\sigma_{ar}/n}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{(only if no mean)}$$

More generally, the working stress design equation is:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{n} \left(1 - \frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^{-1}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Soderberg Criterion

**Step 1 — Replace $\sigma_{ut}$ with $\sigma_y$ (yield strength) in the Goodman equation:**

The Soderberg line anchors at $(0, \sigma_e)$ and $(\sigma_y, 0)$ instead of $(\sigma_{ut}, 0)$. This makes it more conservative since $\sigma_y < \sigma_{ut}$.

**Step 2 — Two-point line equation:**

$$\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{0 - \sigma_e} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{\sigma_y - 0}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}\right)$$

**Step 4 — With factor of safety:**

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{n}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Gerber's Parabolic Criterion

**Step 1 — Gerber observed that the failure locus is actually a parabola, not a straight line:**

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^2 + \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} = 1 \quad \text{(for } n = 1 \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce factor of safety $n$:**

$$\left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^2 + \frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\sigma_a$ in terms of $\sigma_m$ and $n$:**
