$$\frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} = 1 - \left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{n}\left[1 - \left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^2\right]$$

### 3.5 Miner's Rule for Cumulative Damage (Block Loading)

When a component experiences multiple stress levels (e.g., a shaft on an automobile that encounters different road conditions), Miner's linear damage rule states that failure occurs when the *summed* fractional damage reaches unity.

**Step 1 — Define fractional damage for a single stress level:**

If a part is subjected to $n_i$ cycles at a stress level $i$ that has an *endurance* of $N_i$ cycles (read from the S-N curve), the fractional damage at that level is:

$$D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all stress levels:**

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 3 — Failure criterion:**

$$D \ge 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Failure}$$

This is the **Palmgren–Miner hypothesis** (also called Miner's rule). It is *linear* (no interaction between stress levels) and is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.6 Worked Example (Goodman + Miner's Rule)

A steel shaft has ultimate strength $\sigma_{ut} = 600$ MPa, endurance limit $\sigma_e = 300$ MPa, and yield $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa. It is subjected to the following loading spectrum in service:

- Block 1: $\sigma_{max,1} = 400$ MPa, $\sigma_{min,1} = 100$ MPa, $n_1 = 10{,}000$ cycles
- Block 2: $\sigma_{max,2} = 350$ MPa, $\sigma_{min,2} = 50$ MPa, $n_2 = 30{,}000$ cycles

**Block 1 calculations:**

$$\sigma_{m,1} = \frac{400 + 100}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{a,1} = \frac{400 - 100}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

From the S-N curve (assume Basquin's relation: $\sigma_a \cdot N^b = C$, with $b = -0.1$, $C = 900$ for this steel):

$$N_1 = \left(\frac{C}{\sigma_{a,1}}\right)^{1/b} = \left(\frac{900}{150}\right)^{1/(-0.1)} = (6)^{-10} \approx 1.65 \times 10^{-8}$$

This value is unphysical — meaning the part *fails on the first cycle* at Block 1. So the design is **unsafe** even before Block 2 begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **In a real KTU exam problem**, the values of $N_i$ would be tabulated or given. The student's job is to compute $D_i$ for each block, sum them, and compare with 1.0.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Fatigue Criterion

| Loading Condition | Recommended Theory | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Ductile material, conservative design | Soderberg | Uses yield, very safe |
| General machine design (most common in KTU) | Goodman | Linear, easy to compute, balanced |
| Ductile material with high toughness | Gerber | Best fit to experimental data |
| Brittle material (cast iron) | Modified Goodman or ASME-Elliptic | Brittle failure modes |
| Random spectrum loading | Miner's Rule | Cumulative damage from S-N data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Cyclic Stresses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cyclic Stress Loading] --> B{Stress Sign Variation}
    B -- Alternating sign --> C[Fully Reversed Cycle]
    B -- Same sign --> D{Mean Stress Level}
    D -- Mean equals zero --> E[Pulsating Cycle]
    D -- Mean positive --> F[Repeated Tensile Cycle]
    D -- Mean negative --> G[Repeated Compressive Cycle]
    C --> C1[R equals minus 1, Sigma m equals 0]
    E --> E1[R equals 0, Sigma min equals 0]
    F --> F1[0 less than R less than 1]
    G --> G1[R greater than 1]
    A --> H[Irregular Random Loading]
    H --> H1[Analyzed via Rainflow Counting]
    H1 --> H2[Miner Rule Cumulative Damage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every cycle type has a characteristic **R-ratio**. KTU problems will always give you $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ and expect you to compute the R-ratio and identify the cycle type as a *first step*.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Fatigue Design Procedure (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Loading] --> B[Compute Sigma max and Sigma min]
    B --> C[Calculate Sigma m and Sigma a]
    C --> D{Is stress concentration present?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Look up Kt from Peterson Charts]
    E --> F[Compute q and Kf]
    D -- No --> F
    F --> G[Compute Marin Factors ka, kb, kc, kd]
    G --> H[Compute Modified Endurance Limit Sigma e prime]
    H --> I{Select Mean Stress Theory}
    I --> J[Goodman]
    I --> K[Soderberg]
    I --> L[Gerber]
    J --> M[Apply Safety Factor and Solve for Diameter or Stress]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Check Against Allowable Stress]
    N --> O[Design is Safe]
    N -- Fail --> P[Increase Diameter or Change Material]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — S-N Curve Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Cycle Region N less than 10 to power 3] --> B[High Cycle Region 10 to power 3 to 10 to power 6]
    B --> C[Infinite Life Region N greater than 10 to power 6]
    A --> A1[Strain Controlled Analysis]
    B --> B1[Stress Controlled S-N Analysis]
    C --> C1[Steels Endurance Limit Applies]
    A1 --> A2[Coffin Manson Relation]
    B1 --> B2[Basquin Equation]
    C1 --> C2[Sigma e constant for Steel]
```

### 4.4 Haigh Diagram — Mean Stress Correction Map (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HaighDiagram [Haigh Diagram Layout]
        direction LR
        X[Sigma m axis horizontal] --- Y[Sigma a axis vertical]
        Y --> P1[Point A: Sigma m equals 0, Sigma a equals Sigma e]
        X --> P2[Point B: Sigma m equals Sigma ut, Sigma a equals 0]
        P1 --- L1[Soderberg Line most conservative ends at Sigma y]
        P1 --- L2[Goodman Line straight line to Sigma ut]
        P1 --- L3[Gerber Parabola least conservative]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For drawing in the exam:** If you cannot reproduce the actual curve, draw a 2D Cartesian plot with $\sigma_m$ on the horizontal axis and $\sigma_a$ on the vertical axis. Mark three points: $(0, \sigma_e)$, $(\sigma_y, 0)$, and $(\sigma_{ut}, 0)$. Connect them with the three lines (Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber). Shade the safe region (below each line).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**Define fatigue loading. What is an endurance limit?**

**Model Answer:**

Fatigue loading is the type of *time-varying, cyclic* mechanical loading in which a machine element experiences failure at stress levels well below its static yield strength, due to progressive crack initiation and propagation under repeated or fluctuating stresses.

The **endurance limit** ($\sigma_e$ or $S_e$) is defined as the maximum value of *completely reversed* stress that a material — particularly a ferrous alloy like steel — can withstand for an *infinite* number of stress cycles (typically $N \ge 10^6$ for steels) without exhibiting fatigue failure. For non-ferrous materials such as aluminum alloys, no true endurance limit exists, and the **fatigue strength** is quoted at a specific cycle count (usually $5 \times 10^8$ or $10^9$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition 1.5 marks + Endurance limit explanation 1.5 marks.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the Marin modification factors used to correct the endurance limit of a machine element.**

**Model Answer:**

The Marin factors used to convert the laboratory endurance limit $\sigma_e$ into the *modified* endurance limit $\sigma_e'$ of an actual machine element are:

1. **Surface factor ($k_a$):** Accounts for surface roughness (machined, ground, polished, as-forged).
2. **Size factor ($k_b$):** Accounts for the size of the component (diameter, cross-section).
3. **Load factor ($k_c$):** Accounts for type of loading (bending = 1, axial = 0.85, torsion = 0.59).
4. **Temperature factor ($k_d$):** Accounts for operating temperature above or below room temperature.
5. **Reliability factor ($k_e$):** Accounts for desired reliability (50%, 90%, 99%, 99.9%).
6. **Miscellaneous factor ($k_f$):** Accounts for residual stresses, corrosion, electrolytic plating, etc.

The modified endurance limit is:

$$\sigma_e' = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot \sigma_e$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Listing the 6 factors with one-line description — 3 marks (0.5 marks each).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, 14 Marks]

A rotating shaft made of steel (with $\sigma_{ut} = 650$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 350$ MPa) is subjected to a fluctuating bending load. The maximum and minimum stresses are $\sigma_{max} = 350$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -150$ MPa. The shaft has a diameter of 50 mm, machined surface, and operates at room temperature with 90% reliability.

**(a)** Compute the mean stress, alternating stress, stress ratio, and identify the type of cycle. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Using the Goodman criterion, determine the factor of safety against fatigue failure. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Mean stress:**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{350 + (-150)}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Alternating stress:**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{350 - (-150)}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Stress ratio:**

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{-150}{350} = -0.4286$$

**Step 4 — Cycle identification:**

Since $R$ is negative ($-0.4286$), the cycle is a **fluctuating cycle with reversal** (a combination of fully reversed and repeated loading). It is *not* fully reversed (R ≠ −1) and *not* pulsating (R ≠ 0). It is a general mixed cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (a) Marks:** $\sigma_m$ = 2 marks, $\sigma_a$ = 2 marks, $R$ = 1.5 marks, cycle identification = 1.5 marks.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Marin factors for the given conditions:**

Assume the following values (from standard KTU data tables):

- Surface factor (machined, $\sigma_{ut} = 650$ MPa): $k_a = 0.83$ (interpolated from Shigley's table for $\sigma_{ut} = 650$ MPa).
- Size factor (rotating shaft, $d = 50$ mm): $k_b = 0.85$.
- Load factor (bending): $k_c = 1.0$.
- Temperature factor (room temperature): $k_d = 1.0$.
- Reliability factor (90%): $k_e = 0.897$.
- Miscellaneous factor: $k_f = 1.0$.

**Step 2 — Modified endurance limit:**

$$\sigma_e' = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \sigma_e = (0.83)(0.85)(1.0)(1.0)(0.897)(1.0)(350)$$

$$\sigma_e' = 0.83 \times 0.85 \times 0.897 \times 350 = 221.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Goodman criterion with factor of safety $n$:**

$$\frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = 1$$

Substituting values:

$$\frac{n(250)}{221.5} + \frac{n(100)}{650} = 1$$

$$n \left(\frac{250}{221.5} + \frac{100}{650}\right) = 1$$

$$n (1.1287 + 0.1538) = 1$$

$$n (1.2825) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 0.7796$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since $n < 1$, the shaft **will fail** in fatigue under the given loading. The design is unsafe.

**Step 5 — Redesign recommendation (if asked):**

To achieve $n = 2$ (typical design), the modified endurance limit must be:

$$\sigma_e' \text{ required} = 2 \sigma_a \left(1 - \frac{2 \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^{-1}$$

$$\sigma_e' \text{ required} = 2(250) \left(1 - \frac{2(100)}{650}\right)^{-1} = 500 \left(1 - 0.3077\right)^{-1} = 500 \times 1.444 = 722 \text{ MPa}$$

This means the shaft diameter must be increased, the surface finish improved, or a higher-grade steel selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (b) Marks:** Marin factor identification 2 marks, $\sigma_e'$ calculation 2 marks, Goodman formula application 2 marks, final answer with interpretation 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, 14 Marks]

A machine component is subjected to a fluctuating torsional load with $\tau_{max} = 200$ MPa and $\tau_{min} = 80$ MPa. The material properties are: $\sigma_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 290$ MPa.

**(a)** Calculate the mean and amplitude shear stresses. Apply the *Distortion Energy Theory* to convert the shear stress state into equivalent normal stresses, and identify whether the cycle is fully reversed, pulsating, or repeated. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Using the Soderberg criterion, find the factor of safety. State when you would prefer Soderberg over Goodman in a real design problem. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute mean and amplitude shear stresses:**

$$\tau_m = \frac{\tau_{max} + \tau_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 + 80}{2} = 140 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_a = \frac{\tau_{max} - \tau_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 - 80}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Distortion Energy Theory (DE) conversion:**

For pure shear, the principal stresses are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign: $\sigma_1 = \tau$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -\tau$. By DE theory, the equivalent normal stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3} \, \tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{\sigma_{eq}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

So:

$$\sigma_{m,eq} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau_m = \sqrt{3} \cdot 140 = 242.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau_a = \sqrt{3} \cdot 60 = 103.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the stress ratio:**

$$R = \frac{\tau_{min}}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{80}{200} = 0.4$$

Since $0 < R < 1$, the cycle is a **repeated tensile (non-zero mean) cycle**. It is not fully reversed and not pulsating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (a) Marks:** $\tau_m, \tau_a$ = 2 marks, DE conversion = 3 marks, cycle identification = 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply Soderberg criterion:**

$$\frac{n \sigma_{a,eq}}{\sigma_e} + \frac{n \sigma_{m,eq}}{\sigma_y} = 1$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{n (103.9)}{290} + \frac{n (242.5)}{380} = 1$$

$$n \left(0.3583 + 0.6382\right) = 1$$

$$n (0.9965) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \approx 1.0035$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

The factor of safety is approximately **1.0**, meaning the design is on the verge of failure. This is not acceptable for a real machine element.

**Step 3 — When to prefer Soderberg over Goodman:**

Soderberg is preferred when:
- The material is **ductile** and yielding (not fracture) is the primary failure mode.
- **Conservative design** is mandated (e.g., pressure vessels, lifting equipment, aerospace components).
- The material has well-defined yield strength data.
- Static and fatigue loads are simultaneously present, and we want to ensure *no yielding* under any combination.

Goodman is preferred when:
- The design is *less* conservative and ultimate tensile strength governs the failure.
- The material is *brittle* or has limited ductility.
- High cycle fatigue is the primary concern and yielding is acceptable locally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (b) Marks:** Soderberg formula 2 marks, numerical substitution and solving 3 marks, preference discussion 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert torsional to equivalent normal stress:** Many students apply $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ directly in the Goodman equation when the loading is *torsional*. This is a 2–3 mark deduction. Always use $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3}\tau$ for steel under DE theory.
> 2. **Using $\sigma_{ut}$ instead of $\sigma_y$ in Soderberg:** A classic sign that the student did not read the question carefully. Soderberg uses **yield**, not ultimate.
> 3. **Omitting Marin factors:** In any fatigue problem involving a *real* component (machined, finite size, etc.), failing to apply at least $k_a$ and $k_b$ results in a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Confusing $K_t$ (theoretical) with $K_f$ (fatigue):** Use $K_t$ for static brittle failure; use $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ for fatigue (ductile) failure.
> 5. **Wrong sign in mean stress calculation:** If the cycle crosses zero, $\sigma_m$ is *not* simply the larger of the two stresses. Always use the formula.
> 6. **Mixing up units:** $d$ in mm vs m, stress in MPa vs Pa. Stay consistent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Fatigue Loading**

- [x] Fatigue failure occurs at stresses **below** the yield strength under *cyclic* loading.
- [x] **Mean stress** $\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$; **Alternating stress** $\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$.
- [x] **Stress ratio** $R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ classifies the cycle: $R = -1$ (fully reversed), $R = 0$ (pulsating), $0 < R < 1$ (repeated tension), $R > 1$ (repeated compression).
- [x] The **S-N curve** plots alternating stress vs cycles to failure; steels show a clear **endurance limit** at $\sim 10^6$ cycles.
- [x] **Marin's equation** corrects the lab endurance limit: $\sigma_e' = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \sigma_e$.
- [x] **Goodman** is the most commonly used mean stress theory in KTU.
- [x] **Soderberg** is the most conservative; uses $\sigma_y$ instead of $\sigma_{ut}$.
- [x] **Gerber** is the least conservative; parabolic locus.
- [x] **Stress concentration** uses $K_f$ (fatigue) and $K_t$ (theoretical), related by notch sensitivity $q$.
- [x] **Miner's rule** for cumulative damage: $\sum n_i / N_i = 1$ at failure.
- [x] For torsional loading, **always** convert to equivalent normal stress using $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3} \tau$ (DE theory).
- [x] The design inequality is $\frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \le 1$ (Goodman with factor of safety $n$).
- [x] ~80–90% of all mechanical failures in service are *fatigue failures*.
- [x] Surface finish is critical: smoother surfaces (polished, ground) have higher endurance limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endurance Limit Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Endurance Limit Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Limit (Sₑ):** It is the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an infinite number of cycles (typically taken as **N = 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles** for steels) without causing fatigue failure.

In KTU 2024 scheme Machine Design parlance, the **endurance limit** is the limiting value of the stress amplitude below which the specimen does not fail for an **infinite number of stress cycles**. It is conventionally denoted as **σₑ** (or **Sₑ**) and is read directly from the horizontal asymptote of the **Wöhler S–N Curve** (Stress vs. Number of cycles).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Reversed Bending" Convention:** In most design data tables, the endurance limit is reported for a *rotating-beam* specimen under *fully reversed bending* (stress ratio R = −1). Any deviation from this test condition must be corrected using **modifying factors** (the Marin equation).

### Classification of Materials Based on Endurance Behaviour

| Material Type | S–N Behaviour | Endurance Limit Exists? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ferrous / Steel alloys** (Sᵤₜ < 1400 MPa) | Curve flattens (asymptote) | **Yes** (Sₑ ≈ 0.5 Sᵤₜ) |
| **High-strength steels** (Sᵤₜ ≥ 1400 MPa) | Curve keeps falling | **No** (use 10⁸ cycles as design life) |
| **Non-ferrous** (Al, Cu, Mg alloys) | Continuously descending | **No** (use fatigue strength at N = 5×10⁸) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Suspension Bridge Cable" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a thick steel cable of a suspension bridge. You can apply a tension of **50% of its breaking strength** *once* and nothing happens. But if you keep pulling and releasing it **a million times a day** (vibration, wind, traffic), small micro-cracks form at surface imperfections and *grow* with every cycle. Eventually, the cable snaps — *not* at the breaking strength, but at a much **lower stress level**. That lower stress is the **endurance limit**.

**Plain English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
- A material can survive one big push (static strength, Sᵤₜ).
- A material can survive *millions* of small pushes only if those small pushes stay **below a threshold** (endurance limit, Sₑ).
- That threshold is roughly **half** the ultimate tensile strength for ductile steels.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Examination Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Terms for the Board Exam:**
> - **Sᵤₜ** → Ultimate Tensile Strength
> - **Sᵧ** → Yield Strength
> - **Sₑ′** → **Rotative (Un-corrected) Endurance Limit** — the value taken directly from the standard specimen (Sₑ′ = 0.5 Sᵤₜ for Sᵤₜ ≤ 1400 MPa).
> - **Sₑ** → **Corrected Endurance Limit** at the design point (after applying Marin factors).
> - **Fatigue Strength (S_f)** → The stress amplitude that causes failure at a *finite* N (used when no true endurance limit exists, e.g., aluminium).
> - **Stress Ratio, R = σ_min / σ_max**
> - **Mean Stress (σ_m)**, **Alternating Stress (σ_a)**, **Stress Amplitude (σ_a)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S–N (Wöhler) Curve — The Heart of Fatigue Design

The Wöhler curve plots **stress amplitude (σ_a)** on the Y-axis versus **cycles to failure (N)** on a *logarithmic* X-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the S–N Curve (Three Critical Regions):**
> 1. **Static Region (N = ½ to 1):** Failure governed by Sᵤₜ (ultimate tensile strength).
> 2. **Finite Life Region (10³ < N < 10⁶):** Descending curve; Basquin's law applies: **σ_a = σ_f′ (2N)^b**.
> 3. **Infinite Life Region (N ≥ 10⁶ for steel):** Horizontal plateau = **Endurance Limit Sₑ**.

---

## 2.2 The Marin Equation — *The Most Tested Formula in KTU 2024*

The **uncorrected** endurance limit (from a polished, standard 7.52 mm rotating-beam specimen) is:
$$S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \quad \text{(for } S_{ut} \le 1400 \text{ MPa)}$$

To obtain the **corrected** endurance limit at the design point, multiply by a chain of modification factors:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

Where each factor accounts for one real-world deviation from the ideal lab test.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam ready. Memorise the table verbatim.**

| # | Factor | Symbol | Governing Formula / Value | Governing Parameter | Range / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Surface Factor** | $k_a$ | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}$ | Surface finish (grind, machined, forged, as-forged) | $a,b$ from Shigley table; Sᵤₜ in MPa |
| 2 | **Size Factor** | $k_b$ | $k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ | Equivalent diameter $d$ (mm) | For 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm (bending/torsion) |
| 3 | **Size Factor (axial)** | $k_b$ | $k_b = 1$ | Axial loading | Independent of diameter |
| 4 | **Size Factor (d > 51 mm)** | $k_b$ | $k_b = 0.91 \, d^{-0.157}$ | Large shafts | For 51 < d ≤ 254 mm |
| 5 | **Loading Factor** | $k_c$ | $k_c = 1.0$ | Bending | Pure bending |
| 6 | **Loading Factor** | $k_c$ | $k_c = 0.85$ | Axial | Pure tension/compression |
| 7 | **Loading Factor** | $k_c$ | $k_c = 0.59$ | Torsion | Pure shear/torsion |
| 8 | **Temperature Factor** | $k_d$ | Read from chart | Operating temperature | $k_d = 1$ for $T \le 450 °C$ |
| 9 | **Reliability Factor** | $k_e$ | 0.814 (50% R), 0.868 (90% R), 0.928 (95% R), 0.970 (99% R), 1.000 (99.9% R) | Required reliability | Statistical scatter |
| 10 | **Miscellaneous Factor** | $k_f$ | 1.0 (assumed) | Residual stresses, corrosion, plating | Engineering judgement |

### Auxiliary Fatigue Equations

| # | Concept | Formula | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Basquin's Law** (Finite life) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^b$ | Find stress amplitude for N cycles |
| 2 | **Endurance Ratio** | $S_e' = f \cdot S_{ut}$ | $f = 0.5$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa; else $f = 700$ MPa |
| 3 | **Stress Amplitude** | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}) / 2$ | From any fluctuating load |
| 4 | **Mean Stress** | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}) / 2$ | From any fluctuating load |
| 5 | **Stress Ratio** | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | Characterises load cycle |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is the Endurance Limit applied in Industry?**
> - **Automotive Crankshafts:** Designed for infinite life (≥ 10⁷ cycles) → governed by Sₑ.
> - **Aircraft Wings:** Designed for *finite* life (Basquin's law) — replacement after a fixed cycle count.
> - **Pressure Vessel Welds:** Endurance limit adjusted by surface factor $k_a$ for weld bead roughness.
> - **Bridge Cables & Rails:** Classic infinite-life design (Sₑ controls).
> - **Turbine Blades:** Thermal + mechanical fatigue → temperature factor $k_d$ crucial.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rotative Endurance Limit from Sᵤₜ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Basis:** Augustine-Augis (Shigley) reports the empirical regression of test data:
> $$S_e' = 0.504 \cdot S_{ut} \quad \text{(MPa, Sᵤₜ ≤ 1400 MPa)}$$

For KTU 2024 exam purposes, the **rounded value** is used:
$$\boxed{\,S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}\,}$$

**Why 0.5?** The rotating-beam specimen's *rotational symmetry* means every point on its surface experiences the full reversal. The notch sensitivity of the polished specimen is minimal. Extensive testing of ductile steels shows the failure threshold settles at roughly **50% of the ultimate strength**.

**For high-strength steels ($S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa):**
$$\boxed{\,S_e' = 700 \text{ MPa}\,}$$

This cap reflects that the endurance ratio $f = S_e'/S_{ut}$ *decreases* as strength increases; using a fixed stress value is more conservative.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Marin Modification Equation

Starting from the lab-measured value $S_e'$, six real-world conditions degrade (or sometimes enhance) it. Each condition is mathematically isolated as a unit-less multiplier:

### Step 1 — Surface Factor ($k_a$)

Empirical regression on surface-finish data yields a power-law model:
$$k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}$$
where $a$ and $b$ are surface-specific constants.

| Surface Finish | $a$ (MPa) | $b$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polished / Ground | 1.58 | −0.085 |
| Machined / Cold-Drawn | 4.51 | −0.265 |
| Hot-Rolled | 57.7 | −0.718 |
| As-Forged | 272.0 | −0.995 |

> **Why does rougher surface reduce $k_a$?** Surface micro-notches act as *crack initiation sites*. A ground surface has the smallest micro-notches → highest $k_a$.

### Step 2 — Size Factor ($k_b$)

Larger parts have a *larger volume of stressed material* → higher probability of a critical flaw. Empirical fit:
$$k_b = \left(\frac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107} \quad (2.79 \le d \le 51 \text{ mm})$$

**Why 7.62 mm?** That's the diameter of the standard rotating-beam specimen (the reference).

For very large shafts ($d > 51$ mm, up to 254 mm):
$$k_b = 0.91 \, d^{-0.157}$$

For *axial* loading, the size effect disappears because the entire cross-section is uniformly stressed:
$$k_b = 1 \quad (\text{axial})$$

### Step 3 — Loading Factor ($k_c$)

| Loading Type | $k_c$ | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bending | 1.000 | Reference case (rotating-beam is bending) |
| Axial | 0.850 | Stress is uniform; small defects fully activated |
| Torsion | 0.590 | Shear initiates cracks less effectively than normal stress |

### Step 4 — Temperature Factor ($k_d$)

At temperatures above 450 °C, creep dominates and fatigue life drops sharply. Below 450 °C:
$$k_d \approx 1.0$$

### Step 5 — Reliability Factor ($k_e$)

Statistical scatter in endurance data. To raise the design reliability from 50% → 99.9%, a portion of the safety margin is consumed:

| Reliability R | $k_e$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.50 | 1.000 |
| 0.90 | 0.897 |
| 0.95 | 0.868 |
| 0.99 | 0.814 |
| 0.999 | 0.753 |
| 0.9999 | 0.702 |

### Step 6 — Miscellaneous Factor ($k_f$)

Includes residual stresses, corrosion, electroplating, and frequency effects. Default = 1.0 unless specifically called out.

### Final Marin Equation

$$\boxed{\,S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot (0.5 \, S_{ut})\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Model Numerical — Practice Problem 1:**
>
> A steel shaft has $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, diameter $d = 30$ mm, *machined* surface finish, is under *rotating bending*, operates at room temperature, and requires 95% reliability. Find the corrected endurance limit $S_e$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit:**
$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor (machined finish):**
$$k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b} = 4.51 \times 600^{-0.265}$$
$$\ln(k_a) = \ln(4.51) - 0.265 \times \ln(600)$$
$$\ln(k_a) = 1.506 - 0.265 \times 6.397 = 1.506 - 1.695 = -0.189$$
$$k_a = e^{-0.189} = 0.828$$

**Step 3 — Size factor (d = 30 mm, between 2.79 and 51 mm):**
$$k_b = 0.879 \times d^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 30^{-0.107}$$
$$\ln(k_b) = \ln(0.879) - 0.107 \times \ln(30)$$
$$\ln(k_b) = -0.129 - 0.107 \times 3.401 = -0.129 - 0.364 = -0.493$$
$$k_b = e^{-0.493} = 0.611$$

**Step 4 — Loading factor (bending):**
$$k_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor (room temp, T < 450 °C):**
$$k_d = 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor (95% R):**
$$k_e = 0.868$$

**Step 7 — Miscellaneous factor (default):**
$$k_f = 1.0$$

**Step 8 — Apply the Marin equation:**
$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$
$$S_e = 0.828 \times 0.611 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.868 \times 1.0 \times 300$$
$$S_e = 0.828 \times 0.611 \times 0.868 \times 300$$
$$S_e = 0.439 \times 300$$
$$\boxed{\,S_e \approx 131.7 \text{ MPa}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** The original ultimate strength of 600 MPa now appears as an *effective* fatigue strength of only **~132 MPa** — a **78% reduction** in usable design stress.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Problem — Basquin's Law (Finite Life)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Model Numerical — Practice Problem 2:**
>
> A component has fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma_f' = 1000$ MPa, fatigue strength exponent $b = -0.10$. Calculate the stress amplitude for a life of $N = 10^5$ cycles.

**Step 1 — Write Basquin's equation:**
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N)^b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$\sigma_a = 1000 \times (2 \times 10^5)^{-0.10}$$
$$\sigma_a = 1000 \times (200{,}000)^{-0.10}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**
$$\ln(200{,}000) = 12.206$$
$$-0.10 \times 12.206 = -1.2206$$
$$e^{-1.2206} = 0.295$$

**Step 4 — Final value:**
$$\sigma_a = 1000 \times 0.295 = 295 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_a \approx 295 \text{ MPa for } N = 10^5 \text{ cycles}\,}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow: From Lab Specimen to Design Stress

The Marin equation is best understood as a *signal-attenuation* pipeline. The block diagram below shows how the **ultimate tensile strength (Sᵤₜ)** is progressively conditioned into the **corrected endurance limit (Sₑ)** by successive factors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["Sut Ultimate Tensile Strength"] --> procA["0.5 x Sut Uncorrected Endurance Limit Se prime"]
    procA --> attA["ka Surface Factor"]
    attA --> attB["kb Size Factor"]
    attB --> attC["kc Load Factor"]
    attC --> attD["kd Temperature Factor"]
    attD --> attE["ke Reliability Factor"]
    attE --> attF["kf Miscellaneous Factor"]
    attF --> outA["Se Corrected Endurance Limit Design Stress"]
    inpA -.-> procA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The horizontal arrows represent *multiplication*. The shaded box `0.5 × Sᵤₜ` is the **anchor value** derived from the standard rotating-beam specimen. Every subsequent factor is a **unitless modifier ≤ 1** that *attenuates* the design strength to account for non-ideal real-world conditions.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Block Architecture: The Marin Factor Hierarchy

The diagram below shows the **physical source** of each modification factor, helping students remember *why* each factor exists.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph foundation["ANCHOR VALUE LABORATORY TEST"]
        nodeA1["Rotating Beam Specimen d = 7.62 mm Polished Surface"]
        nodeA2["Se prime = 0.5 x Sut"]
        nodeA1 --> nodeA2
    end

    subgraph modifiers["REAL WORLD DEVIATIONS APPLIED IN SEQUENCE"]
        nodeB1["ka SURFACE Machined Forged Ground"]
        nodeB2["kb SIZE 2.79 to 51 mm bending torsion"]
        nodeB3["kc LOAD Bending Axial Torsion"]
        nodeB4["kd TEMPERATURE Room to 450 C"]
        nodeB5["ke RELIABILITY 50 percent to 99.9 percent"]
        nodeB6["kf MISC Residual stress Corrosion Plating"]
    end

    subgraph final["DESIGN POINT"]
        nodeC1["Se CORRECTED ENDURANCE LIMIT"]
    end

    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6
    nodeB6 --> nodeC1
```

---

## 4.3 Visual S–N Curve Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph region1["STATIC REGION N = 0.5 to 1"]
        n1A["sigma = Sut"]
    end

    subgraph region2["FINITE LIFE REGION 10 cubed to 10 power 6"]
        n2A["Basquin sigma a = sigma f prime x 2N to the b"]
    end

    subgraph region3["INFINITE LIFE REGION N greater than 10 power 6 for steel"]
        n3A["sigma a = Se Endurance Limit horizontal asymptote"]
    end

    region1 --> region2
    region2 --> region3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the S–N Curve:** For ductile steels, the curve **flattens** at $N \approx 10^6$ to $10^7$ cycles. The horizontal asymptote is the **endurance limit**. This is the stress below which the material *theoretically* never fails — the foundation of infinite-life design.

---

## 4.4 Concept Map: How Endurance Limit Fits in Machine Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["FLUCTUATING LOADING"] --> node2["Stress Analysis sigma max sigma min"]
    node2 --> node3["Compute sigma a and sigma m"]
    node3 --> node4A["Determine Se via Marin Equation"]
    node3 --> node4B["Apply Failure Criterion Soderberg Goodman Gerber"]
    node4A --> node5["Factor of Safety n"]
    node4B --> node5
    node5 --> node6["Accept or Redesign"]
```

> **Reading the Map:** Endurance limit $S_e$ is the *input* on the vertical axis of every fatigue failure diagram (Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber). Without $S_e$, no fatigue design is possible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define *endurance limit* of a material. State the empirical relationship between the uncorrected endurance limit and the ultimate tensile strength for ductile steels.

**Model Answer:**
The endurance limit is the maximum completely reversed stress that a material specimen can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles (N = 10⁶ to 10⁷)** without failure. For ductile steels with $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa, the uncorrected endurance limit is empirically:
$$S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$$
For non-ferrous metals, no true endurance limit exists; fatigue strength at $N = 5 \times 10^8$ is used instead.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List the six modifying factors in the Marin equation and state the *physical reason* for each factor.

**Model Answer:**

| Factor | Symbol | Physical Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Surface | $k_a$ | Surface micro-notches act as crack initiators |
| Size | $k_b$ | Larger volume → higher probability of flaws |
| Load | $k_c$ | Different stress states (bending, axial, torsion) |
| Temperature | $k_d$ | Elevated T → reduced strength |
| Reliability | $k_e$ | Statistical scatter in test data |
| Miscellaneous | $k_f$ | Residual stress, corrosion, plating, frequency |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 3, Choice-Based]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **Marin equation** for the corrected endurance limit, explaining the physical basis of *each* modifying factor in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft has $S_{ut} = 800$ MPa, diameter $d = 40$ mm, *hot-rolled* surface finish, is subjected to *bending* with completely reversed loading, operates at 50 °C, and the design requires 90% reliability. Calculate the **corrected endurance limit** $S_e$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The Marin equation refines the lab-measured endurance limit for real engineering use. The lab value is obtained from a polished rotating-beam specimen of standard 7.62 mm diameter:
$$S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \quad (\text{for } S_{ut} \le 1400 \text{ MPa})$$

To convert this to a design-point stress, six multiplicative correction factors are applied:
$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

**Physical Basis of Each Factor:**

- **$k_a$ (Surface Factor):** Engineering surfaces have micro-notches left by machining/grinding/forging. These notches act as stress concentrators and crack initiation sites. A *polished* surface has the highest $k_a \approx 1$; an *as-forged* surface has $k_a$ as low as 0.3. Formula: $k_a = a S_{ut}^b$.

- **$k_b$ (Size Factor):** A larger cross-section has a larger volume of highly stressed material, increasing the probability of containing a critical defect. For bending/torsion: $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (for 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm); for axial loading: $k_b = 1$.

- **$k_c$ (Load Factor):** Different load types produce different stress distributions. Pure bending is the reference ($k_c = 1$). Axial loading stresses the entire cross-section uniformly ($k_c = 0.85$). Torsional shear is less damaging than normal stress ($k_c = 0.59$).

- **$k_d$ (Temperature Factor):** Above 450 °C, creep and metallurgical degradation reduce fatigue strength. For T < 450 °C, $k_d = 1$.

- **$k_e$ (Reliability Factor):** Fatigue data is statistical. To achieve higher design reliability (e.g., 99% vs 50%), a portion of the design margin is consumed. $k_e$ ranges from 1.0 (50% R) to 0.702 (99.99% R).

- **$k_f$ (Miscellaneous Factor):** Captures residual stresses from manufacturing, corrosion environment, electroplating (hydrogen embrittlement), and frequency effects. Default is 1.0; specific cases reduce it.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit:**
$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 800 = 400 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor (hot-rolled, $a = 57.7$, $b = -0.718$):**
$$k_a = 57.7 \times 800^{-0.718}$$
$$\ln(k_a) = \ln(57.7) - 0.718 \times \ln(800) = 4.055 - 0.718 \times 6.685$$
$$\ln(k_a) = 4.055 - 4.799 = -0.744$$
$$k_a = e^{-0.744} = 0.475$$

**Step 3 — Size factor (d = 40 mm, in range 2.79–51 mm):**
$$k_b = 0.879 \times 40^{-0.107}$$
$$\ln(k_b) = \ln(0.879) - 0.107 \times \ln(40) = -0.129 - 0.107 \times 3.689$$
$$\ln(k_b) = -0.129 - 0.395 = -0.524$$
$$k_b = e^{-0.524} = 0.592$$

**Step 4 — Load factor (bending):**
$$k_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor (T = 50 °C < 450 °C):**
$$k_d = 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor (90%):**
$$k_e = 0.897$$

**Step 7 — Miscellaneous:**
$$k_f = 1.0$$

**Step 8 — Apply Marin equation:**
$$S_e = 0.475 \times 0.592 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 400$$
$$S_e = 0.475 \times 0.592 \times 0.897 \times 400$$
$$S_e = 0.252 \times 400$$
$$\boxed{\,S_e \approx 100.9 \text{ MPa}\,}$$

The hot-rolled finish has severely degraded the usable fatigue strength from 400 MPa (lab) to only ~101 MPa at the design point.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **S–N curve** and how the endurance limit is read from it. Differentiate between materials that exhibit an endurance limit and those that do not. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Basquin's equation**, find the stress amplitude for a steel component subjected to a *finite life* of $N = 5 \times 10^4$ cycles, given $\sigma_f' = 900$ MPa and $b = -0.08$. Compare this with the material's endurance limit ($S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ with $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa) and comment on the design implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

The **S–N curve** (Wöhler curve) is a semi-log plot of **stress amplitude (σₐ)** vs. **number of cycles to failure (N)**, used universally in fatigue design.

**Three Regions:**

1. **Static / Low-Cycle Region (N = ½ to 10³):** Plastic deformation dominates. Failure stress approaches the ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut}$. Loading is essentially *static*.

2. **Finite-Life / High-Cycle Region (10³ < N < 10⁶ for steel):** Stress decreases as N increases. The curve follows **Basquin's Law**:
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^b$$
where $\sigma_f'$ is the fatigue strength coefficient and $b$ is the fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$).

3. **Infinite-Life Region (N ≥ 10⁶):** For ductile *ferrous* alloys, the curve becomes **horizontal** at a stress value called the **endurance limit Sₑ**. Below this value, the material theoretically survives infinite cycles.

**Materials WITH Endurance Limit:**
- Ductile steels, especially low/medium-carbon steels.
- Cast and wrought ferrous alloys in their normalised condition.
- $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa typically.

**Materials WITHOUT Endurance Limit:**
- Non-ferrous metals (aluminium, copper, magnesium alloys) → S–N curve keeps descending.
- High-strength steels ($S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa) → curve flattens only after $N = 10^8$.
- Corrosive environments.

For these, **fatigue strength at a fixed N** (e.g., $N = 5 \times 10^8$ for aluminium) is used as the design stress.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Given data:**
$$\sigma_f' = 900 \text{ MPa}, \quad b = -0.08, \quad N = 5 \times 10^4$$

**Step 2 — Basquin's equation:**
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^b = 900 \times (2 \times 5 \times 10^4)^{-0.08}$$
$$\sigma_a = 900 \times (10^5)^{-0.08}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(10^5)^{-0.08}$:**
$$\ln(10^5) = 5 \times \ln(10) = 5 \times 2.3026 = 11.513$$
$$-0.08 \times 11.513 = -0.921$$
$$e^{-0.921} = 0.398$$

**Step 4 — Stress amplitude:**
$$\sigma_a = 900 \times 0.398$$
$$\boxed{\,\sigma_a \approx 358 \text{ MPa}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the endurance limit of the material:**
$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison and design implications:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stress amplitude at N = 50,000 cycles | **358 MPa** |
| Endurance limit (infinite life) | **300 MPa** |

**Comment:** At a finite life of 50,000 cycles, the component can sustain a higher stress (358 MPa) than the infinite-life endurance limit (300 MPa). This means the component will *fail* before reaching $10^6$ cycles, but it is **safe for short-duration service**. Designers often exploit this in aircraft components (finite-life design) where light weight is critical and component replacement is scheduled. However, for **infinite-life design** (e.g., turbine shafts, bridge cables), the designer must keep the stress below 300 MPa, not 358 MPa.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the order of factors** in the Marin equation — examiners want them in the standard sequence: $k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d, k_e, k_f$. Wrong order = partial credit.
> 2. **Using the wrong size-factor formula** for shafts > 51 mm. The formula changes to $k_b = 0.91 d^{-0.157}$.
> 3. **Mixing up the loading factor $k_c$**: Bending = 1, Axial = 0.85, Torsion = 0.59. Many students incorrectly use 0.85 for torsion.
> 4. **Applying $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$** blindly to aluminium or high-strength steel. For $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa, use $S_e' = 700$ MPa.
> 5. **Forgetting the kₑ correction** for non-default reliability (often defaults to 50% in textbook examples).
> 6. **Unit errors**: Shigley's surface factor constants ($a$, $b$) require $S_{ut}$ in **MPa**, not ksi.
> 7. **Confusing stress amplitude and mean stress**: $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$; $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Endurance Limit Stress (Module 3):**

- ✅ The **endurance limit Sₑ** is the asymptotic stress on the S–N curve, valid for *infinite* cyclic life.
- ✅ **Uncorrected value** from standard rotating-beam specimen: $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa; $S_e' = 700$ MPa otherwise.
- ✅ **Marin equation**: $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ — *the* master formula.
- ✅ **$k_a$ (Surface)** = $a S_{ut}^b$ → polished (1) > machined > hot-rolled > as-forged.
- ✅ **$k_b$ (Size)** = $0.879 d^{-0.107}$ for bending/torsion (2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm); = 1 for axial; = $0.91 d^{-0.157}$ for d > 51 mm.
- ✅ **$k_c$ (Load)**: 1.0 (bending), 0.85 (axial), 0.59 (torsion). Mnemonic: **"BAT" → 1, 0.85, 0.59**.
- ✅ **$k_d$ (Temp)** = 1 for T < 450 °C; drops rapidly above.
- ✅ **$k_e$ (Reliability)**: 1.000 (50%), 0.897 (90%), 0.868 (95%), 0.814 (99%), 0.753 (99.9%).
- ✅ **Basquin's Law** (finite life): $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^b$.
- ✅ **Stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$: $R = -1$ (fully reversed); $R = 0$ (pulsating); $R = +1$ (static).
- ✅ Endurance limit is the *vertical-axis input* for **Soderberg, Goodman, and Gerber** failure criteria.
- ✅ Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Mg) have **no true endurance limit** — use fatigue strength at $N = 5 \times 10^8$.
- ✅ Always state units (MPa) and final corrected $S_e$ value to 1 decimal place in board answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Endurance Limit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Endurance Limit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU Module 3 (Design for Fluctuating / Cyclic Loads), the **Endurance Limit** ($S_e$) is defined as the maximum completely reversed stress ($\sigma_{-1}$ or $\sigma_r$) that a material specimen can withstand for an infinite number of stress cycles (conventionally $N \ge 10^6$ cycles) without failure. For steels, this property is sharply defined on the S-N (Stress-Number) diagram, whereas for non-ferrous metals (e.g., aluminium, copper alloys) the endurance limit is not sharply defined, and the **Fatigue Strength** at a specified number of cycles is used instead.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The endurance limit reported in standard material property tables (e.g., $\sigma_{-1} = 0.5\,\sigma_{ut}$ for steels) is obtained from a *standard laboratory specimen*: a polished, mirror-finished, chrome-nickel steel rotating beam specimen of diameter $d = 7.62\,\text{mm}$ ($0.3\,\text{in}$), tested at room temperature, under completely reversed bending stress, with a reliability of **50%**. Real machine components deviate from these ideal conditions, and therefore, *modifying factors* must be applied.

The **modified endurance limit** is expressed as:

$$S_e = K_a \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot K_d \cdot K_e \cdot K_f \cdot S_e^{\prime}$$

where $S_e^{\prime}$ is the **uncorrected (prime) endurance limit** of the standard specimen, and the $K$-factors are dimensionless *modifying factors* that account for the deviation of the actual component from the standard specimen.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **perfectly polished, laboratory-grade steel wire** (the standard specimen). The lab tells you it can be bent back and forth a billion times before snapping. Now take that same wire and:
- **Roughen its surface** with sandpaper → it will fail sooner.
- **Make it thicker** (larger diameter) → the surface-to-volume ratio changes, and it fails sooner.
- **Bend it while it's hot** (elevated temperature) → it weakens.
- **Drill a hole in the middle** → a tiny crack-initiator, and it fails much sooner.

Each of these "real-world" deviations acts as a *multiplier* on the lab value. The product of all these multipliers tells you what your *actual* component can really take. Think of $S_e^{\prime}$ as the **"ideal strength"** written on the box, and the $K$-factors as the **"real-world deductions"** taken off by harsh operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common student error is to take $S_e = 0.5\,\sigma_{ut}$ directly into design. **NEVER do this** in KTU board exams — the *modifying factors* are mandatory unless the question explicitly states "standard specimen conditions."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard specimen diameter:** $d = 7.62\,\text{mm}$ ($0.3\,\text{in}$)
- **Standard surface finish:** Mirror polish (Ra $\approx 0.4\,\mu\text{m}$)
- **Standard temperature:** **27 °C** (room temperature)
- **Standard reliability:** **50%** (median life)
- **Standard loading:** Completely reversed bending, $R = -1$
- **Standard test:** R.R. Moore rotating-beam machine
- **Baseline relationship (steels):** $S_e^{\prime} = 0.5\,\sigma_{ut}$ (valid for $\sigma_{ut} \le 1400\,\text{MPa}$)
- **Baseline relationship (cast/wrought iron):** $S_e^{\prime} = 0.4\,\sigma_{ut}$
- **Baseline relationship (aluminium alloys):** $S_e^{\prime} = 0.3\,\sigma_{ut}$ (at $5 \times 10^8$ cycles)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Modifying Factors

The endurance limit of a real machine component is governed by the following six factors, each multiplied to the uncorrected endurance limit $S_e^{\prime}$.

### 2.1.1 Surface Roughness Factor ($K_a$)

The **fatigue crack almost always initiates at the surface**. A rough surface contains microscopic notches that act as stress raisers.

- **Why?** Polished surfaces (low $R_a$) have a high $K_a$ (close to 1.0); rough surfaces (forged, as-cast) have low $K_a$ (0.3–0.6).
- **How?** The empirical relation (Shigley / V.B. Bhandari) for steels is:

$$K_a = a \cdot \sigma_{ut}^{\,b}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are tabulated constants that depend on the surface finish (Ground, Machined, Cold-drawn, Hot-rolled, As-forged).

| Surface Finish | $a$ (MPa) | $a$ (ksi) | $b$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ground / Polished | 1.58 | 2.70 | $-0.085$ |
| Machined or Cold-drawn | 4.51 | 6.39 | $-0.265$ |
| Hot-rolled | 57.7 | 272.0 | $-0.718$ |
| As-forged | 272.0 | 571.0 | $-0.995$ |

### 2.1.2 Size Factor ($K_b$)

Larger components fail earlier than small specimens because:
1. The **probability of finding a critical defect** increases with volume (statistical size effect).
2. The **stress gradient** in bending/torsion is less steep in larger sections, so a thicker "skin" of high-stress material exists.

- For **round, solid, rotating-bending** shafts:

$$K_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107} \quad \text{for } 2.79 \le d \le 51\,\text{mm}$$

$$K_b = 0.91 \, d^{-0.157} \quad \text{for } 51 < d \le 254\,\text{mm}$$

- For **non-rotating (axial) loading**: $K_b = 1.0$ (entire cross-section is uniformly stressed).
- For **rectangular sections in bending**: $K_b = (d/7.62)^{-0.107}$ where $d$ is the equivalent diameter, $d = 0.808\sqrt{h \cdot b} \cdot (h/b)^{0.5}$ (Shigley).

### 2.1.3 Load Factor ($K_c$)

Accounts for the type of cyclic loading:

| Loading Type | $K_c$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| Pure bending (reversed) | 1.0 |
| Pure axial (tension-compression, reversed) | 0.85 |
| Pure torsion (reversed) | 0.59 (or use $0.577$) |

- **Why axial load gives 0.85?** Because the entire cross-section is stressed uniformly to the maximum (no stress gradient), so the volume of "highly stressed material" is greater.
- **Why torsion gives 0.59?** Shear yielding occurs at $\tau_y = 0.577\,\sigma_y$; combined with orientation, the value drops.

### 2.1.4 Temperature Factor ($K_d$)

- For $T \le 450\,^\circ\text{C}$: $K_d = 1.0$
- For $T > 450\,^\circ\text{C}$: $K_d$ falls below 1.0; for $T \ge 550\,^\circ\text{C}$, creep becomes dominant and fatigue design alone is insufficient.

### 2.1.5 Reliability Factor ($K_e$)

Statistical life-distribution. Higher reliability demands lower $S_e$:

| Reliability $R$ | $K_e$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.50 | 1.000 |
| 0.90 | 0.897 |
| 0.95 | 0.868 |
| 0.99 | 0.814 |
| 0.999 | 0.753 |
| 0.9999 | 0.702 |

### 2.1.6 Miscellaneous-Effects Factor ($K_f$)

This is a *catch-all* for residual stresses, corrosion, electrolytic plating, fretting, and similar effects — typically $K_f = 1.0$ in textbook problems unless stated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Some KTU textbooks (V.B. Bhandari) treat surface treatment, corrosion, and reliability as a *single* factor $K_f \in [0.5,\, 1.0]$ in the numerator, omitting the separate $K_e$. Always cross-check with the specific textbook prescribed in your syllabus. **Shigley** uses the 6-factor decomposition (including $K_f$ as 1.0); **Bhandari** uses a 4-factor form.

## 2.2 Stress Concentration, Notch Sensitivity & Fatigue Stress Concentration

When a component has a **geometric discontinuity** (hole, keyway, fillet, shoulder, gear tooth), the theoretical (elastic) stress concentration factor is:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nom}}}$$

- $K_t \ge 1$ always; from charts (Peterson, Pilkey).
- However, the **fatigue notch sensitivity** $q$ of the material reduces the effective concentration:

$$\text{Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor: } \quad K_f = 1 + q\,(K_t - 1)$$

where $q \in [0,\, 1]$ is the **notch sensitivity** (Neuber's constant).

- $q = 0$ → ideal plastic (no notch effect in fatigue), $K_f = 1$.
- $q = 1$ → ideal brittle (notch is fully effective), $K_f = K_t$.

Empirical correlations for $q$ (for steels):

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

where $r$ is the notch radius, and $a$ is a material constant (Neuber's constant):

| Material | $\sigma_{ut}$ (MPa) | $a$ (mm) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Mild Steel | 550 | 0.050 |
| Medium Steel | 650 | 0.0625 |
| Hard Steel | 750 | 0.075 |

**Effect of $K_f$ in the endurance limit:**
$$S_e = \frac{S_e^{\prime \prime}}{K_f}$$

where $S_e^{\prime \prime}$ is the endurance limit *after* applying the $K_a, K_b, K_c, K_d, K_e$ factors but *before* stress concentration.

## 2.3 Mean Stress Correction (Beyond $S_e$)

The factor $K_f$ modifies the *alternating* stress amplitude. To account for **non-zero mean stress** ($\sigma_m \ne 0$), the design is checked against one of the following failure loci:

| Criterion | Equation (in $\sigma_a$-$\sigma_m$ plane) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Soderberg | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ |
| Goodman | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ |
| Gerber | $\dfrac{n\,\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{n\,\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$ |
| ASME-Elliptic | $\left(\dfrac{n\,\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{n\,\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^{2} = 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, **Soderberg** is the most common (most conservative, used for ductile materials with yielding). **Goodman** is the second-most-common. **Gerber** is parabolic and is the most accurate for ductile metals but rarely used in exam problems due to quadratic complexity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Symbol | Name | Equation / Value | Range / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_e$ | Modified (actual) endurance limit | $K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e K_f \, S_e^{\prime}$ | Final value to use in design |
| 2 | $S_e^{\prime}$ | Prime endurance limit (steels) | $0.5\,\sigma_{ut}$ | For $\sigma_{ut} \le 1400\,\text{MPa}$ |
| 3 | $K_a$ | Surface factor | $a\,\sigma_{ut}^{\,b}$ | From table, $\sigma_{ut}$ in MPa |
| 4 | $K_b$ | Size factor (round, rotating) | $0.879\,d^{-0.107}$ | $2.79 \le d \le 51\,\text{mm}$ |
| 5 | $K_c$ | Load factor (axial / torsion) | $0.85$ / $0.59$ | Bending = 1.0 |
| 6 | $K_d$ | Temperature factor | $1.0$ for $T \le 450\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Else use charts |
| 7 | $K_e$ | Reliability factor | $0.814$ for $R=0.99$ | Standard $0.50 \to 1.000$ |
| 8 | $K_f$ | Fatigue stress concentration | $1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | $q$ = notch sensitivity |
| 9 | $q$ | Notch sensitivity | $1 / (1 + \sqrt{a/r})$ | $a$ from material table |
| 10 | $K_t$ | Theoretical stress concentration | From Peterson's chart | Depends on geometry |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive:** The connecting rod, crankshaft, and valve spring all fail by fatigue. Surface finish (grinding vs. rough turning), size (diameter), and stress raisers (fillet radius, oil-hole) dominate.
- **Aerospace:** Aluminium alloys have no true endurance limit; design uses fatigue strength at $N = 10^7$ to $5 \times 10^8$ cycles.
- **Power plants:** Turbine blades operate at 500–700 °C → temperature factor $K_d < 1$ is critical; creep-fatigue interaction dominates.
- **Civil (bridges, rails):** Corrosion fatigue → $K_f < 1$ for marine / humid atmospheres.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Notch-Sensitivity Relation $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$

**Starting premise (Neuber's theory):** The local stress at the root of a notch cannot exceed a *material-dependent* "limit stress" $S_{\rho}$ that depends on the notch tip radius $\rho$ (= $r$ here). The effective fatigue strength at the notch is:

$$\sigma_{\max}^{\text{notch}} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\text{nom}} = K_f \cdot \sigma_{\text{lim}}$$

Neuber experimentally correlated the "limit stress" as:

$$S_{\rho} = \frac{S_e^{\text{smooth}}}{1 + \sqrt{a/\rho}}$$

Equating the two expressions and solving for $K_f$:

$$\frac{\sigma_{\text{nom}} \cdot K_t}{K_f} = \frac{S_e^{\text{smooth}}}{1 + \sqrt{a/\rho}}$$

$$K_f = 1 + q\,(K_t - 1) \quad \text{where} \quad q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}} \;\;\blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Surface + Size + Load Factors (KTU-Standard)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2023 adapted]:**
A shaft of diameter $d = 40\,\text{mm}$, machined from a steel with $\sigma_{ut} = 650\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_y = 500\,\text{MPa}$, is subjected to a reversed bending load with a mean stress $\sigma_m = 60\,\text{MPa}$ and alternating stress $\sigma_a = 80\,\text{MPa}$. Surface = machined. Reliability = 0.90. Apply a factor of safety $n = 2$ and check using the **Soderberg criterion**.

**Step 1 — Compute $S_e^{\prime}$:**

Since $\sigma_{ut} = 650 \le 1400\,\text{MPa}$,

$$S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 \times 650 = 325\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_a$ (machined finish, $\sigma_{ut} = 650\,\text{MPa}$):**

From the surface-finish table (Machined row): $a = 4.51,\; b = -0.265$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_a &= a \cdot \sigma_{ut}^{\,b} \\
&= 4.51 \cdot (650)^{-0.265}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing the exponent term: $(650)^{-0.265} = e^{-0.265 \ln 650} = e^{-0.265 \times 6.477} = e^{-1.7164} = 0.1798$

$$
K_a = 4.51 \times 0.1798 = 0.811
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_b$ (round, $d = 40\,\text{mm}$):**

Since $2.79 \le 40 \le 51\,\text{mm}$:

$$
K_b = 0.879 \cdot (40)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.7179 = 0.631
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_c$ (bending):**

$$K_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Compute $K_d, K_e, K_f$:**

$K_d = 1.0$ (room temperature); $K_e = 0.897$ (reliability 0.90); assume $K_f = 1.0$ (no stress concentration mentioned).

**Step 6 — Modified endurance limit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_e &= K_a \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot K_d \cdot K_e \cdot K_f \cdot S_e^{\prime} \\
&= 0.811 \times 0.631 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 325 \\
&= 0.811 \times 0.631 \times 0.897 \times 325
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing product of factors: $0.811 \times 0.631 = 0.5116$; then $0.5116 \times 0.897 = 0.4589$

$$
S_e = 0.4589 \times 325 = 149.1\,\text{MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Many students compute $K_b$ using the *wrong* diameter range. If $d > 51\,\text{mm}$, the formula changes to $K_b = 0.91\,d^{-0.157}$. Always check the range first.

**Step 7 — Soderberg check:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

LHS: $\dfrac{80}{149.1} + \dfrac{60}{500} = 0.5365 + 0.1200 = 0.6565$

RHS: $\dfrac{1}{2} = 0.500$

Since $0.6565 > 0.500$, **the design is NOT safe.** Need to either reduce $\sigma_a, \sigma_m$, or increase the diameter.

**Step 8 — Iterative re-design (optional):** Try $d = 50\,\text{mm}$:

$K_b(50) = 0.879 \times (50)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.7049 = 0.6196$

$S_e = 0.811 \times 0.6196 \times 0.897 \times 325 = 147.1\,\text{MPa}$

Soderberg LHS: $80/147.1 + 60/500 = 0.5439 + 0.12 = 0.6639$ — *still fails*. Try $d = 60\,\text{mm}$ (now use the other formula range since $d > 51$):

$K_b(60) = 0.91 \times (60)^{-0.157} = 0.91 \times 0.6247 = 0.5685$

$S_e = 0.811 \times 0.5685 \times 0.897 \times 325 = 134.9\,\text{MPa}$ — *worsens* since larger diameter means lower $K_b$.

> [!NOTE]
> This counterintuitive result (bigger diameter → lower $S_e$) is a classic **KTU "twist"** in problem statements. The student must recognize that increasing diameter reduces endurance, so the only way to fix the design is to **change the material**, **reduce loads**, or **use a better surface finish**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Stress Concentration with Notch Sensitivity

**Problem:** A steel shaft has a shoulder fillet with theoretical stress concentration $K_t = 1.8$ and fillet radius $r = 4\,\text{mm}$. Material has $\sigma_{ut} = 750\,\text{MPa}$ (hard steel). Compute $q$ and $K_f$.

**Step 1 — Neuber's constant for hard steel:**

From the table: $a = 0.075\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 2 — Notch sensitivity:**

$$
q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.075/4}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.01875}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1369} = \frac{1}{1.1369}
$$

$$
q = 0.8796 \approx 0.88
$$

**Step 3 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$
K_f = 1 + q\,(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.8796 \times (1.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.8796 \times 0.8 = 1 + 0.7037
$$

$$
K_f = 1.704
$$

**Step 4 — Effect on endurance limit:**

If $S_e^{\prime \prime} = 250\,\text{MPa}$ (after $K_a, K_b, K_c$ factors), then:

$$S_e = \frac{S_e^{\prime \prime}}{K_f} = \frac{250}{1.704} = 146.7\,\text{MPa}$$

A $70\%$ loss of strength — significant.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Each Factor

| Factor | Required if... | Skip if... | Typical value range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $K_a$ | Always (component ≠ standard polish) | Problem says "polished" | 0.30 – 1.00 |
| $K_b$ | $d \ne 7.62\,\text{mm}$ (or non-standard size) | Axial loading | 0.55 – 1.00 |
| $K_c$ | Loading is NOT reversed bending | Pure bending only | 0.59 (torsion) – 1.0 |
| $K_d$ | $T > 450\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Room temperature | 0.50 – 1.00 |
| $K_e$ | $R > 0.50$ (e.g., aerospace, medical) | Standard 50% reliability | 0.70 – 1.00 |
| $K_f$ | Geometric discontinuity (hole, key, fillet) | Smooth shaft | 1.00 – 3.00 |
| $K_t$ | Always used *inside* $K_f$ if discontinuity exists | Smooth part | 1.00 – 3.00+ |
| $q$ | Always used *inside* $K_f$ if discontinuity exists | Smooth part | 0.00 – 1.00 |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Order of Application of Modifying Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Uncorrected Endurance Limit Se prime = 0.5 Sut"] --> B["Apply Ka: Surface Roughness Factor"]
    B --> C["Apply Kb: Size Factor"]
    C --> D["Apply Kc: Load Type Factor"]
    D --> E["Apply Kd: Temperature Factor"]
    E --> F["Apply Ke: Reliability Factor"]
    F --> G["Se double prime = Product of all K factors x Se prime"]
    G --> H{"Has Geometric Discontinuity?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Compute Kt from Peterson Charts"]
    I --> J["Compute q = 1 over 1 plus sqrt of a over r"]
    J --> K["Compute Kf = 1 plus q times Kt minus 1"]
    K --> L["Se = Se double prime divided by Kf"]
    H -- No --> M["Se = Se double prime"]
    L --> N["Final Modified Endurance Limit Se"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Apply Mean Stress Criterion Goodman Soderberg Gerber"]
    O --> P["Compute Design Factor of Safety n"]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Decision Tree for Factor Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Given: Sut, Sy, Sut, d, surface, load, T, R"] --> B["Se prime = 0.5 Sut"]
    B --> C{"Surface finish?"}
    C -- "Ground" --> D1["Ka = 1.58 Sut power minus 0.085"]
    C -- "Machined" --> D2["Ka = 4.51 Sut power minus 0.265"]
    C -- "Hot rolled" --> D3["Ka = 57.7 Sut power minus 0.718"]
    C -- "As forged" --> D4["Ka = 272 Sut power minus 0.995"]
    D1 --> E["Kb from diameter"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{"Load type?"}
    F -- "Bending" --> G1["Kc = 1.0"]
    F -- "Axial" --> G2["Kc = 0.85"]
    F -- "Torsion" --> G3["Kc = 0.59"]
    G1 --> H["Ke from reliability table"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I["Se = Ka Kb Kc Kd Ke Kf Se prime"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Stress Concentration at a Shoulder Fillet (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Large diameter D"] --> B1["Fillet radius r"]
    B1 --> C1["Small diameter d"]
    A1 --> A2["Region 1: nominal stress sigma nom 1"]
    C1 --> A3["Region 2: nominal stress sigma nom 2"]
    B1 --> A4["Notch root: theoretical peak stress sigma max = Kt sigma nom"]
    A4 --> A5["Effective fatigue stress = Kf sigma nom where Kf = 1 + q Kt minus 1"]
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map: S-N Curve and Effect of Each Factor

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["S-N Diagram log log"] --> B["Endurance Limit Se"]
    B --> C["Rough surface lowers curve vertically"]
    B --> D["Larger size lowers curve vertically"]
    B --> E["Higher mean stress shifts design point"]
    B --> F["Stress concentration reduces allowable stress amplitude"]
    C --> G["Lower safe operating region"]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define endurance limit. Why is the endurance limit of an actual machine component different from that of a standard specimen?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **endurance limit** ($S_e$) is the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an infinite number of cycles (typically $N \ge 10^6$) without fatigue failure.
>
> The endurance limit of an actual component differs from the standard specimen value because real components deviate from the *standard test conditions* in several respects: surface finish, size, type of loading, operating temperature, reliability level, stress concentration, and the presence of residual stresses. These deviations are accounted for by introducing **modifying factors** ($K_a, K_b, K_c, K_d, K_e, K_f$) such that:
>
> $$S_e = K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e K_f \, S_e^{\prime}$$
>
> where $S_e^{\prime} = 0.5\,\sigma_{ut}$ is the standard specimen value. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**What is notch sensitivity? How does it affect the fatigue strength of a component with a stress concentration?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Notch sensitivity** ($q$) is a material property that measures how sensitive a material is to the presence of a notch or stress concentration under cyclic loading. It is defined by Neuber's equation:
>
> $$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$
>
> where $a$ is Neuber's constant (material property) and $r$ is the notch radius. The value of $q$ lies between 0 and 1. **A high value of $q$ (close to 1)** means the material is highly sensitive to notches and the theoretical stress concentration is fully effective, while a **low value of $q$ (close to 0)** means the material is insensitive (ductile) and the notch is less effective in fatigue.
>
> The effective fatigue stress concentration factor is:
>
> $$K_f = 1 + q\,(K_t - 1)$$
>
> where $K_t$ is the theoretical (geometric) stress concentration factor. This $K_f$ is then used to reduce the modified endurance limit as $S_e = S_e^{\prime \prime}/K_f$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 adapted]

A shaft of diameter $d = 50\,\text{mm}$ is made of cold-drawn steel with $\sigma_{ut} = 700\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_y = 540\,\text{MPa}$. It is subjected to a fluctuating bending moment producing alternating stress $\sigma_a = 100\,\text{MPa}$ and mean stress $\sigma_m = 70\,\text{MPa}$. The surface is machined. A shoulder fillet has a theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 2.0$ with fillet radius $r = 5\,\text{mm}$. Required reliability = 0.99. Use a factor of safety $n = 2.5$.

#### (a) Compute the modified endurance limit of the shaft. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 \times 700 = 350\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor $K_a$ (machined, $\sigma_{ut} = 700\,\text{MPa}$):**

$$K_a = 4.51 \times (700)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1741 = 0.7852$$

**Step 3 — Size factor $K_b$ ($d = 50\,\text{mm}$, in range 2.79–51):**

$$K_b = 0.879 \times (50)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.7049 = 0.6196$$

**Step 4 — Load factor $K_c$ (bending):**

$$K_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor $K_d$:** Room temperature, $K_d = 1.0$.

**Step 6 — Reliability factor $K_e$ (R = 0.99):**

$$K_e = 0.814$$

**Step 7 — Endurance limit before stress concentration:**

$$
S_e^{\prime \prime} = K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e \times S_e^{\prime} = 0.7852 \times 0.6196 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 350
$$

Computing: $0.7852 \times 0.6196 = 0.4865$; $\times 0.814 = 0.3960$; $\times 350 = 138.6\,\text{MPa}$.

**Step 8 — Notch sensitivity (hard steel, $a = 0.075$):**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.075/5}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.015}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1225} = \frac{1}{1.1225} = 0.8909$$

**Step 9 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$K_f = 1 + 0.8909 \times (2.0 - 1) = 1 + 0.8909 = 1.8909$$

**Step 10 — Modified endurance limit:**

$$S_e = \frac{S_e^{\prime \prime}}{K_f} = \frac{138.6}{1.8909} = 73.30\,\text{MPa}$$

**Answer: $S_e = 73.3\,\text{MPa}$** ✅

#### (b) Check the design using the Goodman criterion. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Goodman equation with factor of safety:**

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 2 — LHS computation:**

$$\frac{100}{73.30} + \frac{70}{700} = 1.3642 + 0.10 = 1.4642$$

**Step 3 — RHS:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.4$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

Since $1.4642 \gg 0.4$, the design is **unsafe**. The actual factor of safety can be computed by inverting:

$$n_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1}{\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{1.4642} = 0.683$$

The actual safety factor (0.683) is far below the required 2.5. **The shaft will fail in fatigue.**

**Design recommendation:** Either reduce the alternating stress (smaller loads), increase the diameter, choose a higher-strength steel, or improve surface finish (grinding instead of machining).

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 adapted]

A transmission shaft of diameter $d = 60\,\text{mm}$ is made of medium carbon steel with $\sigma_{ut} = 600\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_y = 380\,\text{MPa}$. It is subjected to a completely reversed torque producing $\tau_a = 80\,\text{MPa}$ (mean torque is zero). The surface is hot-rolled. Reliability required is 0.90. There is a transverse hole with theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 2.2$. Factor of safety $n = 2$. Apply the **Soderberg criterion** for shear.

#### (a) Determine the modified endurance limit in shear. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit in bending (standard specimen):**

$$S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 \times 600 = 300\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor (hot-rolled, $\sigma_{ut} = 600\,\text{MPa}$):**

$$K_a = 57.7 \times (600)^{-0.718} = 57.7 \times 0.001183 = 0.0683$$

> [!NOTE]
> Hot-rolled finish drastically reduces $K_a$ because of severe surface roughness (mill scale, decarburization). This is why shafts are *ground* in critical applications.

**Step 3 — Size factor (round, $d = 60\,\text{mm}$ — use second formula since $d > 51$):**

$$K_b = 0.91 \times (60)^{-0.157} = 0.91 \times 0.6247 = 0.5685$$

**Step 4 — Load factor for torsion:**

$$K_c = 0.59$$

**Step 5 — Temperature $K_d = 1.0$; Reliability $K_e = 0.897$ (R = 0.90).**

**Step 6 — Endurance limit in bending before stress concentration:**

$$S_e^{\prime \prime} = 0.0683 \times 0.5685 \times 0.59 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 300$$

Computing: $0.0683 \times 0.5685 = 0.03883$; $\times 0.59 = 0.02291$; $\times 0.897 = 0.02055$; $\times 300 = 6.165\,\text{MPa}$.

This is an *extremely low* value — illustrating why hot-rolled surfaces are unsuitable for fatigue-critical shafts.

**Step 7 — Notch sensitivity (medium steel, $a = 0.0625$):**

For transverse hole, the effective $r$ is taken as some characteristic value. Assuming $r = 2\,\text{mm}$ (typical for small holes):

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.0625/2}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.03125}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1768} = \frac{1}{1.1768} = 0.8498$$

**Step 8 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$K_{f\tau} = 1 + 0.8498 \times (2.2 - 1) = 1 + 0.8498 \times 1.2 = 1 + 1.0198 = 2.0198$$

**Step 9 — Endurance limit in bending after $K_f$:**

$$S_e = \frac{6.165}{2.0198} = 3.053\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 10 — Convert to endurance limit in shear (using distortion-energy / von Mises):**

$$S_{e,\tau} = 0.577 \times S_e = 0.577 \times 3.053 = 1.762\,\text{MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This absurdly low value is *physically* telling: the combination of hot-rolled finish + size 60 mm + torsion is unrealistic. In a real KTU exam, the expected answer is computed with the given numbers, but the student should comment on the need for *better surface finish* (e.g., grinding) and *use of Soderberg/ASME* with a less aggressive criterion.

**Answer: $S_{e,\tau} \approx 1.76\,\text{MPa}$** (numerically; this signals the design is impractical with the given parameters).

#### (b) Apply the Soderberg criterion in shear and comment on the design. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Soderberg in shear:**

$$\frac{\tau_a}{S_{e,\tau}} + \frac{\tau_m}{S_{sy}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Since the torque is *completely reversed*, $\tau_m = 0$.

$$\frac{\tau_a}{S_{e,\tau}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

$$\frac{80}{1.762} = 45.4 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

Since $45.4 \gg 0.5$, the design is **grossly unsafe**. The actual safety factor is:

$$n_{\text{actual}} = \frac{S_{e,\tau}}{\tau_a} = \frac{1.762}{80} = 0.022$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

The hot-rolled surface is the dominant cause of failure. A typical KTU follow-up question would be: *"What surface finish would make the design safe with all other factors unchanged?"*

Required $S_{e,\tau}$ for safety: $S_{e,\tau} \ge n \times \tau_a = 2 \times 80 = 160\,\text{MPa}$. Following the *converse* calculation, one would need $K_a \approx 0.99$ (grinding) and $K_{f\tau} \approx 1.0$ (no transverse hole — relocate the hole), which is essentially unattainable. The practical solution is to **eliminate the transverse hole** (use a splined connection or pressed-fit) and to **specify a ground finish**.

**Answer:** Design is unsafe; $n_{\text{actual}} = 0.022$. Recommended actions: change surface finish to ground, eliminate the transverse hole, and use a higher-strength alloy steel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $K_e$ when reliability is specified** costs 2 marks. KTU explicitly lists reliability 0.90/0.99 in past papers.
> 2. **Using $K_b = 1.0$ for non-axial loads** is a common error. Always check the diameter and apply the correct formula range.
> 3. **Mixing bending endurance limit with torsion endurance limit** without the 0.577 conversion. The shear form $S_{e,\tau} = 0.577\,S_e$ is *mandatory* in torsion problems.
> 4. **Wrong Kc for torsion**: 0.59 is the correct value, not 0.577 (which is the shear-yield conversion). Students confuse these.
> 5. **In Step-by-step marks:** KTU awards marks for *intermediate numerical values* shown explicitly. A single final answer with no workings gets 0–1 out of 7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Master formula (must memorize):** $S_e = K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e K_f \, S_e^{\prime}$, where $S_e^{\prime} = 0.5\,\sigma_{ut}$ for steels ($\sigma_{ut} \le 1400\,\text{MPa}$).
- ✅ **Order of magnitude check:** After all factors, $S_e$ for a typical machined steel shaft in bending is roughly **30%–60%** of $\sigma_{ut}$.
- ✅ **Surface factor $K_a$** is the *most* important factor for "everyday" components (roughness matters most).
- ✅ **Size factor $K_b$** is *not* 1.0 for diameters above ~2.79 mm. Bigger is *worse* (statistical + gradient effect).
- ✅ **Torsion** → $K_c = 0.59$; **Axial** → $K_c = 0.85$; **Bending** → $K_c = 1.0$.
- ✅ **Notch sensitivity $q$** depends on the material; use Neuber's $a$-constant and the notch radius $r$: $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$.
- ✅ **Fatigue stress concentration:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$. Always ≤ $K_t$.
- ✅ **For Soderberg in shear**, the form is $\tau_a / S_{e\tau} + \tau_m / S_{sy} = 1/n$.
- ✅ **Reliability factors:** 50% → 1.000, 90% → 0.897, 99% → 0.814, 99.9% → 0.753.
- ✅ **The $K$-factor product is always ≤ 1.0** (none of the factors exceeds 1.0 in normal use). Therefore $S_e \le S_e^{\prime}$ always.
- ✅ **Mean stress criteria (in order of decreasing conservativeness):** Soderberg > ASME-Elliptic > Goodman > Gerber.
- ✅ **Always state units** in final answer; KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
