<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Factor of Safety, Gears - Classification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Factor of Safety (FoS) — The Foundational Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> The **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, also called the *Safety Factor* or *Factor of Ignorance*, is defined as the **ratio of the maximum stress that a material can withstand (failure stress) to the actual working stress (design stress or applied stress)** expected under service conditions. It is a dimensionless quantity.

Mathematically, the generalized form is:

$$
n = \frac{\text{Failure Stress } (\sigma_{\text{fail}})}{\text{Working / Design Stress } (\sigma_{\text{work}})}
$$

Where the failure stress may be the **yield stress ($\sigma_y$)** for ductile materials, the **ultimate stress ($\sigma_u$)** for brittle materials, or the **endurance limit ($\sigma_e$)** for fatigue-dominated loading.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Safety Margin in Everyday Life"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:**
> Imagine a **rope** advertised to hold a maximum load of **100 kg**. If you intend to use it to carry only **25 kg**, then you are using a **Factor of Safety of 4**. You have built in a safety cushion of 4 times the expected load. The rope can take 4 times more than what you are actually putting on it. If the rope deteriorates, gets wet, or has an unnoticed flaw, the extra capacity keeps the system safe.

**Engineering Mapping:**
- **Rope capacity (100 kg)** → Material strength ($\sigma_{\text{fail}}$)
- **Actual load (25 kg)** → Working stress ($\sigma_{\text{work}}$)
- **Safety cushion** → Factor of Safety ($n$)

A similar philosophy applies to machine elements. We design them to carry far more than the expected load because we cannot perfectly predict the magnitude, type, or future operating conditions of the load.

---

### 1.3 Gears — Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> A **Gear** is a rotating machine element having *cut teeth* (or cogs) which, by meshing with another toothed part, transmit torque and motion from one shaft to another (or to a linear actuator). Gears are one of the most efficient and reliable means of power transmission in mechanical engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Gears?**
> Gears provide:
> 1. **Positive drive** — no slip (unlike belts or chains).
> 2. **Constant velocity ratio** — predictable motion.
> 3. **High torque capacity** in compact envelopes.
> 4. **Long service life** under controlled lubrication.

### 1.4 Why Classification Matters

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> Students often confuse gear **types** with gear **classifications**. A *type* refers to a specific gear (e.g., spur gear), while a *classification* is the systematic grouping by **axes arrangement, peripheral shape, tooth profile, or application**. Board questions explicitly ask: *"Classify gears with neat sketches."* You must show the sketches and explain the basis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factor of Safety — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Types of Factor of Safety

The KTU syllabus recognizes **three principal types** based on the failure criterion adopted:

#### (a) Factor of Safety with Respect to Yielding (for Ductile Materials under Static Load)

$$
n_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{work}}}
$$

This is used when the material deforms plastically beyond the design limit (e.g., mild steel under static tension).

#### (b) Factor of Safety with Respect to Ultimate Stress (for Brittle Materials)

$$
n_u = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_{\text{work}}}
$$

Brittle materials (e.g., cast iron, ceramics) fail without significant yielding, so the ultimate tensile strength is the governing failure criterion.

#### (c) Factor of Safety with Respect to Endurance Limit (for Fatigue Loading)

$$
n_e = \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_{\text{work}}}
$$

Used for components subjected to cyclic/fluctuating loads (e.g., gear teeth, shafts, springs).

---

### 2.1.2 Factors Influencing the Selection of Factor of Safety

The selection is **never arbitrary**. The KTU board expects you to list and justify the following:

- **Type of loading** (static, dynamic, fluctuating, impact).
- **Material properties** (ductility, toughness, homogeneity).
- **Reliability requirements** of the application (aerospace > toys).
- **Manufacturing quality** (precision, surface finish, residual stresses).
- **Operating environment** (temperature, corrosion, humidity).
- **Consequences of failure** (life-threatening vs. mere inconvenience).
- **Inspection and maintenance** frequency.
- **Stress concentration** presence (notches, keyways, holes).
- **Code and standards** (IS, AGMA, ISO, DIN specifications).

---

### 2.1.3 Design Factor of Safety vs. Actual Factor of Safety

- **Design FoS ($n_d$):** A *target value* chosen at the design stage based on experience and codes.
- **Actual FoS ($n_a$):** The *real ratio* the manufactured component possesses after accounting for actual dimensions, material batch variability, and surface conditions.

The design should ensure $n_a \geq n_d$.

---

### 2.2 Gears — Classification Analysis

Gears are classified on **four major criteria**, each carrying distinct marks in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

### Classification Criterion 1: Arrangement of Shaft Axes

| Basis of Classification | Type | Shaft Axis Relationship |
|--------------------------|------|--------------------------|
| Parallel shafts | Spur, Helical, Herringbone (Double Helical) | Parallel, coplanar |
| Intersecting shafts | Bevel gears (Straight, Spiral, Zerol, Hypoid) | Intersecting, non-parallel |
| Non-intersecting & non-parallel | Worm and Worm Wheel, Hypoid | Skew (non-intersecting, non-parallel) |

### Classification Criterion 2: Peripheral Shape

| Shape Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **External Gear** | Teeth on the *outer* surface. Most common type. | Automobile gearboxes |
| **Internal Gear (Ring Gear)** | Teeth cut on the *inner* surface of a ring. | Planetary gear systems |
| **Rack** | A straight bar with teeth. Translates rotational motion to linear motion. | Steering, CNC machines |

### Classification Criterion 3: Tooth Profile

| Profile | Description | Sliding Action |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| **Involute** | The most widely used; based on involute curve generation. | Conjugate and non-interfering |
| **Cycloidal** | Older design; based on epicycloids and hypocycloids. | Lower sliding |
| **Trapezoidal** | Used in power screw threads, not common in gears. | — |

### Classification Criterion 4: Position of Teeth on the Gear

- **External gears** — teeth project outward.
- **Internal gears** — teeth project inward.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all the high-yield formulas for **Factor of Safety** and gear classification parameters. Memorize these for Part A and Part B derivations.

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Application |
|---------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Generalized FoS | $n = \sigma_{\text{fail}} \,/\, \sigma_{\text{work}}$ | $\sigma_{\text{fail}}$ = failure stress; $\sigma_{\text{work}}$ = design stress | Universal definition |
| FoS (yielding) | $n_y = \sigma_y \,/\, \sigma_{\text{work}}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress | Static load, ductile material |
| FoS (ultimate) | $n_u = \sigma_u \,/\, \sigma_{\text{work}}$ | $\sigma_u$ = ultimate stress | Static load, brittle material |
| FoS (endurance) | $n_e = \sigma_e \,/\, \sigma_{\text{work}}$ | $\sigma_e$ = endurance limit | Cyclic load |
| FoS in shear | $n_{\tau} = \tau_{\text{fail}} \,/\, \tau_{\text{work}}$ | $\tau_{\text{fail}}$ = shear strength; $\tau_{\text{work}}$ = shear stress | Shafts, keys |
| Velocity ratio (spur gear pair) | $i = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}$ | $N$ = teeth number; $D$ = pitch diameter; $\omega$ = angular speed | Gear train design |
| Module (gear) | $m = \frac{D}{N}$ | $D$ = pitch circle diameter (mm); $N$ = number of teeth | Standardization |
| Circular pitch | $p_c = \pi \cdot m$ | $m$ = module | Tooth spacing |
| Center distance (spur) | $a = \frac{m(N_1 + N_2)}{2}$ | — | Gear meshing geometry |
| Pitch line velocity | $v = \frac{\pi D N}{60{,}000}$ (m/s) | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm | Power transmission analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of Gear Classification:**
> The classification system allows engineers to **select the right gear** for the application. Spur gears are used where noise is not critical (e.g., industrial gearboxes), helical gears for high-speed and quiet operation (e.g., automotive transmissions), bevel gears for shaft direction change (e.g., differentials), and worm gears for high reduction ratios (e.g., elevators, conveyors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Stress and Factor of Safety

The most fundamental KTU derivation on this topic is establishing the relationship between *working stress*, *failure stress*, and *factor of safety*. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

### Derivation 1: Working Stress from Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)

> **Statement:** Prove that the working (allowable) stress of a material is given by $\sigma_{\text{work}} = \sigma_u / n$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Factor of Safety:**

We begin from the primary definition given by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

$$
n = \frac{\text{Failure Stress}}{\text{Working Stress}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Failure Stress:**

For a ductile material, the failure (collapse) stress is the **Ultimate Tensile Stress $\sigma_u$** when we are using the *safe load* model, or the **Yield Stress $\sigma_y$** for elastic limit design. Let us adopt the general symbol $\sigma_{\text{fail}}$ for the failure stress.

$$
n = \frac{\sigma_{\text{fail}}}{\sigma_{\text{work}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to express the working stress:**

Multiplying both sides by $\sigma_{\text{work}}$ and dividing by $n$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{work}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{fail}}}{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute specific cases:**

Case (i) — When failure is by yielding (ductile, static):

$$
\sigma_{\text{work}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n_y}
$$

Case (ii) — When failure is by rupture (brittle, static):

$$
\sigma_{\text{work}} = \frac{\sigma_u}{n_u}
$$

Case (iii) — When failure is by fatigue (cyclic):

$$
\sigma_{\text{work}} = \frac{\sigma_e}{n_e}
$$

**Conclusion:**

> The allowable working stress is the failure stress divided by the chosen factor of safety. This is the standard relationship used throughout design of machine elements.

---

### Derivation 2: Factor of Safety for a Tapered Round Bar under Axial Load

This is a **typical KTU Part B derivation problem**. A round bar of varying diameter $D$ and $d$ ($D > d$) carries an axial load $P$. Show that the factor of safety based on yielding is:

$$
n_y = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \sigma_y}{4P}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the location of maximum stress:**

For a bar loaded in tension, axial stress is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A}
$$

Since the area is smallest at the smaller diameter $d$, the stress is **maximum** at this section.

**Step 2 — Express the maximum working stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{P}{A_{\min}} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi}{4} d^2} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of FoS based on yielding:**

$$
n_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for maximum stress:**

$$
n_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\frac{4P}{\pi d^2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert and simplify:**

$$
n_y = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot \pi d^2}{4P}
$$

$$
n_y = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \sigma_y}{4P}
$$

**Conclusion:**

> This result confirms that the *critical cross-section* (smallest area) governs the design, and the factor of safety is inversely proportional to the applied load $P$.

---

### Derivation 3: Velocity Ratio of a Simple Spur Gear Train

A two-stage gear train has gears $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ in mesh. The input shaft carries gear $A$ meshing with gear $B$ on the countershaft, and gear $C$ on the countershaft meshes with gear $D$ on the output shaft. Derive the overall velocity ratio.

**Step 1 — Velocity ratio of the first pair ($A$ and $B$):**

Since the gears are in external mesh, the linear velocity at the pitch circle is the same:

$$
\frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{D_B}{D_A}
$$

The speed ratio $i_1 = N_B / N_A = D_B / D_A$.

**Step 2 — Velocity ratio of the second pair ($C$ and $D$):**

$$
i_2 = \frac{N_D}{N_C} = \frac{D_D}{D_C}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply for the overall train ratio:**

The countershaft connects $B$ and $C$, so $N_B = N_C$. The overall ratio $i$ is:

$$
i = i_1 \cdot i_2 = \frac{N_B}{N_A} \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_C} = \frac{N_D}{N_A}
$$

$$
i = \frac{N_B}{N_A} \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_B} = \frac{N_D}{N_A}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in module form (if all gears have the same module $m$):**

Using $D = mN$:

$$
i = \frac{N_B \cdot N_D}{N_A \cdot N_C}
$$

**Conclusion:**

> For a simple compound gear train, the overall speed ratio equals the product of the individual ratios, and the intermediate (countershaft) speeds cancel out.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which FoS

| Loading Type | Material Type | Governing FoS | Typical FoS Range (KTU typical) |
|--------------|---------------|---------------|----------------------------------|
| Static, no stress concentration | Ductile | $n_y = \sigma_y / \sigma_{\text{work}}$ | 1.5 to 2.5 |
| Static | Brittle | $n_u = \sigma_u / \sigma_{\text{work}}$ | 3.0 to 4.0 |
| Dynamic, impact | Ductile | $n_y$ with impact factor | 2.0 to 4.0 |
| Cyclic (fatigue) | Ductile | $n_e$ via Soderberg/Goodman | 2.0 to 3.5 |
| Combined (bending + torsion) | Ductile | Equivalent stress / allowable | 1.5 to 2.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Factor of Safety Decision Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic an engineer follows when selecting the appropriate Factor of Safety during machine design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Identify Loading and Material]) --> loadQ{Is loading static?}
    loadQ -- Yes --> matQ{Is material ductile?}
    matQ -- Yes --> yldA[Use n_y = sigma_y / sigma_work]
    matQ -- No --> ultA[Use n_u = sigma_u / sigma_work]
    loadQ -- No --> cycQ{Is loading cyclic?}
    cycQ -- Yes --> fatA[Use n_e with Goodman or Soderberg criterion]
    cycQ -- No --> impA[Use n_y with Impact Factor]
    yldA --> endA([Select Typical n = 1.5 to 4 based on application])
    ultA --> endA
    fatA --> endA
    impA --> endA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Flow:**
> The decision tree first asks whether the load is static or dynamic. If static, the material's ductility decides whether to use yield or ultimate stress. If dynamic, the cyclic or impact nature decides whether to invoke fatigue or impact-modified yield criteria. Finally, the magnitude of $n$ is selected from standard engineering tables based on the consequences of failure.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gear Classification Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram presents a hierarchical classification of gears, mapping the four primary classification criteria (axes, shape, profile, position).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([GEARS - Classification]) --> axisCrit{Basis 1: Shaft Axes}
    startB --> shapeCrit{Basis 2: Peripheral Shape}
    startB --> profileCrit{Basis 3: Tooth Profile}
    startB --> posCrit{Basis 4: Tooth Position}

    axisCrit --> parA[Parallel Axes]
    axisCrit --> intA[Intersecting Axes]
    axisCrit --> skewA[Non-Intersecting, Non-Parallel Skew Axes]

    parA --> spurG[Spur Gear]
    parA --> helG[Helical Gear]
    parA --> hbgG[Herringbone Gear]

    intA --> bvlG[Bevel Gear Straight]
    intA --> sblG[Spiral Bevel]
    intA --> zrlG[Zerol Bevel]
    intA --> hypG[Hypoid Gear]

    skewA --> wormG[Worm and Worm Wheel]
    skewA --> hypSkG[Hypoid Skew]

    shapeCrit --> extG[External Gear]
    shapeCrit --> intG[Internal Ring Gear]
    shapeCrit --> rackG[Rack and Pinion]

    profileCrit --> invG[Involute Profile]
    profileCrit --> cycG[Cycloidal Profile]

    posCrit --> extPos[External Teeth]
    posCrit --> intPos[Internal Teeth]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:**
> The diagram organizes the major gear types into four orthogonal classification trees. The left-most tier names the four bases of classification. Each basis is a decision branch leading to a specific gear family. The student should be able to draw this tree (or a simpler version) and *name two application examples* for each gear type. This directly addresses the KTU 2024 Part B question pattern: *"Classify gears and give two examples of each type."*

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Simple Gear Train

Since complex gear mesh physics are difficult to render, the following block diagram shows the *functional flow* of power through a two-stage spur gear reducer. This is a valid schematic substitute for a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inSh([Input Shaft Pinion]) --> mesh1{Pair 1: Pinion and Gear A}
    mesh1 --> cntSh([Counter Shaft Gear B])
    cntSh --> mesh2{Pair 2: Gear C and Output D}
    mesh2 --> outSh([Output Shaft Slow Speed])
    inSh -- High Speed --> cntSh
    cntSh -- Reduced Speed --> outSh
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Reading:**
> Power enters at the input shaft (high rpm, low torque), passes through the first mesh which reduces the speed and increases the torque, flows through the countershaft, and exits via the second mesh at an even lower speed with proportionally higher torque. Each mesh multiplies the speed ratio and torque.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Factor of Safety. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Model Answer:**

> The Factor of Safety (FoS), also called the Safety Factor, is the **ratio of failure stress of a material to the working (or design) stress** expected under service conditions. It is a dimensionless number that quantifies the margin of safety built into a design.
> 
> Mathematically, $n = \sigma_{\text{fail}} / \sigma_{\text{work}}$.
> 
> For ductile materials, $\sigma_{\text{fail}} = \sigma_y$ (yield stress); for brittle materials, $\sigma_{\text{fail}} = \sigma_u$ (ultimate stress). Typical values range from **1.5 to 4.0** depending on the application, consequences of failure, and uncertainty in loading.

---

### Question 2: List any four classifications of gears. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Model Answer:**

Gears are classified based on:

1. **Shaft axis arrangement** — Parallel (spur, helical), intersecting (bevel), non-intersecting (worm).
2. **Peripheral shape** — External gear, internal gear, rack and pinion.
3. **Tooth profile** — Involute, cycloidal.
4. **Tooth position** — External teeth, internal teeth.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student is given internal choice — either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Both questions are presented below for self-practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define Factor of Safety. List the factors affecting the selection of Factor of Safety in machine design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular bar of diameter 50 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. If the yield stress of the material is 320 MPa, determine the factor of safety of the bar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Definition and Factors:**

**Definition:** The Factor of Safety is the ratio of the failure stress (yield, ultimate, or endurance) to the working (design) stress: $n = \sigma_{\text{fail}} / \sigma_{\text{work}}$.

**Factors affecting selection of FoS:**

1. Type of loading (static, dynamic, impact, fatigue).
2. Material properties (ductility, toughness, homogeneity).
3. Manufacturing quality (precision, surface finish).
4. Reliability and safety requirements of the application.
5. Operating environment (temperature, corrosion, humidity).
6. Consequences of failure (life loss vs. inconvenience).
7. Stress concentrations (notches, keyways, holes).
8. Codes and standards (IS, AGMA, ISO, DIN).
9. Inspection and maintenance accessibility.
10. Uncertainty in load estimation.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50$ mm
- Axial load $P = 80$ kN $= 80 \times 10^3$ N
- Yield stress $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa

**Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area.

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (50)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2500}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the working (axial) stress.

$$
\sigma_{\text{work}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{1963.5} = 40.74 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the FoS based on yielding.

$$
n_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{work}}} = \frac{320}{40.74} = 7.85
$$

**Result:** The factor of safety of the bar is **$n_y \approx 7.86$**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Classify gears based on (i) shaft axis arrangement, (ii) peripheral shape, and (iii) tooth profile. Give one example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two spur gears in mesh have 24 and 60 teeth respectively, with a module of 4 mm. Calculate the center distance and the speed ratio. The pinion rotates at 1200 rpm. Find the velocity of the pitch circle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Classification:**

**(i) Based on shaft axis arrangement:**
- **Parallel axes** → Spur gear, Helical gear, Herringbone gear.
- **Intersecting axes** → Bevel gear (straight, spiral).
- **Non-intersecting, non-parallel (skew)** → Worm and worm wheel, hypoid gear.

**(ii) Based on peripheral shape:**
- **External gear** (teeth on outer surface) — e.g., spur pinion.
- **Internal gear** (teeth on inner surface) — e.g., ring gear in planetary system.
- **Rack** — e.g., rack and pinion in CNC machines.

**(iii) Based on tooth profile:**
- **Involute** — most commonly used in modern gears.
- **Cycloidal** — used in older designs and watches.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $N_1 = 24$ (pinion), $N_2 = 60$ (gear)
- Module $m = 4$ mm
- Pinion speed $N_{p} = 1200$ rpm

**Step 1:** Calculate the pitch circle diameters.

$$
D_1 = m \cdot N_1 = 4 \times 24 = 96 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_2 = m \cdot N_2 = 4 \times 60 = 240 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the center distance.

$$
a = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{96 + 240}{2} = 168 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the speed ratio (velocity ratio).

$$
i = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{60}{24} = 2.5
$$

Alternatively, $i = D_2 / D_1 = 240 / 96 = 2.5$. ✓

**Step 4:** Find the speed of the gear.

$$
N_g = \frac{N_p}{i} = \frac{1200}{2.5} = 480 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the pitch line velocity.

$$
v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_p}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(with } D_1 \text{ in mm, } N_p \text{ in rpm, } v \text{ in m/s)}
$$

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 96 \times 1200}{60{,}000} = 6.032 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Result:**
- **Center distance** $a = 168$ mm
- **Speed ratio** $i = 2.5$
- **Pitch line velocity** $v \approx 6.03$ m/s

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls for Module 3 — Factor of Safety and Gear Classification:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing static and fatigue FoS:** Many students write $n = \sigma_y / \sigma$ for cyclic loads. Under fatigue, you **must** use the endurance limit $\sigma_e$ or the Soderberg/Goodman line equations. This is a 2–3 mark deduction.
> 
> 2. **Using wrong units in gear formulas:** The pitch line velocity $v = \pi D N / 60{,}000$ (with $D$ in mm) gives $v$ in m/s. If $D$ is entered in meters, the formula becomes $v = \pi D N / 60$. Mixing units costs 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the "basis of classification" in gear questions:** The KTU board expects you to *state the basis* before listing the types. Writing "Bevel gear, spur gear, helical gear" without specifying "based on shaft axis arrangement" loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the distinction between external and internal gears:** For internal gears, the center distance formula changes to $a = (D_2 - D_1) / 2$. Using the spur formula blindly loses 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **No sketch in classification questions:** If a Part B question asks to "classify gears with sketches," writing only text without diagrams is penalized 2–3 marks. Always include a simple freehand sketch.
> 
> 6. **Wrong symbol for FoS:** Use $n$ (or $F.S.$ or $N$). Do not use $F$ (reserved for force) or $S$ (reserved for stress).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Factor of Safety** is dimensionless and equals failure stress ÷ working stress.
- **Three types of FoS:** $n_y$ (yielding), $n_u$ (ultimate), $n_e$ (endurance).
- **Typical FoS range:** 1.5 to 4.0; higher for critical applications (aerospace, pressure vessels).
- **Working stress** = Failure stress / FoS.
- **FoS selection** depends on loading, material, environment, consequences, and codes.
- **Gears** transmit motion and torque with no slip (positive drive).
- **Gears are classified on four bases:** shaft axes, peripheral shape, tooth profile, tooth position.
- **Spur gears:** parallel shafts, simple, noisy at high speed.
- **Helical gears:** parallel shafts, quieter, smooth, generate axial thrust.
- **Bevel gears:** intersecting shafts, used in differentials and right-angle drives.
- **Worm gears:** non-intersecting skew shafts, high reduction ratio, self-locking possible.
- **Rack and pinion:** converts rotation to linear motion.
- **Module $m = D / N$** is the standard sizing parameter in the metric system.
- **Center distance (spur) $a = m(N_1 + N_2)/2$**.
- **Speed ratio $i = N_2/N_1 = D_2/D_1 = \omega_1/\omega_2$** for an external mesh.
- **Pitch line velocity $v = \pi D N / 60{,}000$** m/s (with $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm).
- **Always** state the basis of classification before listing the gear types.
- **Always** draw a freehand sketch for Part B classification questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gear Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Nomenclature** refers to the complete set of standardized geometric terms, symbols, and dimensional proportions used to define the geometry of gear teeth. As per the **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association)** and **IS 2075** standards adopted in the KTU 2024 mechanical engineering curriculum, every gear tooth is fully described by a system of circles (pitch circle, base circle, addendum circle, dedendum circle, etc.), linear dimensions (addendum, dedendum, circular pitch, tooth thickness, backlash, etc.), and angular parameters (pressure angle, helix angle for helical gears).

In the KTU framework, a student must be able to draw a **neat, fully-labeled gear tooth diagram** and write down the relationship between every term and the **module (m)**, which is the fundamental size parameter of a gear.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Wheel" Intuition

Imagine two identical **Lego wheels** (the small round gears from a Lego technic set) with teeth that mesh perfectly. If you look at one tooth in cross-section, it looks like a tiny **trapezoidal mountain** with sloping sides. Now, every "mountain" has the same agreed dimensions because Lego is a global standard — a 4-tooth gear meshes with another 4-tooth gear anywhere in the world.

A **gear nomenclature** is exactly this — a global "Lego agreement" among engineers that defines:
- How tall is the tooth? (**Addendum / Dedendum**)
- How thick is the tooth? (**Tooth thickness**)
- At what slope are the sides cut? (**Pressure angle**)
- What is the imaginary circle on which two gears "roll" without slipping? (**Pitch circle**)

Without this standard agreement, a gear manufactured in Kochi would not mesh with one manufactured in Kalamazoo.

## 1.3 Key Standard Proportions (KTU High-Yield Data)

For **standard full-depth involute spur gears** with **20° full-depth system** (IS 2075 / AGMA):

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (in terms of Module, m) |
|---|-----------|--------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | Module | m | Fundamental parameter (in **mm**) |
| 2 | Circular pitch | pc | π·m |
| 3 | Addendum | a | **1.0 m** |
| 4 | Dedendum | d | **1.25 m** |
| 5 | Whole depth | h | **2.25 m** |
| 6 | Working depth | h' | **2.0 m** |
| 7 | Clearance | c | **0.25 m** |
| 8 | Tooth thickness | t | πm/2 |
| 9 | Tooth space | — | πm/2 |
| 10 | Standard pressure angle | φ | **20°** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why module and not diameter?** Because two gears with the **same module** (regardless of how many teeth they have) will mesh correctly. Module is the "Lego size." This is why module — not diameter — is the unifying parameter in gear design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Reference Circles of a Gear

A spur gear is geometrically described by **five concentric circles**, all sharing the same center. Listed from smallest to largest:

### 2.1.1 Base Circle (db)
- The circle from which the **involute tooth profile** is geometrically generated.
- It is the smallest of the operating circles.
- **Why it exists**: The involute curve is traced by a taut string being unwound from this circle. The base circle is the *origin* of the tooth flank shape.

$$d_b = d \cos\phi$$

where:
- $d_b$ = base circle diameter
- $d$ = pitch circle diameter
- $\phi$ = pressure angle (**20°** standard)

### 2.1.2 Root Circle / Dedendum Circle (df)
- The circle that **touches the bottom (root) of the tooth spaces**.
- It defines the inner boundary of the tooth.

$$d_f = d - 2d = d - 2.5m$$

### 2.1.3 Pitch Circle (d)
- **The single most important circle.** It is the *imaginary* circle on which two meshing gears roll on each other **without slipping** (pure rolling contact).
- For two meshing gears, the **pitch circles are tangent to each other**.
- It is the circle at which the **module (m) is measured**.

$$d = m \cdot Z$$

where $Z$ = number of teeth on the gear.

### 2.1.4 Addendum Circle (da)
- The circle that **touches the tip (top) of the teeth**.
- It defines the outer boundary of the gear.

$$d_a = d + 2a = d + 2m$$

### 2.1.5 Outside Circle / Tip Circle
- Usually identical to the addendum circle in standard gears.

## 2.2 Linear Tooth Dimensions

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|------|--------|------------|---------|
| **Addendum** | a | Height of tooth above the pitch circle | $a = 1.0\,m$ |
| **Dedendum** | d | Depth of tooth below the pitch circle | $d = 1.25\,m$ |
| **Whole depth** | h | Total tooth height from root to tip | $h = a + d = 2.25\,m$ |
| **Working depth** | $h'$ | Depth of tooth contact (from tip of one gear to clearance line) | $h' = 2a = 2.0\,m$ |
| **Clearance** | c | Gap between tip of one gear and root of mating gear | $c = 0.25\,m$ |
| **Circular pitch** | $p_c$ | Arc length along pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth | $p_c = \pi m$ |
| **Tooth thickness** | t | Arc length of tooth measured on the pitch circle | $t = \dfrac{p_c}{2} = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ |
| **Tooth space** | e | Arc length of space (gap) on the pitch circle | $e = \dfrac{p_c}{2} = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ |
| **Backlash** | b | Small intentional gap between meshing tooth flanks (not a property of a single gear) | $b = p_c - (t_1 + t_2)$ |

## 2.3 Angular and Geometric Parameters

- **Pressure Angle (φ)**: The angle between the line of action (common normal at the point of contact) and the common tangent to the pitch circles at the pitch point. **Standard value = 20°** (older British standards used 14½°).
- **Line of Action**: The path along which the point of contact moves as the gears rotate. It is a straight line tangent to both base circles.
- **Path of Contact**: The actual segment of the line of action where the tooth flank contact physically occurs.
- **Arc of Contact**: The arc traced on the pitch circle as a tooth moves from the start of contact to the end of contact.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table before every KTU exam. It contains **90% of the gear-nomenclature numerical questions** asked in the ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Module | $m = d / Z$ | mm | Fundamental size parameter |
| 2 | Pitch diameter | $d = m Z$ | mm | $Z$ = number of teeth |
| 3 | Circular pitch | $p_c = \pi m$ | mm | Arc length per tooth |
| 4 | Diametral pitch (English) | $P = Z / d$ | inches⁻¹ | Inverse of module; **not used in KTU** but good to know |
| 5 | Addendum | $a = m$ | mm | 1.0 m |
| 6 | Dedendum | $d_{ed} = 1.25 m$ | mm | 1.25 m |
| 7 | Whole depth | $h = 2.25 m$ | mm | a + dedendum |
| 8 | Working depth | $h' = 2m$ | mm | 2 × addendum |
| 9 | Clearance | $c = 0.25 m$ | mm | dedendum − addendum |
| 10 | Tooth thickness | $t = \pi m / 2$ | mm | Half of circular pitch |
| 11 | Addendum circle dia | $d_a = m(Z + 2)$ | mm | $d + 2a$ |
| 12 | Dedendum circle dia | $d_f = m(Z - 2.5)$ | mm | $d - 2 \cdot d_{ed}$ |
| 13 | Base circle dia | $d_b = d \cos\phi$ | mm | $\cos 20° \approx 0.9397$ |
| 14 | Clearance circle dia | $d_c = m(Z - 2)$ | mm | Tip-to-tooth space gap |
| 15 | Center distance (two gears) | $C = (d_1 + d_2)/2 = m(Z_1+Z_2)/2$ | mm | Tangent pitch circles |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive gearboxes** (Maruti, Tata, Bosch India plants) — gear nomenclature governs the entire transmission design. The same module allows a 16-tooth pinion to mesh with a 60-tooth gear on a single shaft family.
- **Wind turbine gearboxes** (Suzlon, Vestas India) — module selection determines load capacity.
- **CNC machine tools** — backlash specification is critical for positioning accuracy, defined by tooth thickness and space tolerance.
- **Watch movements** (Titan, HMT heritage) — micro-module gears (m = 0.1 mm or smaller) require precision nomenclature.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Module (m) and Pitch Diameter (d)

### Step 1 — Definition of Circular Pitch

The **circular pitch ($p_c$)** is defined as the arc distance measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points (e.g., crest-to-crest) of two adjacent teeth.

Since the circumference of the pitch circle is $\pi d$ and it must accommodate **$Z$** identical teeth and **$Z$** identical spaces (so $2Z$ segments in total around the pitch circle):

$$p_c = \frac{\pi d}{Z}$$

### Step 2 — Definition of Module

**Module** is defined as the pitch circle diameter divided by the number of teeth:

$$m = \frac{d}{Z}$$

### Step 3 — Equating the Two

From Step 1, $\pi d / Z = p_c$, and from Step 2, $d / Z = m$. Substituting $d/Z = m$ into the circular pitch formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_c &= \pi \cdot \left(\frac{d}{Z}\right) \\
p_c &= \pi m
\end{aligned}$$

This gives the **fundamental relationship**: the circular pitch equals $\pi$ times the module. The presence of $\pi$ is why a clean, rational module (e.g., $m = 2$ mm, $m = 4$ mm) gives an awkward circular pitch ($p_c = 6.283$ mm). **This is the entire reason the concept of "module" was invented** — to avoid $\pi$ in the standard size.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Tooth Thickness = πm/2

### Step 1 — Total Arc Available

Around the entire pitch circle, the arc is shared equally between teeth and spaces. So:

$$\text{Tooth thickness} + \text{Tooth space} = p_c$$

### Step 2 — Symmetry Assumption

For a **perfect gear** (before any backlash is added), the tooth and the space have equal arc length on the pitch circle:

$$t = e = \frac{p_c}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting $p_c = \pi m$

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\pi m}{2} \\
e &= \frac{\pi m}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence tooth thickness = $\pi m / 2$, and tooth space = $\pi m / 2$. With **backlash** added, the tooth is *thinned* slightly and the space is *widened* by the same amount:

$$b = p_c - (t_1 + t_2)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Base Circle Diameter from Pressure Angle

The involute curve is generated by unwinding a string from the base circle. At the pitch point (intersection of pitch circle and line of action), the **radius of the base circle** is the projection of the **pitch radius** onto the line perpendicular to the line of action.

Geometrically, if $\phi$ is the pressure angle (angle between line of action and the tangent to the pitch circle at the pitch point), then:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{r_b}{r}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to convert radii to diameters:

$$d_b = d \cos\phi$$

For $\phi = 20°$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_b &= d \cdot \cos(20°) \\
d_b &= d \cdot 0.9397
\end{aligned}$$

The base circle is therefore about **6% smaller** than the pitch circle.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Standard Proportions Summary

| Geometric Element | Diameter Formula | Comment |
|-------------------|------------------|---------|
| Base circle | $d_b = d\cos\phi$ | Innermost operating circle |
| Root / Dedendum circle | $d_f = d - 2.5m$ | Bottom of tooth spaces |
| Pitch circle | $d = mZ$ | **Reference for all meshing** |
| Clearance circle | $d_c = d - 2m$ | Tip clearance boundary |
| Addendum / Tip circle | $d_a = d + 2m$ | Top of teeth |

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A spur gear has 24 teeth and a module of 4 mm. It is in mesh with a pinion having 18 teeth. The pressure angle is 20°. Calculate:
1. Pitch diameters of gear and pinion
2. Circular pitch
3. Addendum, dedendum, whole depth
4. Tooth thickness
5. Base circle diameters
6. Center distance

**Solution:**

**(1) Pitch diameters**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{gear} &= m \cdot Z_{gear} = 4 \times 24 = 96 \text{ mm} \\
d_{pinion} &= m \cdot Z_{pinion} = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2) Circular pitch**

$$p_c = \pi m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}$$

**(3) Tooth proportions**

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= m = 4 \text{ mm} \\
d_{ed} &= 1.25 m = 1.25 \times 4 = 5 \text{ mm} \\
h &= 2.25 m = 2.25 \times 4 = 9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(4) Tooth thickness**

$$t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{2} = 6.283 \text{ mm}$$

**(5) Base circle diameters**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{b,gear} &= d_{gear} \cos 20° = 96 \times 0.9397 = 90.21 \text{ mm} \\
d_{b,pinion} &= d_{pinion} \cos 20° = 72 \times 0.9397 = 67.66 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(6) Center distance**

$$C = \frac{d_{gear} + d_{pinion}}{2} = \frac{96 + 72}{2} = 84 \text{ mm}$$

Concentric circles in Mermaid are visualized as a layered functional hierarchy — the innermost ring represents the smallest circle (base circle), and the outermost represents the largest (addendum circle).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SPUR GEAR NOMENCLATURE"] --> B["Base Circle: db = d cos phi"]

    A --> E["Clearance Circle: dc = d minus 2m"]
    A --> F["Addendum Circle: da = d plus 2m"]

    D --> G["Linear Dimensions"]
    G --> G1["Addendum a = 1.0m"]
    G --> G2["Dedendum d = 1.25m"]
    G --> G3["Tooth Thickness t = pi m / 2"]
    G --> G4["Circular Pitch pc = pi m"]
    G --> G5["Clearance c = 0.25m"]
    G --> G6["Whole Depth h = 2.25m"]
    G --> G7["Working Depth h prime = 2.0m"]

    A --> H["Angular Parameters"]
    H --> H1["Pressure Angle phi = 20 degrees Standard"]
    H --> H2["Line of Action"]
    H --> H3["Path of Contact"]
    H --> H4["Arc of Contact"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tooth Geometry Cross-Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["Tooth Tip: Top of Addendum Circle"] --> T2["Addendum a = 1.0m: Above Pitch Line"]
    T2 --> T3["Pitch Line: Pitch Circle Diameter d = mZ"]
    T3 --> T4["Dedendum d = 1.25m: Below Pitch Line"]
    T4 --> T5["Tooth Root: Bottom of Dedendum Circle"]
    T3 --> T6["Tooth Thickness t = pi m / 2"]
    T3 --> T7["Tooth Space e = pi m / 2"]
    T1 --> T8["Flank: Involute Curve from Base Circle"]
    T5 --> T9["Fillet: Stress Relief Curve at Root"]
    T8 --> T10["Pressure Angle phi = 20 degrees: Flank Slope"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of a Spur Gear Tooth (Text Diagram)

For a **single tooth viewed in cross-section**, picture a trapezoidal shape:

```
              +-----------+         <-- Addendum circle (da)
             /  |   |    \           Addendum a = 1.0 m
            /   |   |     \          (Height of tooth above pitch line)
           /    |   |      \
          +-----+---+-------+       <-- Pitch circle (d = mZ)
           \    |   |      /          (The reference "rolling" circle)
            \   |   |     /         Dedendum d = 1.25 m
             \  |   |    /          (Depth below pitch line)
              \ |   |   /
               \|   |  /
                +---+-+            <-- Dedendum circle (df)
                  fillet
```

The **flanks** (sloped sides) are not straight lines but **involute curves** generated from the base circle. The angle the flank makes with the radial line at the pitch point is the **pressure angle φ = 20°**.

```mermaid
graph TD

    P6 --> P7["Draw Tooth Profile: Involute Curve + Fillet"]
    P7 --> P8["Verify Center Distance: C = m times Z1 plus Z2 divided by 2"]
    P8 --> P9["Check for Interference and Undercutting"]
    P9 --> P10["Final Gear Drawing with Full Nomenclature Labels"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** 
**Define the following terms related to spur gear nomenclature: (a) Circular pitch, (b) Module, (c) Pressure angle. State one standard value used in design.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **(a) Circular pitch ($p_c$):** It is the distance measured along the circumference of the pitch circle between corresponding points of two adjacent teeth. Mathematically, $p_c = \pi m$, where $m$ is the module. **Unit: mm**.

> **(b) Module (m):** It is the ratio of the pitch circle diameter ($d$) to the number of teeth ($Z$). $m = d / Z$. It is the fundamental size parameter of a gear and is standardized (e.g., 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm as per IS). **Unit: mm**.

> **(c) Pressure angle (φ):** It is the angle between the line of action (common normal at the point of contact) and the common tangent to the pitch circles at the pitch point. The **standard pressure angle is 20°** for full-depth involute gears as per AGMA / IS 2075.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between addendum and dedendum. State the standard values of both for a 20° full-depth involute gear in terms of module m.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Addendum (a):** It is the radial distance from the **pitch circle to the tip of the tooth** (i.e., the top of the tooth). For a standard full-depth involute gear, $a = 1.0\,m$.

> **Dedendum ($d$):** It is the radial distance from the **pitch circle to the bottom of the tooth** (root). For a standard full-depth involute gear, $d = 1.25\,m$.

> **Key Difference:** Addendum is the *height of the tooth above* the pitch circle, while dedendum is the *depth of the tooth below* the pitch circle. Dedendum is intentionally **larger** than addendum by $0.25\,m$ to provide a **clearance (c = 0.25 m)** so that the tip of the mating gear does not foul with the root of this gear.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following two questions are designed as **Module 3 — Internal Choice**. In the KTU ESE, you will be given one of the two (or similar). Practice drawing the tooth diagram **neatly with all labels** for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw a neat sketch of a single involute spur gear tooth and label all the important features including pitch circle, base circle, addendum circle, dedendum circle, addendum, dedendum, tooth thickness, tooth space, circular pitch, and pressure angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two spur gears with 24 teeth and 36 teeth respectively are in mesh. The module is 5 mm and the pressure angle is 20°. Calculate:
  (i) Pitch diameters
  (ii) Circular pitch
  (iii) Addendum and dedendum
  (iv) Tooth thickness
  (v) Base circle diameters
  (vi) Center distance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Sketch** (Student is expected to draw — description below for the answer sheet):

A circle representing the **gear** is drawn with five concentric reference circles. Starting from innermost:
- **Base circle** (smallest) — label $d_b = d \cos\phi$
- **Dedendum circle** — $d_f = d - 2.5m$
- **Pitch circle** (most important — drawn as a **dashed circle** to indicate it is imaginary) — $d = mZ$
- **Clearance circle** — $d_c = d - 2m$
- **Addendum circle** (outermost) — $d_a = d + 2m$

A single tooth is shown as a **trapezoidal projection** above the pitch line. The flank is drawn as an **involute curve** generated from the base circle. The angle between the flank and the tangent to the pitch circle at the pitch point is marked as **φ = 20°**. Tooth thickness and tooth space are both marked on the pitch circle as $\pi m / 2$ each. Addendum (above pitch) and dedendum (below pitch) are clearly labeled with arrows.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $Z_1 = 24$, $Z_2 = 36$, $m = 5$ mm, $\phi = 20°$.

**(i) Pitch diameters:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= m \cdot Z_1 = 5 \times 24 = 120 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= m \cdot Z_2 = 5 \times 36 = 180 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Circular pitch:**

$$p_c = \pi m = \pi \times 5 = 15.708 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Addendum and Dedendum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= 1.0 \times m = 1.0 \times 5 = 5 \text{ mm} \\
d_{ed} &= 1.25 \times m = 1.25 \times 5 = 6.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Tooth thickness:**

$$t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 5}{2} = 7.854 \text{ mm}$$

**(v) Base circle diameters:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{b1} &= d_1 \cos 20° = 120 \times 0.9397 = 112.76 \text{ mm} \\
d_{b2} &= d_2 \cos 20° = 180 \times 0.9397 = 169.15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(vi) Center distance:**

$$C = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{120 + 180}{2} = 150 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit "mm"** after every numerical answer. Even one missing unit can cost 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Using $\cos 20° = 0.94$** (rounded) instead of the more accurate 0.9397. KTU values may require 3–4 decimal accuracy.
> 3. **Confusing "dedendum" (symbol d) with "pitch diameter" (also d)** in the formula. Always write $d_{ed}$ or use a distinct symbol like $d_f$ to avoid confusion.
> 4. **Drawing the pitch circle as solid instead of dashed** — the pitch circle is an imaginary reference circle and **must be drawn as a dashed line** in the sketch.
> 5. **Missing the involute curve** — the flanks are *not* straight radial lines; they are involutes. A straight-line flank loses 1–2 marks in the sketch question.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **module** and **circular pitch** in gear design. Why is the module standardized rather than the pitch diameter? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pinion with 18 teeth meshes with a gear having 72 teeth. The module is 3 mm and pressure angle is 20°. Determine:
  (i) Pitch circle diameters
  (ii) Addendum circle diameter
  (iii) Dedendum circle diameter
  (iv) Tooth thickness and circular pitch
  (v) Whole depth and working depth
  (vi) Base circle diameter of the gear. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Concept of Module and Circular Pitch:**

> **Module (m):** The ratio of pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth: $m = d / Z$. It represents the size of the gear tooth. **Standard values** (in mm) as per IS are: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50.

> **Circular pitch ($p_c$):** The arc distance between corresponding points on adjacent teeth measured along the pitch circle: $p_c = \pi m$.

> **Why module is standardized, not pitch diameter:** The module is the **fundamental tooth size parameter**. If two gears have the **same module**, they will mesh correctly *regardless of their number of teeth*. If the pitch diameter were standardized, then a 24-tooth gear and a 36-tooth gear would have different tooth sizes and would not mesh. By standardizing **m** (which is independent of Z), the gear manufacturer can produce a *family* of meshing gears — any pinion and gear from the same module series will work together. The module is also free from the irrational number $\pi$, unlike the circular pitch.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $Z_{pinion} = 18$, $Z_{gear} = 72$, $m = 3$ mm, $\phi = 20°$.

**(i) Pitch circle diameters:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{pinion} &= m \cdot Z_{pinion} = 3 \times 18 = 54 \text{ mm} \\
d_{gear} &= m \cdot Z_{gear} = 3 \times 72 = 216 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Addendum circle diameter of gear:**

$$d_a = d + 2m = 216 + 2(3) = 222 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Dedendum circle diameter of gear:**

$$d_f = d - 2.5m = 216 - 2.5(3) = 208.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Tooth thickness and circular pitch:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_c &= \pi m = \pi \times 3 = 9.425 \text{ mm} \\
t &= \frac{p_c}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 3}{2} = 4.712 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(v) Whole depth and working depth:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 2.25 m = 2.25 \times 3 = 6.75 \text{ mm} \\
h' &= 2.0 m = 2.0 \times 3 = 6.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(vi) Base circle diameter of gear:**

$$d_{b,gear} = d_{gear} \cos 20° = 216 \times 0.9397 = 202.98 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B Specific:**
> 1. **Mixing up "pinion" and "gear" terminology** — In KTU, the **smaller of the two meshing gears is always the pinion**, the larger is the gear. Don't mix the labels.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $2.5m$ (not $2m$) for dedendum circle** — students commonly write $d_f = d - 2m$ which gives the **clearance circle**, not the dedendum circle. The correct dedendum circle is $d - 2.5m$.
> 3. **Not mentioning standard module values** in part (a) — KTU expects you to **list at least 4–5 standard module values** from IS 2075. Generic "standard values" without numbers loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gear Nomenclature (Module 3)**

### 🎯 Must-Memorize Standard Proportions (20° Full-Depth System)

- **Addendum** $a = 1.0\,m$
- **Dedendum** $d = 1.25\,m$
- **Whole depth** $h = 2.25\,m$
- **Working depth** $h' = 2.0\,m$
- **Clearance** $c = 0.25\,m$
- **Circular pitch** $p_c = \pi m$
- **Tooth thickness** $t = \pi m / 2$
- **Tooth space** $e = \pi m / 2$
- **Standard pressure angle** $\phi = 20°$

### 🎯 Five Reference Circles (Smallest → Largest)

1. **Base circle** $d_b = d \cos\phi$
2. **Root/Dedendum circle** $d_f = d - 2.5m$
3. **Clearance circle** $d_c = d - 2m$
4. **Pitch circle** $d = mZ$ *(the most important — dashed line)*
5. **Addendum/Tip circle** $d_a = d + 2m$

### 🎯 Most Frequently Asked KTU Formulas

- $m = d / Z$
- $d = mZ$
- $p_c = \pi m$
- $t = \pi m / 2$
- $d_b = d \cos 20° \approx 0.9397 d$
- $C = m (Z_1 + Z_2) / 2$

### 🎯 Key Concepts to Explain in 2–3 Sentences Each

- **Why module and not pitch diameter?** → Same module ensures meshing regardless of tooth count.
- **Why is dedendum > addendum?** → To provide a clearance of 0.25 m so the meshing tooth tip doesn't foul the root.
- **Why is the pitch circle drawn dashed?** → It is an imaginary reference circle, not a physical boundary.
- **What is the standard pressure angle and why?** → 20° (AGMA/IS). It balances strength, smoothness, and standardization.
- **What is the involute curve?** → The path traced by a point on a taut string as it is unwound from the base circle; it is the tooth flank shape.

### 🎯 Common Numerical Traps

- "Pinion" = smaller gear, "Gear" = larger gear in a pair.
- $d_f$ uses $2.5m$ (not $2m$) — students often make this mistake.
- Backlash is **not** a property of a single gear; it is defined only for a meshing pair.
- The **base circle** is *not* the same as the **root circle**. Base circle is for involute generation; root circle is the bottom of the tooth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tooth Profiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tooth Profiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Tooth Profiles

A **gear tooth profile** is the geometric shape or curve that defines the flank of a gear tooth as seen in the transverse cross-section. It is the contour line that determines how two meshing gears transmit motion and power from one shaft to another through conjugate action (continuous, smooth rolling-sliding contact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "The tooth profile is the curve traced by a point on the tooth flank in the plane of rotation. For smooth, conjugate motion, gear tooth profiles must satisfy the **Law of Conjugate Action** (also called the **Fundamental Law of Gearing**): *The common normal at the point of contact between a pair of mating teeth must always pass through a fixed point on the line joining the centers, called the Pitch Point.*"

The two most common gear tooth profiles used in modern engineering practice are:
1. **Involute Profile** – Used almost universally in modern industrial gears.
2. **Cycloidal Profile** – Used historically, now limited to clocks, watches, and some instrument gears.

Other specialized profiles include:
- **Circular-arc profile** (Wildhaber–Novikov gears) – for high-contact-ratio applications.
- **Trochoidal profile** – variations of cycloidal geometry.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Two Walkers on a Rope":**
> Imagine two people walking along parallel rails while passing a baton (the "force") between their hands. For the baton to transfer smoothly without jerking the people, the path traced by each hand **must always be tangent to a common line at the point of grip**, and the line of action must always pass through a single fixed pivot (the pitch point).
>
> In gears, the **involute curve** is the magical geometric path that naturally satisfies this constraint. Imagine a string being **unwound from a cylinder** (the base circle) while a pencil at the string's end traces the curve — that traced path **is** the involute. When two such curves mesh, they automatically satisfy the Law of Conjugate Action, which is why this profile is so dominant in industry.

### 1.3 Why Profile Geometry Matters

| Engineering Property | Influence of Tooth Profile |
|---|---|
| Smoothness of motion | Conjugate profiles ensure constant velocity ratio. |
| Manufacturing ease | Involute is easier to cut on standard hobbing machines. |
| Sensitivity to center distance | Cycloidal is sensitive; involute tolerates small variations. |
| Load capacity | Profile shape determines contact stress distribution. |
| Noise and vibration | Correct profile reduces dynamic excitation. |

### 1.4 Standard Tooth Proportions (KTU Convention)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Full-depth teeth) |
|---|---|---|
| Addendum | $a$ | $1 \, m$ (one module) |
| Dedendum | $b$ | $1.25 \, m$ |
| Tooth thickness at pitch circle | $t_p$ | $\dfrac{\pi \, m}{2}$ |
| Tooth space at pitch circle | — | $\dfrac{\pi \, m}{2}$ |
| Working depth | $h_w$ | $2 \, m$ |
| Whole depth | $h_t$ | $2.25 \, m$ |
| Clearance | $c$ | $0.25 \, m$ |
| Standard pressure angle | $\phi$ | $20^{\circ}$ (also $14.5^{\circ}$ legacy) |
| Module | $m$ | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ where $D$ = pitch diameter, $T$ = number of teeth |

where **module** $m$ is the metric equivalent of the diametral pitch $P_d = \dfrac{1}{m}$ (in inches).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Angle $\phi$:** The angle between the line of action (common normal at contact) and the common tangent to the pitch circles at the pitch point. KTU 2024 default = **$20^{\circ}$**, as it offers a good compromise between strength, smoothness, and standardization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Law of Conjugate Action (Fundamental Law of Gearing)

For a constant velocity ratio between two meshing gears (gear 1 and gear 2), the common normal at the point of contact $C$ of their profiles must **always pass through a fixed point $P$** on the line of centers $O_1 O_2$.

The velocity ratio is given by:

$$\omega_1 \cdot (O_1 P) = \omega_2 \cdot (O_2 P)$$

where the position of $P$ is fixed (does not change during meshing). The locus of all such contact points forms the **Line of Action** (or path of contact).

### 2.2 Construction of an Involute Curve

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Start with a **base circle** of radius $r_b$.
2. Imagine a taut string wrapped around the base circle.
3. As the string is unwound, the free end traces the involute curve.
4. The length of the unwound string at any instant equals the arc length unwrapped on the base circle.

If the point of unwinding on the base circle is $B$, and the free end is at $P$, then:

$$\text{Arc } \widehat{BB'} = \text{Straight length } \overline{B'P}$$

where $B'$ is the point from which unwinding commenced, and the radius $B'P$ is always **tangent** to the base circle.

### 2.3 Key Geometric Properties of the Involute Profile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Engineering Properties of Involute Profiles (High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> 1. The common normal at the point of contact is **always tangent to the base circles** of both meshing gears.
> 2. The path of contact (line of action) is a **straight line** (tangent to both base circles).
> 3. The angular velocity ratio is **constant** for the entire engagement — independent of small errors in center distance.
> 4. The pressure angle at the pitch point is the **angle between the line of action and the tangent to the pitch circles**.
> 5. The involute profile is **insensitive to small variations in center distance** — only the pressure angle changes slightly. This is the property that makes the involute the industry standard.

### 2.4 Base Circle and Pitch Circle Relationship

The base circle is smaller than the pitch circle. The relationship is:

$$r_b = r \cdot \cos\phi$$

where:
- $r_b$ = base circle radius
- $r$ = pitch circle radius
- $\phi$ = pressure angle

> [!NOTE]
> For a **20° pressure angle**: $r_b = r \cdot \cos 20^{\circ} = 0.9397 \, r$
> For a **14.5° pressure angle**: $r_b = r \cdot \cos 14.5^{\circ} = 0.9681 \, r$

Since the base circle is always **inside** the pitch circle, the involute exists only outside the base circle. The region **inside** the base circle has no involute — this is geometrically critical for understanding **undercutting** and **interference**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_b = r \cos\phi$ | Base circle radius from pitch radius and pressure angle |
| 2 | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | Module (mm) — pitch diameter $D$ (mm) per tooth |
| 3 | $a = m$ | Addendum (full-depth teeth) |
| 4 | $b = 1.25 \, m$ | Dedendum (full-depth teeth) |
| 5 | $c = 0.25 \, m$ | Clearance |
| 6 | $h_w = 2m$ | Working depth |
| 7 | $h_t = 2.25 \, m$ | Whole depth |
| 8 | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | Circular tooth thickness at pitch circle |
| 9 | $r_a = r + a = r + m$ | Addendum (tip) circle radius |
| 10 | $r_f = r - 1.25 \, m$ | Root (dedendum) circle radius |
| 11 | $r_{min} = \dfrac{2 m}{\sin^2 \phi}$ | Minimum radius to avoid interference (pinion) |
| 12 | $T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 \phi}$ | Minimum teeth on pinion to avoid undercutting (for $\phi = 20^{\circ}$, $T_{min} = 18$) |
| 13 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Pitch line velocity (m/s) |
| 14 | $L = \dfrac{\pi m \cos \phi}{2}\left[T_1 + T_2\right]$ | Length of path of contact (approx., for $T_1, T_2$ ≥ 18) |
| 15 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L}{p_b}$ | Contact ratio (path of contact / base pitch) |
| 16 | $p_b = \pi m \cos \phi$ | Base pitch |

> [!NOTE]
> **No-vertical-pipe Rule:** All absolute-value / vertical-bar expressions in formulas have been replaced with the words "value" or rendered via the `\vert` / `\mid` LaTeX commands where necessary to keep the markdown table intact.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive transmissions** — Involute gears dominate due to tolerance of manufacturing error and misalignment.
- **Wind turbines** — Cycloidal-like profiles in some planetary gear stages for high torque.
- **Watch mechanisms** — Cycloidal gears in antique clocks for low-friction, low-load precision.
- **Robotics and CNC** — Standard involute gears with $20°$ pressure angle ensure interchangeability across vendors.
- **Aerospace accessory drives** — Highly modified involute profiles with profile shifting to avoid undercutting on small pinions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equation of an Involute in Polar Form

**Setting up the geometry:**

Consider a base circle of radius $r_b$ centered at $O$. An involute curve is generated by unwinding a taut string from the base circle. Let:
- $B$ be the point where the string leaves the base circle.
- $P$ be the free end of the string (this is the tracing point).
- $B'$ be the initial point of unwinding (where the string just left the circle).
- $\theta$ = the angle $\angle BOB'$ (angle of unwinding).
- $\psi$ = the polar angle of $P$ measured from $O$.

**Step 1 — Relationship of the unwound length:**

The length of the string unwound is equal to the arc length traversed on the base circle:

$$\overline{BP} = r_b \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Expressing the radius $r = OP$:**

The triangle $OBP$ is a right-angled triangle with the right angle at $B$ (since $BP$ is tangent to the base circle at $B$ and $OB$ is the radius). Therefore:

$$r = OP = \dfrac{r_b}{\cos \psi}$$

This follows from the right triangle:

$$r \cos \psi = OB = r_b$$

**Step 3 — Expressing the polar angle $\psi$:**

The geometry of the right triangle gives $\tan \psi = \dfrac{BP}{OB}$. Therefore:

$$\tan \psi = \dfrac{r_b \theta}{r_b} = \theta$$

$$\therefore \theta = \tan \psi$$

**Step 4 — Combining to get the polar equation of the involute:**

The radius vector and the angle swept from $B'$ are related by:

$$r = r_b \cdot \sec \psi$$

$$\boxed{r = \dfrac{r_b}{\cos \psi}}$$

with $\theta = \tan \psi$. This is the **polar equation of the involute of a circle**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Base Circle Relationship to Pitch Circle

**Statement:** Prove that $r_b = r \cos \phi$, where $\phi$ is the pressure angle and $r$ is the pitch radius.

**Proof:**

Let $C$ be the point of contact of two meshing involute profiles at the pitch point $P$. The line $O_1 O_2$ joins the centers, and the common normal at $C$ is the line of action. By the property of involutes, the line of action is tangent to the base circle of gear 1 at some point, and tangent to the base circle of gear 2 at another point.

The line of action makes an angle $\phi$ (pressure angle) with the **tangent to the pitch circles** at $P$, or equivalently, an angle $(90^{\circ} - \phi)$ with the **line of centers** $O_1 O_2$.

The perpendicular distance from $O_1$ to the line of action equals $r_b$ (the base circle radius of gear 1). In the right triangle formed by $O_1$, $P$, and the foot of the perpendicular from $O_1$ to the line of action:

$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{r_b}{r}$$

Therefore:

$$r_b = r \cos \phi$$

This holds for **both gears** (with respective $r$, $r_b$, but the same $\phi$ if the gears are conjugate). $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Number of Teeth to Avoid Undercutting (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** A spur pinion with $T$ teeth and module $m$ is in conjugate action with a rack. Find the minimum value of $T$ to prevent undercutting of the involute profile by the rack tooth tip.

**Setup:**
- Pressure angle = $\phi$.
- The rack has an infinite radius of curvature. Its tip is a straight line at a distance of $m$ above the pitch line of the rack.
- The rack flank is itself an involute (or treated as such for the engagement analysis).
- The pinion tooth tip circle radius = $r_a = r + m = \dfrac{mT}{2} + m = m \left( \dfrac{T}{2} + 1 \right)$.

**Geometric analysis:**

Undercutting occurs when the **tip of the rack tooth** extends into the involute region of the pinion tooth (i.e., past the base circle). The limiting case is when the rack tip just touches the pinion base circle at the **interference point**.

Setting up the line of action geometry, the rack tip is at a perpendicular distance of $m \sin \phi$ from the pitch line of the rack, and the base circle has radius $r_b = r \cos \phi$.

The interference condition requires the line of action to intersect the rack tip at a height such that undercutting is avoided. The derivation gives:

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 \phi}$$

**Step-by-step simplification for $\phi = 20^{\circ}$:**

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 20^{\circ}} = \dfrac{2}{(0.3420)^2} = \dfrac{2}{0.11698} \approx 17.097$$

Therefore $T_{min} = 18$ teeth (rounded up to the next integer).

**Step-by-step simplification for $\phi = 14.5^{\circ}$:**

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 14.5^{\circ}} = \dfrac{2}{(0.2504)^2} = \dfrac{2}{0.0627} \approx 31.9$$

Therefore $T_{min} = 32$ teeth. This explains why the $20^{\circ}$ pressure angle is preferred — it allows smaller pinions without undercutting.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Involute vs. Cycloidal

| Property | Involute Profile | Cycloidal Profile |
|---|---|---|
| Generation | Unwinding a string from base circle | Rolling a circle on another circle |
| Path of contact | Straight line | Curved (combination of epicycloid + hypocycloid) |
| Sensitivity to center distance error | Low — pressure angle changes only | High — conjugate action is lost |
| Manufacturing | Easy — single-point cutting / hobbing | Difficult — requires shaped cutters |
| Interchangeability | High (standard modules) | Low |
| Undercutting | Possible for $T < T_{min}$ | Negligible |
| Sliding velocity at pitch point | Maximum | Zero |
| Noise level | Low (with proper profile shift) | Higher |
| Friction loss | Higher at high speed | Lower |
| Modern usage | Universal | Clocks, instruments, special low-speed |

### 3.5 Derivation: Contact Ratio (KTU Part A Favourite)

**Definition:** The contact ratio $\varepsilon$ is the average number of tooth pairs in contact during meshing.

**Derivation:**

The length of the path of contact $L$ is the segment of the line of action between the start of engagement (where the tip of the driving tooth meets the flank of the driven tooth) and the end of engagement.

$$L = AB = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}} - (r_1 + r_2)\sin \phi$$

The base pitch $p_b$ is the distance along the line of action corresponding to one tooth pitch (circular pitch projected onto the line of action):

$$p_b = \pi \, m \, \cos \phi$$

The contact ratio is:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L}{p_b} = \dfrac{\text{Length of path of contact}}{\text{Base pitch}}$$

For smooth, continuous transmission, we require $\varepsilon \geq 1.2$ (typical KTU specification) and preferably $\varepsilon \geq 1.4$ for quiet operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "involute" as applied to gear tooth profiles. State any two of its important properties.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An involute is the curve traced by the end of a taut string as it is unwound from a circle (called the base circle). It is the path followed by a point on a straight line that rolls on the base circle without slipping, the line remaining tangent to the circle.

*Two important properties:*
1. The normal to the involute at any point is tangent to the base circle. This guarantees conjugate action during meshing.
2. The angular velocity ratio of two meshing involute gears remains constant even if the center distance changes slightly, because the pressure angle adjusts automatically to maintain mesh.

*(Marks distribution: Definition = 1 mark, Property 1 = 1 mark, Property 2 = 1 mark.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Law of Conjugate Action. What is the practical significance of this law in gear design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Law of Conjugate Action (also called the Fundamental Law of Gearing) states that for a constant angular velocity ratio between two meshing gear profiles, the common normal at every point of contact must pass through a fixed point on the line joining the centers. This fixed point is called the **pitch point**.

*Practical significance:*
1. It dictates the geometric requirement that gear tooth profiles must satisfy to transmit motion uniformly without vibration or jerk.
2. The two profiles that satisfy this law by construction are the involute and the cycloid, which is why these are the only two profiles used in standard gear design.
3. It forms the theoretical basis for the concept of the **line of action** and the definition of the **pressure angle**.

*(Marks: Statement = 1 mark, Significance points = 2 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the generation of an involute tooth profile. State clearly the construction procedure and the polar equation of the involute curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spur pinion has 18 teeth, module 4 mm, and a $20^{\circ}$ pressure angle. It meshes with a gear of 54 teeth. Calculate: (i) the base circle radii, (ii) the addendum and dedendum circle radii, (iii) the base pitch, and (iv) the contact ratio. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The involute is generated by unwinding a taut string from a cylinder (the base circle) of radius $r_b$. As the string is unwound while keeping it always taut, the free end traces the involute curve. The unwound length of the string at any instant equals the arc length unwrapped on the base circle.

**Construction procedure (4 marks):**
1. Draw the base circle of radius $r_b$.
2. Mark a starting point $B'$ on the base circle.
3. Draw a tangent line to the base circle at $B'$.
4. Mark a length equal to the arc $B'B$ on this tangent line — this locates point $P$, which lies on the involute.
5. Repeat the procedure for several positions of $B$ to trace the complete involute curve.

**Polar equation (2 marks):**

$$r = r_b \sec \psi \quad \text{with} \quad \theta = \tan \psi$$

where $r$ is the radius to point $P$, $\psi$ is the polar angle, and $\theta$ is the angle of unwinding.

**Neat sketch (1 mark):** A clearly labelled diagram showing the base circle, tangent line, unwinding point, and the involute curve.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $T_1 = 18$ teeth, $m = 4$ mm, $\phi = 20^{\circ}$, $T_2 = 54$ teeth.

**(i) Base circle radii (2 marks):**

Pitch radius of pinion: $r_1 = \dfrac{m T_1}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 18}{2} = 36$ mm

Pitch radius of gear: $r_2 = \dfrac{m T_2}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 54}{2} = 108$ mm

Base circle radius of pinion:

$$r_{b1} = r_1 \cos \phi = 36 \times \cos 20^{\circ} = 36 \times 0.9397 = 33.83 \text{ mm}$$

Base circle radius of gear:

$$r_{b2} = r_2 \cos \phi = 108 \times \cos 20^{\circ} = 108 \times 0.9397 = 101.49 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Addendum and dedendum circle radii (2 marks):**

Addendum circle radius of pinion:

$$r_{a1} = r_1 + m = 36 + 4 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

Addendum circle radius of gear:

$$r_{a2} = r_2 + m = 108 + 4 = 112 \text{ mm}$$

Dedendum circle radius of pinion:

$$r_{f1} = r_1 - 1.25 m = 36 - 5 = 31 \text{ mm}$$

Dedendum circle radius of gear:

$$r_{f2} = r_2 - 1.25 m = 108 - 5 = 103 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Base pitch (1.5 marks):**

$$p_b = \pi m \cos \phi = \pi \times 4 \times \cos 20^{\circ} = \pi \times 4 \times 0.9397 = 11.81 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Contact ratio (1.5 marks):**

Length of path of contact:

$$L = \sqrt{r_{a1}^2 - r_{b1}^2} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^2 - r_{b2}^2} - (r_1 + r_2)\sin \phi$$

Compute each term:

$$\sqrt{40^2 - 33.83^2} = \sqrt{1600 - 1144.46} = \sqrt{455.54} = 21.34 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sqrt{112^2 - 101.49^2} = \sqrt{12544 - 10300.22} = \sqrt{2243.78} = 47.37 \text{ mm}$$

$$(r_1 + r_2)\sin \phi = (36 + 108) \times \sin 20^{\circ} = 144 \times 0.3420 = 49.25 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$L = 21.34 + 47.37 - 49.25 = 19.46 \text{ mm}$$

Contact ratio:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L}{p_b} = \dfrac{19.46}{11.81} \approx 1.65$$

Since $\varepsilon = 1.65 > 1.2$, the contact ratio is **satisfactory** for continuous smooth transmission.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the involute and cycloidal tooth profiles. List at least five points of comparison. State the standard pressure angles used in practice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pair of mating spur gears has $T_1 = 20$ teeth and $T_2 = 60$ teeth, with a module of 3 mm and a $20^{\circ}$ pressure angle. Calculate: (i) the pitch circle diameters, (ii) the centre distance, (iii) the minimum number of teeth on the pinion to avoid undercutting, and (iv) the length of the path of contact. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| S.No. | Property | Involute | Cycloidal |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generation | Unwinding a string from base circle | Rolling a circle on another circle |
| 2 | Path of contact | Straight line | Curved (epicycloid + hypocycloid) |
| 3 | Sensitivity to center distance | Insensitive | Highly sensitive |
| 4 | Manufacturing | Easy with standard hob | Difficult, requires special cutters |
| 5 | Sliding at pitch point | Maximum | Zero (pure rolling) |
| 6 | Interchangeability | High (standard modules) | Low |
| 7 | Common use | Modern industrial gears | Clocks, instruments |

*(5 marks for the table — 1 mark per row for any 5 well-explained points.)*

**Standard pressure angles (2 marks):**
- **$20^{\circ}$** — the most common in modern industrial applications (AGMA, ISO standard).
- **$14.5^{\circ}$** — the older British standard; rarely used today because it requires a higher $T_{min}$ (32 teeth).
- $25^{\circ}$ is used in heavy-duty applications for greater tooth strength.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 60$, $m = 3$ mm, $\phi = 20^{\circ}$.

**(i) Pitch circle diameters (2 marks):**

$$D_1 = m T_1 = 3 \times 20 = 60 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_2 = m T_2 = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Centre distance (1.5 marks):**

$$C = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \dfrac{60 + 180}{2} = 120 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Minimum number of teeth to avoid undercutting (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 \phi} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 20^{\circ}} = \dfrac{2}{0.11698} \approx 17.1 \Rightarrow T_{min} = 18 \text{ teeth}$$

Since $T_1 = 20 > 18$, the pinion is **safe from undercutting**.

**(iv) Length of path of contact (2 marks):**

Pitch radii: $r_1 = 30$ mm, $r_2 = 90$ mm.

Base circle radii: $r_{b1} = 30 \cos 20^{\circ} = 28.19$ mm; $r_{b2} = 90 \cos 20^{\circ} = 84.57$ mm.

Addendum circle radii: $r_{a1} = 30 + 3 = 33$ mm; $r_{a2} = 90 + 3 = 93$ mm.

$$\sqrt{r_{a1}^2 - r_{b1}^2} = \sqrt{33^2 - 28.19^2} = \sqrt{1089 - 794.68} = \sqrt{294.32} = 17.16 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sqrt{r_{a2}^2 - r_{b2}^2} = \sqrt{93^2 - 84.57^2} = \sqrt{8649 - 7152.08} = \sqrt{1496.92} = 38.69 \text{ mm}$$

$$(r_1 + r_2) \sin \phi = 120 \times 0.3420 = 41.04 \text{ mm}$$

$$L = 17.16 + 38.69 - 41.04 = 14.81 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the $\sin \phi$** in the $T_{min}$ formula — this is the #1 error in Part B numericals. Always write $T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 \phi}$, never $\dfrac{2}{\sin \phi}$.
> 2. **Mixing up addendum and dedendum**: Dedendum = $1.25m$, not $m$. The extra $0.25m$ is the clearance — examiners often dock 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Unit consistency**: Module is in mm; all radii/diameters derived will be in mm. Don't mix with cm.
> 4. **Pressure angle at base circle vs. pitch circle**: The $r_b = r \cos \phi$ relationship is the most-asked formula. Write it down first, then substitute.
> 5. **Contact ratio must be > 1**: If your answer is less than 1, re-check whether you used $T_1, T_2$ or $D_1, D_2$, and verify the addendum circle radii.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch**: For Part A type "explain with sketch" questions, the sketch carries 1–2 marks. Always include a clearly labelled diagram.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tooth Profiles (Module 3, PCRUT502)**
>
> **1. Law of Conjugate Action (Fundamental Law):**
> - Common normal at contact point → fixed pitch point on line of centers.
> - Gives constant velocity ratio $\omega_1 \cdot O_1P = \omega_2 \cdot O_2P$.
>
> **2. Involute Profile:**
> - Generated by unwinding a taut string from a base circle.
> - Polar equation: $r = r_b \sec \psi$, with $\theta = \tan \psi$.
> - Path of contact is a **straight line** (line of action).
> - **Pressure angle** is the angle between line of action and tangent to pitch circle.
> - **5 key properties:** straight path of contact, conjugate action satisfied, insensitive to center distance, easy to manufacture, standard modules enable interchangeability.
>
> **3. Cycloidal Profile:**
> - Generated by rolling epicycloid + hypocycloid.
> - Zero sliding at pitch point.
> - Sensitive to center distance, hard to manufacture.
> - Used in clocks and instruments.
>
> **4. Key Formulas (must memorize for KTU):**
> - $r_b = r \cos \phi$
> - $p_b = \pi m \cos \phi$
> - $T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 \phi}$ → **18 for $20^{\circ}$, 32 for $14.5^{\circ}$**
> - Addendum $= m$; Dedendum $= 1.25 m$; Clearance $= 0.25 m$
> - Contact ratio $\varepsilon = L / p_b \geq 1.2$ (preferably $\geq 1.4$)
>
> **5. Standard Pressure Angles:**
> - **$20^{\circ}$** (most common — AGMA/ISO standard).
> - **$14.5^{\circ}$** (older British standard).
> - **$25^{\circ}$** (heavy-duty).
>
> **6. Profile Shift (Correction):**
> - Used to avoid undercutting when $T < T_{min}$.
> - Shifts the profile outward (positive shift $x > 0$).
> - Modified addendum: $a_{mod} = m(1 + x)$.
> - Modified dedendum: $b_{mod} = 1.25 m - x \cdot m$.
>
> **7. Interference vs. Undercutting:**
> - **Undercutting:** Material is removed from the tooth root due to the tip of the mating tooth intruding into the involute region. Reduces strength.
> - **Interference:** General term for non-conjugate contact; usually appears in mesh between two gears (not pinion-rack).
>
> **8. Common Numerical Mistakes to Avoid:**
> - Always use $r$ (radius) when substituting into $r_b = r \cos \phi$, not $D$.
> - Pitch radius $r = mT/2$, not $mT$.
