> - Pressure angle at pitch point ≠ pressure angle at any other point on the line of action.
> - Length of path of contact $L$ is a scalar length, always positive; check signs in final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials of Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials of Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gear Materials** are specifically engineered metallic, non-metallic, or composite substances selected for the manufacturing of gear teeth, possessing a balanced combination of high surface hardness, core toughness, fatigue strength, wear resistance, and dimensional stability to transmit motion and power reliably under varying load and speed conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):** "Gears are power transmission elements, and the material chosen must withstand complex loading conditions including bending fatigue, contact fatigue (pitting), scuffing, and wear, while maintaining dimensional accuracy under operational thermal and mechanical stresses."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the gears inside a heavy-duty truck's gearbox versus the tiny plastic gears inside a hand-cranked pencil sharpener. The truck gears must endure **enormous cyclic impact loads** and **continuous sliding-rolling contact**, much like the bones of a marathon runner. They need to be **hard on the outside (teeth surface)** to resist wear, but **tough on the inside (core)** to absorb shocks without fracturing.

In contrast, the plastic gears in the pencil sharpener only transmit tiny forces at low speeds with no shocks — like the soft cartilage in a child's finger. The material must simply be **easy to mold, quiet, and self-lubricating**.

This contrast illustrates that gear material selection is fundamentally a **trade-off study** between strength, manufacturability, cost, noise, weight, and operating environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Requirement:** A gear is essentially a *spring-loaded rolling-sliding contact element*. Therefore, its material must satisfy the **"Hard Surface, Tough Core"** rule, analogous to a blacksmith quenching a sword — soft and flexible inside, glass-hard at the cutting edge.

### 1.3 Classification of Gear Materials — Overview

Gear materials broadly fall into four primary families, each with distinct engineering utility:

| Family | Example Materials | Typical Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Ferrous Metals** | Cast Iron, Plain Carbon Steel, Alloy Steel | Industrial gearboxes, automotive transmissions |
| **Non-Ferrous Metals** | Bronze, Brass, Aluminium Alloys | Worm gears, lightweight mechanisms |
| **Non-Metallic Materials** | Nylon, Phenolic Resin, Polycarbonate, Delrin | Low-load, quiet, corrosion-resistant applications |
| **Composite / Sintered Materials** | Powder Metallurgy (P/M) gears, Fiber-Reinforced Polymers | Mass-produced, self-lubricating gears |

### 1.4 Essential Properties Required for Gear Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The property list below is a **favourite 3-mark question**. Memorize the order.

1. **High Surface Hardness** — Resists wear, pitting, and scoring.
2. **High Core Toughness / Impact Strength** — Resists tooth fracture under shock loads.
3. **High Fatigue Strength (Bending & Contact)** — Survives millions of load cycles.
4. **High Wear Resistance** — Minimizes surface degradation.
5. **Good Machinability** — Allows accurate tooth profile cutting.
6. **Dimensional Stability** — Resists thermal distortion.
7. **Availability and Cost-effectiveness** — Industrial viability.
8. **Compatibility with Lubricant** — Reduces friction and scuffing.
9. **Heat Treatability** — Allows case-hardening or through-hardening.
10. **Low Notch Sensitivity** — Resists stress concentration at tooth root.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ferrous Metal Gear Materials — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Cast Iron

Cast iron is one of the oldest and most economical gear materials, particularly suited for **large-diameter, low-to-medium speed gears** such as those found in rolling mills, cranes, and mining equipment.

**Why Cast Iron?**
- Excellent **vibration damping capacity** (≈ 5 to 10× better than steel) → quieter operation.
- Good **compressive strength** with reasonable wear resistance.
- Castability into complex tooth geometries.
- Self-lubricating due to graphite flakes.

**Variants Used:**
- **Gray Cast Iron (FC 200, FC 250, FC 300):** Contains graphite flakes. Grades are classified by tensile strength in MPa. Used for lightly loaded gears, large gears.
- **Pearlitic Cast Iron:** Higher strength due to pearlitic matrix.
- **Nodular (Ductile) Cast Iron (FCD 450, FCD 550):** Spheroidal graphite imparts ductility and impact resistance. Used in heavy-duty gears.
- **White Cast Iron & Chilled Cast Iron:** Used where very hard surface is required; chill casting produces a hard, abrasion-resistant rim.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chilled Cast Iron Gears:** Produced by rapidly cooling the tooth surface (chill) against metal inserts in the mould, creating a **hard surface (≥ 400 BHN)** with a tough grey iron core. This is a classic "hard-surface, tough-core" implementation.

**Limitations:**
- Brittle (low impact resistance except for ductile iron).
- Poor suitability for high-speed gears due to lower fatigue limit.
- Difficult to heat-treat.

#### 2.1.2 Plain Carbon Steels

Plain carbon steels are the **workhorse of gear manufacturing**. The carbon content directly controls hardness and strength.

**Sub-categories:**

| Type | Carbon % | Condition | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Carbon Steel | 0.15 – 0.25% | Case-hardened | Pinions (small gears) |
| Medium Carbon Steel | 0.25 – 0.50% | Quenched & Tempered | Heavy-duty transmission gears |
| High Carbon Steel | 0.50 – 0.65% | Hardened & Tempered | Industrial gears, machine tools |

**Standard Grades Used:**
- **EN8, EN9, EN19 (AISI 1040, 1050, 4140):** Medium carbon steels for through-hardened gears.
- **EN36, 20MnCr5, 16MnCr5:** Low carbon case-hardening steels.

**Heat Treatment Methods:**
- **Through-Hardening:** Heating the entire gear to austenitizing temperature (≈ 850–870°C), quenching in oil/water, then tempering. Yields uniform hardness (typically 250–400 BHN).
- **Case-Hardening:**
  - **Carburizing:** Heating in carbon-rich environment (≈ 900°C) → high carbon surface (0.8–1.0% C) → quenching → hard case (~58–62 HRC) with tough low-carbon core.
  - **Nitriding:** Heating in ammonia gas (≈ 500–550°C) → nitrogen diffuses → extremely hard case (~60–65 HRC) without quenching (minimal distortion).
  - **Cyaniding & Carbonitriding:** Hybrid processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Pinion is Harder than Gear:** In a meshing pair, the pinion has fewer teeth and thus undergoes **more load cycles per revolution** than the mating gear. Therefore, the pinion material is always selected to be **5–10 BHN harder** than the gear material to equalize wear — this is a fundamental design rule in KTU exam problems.

#### 2.1.3 Alloy Steels

Alloy steels incorporate elements like **Nickel (Ni), Chromium (Cr), Molybdenum (Mo), Vanadium (V), Manganese (Mn)** to enhance:
- Hardenability (deeper hardening).
- Toughness.
- Fatigue strength.
- High-temperature performance.

**Common Grades:**
- **EN19 (AISI 4140):** 1% Cr, 0.2% Mo — high strength gears, shafts.
- **EN24 (AISI 4340):** Ni-Cr-Mo — heavy-duty aerospace and automotive gears.
- **EN31 (AISI 52100):** High-Cr ball-bearing steel — for heavily loaded precision gears.
- **18CrNiMo7-6, 20NiCrMo2-2:** Premium case-hardening steels for high-stress gears (helicopter, automotive, wind turbine).

#### 2.1.4 Stainless Steels

Used only in **corrosive environments** (marine, chemical, food processing). Grades like **AISI 304, 316, 17-4 PH**. Their lower hardness and high cost limit them to niche applications.

### 2.2 Non-Ferrous Metal Gear Materials

#### 2.2.1 Bronze

**Bronze is the classical worm gear material** due to its excellent compatibility with hardened steel worms.

| Bronze Type | Composition | Property | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phosphor Bronze** | Cu + Sn + P | High strength, wear resistance | Worm wheels, heavy-duty gears |
| **Aluminium Bronze** | Cu + Al (9–12%) | High strength, corrosion resistance | Marine gears |
| **Manganese Bronze** | Cu + Zn + Mn + Al | Tough, shock-resistant | Heavy worm wheels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** A **hardened steel worm (40–60 HRC)** running against a **bronze worm wheel** is the standard pairing because bronze is soft enough to embed any abrasive particles, protecting the costly worm from scoring.

#### 2.2.2 Brass

**Brass (Cu + Zn)** is used for **lightly loaded gears** in instruments, clocks, and small mechanisms. It is easy to cast, machine, and exhibits good corrosion resistance. However, its strength is lower than bronze.

#### 2.2.3 Aluminium Alloys

Used in **aerospace and lightweight applications** (e.g., helicopter rotor gears). Aluminium gears are often **anodized** for surface hardness. Limitations include low fatigue strength and high thermal expansion.

#### 2.2.4 Cast Iron vs. Steel vs. Bronze — When to Use

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Matrix (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Low cost + vibration damping** → Cast Iron
> - **High strength + high speed + heavy load** → Alloy Steel (case-hardened)
> - **Worm gear pair** → Hardened Steel Worm + Bronze Wheel
> - **Corrosion resistance** → Stainless Steel or Bronze
> - **Lightweight** → Aluminium Alloy

### 2.3 Non-Metallic Gear Materials

These materials have gained massive industrial relevance in **light-load, high-speed, or noise-sensitive** applications.

#### 2.3.1 Nylon (Polyamide – PA 6, PA 66)

- **Properties:** Self-lubricating, quiet, corrosion-proof, lightweight, absorbs shock.
- **Limitations:** Absorbs moisture (dimensional change), low operating temperature (≈ 120°C).
- **Reinforced Nylon:** Glass-filled (15–30%) for higher strength.
- **Application:** Washing machines, printers, small appliances, textile machinery.

#### 2.3.2 Phenolic Resin (Bakelite)

- Cotton-fabric, paper, or asbestos-reinforced phenolic laminates.
- **Properties:** High strength, low noise, self-lubricating.
- **Application:** Power tool gears, automotive timing gears (older designs).

#### 2.3.3 Polycarbonate (PC), Delrin (POM), Acetal

- High dimensional accuracy.
- Used in precision instruments, toys, business machines.

#### 2.3.4 Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and UHMWPE

- Ultra-low friction, but very low load capacity.
- Often used as **gear coatings** rather than bulk material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crowned Teeth for Non-Metallic Gears:** Plastic gears expand more than steel pinions due to moisture and temperature. To prevent binding, plastic gears are often given **larger tip relief (crowning)** and **increased tooth clearance** (10× the standard allowance for steel gears).

### 2.4 Sintered (Powder Metallurgy) Gears

**Powder Metallurgy (P/M)** gears are made by:
1. Compacting metal powder (Fe, Fe-Cu, Fe-Ni) in a precision die.
2. Sintering at ≈ 1100–1300°C in a controlled atmosphere.

**Advantages:**
- Near-net shape → minimal machining.
- Self-lubricating grades available (impregnated with oil).
- Mass-production economy (e.g., car oil pump gears).
- Porosity absorbs vibration and noise.

**Limitations:**
- Lower strength and impact resistance than wrought steel.
- Limited to smaller gears (size constraint from press capacity).

### 2.5 Surface Treatments and Coatings for Gears

To enhance surface properties without altering bulk toughness:

| Treatment | Effect | Typical Case Depth | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Induction Hardening** | Localized surface heating → quenching | 1.5 – 4 mm | Large industrial gears |
| **Flame Hardening** | Oxy-fuel flame heating | 2 – 6 mm | Large cast iron/steel gears |
| **Shot Peening** | Induces compressive surface stress | 0.2 – 0.5 mm | Improves fatigue life |
| **Black Oxide / Phosphate Coating** | Mild wear resistance, corrosion protection | < 10 µm | Storage, light duty |
| **Chrome Plating** | Very hard surface | 10 – 100 µm | Old automotive gears |
| **DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon)** | Ultra-hard, low-friction coating | 1 – 5 µm | High-end racing gears |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

| Property / Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum BHN for cast iron gear | $\text{BHN} \geq 1.5 \times \text{circular pitch (mm)}$ | BHN (Brinell) |
| Minimum BHN for steel gear | $\text{BHN} \geq 3.0 \times \text{circular pitch (mm)}$ | BHN |
| Pinion hardness – Gear hardness | $\text{BHN}_{\text{pinion}} \geq \text{BHN}_{\text{gear}} + 10$ to 15 | Equalizes wear |
| Gear material density (Steel) | $\rho = 7.83 \times 10^{-6}$ | kg/mm³ |
| Gear material density (Cast Iron) | $\rho = 7.20 \times 10^{-6}$ | kg/mm³ |
| Gear material density (Bronze) | $\rho = 8.80 \times 10^{-6}$ | kg/mm³ |
| Gear material density (Aluminium) | $\rho = 2.70 \times 10^{-6}$ | kg/mm³ |
| Standard case depth for carburizing | $t = 0.05 \times m + 0.3$ | mm (m = module) |
| Nitriding case depth | $t = 0.02 \times m + 0.3$ | mm |
| Module-to-pitch relation | $m = p / \pi$ | mm |
| Allowable bending stress (standard steel) | $\sigma_b \approx 0.30 \times \text{Ultimate Tensile Strength}$ | MPa |
| Lewis form factor (qualitative) | $Y = \dfrac{2x}{3m}$ (for 20° full-depth teeth) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbol in Tables:** All absolute-value or set-membership notations are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. For example, modulus is denoted $m \vert p$ instead of $m \vert p$ — broken — using `$m \mid p$`.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Gearbox:** Alloy steel (e.g., 20MnCr5) case-hardened pinions mating with through-hardened steel gears.
- **Wind Turbine Planetary Gears:** Case-hardened 18CrNiMo7-6 with ground teeth, hardness ≈ 60 HRC.
- **Washing Machine Gearbox:** Glass-filled nylon gears driven by induction motor.
- **Aircraft Engine Gears:** Vacuum-melted alloy steels (e.g., AISI 9310) for extreme fatigue life.
- **Clock and Watch Gears:** Brass or hard-rolled stainless steel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — The Buckingham Wear Equation (for Material Selection of Gears)

This derivation justifies **why a harder material reduces wear** and gives a quantitative basis for the **pinion-hardness-greater-than-gear** rule.

**Step 1 — State the empirical wear law.**
The wear volume $V$ per unit sliding distance is governed by the Archard equation:

$$V = \dfrac{k \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

Where:
- $V$ = worn volume (mm³)
- $k$ = dimensionless wear coefficient (material property)
- $W$ = normal load (N)
- $L$ = sliding distance (mm)
- $H$ = hardness of the softer surface (Pa)

**Step 2 — Express wear in terms of linear wear depth.**
For a contact area $A$, the linear wear depth $h = V / A$:

$$h = \dfrac{k \cdot p \cdot L}{H}$$

Where $p = W / A$ is the contact pressure (Pa).

**Step 3 — Express sliding distance in revolutions.**
For a meshing gear pair, the sliding distance per revolution of the pinion is proportional to the pitch line velocity. Over $N$ revolutions:

$$L = 2\pi r_p \cdot N$$

**Step 4 — Number of cycles for pinion vs. gear.**
In a meshing pair with $N_p$ teeth on the pinion and $N_g$ teeth on the gear, the **number of load applications** on the pinion tooth is greater than on the gear tooth by the tooth ratio:

$$N_{\text{cycles,pinion}} = \dfrac{N_g}{N_p} \times N_{\text{cycles,gear}}$$

**Step 5 — Equate wear rates of pinion and gear.**
To ensure both gears wear out simultaneously (i.e., equal linear wear depth $h_p = h_g$):

$$\dfrac{k_p \cdot p \cdot L_p}{H_p} = \dfrac{k_g \cdot p \cdot L_g}{H_g}$$

Since sliding distance per tooth is symmetric, and the pressure is the same on both bodies, simplification yields:

$$\dfrac{k_p}{H_p} = \dfrac{k_g}{H_g}$$

Assuming $k_p \approx k_g$ (same family of materials), we obtain:

$$H_p = H_g \quad \text{or} \quad H_p \geq H_g + 10\text{ BHN}$$

The extra 10 BHN is the practical safety margin to account for the **higher number of cycles** experienced by the pinion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This is the **formal justification** for the rule "Pinion is harder than the gear by 10–15 BHN", which is a recurring KTU question.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Case Depth Selection

**Step 1 — Define required case depth.**
The case must be deep enough to:
- Resist Hertzian contact stress (sub-surface shear).
- Resist fatigue crack initiation from inclusions at the case-core interface.

**Step 2 — Hertzian contact shear location.**
For two meshing spur gears, the maximum shear stress occurs at a depth $z$ below the surface:

$$z \approx 0.48 \cdot b$$

Where $b$ is the semi-width of the line contact (≈ half tooth face width).

**Step 3 — Empirical case depth formula (Lewis-AGMA basis).**
For a gear of module $m$ (mm), the recommended case depth $t$ is:

$$t = 0.05 \, m + 0.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
For a gear with module $m = 5$ mm:

$$t = 0.05 \times 5 + 0.3 = 0.55 \text{ mm}$$

The case must therefore be at least **0.55 mm** deep, with the core retaining a hardness of ≈ 30–35 HRC for toughness.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Decision Table

| Operating Condition | Recommended Material | Heat Treatment | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low speed, low load, low cost | Gray Cast Iron (FC 200) | As-cast | Cheap, vibration damping |
| Medium speed, medium load | Medium Carbon Steel (EN8) | Quenched & Tempered | Balanced strength-toughness |
| High speed, high load (automotive) | Case-hardening steel (20MnCr5) | Carburized & Quenched | Hard case + tough core |
| High cycle fatigue (helicopter gears) | Vacuum-melted alloy steel (AISI 9310) | Carburized, shot-peened | Maximum fatigue strength |
| Worm wheel | Phosphor bronze (Cu-Sn-P) | Sand-cast | Anti-scoring with steel worm |
| Corrosive environment (marine) | Aluminium bronze or stainless steel | Solution-treated | Corrosion resistance |
| Office/domestic quiet gears | Nylon 66 (glass-filled) | Injection moulded | Self-lubricating, silent |
| Mass-produced car oil-pump gear | Sintered Fe-Ni-Cu (P/M) | Sintered + sized | Near-net shape, low cost |
| High-precision instrument gear | Brass (Cu-Zn 60/40) | Hard-rolled | Dimensional accuracy, easy machining |

### 3.4 Quantitative Comparison — Mechanical Properties of Common Gear Materials

| Material | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Hardness | Density (g/cm³) | Max Useful Temp (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gray Cast Iron (FC 250) | 250 | 180–220 BHN | 7.20 | 400 |
| Ductile Cast Iron (FCD 450) | 450 | 150–200 BHN | 7.10 | 400 |
| Plain Carbon Steel (0.4% C, Q&T) | 600–800 | 200–280 BHN | 7.85 | 500 |
| Alloy Steel (4140 Q&T) | 1000–1200 | 300–400 BHN | 7.85 | 550 |
| Carburized Steel (20MnCr5) | 1500 (core 700) | 58–62 HRC case | 7.85 | 550 |
| Nitrided Steel (EN41B) | 1200 | 60–65 HRC surface | 7.85 | 550 |
| Phosphor Bronze | 400–600 | 80–120 BHN | 8.80 | 250 |
| Aluminium Bronze | 600–800 | 150–200 BHN | 7.60 | 300 |
| Nylon 66 | 80 | R 115 (Rockwell) | 1.14 | 120 |
| Glass-filled Nylon 66 | 130 | R 120 | 1.30 | 150 |
| Sintered Iron (Fe-Cu) | 300 | 80 BHN | 6.50 | 400 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is alloy steel preferred over plain carbon steel for highly loaded gears?", answer: "Alloy steels (with Cr, Ni, Mo) have **higher hardenability**, allowing deeper through-hardening or case-hardening in thick sections, with reduced quenching distortion and improved fatigue strength."

### 3.5 Worked Example — Selection of Gear Material for an Industrial Gearbox

**Given:**
- Power = 22 kW
- Pinion speed = 1450 rpm
- Pinion teeth = 20
- Gear teeth = 60
- Application: 16 hours/day, shock-loaded cement mill drive
- Required life = 20,000 hours

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the velocity and load.**
Pitch line velocity $v = \dfrac{\pi d_p n_p}{60,000}$ (m/s)
With $m = 6$ mm, $d_p = 120$ mm:

$$v = \dfrac{\pi \times 120 \times 1450}{60{,}000} = 9.11 \text{ m/s}$$

Tangential load $W_T = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{22{,}000}{9.11} = 2415$ N

**Step 2 — Identify failure modes.**
Shock-loaded, long life → fatigue and pitting are dominant.
Noise is acceptable, cost is moderate.

**Step 3 — Material recommendation.**
**Pinion:** 20MnCr5 (case-hardening low-alloy steel) — Case hardened to **60 HRC**, core **30 HRC**.
**Gear:** EN9 (medium carbon steel) — Quenched and tempered to **280 BHN**.

**Step 4 — Pinion-hardness-greater-than-gear check.**

$$\text{Hardness}_{\text{pinion}} = 600 \text{ HV} \approx 580 \text{ BHN}$$

$$\text{Hardness}_{\text{gear}} = 280 \text{ BHN}$$

$$\Delta \text{BHN} = 580 - 280 = 300 \gg 10 \text{ BHN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Case depth check.**
$$t = 0.05 \times 6 + 0.3 = 0.6 \text{ mm (minimum)}$$
Specify $t = 0.8$ mm for safety.

**Step 6 — Final selection summary.**
This combination provides a high-load-capacity, long-life, and economical solution appropriate for a cement mill.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Gear Material Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Gear Material Selection Master Block] --> B[Ferrous Metals]
    A --> C[Non-Ferrous Metals]
    A --> D[Non-Metallic Materials]
    A --> E[Composite and Sintered Materials]

    B --> B1[Cast Iron Family]
    B --> B2[Plain Carbon Steel Family]
    B --> B3[Alloy Steel Family]
    B --> B4[Stainless Steel Family]

    B1 --> B1A[Gray Cast Iron FC 200]
    B1 --> B1B[Ductile Cast Iron FCD 450]
    B1 --> B1C[Chilled Cast Iron]
    B1 --> B1D[White Cast Iron]

    B2 --> B2A[Low Carbon 0.15 to 0.25 percent]
    B2 --> B2B[Medium Carbon 0.25 to 0.50 percent]
    B2 --> B2C[High Carbon 0.50 to 0.65 percent]

    B3 --> B3A[EN19 Chromium Molybdenum]
    B3 --> B3B[EN24 Nickel Chromium Molybdenum]
    B3 --> B3C[20MnCr5 Case Hardening]
    B3 --> B3D[18CrNiMo7-6 Premium Grade]

    C --> C1[Bronze Family]
    C --> C2[Brass Family]
    C --> C3[Aluminium Alloys]

    C1 --> C1A[Phosphor Bronze]
    C1 --> C1B[Aluminium Bronze]
    C1 --> C1C[Manganese Bronze]

    D --> D1[Thermoplastics]
    D --> D2[Thermosets]

    D1 --> D1A[Nylon 66]
    D1 --> D1B[Acetal POM Delrin]
    D1 --> D1C[Polycarbonate PC]
    D1 --> D1D[UHMWPE Ultra High Mol Weight]

    D2 --> D2A[Phenolic Resin Cotton Reinforced]
    D2 --> D2B[Phenolic Resin Fabric Reinforced]

    E --> E1[Sintered Powder Metallurgy]
    E --> E2[Fiber Reinforced Polymer]

    E1 --> E1A[Iron Copper Sintered]
    E1 --> E1B[Iron Nickel Sintered]
    E1 --> E1C[Bronze Powder Sintered]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Case Hardening Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Low Carbon Steel Gear Blank] --> B[Step 1 Machining Pre Hardening]
    B --> C[Step 2 Cleaning and Degreasing]
    C --> D[Step 3 Heating to 900 degrees Celsius]
    D --> E[Step 4 Carburizing in Carbon Rich Atmosphere]
    E --> F[Step 5 Soaking for Required Case Depth]
    F --> G[Step 6 Quenching in Oil or Water]
    G --> H[Step 7 Tempering at 200 degrees Celsius]
    H --> I[Step 8 Grinding of Tooth Profile]
    I --> J[Final Case Hardened Gear 60 HRC Surface]

    K[Alternative Path] -.-> D
    K --> L[Step 4A Nitriding at 520 degrees Celsius]
    L --> M[Step 5A Slow Cooling Without Quenching]
    M --> N[Step 6A Final Nitrided Gear 65 HRC Surface]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Pinion-Harder-than-Gear Wear Equalization Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Load on Tooth] --> B{Pinion or Gear}
    B -->|Pinion| C[Higher Number of Load Cycles]
    B -->|Gear| D[Lower Number of Load Cycles]
    C --> E[Pin material Harder 580 BHN]
    D --> F[Gear material Softer 280 BHN]
    E --> G[Wear Rate per Cycle Equalized]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Both Gears Reach End of Life Simultaneously]
    H --> I[Optimum Service Life and Cost]
```

### 4.4 Application-to-Material Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Gear Application Type] --> B{High Speed and High Load}
    A --> C{Moderate Load Industrial}
    A --> D{Low Load Silent Operation}
    A --> E{Worm Gear Pair}
    A --> F{Corrosive Environment}

    B -->|Yes| B1[Alloy Steel 20MnCr5 Carburized]
    C -->|Yes| C1[Medium Carbon Steel EN8 Quenched and Tempered]
    D -->|Yes| D1[Glass Filled Nylon 66]
    E -->|Yes| E1[Steel Worm and Phosphor Bronze Wheel]
    F -->|Yes| F1[Aluminium Bronze or Stainless Steel 17-4 PH]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:** List any **six essential properties** required for a gear material.

**Model Answer (6 points, 0.5 mark each):**
1. **High surface hardness** — to resist wear, pitting, and scoring.
2. **High core toughness and impact strength** — to absorb shock loads without tooth fracture.
3. **High fatigue strength (bending and contact)** — to survive millions of load cycles.
4. **Good wear resistance** — to minimize surface degradation over service life.
5. **Good machinability** — to allow accurate tooth profile generation.
6. **Heat-treatability** — to enable case-hardening or through-hardening for required hardness profile.
7. **Dimensional stability** — to maintain tooth geometry under thermal and mechanical stresses.
8. **Availability and economy** — for industrial viability.

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:** State the **rule for hardness relationship between pinion and gear** and explain why it is necessary.

**Model Answer:**
For a meshing gear pair, the pinion is always made **harder than the gear by at least 10 to 15 BHN**.

**Reason:**
- The pinion has fewer teeth than the gear, hence it undergoes a **greater number of load cycles per revolution**.
- To equalize the wear rates so that both gears reach the end of their service life simultaneously, the pinion is hardened to a higher value.
- This is given by:

$$\text{Hardness}_{\text{pinion}} \geq \text{Hardness}_{\text{gear}} + 10 \text{ to } 15 \text{ BHN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (3-mark loss):** Students often write only "pinion is harder than gear" without specifying the **magnitude (10–15 BHN)** or the **reason (cycle ratio equalization)**. Both are mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]:**

> **(a)** Classify gear materials in detail. Discuss the properties, applications, advantages, and limitations of **cast iron and steel** as gear materials. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain with neat sketches the various **heat treatment processes** used for gear materials. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution for Question A (a):**

**Classification of Gear Materials:**

Gear materials are classified into four major families:

**(1) Ferrous Metals:**
- Cast Iron (Gray, White, Chilled, Ductile)
- Plain Carbon Steel (Low, Medium, High Carbon)
- Alloy Steel (EN-series, case-hardening grades)
- Stainless Steel (corrosive applications)

**(2) Non-Ferrous Metals:**
- Bronze (Phosphor, Aluminium, Manganese)
- Brass
- Aluminium Alloys

**(3) Non-Metallic Materials:**
- Thermoplastics (Nylon, Acetal, Polycarbonate)
- Thermosets (Phenolic resin, fabric reinforced)

**(4) Composite / Sintered Materials:**
- Powder Metallurgy gears
- Fibre-reinforced polymers

**Cast Iron as Gear Material:**

*Types and Properties:*
- **Gray Cast Iron (FC 200, FC 250):** Compressive strength 600–1000 MPa, BHN 180–220, excellent vibration damping.
- **Chilled Cast Iron:** Hard surface (400+ BHN) and tough core, made by chill casting.
- **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron (FCD 450, FCD 550):** Tensile strength up to 550 MPa, with impact resistance, suitable for heavy-duty gears.

*Advantages:*
- Low cost and easy to cast into complex shapes.
- Excellent damping capacity → quiet operation.
- Good machinability (especially gray iron).
- Self-lubricating due to graphite flakes.

*Limitations:*
- Brittle (except ductile iron) → poor impact resistance.
- Low fatigue strength → unsuitable for high-speed gears.
- Difficult to heat-treat.

*Applications:* Rolling mill gears, crane gears, large industrial gears, mining machinery.

**Steel as Gear Material:**

*Types and Properties:*
- **Plain Carbon Steel (0.1–0.65% C):** Tensile strength 400–700 MPa (Q&T).
- **Alloy Steel (with Cr, Ni, Mo):** Tensile strength up to 1500 MPa after heat treatment.
- **Case-Hardening Steel (20MnCr5, 18CrNiMo7-6):** Surface hardness 60 HRC, core 30 HRC.

*Advantages:*
- Very high strength, toughness, and fatigue resistance.
- Heat-treatable to a wide range of hardness.
- Suitable for high-speed and high-load applications.
- Dimensional accuracy achievable.

*Limitations:*
- Higher cost than cast iron.
- Requires precise heat treatment.
- Lower damping → noisy operation.

*Applications:* Automotive transmission gears, aerospace, wind turbine, machine tool, industrial gearboxes.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Cast Iron | Steel |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Low | High |
| Strength | Moderate | High |
| Damping | Excellent | Poor |
| Machinability | Excellent | Moderate |
| Heat-treatability | Poor | Excellent |
| Speed capability | Low–Medium | High |
| Typical application | Large industrial | High-speed transmission |

---

**Solution for Question A (b):**

**Heat Treatment Processes for Gear Materials:**

**(1) Annealing:**
- Heating to 800–900°C, holding, then slow furnace cooling.
- **Purpose:** Softens material for machining, relieves internal stresses.
- Used for **rough machining of gear blanks** before final hardening.

**(2) Normalizing:**
- Heating to 870–950°C, holding, then air cooling.
- **Purpose:** Refines grain structure, improves machinability, homogenizes structure.
- Used for **medium carbon steel gears** as pre-treatment.

**(3) Hardening (Through-Hardening):**
- Heating to 850–870°C (austenitizing), then quenching in oil or water.
- **Purpose:** Achieves uniform hardness (250–400 BHN) throughout the cross-section.
- Followed by **tempering** to reduce brittleness.
- Used for **medium carbon steel gears (EN8, EN9, EN19)**.

**(4) Tempering:**
- Reheating the hardened gear to 200–600°C, holding, then cooling.
- **Purpose:** Reduces brittleness, achieves desired hardness-toughness balance.
- Tempering temperature determines final hardness: lower temperature → higher hardness, higher temperature → higher toughness.

**(5) Case-Hardening — Carburizing:**
- Heating to 900–950°C in carbon-rich medium (solid, liquid, or gas).
- Carbon diffuses into the surface (depth = $0.05m + 0.3$ mm).
- Then quenched and tempered.
- **Purpose:** Hard, wear-resistant case (58–62 HRC) with tough low-carbon core.
- Used for **low-carbon alloy steel gears (20MnCr5)**.

**(6) Case-Hardening — Nitriding:**
- Heating to 500–550°C in ammonia gas; nitrogen diffuses into surface.
- **No quenching required** → minimal distortion.
- **Purpose:** Extremely hard surface (60–65 HRC) with excellent wear and fatigue resistance.
- Used for **EN41B, 38CrMoAl** gears requiring high precision.

**(7) Induction Hardening:**
- High-frequency induction heating of tooth surface only, followed by water spray quenching.
- **Purpose:** Localized surface hardening (1.5–4 mm deep).
- Used for **large gears** that cannot be carburized easily.

**(8) Flame Hardening:**
- Oxy-fuel flame heating of tooth surface, followed by quenching.
- **Purpose:** Surface hardening (2–6 mm deep) for large gears.
- Suitable for cast iron and medium carbon steel.

**(9) Shot Peening:**
- Bombarding tooth surface with small steel shots.
- **Purpose:** Induces compressive residual stress → increases fatigue life by 30–50%.
- Often used as a **post-treatment after case hardening**.

**Schematic Diagram of Case-Hardened Tooth Cross-Section:**

```
        ←—————— Hard Case (58-62 HRC) ——————→
        |     ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓       |   ← Surface Hardness
        |     ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓       |   ← Case Depth (0.05m + 0.3mm)
        | ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ |   ← Transition Zone
        | ░░░░░░░░░░░ TOUGH CORE (30 HRC) ░░░ |   ← Core (Low Carbon)
        | ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ |
        ←——————— Gear Cross-Section ———————→
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (7-mark question):** Students frequently omit the **tempering step** when describing hardening. Hardening without tempering produces a brittle gear that cracks in service — examiner will deduct **2 marks** if tempering is missed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]:**

> **(a)** Discuss the suitability of **non-ferrous and non-metallic materials** for gears. Compare their properties with steel gears. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pair of spur gears is to transmit **15 kW at 720 rpm** of the pinion. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 54 teeth. The module is 5 mm. Recommend a suitable **gear material and heat treatment** for the pair, justifying your choice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution for Question B (a):**

**Non-Ferrous Materials for Gears:**

**(i) Bronze (Cu-Sn Alloy):**
- *Phosphor Bronze (Cu + 10% Sn + 0.5% P):* High strength, wear resistance.
- *Aluminium Bronze (Cu + 10% Al):* Strength up to 700 MPa, corrosion resistance.
- *Manganese Bronze:* Tough and shock-resistant.

*Suitability:* Bronze is the **classical worm wheel material** because:
- It is soft enough to embed abrasive particles, protecting the hardened steel worm.
- Has a low coefficient of friction against steel.
- Exhibits good conformability.

*Limitations:* Lower strength than steel, expensive, cannot be hardened significantly.

**(ii) Brass (Cu-Zn Alloy):**
- *Properties:* Easy to cast and machine, good corrosion resistance, moderate strength.
- *Suitability:* Used for **lightly loaded instrument gears**, clocks, watches, locks, and small mechanisms.
- *Limitations:* Not suitable for high-load or high-speed applications.

**(iii) Aluminium Alloys:**
- *Properties:* Lightweight (1/3 of steel), good thermal conductivity, can be anodized.
- *Suitability:* Used in **aerospace and racing applications** where weight is critical.
- *Limitations:* Low fatigue strength, high thermal expansion, low hardness.

**Non-Metallic Materials for Gears:**

**(i) Nylon (PA 66):**
- *Properties:* Self-lubricating, silent, lightweight, corrosion-proof.
- *Reinforced Nylon (Glass-filled 15–30%):* Higher strength and stiffness.
- *Suitability:* Washing machines, printers, toys, office equipment.
- *Limitations:* Moisture absorption (dimensional change), low temperature limit (120°C).

**(ii) Acetal / Delrin (POM):**
- *Properties:* High dimensional stability, low friction, high strength.
- *Suitability:* Precision gears in instruments, fuel pumps.

**(iii) Phenolic Resin (Cotton-fabric or Paper-reinforced):**
- *Properties:* High strength, low noise, self-lubricating.
- *Suitability:* Automotive timing gears, power tools, heavy-duty textile machinery.

**(iv) Polycarbonate (PC):**
- *Properties:* High impact strength, transparent, dimensionally stable.
- *Suitability:* Instrument gears, small consumer products.

**Comparison with Steel Gears:**

| Property | Non-Ferrous (Bronze) | Non-Metallic (Nylon) | Steel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | High (8.8 g/cm³) | Low (1.14 g/cm³) | High (7.85 g/cm³) |
| Tensile Strength | 400–600 MPa | 80–130 MPa | 600–1500 MPa |
| Hardness | 80–200 BHN | R 115–120 | 200–600 BHN |
| Wear Resistance | Moderate–Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| Noise Level | Low | Very Low | High |
| Operating Speed | Low–Medium | Low | High |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Lubrication Required | Yes | No (self-lubricating) | Yes |
| Thermal Limit | 250–300°C | 120–150°C | 500–550°C |

**Conclusion:**
- **Steel** dominates in **high-strength, high-speed** applications.
- **Bronze** is preferred for **worm gear wheels and corrosive environments**.
- **Non-metallic materials** are used where **low noise, no lubrication, and corrosion resistance** are essential, with low-to-moderate load capacities.

---

**Solution for Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- Power $P = 15$ kW
- Pinion speed $n_p = 720$ rpm
- Pinion teeth $Z_p = 18$
- Gear teeth $Z_g = 54$
- Module $m = 5$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate pitch circle diameters.**
$$d_p = m \cdot Z_p = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = m \cdot Z_g = 5 \times 54 = 270 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate pitch line velocity.**
$$v = \dfrac{\pi d_p n_p}{60{,}000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 90 \times 720}{60{,}000} = 3.39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate tangential load.**
$$W_T = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{3.39} = 4425 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Identify operating conditions.**
- Moderate speed (3.39 m/s)
- Moderate-to-high load (4.4 kN)
- Continuous industrial service
- Tooth ratio $i = 54/18 = 3$ → pinion experiences 3× more cycles

**Step 5 — Material recommendation.**

**Pinion (more cycles, smaller diameter):**
- **Material:** 20MnCr5 (low-carbon case-hardening alloy steel)
- **Heat treatment:** Carburizing at 920°C, oil quenching, tempering at 200°C
- **Surface hardness:** 58–62 HRC
- **Core hardness:** 30–35 HRC
- **Case depth:** $t = 0.05 \times 5 + 0.3 = 0.55$ mm → specify **0.8 mm**

**Gear (fewer cycles, larger diameter):**
- **Material:** EN9 (medium carbon steel, 0.5% C)
- **Heat treatment:** Quench and temper to obtain uniform hardness
- **Surface hardness:** 250–280 BHN (≈ 26 HRC)
- **Heat treatment sequence:** Heat to 850°C → oil quench → temper at 450°C

**Step 6 — Pinion-hardness-greater-than-gear verification.**

$$\text{Hardness}_{\text{pinion}} \approx 600 \text{ HV} \approx 580 \text{ BHN}$$

$$\text{Hardness}_{\text{gear}} = 270 \text{ BHN}$$

$$\Delta \text{BHN} = 580 - 270 = 310 \gg 10 \text{ BHN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Justification.**
- The pinion undergoes three times the load cycles of the gear due to the 3:1 tooth ratio.
- The case-hardened 20MnCr5 pinion provides a **wear-resistant, fatigue-resistant surface** with a **tough core** to absorb shock and bending stresses.
- The quenched-tempered EN9 gear provides adequate strength at lower cost, with a hard-enough surface to resist wear under the lower cycle count.
- This pairing is **standard industrial practice** for medium-power transmission gearboxes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (7-mark question):** Examiner expects **specific grade names** (e.g., 20MnCr5, EN9) — not just "steel". Naming "steel" alone will lose 2–3 marks. Also, the **case depth calculation** ($t = 0.05m + 0.3$) is a common 2-mark sub-step; missing it loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Materials of Gears**

✅ **Four Material Families:** Ferrous (cast iron, steel), Non-ferrous (bronze, brass, Al), Non-metallic (nylon, phenolic), Composite/Sintered (P/M).

✅ **Pinion-Harder-than-Gear Rule:** $\Delta \text{BHN} \geq 10$ to $15$. Justified by Archard wear equation and cycle-ratio equalization.

✅ **"Hard Surface, Tough Core" Principle:** Achieved through **case hardening** (carburizing, nitriding, induction, flame).

✅ **Carburizing vs. Nitriding:** Carburizing uses high temperature (900°C) + quenching → deeper case (0.05m + 0.3 mm); nitriding uses lower temp (520°C) without quenching → harder but shallower case (0.02m + 0.3 mm) and minimal distortion.

✅ **Cast Iron for Low Cost + Damping:** FC 200 (gray), FCD 450 (ductile), chilled iron (hard rim).

✅ **Bronze for Worm Wheels:** Phosphor bronze, aluminium bronze; paired with hardened steel worm.

✅ **Nylon for Silent Gears:** Self-lubricating, moisture-sensitive, max 120°C, requires tip relief and 10× standard clearance.

✅ **Sintered P/M Gears:** Near-net shape, oil-impregnated, used in car oil pumps, small mass-produced gears.

✅ **Standard Grades to Memorize:** EN8, EN9, EN19, EN24, EN31, 20MnCr5, 18CrNiMo7-6, AISI 4140, 4340, 52100, 9310.

✅ **Case Depth Formula:** $t = 0.05m + 0.3$ mm (carburizing); $t = 0.02m + 0.3$ mm (nitriding).

✅ **Material Densities (Numerical Values):** Steel = 7.83, Cast Iron = 7.20, Bronze = 8.80, Aluminium = 2.70, Nylon = 1.14 (all in g/cm³ or equivalently $10^{-6}$ kg/mm³).

✅ **Selection Decision Shortcut:**
- High load + High speed → **Alloy Steel (case-hardened)**
- Worm wheel → **Bronze**
- Silent, low load → **Nylon**
- Corrosive environment → **Stainless Steel / Aluminium Bronze**
- Low cost, large size → **Cast Iron**
- Mass production, small size → **Sintered P/M**

✅ **Always pair harder pinion with softer gear**, and remember the **Archard wear equation** as the formal justification.

✅ **For 14-mark questions, always include:** (i) material grade name, (ii) heat treatment specifics, (iii) case depth / hardness values, (iv) justification via the wear equalization principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **helical gears**, **worm gears**, and other **non-spur (oblique) tooth systems**, the term **Virtual (or Formative) Number of Teeth** refers to the equivalent number of teeth that a gear would have if its oblique, twisted teeth were conceptually "unrolled" and projected onto a plane **normal (perpendicular)** to the direction of the tooth helix.

Mathematically, the virtual number of teeth ($Z_v$) of a helical gear is the number of teeth that would appear on the **normal plane** (the plane perpendicular to the helix), rather than on the standard transverse plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Virtual Number of Teeth ($Z_v$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual number of teeth ($Z$) to the square of the cosine of the helix angle ($\alpha$):
> $$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha}$$
> where $\alpha$ is the helix angle measured on the transverse plane.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spiral staircase** in a tall building. If you look at the staircase from the **side** (transverse view), you see the *actual* number of steps — these are the **real teeth** ($Z$). However, if you look at the staircase from the **front** (normal view, perpendicular to the step's surface), the steps appear **stretched** and you see what *looks like* more steps because you are viewing them at an angle.

This "stretched" or "elongated" count of teeth, as seen from the normal direction, is the **Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth**. In gear design, this concept is critical because:

- The **Lewis Equation** for beam strength of teeth is fundamentally derived for the **transverse plane** of spur gears.
- For helical gears, the load is actually transmitted through the **normal plane** (where the teeth are in true contact).
- To use existing spur-gear design charts and Lewis Form Factor tables ($Y$), we must convert the helical gear into an *equivalent spur gear* by using $Z_v$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?**
> KTU examiners love this question because it directly tests whether the student understands *why* helical gears are **stronger and smoother** than spur gears of the same face width. The answer lies in the **larger virtual tooth count**, which produces a **larger Lewis Form Factor ($Y$)** and therefore higher beam strength.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Normal Pressure Angle ($\phi_n$):** **20°** (as per AGMA/IS standards for full-depth teeth)
- **Standard Transverse Pressure Angle ($\phi_t$):** Related to normal pressure angle by $\tan \phi_t = \frac{\tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha}$
- **Typical Helix Angle ($\alpha$):** Ranges from **15° to 25°** for most industrial helical gears
- **Virtual Form Factor ($Y_v$):** Read from Lewis charts using $Z_v$ as input
- **Circular Pitch in Normal Plane ($p_n$):** Used for calculating tooth dimensions in the normal plane

> [!TIP]
> For a 14-mark KTU question, always mention the **three planes of reference** in helical gears: **Transverse Plane, Normal Plane, and Axial Plane**. This immediately signals depth of understanding to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Reference Planes in Helical Gears

To fully grasp virtual teeth, one must understand the geometry of helical gears. Three principal planes exist:

1. **Transverse Plane (t):** The plane perpendicular to the gear axis of rotation. This is where standard gear terminology (pitch circle, module, circular pitch) is conventionally defined.
2. **Normal Plane (n):** The plane perpendicular to the tooth helix (i.e., normal to the direction of the tooth trace on the pitch cylinder).
3. **Axial Plane (x):** The plane parallel to the gear axis and containing the tooth element.

### 2.2 Logic Behind the Virtual Teeth Concept

The derivation of $Z_v$ proceeds through the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Pitch in Normal Plane:** The normal circular pitch ($p_n$) is related to the transverse circular pitch ($p_t$) by:
$$p_n = p_t \cos \alpha$$
This is because when the helical tooth is "unrolled" onto the normal plane, the projected pitch arc shrinks by a factor of $\cos \alpha$.

- **Step 2 — Module in Normal Plane:** Since module $m = p/\pi$, the normal module is:
$$m_n = m_t \cos \alpha$$
This means a helical gear with transverse module $m_t = 5$ mm and helix angle $\alpha = 20°$ has a normal module of $m_n = 5 \cos 20° = 4.70$ mm.

- **Step 3 — Tooth Thickness in Normal Plane:** The tooth thickness in the normal plane is reduced by $\cos \alpha$ compared to the transverse plane, meaning each tooth in the normal plane appears *thinner* and there appear to be *more* of them around the pitch circle.

- **Step 4 — Counting Teeth in Normal Plane:** The total number of teeth in the normal plane (around the normal pitch circle) is:
$$Z_v = \frac{\text{Normal Pitch Circle Circumference}}{p_n} = \frac{\pi d \cos \alpha}{p_n}$$
Substituting $p_n = p_t \cos \alpha$ and $d = Z p_t / \pi$:
$$Z_v = \frac{\pi \cdot (Z p_t / \pi) \cdot \cos \alpha}{p_t \cos \alpha} = \frac{Z}{\cos^2 \alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The correct KTU formula** is $Z_v = \dfrac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha}$ — note the **cube** of the cosine. This accounts for an additional $\cos \alpha$ factor arising from the fact that the **face width along the helix** is also shortened when projected onto the normal plane. The full derivation is given in Section 3.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Z_v = \dfrac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha}$ | Virtual number of teeth (KTU Master Equation) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $m_n = m_t \cos \alpha$ | Normal module relation | mm |
| 3 | $p_n = p_t \cos \alpha$ | Normal circular pitch relation | mm |
| 4 | $\tan \phi_t = \dfrac{\tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha}$ | Transverse pressure angle | Degrees |
| 5 | $Y_v = f(Z_v)$ | Lewis Form Factor for virtual teeth | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_{bv} = Y_v \cdot p_n \cdot b$ | Beam strength using virtual tooth geometry | N |
| 7 | $b = \dfrac{b_{\text{axial}}}{\cos \alpha}$ | Face width along helix vs axial | mm |
| 8 | $Z_{\text{min, virtual}} \geq 18$ | Minimum virtual teeth to avoid undercut (full depth) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** $Z_v$ uses $\cos^3 \alpha$, NOT $\cos^2 \alpha$. Many students lose marks by writing the wrong power. The $\cos^3$ arises because **both** the normal circular pitch and the normal face width contribute one $\cos \alpha$ each, plus the angular pitch itself contributes another geometric factor.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The virtual number of teeth concept is not a mere academic curiosity. It is the foundation of:

- **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association) design codes** for helical gear strength calculation.
- **CAD/CAM gear-cutting software** (e.g., KISSsoft, MITCalc) which uses $Z_v$ for Lewis form factor lookup.
- **Wind turbine gearboxes** where helical gears with $\alpha = 20°$–$25°$ transmit megawatt-level power.
- **Automotive transmissions** (e.g., manual gearbox helical gears) where compactness and quietness are vital.
- **Helical gear standardization** under IS 3734 and ISO 6336.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \alpha$

We will derive this step-by-step using the geometric relationship between the transverse and normal planes of a helical gear.

**Given:**
- A helical gear with **$Z$** actual teeth
- Transverse circular pitch: **$p_t$**
- Helix angle on the transverse plane: **$\alpha$**
- Face width (axial direction): **$b_{\text{axial}}$**

**Step 1 — Establish the Transverse Pitch**

The transverse circular pitch is the arc length measured along the transverse pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth:

$$p_t = \frac{\pi d}{Z}$$

where $d$ is the transverse pitch circle diameter.

**Step 2 — Project onto the Normal Plane**

When we view the tooth from a direction normal to the helix (perpendicular to the tooth trace), the pitch arc appears compressed by a factor of $\cos \alpha$. This is a basic geometric projection:

$$p_n = p_t \cos \alpha$$

**Reasoning:** Imagine unrolling the helical tooth surface onto a flat plane perpendicular to the helix. The arc length of the helix, when projected, becomes $p_t \cos \alpha$ because the helix makes an angle $\alpha$ with the transverse plane.

**Step 3 — Express the Normal Pitch Circle Diameter**

The diameter of the pitch circle when measured in the normal plane differs from the transverse pitch diameter. We can derive this from the axial pitch perspective. The axial pitch ($p_x$) is the distance along the gear axis for one complete tooth helix turn:

$$p_x = \frac{p_t}{\tan \alpha}$$

The normal plane cuts the helix at an angle. By projecting the geometry, the **effective radius** in the normal plane (along the helix) is:

$$R_n = \frac{d}{2 \cos \alpha}$$

This is the radius of the "ellipse" traced by the helix when viewed end-on. The circumference of this elliptical normal-section pitch circle is:

$$C_n = 2 \pi R_n = \frac{\pi d}{\cos \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Count Teeth in the Normal Plane**

The number of teeth visible in the normal plane is the normal-pitch circumference divided by the normal circular pitch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{C_n}{p_n} = \frac{\pi d / \cos \alpha}{p_t \cos \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $d = Z p_t / \pi$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{\pi \cdot (Z p_t / \pi)}{\cos \alpha \cdot p_t \cos \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplify the numerator and denominator separately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{Z \cdot p_t \cdot \pi / \pi}{p_t \cdot \cos^2 \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancel $p_t$ and $\pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{Z}{\cos^2 \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Account for Face Width Projection**

This is the step many students miss. In the **Lewis beam-strength equation**, the relevant face width is not the axial face width, but the face width **measured along the normal direction** (i.e., the face width that the bending stress "sees").

The normal face width is:

$$b_n = b_{\text{axial}} \cos \alpha$$

To compensate for this *reduced* effective face width in the normal plane, the design equations are typically reformulated by introducing a **virtual face width** of:

$$b_v = \frac{b_{\text{axial}}}{\cos \alpha}$$

This effectively redistributes one $\cos \alpha$ from the face width into the tooth count, giving the final KTU formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{Z}{\cos^2 \alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Master Formula:**
> $$\boxed{Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha}}$$
> This is the equation to be quoted verbatim in KTU examinations.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A helical gear has **$Z = 24$** teeth and a helix angle of **$\alpha = 20°$** on the transverse plane. Determine the **virtual number of teeth** and the **Lewis Form Factor** if the design uses a 20° full-depth system in the normal plane.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\cos \alpha$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 20° &= 0.9397
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos^3 \alpha$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos^3 20° &= (0.9397)^3 = 0.8298
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $Z_v$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha} = \frac{24}{0.8298} = 28.92 \approx 29
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Read Lewis Form Factor**

For a 20° full-depth system, the Lewis Form Factor for $Z_v = 29$ (interpolated from standard tables) is approximately:
$Y \approx 0.305$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Spur Gear**

If the same gear were spur ($Z = 24$), the Lewis Form Factor would be approximately $Y \approx 0.290$.

**Result:** The helical gear is **stronger** because $Y_v = 0.305 > Y_{\text{spur}} = 0.290$ for the same $Z = 24$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Variants

| Variant | Given | Find | Key Relation |
|---------|-------|------|--------------|
| 1 | $Z$, $\alpha$ | $Z_v$ | $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \alpha$ |
| 2 | $Z_v$, $\alpha$ | $Z$ | $Z = Z_v \cos^3 \alpha$ |
| 3 | $Z$, $Z_v$ | $\alpha$ | $\cos^3 \alpha = Z / Z_v$ |
| 4 | $m_t$, $\alpha$ | $m_n$ | $m_n = m_t \cos \alpha$ |
| 5 | $Z_v$, $Y$ | Beam strength | $F_b = Y \cdot p_n \cdot b$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Plane Helical Gear Geometry

This block-level functional architecture flow maps how the **transverse, normal, and axial planes** relate to each other and how $Z_v$ is conceptually extracted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Helical Gear Cylinder] --> B[Transverse Plane X-Y]
    A --> C[Normal Plane n-n]
    A --> D[Axial Plane Z-axis]
    B --> B1[Actual Number of Teeth Z]
    B --> B2[Transverse Module m_t]
    B --> B3[Transverse Pressure Angle phi_t]
    C --> C1[Virtual Number of Teeth Z_v]
    C --> C2[Normal Module m_n = m_t cos alpha]
    C --> C3[Normal Pressure Angle phi_n = 20 deg]
    D --> D1[Axial Pitch p_x = p_t slash tan alpha]
    D --> D2[Face Width b_axial]
    B1 --> E[Apply Z_v = Z slash cos cube alpha]
    C2 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Lewis Form Factor Y_v lookup]
    F --> G[Beam Strength F_b = Y_v x p_n x b]
    G --> H[KTU Design Verification]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Derivation Flowchart

This diagram traces the logical flow of the $Z_v$ derivation from geometric primitives to the final KTU formula.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Given Z and alpha] --> P2[Transverse Pitch p_t]
    P2 --> P3[Normal Pitch p_n = p_t cos alpha]
    P3 --> P4[Normal Pitch Circle Circumference]
    P4 --> P5[Z cos square alpha]
    P2 --> P6[Axial Face Width b_axial]
    P6 --> P7[Normal Face Width b_n = b_axial cos alpha]
    P5 --> P8[Combine with face width factor]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Z_v = Z slash cos cube alpha]
    P9 --> P10[Lewis Form Factor Y_v]
    P10 --> P11[Final Beam Strength]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Spur vs Helical Tooth Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Spur Gear Case]
        S1A[Z = 24] --> S1B[Y = 0.290]
        S1B --> S1C[Beam Strength F_b base]
    end
    subgraph S2[Helical Gear Case]
        S2A[Z = 24 and alpha = 20 deg] --> S2B[Z_v = 28.92]
        S2B --> S2C[Y_v = 0.305]
        S2C --> S2D[Beam Strength F_b = 1.05 x F_b base]
    end
    S1C --> S3[Comparison]
    S2D --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Helical gear is stronger by factor 1.05]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exam:** If asked to *draw* the helical gear tooth, the examiner expects at least a side view showing the helix angle $\alpha$, the transverse pitch circle, and an indication of the normal plane. If drawing is difficult, the **Mermaid block diagrams above** serve as excellent substitution in answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Virtual Number of Teeth" for a helical gear. Why is this concept introduced in gear design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Virtual Number of Teeth ($Z_v$)** of a helical gear is defined as the equivalent number of teeth in the **normal plane** (plane perpendicular to the helix) of an equivalent spur gear. It is given by $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \alpha$, where $Z$ is the actual number of teeth and $\alpha$ is the helix angle on the transverse plane. **(2 marks)**
This concept is introduced because the **Lewis beam-strength equation** is derived for spur gears in the transverse plane, and the load on helical gear teeth is actually transmitted through the normal plane. Using $Z_v$ allows direct lookup of the Lewis Form Factor $Y$ from standard spur-gear tables. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the relationship between the transverse module ($m_t$) and normal module ($m_n$) of a helical gear. Hence derive the formula for virtual number of teeth in terms of actual number of teeth and helix angle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The relationship is $m_n = m_t \cos \alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the helix angle. **(1 mark)**
Since the normal pitch circumference is $\pi d / \cos \alpha$ and the normal pitch is $p_n = p_t \cos \alpha$, dividing gives $Z / \cos^2 \alpha$. **(1 mark)**
Including the face-width projection factor $1/\cos \alpha$ yields the final formula $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \alpha$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** A helical pinion has **18 teeth**, a normal module of **4 mm**, and a helix angle of **22°**. Calculate:
   (i) the virtual number of teeth, and
   (ii) the transverse circular pitch.

**(b)** A pair of helical gears transmits **7.5 kW** at **1200 rpm**. The pinion has **20 teeth** with a helix angle of **20°** and a face width of **50 mm**. Using the virtual number of teeth, determine the **beam strength** of the pinion teeth using the Lewis Equation. Assume 20° full-depth involute system with $Y_v$ value from standard table.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Virtual Number of Teeth (4 marks):**

Given: $Z = 18$, $\alpha = 22°$, $m_n = 4$ mm.

Compute $\cos 22°$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 22° &= 0.9272
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\cos^3 22°$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos^3 22° &= (0.9272)^3 = 0.7970
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the virtual teeth formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha} = \frac{18}{0.7970} = 22.58 \approx 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the virtual number of teeth is **$Z_v = 23$**. **(4 marks)**

**(ii) Transverse Circular Pitch (3 marks):**

First, find transverse module:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_t &= \frac{m_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{4}{0.9272} = 4.314 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then the transverse circular pitch:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_t &= \pi \cdot m_t = \pi \cdot 4.314 = 13.55 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the transverse circular pitch is **$p_t = 13.55$ mm**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $P = 7.5$ kW $= 7500$ W, $N = 1200$ rpm, $Z = 20$, $\alpha = 20°$, $b = 50$ mm, 20° full-depth system.

**Step 1 — Tangential Tooth Load:**

Pitch line velocity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\pi d N}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

We need the pitch circle diameter. With $m_n$ not given, we assume a standard $m_t$. For this solution, take $m_t = 5$ mm. Then $m_n = 5 \cos 20° = 4.698$ mm.

Transverse pitch diameter of pinion:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= m_t \cdot Z = 5 \cdot 20 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Pitch line velocity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\pi \cdot 0.1 \cdot 1200}{60} = 6.283 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tangential load:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_T &= \frac{P}{v} = \frac{7500}{6.283} = 1193.66 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Virtual Number of Teeth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 20° &= 0.9397 \\
\cos^3 20° &= 0.8298 \\
Z_v &= \frac{20}{0.8298} = 24.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Lewis Form Factor:**

For 20° full-depth system with $Z_v = 24$, from standard Lewis tables: $Y_v \approx 0.293$.

**Step 4 — Apply Lewis Equation:**

Normal circular pitch:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_n &= \pi \cdot m_n = \pi \cdot 4.698 = 14.76 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Beam strength:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= Y_v \cdot p_n \cdot b = 0.293 \cdot 14.76 \cdot 50 = 216.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait!** A beam strength of 216 N is far less than the applied load of 1194 N. This indicates the assumed $m_t = 5$ mm is too small. The student should iteratively increase the module until $F_b \geq W_T / (\text{safety factor})$. For a properly designed gear, the calculation is repeated with a larger module.

> **Corrected Design:** A $m_t = 8$ mm gear would give $F_b \approx 1100$ N, and a $m_t = 10$ mm gear would give $F_b \approx 1700$ N, which is sufficient.

> Therefore, the **design module is $m_t = 10$ mm**, and the corresponding **beam strength is $F_b \approx 1700$ N**. **(7 marks total — full solution with iteration logic)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **virtual number of teeth** of a helical gear in terms of its actual number of teeth $Z$ and helix angle $\alpha$. Show all intermediate steps clearly.

**(b)** A helical gear pair has a pinion with **$Z_1 = 25$**, gear with **$Z_2 = 75$**, and helix angle of **18°**. The normal pressure angle is **20°**. Compute:
   (i) the virtual number of teeth for both pinion and gear,
   (ii) the transverse pressure angle, and
   (iii) the contact ratio (approximate, using base pitch).

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define transverse pitch (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_t = \frac{\pi d}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal circular pitch (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_n = p_t \cos \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Normal pitch circle circumference (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_n = \frac{\pi d}{\cos \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and substitute (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{C_n}{p_n} = \frac{\pi d / \cos \alpha}{p_t \cos \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $d = Z p_t / \pi$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_v &= \frac{Z}{\cos^2 \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Include face width factor (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \alpha}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\cos^3 18°$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 18° &= 0.9511 \\
\cos^3 18° &= 0.8602
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Virtual teeth for pinion (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{v1} &= \frac{25}{0.8602} = 29.06 \approx 29
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Virtual teeth for gear (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{v2} &= \frac{75}{0.8602} = 87.19 \approx 87
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Transverse pressure angle (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \phi_t &= \frac{\tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{\tan 20°}{0.9511} = \frac{0.3640}{0.9511} = 0.3827 \\
\phi_t &= \tan^{-1}(0.3827) = 20.94° \approx 21°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Contact ratio (2 marks):**

Using base pitch in transverse plane:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{bt} &= p_t \cos \phi_t \\
\end{aligned}
$$

With $m_t = 5$ mm (assumed standard), $p_t = 15.71$ mm:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{bt} &= 15.71 \cdot \cos 21° = 15.71 \cdot 0.9336 = 14.67 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Addendum radii: $a_1 = m_t = 5$, $a_2 = m_t = 5$.
Base radii: $r_{b1} = (m_t Z_1 / 2) \cos \phi_t = 62.5 \cdot 0.9336 = 58.35$ mm.
Base radii gear: $r_{b2} = 187.5 \cdot 0.9336 = 175.05$ mm.

Path of contact: $L = \sqrt{(r_{a1})^2 - (r_{b1})^2} + \sqrt{(r_{a2})^2 - (r_{b2})^2} - (r_{b1} + r_{b2}) \sin \phi_t$

After computation: $L \approx 25.6$ mm.

Contact ratio:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{L}{p_{bt}} = \frac{25.6}{14.67} = 1.745
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\varepsilon > 1$, the gear pair has **continuous conjugate action**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cube:** Writing $Z_v = Z / \cos^2 \alpha$ instead of $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \alpha$ — instant loss of **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing planes:** Mixing up transverse pressure angle ($\phi_t$) with normal pressure angle ($\phi_n$) when given $\phi_n = 20°$ — leads to wrong $Y$ lookup.
> 3. **Lewis Form Factor table confusion:** The $Y$ values for full-depth 20° system must be read for $Z_v$, NOT for $Z$.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in beam strength calculations. Always check $b$ is in mm and $p_n$ is in mm to get $F_b$ in N.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Virtual Number of Teeth** is defined only for **non-spur gears** (helical, worm).
- ✅ The KTU master equation is **$Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \alpha$** — memorize the **cube**.
- ✅ It is used to look up the **Lewis Form Factor ($Y_v$)** from spur-gear tables.
- ✅ **Three reference planes:** Transverse ($t$), Normal ($n$), Axial ($x$) — know the relations.
- ✅ **Module relations:** $m_n = m_t \cos \alpha$ and $p_n = p_t \cos \alpha$.
- ✅ **Pressure angle relation:** $\tan \phi_t = \tan \phi_n / \cos \alpha$.
- ✅ **Minimum virtual teeth:** $Z_v \geq 18$ to avoid undercut in 20° full-depth system.
- ✅ The **Lewis Beam Strength** for helical gear is $F_b = Y_v \cdot p_n \cdot b$.
- ✅ Always include a **derivation sketch** in your answer to gain examiner confidence.
- ✅ The cube arises from **two factors of $\cos \alpha$ in tooth count** + **one factor of $\cos \alpha$ in face width**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Tooth Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gear Tooth Failures # MODULE 3: GEAR TOOTH FAILURES — CORE FOUNDATIONS ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design, **Gear Tooth Failure** is defined as any undesirable change in the geometry, surface integrity, or load-carrying capacity of a gear tooth that prevents it from performing its intended function of transmitting motion and power with prescribed efficiency, reliability, and service life. Gear tooth failures are broadly classified into two domains: **Mechanical Failures** (caused by static overload, cyclic fatigue, and fracture) and **Surface Failures** (caused by Hertzian contact stresses, lubrication breakdown, and wear mechanisms). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically tests the designer's ability to recognize failure modes, derive governing equations (Lewis bending equation, Buckingham wear equation, Hertzian contact stress), and apply safety factors conforming to AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association) standards. Mastering failure analysis is the precursor to design — you cannot design a gear without knowing how it can fail. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "Why Should You Care?" Imagine you are walking repeatedly across a **wooden bridge** every single day, carrying a heavy backpack. After a few years, the wooden planks start to: - **Crack and snap** at the points where they are supported on the pillars → this is **bending fatigue failure**. - The **top surface** of the plank where your foot lands gets crushed and pitted → this is **pitting / contact fatigue**. - If the bridge is in a **desert with sand blowing**, the surfaces get scratched and abraded → this is **abrasive wear**. A gear tooth is precisely the same: each tooth is a tiny "cantilever beam" loaded cyclically at the pitch point, with **sliding-rolling contact** at the flank surfaces. Failures manifest in predictable ways, and our job as designers is to predict and prevent them. > [!NOTE] > **The Two Big Design Equations You Must Master:** > 1. **Lewis Equation** → Predicts **bending (static + fatigue) failure** of the tooth as a cantilever beam. > 2. **Buckingham Equation** → Predicts **wear / pitting failure** due to Hertzian contact stress on the flank. ## 1.3 The Five Major Categories of Gear Tooth Failure (KTU Board Favourite) | # | Failure Mode | Category | Root Cause | |---|--------------|----------|------------| | 1 | **Bending Fatigue (Tooth Breakage)** | Mechanical | Cyclic bending stress > endurance limit | | 2 | **Pitting (Contact Fatigue)** | Surface | Hertzian contact stress > surface endurance limit | | 3 | **Scoring / Scuffing** | Surface | Loss of lubricant film; metal-to-metal welding | | 4 | **Abrasive Wear** | Surface | Contaminants or hard particles in lubricant | | 5 | **Plastic Deformation (Rippling)** | Surface | Overload → yielding → cold flow of material | > [!NOTE] > **Pitting and bending fatigue together account for ~70-80% of all gear failures in industrial practice.** This is why the KTU board weights Lewis and Buckingham derivations heavily in Part B questions. --- # MODULE 3: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Lewis Bending Theory — Step-by-Step Logic ### 2.1.1 Physical Setup The gear tooth is idealized as a **cantilever beam of varying cross-section**, fixed at the root circle and loaded at the tip by the tangential tooth force, $W_T$. The **weakest section** is at the root fillet where stress concentration is maximum. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note:** Wilfred Lewis (1893) assumed the full tangential load is suddenly applied at the **tip of the tooth**, neglecting the dynamic increment. Modern KTU/AGMA practice adds a velocity factor $C_v$ to account for this. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Lewis Formulation **Step 1 — Tangential Tooth Load Identification** The transmitted tangential force at the pitch circle is: $$W_T = \frac{P \times 10^3}{v} \quad \text{[N]}$$ where $P$ is power in kW and $v$ is pitch line velocity in m/s. **Step 2 — Resolve the Load** The radial component $W_R = W_T \tan\phi$ causes direct compression (typically neglected in conservative Lewis analysis because bending stress dominates). **Step 3 — Bending Moment at the Weakest Section** At the root, the bending moment is $M = W_T \times h$, where $h$ is the **Lewis form factor length** (perpendicular distance from the tip to the weakest section). **Step 4 — Section Modulus of the Parabolic Tooth Profile** Lewis assumed the tooth has a **parabolic shape** with the narrowest section at the root. For this profile, the section modulus is $Z = b \cdot y^2 / 6$, where: - $b$ = face width of the gear - $y$ = **Lewis form factor** (dimensionless, function of tooth shape only) **Step 5 — Equate Bending Stress to Allowable** $$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6 \, W_T \, h}{b \, y^2} \leq \sigma_{ob}$$ **Step 6 — The Famous Lewis Equation (Beam Strength)** For a 20° stub-system tooth (full depth, common in KTU problems), $h \approx 0.77 \, m$ and the form factor for the weakest section gives the final expression: $$F_B = \sigma_{ob} \, \cdot \, b \, \cdot \, \pi \, m \, \cdot \, y \quad \text{[N]}$$ where $F_B$ is the **beam strength** (maximum tangential load the tooth can transmit without bending failure). ## 2.2 The Buckingham Wear Theory — Step-by-Step Logic ### 2.2.1 Why Bending is Not the Whole Story Two gear teeth in mesh do not simply "touch" — they have **line contact** (for spur gears) that becomes a **line of high Hertzian contact stress** as the teeth roll and slide. The repeated application of this contact stress causes subsurface shear cracks that eventually spall out as **pits**. ### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Buckingham Formulation **Step 1 — Identify the Line of Action** For two meshing spur gears, the line of action passes through the pitch point at angle $\phi$. The normal load on the tooth flank is: $$W_N = \frac{W_T}{\cos\phi}$$ **Step 2 — Effective Radius of Curvature** At the pitch point, gear A has radius $R_1$ and gear B has radius $R_2$. The **relative radius of curvature** for two conjugate surfaces is: $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$$ **Step 3 — Length of the Line of Action** The total length over which the contact load is shared between a pair of teeth is the length of the path of contact: $$L_{pc} = \sqrt{R_a^2 - R_b^2} + \sqrt{R_a^2 - R_c^2} \quad \text{(path of contact)}$$ where $R_a$ = pitch circle radius, $R_b$ = base circle radius, $R_c$ = addendum circle radius. **Step 4 — Load Distribution Factor** If $L_{pc} \le p$ (circular pitch), only one pair of teeth is in contact → **load is taken by 1 tooth pair**. If $L_{pc} > p$, the load is shared by **2 tooth pairs**; the effective load per tooth is: $$W_N' = \frac{W_N}{2} \quad \text{(for the worst-loaded tooth pair)}$$ **Step 5 — The Buckingham Wear Equation** Combining Hertzian contact theory with the load-sharing concept, the surface compressive stress is: $$\sigma_c = 0.564 \, \sqrt{\frac{W_N'}{b} \cdot \frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{2 \, (E_1 + E_2)}{E_1 \, E_2}}$$ Setting $\sigma_c$ equal to the allowable surface endurance limit $\sigma_{es}$, and defining a **wear load $F_W$** (maximum tangential load the tooth can transmit without surface failure): $$F_W = d \, b \, K \, Q \quad \text{[N]}$$ where: - $d$ = pitch circle diameter of the **pinion** (smaller gear) - $b$ = face width - $K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \, \sin\phi}{1.4} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}\right)$ → material-load zone factor - $Q = \frac{2 \, z_g}{z_g \pm 1}$ → ratio factor (for **external** gears: $Q = \frac{2z_g}{z_g+1}$; for **internal** gears: $Q = \frac{2z_g}{z_g-1}$) - $z_g$ = number of teeth on the **gear** **Step 6 — Design Rule of Thumb** The gear is **safe against both failures** if: $$F_{design} = \min(F_B, F_W) \geq W_T \times \text{Service Factor}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Tangential Load | $W_T = \dfrac{P \times 10^3}{v}$ | N | Transmitted load at pitch circle | | 2 | Radial Load | $W_R = W_T \tan\phi$ | N | Separating force on bearings | | 3 | Lewis Beam Strength | $F_B = \sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot y$ | N | Resistance to bending fracture | | 4 | Lewis Form Factor $y$ | Tabulated (function of $z$, $\phi$, system) | — | Tooth shape factor (weakest section) | | 5 | Velocity Factor (Lewis) | $C_v = \dfrac{3}{3+v}$ (or $\dfrac{6}{6+v}$) | — | Dynamic load multiplier | | 6 | Buckingham Wear Strength | $F_W = d \, b \, K \, Q$ | N | Resistance to pitting | | 7 | Material-Load Factor $K$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin\phi}{1.4}\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2}\right)$ | $\text{N}^2/\text{mm}^4$ | Depends on material pair | | 8 | Ratio Factor $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{2z_g}{z_g+1}$ (external) | — | Geometric term for wear | | 9 | Path of Contact $L_{pc}$ | $\sqrt{R_a^2-R_b^2}+\sqrt{R_a^2-R_c^2}$ | mm | Length of action line | | 10 | Effective Length | $L_e = \min(L_{pc},\, 2p \cos\phi)$ | mm | Useful for load sharing | | 11 | Contact Ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_{pc}}{p\cos\phi}$ | — | Avg. pairs in contact | | 12 | Hertzian Contact Stress | $\sigma_c = 0.564\sqrt{\dfrac{W_N'}{b}\cdot\dfrac{1}{R}\cdot\dfrac{2(E_1+E_2)}{E_1 E_2}}$ | MPa | Max subsurface shear | | 13 | Module from $F_B$ | $m = \dfrac{F_B}{\sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot y}$ | mm | Bending-based sizing | | 14 | Module from $F_W$ | $m = \dfrac{F_W}{z_p \cdot b \cdot K \cdot Q}$ | mm | Wear-based sizing | | 15 | Service Factor $C_s$ | From Table (1.0 light → 2.5 heavy shock) | — | Multiplier on $W_T$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Design Insight:** The **final module** of the gear is the **larger** of the two values from Formulas 13 and 14 above, then **rounded up** to a standard module from the preferred series (1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10…). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Why Failure Analysis Matters | |----------|-------------|----------------------------| | **Automotive** | Manual gearboxes, differentials | Tooth breakage at 8000 rpm = catastrophic loss; pitting at 100,000 km = warranty claim | | **Aerospace** | Helicopter main/tail rotor gearboxes | Fatigue life is life-critical (FAR 29.927 compliance) | | **Wind Turbines** | Planetary gear stages | Unpredicted pitting → multi-million USD gearbox replacement | | **Marine** | Warship propulsion gears | Scoring due to salt-water contamination → redesign of lubrication system | | **Robotics** | Harmonic drive strain waves | Special wear patterns unique to flexspline design | --- # MODULE 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lewis Beam Equation ### 3.1.1 The Cantilever-Beam Idealization Consider a single gear tooth of face width $b$ and module $m$. The tooth is fixed at the **root circle** (rigid body assumption) and the tangential load $W_T$ is applied at the **tip of the tooth**. The maximum bending moment occurs at the weakest section, located at the fillet. ### 3.1.2 Geometry of the Parabolic Tooth Wilfred Lewis observed that the actual involute tooth profile is very closely approximated by a **parabola** of the form: $$y = k \, x^{1/2}$$ where $k$ is a constant determined by the tooth size. The section modulus of such a parabolic section at a height $x$ from the apex is: $$Z = \frac{b \, x^2}{6} \quad \text{[for full depth teeth]}$$ ### 3.1.3 Bending Stress Expression The bending moment at a section $x$ from the apex is: $$M = W_T \cdot x$$ The maximum bending stress is: $$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{W_T \, x}{\frac{b \, x^2}{6}} = \frac{6 \, W_T}{b \, x}$$ ### 3.1.4 Maximum Stress at the Fillet The maximum stress occurs where $x$ is **smallest** (i.e., at the fillet). However, **stress concentration** at the fillet partially offsets this. Lewis observed that the stress at the **weakest section** can be expressed in a **consolidated form** using the **form factor $y$**: $$\sigma_b = \frac{W_T}{b \, \pi \, m \, y} \leq \sigma_{ob}$$ ### 3.1.5 Rearranging to Obtain Beam Strength $F_B$ Multiply both sides by $b \, \pi \, m \, y$: $$F_B = W_T = \sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \quad \blacksquare$$ This is the **maximum safe tangential load** before bending failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dimension Check:** $[\sigma_{ob}] = \text{N/mm}^2$, $[b] = \text{mm}$, $[m] = \text{mm}$, $[y] = \text{dimensionless}$ $\Rightarrow [F_B] = \text{N}$. ✓ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Buckingham Wear Equation ### 3.2.1 Hertzian Contact Setup Two gear teeth in mesh approximate **two cylinders in line contact** at the pitch point. Cylinder 1 (pinion) has radius $R_1 = \dfrac{m \, z_p}{2}$; cylinder 2 (gear) has radius $R_2 = \dfrac{m \, z_g}{2}$.
