The **equivalent radius of curvature** is: $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{2}{m}\left(\frac{1}{z_p} + \frac{1}{z_g}\right) = \frac{2(z_p + z_g)}{m \, z_p \, z_g}$$ ### 3.2.2 Length of the Line of Action (Path of Contact) The path of contact has two segments: - **Approach**: from addendum of pinion to pitch point - **Recess**: from pitch point to addendum of gear $$L_{pc} = \sqrt{R_{a,p}^2 - R_{b,p}^2} + \sqrt{R_{a,g}^2 - R_{b,g}^2}$$ where $R_a = $ addendum radius $= \dfrac{m(z+2)}{2}$ and $R_b = $ base radius $= R \cos\phi$. ### 3.2.3 Number of Tooth Pairs in Contact The **circular pitch** is $p = \pi m$. The **contact ratio** is: $$\varepsilon = \frac{L_{pc}}{p \cos\phi}$$ - If $\varepsilon < 1$: at some instants **no teeth are in contact** → impossible in practice (design error). - If $1 \le \varepsilon < 2$: **one or two pairs** alternate; the worst case is one pair carrying the full load. - If $\varepsilon \ge 2$: **always at least 2 pairs**; the maximum load per pair is half. ### 3.2.4 Maximum Load per Tooth Pair The **maximum normal load per tooth pair** is: $$W_N' = \frac{W_T}{\cos\phi} \cdot \frac{1}{n_p}$$ where $n_p$ = number of tooth pairs in contact at the worst instant. Conservatively, $n_p = 1$. ### 3.2.5 Hertzian Contact Stress For line contact between two cylinders of equivalent radius $R$ and effective modulus $E_{eq}$: $$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{W_N' \, E_{eq}}{\pi \, b \, R}}$$ where $E_{eq} = \dfrac{2 E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$. Setting $\sigma_c = \sigma_{es}$ (allowable surface endurance limit) and squaring: $$\sigma_{es}^2 = \frac{W_N' \, E_{eq}}{\pi \, b \, R} = \frac{W_N'}{\pi \, b \, R} \cdot \frac{2 E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$$ ### 3.2.6 Solving for Wear Load $F_W$ Rearrange to isolate $W_N'$, then convert $W_N' = W_T / \cos\phi$: $$W_T = \sigma_{es}^2 \, b \, R \, \pi \, \cos\phi \cdot \frac{E_1 + E_2}{2 E_1 E_2}$$ Substitute $R = \dfrac{m \, z_p \, z_g}{2(z_p + z_g)}$ and $W_T = F_W$: $$F_W = \sigma_{es}^2 \cdot \frac{\pi \cos\phi}{2} \cdot \frac{E_1 + E_2}{E_1 E_2} \cdot b \cdot m \cdot \frac{z_p z_g}{z_p + z_g}$$ ### 3.2.7 Simplification to Standard Form Using $d_p = m \, z_p$ and $Q = \dfrac{2 z_g}{z_g + 1}$ (for external gears, after substituting $z_p = z_g$ in the form factor approximation), the expression reduces to: $$F_W = d_p \, b \, K \, Q$$ where: $$K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin\phi}{1.4} \left(\frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Origin of the constant 1.4:** It comes from $\dfrac{\pi \cos\phi}{2} \cdot (\text{unit conversion})$ and is a standard empirical KTU/AGMA constant for SI units. ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem:** A pair of 20° full-depth spur gears is to transmit 15 kW at 1450 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 63 teeth. The face width is 10 times the circular pitch. Material: pinion and gear both steel with $\sigma_{ob} = 600$ MPa and $\sigma_{es} = 600$ MPa; $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa. Service factor $C_s = 1.75$. Form factor $y = 0.308 - \dfrac{1.77}{z}$ (full-depth 20°). Determine the module based on both Lewis bending and Buckingham wear, and select the standard module. ### 3.3.1 Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Pitch Line Velocity** $$v = \frac{\pi \, d_p \, N_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \, m \, z_p \, N_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot m \cdot 18 \cdot 1450}{60} = 1366.4 \, m \text{ per unit-}m$$ **Step 2 — Tangential Load (Design)** $$W_T = \frac{10^3 \cdot P}{v} = \frac{10^3 \cdot 15}{1366.4 \, m} = \frac{10.98}{m} \text{ kN per unit-}m = \frac{10980}{m} \text{ N per unit-}m$$ Applying service factor: $$W_{T,design} = 1.75 \times \frac{10980}{m} = \frac{19215}{m} \text{ N per unit-}m$$ **Step 3 — Form Factor** $$y = 0.308 - \frac{1.77}{18} = 0.308 - 0.0983 = 0.2097$$ **Step 4 — Face Width** $$b = 10 \, p = 10 \, \pi m = 31.416 \, m \text{ mm per unit-}m$$ **Step 5 — Lewis Module** Equating $F_B = W_{T,design}$: $$\sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot y = W_{T,design}$$ $$600 \times 31.416 \, m \times \pi \, m \times 0.2097 = \frac{19215}{m}$$ $$600 \times 31.416 \times 3.1416 \times 0.2097 \times m^2 = \frac{19215}{m}$$ $$12418.7 \, m^2 = \frac{19215}{m}$$ $$m^3 = \frac{19215}{12418.7} = 1.5475$$ $$m_{Lewis} = (1.5475)^{1/3} = 1.157 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Material Factor K** $$K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin 20°}{1.4} \left(\frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}\right) = \frac{600^2 \times 0.342}{1.4} \times \frac{2}{2.1 \times 10^5}$$ $$K = \frac{600^2 \times 0.342}{1.4} \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$K = \frac{123120}{1.4} \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6} = 87942.86 \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6} = 0.8375 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 7 — Ratio Factor Q** $$Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + 1} = \frac{2 \times 63}{64} = 1.96875$$ **Step 8 — Buckingham Module** $$F_W = d_p \, b \, K \, Q = W_{T,design}$$ $$(m \, z_p) \times (10 \pi m) \times K \times Q = \frac{19215}{m}$$ $$m^2 \times 18 \times 10 \pi \times 0.8375 \times 1.96875 = \frac{19215}{m}$$ $$m^2 \times 932.31 = \frac{19215}{m}$$ $$m^3 = \frac{19215}{932.31} = 20.61$$ $$m_{Buckingham} = (20.61)^{1/3} = 2.747 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 9 — Standard Module Selection** $$m = \max(1.157, \, 2.747) = 2.747 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Next standard: } \mathbf{3 \, mm}$$ **Step 10 — Final Check on the Selected Module** - Recompute $W_T$ with $m=3$, $v = 1366.4 \times 3 = 4099.2$ m/min $= 68.32$ m/s. - $W_T = 15000/68.32 = 219.6$ N; design = $1.75 \times 219.6 = 384.3$ N. - $F_B = 600 \times 10\pi(3) \times \pi(3) \times 0.2097 = 111,768$ N $\gg 384.3$ N ✓ - $F_W = 3(18) \times 10\pi(3) \times 0.8375 \times 1.96875 = 8956$ N $> 384.3$ N ✓ > **Result: Module = 3 mm. Both Lewis and Buckingham criteria are satisfied with comfortable safety margin.** --- # MODULE 3: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Gear Tooth Failure Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Gear Tooth in Service] --> B{Load exceeds
capacity?} B -- Yes, at root --> C[Bending Failure] B -- Yes, at flank --> D[Surface Failure] B -- No --> Z[Safe Operation] C --> C1[Static Tooth Breakage] C --> C2[Bending Fatigue
Progressive Crack] D --> D1[Pitting / Spalling] D --> D2[Scoring / Scuffing] D --> D3[Abrasive Wear] D --> D4[Plastic Flow / Rippling] C1 --> F1[Apply Lewis Equation
Use F_B] C2 --> F2[Use Modified Lewis
Include Cv and Cs] D1 --> G1[Apply Buckingham Equation
Use F_W] D2 --> G2[Improve Lubrication
EP additives] D3 --> G3[Filter Lubricant
Seal bearings] D4 --> G4[Reduce Load
Harder material] F1 --> H[Final Module =
max m_Lewis, m_Buck] F2 --> H G1 --> H H --> I[Round up to
Standard Module] I --> J[Validate:
F_B and F_W >= W_T*Cs] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Lewis vs. Buckingham Design Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inputs:
P, N, z_p, z_g, b/p ratio,
Material, sigma_ob, sigma_es] --> B[Compute W_T
T = P*1000 / v] B --> C[Apply Service Factor
W_design = Cs * W_T] C --> D[Lewis Branch:
F_B = sigma_ob * b * pi * m * y] D --> E[Solve for m_Lewis] C --> F[Buckingham Branch:
F_W = d_p * b * K * Q] F --> G[Solve for m_Buckingham] E --> H{max_Lewis vs m_Buck} G --> H H --> I[m = max + Standard Series] I --> J[Final Verification:
F_B and F_W vs W_design] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Failure Mode to Material/Process Map ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph MechanicalFailures [Mechanical Failure Zone] M1[Tooth Breakage] --> M2[Material: Through-Hardened Steel] M1 --> M3[Process: Case Carburizing] M1 --> M4[Process: Induction Hardening] end subgraph SurfaceFailures [Surface Failure Zone] S1[Pitting] --> S2[Material: Nitrided Steel] S1 --> S3[Process: Shot Peening] S4[Scoring] --> S5[Material: Surface Coated] S4 --> S6[Lubricant: EP Grade] S7[Abrasive Wear] --> S8[Lubricant Filtration: 10 micron] S7 --> S9[Seal Integrity Improvement] end subgraph DesignControls [Design Control Levers] D1[Increase Module] --> D2[Reduces Bending Stress] D3[Increase Face Width] --> D4[Reduces Surface Stress] D5[Improve Surface Finish] --> D6[Reduces Stress Concentration] D7[Increase Tooth Count] --> D8[Reduces Sliding Velocity] end ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Contact Stress Distribution on Tooth Flank ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ToothFlank [Single Tooth Flank Analysis] T1[Tip of Tooth
Low Contact Stress] --> T2[Upper Flank
Rising Stress] T2 --> T3[Pitch Point
MAXIMUM Hertzian Stress] T3 --> T4[Lower Flank
Declining Stress] T4 --> T5[Root Fillet
Zero Contact Stress] end subgraph FailureRegion [Critical Failure Region] F1[Sub-surface Shear
at depth ~0.5b Hertz] F2[Surface Initiation
at Pitch Point] end T3 --> F1 T3 --> F2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Pitch Point is Critical:** At the pitch point, the velocity of sliding between teeth is **zero** (pure rolling), so no frictional heat is generated. However, the **load concentration** is maximum because the line of action passes through this point and only one tooth pair is in contact at any instant (for low contact ratios). The Hertzian contact stress peaks here, making it the nucleation site for pitting. --- # MODULE 3: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **"Differentiate between pitting failure and scoring failure of gear teeth. Mention the design equation used to predict pitting failure."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Pitting | Scoring | |---------|---------|---------| | **Mechanism** | Hertzian contact fatigue → sub-surface shear cracks → spalling | Loss of lubricant film → metal-to-metal welding → tearing | | **Appearance** | Small pits/holes on flank, initially on dedendum | Scratches, scuff marks, weld transfer on flanks | | **Location** | Initiation below surface; surfaces pitting near pitch point | At the dedendum and addendum (where sliding is high) | | **Cause** | Contact stress > surface endurance limit | Lubricant failure due to excessive sliding velocity or temperature | | **Equation** | **Buckingham's wear equation** | Empirical scuffing temperature criterion (Block, 1937) | Pitting is a **fatigue phenomenon**; scoring is a **thermal/welding phenomenon**. The Buckingham equation $F_W = d \, b \, K \, Q$ predicts the maximum tangential load before pitting initiates. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **"State the assumptions made by Wilfred Lewis in deriving the bending stress equation for gear teeth. Why is the form factor 'y' introduced?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Lewis's Key Assumptions:** 1. The gear tooth is treated as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the root circle. 2. The full **tangential load $W_T$ is applied at the tip** of the tooth. 3. The tooth profile is approximated by a **parabola** for stress analysis. 4. The **stress is uniformly distributed** across the weakest section. 5. **Radial load** $W_R$ is neglected (conservative — bending dominates). 6. **Dynamic effects** are not explicitly included (later corrected by velocity factor $C_v$). **Role of the Form Factor $y$:** The form factor $y$ accounts for the **shape of the tooth** and the **concentration of stress at the fillet**. It converts the complex parabolic geometry into a **single dimensionless number** that the designer can use in the Lewis equation. $y$ depends on: - Number of teeth $z$ - Pressure angle $\phi$ - Tooth system (full depth / stub) For full-depth 20° teeth: $y = 0.308 - \dfrac{1.77}{z}$ (Lewis's empirical formula). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **A pair of 20° full-depth involute spur gears has 20 teeth on the pinion and 50 teeth on the gear. The pinion runs at 1440 rpm and transmits 10 kW. The face width is 12 times the module. The material of both gears is steel with $\sigma_{ob} = 550$ MPa, $\sigma_{es} = 550$ MPa, and $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa. The service factor is 1.5. Form factor $y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$ (full-depth 20°). Use Lewis and Buckingham theories to find the module of the gear.** **Sub-parts:** - **(a) Determine the module based on Lewis bending equation.** (7 marks) - **(b) Determine the module based on Buckingham wear equation, and select the standard module.** (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** ### Part (a) — Lewis Module (7 marks) **Step 1 — Pitch Line Velocity per unit module $m$:** $$v = \frac{\pi \, d_p \, N_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \times m \times 20 \times 1440}{60} = 1507.96 \, m \text{ per unit-}m \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Tangential Load per unit module:** $$W_T = \frac{10^3 \times P}{v} = \frac{10^3 \times 10}{1507.96 \, m} = \frac{6.631}{m} \text{ kN} = \frac{6631}{m} \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Design Load with Service Factor:** $$W_{T,design} = 1.5 \times \frac{6631}{m} = \frac{9946.5}{m} \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Form Factor for Pinion ($z = 20$):** $$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{20} = 0.154 - 0.0456 = 0.1084$$ **Step 5 — Face Width:** $$b = 12 \, m$$ **Step 6 — Apply Lewis Equation:** $$F_B = \sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y = W_{T,design}$$ $$550 \times 12m \times \pi \times m \times 0.1084 = \frac{9946.5}{m}$$ $$550 \times 12 \times 3.1416 \times 0.1084 \times m^2 = \frac{9946.5}{m}$$ $$2248.3 \, m^2 = \frac{9946.5}{m}$$ $$m^3 = \frac{9946.5}{2248.3} = 4.424$$ $$\boxed{m_{Lewis} = (4.424)^{1/3} = 1.642 \text{ mm}}$$ ### Part (b) — Buckingham Module and Final Selection (7 marks) **Step 7 — Material-Load Factor K:** $$K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin 20°}{1.4} \left(\frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}\right) = \frac{550^2 \times 0.342}{1.4} \times \frac{2}{2.1 \times 10^5}$$ $$K = \frac{103455}{1.4} \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6} = 73896.4 \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{K = 0.7038 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$ **Step 8 — Ratio Factor Q (External Gear):** $$Q = \frac{2 \, z_g}{z_g + 1} = \frac{2 \times 50}{51} = 1.9608$$ **Step 9 — Pinion Pitch Diameter:** $$d_p = m \, z_p = 20 \, m$$ **Step 10 — Apply Buckingham Equation:** $$F_W = d_p \, b \, K \, Q = W_{T,design}$$ $$(20m) \times (12m) \times 0.7038 \times 1.9608 = \frac{9946.5}{m}$$ $$m^2 \times 331.16 = \frac{9946.5}{m}$$ $$m^3 = \frac{9946.5}{331.16} = 30.035$$ $$\boxed{m_{Buckingham} = (30.035)^{1/3} = 3.108 \text{ mm}}$$ **Step 11 — Select Standard Module:** $$m = \max(1.642, 3.108) = 3.108 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \textbf{Standard module: 4 mm}$$ > **Final Answer: Module = 4 mm.** --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **A spur gear pair with 20° full-depth involute teeth is to transmit 25 kW at 720 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 25 teeth, the gear has 75 teeth, and the face width is 50 mm. The material is steel with $\sigma_{ob} = 450$ MPa, $\sigma_{es} = 600$ MPa, and $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa. The service factor is 1.75. Form factor $y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$. Verify the design for a module of 6 mm using both Lewis and Buckingham theories.** **Sub-parts:** - **(a) Apply the Lewis bending equation to find the maximum safe power.** (7 marks) - **(b) Apply the Buckingham wear equation and find the factor of safety against pitting.** (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** ### Part (a) — Lewis Safe Power (7 marks) **Step 1 — Pitch Diameter of Pinion (with $m = 6$):** $$d_p = m \, z_p = 6 \times 25 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Pitch Line Velocity:** $$v = \frac{\pi \, d_p \, N_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.15 \times 720}{60} = 5.6549 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Form Factor:** $$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{25} = 0.154 - 0.0365 = 0.1175$$ **Step 4 — Beam Strength:** $$F_B = \sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y = 450 \times 50 \times \pi \times 6 \times 0.1175$$ $$F_B = 450 \times 50 \times 3.1416 \times 6 \times 0.1175 = 49847 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum Safe Tangential Load:** $$W_{T,max} = F_B = 49847 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum Safe Power:** $$P_{max} = \frac{W_{T,max} \times v}{1000} = \frac{49847 \times 5.6549}{1000} = 281.88 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Compare with Applied Power (with Service Factor):** $$P_{applied} \times C_s = 25 \times 1.75 = 43.75 \text{ kW}$$ Since $281.88 \text{ kW} \gg 43.75 \text{ kW}$, the design is **safe in bending** with a high factor of safety. $$\boxed{\text{FS}_{bending} = \frac{281.88}{43.75} = 6.44}$$ ### Part (b) — Buckingham Pitting Safety (7 marks) **Step 8 — Material-Load Factor K:** $$K = \frac{600^2 \times \sin 20°}{1.4} \times \frac{2}{2.1 \times 10^5} = \frac{360000 \times 0.342}{1.4} \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$K = 87942.86 \times 9.5238 \times 10^{-6} = 0.8375 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 9 — Ratio Factor Q:** $$Q = \frac{2 \times 75}{75 + 1} = \frac{150}{76} = 1.9737$$ **Step 10 — Wear Strength:** $$F_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot K \cdot Q = 150 \times 50 \times 0.8375 \times 1.9737 = 12398 \text{ N}$$ **Step 11 — Applied Design Tangential Load:** $$W_{T,design} = C_s \times W_T = 1.75 \times \frac{25000 \times 1000}{5.6549 \times 1000} = 1.75 \times 4420.6 = 7736 \text{ N}$$ **Step 12 — Factor of Safety against Pitting:** $$\text{FS}_{wear} = \frac{F_W}{W_{T,design}} = \frac{12398}{7736} = 1.603$$ > **Conclusion:** The design is **safe** in both bending and wear, but the **wear (pitting) check is the more critical** with a lower safety factor of 1.6, whereas bending is over-designed by 6.4×. This indicates the **gear is over-sized for bending** but appropriately sized for surface durability. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply the service factor** before equating $W_T$ to $F_B$ or $F_W$. This is the #1 mistake in KTU valuations. > 2. **Using $z_p$ instead of $z_g$** in the ratio factor $Q$. Always $Q$ uses the **larger gear's** tooth count for external meshes. > 3. **Mixing up the form factor formula** — the constant term (0.154 vs 0.308) and the numerator (0.912 vs 1.77) **change with tooth system** (full-depth 20° has $y = 0.154 - 0.912/z$ per Shigley; some textbooks use $0.308 - 1.77/z$ for 14.5°). **Verify your data book before the exam.** > 4. **Neglecting to round up** to the next standard module. KTU examiners explicitly check this; a non-standard module gets zero. > 5. **Unit errors in $K$** — the constant 1.4 is dimensionally critical. Using 1.4 when it should be 0.28 (Imperial version) loses 2-3 marks. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section. Read it 10 minutes before your exam.** - ☐ **Two main failure modes** in KTU Module 3: **Bending** (Lewis) and **Pitting** (Buckingham). The **final module** is the **larger** of the two and rounded up to a **standard value**. - ☐ **Lewis Beam Equation:** $F_B = \sigma_{ob} \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$. The form factor $y$ is a function of tooth count, pressure angle, and tooth system only. - ☐ **Buckingham Wear Equation:** $F_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot K \cdot Q$. The pinion pitch diameter $d_p = m z_p$ is used (smaller gear). - ☐ **Material-Load Factor $K$ formula:** $K = \dfrac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin\phi}{1.4} \left(\dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2}\right)$. For same-material pair: $K = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{es}^2 \sin\phi}{1.4 \, E}$. - ☐ **Ratio Factor $Q$:** External gear → $Q = \dfrac{2 z_g}{z_g+1}$; Internal gear → $Q = \dfrac{2 z_g}{z_g-1}$. Always use the **larger gear's** tooth count. - ☐ **Service Factor $C_s$:** Multiply the **tangential load $W_T$** (not the design power) by $C_s$ before comparing with $F_B$ and $F_W$. - ☐ **Pitting is the most common failure** in industrial gearboxes (~50% of cases). Scoring is common in **high-speed, lightly loaded** gears; tooth breakage is common in **overloaded or shock-loaded** gears. - ☐ **Standard Module Series (mm):** 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50. Always round **up**, never down. - ☐ **Prevention strategies:** - **Bending failure** → Increase module, use stronger material, shot peening, case hardening. - **Pitting failure** → Increase face width, use nitrided steel, improve surface finish, EP lubricants. - **Scoring** → Better viscosity grade, EP additives, lower pitch line velocity. - **Wear** → Seal bearings, filter lubricant (10 μm typical). - ☐ **For 14-mark questions, always show 5-6 intermediate steps** with units — KTU examiners reward stepwise work, not just the final answer. - ☐ **Numerical units check:** $[F_B] = \text{N/mm}^2 \times \text{mm} \times \text{mm} \times \text{dimensionless} = \text{N}$ ✓; $[F_W] = \text{mm} \times \text{mm} \times \text{N/mm}^2 \times \text{dimensionless} = \text{N}$ ✓. - ☐ **Velocity factor $C_v$ (advanced):** $C_v = \dfrac{3}{3+v}$ (Lewis) or $\dfrac{6}{6+v}$ — use when the problem statement specifies it; default is to ignore $C_v$ for KTU standard problems. - ☐ **Pressure angle default in KTU exams:** 20° full-depth involute unless explicitly stated otherwise. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Gear Tooth Failures** > **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE Examination!** 🎓

topics.title: Lewis Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lewis Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Lewis Equation** is a classical gear-tooth strength equation developed by **Wilfred Lewis (1892)**. It is used to determine the **beam strength (static bending strength)** of a gear tooth by treating the tooth as a **cantilever beam of parabolic cross-section** fixed at the root and loaded tangentially at the pitch point (or tip). It provides the maximum tangential load a gear tooth can transmit **without failing in bending**, assuming static loading conditions.

The standard form of the Lewis Equation is:

$$F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Tangential tooth load (N)
- $\sigma_b$ = Allowable bending stress of the gear material (N/mm²)
- $b$ = Face width of the gear (mm)
- $m$ = Module (mm)
- $y$ = **Lewis Form Factor** (dimensionless)

The quantity $\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ is called the **Beam Strength ($F_b$)** of the gear tooth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The Lewis Equation is the foundation of **static gear-tooth design**. It must be clearly understood before progressing to Buckingham's dynamic load equation and wear strength (Hertzian contact stress) analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cantilever Tooth"

Imagine **pushing a door that is hinged at one end** — that is essentially what a gear tooth experiences. The tooth is anchored at the **root (dedendum circle)** like a hinge, and the tangential force from the meshing gear acts near the **tip** like a hand pushing at the end of the door. The tooth bends like a small cantilever beam, and the **maximum bending stress develops at the root fillet** (the curved junction where the tooth meets the gear rim).

Wilfred Lewis, instead of doing complex stress analysis on the curved involute profile, made a brilliant simplification: he **approximated the tooth shape as a parabola** (a shape similar to the involute flank). This made it possible to derive a closed-form engineering equation — now famously known as the **Lewis Equation**.

**Real-world analogy:** Just like a civil engineer uses a simple beam-bending formula ($M/Z$) for a building beam, Lewis used the same principle for a gear tooth. The *Lewis form factor* $y$ acts as a **geometric correction term** that accounts for the actual shape of the involute tooth, the pressure angle, and the number of teeth.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure angle (full-depth involute) | **20°** | Most common in KTU problems |
| Pressure angle (old British standard) | **14.5°** | Legacy systems |
| Module range | **1 mm – 25 mm** | Standard ISO values |
| Lewis form factor range | **0.108 – 0.314** | Increases with teeth number |
| Number of teeth to avoid interference | **≥ 18 (20° full-depth)** | Below this, profile shift needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Note:** The Lewis Form Factor $y$ is a *dimensionless* geometric property of the tooth. It depends on the **pressure angle**, the **tooth system (full-depth/stub)**, and the **number of teeth (N)**. It does **NOT** depend on the module or face width.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Lewis Equation

To use Lewis equation correctly, the following assumptions must be understood:

1. **Static Loading** — The equation considers only static beam strength. It does **NOT** account for dynamic effects, impact loads, or vibrations.
2. **Single Tooth Load** — At any instant, **only one tooth carries the full tangential load**, while the other is just making/unmaking contact.
3. **Cantilever Beam Analogy** — The tooth is modeled as a cantilever beam fixed at the root circle, with the load applied at the **pitch point** (Lewis's simplification) or the tip.
4. **Parabolic Tooth Profile** — The involute tooth flank is approximated as a **parabolic curve**, making the bending analysis mathematically tractable.
5. **Uniform Material** — The gear material is assumed homogeneous and isotropic, with a single allowable bending stress.
6. **No Stress Concentration Considered** — Lewis's original equation ignores stress concentration at the root fillet. (Later modified by various factors.)

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

| Step | Logic | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Cantilever modeling | Tooth rooted at the dedendum circle | Mimics the actual constraint of the gear rim |
| Load at pitch point | Simplifies arm length = $h$ (height to pitch circle) | Gives a conservative, repeatable design value |
| Parabolic approximation | The involute is geometrically similar to a parabola in the critical region | Enables closed-form derivation |
| Bending stress $\sigma_b = M/Z$ | Classic beam theory | Foundation of structural analysis |
| Form factor $y$ introduction | Groups all geometry-dependent terms | Removes complexity from the main equation |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU exam-ready** and frequently appear in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | **Lewis Equation** (basic) | N |
| 2 | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | **Beam Strength** of gear tooth | N |
| 3 | $y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{N}$ | Lewis form factor (**20° full-depth involute**, $N \geq 18$) | – |
| 4 | $y = 0.175 - \dfrac{0.841}{N}$ | Lewis form factor (**20° stub tooth**) | – |
| 5 | $y = 0.124 - \dfrac{0.684}{N}$ | Lewis form factor (**14.5° full-depth involute**) | – |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{1000}$ | Power transmitted (using $v$ in m/s) | kW |
| 7 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N_{rpm}}{60 \times 1000}$ | Pitch line velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $d = m \cdot z$ | Pitch circle diameter | mm |
| 9 | $F_t = \dfrac{2P}{v} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{v}$ | Tangential load from power | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In a meshing pair, the **Lewis form factor of the pinion** is always used in design because the **pinion has fewer teeth** and hence a *smaller* form factor — making it the weaker member. The **design is based on the pinion**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Lewis Equation is the **starting point** of every gear design in industry. It is used in:

- **Automotive gearboxes** — preliminary sizing of spur gears
- **Industrial machinery** — conveyor drives, rolling mills
- **Wind turbine gearboxes** — initial stage spur gear design
- **Machine tool gear trains** — speed reducers

Even though modern standards (AGMA, ISO 6336) use more sophisticated equations with dynamic factors, load distribution factors, and contact stress analysis, **the Lewis Equation remains the foundation of academic gear design** and is **explicitly tested in KTU examinations**.

### 2.5 Limitations of the Lewis Equation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often lose marks by **not stating limitations** when asked to "explain the Lewis equation."

1. Only considers **static bending failure** — ignores fatigue, wear, and scuffing.
2. Assumes **perfect load sharing** — but in reality, load is shared between 1–2 teeth.
3. **No dynamic load factor** — ignores vibration and impact.
4. **No stress concentration** at the root fillet.
5. **Pin load assumed at one point** (pitch point), but the actual line of action shifts.
6. Valid only for **external spur gears** with adequate number of teeth (avoiding undercutting).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Equation

Consider a gear tooth subjected to a tangential load $F_t$ acting at the pitch circle. We model the tooth as a **cantilever beam** of face width $b$, fixed at the root, with the load applied at the pitch point.

#### Step 1: Identify the Cantilever Beam Geometry

The tooth is treated as a cantilever beam:
- **Length (l):** Vertical distance from root to the pitch point = $h$
- **Width (b):** Face width of the gear (perpendicular to the page)
- **Cross-section:** Tooth profile approximated as a **parabola** (Lewis's key insight)

The tangential load $F_t$ is assumed to act along the **line of action**, which makes an angle $\phi$ (pressure angle) with the tangential direction at the pitch point. For simplicity, Lewis resolved this into a tangential component $F_t$ acting at the pitch point.

#### Step 2: Bending Moment at the Root

The maximum bending moment occurs at the **root section** (critical section):

$$M = F_t \cdot h$$

Where $h$ is the **height of the tooth from the root to the pitch point**.

For a standard full-depth involute tooth with addendum $a = m$ and dedendum $d_f = 1.25m$, and assuming the pitch circle lies at the center between the addendum and dedendum, the height to the pitch circle is:

$$h = m + \frac{1.25m}{2} = m + 0.625m = 1.625m$$

However, in the classical Lewis derivation, this is absorbed into the form factor. The bending moment is simply expressed as:

$$M = F_t \cdot h$$

#### Step 3: Section Modulus of a Parabolic Beam

The tooth is approximated as a **parabolic section** with thickness $t$ at the critical (root) section. For a parabolic beam, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot t^2$$

This is the classical result for a parabolic cross-section, where the **bending occurs about the neutral axis** at the centroid of the parabolic area.

#### Step 4: Apply Bending Stress Formula

Using the simple bending equation $\sigma_b = M/Z$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{F_t \cdot h}{\frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot t^2}$$

Rearranging to solve for $F_t$:

$$F_t = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \frac{t^2}{h}$$

#### Step 5: Express Tooth Thickness in Terms of Module

For a standard involute gear, the tooth thickness at the **pitch circle** is half the circular pitch:

$$t = \frac{p_c}{2} = \frac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$$

Substituting into the equation for $F_t$:

$$F_t = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{\pi m}{2}\right)^2}{h}$$

$$F_t = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \frac{\pi^2 \cdot m^2}{4 \cdot h}$$

#### Step 6: Define the Lewis Form Factor

The **Lewis Form Factor** $y$ is defined to consolidate the geometric terms $\left(\frac{\pi^2 m}{6h}\right)$ and the parabolic correction:

$$y = \frac{p_c \cdot t}{6 \cdot h} = \frac{\pi \cdot m \cdot t}{6 \cdot h}$$

Substituting $t = \pi m / 2$ and simplifying gives the standard form:

$$\boxed{F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y}$$

This is the **Lewis Equation** in its final form, where $y$ is the Lewis form factor determined from tooth geometry and pressure angle.

#### Step 7: Empirical Expression for the Form Factor (20° Full-Depth Involute)

Based on detailed geometric analysis of the involute profile, Lewis established the following empirical formula for **20° full-depth involute teeth** with $N \geq 18$:

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{N}$$

**Verification of the form factor formula:**

For a gear with **18 teeth** (minimum to avoid undercutting):

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{18} = 0.154 - 0.0507 = 0.1033$$

For a gear with **30 teeth**:

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{30} = 0.154 - 0.0304 = 0.1236$$

For a gear with **100 teeth**:

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{100} = 0.154 - 0.0091 = 0.1449$$

As $N \to \infty$, $y \to 0.154$ (the limiting form factor for a rack).

This confirms that **the form factor increases with the number of teeth** — meaning larger gears are stronger per tooth in bending. This is why **pinions (fewer teeth) are always the design-limiting member**.

### 3.2 Numerical Demonstration — Beam Strength Calculation

**Problem:** A spur pinion has **18 teeth**, mates with a 60-tooth gear, with **module $m = 5$ mm** and **face width $b = 50$ mm**. The pinion material has an allowable bending stress of $\sigma_b = 100$ N/mm². Find the beam strength.

**Step 1: Calculate the Lewis form factor for the pinion (N = 18):**

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{18}$$

$$y = 0.154 - 0.0507 = 0.1033$$

**Step 2: Apply the Lewis Equation:**

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

$$F_b = 100 \times 50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.1033$$

**Step 3: Compute numerically:**

$$F_b = 100 \times 50 \times 3.1416 \times 5 \times 0.1033$$

$$F_b = 5000 \times 3.1416 \times 0.5165$$

$$F_b = 5000 \times 1.6226 = 8113.1 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_b \approx 8113 \text{ N} \approx 8.11 \text{ kN}}$$

This is the **maximum safe tangential load** the pinion tooth can transmit under static conditions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lewis Form Factor for Different Systems

| Tooth System | Pressure Angle | Form Factor Equation | Range of $N$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full-depth involute | 20° | $y = 0.154 - 0.912/N$ | $N \geq 18$ |
| Stub tooth | 20° | $y = 0.175 - 0.841/N$ | $N \geq 14$ |
| Full-depth involute | 14.5° | $y = 0.124 - 0.684/N$ | $N \geq 32$ |
| Stub tooth | 15° | $y = 0.151 - 0.659/N$ | $N \geq 15$ |
| Full-depth involute | 25° | $y = 0.175 - 0.910/N$ | $N \geq 12$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **20° full-depth involute** system is the **default** in nearly all KTU problems. Always verify the tooth system from the problem statement before selecting the form factor equation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cantilever Beam Model of a Gear Tooth (Mermaid Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual model of a gear tooth as a cantilever beam, the load application point, and the critical root section.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Tangential Force F_t] --> B[Acts at Pitch Point]
    B --> C[Resolves along Line of Action]
    C --> D[Component 1: Tangential F_t]
    C --> E[Component 2: Radial F_r]
    D --> F[Bending Moment M = F_t times h at Root]
    E --> G[Compressive / Tensile on Tooth Flank]
    F --> H[Maximum Bending Stress at Root Fillet]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Beam Strength Check: sigma_b less than sigma_allow}
    I --> J[Safe Design]
    I --> K[Tooth Failure in Bending]
    L[Module m, Face Width b] --> M[Lewis Form Factor y depends on N and phi]
    M --> N[Lewis Equation: F_t = sigma_b times b times pi times m times y]
    N --> I
    P[Number of Teeth N] --> M
    Q[Pressure Angle phi] --> M
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lewis Equation in Gear Design Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT1[Input Power P in kW] --> CALC1[Calculate Pitch Line Velocity v]
    INPUT2[Pinion Speed N in rpm] --> CALC1
    INPUT3[Module m] --> CALC1
    INPUT4[Number of Teeth z] --> CALC1
    CALC1 --> CALC2[Calculate Tangential Tooth Load F_t = 1000P/v]
    INPUT5[Material Property sigma_b] --> LEWIS[Apply Lewis Equation]
    INPUT6[Face Width b] --> LEWIS
    INPUT7[Form Factor y] --> LEWIS
    CALC2 --> CHECK{Compare F_t with Beam Strength F_b}
    LEWIS --> CHECK
    CHECK --> SAFE[F_t less than F_b: Design Safe]
    CHECK --> UNSAFE[F_t greater than F_b: Modify Design]
    UNSAFE --> MOD1[Increase b]
    UNSAFE --> MOD2[Increase m]
    UNSAFE --> MOD3[Increase sigma_b Change Material]
    UNSAFE --> MOD4[Use Profile Shifted Gear]
    MOD1 --> LEWIS
    MOD2 --> LEWIS
    MOD3 --> LEWIS
    MOD4 --> LEWIS
```

### 4.3 Force Resolution at the Pitch Point (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Total Tooth Force W_t] --> RES[Resolve at Pitch Point]
    RES --> FT[Tangential Component F_t]
    RES --> FR[Radial Component F_r]
    FT --> EQ1[F_t = W_t times cos phi]
    FR --> EQ2[F_r = W_t times sin phi]
    FT --> LEWIS[Lewis Equation uses F_t]
    style LEWIS fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid diagrams above map the **logical flow** of how the Lewis Equation integrates with other design parameters (power, speed, materials). The **highlighted block** in yellow represents the core equation application. For physical drawings of gear meshing and tooth profiles, students are advised to refer to the standard textbook figures.

### 4.4 Schematic of the Critical Section in a Gear Tooth

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOP[Tip of Tooth - Addendum Circle] --> MID1[Pitch Point - Pitch Circle]
    MID1 --> MID2[Critical Root Section]
    MID2 --> BOT[Root Fillet - Dedendum Circle]
    BOT --> RIM[Anchored to Gear Rim]
    LOAD[Tangential Load F_t at Pitch Point] --> MID1
    MID1 --> BM[Bending Moment M = F_t times h at Root]
    BM --> STRESS[Maximum Bending Stress sigma_max at Critical Section]
    style STRESS fill:#ff7c80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **red-highlighted block** identifies the **critical section** — the location where maximum bending stress develops and where failure initiates in bending. This is the section whose geometry defines the Lewis form factor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Lewis form factor. State the Lewis equation for gear tooth design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lewis form factor** $y$ is a dimensionless geometric parameter that accounts for the shape of the gear tooth, the pressure angle, and the number of teeth. It is defined as the ratio of the beam strength of the tooth to the maximum stress, considering the tooth as a parabolic cantilever beam.

The Lewis form factor for a **20° full-depth involute tooth system** is:

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{N}$$

where $N$ is the number of teeth on the gear.

**Lewis Equation:**

$$F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

where $F_t$ = tangential load (N), $\sigma_b$ = allowable bending stress (N/mm²), $b$ = face width (mm), $m$ = module (mm), and $y$ = Lewis form factor.

---

**Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four limitations of the Lewis equation in gear design.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The equation considers only **static loading** and does not account for **dynamic loads, vibrations, or impact** during operation.
2. It assumes that **only one tooth carries the full tangential load** at a time, ignoring the actual load-sharing between two or more teeth in contact.
3. The equation **ignores stress concentration** at the root fillet of the tooth, where the actual maximum stress can be significantly higher.
4. It is applicable **only to spur gears** (or simple gear geometries) and does not accurately predict the strength of helical, bevel, or worm gears.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

## QUESTION A (14 Marks)

[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** Derive the Lewis equation for the design of a gear tooth, clearly stating the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The gear tooth is treated as a **cantilever beam** of uniform face width.
2. The tooth profile is approximated as a **parabolic curve**.
3. The tangential load $F_t$ is assumed to act at the **pitch point** of the tooth.
4. The load is **static** and **uniformly distributed** across the face width.
5. Only **one tooth** carries the full load at any instant.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Bending moment at the critical (root) section:

$$M = F_t \cdot h$$

where $h$ is the height of the tooth from the root to the pitch point.

**Step 2:** Section modulus of a parabolic cross-section:

$$Z = \frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot t^2$$

where $b$ is the face width and $t$ is the tooth thickness at the root.

**Step 3:** Maximum bending stress using $\sigma_b = M / Z$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_t \cdot h}{\frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot t^2} = \frac{3 \cdot F_t \cdot h}{2 \cdot b \cdot t^2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $F_t$:

$$F_t = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot t^2}{3 \cdot h}$$

**Step 5:** Express tooth thickness at the pitch circle: $t = \frac{\pi m}{2}$. Substituting and consolidating geometric terms into the form factor $y$:

$$F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

This is the **Lewis Equation**, where $y = \frac{\pi m \cdot t}{6h}$ is the Lewis form factor.

---

**b)** A spur pinion running at 1200 rpm drives a gear with a velocity ratio of 4:1. The pinion has 20 teeth with a module of 3 mm and face width of 30 mm. The allowable bending stress for the pinion material is 120 N/mm². Determine the power that can be safely transmitted using the Lewis equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of teeth on pinion** $N = 20$

**Step 2: Lewis form factor** (20° full-depth involute):

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{20} = 0.154 - 0.0456 = 0.1084$$

**Step 3: Pitch circle diameter of pinion:**

$$d = m \cdot z = 3 \times 20 = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Beam strength using Lewis equation:**

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

$$F_b = 120 \times 30 \times \pi \times 3 \times 0.1084$$

$$F_b = 120 \times 30 \times 3.1416 \times 3 \times 0.1084$$

$$F_b = 10800 \times 3.1416 \times 0.3252$$

$$F_b = 10800 \times 1.0216 = 11033.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Pitch line velocity:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N_{rpm}}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 1200}{60000}$$

$$v = \frac{226195}{60000} = 3.7699 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Power transmitted:**

$$P = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{1000} = \frac{11033.3 \times 3.7699}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 41.59 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## QUESTION B (14 Marks)

[KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**a)** Explain how the Lewis form factor is determined for a 20° full-depth involute tooth system. How does it vary with the number of teeth? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Determination of Lewis Form Factor:**

The Lewis form factor $y$ is determined from the **geometry of the tooth profile** and the **load application point**. For a 20° full-depth involute tooth system with $N \geq 18$ teeth, Lewis established the empirical relation:

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{N}$$

This formula was derived by:
1. Modeling the tooth as a **parabolic cantilever beam**.
2. Calculating the section modulus at the critical root section.
3. Expressing the tooth thickness at the root in terms of the module and pressure angle.
4. Consolidating all geometry-dependent terms into a single factor $y$.

**Variation with Number of Teeth:**

| $N$ | $y$ |
|---|---|
| 18 | 0.1033 |
| 20 | 0.1084 |
| 25 | 0.1175 |
| 30 | 0.1236 |
| 50 | 0.1358 |
| 100 | 0.1449 |
| $\infty$ (Rack) | 0.154 |

**Key observations:**
- As $N$ **increases**, $y$ **increases** and approaches a limiting value of **0.154** (corresponding to a rack).
- The **minimum value** occurs at $N = 18$ (minimum to avoid undercutting).
- Since the **pinion has fewer teeth** than the mating gear, it has a **lower $y$** and hence **lower beam strength**. The design is therefore **always based on the pinion**.

---

**b)** Two 20° full-depth involute spur gears have a velocity ratio of 3:1. The pinion has 20 teeth, module 4 mm, and face width 35 mm. The allowable bending stress for both gears is 80 N/mm². Using the Lewis equation, find the safe tangential load and the power that can be transmitted at 900 rpm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Lewis form factor of pinion** ($N = 20$):

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{20} = 0.154 - 0.0456 = 0.1084$$

**Step 2: Beam strength (Lewis equation):**

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

$$F_b = 80 \times 35 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.1084$$

$$F_b = 80 \times 35 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.1084$$

$$F_b = 2800 \times 3.1416 \times 0.4336$$

$$F_b = 2800 \times 1.3621 = 3813.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Safe tangential load** $F_t = F_b \approx 3813.9$ N (since design is based on the weaker pinion).

**Step 4: Pitch circle diameter of pinion:**

$$d = m \cdot z = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Pitch line velocity:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 900}{60000} = \frac{226195}{60000} = 3.7699 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Power transmitted:**

$$P = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{1000} = \frac{3813.9 \times 3.7699}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 14.38 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Using the gear's form factor instead of the pinion's** — Always design based on the **pinion** (fewer teeth, lower $y$). This is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — Power must be in **kW**, velocity in **m/s**, and dimensions in **mm**. Mixing units gives wrong answers and full mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong form factor formula** — For 20° full-depth involute, use $y = 0.154 - 0.912/N$. For stub teeth or 14.5° systems, the formula changes.
> 4. **Not stating the equation properly** — When asked to "state Lewis equation," write the **complete formula with all variables defined** in one go. Vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Missing assumptions in derivation** — Examiners allocate at least **1–2 marks** for clearly listing assumptions before the derivation.
> 6. **Velocity formula errors** — Use $v = \pi d N / 60000$ with $d$ in mm and $N$ in rpm to get $v$ in m/s.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist — Lewis Equation**

- ✦ The **Lewis Equation** gives the **static beam strength** of a gear tooth, assuming a **cantilever beam** model with a **parabolic cross-section**.
- ✦ Standard form: $F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ (or $F_b$ for beam strength).
- ✦ The **Lewis form factor** $y$ depends only on **pressure angle**, **tooth system**, and **number of teeth** — NOT on module or face width.
- ✦ For **20° full-depth involute** (default in KTU): $y = 0.154 - 0.912/N$, valid for $N \geq 18$.
- ✦ The **pinion (fewer teeth) is always the design-limiting member** because $y$ is smaller.
- ✦ The form factor **increases with $N$** and approaches **0.154** as $N \to \infty$ (rack condition).
- ✦ Pitch line velocity: $v = \pi d N / 60000$ (m/s, with $d$ in mm, $N$ in rpm).
- ✦ Power relation: $P = F_t \cdot v / 1000$ (kW).
- ✦ **Limitations:** static analysis only, no dynamic load factor, no stress concentration, no wear analysis — for these, Buckingham's equation and Hertzian contact stress equations are needed.
- ✦ The Lewis Equation is the **first step** in gear design; it must be followed by **dynamic load analysis** and **wear strength check** for a complete design.

---

**End of Notes — Lewis Equation (Module 3, PCRUT502, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buckingham's Equation for Dynamic Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Buckingham's Equation for Dynamic Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckingham's Equation for Dynamic Load** is an analytical relationship proposed by **Edgar Buckingham (1898)** to quantify the **total effective load transmitted by a pair of meshing gear teeth**, accounting for the impact and inertial effects caused by manufacturing inaccuracies, tooth deflection, and pitch line velocity. It expresses the dynamic load as the sum of the steady tangential (transmitted) load and a **dynamic increment** arising from tooth deformation and engagement impact.

Mathematically, the total dynamic load is expressed as:

$$W_d = W_t + W_i$$

where $W_t$ is the **static tangential (transmitted) load** and $W_i$ is the **dynamic increment** (impact load). The dynamic increment is given by Buckingham's empirical formulation:

$$W_i = \frac{21 \, v \, b \, C}{21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$$

This is the canonical Buckingham's equation used in the KTU 2024 scheme for the design of spur gears, helical gears, and other power-transmitting toothed elements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bumpy Train Track**
> Imagine two metallic train tracks (the gear teeth) meeting at a coupling. When the train moves slowly, the carriages couple with a **soft, predictable click** (low dynamic load). However, when the train moves fast, even a **tiny misalignment** in the tracks causes the coupling to **slap, bounce, and hammer** (high dynamic load). The faster the train and the more uneven the tracks, the harder the impact.

**What this analogy means for gears:**
- $W_t$ (static load) = the steady force the gear is *supposed* to carry.
- $W_i$ (dynamic increment) = the **extra hammering force** that appears at high speeds due to tooth deflection, errors in tooth profile, and misalignment.
- $v$ (pitch line velocity) = how fast the train is moving.
- $b$ (face width) = the **width of the track** (a wider track can absorb more load).
- $C$ (deformation factor) = the **stiffness/resilience** of the track material.

So Buckingham's equation essentially says: **"The total load on a gear tooth equals the useful load PLUS the extra impact caused by speed and material compliance."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following boldfaced constants and parameters are central to Buckingham's equation:

- **21** — Empirical constant (with units: $\sqrt{\text{m/s} \cdot \text{mm/N}}$) derived by Buckingham from extensive gear-testing experiments.
- **C** — Deformation factor (in N/(mm·m/s)); depends on the **elastic modulus** of the pinion and gear materials and the **pressure angle**.
- **Standard values of C (per AGMA):**
    - Steel with Steel: $C \approx 114.6$ N/(mm·m/s)
    - Cast Iron with Steel: $C \approx 110.0$ N/(mm·m/s)
    - Phosphor Bronze with Steel: $C \approx 122.0$ N/(mm·m/s)
- **b** — Face width (mm).
- **v** — Pitch line velocity (m/s).
- **$W_t$** — Tangential load (N) = $\frac{P \times 1000}{v}$ (where P is power in kW).

## 1.4 Why Buckingham's Equation is Preferred over the Velocity Factor

> [!NOTE]
> The classical **Lewis velocity factor** ($C_v = \frac{A}{A+v}$) gives reasonable results at low speeds but **fails at high velocities** ($v > 10$ m/s). Buckingham's equation is preferred because it is derived from **rigorous impact and energy considerations** and is valid across the entire practical speed range of gear operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Basis of Buckingham's Equation

Buckingham's analysis rests on three foundational observations from gear-testing experiments:

1. **Static Load ($W_t$):** The steady tangential force transmitted across the pitch circle, dictated purely by the torque and pitch radius.
2. **Inertia and Impact at Engagement:** As the velocity increases, the time available for a tooth pair to engage decreases. The relative velocity between the driving and driven flanks causes an **impact** that superimposes a dynamic increment $W_i$ on the static load.
3. **Tooth Deformation and Energy Storage:** The tooth acts as a **spring**, storing strain energy during engagement. The energy stored is proportional to the deformation constant $C$, the face width $b$, and the square of the deformation per unit load.

## 2.2 Logical Derivation Steps (Buckingham's Original Approach)

- **Step 1 — Energy of Impact:** When a pair of teeth engage, the relative velocity $v$ generates a kinetic energy of impact. This energy is absorbed by the elastic deformation of the contacting teeth.
- **Step 2 — Deformation Energy:** The strain energy stored due to a deformation $\delta$ across a face width $b$ and an effective elastic compliance $C$ is given by:
$$U_{strain} = \frac{W^2 \cdot b \cdot C}{2}$$
- **Step 3 — Energy Equating:** Equating the kinetic energy of impact to the strain energy absorbed by the tooth pair:
$$\frac{W_i^2 \cdot b \cdot C}{2} = K \cdot v$$
where K is a constant combining geometric and material factors.
- **Step 4 — Solving for $W_i$:** After introducing the empirical constants and simplifying using test data:
$$W_i = \frac{21 \, v \, b \, C}{21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$$
- **Step 5 — Total Dynamic Load:** The total effective load that the tooth must resist:
$$W_d = W_t + \frac{21 \, v \, b \, C}{21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Standard Unit** |
|------------|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| $W_t$ | Static tangential load | $\frac{P \times 1000}{v}$ | N |
| $W_i$ | Dynamic increment (impact) | $\dfrac{21 \, v \, b \, C}{21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$ | N |
| $W_d$ | Total dynamic load | $W_t + W_i$ | N |
| $v$ | Pitch line velocity | $\dfrac{\pi \, d \, N}{60}$ | m/s |
| $C$ | Deformation factor | $0.111 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{E_p} + \dfrac{1}{E_g}}$ (simplified form) | N/(mm·m/s) |
| $b$ | Face width | Design parameter ($6m$ to $10m$ for spur gears) | mm |
| $P$ | Power transmitted | Given in problem | kW |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | Given in problem | rpm |
| $d$ | Pitch circle diameter | $m \times z$ | mm |
| $\sigma_b$ | Bending stress (Lewis form) | $\dfrac{W_d}{b \, \pi \, m \, y}$ | N/mm² |
| $y$ | Lewis form factor | From standard tables (function of $z$) | dimensionless |
| $FOS$ | Factor of safety | $\dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_b}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** The term $21 \, v \, b \, C$ has units of $\text{m/s} \times \text{mm} \times \text{N/(mm·m/s)} = \text{N}$. The term $\sqrt{W_t}$ has units of $\sqrt{\text{N}}$. To reconcile, the constant **21** carries the unit $\sqrt{\text{N} \cdot \text{s} / (\text{m} \cdot \text{mm})}$ to make the equation dimensionally consistent. **Always keep units consistent** when solving numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Buckingham's equation is indispensable in the following engineering applications:

- **Automotive Gearboxes:** Designing spur and helical gears that withstand shock loads at high RPMs.
- **Industrial Gear Reducers:** Predicting dynamic tooth loads for ratings of gearbox assemblies per AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association) and IS standards.
- **Aerospace Transmissions:** High-speed gear trains in helicopter and turboprop transmissions.
- **Wind Turbine Gearboxes:** Variable load and speed conditions demand accurate dynamic load estimation to predict fatigue life.
- **CNC Machine Spindles:** Precision gears that operate at high speeds with tight tolerances.

## 2.5 Conditions and Limitations

- **Valid Range:** The equation is valid for $v \leq 20$ m/s for most standard gear materials. For $v > 20$ m/s, special high-precision gears with profile modifications are required.
- **Profile Error:** Buckingham assumed small profile errors; the equation is less accurate for severely worn or misaligned gears.
- **Single Pair of Teeth:** The equation assumes that only **one pair of teeth** carries the load at the pitch point (true for low $v$). For high accuracy, the load-sharing ratio must be considered.
- **Lubrication Effects:** The constant $C$ implicitly assumes hydrodynamic or boundary lubrication. Starved lubrication can substantially increase $C$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of Buckingham's Dynamic Increment

**Given:**
- A pair of meshing gear teeth with pitch line velocity $v$ (m/s).
- Effective mass $m_{eff}$ of the engaging tooth pair.
- Tooth deformation under load characterized by compliance $C$ and face width $b$.

**To derive:** The dynamic increment $W_i$ added to the static load $W_t$.

### Step 1 — Model the Tooth Pair as a Spring

When the tooth deflects by a small amount $\delta$ under a load $W$, the strain energy stored in the tooth is:

$$U_{strain} = \frac{1}{2} \, W \, \delta$$

The deflection per unit load is the **deformation factor** times some length term. Buckingham's empirical constant $C$ combines this deflection coefficient with the face width $b$:

$$\delta = \frac{W \cdot C}{b} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad U_{strain} = \frac{1}{2} \, \frac{W^2 \, C}{b}$$

Multiplying by face width to get the total strain energy of the tooth pair:

$$U_{strain, total} = \frac{W^2 \, b \, C}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Model the Inertial Impact

The effective mass of the engaging tooth pair (a small portion of the gear) carries the velocity $v$. The kinetic energy available for impact per unit cycle is:

$$U_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \, m_{eff} \, v^2$$

Buckingham's experimental data allowed him to lump the mass and geometric factors into a single empirical constant $K$:

$$U_{kinetic} = K \, v$$

where $K$ has units that make the equation dimensionally consistent.

### Step 3 — Energy Equating (Conservation of Energy)

The kinetic energy of impact is absorbed by the strain energy of the tooth deformation:

$$\frac{W_i^2 \, b \, C}{2} = K \, v$$

Solving for $W_i$:

$$W_i = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, K \, v}{b \, C}}$$

### Step 4 — Empirical Calibration

Buckingham's experiments showed that the dynamic increment is not purely inertial — it also depends on the static load (because a stiffer system reduces the relative impact). He proposed the modified form:

$$W_i = \frac{K_1 \, v \, b \, C}{K_1 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$$

By fitting experimental data for standard gears, the constant $K_1$ was found to be **21** (with appropriate units):

$$W_i = \frac{21 \, v \, b \, C}{21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$$

### Step 5 — Total Dynamic Load

The total load that the gear tooth must resist is the **sum** of the static tangential load and the dynamic increment:

$$W_d = W_t + W_i = W_t + \frac{21 \, v \, b \, C}{21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}}$$

This is **Buckingham's Equation for Dynamic Load** as used in KTU 2024 scheme design problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Numerical Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A pair of spur gears with 20° full-depth involute teeth is used to transmit 10 kW at 1200 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 60 teeth. The face width is 50 mm. Both gears are made of steel with a deformation factor $C = 114.6$ N/(mm·m/s). Calculate:
> (a) The static tangential load $W_t$.
> (b) The dynamic increment $W_i$ using Buckingham's equation.
> (c) The total dynamic load $W_d$.
> (d) The bending stress using the Lewis equation (form factor $y = 0.32$ for 18 teeth).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Pitch Line Velocity**

Let the module be $m = 5$ mm (assumed, since not specified).

The pitch circle diameter of the pinion is:

$$d_p = m \times z_p = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090 \text{ m}$$

The pitch line velocity is:

$$v = \frac{\pi \times d_p \times N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.090 \times 1200}{60}$$

\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\pi \times 0.090 \times 1200}{60} \\
&= \frac{339.292}{60} \\
&= 5.6549 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Static Tangential Load**

The power transmitted is $P = 10$ kW.

$$W_t = \frac{P \times 1000}{v} = \frac{10 \times 1000}{5.6549}$$

\begin{aligned}
W_t &= \frac{10000}{5.6549} \\
&= 1768.4 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Dynamic Increment $W_i$ (Buckingham's Equation)**

Given:
- $v = 5.6549$ m/s
- $b = 50$ mm
- $C = 114.6$ N/(mm·m/s)
- $W_t = 1768.4$ N
- Empirical constant = 21

First, compute the numerator $21 \, v \, b \, C$:

\begin{aligned}
21 \, v \, b \, C &= 21 \times 5.6549 \times 50 \times 114.6 \\
&= 21 \times 5.6549 \times 5730 \\
&= 21 \times 32402.6 \\
&= 680454.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

Next, compute $\sqrt{W_t}$:

\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{W_t} &= \sqrt{1768.4} \\
&= 42.05 \sqrt{\text{N}}
\end{aligned}

Now compute the denominator $21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t}$:

\begin{aligned}
21 \, v + \sqrt{W_t} &= 21 \times 5.6549 + 42.05 \\
&= 118.75 + 42.05 \\
&= 160.80
\end{aligned}

Therefore:

\begin{aligned}
W_i &= \frac{680454.6}{160.80} \\
&= 4231.7 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Total Dynamic Load**

\begin{aligned}
W_d &= W_t + W_i \\
&= 1768.4 + 4231.7 \\
&= 6000.1 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Bending Stress (Lewis Equation with Buckingham's Dynamic Load)**

The Lewis bending stress equation is:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{W_d}{b \, \pi \, m \, y}$$

Substituting the values:

\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{6000.1}{50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.32} \\
&= \frac{6000.1}{251.33} \\
&= 23.87 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** In this example, the dynamic increment ($4231.7$ N) is **larger** than the static load ($1768.4$ N). This is because at moderate velocities, the impact effects dominate. This highlights why Buckingham's equation is **superior to the simple velocity factor method**, which would have given a much smaller dynamic load for the same velocity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Buckingham vs. Lewis Velocity Factor

| **Aspect** | **Lewis Velocity Factor** | **Buckingham's Equation** |
|------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Form** | $W_d = \dfrac{W_t}{C_v}$ where $C_v = \dfrac{A}{A+v}$ | $W_d = W_t + \dfrac{21v \, b \, C}{21v + \sqrt{W_t}}$ |
| **Basis** | Empirical curve fit to gear-test data | Energy conservation + empirical constants |
| **Accuracy at low $v$** | Good | Good |
| **Accuracy at high $v$** | Poor (overestimates $W_d$) | Excellent |
| **Incorporates face width $b$** | No | Yes (explicitly) |
| **Incorporates material $C$** | Indirectly (via constant A) | Yes (explicitly) |
| **KTU Preferred** | Older problems only | **All new KTU 2024 problems** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Buckingham's Dynamic Load Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Parameters: P, N, m, z_p, z_g, b, C]:::inputNode
    nodeB[Compute Pitch Diameter: d = m × z]:::processNode
    nodeC[Compute Pitch Line Velocity: v = π d N / 60]:::processNode
    nodeD[Compute Static Load: W_t = 1000 P / v]:::processNode
    nodeE[Compute Dynamic Increment Numerator: 21 v b C]:::processNode
    nodeF[Compute Square Root Term: sqrt W_t]:::processNode
    nodeG[Sum Denominator: 21 v + sqrt W_t]:::processNode
    nodeH[Compute Dynamic Increment: W_i = 21 v b C / Denominator]:::processNode
    nodeI[Compute Total Dynamic Load: W_d = W_t + W_i]:::processNode
    nodeJ[Apply Lewis Equation: sigma_b = W_d / b pi m y]:::processNode
    nodeK[Output: Dynamic Load W_d and Bending Stress sigma_b]:::outputNode
    
    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeA --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    
    classDef inputNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef processNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef outputNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart traces the **computation pipeline** for Buckingham's dynamic load, starting from the input parameters (top-left), passing through the calculation of static and dynamic components (middle), and ending with the design output (right).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy and Force Components in Buckingham's Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE [Energy and Force Source]
        direction TB
        eng1[Static Tangential Load W_t]:::staticLoad
        eng2[Pitch Line Velocity v]:::velocity
        eng3[Face Width b]:::geometry
        eng4[Deformation Factor C]:::material
    end
    
    subgraph TRANSFORM [Transformation Stage]
        direction TB
        tr1[Kinetic Energy of Impact: U_k = K v]:::energy
        tr2[Strain Energy of Tooth: U_s = W_i^2 b C / 2]:::energy
        tr3[Energy Conservation: U_k = U_s]:::energy
        tr4[Buckingham Empirical Calibration]:::calibration
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT [Dynamic Load Output]
        direction TB
        out1[Dynamic Increment W_i]:::dynamic
        out2[Total Dynamic Load W_d = W_t + W_i]:::dynamic
        out3[Bending Stress sigma_b = W_d / b pi m y]:::stress
    end
    
    eng1 --> tr1
    eng2 --> tr1
    eng3 --> tr2
    eng4 --> tr2
    tr1 --> tr3
    tr2 --> tr3
    tr3 --> tr4
    tr4 --> out1
    eng1 --> out2
    out1 --> out2
    out2 --> out3

    classDef velocity fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef geometry fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92
    classDef material fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20

    classDef calibration fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C

    classDef stress fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#004D40
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Behaviour of $W_d$ vs. Velocity

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LowVelocity : v < 5 m/s
    LowVelocity --> ModerateVelocity : 5 to 10 m/s
    ModerateVelocity --> HighVelocity : 10 to 20 m/s
    HighVelocity --> VeryHighVelocity : v > 20 m/s
    
    LowVelocity : Static Dominant : W_t dominates, W_i small
    ModerateVelocity : Impact Significant : W_i comparable to W_t
    HighVelocity : Impact Dominant : W_i > W_t, design critical
    VeryHighVelocity : Precision Required : Profile modification mandatory
    
    VeryHighVelocity --> [*] : Buckingham's equation needs modification
```

> **Interpretation:** As velocity increases, the relative contribution of the dynamic increment $W_i$ grows nonlinearly. At low speeds, the simple static design is adequate; at high speeds, the impact component dominates and Buckingham's equation is **essential** for safe design.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "dynamic load" as applied to gear tooth design. Why is Buckingham's equation preferred over the Lewis velocity factor method?**

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Load:** The dynamic load on a gear tooth is the total effective load acting on the tooth during meshing. It is the sum of the steady tangential (static) load and the additional impact load caused by tooth deformation, manufacturing errors, and high engagement velocity. Mathematically:

$$W_d = W_t + W_i$$

**Why Buckingham is Preferred:**

1. **Accuracy at High Velocities:** The Lewis velocity factor method becomes unreliable for pitch line velocities greater than 10 m/s, while Buckingham's equation remains valid up to 20 m/s.
2. **Explicit Material Properties:** Buckingham's equation explicitly incorporates the deformation factor $C$ (a function of Young's modulus) and the face width $b$, which the Lewis method does not.
3. **Energy-Based Derivation:** Buckingham's equation is derived from the conservation of energy (kinetic energy of impact = strain energy of tooth deflection), giving it a stronger physical foundation.
4. **Better Predicts Impact Loads:** The equation accounts for the additional impact due to tooth profile errors, which is critical in high-speed applications such as automotive and aerospace gearboxes.

---

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: List the factors that influence the dynamic increment $W_i$ in Buckingham's equation. Explain the role of the deformation factor $C$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Factors influencing $W_i$:**

1. **Pitch Line Velocity ($v$):** Higher velocity increases the kinetic energy of impact, raising $W_i$ nonlinearly.
2. **Face Width ($b$):** Wider gears distribute the load and absorb more energy, slightly reducing $W_i$ per unit width (but the total $W_i$ still scales with $b$).
3. **Deformation Factor ($C$):** Higher $C$ (softer materials) increases $W_i$ because the tooth deforms more under impact.
4. **Static Load ($W_t$):** The denominator contains $\sqrt{W_t}$, so a higher static load reduces the relative impact of $W_i$.

**Role of Deformation Factor $C$:**

The deformation factor $C$ is an **empirical material-property constant** that quantifies the elastic compliance of the gear tooth. It depends on the **Young's modulus** of both the pinion and the gear materials and the **pressure angle** of the tooth. Higher $C$ means the tooth is **softer** (deforms more under load) and vice versa.

- **Steel with Steel:** $C \approx 114.6$ N/(mm·m/s)
- **Cast Iron with Steel:** $C \approx 110.0$ N/(mm·m/s)
- **Phosphor Bronze with Steel:** $C \approx 122.0$ N/(mm·m/s)

Mathematically, $C$ is related to the elastic modulus by:

$$C = 0.111 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}}$$

where $E_p$ and $E_g$ are the Young's moduli of the pinion and gear in N/mm².

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix units — $v$ in m/s but $b$ in mm without converting. The constant 21 has implicit units, so mixing mm and m for length will give **wrong answers**. Always declare units clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{W_t}$ Term:** A common error is to write $W_i = 21 v b C$ (missing the denominator). The full denominator is $21v + \sqrt{W_t}$.
> 3. **Confusing $C$ (deformation factor) with $C_v$ (velocity factor):** These are two different constants. $C$ is for Buckingham, $C_v$ is for Lewis.
> 4. **Skipping the Face Width Assumption:** If the problem doesn't specify $b$, assume $b = 10m$ for spur gears (or as given in the data tables).
> 5. **Not Verifying Lewis Form Factor:** The Lewis form factor $y$ is a function of the number of teeth. Always confirm $y$ from the standard 20° full-depth involute table.

---

### Question B.1 — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — **OPTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q: A pair of 20° full-depth involute spur gears is used to transmit 15 kW at 1440 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 20 teeth and the gear has 50 teeth. The module is 4 mm, and the face width is 40 mm. Both gears are made of steel with a deformation factor $C = 114.6$ N/(mm·m/s).**

**(a) (7 marks)** Calculate the static tangential load and the dynamic load on the gear tooth using Buckingham's equation.

**(b) (7 marks)** Using the Lewis equation, determine the bending stress in the pinion tooth. Take the Lewis form factor $y = 0.320$ for 20 teeth and an allowable bending stress of 100 N/mm². Comment on the safety of the design.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter of Pinion**

$$d_p = m \times z_p = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Pitch Line Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times d_p \times N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.080 \times 1440}{60}$$

\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{362.0}{60} \\
&= 6.032 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Static Tangential Load**

$$W_t = \frac{P \times 1000}{v} = \frac{15 \times 1000}{6.032} = 2487.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Increment (Buckingham's Equation)**

\begin{aligned}
W_i &= \frac{21 \times v \times b \times C}{21 \times v + \sqrt{W_t}} \\
&= \frac{21 \times 6.032 \times 40 \times 114.6}{21 \times 6.032 + \sqrt{2487.0}} \\
&= \frac{21 \times 6.032 \times 40 \times 114.6}{126.67 + 49.87} \\
&= \frac{580{,}700}{176.54} \\
&= 3289.5 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Total Dynamic Load**

$$W_d = W_t + W_i = 2487.0 + 3289.5 = 5776.5 \text{ N}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Lewis Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{W_d}{b \times \pi \times m \times y} = \frac{5776.5}{40 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.320}$$

\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{5776.5}{160.85} \\
&= 35.91 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Factor of Safety**

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_{allowable}}{\sigma_b} = \frac{100}{35.91} = 2.78$$

**Step 3 — Design Comment**

Since the factor of safety (2.78) is **greater than 1**, the design is **safe** under static considerations. However, for sustained dynamic loading, KTU recommends a minimum FOS of 1.5 for steady loads and 2.0 for fluctuating loads. With $FOS = 2.78$, the design has an adequate margin and is **safe for long-term operation**.

---

### Question B.2 — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — **OPTION B (14 Marks)**

**Q: Two mating spur gears have 18 and 72 teeth with a module of 5 mm and face width of 50 mm. The pinion rotates at 900 rpm and transmits 7.5 kW. The deformation factor for the steel pair is $C = 114.6$ N/(mm·m/s).**

**(a) (7 marks)** Determine the pitch line velocity, the static tangential load, and the dynamic load using Buckingham's equation.

**(b) (7 marks)** Calculate the dynamic factor and the effective load. Compare the result with the Lewis velocity factor method (take $A = 50$ for the Lewis constant).

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter of Pinion**

$$d_p = m \times z_p = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Pitch Line Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.090 \times 900}{60} = \frac{254.47}{60} = 4.241 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Static Tangential Load**

$$W_t = \frac{7.5 \times 1000}{4.241} = 1768.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Increment**

\begin{aligned}
W_i &= \frac{21 \times 4.241 \times 50 \times 114.6}{21 \times 4.241 + \sqrt{1768.4}} \\
&= \frac{21 \times 4.241 \times 50 \times 114.6}{89.06 + 42.05} \\
&= \frac{510{,}340}{131.11} \\
&= 3892.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Total Dynamic Load**

$$W_d = 1768.4 + 3892.3 = 5660.7 \text{ N}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Dynamic Factor (Buckingham)**

$$C_d = \frac{W_d}{W_t} = \frac{5660.7}{1768.4} = 3.20$$

**Step 2 — Effective Load**

The effective design load for stress calculations is the dynamic load:

$$W_{eff} = W_d = 5660.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Lewis Velocity Factor**

$$C_v = \frac{A}{A + v} = \frac{50}{50 + 4.241} = \frac{50}{54.241} = 0.9218$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Load by Lewis Method**

$$W_{d,Lewis} = \frac{W_t}{C_v} = \frac{1768.4}{0.9218} = 1918.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison**

| **Method** | **Dynamic Load (N)** | **% of Static Load** |
|------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Static (No factor) | 1768.4 | 100% |
| Lewis Method | 1918.4 | 108.5% |
| **Buckingham** | **5660.7** | **320.1%** |

**Conclusion:** Buckingham's equation predicts a significantly higher dynamic load (3.20× the static) than the Lewis method (1.085× the static). This is because at the given velocity, the impact effects on the tooth are substantial, and Buckingham's energy-based model captures this rigorously. **For KTU 2024 examinations, Buckingham's equation is the preferred method** for all but the simplest introductory problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Buckingham's Equation** = $W_d = W_t + \dfrac{21 v b C}{21 v + \sqrt{W_t}}$.
- ✅ **The constant 21** is empirical; do not change it.
- ✅ **Deformation factor $C$** for steel-steel is $114.6$ N/(mm·m/s).
- ✅ **Pitch line velocity** $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ (m/s) — always convert to m/s.
- ✅ **Static tangential load** $W_t = \dfrac{1000 P}{v}$ (where P in kW).
- ✅ **Buckingham is preferred over Lewis** for $v > 5$ m/s.
- ✅ **Always check units:** $v$ in m/s, $b$ in mm, $C$ in N/(mm·m/s), $W$ in N.
- ✅ **Lewis bending stress** with dynamic load: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_d}{b \pi m y}$.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety** must be $\geq 1.5$ for steady loads, $\geq 2.0$ for fluctuating loads.
- ✅ **Face width** for spur gears: typically $b = 6m$ to $10m$; default to $10m$ if not specified.
- ✅ **Lewis form factor $y$** depends on number of teeth; refer to standard 20° full-depth involute table.
- ✅ **Buckingham's equation accounts for:** (1) pitch line velocity, (2) face width, (3) material elasticity, (4) impact amplification.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Valid up to $v = 20$ m/s; for higher speeds, profile modification is needed.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 pattern:** Direct numerical on dynamic load using Buckingham is worth 7–8 marks.
- ✅ **Common examiner traps:** Missing the $\sqrt{W_t}$ term, wrong units for $C$, confusing $C$ with $C_v$.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Buckingham's Equation for Dynamic Load.**
> For further practice, solve at least **three numerical problems** of varying velocity (low, moderate, and high $v$) to internalize the trend of $W_d$ vs. $v$. Always verify your answers by computing both Buckingham's and Lewis's methods and comparing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
