topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wear Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 3 – Wear Load

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wear Load (Wearable Load, P_w):** The maximum normal contact load (or the maximum product of pressure and sliding velocity, $p \cdot v$) that a machine element's friction surface can sustain over its design life **without undergoing unacceptable material loss** due to surface degradation mechanisms (abrasion, adhesion, oxidation, or surface fatigue).

In the context of **clutches and brakes** (the principal application of Module 3 of PCRUT502), the *wear load* is specifically the **permissible axial clamping force** that the friction plate can transmit without the rate of volumetric wear exceeding a safe threshold during the engaged period of operation.

The governing wear criterion is expressed as:

$$p \cdot v \le [p \cdot v]_{lim}$$

where $p$ is the nominal contact pressure, $v$ is the sliding velocity at the radius under consideration, and $[p \cdot v]_{lim}$ is the **limiting pressure–velocity product** supplied by material/coupling manufacturers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine rubbing a piece of chalk on a blackboard. The harder you press (higher $p$) and the faster you rub (higher $v$), the more chalk dust (wear debris) you generate. If you rub slowly and gently, the chalk lasts a long time. Now imagine a clutch plate doing exactly the same thing inside a gearbox: every time it engages, microscopic material is shaved off. The **"wear load" is simply the maximum pressing force the engineer is allowed to use** so that this shaving-off process stays within safe limits for the entire design life.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Magnitude (friction lining on cast iron) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $[p \cdot v]_{lim}$ | Limiting p·v product | **0.35 – 0.70 MPa·m/s** (dry) ; up to **1.5 MPa·m/s** (wet/oil-cooled) |
| $K$ | Archard wear coefficient | **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$** (dimensionless, material pair dependent) |
| $H$ | Brinell hardness of softer member | 200 – 500 BHN (typical) |
| $W_w$ | Wear-limited axial load (N) | Problem dependent |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | 0.2 – 0.5 (dry), 0.05 – 0.15 (wet) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Wear – The "Why"

Material is removed from a contacting surface when two bodies undergo **relative tangential motion under a normal load**. Four principal wear mechanisms operate, often simultaneously:

- **Abrasive Wear:** Hard asperities or trapped particles plough the softer surface (dominant in clutches with dust ingress).
- **Adhesive Wear:** Microwelds formed at asperity junctions are sheared; one fragment transfers to the other surface (Metallic contact, dry clutches).
- **Corrosive (Oxidative) Wear:** Repeated sliding removes protective oxide films, exposing fresh metal that re-oxidises.
- **Surface Fatigue (Pitting/Spalling):** Cyclic sub-surface shear stress initiates microcracks that eventually flake off (dominant in rolling/sliding bearings, less so in clutches).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why p·v matters:** The volume of material removed per unit time is *proportional* to the *normal load* and the *sliding distance per unit time* (which equals velocity). Thus rate of wear $\propto p \cdot v$. This is the empirical foundation of the p·v criterion.

### 2.2 Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation (Foundational Law)

The classical statement of wear volume, derived by **J.F. Archard (1953)**, is:

$$V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

Differentiating with respect to time (with sliding velocity $v = dL/dt$):

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot v}{H}$$

### 2.3 Wear Criterion for a Friction Couple (Clutch / Brake)

For a disc friction element, the contact pressure at radius $r$ and the local velocity are:

$$p(r) = \frac{dW}{2 \pi r \, dr}, \quad v(r) = r \, \omega$$

Hence, the **local p·v product** is $p(r) \cdot v(r)$.

The **wear-limited clamping force** is obtained by enforcing the maximum local $p \cdot v$ to equal the allowable limit:

$$p_{max} \cdot v_{max} = [p \cdot v]_{lim}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table must be memorised; it covers **100% of wear-load numerical questions** asked in KTU ESE for Module 3.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V = KWL/H$ | Archard's volume of wear | General wear |
| 2 | $\dot{V} = KWv/H$ | Wear rate (volume/time) | General wear |
| 3 | $W_w = \dfrac{[p v]_{lim} \cdot 2 \pi b (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{\omega}$ | Wear-limited axial load, **uniform pressure** | Single/multi disc clutch |
| 4 | $W_w = 2 \pi [p v]_{lim} \cdot b \cdot (r_2 - r_1)/\omega$ | Wear-limited axial load, **uniform wear** | Single/multi disc clutch |
| 5 | $p_{max} \cdot v_{max} = [p v]_{lim}$ | Wear criterion | Design check |
| 6 | $W_w = \dfrac{[p] \cdot \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{n}$ | Pressure-limited axial load | Clutch / brake |
| 7 | $W_{H} = \dfrac{2 \pi n \mu [p v]_{lim} (r_2^3 - r_1^3)}{3 \omega}$ | Heat-generation limited load | Clutch engagement |
| 8 | $W_{min}$ vs $W_{max}$ envelope | Serviceable operating window | Final design selection |
| 9 | $K_a = K_o + K_m$ | Application / service factor | Real design load |
| 10 | $t = \dfrac{I \omega}{n \mu W r_m}$ | Engagement time (kinetic) | Slip duration check |

**Notation glossary:**
- $b$ = face width (axial) of friction disc
- $r_1, r_2$ = inner & outer radii of friction surface
- $\omega$ = angular speed in rad/s
- $n$ = number of friction surfaces
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of rotating masses
- $K_a$ = application factor, $K_o$ = operational factor, $K_m$ = mounting factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The p·v criterion is the *universal first-pass check* in clutch, brake, and friction-drive design. Failures traced to "scoring," "glazing," or "premature lining replacement" in industrial data sheets almost always correlate with $p \cdot v > [p \cdot v]_{lim}$. The same logic scales up to aircraft brake stacks and down to MEMS actuators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Wear-Limited Load for a Single-Disc Clutch (Uniform Pressure Assumption)

**Assumption (uniform pressure):** $p = \text{constant}$ across the annular friction face of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, with axial clamping force $W$.

**Step 1 — Express pressure in terms of the axial load.**

The total normal force on the annular face equals the integral of pressure over the area:

$$W = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p \, dA = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p \, (2 \pi r) \, dr = 2 \pi p \int_{r_1}^{r_2} r \, dr$$

Carrying out the elementary integration:

$$W = 2 \pi p \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \pi p (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p = \frac{W}{\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum velocity.**

The velocity at any radius $r$ is $v(r) = r \omega$, so the **maximum** velocity occurs at the outer radius:

$$v_{max} = r_2 \, \omega$$

**Step 3 — Apply the wear criterion $p \cdot v_{max} = [p v]_{lim}$.**

The wear rate is dominated by the location where the p·v product is largest. For uniform pressure, that is the **outer radius**:

$$p \cdot v_{max} = [p v]_{lim}$$

Substituting the expressions for $p$ and $v_{max}$:

$$\left[\frac{W}{\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}\right] \cdot (r_2 \, \omega) = [p v]_{lim}$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $W$ (the wear-limited axial load).**

Multiply both sides by $\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$:

$$W \cdot r_2 \, \omega = \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \, [p v]_{lim}$$

Divide by $r_2 \, \omega$:

$$\boxed{\,W_w = \frac{\pi \, [p v]_{lim} \, (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{r_2 \, \omega}\,}$$

This is the **maximum axial load the clutch can carry continuously without exceeding the wear limit**, assuming uniform pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** In a *multi-disc* clutch with $n$ friction surfaces, the same $W$ is shared by $n$ pairs. The pressure per surface becomes $p = W / [n \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)]$, so the wear-limited load **scales linearly with $n$**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Wear-Limited Load for a Single-Disc Clutch (Uniform Wear Assumption)

**Assumption (uniform wear):** $p \cdot r = C$ (constant), so the product is highest at $r_1$ and the rate of wear is identical at every radius.

**Step 1 — Relate $C$ to the axial load $W$.**

From the standard uniform-wear derivation already covered in clutch modules:

$$C = p \, r = \frac{W}{2 \pi b \, r}$$

Wait — this formula assumes a *cylindrical* drum of width $b$. For an **annular flat disc** of width $b$:

$$W = 2 \pi b \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p \, r \, dr = 2 \pi b \int_{r_1}^{r_2} C \, dr = 2 \pi b \, C (r_2 - r_1)$$

Therefore:

$$C = \frac{W}{2 \pi b (r_2 - r_1)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p \cdot v$ at the maximum location.**

Since $p \cdot r = C$, the value of $p \cdot v$ is:

$$p \cdot v = p \cdot (r \omega) = \omega \cdot p \cdot r = \omega \cdot C$$

This is **constant over the entire face**, which is the hallmark of uniform-wear theory.

**Step 3 — Apply the wear criterion.**

$$\omega \cdot C = [p v]_{lim}$$

Substitute $C$:

$$\omega \cdot \frac{W}{2 \pi b (r_2 - r_1)} = [p v]_{lim}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W$.**

$$\boxed{\,W_w = \frac{2 \pi b \, [p v]_{lim} \, (r_2 - r_1)}{\omega}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why uniform-wear is the *preferred* design assumption:** Because $p \cdot v = $ const across the surface, **every element of the lining wears at the same rate**, and the entire lining life is fully utilised. The uniform-pressure theory over-constrains the inner radius (which never reaches its allowable $p \cdot v$), so it is conservative and less economical.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Archard's Equation from First Principles (Microscopic Asperity View)

**Step 1 — Asperity contact model.** Real surfaces touch only at microscopic asperities; the **real area of contact** is:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

where $H$ is the indentation hardness of the softer asperity (≈ Brinell hardness).

**Step 2 — Probability of weld formation.** Only a fraction $K$ of asperity junctions actually form a wear particle upon sliding (rest detach, deform elastically, or fracture):

$$dV = K \cdot A_r \cdot dL$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A_r$.**

$$dV = K \cdot \frac{W}{H} \cdot dL$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over sliding distance $L$ (with $W$ assumed constant):**

$$V = \int_0^{L} \frac{K W}{H} \, dL = \frac{K W L}{H} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is **Archard's equation**. The dimensionless factor $K$ (typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$) aggregates the asperity-yield probability, geometry, and wear-debris shape factor.

---

### 3.4 Wear Life Estimation (Extension Useful in KTU Problems)

Given an allowable volumetric loss $V_{allow}$ (determined by lining thickness $t$ and area $A$):

$$V_{allow} = t_{allow} \cdot \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$$

Substituting into Archard's equation and solving for total sliding distance $L$:

$$L = \frac{V_{allow} \, H}{K \, W} = \frac{t_{allow} \, H \, \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{K \, W}$$

**Operating life (number of engagements) when each engagement has sliding distance $s$:**

$$N = \frac{L}{s} = \frac{t_{allow} \, H \, \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{K \, W \, s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite twist:** Examiners may give you $N$ (engagements) and ask for the lining thickness $t_{allow}$, or vice versa. The above three equations are the complete toolkit.

---

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Uniform-Pressure vs Uniform-Wear (For Exam Time-Efficiency)

| Feature | Uniform Pressure (U.P.) | Uniform Wear (U.W.) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure distribution | $p$ = constant | $p \cdot r$ = constant |
| Wear rate distribution | Non-uniform (max at $r_2$) | **Uniform** across face |
| Governing design criterion | $p \le [p]$ and $p_{max} v_{max} \le [pv]$ | $p \cdot v = \omega C \le [pv]_{lim}$ |
| Wear-limited load $W_w$ | $\dfrac{\pi [pv] (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{r_2 \omega}$ | $\dfrac{2 \pi b [pv] (r_2 - r_1)}{\omega}$ |
| Torque capacity $T$ (U.P.) | $\dfrac{\mu W}{3} (r_2^3 - r_1^3) \cdot \frac{2 \pi}{\dots}$ (depends) | $\dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (r_2 + r_1)$ |
| Used in practice? | New clutches (during *run-in*) | Worn-in, steady-state clutches |
| KTU preference? | Often the safer exam answer | Often the *more accurate* exam answer |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wear-Mechanism Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wear in Machine Elements] --> B[Abrasive Wear]
    A --> C[Adhesive Wear]
    A --> D[Corrosive Oxidative Wear]
    A --> E[Surface Fatigue Wear]

    B --> B1[Two-Body: hard asperity cuts softer surface]
    B --> B2[Three-Body: loose particle trapped between faces]

    C --> C1[Micro-weld at asperity junction]
    C --> C2[Shear failure: material transfer]
    C --> C3[Archard Equation governs: V = KWL/H]

    D --> D1[Sliding removes oxide film]
    D --> D2[Fresh metal re-oxidises]
    D --> D3[Oxide debris accelerates third-body abrasion]

    E --> E1[Sub-surface shear stress cycles]
    E --> E2[Microcrack initiation and propagation]
    E --> E3[Pit or spall formation]

    A --> F[Design Counter-Measure]
    F --> F1[Limit p times v to allowable value]
    F --> F2[Increase surface hardness H]
    F --> F3[Select self-lubricating lining]
    F --> F4[Use uniform-wear geometry]
    F --> F5[Oil cooling for wet clutches]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wear-Load Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input: Torque T to transmit] --> P2[Input: Speed omega in rad/s]
    P2 --> P3[Input: Geometry r1 r2 b n]
    P3 --> P4{Compute Three Limits}

    P4 --> L1[Pressure Limit W_p from p allow]
    P4 --> L2[Wear Limit W_w from p v allow]
    P4 --> L3[Heat Limit W_h from temperature rise]

    L1 --> X1[W_p = n pi p_allow r2 sq minus r1 sq]
    L2 --> X2[W_w = pi p_v_allow r2 sq minus r1 sq divided by r2 omega]
    L3 --> X3[W_h from energy balance]

    X1 --> S[Select W_allow = MIN of W_p W_w W_h]
    X2 --> S
    X3 --> S

    S --> O1[Apply service factor K_a]
    O1 --> O2[Final design clamping force W_design]
    O2 --> OUT[Output: Safe clutch design within wear envelope]
```

---

### 4.3 p·v Envelope Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Zone_A[SAFE OPERATING REGION]
        D1[p times v less than p v allow]
    end

    subgraph Zone_B[MARGINAL: Accelerated wear]
        D2[p times v approx equal to p v allow]
    end

    subgraph Zone_C[FORBIDDEN: Rapid scoring and seizure]
        D3[p times v greater than p v allow]
    end

    Zone_A -->|Increase load or speed| Zone_B
    Zone_B -->|Further increase| Zone_C
    Zone_C -->|Catastrophic failure| FAIL[Clutch seizure or lining burn-out]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define wear load. What is its significance in the design of friction clutches?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Wear load is the maximum permissible axial clamping force on a friction surface such that the product of contact pressure $p$ and sliding velocity $v$ does not exceed the material's limiting $p \cdot v$ value: $p \cdot v \le [p v]_{lim}$.

> **Significance (1.5 marks):** Beyond this load, the lining wears at an unacceptable rate, leading to reduced life, loss of torque capacity, glazing, scoring, and eventual seizure. It sets the *upper bound* on the operating window alongside the pressure limit and the heat-dissipation limit.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State Archard's equation of adhesive wear and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Equation (1 mark):** $V = \dfrac{K \, W \, L}{H}$

> **Term-by-term (2 marks):**
> - $V$ — volume of material worn away (m³)
> - $K$ — dimensionless wear coefficient (depends on material pair, lubrication, environment); range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$
> - $W$ — normal contact load (N)
> - $L$ — sliding distance (m)
> - $H$ — hardness of the softer contacting surface (Pa)

> The equation states that wear volume is **directly proportional** to normal load and sliding distance, and **inversely proportional** to surface hardness. Harder surfaces last longer under the same operating conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** A single-disc clutch has a friction lining with inner radius $r_1 = 75$ mm and outer radius $r_2 = 150$ mm. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.3$. The clutch operates at 1440 rpm under dry conditions. Using the **uniform pressure theory** and the **p·v wear criterion**, determine:
1. The maximum axial clamping force $W_w$ the clutch can sustain without exceeding the wear limit, given $[p v]_{lim} = 0.6$ MPa·m/s.
2. The torque capacity at this wear-limited load.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-disc clutch with $n = 4$ friction surfaces is required to transmit 250 N·m at 1500 rpm. The lining material has $[p] = 1.0$ MPa and $[p v]_{lim} = 0.8$ MPa·m/s. The outer radius is twice the inner radius ($r_2 = 2 r_1$), and the face width $b = 30$ mm. Using **uniform wear theory**, determine the wear-limited axial load and the lining life in terms of engagements, given $K = 5 \times 10^{-5}$, $H = 250$ MPa, allowable wear thickness $t_{allow} = 2$ mm, and sliding distance per engagement $s = 12$ m.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) – Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440}{60} = 48 \pi \approx 150.80 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert units.**

- $r_1 = 0.075$ m, $r_2 = 0.150$ m
- $[p v]_{lim} = 0.6 \times 10^6$ Pa·m/s

**Step 3 — Apply uniform-pressure wear formula.**

$$W_w = \frac{\pi \, [p v]_{lim} \, (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{r_2 \, \omega}$$

$$r_2^2 - r_1^2 = (0.150)^2 - (0.075)^2 = 0.0225 - 0.005625 = 0.016875 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$W_w = \frac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.016875}{0.150 \times 150.80}$$

$$W_w = \frac{3.1809 \times 10^4}{22.62} \approx 1406 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{W_w \approx 1.41 \; \text{kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Torque capacity under uniform pressure.**

$$T_w = \frac{2}{3} \, \mu \, W_w \, \frac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}$$

$$r_2^3 - r_1^3 = (0.150)^3 - (0.075)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 4.219 \times 10^{-4} = 2.953 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3$$

$$T_w = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 1406 \times \frac{2.953 \times 10^{-3}}{0.016875}$$

$$T_w = 0.2 \times 1406 \times 0.1750 \approx 49.2 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_w \approx 49.2 \; \text{N·m}}$$

---

**Part (b) – Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Angular speed.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Express radii.** Let $r_1 = r$, then $r_2 = 2r$, so $r_2 - r_1 = r$.

**Step 3 — Apply uniform-wear formula for multi-disc clutch:**

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi n \, [p v]_{lim} \, b \, (r_2 - r_1)}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 — First, determine $r$ from the torque requirement to check feasibility.**

For uniform wear, torque is $T = \dfrac{1}{2} n \mu W (r_1 + r_2) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.3 \cdot W \cdot 3r = 1.8 \, W r$.

Set $T = 250$ N·m:

$$1.8 \, W r = 250 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W r = 138.89$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into wear formula.** Need $W$ in terms of $r$:

From Step 3: $W_w = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 4 \times 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.030 \times r}{157.08} = \dfrac{6.032 \times 10^5 \, r}{157.08} \approx 3840 \, r$ (N, with $r$ in metres).

**Step 6 — Equate to torque-derived W (set wear limit = operating load):**

$$3840 \, r^2 = 138.89 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r^2 = 0.03617 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r \approx 0.190 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Wear-limited load.**

$$W_w = 3840 \times 0.190 \approx 730 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{W_w \approx 730 \; \text{N}}$$

**Step 8 — Lining life (engagements).**

Using $N = \dfrac{t_{allow} H \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{K \, W \, s}$:

- $r_2^2 - r_1^2 = (0.380)^2 - (0.190)^2 = 0.1444 - 0.0361 = 0.1083$ m²
- $t_{allow} = 0.002$ m, $H = 250 \times 10^6$ Pa, $K = 5 \times 10^{-5}$, $s = 12$ m

$$N = \frac{0.002 \times 250 \times 10^6 \times \pi \times 0.1083}{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 730 \times 12}$$

$$N = \frac{1.701 \times 10^5}{0.438} \approx 3.88 \times 10^5$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 3.88 \times 10^5 \;\text{engagements}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** A brake drum has an effective friction radius of $r_e = 200$ mm, width $b = 80$ mm, and is pressed against a single friction lining with $\mu = 0.35$ and $[p v]_{lim} = 0.5$ MPa·m/s. The drum rotates at 600 rpm. Using the **uniform wear theory**, find:
1. The wear-limited actuating force $W_w$.
2. The braking torque at this load.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cone clutch has a semi-cone angle $\alpha = 12.5°$, mean radius $R = 90$ mm, face width $b = 50$ mm, and is to be used at 900 rpm with $\mu = 0.25$ and $[p v]_{lim} = 0.7$ MPa·m/s. The number of friction surfaces is $n = 2$. Determine:
1. The axial wear-limited load.
2. The torque capacity and the required spring force (without considering any safety factor).
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) – Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Angular speed.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 600}{60} = 20 \pi \approx 62.83 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply uniform-wear formula for a drum brake:**

For a drum of width $b$ and effective radius $r_e$:

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi b \, [p v]_{lim} \, r_e}{\omega}$$

(Note: for a drum, $r_2 - r_1 \to r_e$ when wear is uniform across the contact patch.)

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.080 \times 0.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.200}{62.83}$$

$$W_w = \frac{5.0265 \times 10^4}{62.83} \approx 800 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{W_w = 800 \; \text{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Braking torque under uniform wear (drum):**

$$T = \mu \, W \, r_e = 0.35 \times 800 \times 0.200 = 56 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T = 56 \; \text{N·m}}$$

---

**Part (b) – Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Angular speed.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 900}{60} = 30 \pi \approx 94.25 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Axial wear-limited load for cone clutch.**

For a cone clutch with $n$ friction surfaces, the wear-limited axial load is:

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi n \, [p v]_{lim} \, b \, (R \sin \alpha)}{\omega \sin \alpha} = \frac{2 \pi n \, [p v]_{lim} \, b \, R}{\omega}$$

Wait — let me derive carefully. The cone geometry projects the axial force onto a normal force per unit area. The mean radius and face width give:

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi n \, [p v]_{lim} \, b \, R \, \sin \alpha}{\omega \cdot \sin \alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin \alpha}$$

After simplification (as in standard KTU texts):

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi n \, [p v]_{lim} \, b \, R}{\omega \, \sin \alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

- $n = 2$, $[p v]_{lim} = 0.7 \times 10^6$ Pa·m/s, $b = 0.050$ m, $R = 0.090$ m
- $\sin(12.5°) \approx 0.2164$

$$W_w = \frac{2 \pi \times 2 \times 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.050 \times 0.090}{94.25 \times 0.2164}$$

$$W_w = \frac{3.958 \times 10^4}{20.40} \approx 1940 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{W_w \approx 1.94 \; \text{kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Torque capacity (uniform wear, cone clutch):**

$$T = \frac{1}{2} n \mu W_w R = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 0.25 \times 1940 \times 0.090$$

$$T = 0.25 \times 1940 \times 0.090 = 43.65 \; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Required spring (axial) force.** The actuating force on a cone clutch must overcome the axial component of the normal friction force. For a self-energising cone clutch, the spring force $F_s$ satisfies:

$$F_s = W_w (\sin \alpha \pm \mu \cos \alpha)$$

The '+' is for self-locking (de-energising); the '−' is for the engaged state. Taking the engaged condition:

$$F_s = 1940 \times (\sin 12.5° + 0.25 \cos 12.5°)$$
$$F_s = 1940 \times (0.2164 + 0.25 \times 0.9763) = 1940 \times (0.2164 + 0.2441) = 1940 \times 0.4605$$
$$F_s \approx 893 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_s \approx 893 \; \text{N}, \quad T \approx 43.65 \; \text{N·m}}$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Wear-Load questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pressure-limit and wear-limit formulas (2–3 marks lost):** $[p]$ and $[p v]$ are *two different* allowable quantities. The pressure limit depends on lining strength; the wear limit depends on lining *life*. Use the wrong one and the answer is dimensionally valid but physically wrong.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s (1–2 marks lost):** $\omega$ in every formula is in **rad/s**, *not* rpm. The classic slip is plugging $N = 1440$ directly into the wear formula.
>
> 3. **Mixing up uniform-pressure and uniform-wear theories (up to 7 marks lost):** In multi-part problems, examiners often specify one theory in part (a) and the other in part (b). Using the same formula for both is a guaranteed partial-zero.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the number of friction surfaces $n$ in multi-disc clutches:** A 4-disc clutch has *4* friction surfaces, not 2. The load per surface is $W/n$, and the wear-limited $W$ *scales linearly with $n$*.
>
> 5. **Skipping the service factor $K_a$ in final design:** The KTU 2024 rubric explicitly allocates 2 marks for applying a real-world service factor. Always end with: $W_{design} = K_a \cdot W_{operating}$.
>
> 6. **Sign error in cone-clutch self-energising direction:** Use the '−' sign for engaging force, the '+' sign for de-energising (release) force. Reversing them inverts the safety logic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)**

- ✅ **Wear Load Definition:** Maximum axial force such that $p \cdot v \le [p v]_{lim}$.
- ✅ **Archard's Equation:** $V = KWL/H$. Memorise all five terms and units.
- ✅ **Two theories to know cold:**
  - *Uniform pressure:* $p = $ const, wear concentrated at $r_2$.
  - *Uniform wear:* $p \cdot r = $ const, full-face wear, **preferred in real design**.
- ✅ **Two key formulas (U.P.):**
  - Pressure limit: $W_p = n \pi [p] (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$
  - Wear limit: $W_w = \dfrac{\pi [p v] (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{r_2 \omega}$
- ✅ **Two key formulas (U.W.):**
  - Pressure limit: $W_p = 2 \pi n [p] b (r_2 - r_1)$
  - Wear limit: $W_w = \dfrac{2 \pi n [p v] b (r_2 - r_1)}{\omega}$
- ✅ **Always convert rpm → rad/s:** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- ✅ **Multi-disc clutches scale with $n$**; **cone clutches divide by $\sin \alpha$**.
- ✅ **Design operating window:** $W_{allow} = \min(W_p, W_w, W_h)$.
- ✅ **Apply service factor** $K_a$ for final design: $W_{design} = K_a \cdot W_{operating}$.
- ✅ **Lining life equation:** $N = \dfrac{t_{allow} \, H \, \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{K \, W \, s}$ — useful in long-answer design problems.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Don't confuse the **heat-generation limit** $W_h$ with the **wear limit** $W_w$ — they are different limits with different physical bases (temperature rise vs. material loss rate).
- ✅ **Material hint:** Wet clutches/brakes allow $[p v]_{lim}$ **2–3×** higher than dry counterparts due to oil cooling and debris flushing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endurance Strength of Tooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Endurance Strength of Tooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Endurance Strength of a Gear Tooth** is defined as the maximum repetitive (fluctuating) bending stress that the gear tooth material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (typically $10^6$ to $10^8$ stress cycles, depending on material) without undergoing fatigue failure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the endurance strength of a tooth is the **modified endurance limit** ($S_e$) of the gear material, after applying various modifying factors that account for the operating conditions of the gear drive. This value is compared against the actual induced bending stress (Lewis bending stress) to determine the **factor of safety** against tooth failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** The phrase "endurance strength of tooth" specifically refers to the fatigue resistance of the gear tooth when subjected to repeated bending stresses due to the cyclic meshing action. It is **NOT** the ultimate tensile strength or the static strength.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine bending a paperclip back and forth repeatedly. Initially, the paperclip is strong, but after many cycles, it breaks at a stress much lower than its single-bend breaking strength. This phenomenon is called **fatigue failure**.

A gear tooth experiences exactly this kind of loading:
- Every time a tooth meshes, it bends slightly under the transmitted tangential load ($F_t$).
- After one full revolution, the same tooth is loaded again.
- Over millions of revolutions, the tooth is subjected to **fluctuating bending stress**.

**The endurance strength of the tooth** is essentially asking: *"What is the maximum stress this tooth can handle for millions of loading cycles before it cracks at the root?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Gears typically fail by **fatigue** at the tooth root, not by static fracture. Hence, the design is governed by endurance strength, not ultimate strength.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Cycle Count for Gears:** $10^6$ to $10^8$ cycles (varies by application — automobile gears: $10^8$; industrial: $10^6$).
- **Reference Endurance Limit (Unmodified):** $S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut}$ for **steels** with $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa.
- **Reference Endurance Limit (Cast Irons):** $S_e' = 0.4 \times S_{ut}$.
- **Standard Reference Diameter for Size Factor:** $d = 7.62$ mm (0.3 inches) for bending and axial loading.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module on gear design (Module 3) links the endurance strength of the tooth directly to the **Lewis equation** and the **design tangential load** equation. Mastering the endurance strength is a prerequisite for solving any gear design numerical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The design of a gear tooth against **bending fatigue failure** is governed by equating the **induced Lewis bending stress** to the **endurance strength of the material**, divided by a factor of safety.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Tangential Load on Tooth ($F_t$):** A gear tooth in mesh carries the tangential component of the transmitted force.
2. **Bending Stress (Lewis Equation):** The tooth is treated as a cantilever beam, and the bending stress at the critical section (tooth root) is calculated.
3. **Material Endurance Strength ($S_e$):** The base endurance limit $S_e'$ is modified by various factors (Marin's factors) to get the in-service endurance strength.
4. **Comparison:** The induced bending stress must be less than $S_e$ divided by the factor of safety ($n$).
5. **Design Equation:** The equality is rearranged to find the required face width ($b$), module ($m$), or transmitted power.

## 2.2 Why Endurance Strength and Not Ultimate Strength?

A gear tooth in a transmission is loaded **once per revolution** (per pair of teeth). If the gear runs at 1500 RPM for 8 hours a day, 5 days a week, 50 weeks a year, the tooth experiences:
- $1500 \times 60 \times 8 \times 5 \times 50 = 1.8 \times 10^8$ cycles per year.

This is an **infinite-life regime**, so fatigue (endurance) governs the design — not static strength.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Lewis Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot C}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | $F_t$ = Tangential load (N), $C$ = Velocity factor, $b$ = Face width (mm), $m$ = Module (mm), $Y$ = Lewis form factor |
| Design Tangential Load (based on endurance) | $F_t = \dfrac{\sigma_e \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}{C \cdot n}$ | $\sigma_e$ = Endurance strength of tooth (MPa), $n$ = Factor of safety |
| Unmodified Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}$ | $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa |
| Unmodified Endurance Limit (Cast Iron) | $S_e' = 0.4 \cdot S_{ut}$ | — |
| Modified Endurance Limit (Marin) | $S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$ | $k_a, k_b, \ldots$ = Modifying factors |
| Surface Finish Factor | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}$ | $a, b$ depend on finish (e.g., $a = 4.51$, $b = -0.265$ for machined) |
| Size Factor (bending) | $k_b = \left(\dfrac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107}$ | $d$ in mm, $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm |
| Loading Factor | $k_c = 1$ (pure bending), $0.85$ (combined) | — |
| Temperature Factor | $k_d = \dfrac{S_T}{S_{RT}}$ | For $T \le 350^\circ$C, $k_d \approx 1$ |
| Reliability Factor | $k_e = 1.0$ (50%), $0.897$ (90%), $0.753$ (99.9%) | — |
| Factor of Safety | $n = \dfrac{S_e}{\sigma_b}$ | Dimensionless |
| Beam Strength (Endurance-based) | $F_b = \sigma_e \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$ | (N) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In KTU exam questions, the **endurance strength** is sometimes directly given as $\sigma_e$ or $S_e$ in the problem statement. Always check whether you are dealing with the **unmodified** ($S_e'$) or the **modified** ($S_e$) value.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of endurance strength of tooth is critical in:
- **Automotive Gearboxes:** Where gears run at high RPM for the entire vehicle life.
- **Aerospace Transmissions:** Helicopter main rotor gears, where failure is catastrophic.
- **Wind Turbine Gearboxes:** Subjected to billions of cycles.
- **Industrial Reducers:** Continuous duty industrial gearboxes.
- **Machine Tool Spindles:** Where vibration-induced fatigue is severe.

In all these applications, the **factor of safety against fatigue** is the primary design criterion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Modified Endurance Limit (Marin's Equation)

The **modified endurance limit** represents the actual fatigue strength of the gear material under in-service conditions. The derivation is a multiplicative accumulation of correction factors.

### Starting Point
The unmodified endurance limit for steel is empirically related to the ultimate tensile strength:

$$
S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}
$$

This is a curve-fit from rotating-beam test data (Moore's rotating beam test) for polished specimens at standard reference conditions (diameter = 7.62 mm, temperature = 20°C, 50% reliability).

### Application of Modifying Factors

**Step 1 — Surface Finish Factor ($k_a$):**
The real gear tooth surface is not polished; it has machining marks, heat treatment scale, or grind marks. These surface irregularities act as **micro-stress concentrators**, reducing endurance.

$$
k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}
$$

Typical values:

| Surface Finish | $a$ | $b$ |
|---|---|---|
| Ground | 1.58 | -0.085 |
| Machined or Cold-drawn | 4.51 | -0.265 |
| Hot-rolled | 57.7 | -0.718 |
| As-forged | 272 | -0.995 |

> **Logic:** Higher $S_{ut}$ (stronger steel) is more sensitive to surface finish because it has less ductility to "smooth out" stress peaks.

**Step 2 — Size Factor ($k_b$):**
Larger cross-sections have a higher statistical probability of containing inclusions, voids, and defects. The effective stress also increases with size due to stress gradient effects.

For the bending case applicable to gear teeth:

$$
k_b = \left(\frac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = 1.24 \cdot d^{-0.107}
$$

where $d$ is the effective diameter in mm. For a gear tooth, $d$ is taken as the **pitch circle diameter** of the gear (or an equivalent diameter for the tooth section).

For $d < 2.79$ mm, use $k_b = 1.24$. For $d > 51$ mm, use $k_b = 0.6$ (lower bound).

**Step 3 — Loading Factor ($k_c$):**
For pure bending (which is the case for gear teeth), $k_c = 1$. If the loading is combined (bending + torsion + axial), $k_c < 1$. Typical combined loading: $k_c = 0.85$.

**Step 4 — Temperature Factor ($k_d$):**
Elevated temperatures reduce yield strength. For temperatures up to 350°C, the reduction is small; for higher temperatures, it drops significantly.

$$
k_d = \frac{S_T}{S_{RT}}
$$

For most KTU problems, $T \le 100^\circ$C, so $k_d = 1$.

**Step 5 — Reliability Factor ($k_e$):**
The unmodified endurance limit $S_e'$ corresponds to **50% reliability** (i.e., 50% of specimens survive). For higher reliability:

| Reliability (%) | $k_e$ |
|---|---|
| 50 | 1.000 |
| 90 | 0.897 |
| 95 | 0.868 |
| 99 | 0.814 |
| 99.9 | 0.753 |
| 99.99 | 0.702 |

**Step 6 — Miscellaneous Effects Factor ($k_f$):**
Accounts for residual stresses, corrosion, plating, etc. Default: $k_f = 1$.

### Final Combined Equation

$$
S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'
$$

$$
S_e = (k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f) \cdot (0.5 \cdot S_{ut})
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Design Tangential Load from Endurance Strength

The design equation for the gear tooth based on endurance strength is derived by combining the Lewis bending equation with the factor of safety.

### Starting Point — Lewis Equation

The Lewis equation gives the bending stress at the tooth root:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{F_t \cdot C}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

Rearranging for the transmitted tangential load:

$$
F_t = \frac{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}{C}
$$

### Introducing Endurance Strength

For safe design, the induced bending stress must be less than the endurance strength divided by the factor of safety:

$$
\sigma_b \le \frac{S_e}{n}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{S_e}{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_t = \frac{S_e}{n} \cdot \frac{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}{C}
$$

Or equivalently, the **beam strength** (endurance-based) of the tooth is:

$$
F_b = S_e \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y
$$

And the design tangential load becomes:

$$
F_t = \frac{F_b}{C \cdot n}
$$

> **Key Insight:** The **beam strength $F_b$** is the maximum tangential load the tooth can transmit for infinite life without fatigue failure. The **effective load** (with velocity factor $C$ and safety factor $n$) must be less than $F_b$.

## 3.3 Design Verification Workflow

For a KTU-style numerical problem, the workflow is:

**Step 1:** Compute the tangential load $F_t$ from the given power and speed.
**Step 2:** Calculate the Lewis bending stress $\sigma_b$ from given geometry.
**Step 3:** Compute the endurance strength $S_e$ using Marin's factors.
**Step 4:** Calculate the factor of safety $n = S_e / \sigma_b$.
**Step 5:** Compare $n$ with the recommended value (1.5 to 2.5 for gears).
**Step 6:** If $n$ is too low, increase face width $b$ or module $m$ and iterate.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example

**Given:**
- Spur gear pair, $P = 10$ kW, $N_p = 1000$ RPM, $N_g = 500$ RPM.
- Pinion: $Z_p = 20$, Material: Steel, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, surface ground.
- Gear: $Z_g = 40$, Material: Cast Iron, $S_{ut} = 400$ MPa, surface machined.
- Module $m = 5$ mm, Face width $b = 50$ mm, $Y_p = 0.320$, $Y_g = 0.390$, $C = 1.25$.
- Reliability: 90%, Temperature: 50°C.

**Find:** Factor of safety against bending failure for both pinion and gear.

### Solution — Pinion (Steel)

**Step 1:** Pitch circle diameter $d_p = m \cdot Z_p = 5 \times 20 = 100$ mm.

**Step 2:** Tangential load:
$$
F_t = \frac{60 \cdot P}{v} = \frac{60 \cdot 10{,}000}{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N_p / 60{,}000} = \frac{600{,}000}{\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 1000 / 60{,}000}
$$

Computing velocity:
$$
v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N_p}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 1000}{60{,}000} = 5.236 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Tangential load:
$$
F_t = \frac{60 \cdot P}{v} = \frac{60 \cdot 10{,}000}{5.236} = 114{,}591 \, \text{N} \approx 114.6 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3:** Lewis bending stress:
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{F_t \cdot C}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y_p} = \frac{114{,}591 \cdot 1.25}{50 \cdot \pi \cdot 5 \cdot 0.320}
$$

Numerator: $114{,}591 \times 1.25 = 143{,}239$ N.

Denominator: $50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.320 = 251.33$ mm².

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{143{,}239}{251.33} = 569.9 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Endurance strength (Marin's factors for pinion):

Unmodified: $S_e' = 0.5 \times 600 = 300$ MPa.

Surface factor (ground, $S_{ut} = 600$):
$$
k_a = 1.58 \times 600^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.795 = 1.256
$$

Size factor ($d = 100$ mm): Since $d > 51$ mm, use $k_b = 0.6$ (lower bound, conservative).

Loading factor: $k_c = 1$ (pure bending).

Temperature factor: $k_d = 1$ (50°C).

Reliability factor (90%): $k_e = 0.897$.

Miscellaneous: $k_f = 1$.

$$
S_e = 1.256 \times 0.6 \times 1 \times 1 \times 0.897 \times 1 \times 300 = 202.8 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor of safety:
$$
n_p = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_b} = \frac{202.8}{569.9} = 0.356
$$

> **Interpretation:** $n_p < 1$ means the design is unsafe for the pinion — bending stress exceeds endurance strength. The pinion will fail by fatigue.

### Solution — Gear (Cast Iron)

**Step 1:** $d_g = 5 \times 40 = 200$ mm.

**Step 2:** Velocity at gear: $v_g = \frac{\pi \times 200 \times 500}{60{,}000} = 5.236$ m/s (same as pinion — as expected for meshing).

**Step 3:** Same $F_t = 114{,}591$ N.

**Step 4:** Lewis bending stress:
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{114{,}591 \times 1.25}{50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.390} = \frac{143{,}239}{306.30} = 467.6 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Endurance strength (Marin's factors for cast iron):

Unmodified: $S_e' = 0.4 \times 400 = 160$ MPa.

Surface factor (machined, $S_{ut} = 400$):
$$
k_a = 4.51 \times 400^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.270 = 1.218
$$

Size factor ($d = 200$ mm): $k_b = 0.6$ (lower bound).

Loading: $k_c = 1$. Temperature: $k_d = 1$. Reliability: $k_e = 0.897$. Misc: $k_f = 1$.

$$
S_e = 1.218 \times 0.6 \times 1 \times 1 \times 0.897 \times 1 \times 160 = 104.9 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 6:** Factor of safety:
$$
n_g = \frac{104.9}{467.6} = 0.224
$$

> **Interpretation:** Gear is even more critical. Both the pinion and gear fail the design check. The remedy: increase $b$ or $m$ — the module $m$ has a stronger effect (linear) and face width $b$ (linear), but increasing $m$ also requires re-checking $v$, $F_t$, and $Y$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Gear Tooth Endurance Strength Evaluation

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** for evaluating whether a gear tooth design is safe against bending fatigue failure based on endurance strength.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Power P] --> calcA[Compute Velocity v]
    inputB[Input Speed N] --> calcA
    inputC[Gear Geometry m, b, Y] --> calcA
    inputD[Material Sut] --> calcA
    
    calcA --> calcB[Compute Tangential Load Ft]
    
    inputC --> calcC[Compute Lewis Bending Stress]
    calcB --> calcC
    
    inputD --> calcD[Compute Unmodified Endurance Limit Se]
    inputE[Surface Finish] --> calcE[Marin Factor ka]
    inputF[Pitch Diameter] --> calcF[Marin Factor kb]
    inputG[Load Type] --> calcG[Marin Factor kc]
    inputH[Operating Temp] --> calcH[Marin Factor kd]
    inputI[Reliability] --> calcI[Marin Factor ke]
    inputJ[Misc Effects] --> calcJ[Marin Factor kf]
    
    calcD --> calcK
    calcE --> calcK
    calcF --> calcK
    calcG --> calcK
    calcH --> calcK
    calcI --> calcK
    calcJ --> calcK[Multiply All Factors to Get Se]
    
    calcC --> compA[Compare sb vs Se]
    calcK --> compA
    
    compA --> calcL[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    calcL --> decisionA{Is n Greater Than 1.5?}
    
    decisionA -->|Yes| outputA[DESIGN IS SAFE]
    decisionA -->|No| outputB[INCREASE b OR m]
    outputB --> inputC
```

## 4.2 Stress-Number of Cycles (S-N) Curve for Gear Tooth

The endurance strength of a gear tooth is best understood through the **S-N curve** (Wöhler curve) of the gear material.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regionA[HIGH CYCLE FATIGUE ZONE] --> regionB[ENDURANCE LIMIT ZONE]
    regionB --> regionC[FINITE LIFE ZONE]
    regionC --> regionD[STATIC FAILURE ZONE]
    
    regionA --> label1[Stress sb on Y axis]
    regionA --> label2[Cycles N on X axis]
    regionB --> point1[Se Endurance Limit]
    regionC --> point2[su Ultimate Strength]
    regionD --> point3[N = 1 cycle]
    
    point1 --> value1[10^6 to 10^8 cycles]
    point2 --> value2[Approximately 1000 cycles]
    point3 --> value3[Single application]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the S-N Diagram:**
> - The horizontal asymptote at $S_e$ (endurance limit) corresponds to the gear's infinite life region.
> - Below $S_e$, the tooth has theoretically infinite life — this is the design target.
> - Above $S_e$, the tooth has a finite life (e.g., $10^3$ cycles) until it fails.
> - For steels, the curve has a true endurance limit; for non-ferrous metals and cast iron, the curve keeps decreasing, and a "knee" does not exist — instead, an arbitrary life of $10^8$ is used.

## 4.3 Stress Distribution at Tooth Root (Schematic Description)

Although a precise photoelastic stress map cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the **functional flow** of stress in a gear tooth is as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pointA[Tooth Tip] --> pointB[Meshing Point]
    pointB --> pointC[Applied Tangential Force Ft]
    pointC --> pointD[Tooth Treated as Cantilever Beam]
    pointD --> pointE[Critical Section at Fillet Root]
    pointE --> pointF[Maximum Bending Stress sb]
    pointF --> pointG[Also Tensile Stress on Fillet Side]
    pointF --> pointH[Compressive Stress on Flank Side]
    pointG --> pointI[Crack Initiation Site]
    pointH --> pointJ[Fatigue Failure Region]
```

> **Visual Description (for student understanding):** Imagine a gear tooth as a tapered cantilever beam fixed at the root circle. The tangential force $F_t$ acts at the pitch point. The tooth bends like a cantilever, with the **maximum tensile stress** on the tension side of the fillet and **compressive stress** on the opposite side. The **fillet radius** is critical — a smaller fillet causes higher stress concentration, reducing endurance. The Lewis form factor $Y$ accounts for the tooth shape (20° full-depth involute has $Y \approx 0.32$ for 20 teeth).
