The **Design of Spur Gears** is the engineering procedure that ensures the gear teeth possess sufficient strength to resist **bending failure (Lewis Beam Strength)** and **pitting resistance / surface failure (Buckingham Wear Strength)** under the transmitted tangential load, while maintaining a reasonable service life.

The design of a spur gear drive is fundamentally a two-fold problem governed by:
1. **Strength Criterion (Lewis Equation, 1892):** Prevents tooth breakage by bending.
2. **Wear Criterion (Buckingham's Equation, 1920):** Prevents surface pitting/fatigue by contact stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must be capable of designing a spur gear pair based on given power, speed, and speed ratio, with explicit derivation of the module, face width, and final validation against dynamic load and wear conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Lever" & "Hammer" of Machines)

Imagine two interlocking **saw blades** (the gear teeth) pressing sideways against each other. When one blade (the pinion) pushes the other (the gear), two things can go wrong:

1. **The teeth may snap like a cantilever beam** anchored at the root (this is **bending failure** — analyzed by the **Lewis Equation**).
2. **The contact surface may crack, flake, or pit** under repeated hammering impacts (this is **surface fatigue** — analyzed by **Buckingham's Wear Equation**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Designing a spur gear is like designing a hammer that never breaks and a chisel that never dulls, simultaneously, while rolling on each other at high speed. The **Lewis equation** prevents the hammer from breaking; **Buckingham's equation** prevents the chisel from wearing out.

## 1.3 Standard Terms & Notations (KTU Board Terminology)

The following are the **canonical KTU symbols** that the examiner expects in your answer scripts:

- **P** — Transmitted power (kW or W)
- **N** — Speed of the pinion (rpm)
- **i** or **n₁/n₂** — Speed ratio
- **m** — Module (mm) — the fundamental unit of gear size
- **z** — Number of teeth
- **d** — Pitch circle diameter (mm)
- **b** — Face width (mm)
- **Φ** — Pressure angle (typically **20° full depth** or **20° stub**)
- **y** — Lewis form factor
- **σ_d** — Allowable design stress (N/mm²)
- **σ_ut** — Ultimate tensile strength of gear material
- **σ_w** — Wear load stress factor (N/mm²)
- **C_v** — Velocity factor
- **K** — Load stress factor

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic

The standard KTU-accepted procedure for spur gear design proceeds in the following logical sequence:

1. **Determine Design Power:** Apply a service factor (Cs) to the rated power to account for shock, vibration, and operating hours.
2. **Compute the Tangential Tooth Load (P_t):** This is the actual tooth force based on design torque and pitch circle diameter.
3. **Select Gear Materials:** Choose pinion material harder (typically 5–10% more) than gear material to equalize wear.
4. **Compute Allowable Stress (σ_d):** Based on material UTS, type of teeth (stub/full), and shock conditions.
5. **Apply Lewis Equation** to find the minimum required **module (m)**.
6. **Standardize the module** to standard IS/AGMA values.
7. **Determine Face Width (b)** = (8 to 14) × m.
8. **Check for Wear** using Buckingham's equation.
9. **Check for Dynamic Load** using Buckingham's dynamic load equation.
10. **Final Validation** of center distance and all dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Order:** We design against bending first because gear teeth fail by bending more commonly than by pitting in standard industrial applications. The wear check is a verification, not the primary design driver in most KTU problems.

## 2.2 The Lewis Beam Strength Equation (The Core Equation)

**Theoretical Basis:** Lewis (1892) modeled the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam of parabolic shape**, with the load applied at the tip. The weakest section is at the tooth root, and the tooth is treated as having uniform strength.

$$W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

Where:

- $W_B$ = Beam strength of the tooth (N)
- $\sigma_w$ = Allowable bending stress for the gear material (N/mm²)
- $b$ = Face width of the gear (mm)
- $m$ = Module of the gear (mm)
- $y$ = Lewis form factor (depends on tooth profile, system, and number of teeth)

**Design Rule (KTU Board Standard):**
The tangential load $P_t$ transmitted by the gear must always be **less than** the beam strength of the **weaker** of the two gears:

$$P_t \leq W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

## 2.3 Lewis Form Factor (y)

The Lewis form factor depends on:
- **Pressure angle (Φ)**: 14.5° or 20°
- **Tooth system**: Full depth or stub
- **Number of teeth (z)**

| Tooth System | Pressure Angle | Approximate y (for 32 teeth) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 14.5° Full Depth | 14.5° | 0.353 |
| 20° Full Depth | 20° | 0.314 |
| 20° Stub | 20° | 0.32 – 0.36 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For unequal pair, the **weaker gear's y** is used. Since y decreases with decreasing z, the **pinion is the weaker member** in most designs. Hence we use $y_p$ (pinion form factor) for design.

## 2.4 The Buckingham Wear Equation

When two non-conforming surfaces (gear teeth) roll and slide against each other, contact stress causes **pitting**. Buckingham proposed that the load is supported by the **curvature of the pinion alone**, since the pinion is the smaller member.

$$W_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$$

Where:

- $W_W$ = Wear strength of the tooth (N)
- $d_p$ = Pitch circle diameter of the **pinion** (mm)
- $b$ = Face width (mm)
- $Q$ = Ratio factor = $\frac{2 \cdot z_g}{z_g \pm z_p}$ (with '+' for external, '−' for internal gears)
- $K$ = Load stress factor (N/mm²) — depends on material

**Design Rule:** $P_t \leq W_W$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** This is the single most important table. Memorize it before every KTU test.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $d = m \cdot z$ | mm |
| 2 | Tangential Load | $P_t = \frac{60 \cdot 10^3 \cdot P}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot T}{d}$ | N |
| 3 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60 \cdot 10^3}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Design Power | $P_d = P \cdot C_s$ | kW |
| 5 | Allowable Stress (Steel) | $\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{3 \cdot \text{S.F.} \cdot C_s}$ | N/mm² |
| 6 | Lewis Beam Strength | $W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | N |
| 7 | Module from Lewis Eq. | $m = \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot C_s}{\sigma_d \cdot \pi \cdot y \cdot \psi}}$ | mm |
| 8 | Face Width | $b = \psi \cdot m$ (where $\psi = 8$ to $14$) | mm |
| 9 | Buckingham Wear | $W_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | N |
| 10 | Ratio Factor | $Q = \frac{2 \cdot z_g}{z_g + z_p}$ (external) | dimensionless |
| 11 | Dynamic Load (Buckingham) | $P_d = P_t + \frac{21 \cdot v \cdot (C \cdot b \cdot \cos\Phi + P_t)}{21 \cdot v + \sqrt{C \cdot b \cdot \cos\Phi + P_t}}$ | N |
| 12 | Dynamic Load Check | $P_d \leq W_B$ and $P_d \leq W_W$ | N |

**Note:** The symbols $C$, $\Phi$, $\psi$ above represent the dynamic load constant, the pressure angle, and the face-width-to-module ratio respectively, as used in KTU standard notations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Transmissions:** Spur gears (and helical variants) transmit torque from engine to wheels.
- **Industrial Gearboxes:** Every conveyor, mixer, and crane uses spur gears in reduction stages.
- **CNC Machines:** High-precision spur gears drive lead screws and indexing tables.
- **Hand Tools:** Hand-cranked winches, windlasses, and mechanical calculators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Equation (Board Exam Favorite)

**Assumption:** The gear tooth is treated as a **cantilever beam of uniform strength**, parabolic in cross-section, fixed at the root.

**Step 1: Tangential Load at the Pitch Circle**
The tangential force $P_t$ acting at the pitch circle is equivalent to a horizontal component $P_H$ at the tooth tip:

$$P_H = P_t \cdot \cos\Phi$$

**Step 2: Bending Moment at the Tooth Root**
The bending moment at the root section of the cantilever beam (tooth) of length $h$ is:

$$M = P_H \cdot h = P_t \cdot \cos\Phi \cdot h$$

**Step 3: Section Modulus of the Tooth Root**
For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ (face width) and thickness $t$ at the root:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

**Step 4: Bending Stress Equation**
The maximum bending stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6 \cdot P_t \cdot \cos\Phi \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2}$$

**Step 5: Lewis Form Factor Substitution**
Lewis observed that for a parabolic tooth profile, the ratio $\frac{t^2}{h}$ is approximately constant and depends only on the **number of teeth and pressure angle**. He defined:

$$y = \frac{t^2}{6 \cdot h \cdot \cos\Phi}$$

**Step 6: Substituting Back**
Substituting y into the bending stress equation and rearranging:

$$P_t = \sigma \cdot b \cdot \frac{t^2}{6 \cdot h \cdot \cos\Phi} = \sigma \cdot b \cdot y$$

**Step 7: The Lewis Beam Strength Formula**
The **beam strength** of the tooth is defined as the maximum load the tooth can transmit at the pitch circle without failure:

$$\boxed{W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot y}$$

Wait — this is the **plain (per unit face width)** form. For the full equation including the **module**:

$$W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

Where $\pi \cdot m$ represents the circular pitch ($p_c$).

## 3.2 Standard 14-Mark KTU Numerical Problem (Complete Solved Example)

**Problem:** `[KTU University Exam Model - Module 3, 14 Marks]`

Design a pair of spur gears to transmit **12 kW** at a pinion speed of **1440 rpm**, with a **speed ratio of 3:1**. The gears are to be made of **steel with UTS = 600 N/mm²** for the pinion and **550 N/mm²** for the gear, with a service factor of **1.75**. Use 20° full-depth involute teeth with a Lewis form factor of **0.32** for the pinion and **0.34** for the gear. The face width may be taken as **10 × m**. Check for wear and dynamic load. Take the load stress factor K = 0.55 N/mm².

---

### SOLUTION (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Design Power**
$$P_d = P \times C_s = 12 \times 1.75 = 21 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Speeds**
$$N_1 = 1440 \text{ rpm (pinion)}, \quad N_2 = \frac{1440}{3} = 480 \text{ rpm (gear)}$$

**Step 3: Assume Module (Iterative start)**
Initial trial: take $m = 6$ mm, so $d_p = m \cdot z_p$. We need to assume $z_p$ as well.

For minimum pinion teeth avoiding interference at 20° full depth: $z_p \geq 18$. Take $z_p = 20$.

Therefore $z_g = 3 \times 20 = 60$.

**Step 4: Pitch Circle Diameters**
$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = m \cdot z_g = 6 \times 60 = 360 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Pitch Line Velocity**
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N_1}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 120 \times 1440}{60 \times 1000} = 9.048 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Tangential Tooth Load**
$$P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times P_d}{v} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 21}{9.048} = 139,261 \text{ N}$$

Alternatively, $P_t = \frac{2 \cdot T}{d_p}$, where $T = \frac{60 \cdot P_d \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N_1} = \frac{60 \times 21 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 1440} = 139,262$ N·mm.

**Step 7: Allowable Design Stress for Pinion**
For steel gears, the recommended design stress (per Buckingham/KTU):

$$\sigma_{d,p} = \frac{\sigma_{ut,p}}{3 \times \text{S.F.} \times C_s} = \frac{600}{3 \times 1.5 \times 1.75} = 76.19 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Taking a standard value: $\sigma_{d,p} = 84$ N/mm² (or as per Shigley/Khurmi convention).

**Step 8: Face Width**
$$b = 10 \cdot m = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9: Apply Lewis Equation for the Pinion**
$$W_B = \sigma_d \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_p = 84 \times 60 \times \pi \times 6 \times 0.32 = 30,415 \text{ N}$$

**Check:** $W_B = 30,415$ N is **much less** than $P_t = 139,261$ N. **The assumed module is insufficient.**

**Step 10: Revised Module Calculation**
We need $W_B \geq P_t$, so:

$$m \geq \sqrt{\frac{P_t}{\sigma_d \cdot \pi \cdot y_p \cdot b/m}}$$

Or directly, treating $b = 10m$:

$$P_t = \sigma_d \cdot (10m) \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_p$$
$$P_t = \sigma_d \cdot 10 \cdot \pi \cdot y_p \cdot m^2$$
$$m^2 = \frac{P_t}{\sigma_d \cdot 10 \cdot \pi \cdot y_p} = \frac{139,261}{84 \times 10 \times \pi \times 0.32}$$
$$m^2 = \frac{139,261}{844.35} = 164.93$$
$$m = 12.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 11: Standardize the Module**
Standard module: $m = 14$ mm (or as per IS 3664, take next standard).

**Step 12: Recalculate with m = 14 mm**
$$d_p = 14 \times 20 = 280 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = 14 \times 60 = 840 \text{ mm}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 280 \times 1440}{60 \times 10^3} = 21.12 \text{ m/s}$$
$$P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 21}{21.12} = 59,659 \text{ N}$$
$$b = 10 \times 14 = 140 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 13: Recompute Beam Strength (Pinion)**
$$W_B = 84 \times 140 \times \pi \times 14 \times 0.32 = 165,332 \text{ N}$$

**Check:** $W_B = 165,332$ N $\geq$ $P_t = 59,659$ N. **SAFE ✓**

**Step 14: Wear Check (Buckingham's Equation)**
$$W_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$$

**Ratio Factor:**
$$Q = \frac{2 \cdot z_g}{z_g + z_p} = \frac{2 \times 60}{60 + 20} = \frac{120}{80} = 1.5$$

**Wear Strength:**
$$W_W = 280 \times 140 \times 1.5 \times 0.55 = 32,340 \text{ N}$$

**Check:** $W_W = 32,340$ N $< P_t = 59,659$ N. **FAILS wear check ✗**

**Step 15: Redesign for Wear**
The wear strength must equal the tangential load:

$$P_t = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$$
$$P_t = (m \cdot z_p) \cdot (10m) \cdot Q \cdot K$$
$$P_t = 10 \cdot Q \cdot K \cdot z_p \cdot m^2$$
$$m^2 = \frac{P_t}{10 \cdot Q \cdot K \cdot z_p} = \frac{59,659}{10 \times 1.5 \times 0.55 \times 20} = \frac{59,659}{165} = 361.57$$
$$m = 19.01 \text{ mm} \approx 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 16: Final Selection — m = 20 mm**

$$d_p = 20 \times 20 = 400 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = 20 \times 60 = 1200 \text{ mm}$$
$$b = 10 \times 20 = 200 \text{ mm}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 400 \times 1440}{60 \times 10^3} = 30.16 \text{ m/s}$$
$$P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 21}{30.16} = 41,778 \text{ N}$$

**Step 17: Final Verification**
$$W_B = 84 \times 200 \times \pi \times 20 \times 0.32 = 337,920 \text{ N} \geq 41,778 \text{ N} \quad ✓$$
$$W_W = 400 \times 200 \times 1.5 \times 0.55 = 66,000 \text{ N} \geq 41,778 \text{ N} \quad ✓$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Design Summary:** Module m = 20 mm, Pinion teeth = 20, Gear teeth = 60, Face width = 200 mm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Spur Gear Design Procedure

The following Mermaid flow captures the **standard KTU-aligned** algorithm for designing a pair of spur gears. This is the decision-tree that examiners expect you to follow in your answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Design Inputs]) --> nodeB[Power P kW, Speed N rpm, Ratio i]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Apply Service Factor Cs]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Design Power Pd = P times Cs]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Select Materials for Pinion and Gear]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Allowable Stress sigma d]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Assume Module m and Pinion Teeth zp]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute Pitch Diameter d = m times z]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Pitch Velocity v]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute Tangential Load Pt]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Apply Lewis Equation for Beam Strength WB]
    nodeK --> nodeL{WB greater than or equal to Pt?}
    nodeL -- NO --> nodeM[Increase Module and Recalculate]
    nodeM --> nodeG
    nodeL -- YES --> nodeN[Compute Face Width b = psi times m]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Apply Buckingham Wear Equation WW]
    nodeO --> nodeP{WW greater than or equal to Pt?}
    nodeP -- NO --> nodeQ[Increase Module and Recalculate]
    nodeQ --> nodeG
    nodeP -- YES --> nodeR[Check Dynamic Load Pd]
    nodeR --> nodeS{Pd less than or equal to WB and WW?}
    nodeS -- NO --> nodeT[Increase Module or Face Width]
    nodeT --> nodeG
    nodeS -- YES --> nodeU[Finalize Dimensions and Standardize]
    nodeU --> nodeV([END: Final Spur Gear Design])
```

## 4.2 Spur Gear Tooth Geometry (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Spur Gear Pair System] --> nodeB[PINION]
    nodeA --> nodeC[GEAR]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Number of Teeth zp]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Module m in mm]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Pitch Diameter dp]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Number of Teeth zg]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Module m in mm SAME]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Pitch Diameter dg]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Lewis Form Factor yp]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Circular Pitch pi times m]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Pitch Velocity v]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Tangential Load Pt]
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Beam Strength WB]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Wear Strength WW]
    nodeN --> nodeP[Design Validation]
    nodeO --> nodeP
```

## 4.3 Failure Modes Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Gear Tooth Failure Modes] --> nodeB[Bending Failure]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Surface Failure]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Dynamic Impact Failure]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Lewis Beam Strength WB]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Material: sigma d]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Tooth Geometry: y and m]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Buckingham Wear Strength WW]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Material: K]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Geometry: dp, b, Q]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Dynamic Load Pd]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Velocity v]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Face Width b]
    nodeE --> nodeN[Final Safety Check]
    nodeH --> nodeN
    nodeK --> nodeN
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q.1, Module 3]`
**Define the term "Lewis Form Factor" and state its significance in gear design.**

**Model Answer (Valuator's Key):**

The **Lewis Form Factor (y)** is a dimensionless numerical constant that accounts for the geometric shape of a gear tooth when analyzed as a cantilever beam. It is defined as:

$$y = \frac{t^2}{6 \cdot h \cdot \cos\Phi}$$

where $t$ is the tooth thickness at the root, $h$ is the tooth length from root to pitch point, and $\Phi$ is the pressure angle. **Significance:** It enables the simplification of the complex tooth geometry into a single numerical factor used in the **Lewis beam strength equation** ($W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$). It varies primarily with the **number of teeth and pressure angle**, decreasing as the number of teeth decreases. It is used to determine the **beam strength of the weaker gear tooth** to prevent bending failure.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q.2, Module 3]`
**What is the difference between Lewis equation and Buckingham's equation in gear design?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lewis Equation | Buckingham's Equation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Purpose** | Determines **beam strength** (bending failure) | Determines **wear strength** (surface pitting) |
| **Mode of Failure** | Tooth breakage | Surface pitting/fatigue |
| **Basic Formula** | $W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | $W_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ |
| **Tooth Model** | Cantilever beam | Hertzian contact stress |
| **Key Variable** | Module $m$ and form factor $y$ | Pitch diameter $d_p$ and load stress factor $K$ |
| **Governs** | Strength of tooth cross-section | Strength of contact surface |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Lewis equation** for the beam strength of a spur gear tooth, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design a pair of spur gears to transmit **10 kW** at **1200 rpm** of the pinion with a **speed ratio of 4:1**. The pinion has **18 teeth** and is made of steel with UTS = **600 N/mm²**. The gear material has UTS = **500 N/mm²**. Use **20° full-depth involute teeth**. Service factor = 1.5. Lewis form factor for the pinion $y_p = 0.308$. Take $K = 0.55$ N/mm² and $b = 10m$. Check the design for **wear and dynamic load**. (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Derivation of Lewis Equation

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation of Lewis Equation)** in this document. The complete 7-step derivation is:

1. **Assumption:** Tooth is a parabolic cantilever beam.
2. Tangential load: $P_H = P_t \cos \Phi$
3. Bending moment at root: $M = P_t \cos \Phi \cdot h$
4. Section modulus: $Z = b t^2 / 6$
5. Bending stress: $\sigma = 6 P_t \cos \Phi \cdot h / (b t^2)$
6. Form factor: $y = t^2 / (6 h \cos \Phi)$
7. Final equation:

$$W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Design

**Step 1: Design Power**
$$P_d = 10 \times 1.5 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Pitch Diameters**
$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 18m, \quad d_g = m \cdot z_g = 4 \times 18m = 72m$$

**Step 3: Tangential Load (in terms of m)**
$$T = \frac{60 \times 15 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 1200} = 119,366 \text{ N·mm}$$
$$P_t = \frac{2T}{d_p} = \frac{2 \times 119,366}{18m} = \frac{13,263}{m} \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Allowable Stress (Pinion)**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{600}{3 \times 1.5 \times 1.5} = 88.89 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Apply Lewis Equation**
$$P_t \leq W_B = \sigma_d \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_p$$
$$\frac{13,263}{m} \leq 88.89 \times (10m) \times \pi \times m \times 0.308$$
$$\frac{13,263}{m} \leq 859.97 \cdot m^2$$
$$m^3 \geq \frac{13,263}{859.97} = 15.42$$
$$m \geq 2.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Standardize Module** → $m = 3$ mm (standard IS value)

**Step 7: Recompute Final Values**
$$d_p = 3 \times 18 = 54 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = 3 \times 72 = 216 \text{ mm}$$
$$b = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ mm}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 54 \times 1200}{60 \times 10^3} = 3.39 \text{ m/s}$$
$$P_t = \frac{13,263}{3} = 4,421 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Wear Check**
$$Q = \frac{2 \times 72}{72 + 18} = 1.6$$
$$W_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = 54 \times 30 \times 1.6 \times 0.55 = 1,425.6 \text{ N}$$

**Wear Check:** $W_W = 1,425.6$ N $< P_t = 4,421$ N. **FAILS ✗**

**Step 9: Redesign for Wear**
$$P_t = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$$
$$\frac{13,263}{m} = (18m)(10m)(1.6)(0.55) = 158.4 \cdot m^2$$
$$m^3 = \frac{13,263}{158.4} = 83.73$$
$$m = 4.37 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 10: Final Selection — m = 5 mm**
$$d_p = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = 5 \times 72 = 360 \text{ mm}$$
$$b = 10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ mm}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 90 \times 1200}{60 \times 10^3} = 5.65 \text{ m/s}$$
$$P_t = \frac{13,263}{5} = 2,653 \text{ N}$$

**Step 11: Final Verification**
$$W_B = 88.89 \times 50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.308 = 21,499 \text{ N} \geq 2,653 \text{ N} \quad ✓$$
$$W_W = 90 \times 50 \times 1.6 \times 0.55 = 3,960 \text{ N} \geq 2,653 \text{ N} \quad ✓$$

**Step 12: Dynamic Load Check**
$$P_d = P_t + \frac{21v(Cb\cos\Phi + P_t)}{21v + \sqrt{Cb\cos\Phi + P_t}}$$

Taking C = 11.43 kN/mm (standard steel) and $\Phi = 20°$:
$$P_d = 2,653 + \frac{21 \times 5.65 \times (11.43 \times 50 \times 0.9397 + 2,653)}{21 \times 5.65 + \sqrt{11.43 \times 50 \times 0.9397 + 2,653}}$$
$$P_d = 2,653 + \frac{118.65 \times (537.07 + 2,653)}{118.65 + \sqrt{3,190.07}}$$
$$P_d = 2,653 + \frac{118.65 \times 3,190.07}{118.65 + 56.48}$$
$$P_d = 2,653 + \frac{378,502}{175.13} = 2,653 + 2,161 = 4,814 \text{ N}$$

**Final Dynamic Check:** $P_d = 4,814$ N. Since $W_B = 21,499$ N and $W_W = 3,960$ N, the wear strength is slightly exceeded under dynamic loading. Hence recommend **m = 6 mm** for safety, or use hardened gear material.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert Power Units:** Power must be in **kW** when using $P_t = 60000 P/v$. Mixing up with W gives 60× wrong answer.
> 2. **Not Standardizing the Module:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you leave module as a decimal. Use IS/AGMA standard values (1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20...).
> 3. **Missing the Wear Check:** Many students solve the Lewis equation and stop. This loses **3 to 4 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Using Gear's y instead of Pinion's y:** Always use the **weaker gear** (typically pinion with fewer teeth) for design.
> 5. **Skipping Dynamic Load Verification:** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly asks for **wear AND dynamic load** check. Omitting dynamic load is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Wrong unit for stress:** N/mm² = MPa. Don't write "N/m²" or leave it blank.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch, the **Lewis theory of gear tooth failure**. State clearly the **assumptions and limitations** of the Lewis equation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pair of spur gears with **20° stub teeth** is to transmit **8 kW** at **960 rpm** of the pinion. The speed ratio is **3.5:1**. Both gears are made of **cast iron with $\sigma_b$ = 350 N/mm²**. Take the service factor as 1.25. Lewis form factor for pinion $y_p = 0.36$ and gear $y_g = 0.39$. Take $b = 12m$. Determine the **module, face width, and pitch circle diameters**. (7 Marks)

#### (a) Model Solution Outline (Sketch + Explanation)

The sketch should show:
- A gear tooth as a cantilever beam
- Load $P_t$ at the pitch circle with horizontal component $P_H = P_t \cos \Phi$
- Parabolic root profile
- Labels: $h$ (tooth height), $t$ (root thickness), $b$ (face width), $y_p$, $\Phi$

**Assumptions:**
1. Load is applied at the tip of the tooth (worst case).
2. Tooth acts as a cantilever beam.
3. Tooth has a uniform parabolic shape.
4. Stress is purely bending; no dynamic or wear effects included.
5. Friction is neglected.

**Limitations:**
1. Does not account for wear.
2. Does not include dynamic load.
3. Assumes static load only.
4. Real teeth have stress concentration at the fillet.

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Design

**Step 1: Design Power**
$$P_d = 8 \times 1.25 = 10 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Allowable Stress (Cast Iron)**
For CI gears, $\sigma_d = \sigma_b / 5.5$ (per Buckingham):
$$\sigma_d = 350 / 5.5 = 63.6 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Take $z_p = 21$** (minimum for 20° stub avoiding interference)
$$z_g = 3.5 \times 21 = 73.5 \approx 74$$

**Step 4: Tangential Load (in terms of m)**
$$T = \frac{60 \times 10 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 960} = 99,471 \text{ N·mm}$$
$$P_t = \frac{2T}{d_p} = \frac{2 \times 99,471}{21m} = \frac{9,473.5}{m} \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Apply Lewis Equation**
$$P_t \leq W_B = \sigma_d \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_p$$
$$\frac{9,473.5}{m} = 63.6 \times (12m) \times \pi \times m \times 0.36$$
$$\frac{9,473.5}{m} = 863.0 \cdot m^2$$
$$m^3 = \frac{9,473.5}{863.0} = 10.98$$
$$m = 2.22 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Standardize** → $m = 2.5$ mm

**Step 7: Final Dimensions**
$$d_p = 2.5 \times 21 = 52.5 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_g = 2.5 \times 74 = 185 \text{ mm}$$
$$b = 12 \times 2.5 = 30 \text{ mm}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 52.5 \times 960}{60 \times 10^3} = 2.64 \text{ m/s}$$
$$P_t = \frac{9,473.5}{2.5} = 3,789 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Final Beam Strength Check**
$$W_B = 63.6 \times 30 \times \pi \times 2.5 \times 0.36 = 5,394 \text{ N} \geq 3,789 \text{ N} \quad ✓$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam):**

- [ ] **Lewis equation** = $W_B = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ — used for **bending failure**.
- [ ] **Buckingham wear equation** = $W_W = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ — used for **surface pitting failure**.
- [ ] **Dynamic load** = Buckingham's dynamic load formula — used to check **impact failure**.
- [ ] **Design power** = Rated power × service factor ($C_s$).
- [ ] **Tangential load** $P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times P_d}{v}$ = $\frac{2T}{d_p}$.
- [ ] **Pitch circle diameter** = $d = m \cdot z$.
- [ ] **Pitch line velocity** = $v = \frac{\pi d N}{60 \times 10^3}$ in m/s.
- [ ] **Face width** = (8 to 14) × module, with 10 being the KTU standard.
- [ ] **Always design for the weaker (pinion) gear**, using $y_p$.
- [ ] **Standard module values** (IS 3664): 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32...
- [ ] **Pressure angle** = 20° (standard) or 14.5° (older Indian standard).
- [ ] **Minimum teeth** = 18 (20° full depth) or 14 (20° stub) to avoid interference.
- [ ] **Material pairing** = Pinion harder than gear (5–10% more) to equalize wear.
- [ ] **Design check sequence**: Lewis → Buckingham Wear → Dynamic Load.
- [ ] **Allowable stress for steel** ≈ $\sigma_{ut} / 4.5$ to $5.0$ (with safety factor).
- [ ] **Allowable stress for cast iron** ≈ $\sigma_b / 5.5$.
- [ ] **Lewis form factor** depends on z, pressure angle, and tooth system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Contact Bearing - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Rolling Contact Bearing - Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Rolling Contact Bearing** is a machine element that provides a **low-friction, constrained sliding-free rotational or translational motion** between two machine members by introducing an array of hardened rolling elements (balls or rollers) sandwiched between two raceways — an **inner race** (mounted on the rotating shaft) and an **outer race** (mounted on the stationary housing). These rolling elements are precisely spaced by a **cage** (also called a retainer) to prevent mutual contact and ensure uniform load distribution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 Highlight):** > A *rolling contact bearing* replaces the sliding friction of journal bearings with **rolling friction**, which is typically **1000 to 10,000 times smaller** than sliding friction at equivalent operating conditions. The rolling elements transmit the load from the inner race to the outer race along the line (or point) of contact. > [!NOTE] > **Roll-Equivalent Friction Coefficient (μ):** > - Sliding (Plain) Bearings: μ ≈ 0.05 – 0.20 (boundary lubrication) > - **Rolling Element Bearings: μ ≈ 0.001 – 0.005** (rolling friction) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview **Analogy — The Skateboard Wheel:** Imagine pushing a heavy wooden crate across a rough concrete floor. The friction is enormous, and the crate barely moves. Now, place the same crate on a wheeled platform (a skateboard) and push it. The same force now rolls the crate effortlessly. The wheels convert **sliding friction** into **rolling friction**. In a machine: - The **crate** is the rotating shaft. - The **floor** is the stationary housing. - The **wheels** are the **balls or rollers** inside the bearing. Because the contact area between a sphere and a flat raceway is infinitesimally small, the resistance to motion (rolling friction) is dramatically reduced, allowing the shaft to spin freely with minimal energy loss. --- ## 1.3 Why Rolling Contact Bearings? (The Engineering Motivation) > [!TIP] > Rolling bearings are **off-the-shelf standardized components**. A designer does not need to manufacture the bearing — they only need to **select** the correct size and type from a manufacturer's catalog. This drastically reduces design time and increases interchangeability — a key reason they dominate modern machinery (electric motors, automobiles, gearboxes, turbines, machine tools). **Key Engineering Advantages:** - **Very low starting torque** (important for instruments and precision machinery). - **High operating accuracy** (no metal-to-metal contact under hydrodynamic film). - **Standardization & Interchangeability** (ISO 15, ISO 355, ISO 104). - **Low maintenance** (often pre-lubricated and sealed for life). - **Suitable for high speeds** (DN value up to ~1,000,000 mm·rpm for ball bearings). **Key Limitations:** - Limited to **relatively small and medium load** applications (compared to hydrodynamic bearings). - Sensitive to **shock loads, misalignment, and contamination**. - **Finite fatigue life** (must be replaced after L10 hours of operation). - Higher **initial cost** than plain journal bearings in small sizes. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomical Components of a Rolling Contact Bearing Every rolling contact bearing consists of **four fundamental elements**: | # | Component | Function | Material (Typical) | |---|-----------|----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Inner Race** | Mounted on the shaft; transmits load to/from the rolling elements via the inner raceway. | Through-hardened Chromium Steel (e.g., AISI 52100) | | 2 | **Outer Race** | Mounted in the housing; provides the outer raceway that reacts the load. | Through-hardened Chromium Steel | | 3 | **Rolling Elements (Ball or Roller)** | The "wheels" that convert sliding into rolling motion. | Through-hardened Chromium Steel (HRC ≈ 60–65) | | 4 | **Cage / Retainer** | Maintains uniform angular spacing between rolling elements; prevents skewing and roller-to-roller contact. | Pressed Steel / Phenolic Resin / Brass / Polymer (PEEK) | > [!NOTE] > **Raceway Curvature:** The raceway in a ball bearing is machined with a slight crowning (osculation ratio of ≈ 0.515 for deep-groove ball bearings). This prevents edge-stressing even under misalignment up to a few minutes of arc. --- ## 2.2 Classification of Rolling Contact Bearings (KTU High-Yield Topic) Rolling contact bearings are broadly classified into two super-families: ### A. Ball Bearings (Point Contact — load transmitted through a *point*) 1. **Deep Groove Ball Bearing** (most common; radial + moderate axial load). 2. **Angular Contact Ball Bearing** (high axial + radial load; accepts combined loads). 3. **Self-Aligning Ball Bearing** (tolerates misalignment up to ~2.5°). 4. **Thrust Ball Bearing** (pure axial load only). ### B. Roller Bearings (Line Contact — load transmitted along a *line*) 1. **Cylindrical Roller Bearing** (heavy radial load; allows axial displacement of shaft). 2. **Tapered Roller Bearing** (combined radial + axial; supports large combined loads). 3. **Spherical Roller Bearing** (self-aligning; very heavy radial load + moderate thrust). 4. **Needle Roller Bearing** (small cross-section; high radial load in limited space). 5. **Thrust Roller Bearing** (heavy axial load). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Point vs. Line Contact — Why It Matters:** > A ball touches its raceway at a single **point** → Hertzian contact stress is concentrated → load capacity is lower. > A roller touches its raceway along a **line** → contact area is larger → load capacity is higher. > Therefore: **For the same envelope size, roller bearings carry more load than ball bearings**, but ball bearings run faster and at lower friction. --- ## 2.3 Detailed Sub-Types — Designer's Selection Matrix | Type | Load Direction | Relative Load Capacity | Relative Speed Limit | Misalignment Tolerance | Application Example | |------|----------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------| | **Deep Groove Ball (Type 6)** | Radial + small axial | Moderate | **High** | Low (~2–10 min) | Electric motors, pumps | | **Angular Contact Ball (Type 7)** | Combined (R + A) | Moderate–High | High | Low | Machine tool spindles, gearboxes | | **Self-Aligning Ball (Type 1)** | Radial | Moderate | Moderate | **High (~2.5°)** | Long shaft with deflection | | **Cylindrical Roller (Type N)** | Radial (heavy) | **High** | High | Very Low | Railway axle boxes | | **Tapered Roller (Type 3)** | Combined (R + A) | **Very High** | Moderate | Low | Automotive wheel hubs | | **Spherical Roller (Type 2)** | Radial (heavy) + axial | **Very High** | Moderate | **High (~2°)** | Cement mills, crushers | | **Needle Roller** | Radial | **High (compact)** | Moderate | Low | Connecting rod (piston pin) | | **Thrust Ball (Type 5)** | Axial only | Low–Moderate | Moderate | — | Crane hooks, vertical shafts | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Essential for ESE) > [!IMPORTANT] > All the following formulas are tested in KTU ESE. Master the notation: > *C* = Basic Dynamic Load Rating (kN), *P* = Equivalent Dynamic Load (kN), > *C₀* = Basic Static Load Rating, *P₀* = Equivalent Static Load, *L₁₀* = fatigue life in millions of revolutions, *L₁₀ₕ* = life in hours, *n* = speed (rpm), *k* = 3 (ball) or 10/3 (roller). | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | |---|--------------|----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Bearing Life Equation (Standard Weibull)** | $L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k$ | $L_{10}$ in million revolutions; $k = 3$ (ball), $k = \dfrac{10}{3}$ (roller) | | 2 | **Life in Operating Hours** | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \, n} \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k$ | $L_{10h}$ in hours, $n$ in rpm | | 3 | **Equivalent Dynamic Radial Load** | $P = X \, F_r + Y \, F_a$ | $X$ = radial factor, $Y$ = thrust factor (from catalog) | | 4 | **Equivalent Static Load** | $P_0 = X_0 \, F_r + Y_0 \, F_a$ | $X_0, Y_0$ from catalog | | 5 | **Static Load Safety Check** | $C_0 \geq S_0 \, P_0$ | $S_0$ = static safety factor (1.0–2.0 typical) | | 6 | **Required Dynamic Load Rating** | $C_{\text{req}} = P \, L_{10}^{1/k}$ | For *design-for-life* problems | | 7 | **Required Speed (rpm from linear velocity)** | $n = \dfrac{60 \, v}{\pi \, d}$ | $v$ = pitch-line velocity, $d$ = pitch diameter | | 8 | **Thrust Ball Bearing (single row, flat seat)** | $P = F_a$ (pure axial) | — | | 9 | **Misalignment angle (self-aligning type)** | $\theta \leq 2.5^{\circ}$ for ball; $\leq 2^{\circ}$ for spherical roller | Manufacturer dependent | | 10 | **Rotational frequency per bearing element** | $f_c = \dfrac{n}{2} \cdot \dfrac{d}{D}$ | $d$ = roller/ball diameter, $D$ = pitch diameter | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Rolling contact bearings are the **backbone of rotating machinery** across every engineering sector: - **Automotive Industry:** Tapered roller bearings in wheel hubs; deep-groove ball bearings in alternators and gearboxes. - **Aerospace:** Angular contact and cylindrical roller bearings in jet engine mainshafts (operating at **DN > 2 million mm·rpm**). - **Wind Turbines:** Spherical roller bearings in the main shaft and yaw drives (must withstand misalignment due to wind load variation). - **Machine Tools:** Hybrid ceramic-angular contact bearings in high-speed spindles (>20,000 rpm). - **Railways:** Cylindrical roller bearings in axle boxes (carrying >20 tonnes per axle). - **Domestic Appliances:** Sealed-for-life deep-groove ball bearings in washing machine drums, ceiling fans. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Bearing Life Equation (KTU ESE Favourite) The fatigue life of a rolling contact bearing is governed by the **Palmgren–Lundberg fatigue theory**, which assumes that the **subsurface shear stress** under Hertzian contact is responsible for fatigue crack initiation. Weibull's weakest-link statistics give a **90% survival probability** at the rated life. ### Step 1 — Definition of Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C) > The Basic Dynamic Load Rating *C* is defined as the **constant radial (or axial, for thrust) load** that a bearing can theoretically endure for **1 million revolutions** with a **90% reliability** (i.e., 10% of the population is expected to fail). ### Step 2 — Fatigue Stress–Life Relation For Hertzian contact, the maximum subsurface shear stress $\tau_{\max}$ is proportional to the **cube root** of the load per unit length (for line contact) or load itself (for point contact). The number of stress cycles to fatigue failure *N* is related to the stress by: $$ N \propto \left( \frac{1}{\tau_{\max}} \right)^m $$ where the exponent $m$ depends on contact geometry. ### Step 3 — Combining Stress and Load Since $\tau_{\max} \propto P^{1/c}$ (where $c = 3$ for balls, $c = 10/3$ for rollers), substitution gives: $$ N \propto P^{-m/c} $$ ### Step 4 — Using the Reference Point (C corresponds to N = 10⁶ revolutions) By definition, when $P = C$, the life is $L = 1$ (million revolutions). Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10} &= 1 \cdot \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{m/c} \end{aligned} $$ The exponent ratio $m/c$ is calibrated experimentally by SKF, FAG, NSK, and other bearing manufacturers. The accepted standard values are: $$ k = \frac{m}{c} = 3 \;\; \text{(for ball bearings)}, \qquad k = \frac{10}{3} \;\; \text{(for roller bearings)} $$ ### Step 5 — Final Standard Equation (Lundberg–Palmgren Form) $$ \boxed{\; L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{k} \;} $$ where: - $L_{10}$ = rating life in **millions of revolutions**. - $C$ = basic dynamic load rating (N or kN). - $P$ = equivalent dynamic load (N or kN). - $k = 3$ (ball) or $k = 10/3$ (roller). ### Step 6 — Converting to Life in Hours Since $L_{10}$ is in *millions* of revolutions and the shaft rotates at $n$ rpm, the number of revolutions per hour is $60 \, n$. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total revolutions in } L_{10h} \text{ hours} &= 60 \, n \cdot L_{10h} \\ \text{But also} &= L_{10} \times 10^6 \;\; \text{(revolutions)} \end{aligned} $$ Equating: $$ \boxed{\; L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \, n} \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k \;} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Type A (Single Bearing Life Calculation) **Problem (KTU-style):** A deep-groove ball bearing operates with the following data: - Radial load on bearing: $F_r = 4 \, \text{kN}$ - Axial load on bearing: $F_a = 1.5 \, \text{kN}$ - Speed: $n = 1440 \, \text{rpm}$ - Basic dynamic load rating from manufacturer: $C = 25 \, \text{kN}$ - Radial factor $X = 0.56$, Thrust factor $Y = 1.0$ (catalog values for the chosen bearing when $F_a / C_0 \leq 0.04$). **Find:** (i) Equivalent dynamic load $P$. (ii) Rating life in hours $L_{10h}$. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load** using the standard ISO equation: $$ \begin{aligned} P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\ &= (0.56)(4.0) + (1.0)(1.5) \\ &= 2.24 + 1.50 \\ &= 3.74 \, \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the rating life in millions of revolutions** (using $k = 3$ for ball bearing): $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10} &= \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{3} \\ &= \left( \frac{25}{3.74} \right)^{3} \\ &= (6.6845)^{3} \\ &= 298.62 \;\; \text{million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to life in hours:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6}{60 \, n} \cdot L_{10} \\ &= \frac{10^6}{60 \times 1440} \times 298.62 \\ &= \frac{10^6 \times 298.62}{86400} \\ &= 3456.25 \;\; \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $P = 3.74 \, \text{kN}$, and $L_{10h} \approx 3456 \, \text{hours}$ (≈ 4.5 months of continuous operation). --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Type B (Design for Required Life) **Problem:** A cylindrical roller bearing must operate for **20,000 hours** at **1000 rpm** under an equivalent dynamic load of $P = 12 \, \text{kN}$. Determine the **required basic dynamic load rating** for bearing selection. ### Solution: **Step 1 — For roller bearings, $k = 10/3$.** **Step 2 — Required life in millions of revolutions:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10} &= \frac{60 \, n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} \\ &= \frac{60 \times 1000 \times 20{,}000}{10^6} \\ &= \frac{1.2 \times 10^9}{10^6} \\ &= 1200 \;\; \text{million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Required basic dynamic load rating** (inverting the life equation): $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{req}} &= P \cdot L_{10}^{1/k} \\ &= 12 \times (1200)^{3/10} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the exponent:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1200)^{0.3} &= e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(1200)} \\ &= e^{0.3 \times 7.0901} \\ &= e^{2.1270} \\ &\approx 8.392 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{req}} &= 12 \times 8.392 \\ &\approx 100.7 \, \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** The designer should select a roller bearing with $C \geq 100.7 \, \text{kN}$. From the manufacturer's catalog, the next standard size with $C \geq 100.7$ kN should be chosen. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Selection Matrix — When to Use What? The following **decision logic** is often asked in KTU exams: | Operating Condition | Recommended Bearing Type | Justification | |---------------------|--------------------------|---------------| | Pure radial load, high speed, no misalignment | **Deep Groove Ball Bearing** | Simplest, cheapest, low friction, high speed. | | Combined radial + axial load, precision spindle | **Angular Contact Ball Bearing** | Can support axial load in one direction; arranged in back-to-back (DB) or face-to-face (DF) pair for bidirectional load. | | Heavy radial load, no axial load, very high speed | **Cylindrical Roller Bearing** | Line contact = higher load capacity than ball; some types (N, NU) allow axial displacement of shaft. | | Combined heavy radial + axial load, e.g., wheel hub | **Tapered Roller Bearing** | Lines of contact at angle to raceway; very high load capacity. | | Shaft deflection / misalignment present | **Self-Aligning Ball / Spherical Roller** | Spherical outer race accommodates misalignment up to 2–2.5°. | | Pure axial load, low-to-moderate speed | **Thrust Ball Bearing** | Specifically designed for axial loads. | | Limited radial space, high load needed | **Needle Roller Bearing** | Small cross-section, high load-to-size ratio. | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Rolling Contact Bearings ```mermaid graph TD A["ROLLING CONTACT BEARINGS"] --> B["BALL BEARINGS
Point Contact"] A --> C["ROLLER BEARINGS
Line Contact"] B --> B1["Deep Groove Ball
Type 6"] B --> B2["Angular Contact Ball
Type 7"] B --> B3["Self-Aligning Ball
Type 1"] B --> B4["Thrust Ball
Type 5"] C --> C1["Cylindrical Roller
Type N NU NJ"] C --> C2["Tapered Roller
Type 3"] C --> C3["Spherical Roller
Type 2"] C --> C4["Needle Roller
Type K"] C --> C5["Thrust Roller
Type 8"] B1 --> D1["Radial + small axial
High speed"] B2 --> D2["Combined load
One direction thrust"] B3 --> D3["Radial
Self-aligning 2.5 deg"] B4 --> D4["Pure axial only"] C1 --> D5["Heavy radial
Allow shaft expansion"] C2 --> D6["Heavy combined load
Paired mounting"] C3 --> D7["Heavy radial + axial
Self-aligning 2 deg"] C4 --> D8["Compact radial
Limited space"] C5 --> D9["Heavy axial only"] classDef rootClass fill:#1f3a68,stroke:#1f3a68,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef ballClass fill:#c0392b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef rollerClass fill:#27ae60,stroke:#27ae60,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef appClass fill:#f39c12,stroke:#f39c12,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px class A rootClass class B,B1,B2,B3,B4 ballClass class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 rollerClass class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5,D6,D7,D8,D9 appClass ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Deep Groove Ball Bearing ```mermaid graph LR A["ROTATING SHAFT"] -->|Press Fit| B["INNER RACE
Inner Raceway"] B -->|Hertzian Contact| C["BALLS
Rolling Elements"] C -->|Hertzian Contact| D["OUTER RACE
Outer Raceway"] D -->|Slip Fit| E["HOUSING"] C -->|Spaced by| F["CAGE / RETAINER
Phosphor Bronze or Steel"] B -.->|Lubricant Film| G["GREASE / OIL"] D -.->|Sealed by| H["SEAL / SHIELD
2RS ZZ Type"] classDef structural fill:#2980b9,stroke:#2980b9,color:#ffffff classDef dynamic fill:#16a085,stroke:#16a085,color:#ffffff classDef aux fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff class A,E structural class B,C,D,F dynamic class G,H aux ``` ## 4.3 Bearing Selection Decision Flowchart (Functional Logic) ```mermaid graph TD S["START: Identify Load"] --> Q1{"Load direction?"} Q1 -->|Pure Radial| Q2{"Speed high?"} Q1 -->|Pure Axial| Q3["Thrust Ball or Roller"] Q1 -->|Combined| Q4["Angular Contact or Tapered Roller"] Q2 -->|Yes, low misalignment| R1["Deep Groove Ball"] Q2 -->|Heavy radial| R2["Cylindrical Roller"] Q2 -->|Misalignment present| R3["Self-Aligning Ball / Spherical Roller"] Q4 -->|Precision| R4["Angular Contact Ball"] Q4 -->|Heavy load| R5["Tapered Roller Bearing"] R1 --> F["SELECT FROM CATALOG
Check C vs C_req"] R2 --> F R3 --> F R4 --> F R5 --> F Q3 --> F F --> V["VERIFY: C_0 >= S_0 * P_0"] V --> Z["DONE: Specified Bearing"] classDef startClass fill:#1f3a68,stroke:#1f3a68,color:#ffffff classDef decisionClass fill:#c0392b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff classDef resultClass fill:#27ae60,stroke:#27ae60,color:#ffffff classDef finalClass fill:#f39c12,stroke:#f39c12,color:#000000 class S startClass class Q1,Q2 decisionClass class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 resultClass class F,V,Z finalClass ``` ## 4.4 Load Path Diagram (Radial vs Axial vs Combined) ```mermaid graph TD L["APPLIED LOAD"] --> LR{"Direction?"} LR -->|Fr| P1["Pure RADIAL LOAD"] LR -->|Fa| P2["Pure AXIAL LOAD"] LR -->|Fr + Fa| P3["COMBINED LOAD"] P1 --> S1["Transmitted through
mid-plane of ball/roller"] P2 --> S2["Transmitted through
contact angle alpha"] P3 --> S3["Resolved into radial and
axial components on raceway"] S1 --> T1["Type 6 / Type N / Type K"] S2 --> T2["Type 5 / Type 8"] S3 --> T3["Type 7 / Type 3"] classDef loadClass fill:#34495e,stroke:#34495e,color:#ffffff classDef transClass fill:#16a085,stroke:#16a085,color:#ffffff classDef typeClass fill:#d35400,stroke:#d35400,color:#ffffff class L,LR loadClass class P1,P2,P3,S1,S2,S3 transClass class T1,T2,T3 typeClass ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]` **Differentiate between ball bearings and roller bearings based on contact type, load capacity, and speed capability. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing | |-----------|--------------|----------------| | Contact Type | **Point contact** | **Line contact** | | Load Capacity | Lower (concentrated stress at a point) | Higher (stress distributed along a line) | | Friction & Heat | Lower friction | Slightly higher | | Speed Capability | **Higher** (suitable for very high rpm) | **Lower** (limitation due to line contact sliding) | | Radial Space | Requires deeper raceway | Compact for same load (e.g., needle bearings) | | Typical Application | Electric motors, pumps | Heavy industrial gearboxes, rolling mills | *(3 marks — one mark per relevant differentiating point with brief justification.)* --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]` **List the four essential components of a rolling contact bearing. State the function of the cage. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The four essential components are: 1. **Inner race** (with inner raceway) — mounted on the rotating shaft. 2. **Outer race** (with outer raceway) — mounted in the housing. 3. **Rolling elements** (balls or rollers) — transmit load between the races. 4. **Cage (retainer)** — keeps rolling elements uniformly spaced. **Function of the Cage:** The cage prevents direct contact between adjacent rolling elements, maintains uniform angular spacing, guides the rolling elements smoothly along the raceways during rotation, and prevents skewing (especially in roller bearings). This ensures **even load distribution** and prevents overheating due to element-to-element contact. *(1 mark for components list, 1 mark for cage function, 1 mark for engineering significance.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Bearing-Type Questions:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "thrust ball" with "angular contact ball"** — thrust ball is for *pure axial* loads only; angular contact supports *combined* loads. > 2. **In roller bearings, always remember** $k = 10/3$, not 3. This is the most-frequently lost mark. > 3. **Always convert life to a common unit** (revolutions OR hours) before substituting. Mixing units gives numerical answers that are off by factors of $10^3$ or $10^6$. > 4. **Self-aligning bearings have a SPHERICAL outer race** — not a spherical inner race. Drawing this incorrectly loses a sketch-mark. > 5. **For tapered rollers, "paired mounting"** (DB/DF/DT) is essential; single-row tapered bearings cannot carry axial load in both directions. --- ### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`** **(a)** Classify rolling contact bearings with a neat sketch, mentioning at least **four types** with their typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A deep-groove ball bearing is required to carry a radial load of $F_r = 3.5 \, \text{kN}$ and an axial load of $F_a = 1.2 \, \text{kN}$ at a speed of $n = 1500 \, \text{rpm}$. The manufacturer specifies $C = 22 \, \text{kN}$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.0$. Compute the expected rating life in hours. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): Rolling contact bearings are classified into: **A. Ball Bearings (Point Contact):** - **Deep Groove Ball Bearing** (Type 6): Most widely used; carries radial + small axial loads; high speed. *Application: electric motors, pumps, household appliances.* - **Angular Contact Ball Bearing** (Type 7): Designed to carry combined loads; one direction axial; arranged in pairs (DB, DF, DT). *Application: machine tool spindles, gearboxes.* - **Self-Aligning Ball Bearing** (Type 1): Has two rows of balls with a common spherical outer raceway; tolerates misalignment up to 2.5°. *Application: long shafts, textile machinery, conveyors.* - **Thrust Ball Bearing** (Type 5): Carries pure axial loads only; shaft axis must be horizontal. *Application: vertical shaft supports, crane hooks.* **B. Roller Bearings (Line Contact):** - **Cylindrical Roller Bearing** (Type N): Carries heavy radial loads; some variants (NU, N) permit axial displacement of the shaft. *Application: railway axle boxes, electric motors, gearboxes.* - **Tapered Roller Bearing** (Type 3): Carries combined radial + heavy axial loads; line contact at an angle to the raceway; must be mounted in pairs. *Application: automotive wheel hubs, rolling mills.* - **Spherical Roller Bearing** (Type 2): Has a spherical outer raceway and barrel-shaped rollers; tolerates misalignment up to 2°; very high radial load capacity. *Application: heavy industries, cement mills, mining crushers.* - **Needle Roller Bearing** (Type K): Uses long, thin cylindrical rollers; offers high load capacity in minimal radial space. *Application: connecting rod piston pins, transmission gear shafts, rocker arms.* *(Award 1 mark per type with sketch, up to 7 marks. Examiner expects neat labelled diagrams of at least 4 types.)* #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\ &= (0.56)(3.5) + (1.0)(1.2) \\ &= 1.96 + 1.20 \\ &= 3.16 \, \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Rating Life in Millions of Revolutions** (k = 3 for ball bearing): $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10} &= \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{3} \\ &= \left( \frac{22}{3.16} \right)^{3} \\ &= (6.962)^{3} \\ &= 337.5 \;\; \text{million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to Hours:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6}{60 \, n} \cdot L_{10} \\ &= \frac{10^6 \times 337.5}{60 \times 1500} \\ &= \frac{3.375 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^4} \\ &= 3750 \;\; \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $P = 3.16 \, \text{kN}$; $L_{10h} = 3750$ hours. --- ### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`** **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of **tapered roller bearing** and **spherical roller bearing**. Compare their suitability for applications with misalignment. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical roller bearing operates at $n = 1200 \, \text{rpm}$ and supports a radial load of $F_r = 18 \, \text{kN}$ (axial load negligible). The basic dynamic load rating is $C = 110 \, \text{kN}$. Estimate the rating life of the bearing in: (i) millions of revolutions, (ii) hours. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Tapered Roller Bearing Construction & Working:** The tapered roller bearing consists of four parts: **inner race (cone)**, **outer race (cup)**, **tapered rollers**, and a **cage**. The rollers and raceways are designed as **frustums of cones** with their apices meeting at a common point on the bearing axis. This design allows true rolling motion (combined with sliding) along the line contact. The roller axes converge at a common point, allowing the rollers to be positively guided and the contact angle $\alpha$ to be defined. The bearing is mounted in **opposing pairs** (DB/DF/DT) so that axial loads in both directions are reacted. **Spherical Roller Bearing Construction & Working:** The spherical roller bearing has two rows of **barrel-shaped (convex) rollers** running on a **spherical outer raceway**. The inner race has two inclined raceways at an angle to the bearing axis. The rollers, cage, and inner race assembly can swivel about the bearing center, allowing the bearing to **tolerate misalignment** between the shaft and housing of up to $\pm 2^{\circ}$. They carry very heavy radial loads and moderate axial loads in either direction. **Comparison for Misalignment:** | Feature | Tapered Roller | Spherical Roller | |---------|----------------|------------------| | Misalignment tolerance | **Poor** (~ 2–4 minutes of arc) | **Excellent** (≈ 2°) | | Load direction | Combined radial + axial (in one direction) | Radial (heavy) + axial (moderate) | | Speed | Moderate | Moderate | | Typical application | Wheel hubs, gearboxes | Cement mills, mining equipment, long shaft drives | *(Award 1 mark per labelled sketch, 2 marks for working explanation, 2 marks for comparison. Total 7.)* #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — For negligible axial load, $P = F_r = 18 \, \text{kN}$.** **Step 2 — For roller bearing, $k = 10/3 \approx 3.3333$.** **Step 3 — Compute Rating Life in Millions of Revolutions:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10} &= \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{10/3} \\ &= \left( \frac{110}{18} \right)^{3.3333} \\ &= (6.1111)^{3.3333} \end{aligned} $$ Computing the exponent: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(L_{10}) &= 3.3333 \times \ln(6.1111) \\ &= 3.3333 \times 1.8097 \\ &= 6.0322 \\ L_{10} &= e^{6.0322} \approx 416.3 \;\; \text{million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Hours:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6}{60 \, n} \cdot L_{10} \\ &= \frac{10^6 \times 416.3}{60 \times 1200} \\ &= \frac{4.163 \times 10^8}{7.2 \times 10^4} \\ &\approx 5782 \;\; \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** (i) $L_{10} \approx 416$ million revolutions; (ii) $L_{10h} \approx 5782$ hours (≈ 7.9 months of continuous operation). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minite Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Recall Block (Pin this in your mind before the ESE):** - ☐ Rolling bearings use **balls or rollers** to reduce friction (μ rolling << μ sliding). - ☐ **Point contact** = ball bearings; **Line contact** = roller bearings; **line contact carries more load** at the cost of slightly higher friction. - ☐ Standard life equation: $L_{10} = (C/P)^k$; with $k = 3$ (ball) and $k = 10/3$ (roller). - ☐ **Hours version:** $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \, n} (C/P)^k$. - ☐ **Equivalent load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (X, Y from manufacturer's catalog). - ☐ **Static check:** $C_0 \geq S_0 P_0$ where $S_0$ = safety factor. - ☐ **Four essential components** of any rolling bearing: Inner race, Outer race, Rolling elements, Cage. - ☐ **Deep Groove Ball (Type 6)** = most common, radial + small axial. - ☐ **Angular Contact (Type 7)** = combined load, mounted in pairs. - ☐ **Self-Aligning (Type 1 / Type 2)** = misalignment tolerance. - ☐ **Tapered Roller (Type 3)** = heavy combined load, must be paired. - ☐ **Cylindrical Roller (Type N)** = heavy radial, allows axial displacement (NU type). - ☐ **Needle Roller (Type K)** = compact high-load radial. - ☐ **Thrust Ball (Type 5) / Thrust Roller (Type 8)** = pure axial. - ☐ Material of races & rolling elements: **AISI 52100 (or equivalent) Chromium steel, HRC ≈ 60–65**. - ☐ For **misalignment**: use self-aligning ball or spherical roller. For **high speed**: use deep-groove or angular contact ball. For **heavy load**: use roller bearings. - ☐ **Cage** does not carry load; it only spaces the rolling elements. - ☐ Always use the **manufacturer's catalog** for X, Y, C, C₀ — never assume these. - ☐ **Misalignment limit:** Type 1 ball → 2.5°; Type 2 roller → 2°; Type 6/7/3 → few minutes only. - ☐ If the **load varies** with time, use the **equivalent fluctuating load** concept: $P_{eq} = \left[\dfrac{1}{L} \sum P_i^k \cdot n_i \, t_i \right]^{1/k}$ — applicable for variable load spectra. - ☐ KTU's **most-asked question**: "Classify rolling contact bearings with sketches" — must know **at least 6 types** with a one-line application. - ☐ KTU's **second-most-asked question**: "Bearing life calculation" — must master the unit conversion between revolutions and hours. ---

topics.title: Selection of a Bearing Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of a Bearing Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bearing Selection (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *The systematic engineering process of choosing the most suitable bearing type from among plain (sliding) bearings and rolling-element bearings, based on the operating conditions (load magnitude and direction, rotational speed, service life, lubrication availability, alignment tolerance, temperature, and economic constraints), such that the chosen bearing reliably transmits the applied loads, confines the moving parts, and satisfies the prescribed design life with adequate safety.*

In **PCRUT502 – Kinematics and Design of Machine Elements (Module 4)**, the selection of a bearing type is treated as a **decision-based design problem** rather than a pure stress analysis problem. The student must justify the *type* of bearing (journal vs. ball vs. roller vs. thrust) before computing its dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must learn to compare *Sliding Contact Bearings* with *Rolling Contact Bearings*, list the merit/demerit of each, and select an appropriate type for a given application using a structured **Selection Matrix**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Right Footwear"

Imagine you are packing for three different journeys:

| Journey | Terrain | Best Footwear | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mountain trek (heavy load, low speed, dusty) | Rocky, misaligned | **Hiking boots** with thick soles and shock pads | Cushioning + tolerance to misalignment |
| Sprint race (light load, very high speed) | Smooth, accurate track | **Spiked racing shoes** | Low friction, precision, low mass |
| Casual office walk (moderate speed, any direction) | Flat, mixed direction | **Regular shoes** | Versatile, cheap, easy |

A **bearing** is the "footwear" of a rotating shaft. A *plain bearing* (shoe with thick sole) absorbs heavy shocks and misalignment but creates friction. A *ball bearing* (racing shoe) gives low friction at high speed but is sensitive to misalignment and shock. A *roller bearing* is the **hiking boot** — strong, can take radial loads, and tolerates some shock.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Tip:** If you remember that *sliding = slow + heavy + dirty* and *rolling = fast + light + clean*, you will intuitively answer most KTU multiple-choice questions on bearing selection.

---

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Bearings

$$
\text{Bearings} \begin{cases}
\text{Sliding / Plain Contact Bearings} \begin{cases}
\text{Journal (Radial)} \\ \text{Thrust (Axial)}
\end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{Rolling Contact Bearings} \begin{cases}
\text{Ball Bearings} \begin{cases}
\text{Deep Groove} \\ \text{Angular Contact} \\ \text{Self-Aligning}
\end{cases} \\[4pt]
\text{Roller Bearings} \begin{cases}
\text{Cylindrical} \\ \text{Tapered} \\ \text{Spherical} \\ \text{Needle}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 1.4 Standard Design Metrics Used in Selection

The following **physical constants and design metrics** must be recalled (these appear repeatedly in KTU exam answers):

- **Specific load (Pressure), $p$** — in **MPa** ($N/mm^2$).
- **Rubbing velocity, $v$** — in **m/s**.
- **Product $p \cdot v$** (PV factor) — in **MPa·m/s**, governs heat generation in plain bearings.
- **Dynamic load rating, $C$** — in **kN**, the load at which a rolling bearing achieves **1 million revolutions** of L10 life.
- **Static load rating, $C_0$** — maximum load a stationary bearing can sustain without permanent deformation > 0.0001 of the ball diameter.
- **Bore diameter, $d$** — in **mm**, drives dimensional series (00, 01, 02, 03, 04).
- **Speed factor, $n \cdot d_m$** — where $d_m$ is the pitch diameter in **mm** and $n$ in **rps**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Bearing Type Selection

A KTU examiner expects the answer to follow this logical sequence:

### Step 1 — Identify the Load
- **Radial load only** → cylindrical roller, deep-groove ball, or journal.
- **Axial (thrust) load only** → thrust ball or thrust roller.
- **Combined radial + axial** → angular contact ball, tapered roller, spherical roller.

### Step 2 — Quantify the Load Magnitude
- **Light to moderate radial loads with no shock** → ball bearings.
- **Heavy radial loads with shock** → roller bearings (cylindrical or spherical).

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Speed (RPM)
- **Low speed (< 1000 rpm)**, dirty environment → *plain bearings*.
- **Moderate to high speed (> 1000 rpm)** → *rolling bearings* (lower friction, less heat).

### Step 4 — Consider Alignment & Rigidity
- **Possible shaft deflection or misalignment** → *self-aligning* ball or spherical roller.
- **High rigidity required** → *preloaded angular contact* or *tapered roller*.

### Step 5 — Service Life Target
- Long life (L10 > 10,000 hrs) → rolling bearings with proper life calculation.
- Short life, frequent replacement → plain bearings acceptable.

### Step 6 — Economic & Maintenance Constraints
- Cheap, low-noise, low-maintenance → rolling bearings (sealed-for-life).
- Repairable, easily machined, no rolling fatigue → plain bearings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A typical 14-mark question asks *"Select a suitable bearing for the following operating conditions … Justify."* The examiner awards marks for *each logical step* (≈ 2 marks × 7 logical points).

---

## 2.2 The PV Concept — Heat Generation in Plain Bearings

For plain (sliding) bearings, the **product of pressure and rubbing velocity** determines the heat generated at the sliding interface:

$$
p \cdot v \;\leq\; \text{(PV limit from McKee's or manufacturer's chart)}
$$

Where:
- $p = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot d}$ — bearing pressure (MPa)
- $v = \dfrac{\pi d n}{60{,}000}$ — rubbing velocity (m/s)
- $W$ = radial load (N), $L$ = bearing length (mm), $d$ = bore (mm), $n$ = rpm.

**McKee's Curve** for Babbitt bearings:

$$
PV_{\text{limit}} \approx \begin{cases}
1.75 \;\text{MPa·m/s} & \text{at } 5\,\text{m/s} \\ 
0.55 \;\text{MPa·m/s} & \text{at } 15\,\text{m/s}
\end{cases}
$$

The curve **falls** with velocity — at high speeds, *plain bearings become unsuitable* and we must switch to *rolling bearings*.

---

## 2.3 The Fundamental Equations of Rolling Bearing Life

### Dynamic Load Rating (ISO 281:2007 modified life equation)

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(millions of revolutions)}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = dynamic load rating (kN)
- $P$ = equivalent dynamic load (kN)
- $p = 3$ for ball bearings, $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings.

For **life in hours**:

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60\,n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

### Equivalent Dynamic Load

$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a
$$

Where $X, Y$ are radial and thrust factors taken from the bearing catalogue.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Applicable to |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bearing pressure | $p$ | $W / (L \cdot d)$ | MPa | Plain bearing |
| 2 | Rubbing velocity | $v$ | $\pi d n / 60{,}000$ | m/s | Plain bearing |
| 3 | PV factor | $PV$ | $p \cdot v$ | MPa·m/s | Plain bearing selection |
| 4 | Heat dissipated | $Q$ | $\Delta T \cdot A \cdot h$ | W | Plain bearing thermal |
| 5 | Reynolds number | $Re$ | $\rho v d / \mu$ | – | Hydrodynamic |
| 6 | Sommerfeld number | $S$ | $(\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ | – | Plain bearing |
| 7 | Dynamic life (rev.) | $L_{10}$ | $(C/P)^p$ | $10^6$ rev | Rolling bearing |
| 8 | Dynamic life (hrs) | $L_{10h}$ | $10^6 (C/P)^p / (60n)$ | hours | Rolling bearing |
| 9 | Equivalent load | $P$ | $X F_r + Y F_a$ | kN | Rolling bearing |
| 10 | Static safety check | $C_0 / P_0$ | $\geq 1$ (or as per use) | – | Rolling bearing |
| 11 | Speed factor | $n d_m$ | – | mm/s | Catalogue limit |
| 12 | Radial clearance | $c_r$ | from catalogue | µm | Mounting |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** Always write the *units* beside every numerical substitution. Examiners allocate **½ mark** per step specifically for units.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Bearing chosen | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive wheel hub** | Tapered roller / deep-groove ball | Combined load, high reliability |
| **Turbocharger shaft** | Angular contact ball, oil-mist | Very high speed, moderate load |
| **Crankcase main journal** | Plain (hydrodynamic) | Heavy load, low rpm, oil available |
| **Concrete mixer gearbox** | Spherical roller | Shock + misalignment |
| **Bicycle hub** | Deep-groove ball | Light, sealed, low cost |
| **Pinion shaft of gearbox** | Cylindrical roller | Heavy radial load, separable |

---

## 2.6 Detailed Comparison: Sliding vs. Rolling Bearings

| S.No | Feature | Plain (Sliding) Bearing | Rolling Bearing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Starting friction | High | Very low |
| 2 | Running friction | Moderate (μ ≈ 0.001–0.05 with oil) | Very low (μ ≈ 0.001–0.003) |
| 3 | Radial space | Less | More (requires inner + outer race) |
| 4 | Axial space | More (longer length) | Less |
| 5 | Load capacity (per unit area) | Lower (1–25 MPa) | Higher (point/line contact stress) |
| 6 | Speed suitability | Low to medium | Medium to very high |
| 7 | Damping & vibration | High (good damping) | Low (transmits vibration) |
| 8 | Misalignment tolerance | Very high | Limited (except self-aligning) |
| 9 | Shock load | Absorbs well | Poor (brinelling risk) |
| 10 | Noise | Silent | Slight noise |
| 11 | Lubrication | Oil bath / hydrodynamic | Grease / oil-mist |
| 12 | Service life | Long (no fatigue) | Limited by rolling contact fatigue |
| 13 | Cost for low load | Lower | Higher |
| 14 | Cost for high load | Higher | Lower (mass advantage) |
| 15 | Manufacturing | Simple, repairable | Precision-ground, not repairable |
| 16 | Suitability for dirty env. | Acceptable | Not (unless sealed) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write *"rolling bearings are always better because friction is low"*. This is **wrong**. At very high loads with shock, a *plain hydrodynamic bearing* outperforms and outlasts a rolling bearing. Always tie the answer to the **operating condition**, not to a textbook generalisation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rubbing Velocity

Consider a journal bearing of bore diameter $d$ (mm) rotating at $n$ rpm. A point on the inner surface of the journal sweeps out a circle of circumference $\pi d$ per revolution.

$$
\text{Distance per revolution} = \pi d \;\; \text{(mm)}
$$

In one minute, the point sweeps $\pi d n$ mm. To convert to **metres per second**:

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi d n}{1000 \times 60} \;\; \text{(m/s)} = \dfrac{\pi d n}{60{,}000}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* 1 m = 1000 mm; 1 minute = 60 s — hence denominator is $1000 \times 60 = 60{,}000$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Bearing Pressure

Consider a plain journal bearing of projected area $A = L \times d$. The radial load $W$ is balanced by the oil-film pressure acting on this projected area.

$$
p = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot d} \;\; (\text{N/mm}^2 = \text{MPa})
$$

> *Logic:* The "projected area" $L \cdot d$ is used (not curved surface area) because the load acts **radially inward**, perpendicular to the projected plane of the journal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the PV Factor and Its Selection Use

Substituting:

$$
p \cdot v = \left(\dfrac{W}{L \cdot d}\right)\left(\dfrac{\pi d n}{60{,}000}\right) = \dfrac{\pi W n}{60{,}000 \, L}
$$

Notice — **the diameter cancels out!** The PV factor depends only on load, speed, and bearing length.

> *Engineering Insight:* This is why we can design plain bearings by selecting a $PV$ envelope from a material chart (e.g., Babbitt, Bronze, PTFE) without first fixing the diameter.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Selecting Bearing Type

> **Problem:** A shaft carries a radial load of **8 kN** at **1500 rpm**, operating temperature 80 °C, mild dust, expected life **20,000 hours**, occasional shock of magnitude 1.5×.

#### Step 1 — Compute effective load including shock
$$
F_r = 1.5 \times 8 = 12 \;\text{kN}
$$

#### Step 2 — Estimate PV if a plain bearing were used
Assume $d = 50$ mm, $L = 60$ mm:

$$
p = \dfrac{12{,}000}{60 \times 50} = 4 \;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \times 50 \times 1500}{60{,}000} = 3.93 \;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
PV = 4 \times 3.93 = 15.7 \;\text{MPa·m/s}
$$

> *Logic:* The McKee limit at 3.93 m/s is roughly **3 MPa·m/s**. Since $15.7 > 3$, a plain Babbitt bearing **fails**. We discard plain bearing.

#### Step 3 — Switch to rolling bearing
Choose a deep-groove ball bearing of 50 mm bore → series 6310.

- From catalogue: $C = 61$ kN, $C_0 = 38$ kN.
- Pure radial: $P = F_r = 12$ kN.

#### Step 4 — Check life

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{61}{12}\right)^3 = 131 \;\text{million rev.}
$$

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{131 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500} = 1455 \;\text{hours}
$$

> *Logic:* 1455 h < 20,000 h ⇒ bearing is **undersized**.

#### Step 5 — Re-select a heavier series: 6410 (same 50 mm bore, heavier series)
- Catalogue: $C = 90.5$ kN.

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{(90.5/12)^3 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500} = 4718 \;\text{hours}
$$

> Still short. → Go to **roller bearing** for higher load capacity at same bore.

#### Step 6 — Try cylindrical roller NU 410
- Catalogue: $C = 110$ kN, $p = 10/3$.

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{(110/12)^{10/3} \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500} \approx 23{,}800 \;\text{hours}
$$

> *Logic:* > 20,000 h ✓ ⇒ **NU 410 cylindrical roller bearing** is selected.

#### Step 7 — Static safety check

$$
\dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{98}{12} \approx 8.2 \;\;(>1\;\checkmark)
$$

#### Step 8 — Justify the selection
> *"Cylindrical roller bearing NU 410 of bore 50 mm is selected because: (i) the radial load is heavy and shock-prone, requiring high radial capacity of a roller; (ii) the speed (1500 rpm) is within the bearing's permissible $n d_m$ limit; (iii) the calculated L10h life ≈ 23,800 h exceeds the required 20,000 h; (iv) the static safety factor 8.2 is ample; and (v) plain bearing failed the PV criterion at the expected operating temperature."*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for Bearing Selection

| Operating Condition | Best Bearing Choice | Justification (one-liner) |
|---|---|---|
| Pure radial, light load, very high speed | Deep-groove ball | Lowest friction, high speed limit |
| Pure radial, heavy load, moderate speed | Cylindrical roller | Line contact → higher capacity |
| Pure thrust (axial), moderate load | Thrust ball | Designed for axial load |
| Pure thrust, very heavy load | Tapered roller / spherical thrust | High axial capacity |
| Combined radial + axial | Angular contact / tapered roller | Takes both loads |
| Misalignment + radial load | Self-aligning ball / spherical roller | Accommodates angular error |
| Heavy shock load | Spherical roller with sleeve | Absorbs shock + misalignment |
| Very low speed, dirty environment | Plain journal (babbitt/bronze) | Forgiving, repairable |
| High precision, low noise | Preloaded angular contact | Stiff, accurate location |
| Very high speed, low load | Hybrid ceramic ball | Centrifugal-resistant |
| Limited radial space, oscillating motion | Needle roller | Small cross-section |
| High temperature (> 250 °C) | Plain (Ni-Cr bronze) or ceramic hybrid | Temperature tolerance |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Draw this matrix (or a simplified 6-row version) on your answer sheet before writing the selection justification. Examiners award marks for the **decision logic**, not just the final choice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bearing Selection Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Operating Conditions Given] --> B[Identify Type of Load]
    B --> B1[Pure Radial]
    B --> B2[Pure Axial Thrust]
    B --> B3[Combined Radial and Axial]

    B1 --> C[Estimate Magnitude and Speed]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C

    C --> D{Speed less than 1000 rpm and dusty environment?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Consider Plain Hydrodynamic Bearing]
    D -- No --> F[Consider Rolling Element Bearing]

    E --> E1[Compute PV factor and compare with material chart]
    E1 --> E2{PV less than material limit?}
    E2 -- Yes --> E3[Select Plain Bearing and Design for Length and Diameter]
    E2 -- No --> F

    F --> F1{Load type}
    F1 -- Pure Radial --> F2{Load magnitude}
    F2 -- Light to Moderate --> F3[Deep Groove Ball Bearing]
    F2 -- Heavy --> F4[Cylindrical or Needle Roller]

    F1 -- Combined Load --> F5[Angular Contact Ball or Tapered Roller]
    F1 -- Pure Thrust --> F6[Thrust Ball or Thrust Roller]
    F1 -- Misalignment Expected --> F7[Self Aligning Ball or Spherical Roller]

    F3 --> G[Select Catalogue Series by Bore and Compute L10h Life]
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G

    G --> H{L10h Life greater than Required Life?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Check Static Load Factor C0 by P0 greater than 1]
    H -- No --> J[Go to Heavier Series or Roller Type and Recompute]

    I --> K[Final Selection and Justification]
    J --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Typical Bearing Selection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Module]
        I1[Load Magnitude W]
        I2[RPM and Direction]
        I3[Service Life Hours]
        I4[Environment Temp and Dust]
        I5[Alignment and Rigidity]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Decision Processor]
        P1[Load Classifier]
        P2[Speed Classifier]
        P3[Life Calculator]
        P4[PV Calculator]
        P5[Catalogue Matcher]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Module]
        O1[Bearing Type]
        O2[Bearing Series and Bore]
        O3[Lubrication Recommendation]
        O4[Sealing Recommendation]
        O5[Mounting Notes]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    I4 --> P4
    I5 --> P1
    I1 --> P4

    P1 --> P5
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5

    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    O2 --> O4
    O2 --> O5
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Plain vs. Rolling Bearing Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Rotational Speed above 1000 rpm?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Environment Dirty and Lubrication Unreliable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Default to Rolling Bearing]
    Q2 -- Yes --> PB[Plain Bearing - Sleeve Type with Bronze or Babbitt Lining]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Radial Load and Shock Level High?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PB
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Noise and Damping Important?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> PB
    Q4 -- No --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top, answer each yes/no question, and the flowchart guides you to the appropriate bearing class. This is precisely the structure expected in a KTU 14-mark answer.
