<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List any three factors that govern the selection of bearing type, with one-line justification each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Type and magnitude of load (1 mark):** Pure radial, pure thrust, or combined load directs us toward cylindrical roller, thrust bearing, or angular contact bearing respectively. Heavy shock loads rule out delicate ball bearings.

2. **Speed of operation (1 mark):** Low speeds (< 1000 rpm) with available lubrication suit plain hydrodynamic bearings. Higher speeds demand rolling bearings because plain bearings overheat beyond the McKee PV limit.

3. **Service life and alignment tolerance (1 mark):** Long life with misaligned shafts requires self-aligning spherical roller; precise alignment with high rigidity needs preloaded angular-contact ball bearings.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define "PV factor" of a plain bearing. What is its significance in bearing selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The product of bearing pressure $p$ (MPa) and rubbing velocity $v$ (m/s) is called the **PV factor**, expressed in **MPa·m/s**. It represents the **heat generated per unit projected area** of the bearing.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- It is the **primary selection criterion for plain bearings**, compared against the material's PV limit (McKee curve).
- A higher PV than the material limit implies **overheating and seizure**.
- The PV envelope decreases as velocity rises, restricting plain bearings to **low-speed applications**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> *Examination Note:* KTU 2024 Scheme permits an **internal choice** between two Part-B questions within the same module. Below are two alternative questions, each carrying sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Set A]`

**Q: A counter-shaft of a gearbox carries a radial load of 12 kN at 1450 rpm. The service life required is 25,000 hours, with occasional shock of 1.5× and operating temperature of 70 °C. Select a suitable bearing type, justifying every step.**

#### Part (a) [7 marks] — Decision and Type Selection

**Step 1: Identify load direction** (0.5 mark)
- Pure radial load → candidates: deep-groove ball, cylindrical roller, spherical roller.

**Step 2: Check suitability of plain bearing** (2 marks)

Assume $d = 55$ mm, $L = 70$ mm:

$$
p = \dfrac{12{,}000 \times 1.5}{70 \times 55} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{3850} = 4.68 \;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \times 55 \times 1450}{60{,}000} = 4.18 \;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
PV = 4.68 \times 4.18 = 19.56 \;\text{MPa·m/s}
$$

McKee limit at 4.18 m/s ≈ **2.5 MPa·m/s**. Since 19.56 ≫ 2.5, plain bearing is **rejected**.

**Step 3: Choose rolling bearing type** (2 marks)
- Heavy shock + radial load → **roller bearing preferred over ball bearing** because of line contact and higher load capacity.
- Moderate misalignment possible → **spherical roller bearing** chosen over cylindrical roller.

**Step 4: State the series** (1 mark)
- Bore 55 mm, series 22 (medium) → bearing designation **22211** spherical roller (or 22311 for heavy series).

**Step 5: Static check** (1 mark)
- Catalogue $C_0 \approx 90$ kN; $P_0 = 1.5 \times 12 = 18$ kN.

$$
\dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{90}{18} = 5 \;\;(>1\;\checkmark)
$$

**Answer to (a):** *A spherical roller bearing of series 22211 (bore 55 mm) is selected.*

#### Part (b) [7 marks] — Life Verification and Justification

**Step 1: Catalogue data** (1 mark)
- Dynamic load rating $C = 100$ kN (assumed for 22211).

**Step 2: Compute L10 in million revolutions** (2 marks)
- For spherical roller, $p = 10/3$:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{100}{1.5 \times 12}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\dfrac{100}{18}\right)^{10/3} = 5.56^{3.333}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
\ln L_{10} = 3.333 \times \ln 5.56 = 3.333 \times 1.716 = 5.72
$$

$$
L_{10} \approx 304 \;\text{million rev.}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to hours** (2 marks)

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{304 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1450} = 3494 \;\text{hours}
$$

> **Insight (1 mark):** 3494 h < 25,000 h — *bearing is undersized.*

**Step 4: Re-select heavier series — 22311 spherical roller** (1 mark)
- Catalogue $C = 150$ kN:

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{(150/18)^{10/3} \times 10^6}{60 \times 1450} \approx 11{,}700 \;\text{h}
$$

Still short. → **Try 22312 with bore 60 mm and $C = 180$ kN:**

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{(180/18)^{10/3} \times 10^6}{60 \times 1450} \approx 20{,}000 \;\text{h}
$$

> **Step 5 Final Justification (1 mark):** *22312 spherical roller bearing gives ≈ 20,000 h, marginally short of 25,000 h. We therefore choose 22313 (bore 65 mm, $C = 210$ kN), giving L10h ≈ 30,000 h which exceeds the requirement. The selection is justified by (i) roller type for shock, (ii) spherical type for misalignment, (iii) life criterion satisfied, and (iv) static safety factor ≈ 6.7, well above the minimum.*

**Answer to (b):** *Spherical roller bearing 22313 (bore 65 mm) selected; L10h ≈ 30,000 hours > 25,000 hours requirement.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Set B]`

**Q: Compare sliding contact bearings and rolling contact bearings. Under what operating conditions would you prefer a sliding (journal) bearing over a rolling bearing? Justify with a numerical example using the PV concept.**

#### Part (a) [7 marks] — Detailed Comparison

**(Each correct contrasting point = 1 mark; choose any 7 for full marks)**

| S.No | Aspect | Sliding Bearing | Rolling Bearing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction | Higher (μ ≈ 0.001–0.05 hydrodynamic) | Very low (μ ≈ 0.001) |
| 2 | Starting torque | High | Negligible |
| 3 | Radial space | Compact | Requires inner + outer ring |
| 4 | Load capacity | Lower per unit area | High (point/line contact) |
| 5 | Speed | Low–medium | Medium–very high |
| 6 | Misalignment | Very tolerant | Limited (except self-aligning) |
| 7 | Shock absorption | Good | Poor (brinelling) |
| 8 | Noise | Silent | Slight noise |
| 9 | Lubrication | Oil bath / hydrodynamic | Grease / oil-mist |
| 10 | Maintenance | Refill oil periodically | Sealed-for-life (grease) |
| 11 | Repair | Machinable, reusable | Not repairable |
| 12 | Cost at low load | Lower | Higher |
| 13 | Cost at high load | Higher (large size) | Lower |
| 14 | Life mechanism | Wear (no fatigue limit) | Rolling contact fatigue |
| 15 | Damping | High | Low |
| 16 | Suitability for dusty | Tolerable | Requires sealing |

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks] — Numerical Example Using PV

**Step 1: Choose operating conditions** (1 mark)
- $W = 5$ kN, $n = 600$ rpm, $d = 60$ mm, $L = 80$ mm.

**Step 2: Compute pressure** (1 mark)

$$
p = \dfrac{5000}{60 \times 80} = 1.04 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Compute rubbing velocity** (1 mark)

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \times 60 \times 600}{60{,}000} = 1.885 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute PV** (1 mark)

$$
PV = 1.04 \times 1.885 = 1.96 \;\text{MPa·m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Check against McKee curve** (2 marks)
- At $v = 1.885$ m/s, McKee's PV limit for Babbitt ≈ **1.75 MPa·m/s**.
- Computed PV = 1.96 MPa·m/s **slightly exceeds** Babbitt → select **phosphor bronze** (limit ≈ 3 MPa·m/s).

**Step 6: State the conclusion** (1 mark)

> *A phosphor-bronze plain journal bearing of 60 mm bore, 80 mm length is selected because (i) the low speed (600 rpm) is within hydrodynamic regime, (ii) PV = 1.96 MPa·m/s is safe for bronze, and (iii) a rolling bearing of equivalent capacity would be more expensive and would not provide the damping required in this low-speed application.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **No justification for the type choice** — selecting a bearing *without* explaining why a roller is preferred over a ball, or why plain is preferred over rolling. (Loses 3–4 marks.)
> 2. **Wrong exponent $p$** — using $p = 3$ for a roller bearing; correct is $p = 10/3$. (Loses 2 marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting shock factor** — plugging $F_r$ directly without applying a service factor of 1.5–2.0. (Loses 2 marks.)
> 4. **Mixing units** — using N without converting to kN in the life equation. (Loses 2 marks.)
> 5. **No static load check** — examiner awards 1 mark specifically for the $C_0 / P_0$ ratio; omitting it costs that mark.
> 6. **Writing only the final bearing number** without showing the calculation chain — partial marking cannot be awarded.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Two broad families:** sliding (plain) and rolling (ball, roller, needle).
- **Plain bearing selection** is governed by **PV factor** (McKee curve), rubbing velocity, viscosity, and Sommerfeld number.
- **Rolling bearing selection** is governed by **type** (radial / thrust / angular), **load rating $C$**, **equivalent load $P$**, **life exponent $p$**, and **speed factor $n d_m$**.
- **Ball bearing** → low friction, high speed, light–moderate load, $p = 3$.
- **Roller bearing** → higher load, shock, lower speed, $p = 10/3$.
- **Tapered roller / angular contact** → combined radial + axial loads.
- **Spherical roller / self-aligning ball** → misaligned shafts, heavy loads.
- **Thrust bearing** → axial load only; can be ball or roller type.
- **Life equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ million rev → $L_{10h} = 10^6 (C/P)^p / (60n)$.
- **Static safety check:** $C_0 / P_0 \geq 1$ (preferably 1.5–2 for steady, 2–3 for shock).
- **Service factor** must be applied **before** computing $P$.
- **Plain bearing is preferred** when:
  - Speed is very low (< 500 rpm)
  - Loads are very heavy
  - Misalignment is unavoidable
  - Damping of vibration is critical
  - Repair and re-babbitting are feasible
  - Lubrication oil is freely available
- **Rolling bearing is preferred** when:
  - Speed is medium to high
  - Friction losses must be minimised
  - Starting torque must be very low
  - Sealed-for-life maintenance is desired
  - Standardised interchangeability is required
- **Bearing series by bore** (50 mm example): **00 (extra-light 6 100), 01 (extra-light 6 200), 02 (light 6 300), 03 (medium 6 400), 04 (heavy 6 500)** — increasing radial capacity.
- **PV limit table (quick recall):**
  - Babbitt: 1.0 – 1.75 MPa·m/s
  - Bronze: 2.5 – 4 MPa·m/s
  - PTFE: 0.4 – 0.6 MPa·m/s
  - Rubber: 0.2 – 0.4 MPa·m/s
- **Always justify** your selection with: **(i) load direction + magnitude, (ii) speed, (iii) life, (iv) environment, (v) cost, (vi) static safety.**

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (PCRUT502):** Most KTU questions on Module 4 combine *plain bearing PV analysis* with *rolling bearing life calculation* in a single 14-mark problem. Practice both on the same paper — they often appear together.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bearing Life** is defined as the total number of revolutions (or the number of operating hours at a given constant speed) that a rolling contact bearing can endure before the first evidence of fatigue failure (spalling, pitting, or flaking) develops on the inner race, outer race, or rolling elements.

In KTU Board Examination language, we deal with two distinct, formal definitions:

- **Basic Rating Life (L10)**: The number of revolutions (or hours at constant speed) that **90% of a group of apparently identical bearings** will complete or exceed before the first evidence of fatigue develops. This is the most commonly used design life.

- **Modified Rating Life (Lna)**: The bearing life achieved under specific, non-ideal operating conditions (reliability > 90%, special bearing material, or non-standard lubrication). It accounts for **application factor (a1), material factor (a2), and operating conditions factor (a3)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *"The rating life of a bearing is the number of revolutions at a constant speed which 90% of a group of apparently identical bearings, operating under identical conditions, will complete or exceed before the first evidence of fatigue failure develops."* — ISO 281 / KTU Approved Standard

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you buy a packet of **10 brand-new LED bulbs**. The manufacturer guarantees that **9 out of 10 bulbs (90%)** will last at least **25,000 hours** (L10). That guarantee number is the *basic rating life*. The 10th bulb *might* fail earlier or last much longer — that's the statistical reality of fatigue.

Now apply the same logic to a bearing:
- A bearing is a **fatigue-limited component** — it does NOT wear out gradually. It operates in a complex Hertzian contact stress cycle, and after millions of tiny cyclic stress reversals, a sub-surface fatigue crack nucleates and propagates, finally causing a piece of raceway material to flake off (this is called **spalling**).
- The manufacturer runs huge fleets of test bearings at known loads and counts revolutions until spalling appears. From that data, they tabulate the **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** — the load at which L10 = 1 million revolutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary**: L10 life is like a "warranty" — 90% confidence. It is NOT the average life, and it is NOT the minimum life. It is the life below which only 10% of bearings will fail.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Units

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic Dynamic Load Rating | **C** | **N (Newtons)** | Constant radial load that a bearing can carry for 1 million revolutions with 90% reliability |
| Basic Static Load Rating | **C₀** | **N (Newtons)** | Load below which permanent deformation < 0.0001 of ball diameter |
| Dynamic Equivalent Load | **P** | **N (Newtons)** | Hypothetical constant load that produces same fatigue as actual combined load |
| Rating Life (revolutions) | **L10** | millions of revolutions | Fatigue life in millions of revs |
| Rating Life (hours) | **L10h** | **hours** | Fatigue life in operating hours |
| Exponent (ball) | **p** | **3** | Hertzian contact geometry exponent for ball bearings |
| Exponent (roller) | **p** | **10/3 ≈ 3.33** | Hertzian contact geometry exponent for roller bearings |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics: Why a Cubic Relationship?

The load-life relationship is **not empirical guesswork** — it is rooted in the **Hertzian contact stress theory** combined with **Weibull's weakest-link fatigue statistics**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Hertzian Contact Stress** between a ball and a raceway creates a non-uniform elliptical pressure distribution. The maximum contact pressure scales with the **cube root of the normal load** (for line contact in roller bearings, the exponent is different).

2. **Fatigue crack nucleation** in the sub-surface shear zone (typically 0.5a below the surface, where `a` is the contact half-width) is driven by **shear stress amplitude**. The number of cycles to crack initiation follows an **inverse power law** with stress amplitude.

3. **Combining** the contact mechanics (load ∝ stress³) with the fatigue law (life ∝ stress⁻⁹ for steels), we get:

$$L_{10} \propto \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$$

where the exponent **p = 3 for ball bearings** and **p = 10/3 for roller bearings**. The 10/3 exponent arises because line contact in rollers gives a different stress distribution than point contact in balls.

4. **The 90% reliability figure** comes from Weibull's statistical analysis. A higher required reliability (e.g., 99% L5 life, or 95% L20 life) gives a *shorter* expected life, while 90% L10 is the industry standard baseline.

## 2.2 The Core Load-Life Equation (Foundational Equation)

The single most important equation in bearing design:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[millions of revolutions]}$$

where:
- **L10** = Basic rating life in millions of revolutions
- **C** = Basic dynamic load rating (N) — from manufacturer catalogue
- **P** = Dynamic equivalent load (N) — calculated from applied loads
- **p** = Exponent = 3 (ball) or 10/3 (roller)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cube?** A doubling of load reduces life by a factor of 8 (for ball bearings). A tripling reduces life by 27×. This is **extremely sensitive** — students who treat it linearly in KTU Board Exams will lose all 14 marks.

## 2.3 Conversion to Operating Hours

Machines don't operate in millions of revolutions; they run in **hours**. Convert:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

where **n** = rotational speed in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**.

**Derivation of the 10⁶/60·n factor:**

$$L_{10h} = L_{10} \times \frac{10^{6} \text{ rev}}{1 \text{ (unit)}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{n \text{ rev}} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10}$$

## 2.4 Dynamic Equivalent Radial Load (P)

Real bearings carry a combination of radial (Fr) and axial/thrust (Fa) loads. We collapse them into a single equivalent radial load:

$$P = X \cdot F_{r} + Y \cdot F_{a} \quad \text{[N]}$$

where:
- **Fr** = Actual radial load on bearing (N)
- **Fa** = Actual axial/thrust load on bearing (N)
- **X** = Radial load factor (from catalogue, depends on Fa/C₀ ratio)
- **Y** = Axial load factor (from catalogue, depends on Fa/C₀ ratio)

For **pure radial load** (Fa = 0): **P = Fr**
For **pure thrust load** (Fr = 0): **P = Fa** (for thrust bearings)

## 2.5 Modified Rating Life (Advanced — KTU Frequently Asked)

When the application demands higher reliability (> 90%) or special operating conditions, the **basic** L10 is corrected:

$$L_{na} = a_{1} \cdot a_{2} \cdot a_{3} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[millions of revolutions]}$$

| Factor | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability factor | **a1** | Accounts for required reliability > 90% | a1 < 1 (e.g., a1 = 0.62 for 99% reliability) |
| Material factor | **a2** | Accounts for special bearing steel, cleanliness, manufacturing | a2 ≥ 1 (typical a2 = 1.0 to 3.0) |
| Operating conditions factor | **a3** | Accounts for lubrication, temperature | a3 ≤ 1 (e.g., a3 = 0.8 for poor lubrication) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Forgetting that **a1 REDUCES** life (counter-intuitive — higher reliability requirement gives a shorter guaranteed life). The **a2 and a3** are different: a2 is the quality bonus, a3 is the operating penalty.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | Basic rating life | Millions of revs |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10}$ | Life in hours | Hours |
| 3 | $P = X F_{r} + Y F_{a}$ | Dynamic equivalent radial load | N |
| 4 | $L_{na} = a_{1} a_{2} a_{3} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | Modified rating life | Millions of revs |
| 5 | $P = \frac{C}{L_{10}^{1/p}}$ | Required dynamic load rating | N |
| 6 | $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/p}$ | Catalogue selection check | N |
| 7 | $p = 3$ | Exponent for ball bearings | — |
| 8 | $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$ | Exponent for roller bearings | — |
| 9 | $f_{w}$ | Application/Service factor (1.0 to 3.0) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $P = f_{w} \cdot P_{calc}$ | Adjusted load with application factor | N |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bearing life calculations are **mission-critical** in:
- **Aerospace**: Turbine engine mainshaft bearings must achieve L10h ≥ 30,000 hours (often with a1 = 0.62 for 99% reliability).
- **Wind turbines**: Gearbox bearing L10h ≥ 175,000 hours (≈ 20 years of operation).
- **Automotive**: Wheel bearing L10h ≈ 1,000–3,000 hours (vehicles rarely exceed this before tire/vehicle replacement).
- **Electric motors**: Standard L10h = 25,000–40,000 hours for general industrial duty.
- **Paper mills, rolling mills**: Heavy shock loads → high application factor **fw = 1.5 to 3.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **inverse problem** (selecting a bearing from a catalogue) is the most common KTU Board Exam question type: Given a desired L10h, speed, and load, calculate the **required C**, then look up the bearing whose catalogue C ≥ required C.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Rating Life Equation (L10)

The relationship is derived by combining the Hertzian contact stress formula and the S-N fatigue curve for bearing steel. We present the classical Lundberg-Palmgren derivation in linearized form:

**Step 1**: Hertzian maximum contact pressure for a ball-on-raceway contact:

$$p_{max} \propto \left(\frac{Q}{d^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

where `Q` is the normal load on one ball and `d` is the ball diameter. This shows the **cube root** scaling.

**Step 2**: The maximum alternating shear stress (at depth ~0.5a below the surface) drives crack nucleation:

$$\tau_{0} \propto p_{max} \propto Q^{1/3}$$

**Step 3**: The Weibull weakest-link fatigue model states the probability of survival `S`:

$$\ln \frac{1}{S} \propto \tau_{0}^{c} \cdot N \cdot V$$

where `c ≈ 9` for bearing steel, `N` is the number of stress cycles, and `V` is the stressed volume.

**Step 4**: Substituting the shear stress scaling:

$$\ln \frac{1}{S} \propto Q^{c/3} \cdot N$$

**Step 5**: For `c = 9` (ball bearings) and `c = 8` (roller bearings):

$$\text{Ball: } \quad N \propto Q^{-3}, \qquad \text{Roller: } \quad N \propto Q^{-10/3}$$

**Step 6**: For a bearing carrying load `P` with `i` rolling elements sharing the load equally, `Q = P / i`. The exponent remains the same. Normalizing to L10 = 1 at P = C:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$$

where `p = 3` for balls and `p = 10/3` for rollers. **Derivation complete.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Life in Operating Hours

Starting from L10 in millions of revolutions:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[in millions of revs]}$$

Multiply by 10⁶ to get actual revolutions:

$$N_{rev} = 10^{6} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[revolutions]}$$

Convert revs to hours using speed `n` (rev/min):

$$L_{10h} = \frac{N_{rev}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

**Therefore the complete operating-life equation is:**

$$\boxed{L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{[hours]}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Board Style)

**Problem**: A deep-groove ball bearing (p = 3) has a dynamic load rating **C = 35 kN**. It is subjected to a dynamic equivalent radial load **P = 7 kN** at a speed of **n = 1440 rpm**. Determine the L10 life in millions of revolutions and in hours.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute the load ratio:

$$\frac{C}{P} = \frac{35}{7} = 5$$

**Step 2**: Apply the basic rating life equation (p = 3 for ball bearing):

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = 5^{3} = 125 \quad \text{[millions of revolutions]}$$

**Step 3**: Convert to hours:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1440} \times 125$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{86400} \times 125 = 11.574 \times 125$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h} = 1446.76 \text{ hours}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Inverse Problem — Bearing Selection)

**Problem**: An industrial gearbox shaft requires a Cylindrical Roller Bearing (p = 10/3) to support a dynamic equivalent radial load **P = 25 kN** at **n = 1200 rpm**. The desired L10h is **10,000 hours**. Determine the required dynamic load rating `C`.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Rearrange the L10h equation to solve for C:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$$

$$\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^{6}}$$

$$\frac{C}{P} = \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Step 2**: Plug in values with p = 10/3:

$$\frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^{6}} = \frac{10000 \times 60 \times 1200}{10^{6}} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{8}}{10^{6}} = 720$$

**Step 3**: Take the 3/10 root (1/p = 3/10 = 0.3):

$$\frac{C}{P} = (720)^{0.3}$$

Taking log: $\log(720) = 2.857$, so $0.3 \times 2.857 = 0.857$.

$$10^{0.857} = 7.197$$

**Step 4**: Solve for C:

$$C = P \times 7.197 = 25000 \times 7.197$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 179.93 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catalogue Step (KTU Board Exam Tip)**: Once required C is calculated, the student must select the next-higher catalogue C value. The bearing whose **C_catalogue ≥ 179.93 kN** is selected. Always round **UP** for safety.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 (Modified Rating Life)

**Problem**: A bearing has L10 = 5000 hours. The application demands 96% reliability, uses premium bearing steel, and operates under marginal lubrication. Find the modified rating life Lna in hours.

**Given**:
- Reliability 96% → a1 = 0.85 (from table)
- Premium steel (a2 = 1.5)
- Marginal lubrication → a3 = 0.7

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute the combined correction factor:

$$a_{1} \cdot a_{2} \cdot a_{3} = 0.85 \times 1.5 \times 0.7 = 0.8925$$

**Step 2**: Apply the modified life equation:

$$L_{na} = 0.8925 \times 5000$$

$$\boxed{L_{na} = 4462.5 \text{ hours}}$$

Notice that even though the steel is **better** (a2 > 1), the **poor lubrication and higher reliability** outweigh it, giving a shorter Lna than L10.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Given Quantity | Find | Use This Formula |
|---|---|---|
| C, P, n | L10h | $L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60n}\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ |
| L10h, P, n | C (required) | $C = P \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60n}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}$ |
| C, L10h, n | P (allowable) | $P = C \left(\frac{10^{6}}{L_{10h} \cdot 60n}\right)^{1/p}$ |
| C, P, n, reliability | Lna | $L_{na} = a_{1}a_{2}a_{3}\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ |
| Fr, Fa, X, Y | P | $P = XF_{r} + YF_{a}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bearing Life Calculation Flow (Mermaid Block)

The complete logical flow for solving a KTU Board Exam problem on bearing life:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Problem Statement]) --> nodeB[Identify Bearing Type: Ball or Roller]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Ball = 3, Roller = 10/3}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Extract Catalogue Data: C, C0]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Identify Applied Loads: Fr, Fa]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Look up X, Y from Catalogue using Fa/C0]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Dynamic Equivalent Load: P = X*Fr + Y*Fa]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Required Reliability > 90 percent?}
    nodeH -->|No| nodeI[Compute L10 in revs = C/P power p]
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeJ[Look up a1 from Reliability Table]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Look up a2, a3 for material and lubrication]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compute Lna = a1*a2*a3 * C/P power p]
    nodeI --> nodeM[Convert to Hours: L10h = 10 power 6 / 60n * L10]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Convert Lna to Hours similarly]
    nodeM --> nodeO{Is L10h >= Target Life?}
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO -->|Yes| nodeP([END: Bearing is Safe])
    nodeO -->|No| nodeQ[Repeat with Higher Capacity Bearing]
    nodeQ --> nodeD
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Factors Affecting Bearing Life

This block diagram shows how various engineering and material factors combine to influence the final life of a rolling bearing:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input: Applied Load Fr, Fa] --> nodeB[Dynamic Equivalent Load P]
    nodeC[Input: Rotational Speed n] --> nodeB
    nodeD[Input: Catalogue Data C, C0] --> nodeE[Required C calculation]
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Selected Bearing Catalogue Match]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Basic Rating Life L10]
    nodeH[Reliability Factor a1] --> nodeI[Modified Rating Life Lna]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeJ[Material Factor a2] --> nodeI
    nodeK[Operating Factor a3] --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeL[Final Output: L10h in hours]
    nodeM[Lubrication Quality] --> nodeK
    nodeN[Operating Temperature] --> nodeK
    nodeO[Contamination Level] --> nodeK
    nodeP[Shock Loading fw] --> nodeB
```

## 4.3 Visualizing the L10 vs Load Relationship

For a **ball bearing** (p = 3), the rating life is a **cubic inverse** of the load ratio:

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Bearing Life vs Load Ratio C over P (Ball Bearing, p = 3)"
    x-axis "Load Ratio C / P" [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10]
    y-axis "L10 in Millions of Revolutions" 0 1300
    line [1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, 512, 1000]
```

**Interpretation**:
- At C/P = 1 (load equals capacity): L10 = 1 million revs.
- At C/P = 5: L10 = 125 million revs.
- At C/P = 10: L10 = 1000 million revs (1 billion revs).

A **2× increase in C/P ratio** yields an **8× increase in life** — this is why we **over-spec bearings** in KTU problems for industrial safety.

## 4.4 Failure Mode vs Bearing Life Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Bearing Operational Timeline] --> nodeB[Run-in Period: 0 to 20 hours]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Useful Life Region: 1000s of hours]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Fatigue Failure Region]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Initial wear, surface adjustment]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Stable L10 life region]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Spalling on raceway]
    nodeG --> nodeH[End of Useful Life]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Spalling initiates on inner race 50 percent]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Spalling on outer race 30 percent]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Spalling on rolling element 20 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define the term 'rating life' of a rolling contact bearing. What is the statistical basis of the 90% reliability figure used in bearing life calculations?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **rating life** of a rolling contact bearing is defined as the total number of revolutions (or hours at a constant speed) that 90% of a group of apparently identical bearings, operating under identical conditions, will complete or exceed before the first evidence of fatigue failure (spalling/pitting) develops on the raceway or rolling element. **(2 marks)**

The **90% reliability** figure arises from **Weibull's weakest-link statistical model**, which is appropriate for fatigue processes that initiate at microscopic defects. ISO 281 standardizes this 90% (L10) reliability as the baseline for bearing life, because higher reliability (e.g., 99% L5) would yield unrealistically conservative (and uneconomical) bearing sizes, while lower reliability (e.g., 50% L50) would risk premature failures. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Why is the exponent 'p' different for ball bearings (p = 3) and roller bearings (p = 10/3)? Explain physically."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The exponent 'p' arises from the **Hertzian contact stress distribution** combined with the **fatigue strength** of bearing steel. **(1 mark)**

- In **ball bearings**, the contact between the ball and the raceway is **point contact** (elliptical). The maximum Hertzian contact pressure scales as the **cube root of the load** (σ ∝ Q^(1/3)). Combining this with the Lundberg-Palmgren fatigue relation (life ∝ stress⁻⁹) gives **p = 3**. **(1 mark)**

- In **roller bearings**, the contact is **line contact** (rectangular). The maximum contact pressure scales as the **square root of the load** (σ ∝ Q^(1/2)). Combined with the same fatigue law, this gives **p = 10/3** (≈ 3.33). **(1 mark)**

**Physical interpretation**: Roller bearings, due to line contact, are more sensitive to load changes than ball bearings. A 2× load increase causes an 8× (ball) vs 10× (roller) reduction in life.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **MOST COMMON MARK LOSS**: Forgetting to convert `L10` (in millions of revs) to `L10h` (in hours) using the 10⁶/60n factor. KTU Examiners **specifically deduct 2 marks** for this.
> - **Second most common**: Using p = 3 for a roller bearing (or vice versa). Always **read the problem twice** to confirm the bearing type.
> - **Third pitfall**: Not selecting the **next-higher catalogue C** value — students lose 1 mark by selecting a bearing with C exactly equal to or just below the required value.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A radial ball bearing (p = 3) has a dynamic load rating C = 25 kN. It is subjected to a radial load of 4.5 kN and a thrust load of 2 kN. The bearing rotates at 1500 rpm. The radial factor X = 0.56 and axial factor Y = 1.2 (assume the e value condition is satisfied).**

**(a)** Calculate the dynamic equivalent radial load P on the bearing. **(5 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the L10 life of the bearing in millions of revolutions and in operating hours. **(7 marks)**
**(c)** If the application factor fw = 1.5 is now considered (heavy shock loading), recalculate the L10h. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution A:

**Part (a) — Dynamic Equivalent Radial Load (5 marks):**

Given: Fr = 4.5 kN = 4500 N, Fa = 2 kN = 2000 N, X = 0.56, Y = 1.2

Apply the standard equivalent load formula:

$$P = X \cdot F_{r} + Y \cdot F_{a}$$

$$P = (0.56 \times 4500) + (1.2 \times 2000)$$

$$P = 2520 + 2400$$

$$\boxed{P = 4920 \text{ N} = 4.92 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) — L10 Life Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Compute the load ratio C/P:

$$\frac{C}{P} = \frac{25000}{4920} = 5.081$$

Step 2: Apply the basic rating life equation (p = 3 for ball bearing):

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = (5.081)^{3}$$

$$(5.081)^{3} = 5.081 \times 5.081 \times 5.081 = 131.18$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} = 131.18 \text{ million revolutions}}$$

Step 3: Convert to hours at n = 1500 rpm:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1500} \times 131.18$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{90000} \times 131.18 = 11.111 \times 131.18$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h} = 1457.6 \text{ hours}}$$

**Part (c) — With Application Factor (2 marks):**

When fw = 1.5, the dynamic equivalent load becomes:

$$P_{new} = f_{w} \times P = 1.5 \times 4920 = 7380 \text{ N}$$

New load ratio:

$$\frac{C}{P_{new}} = \frac{25000}{7380} = 3.388$$

New life:

$$L_{10}^{new} = (3.388)^{3} = 38.89 \text{ million revs}$$

New life in hours:

$$L_{10h}^{new} = 11.111 \times 38.89$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h}^{new} = 432.1 \text{ hours}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discussion**: The application factor fw = 1.5 (heavy shock) reduced bearing life from **1457.6 hours to 432.1 hours** — a **70% reduction**. This shows why proper assessment of service conditions is **critical** in KTU Board Exam design problems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A cylindrical roller bearing (p = 10/3) is to be selected for a machine operating at 1000 rpm. The equivalent radial load on the bearing is 20 kN. The required L10 life is 8000 hours. The application experiences light shock loading (fw = 1.2). The required reliability is 95% (a1 = 0.85).**

**(a)** Calculate the **required basic dynamic load rating** C for the bearing. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the catalogue offers bearings with C = 110 kN, 130 kN, and 160 kN, select the appropriate bearing. **(3 marks)**
**(c)** Compute the **actual L10h** of the selected bearing and comment on safety. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution B:

**Part (a) — Required C Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Adjust the load for the application factor:

$$P_{adj} = f_{w} \times P = 1.2 \times 20 = 24 \text{ kN} = 24000 \text{ N}$$

Step 2: Adjust for reliability (since L10 is 90% reliability, we need to convert required life to the 90% baseline):

$$L_{10}^{req} = \frac{L_{target}}{a_{1}} = \frac{8000}{0.85} = 9411.76 \text{ hours}$$

(This step is critical — KTU examiners often test if students know that Lna = a1 × L10.)

Step 3: Use the L10h formula to find required C:

$$L_{10h}^{req} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P_{adj}}\right)^{p}$$

$$9411.76 = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1000} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{24000}\right)^{10/3}$$

$$9411.76 = 16.667 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{24000}\right)^{10/3}$$

Step 4: Isolate the load ratio term:

$$\left(\frac{C}{24000}\right)^{10/3} = \frac{9411.76}{16.667} = 564.71$$

Step 5: Take the 3/10 root:

$$\frac{C}{24000} = (564.71)^{0.3}$$

Taking log: $\log(564.71) = 2.7519$, so $0.3 \times 2.7519 = 0.8256$.

$$10^{0.8256} = 6.691$$

Step 6: Solve for C:

$$C_{req} = 6.691 \times 24000$$

$$\boxed{C_{req} = 160.59 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) — Bearing Selection (3 marks):**

The available catalogue values are 110, 130, and 160 kN. The required C is 160.59 kN. Selecting the next-higher catalogue value:

$$\boxed{\text{Selected bearing: } C_{cat} = 160 \text{ kN} \text{ — but this is just below required!}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert**: 160 kN is **less than** the required 160.59 kN. Strictly, the next-higher value would be a **180 kN or 200 kN** bearing. In real KTU exams, students should note this. For this problem, we select the 160 kN bearing assuming a 0.4% under-design is tolerable (industry practice), OR we specify the next available larger bearing. **Best answer: 160 kN with engineering commentary** OR explicitly state next size up.

**Part (c) — Actual L10h Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Actual load ratio with C = 160 kN:

$$\frac{C}{P_{adj}} = \frac{160000}{24000} = 6.667$$

Step 2: Compute L10:

$$L_{10} = (6.667)^{10/3}$$

$$10/3 = 3.333, \quad \log(6.667) = 0.8239, \quad 3.333 \times 0.8239 = 2.746$$

$$L_{10} = 10^{2.746} = 557.2 \text{ million revs}$$

Step 3: Convert to hours:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60000} \times 557.2 = 16.667 \times 557.2$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h}^{actual} = 9286 \text{ hours}}$$

**Comment on safety**: 
- Required L10h (adjusted for 95% reliability) = 9411.76 hours.
- Achieved L10h (90% basis) = 9286 hours → at 95% reliability, actual life is $a_1 \times 9286 = 0.85 \times 9286 = 7893$ hours, which is **marginally below** the 8000-hour target.
- The 160 kN bearing is **borderline acceptable** but not ideal. In practice, the engineer would **select the next larger catalogue bearing (e.g., 180 kN)** for safety margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — Bearing Life (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Bearing life** is the revolutions/hours until fatigue failure, with **90% reliability (L10)** as the industry standard.
- ✅ The **load-life equation** is: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ where **p = 3 (ball)** or **10/3 (roller)**.
- ✅ **C** is the **catalogue dynamic load rating** — the load causing L10 = 1 million revolutions.
- ✅ **P** is the **dynamic equivalent radial load**: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.
- ✅ Convert L10 (millions of revs) to L10h (hours) using: $L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60n} \cdot L_{10}$.
- ✅ **Modified life**: $L_{na} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot (C/P)^p$ — a1 reduces life (higher reliability), a2 improves (better material), a3 reduces (poor lubrication/temperature).
- ✅ **Application factor fw** is multiplied with P to account for shock loading (fw = 1.0 to 3.0).
- ✅ **Inverse problem**: To select a bearing, solve $C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60n}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$ and select the **next-higher catalogue C**.
- ✅ **Cube sensitivity**: Doubling the load cuts ball bearing life by **8×**; tripling cuts it by **27×** — never treat load-life linearly.
- ✅ The **exponent p = 10/3** for rollers is NOT a typo — it comes from line contact Hertzian theory, not ball contact theory.
- ✅ **KTU most-asked numericals**: (i) Compute L10h given C, P, n, and p; (ii) Compute required C given target L10h, P, n; (iii) Apply fw and reliability correction a1.
- ✅ **Static load check** $P_0 \leq C_0$ is separate from fatigue life — both must be satisfied in design.
- ✅ **Common units in KTU**: C in kN, P in kN, n in rpm, L10h in hours, a1/a2/a3 dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** is defined as the load acting on a non-rotating or very slowly oscillating rolling element bearing that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 times the ball diameter** (d_w) at the most heavily loaded contact point. It is the limiting static load, expressed in **Newtons (N)** or **kN**, beyond which plastic deformation at the contact zones becomes unacceptable for smooth operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** is defined as the constant stationary radial (or axial) load that a rolling element bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **1,000,000 revolutions (10⁶)** without developing fatigue spalling. It is expressed in **Newtons (N)** or **kN** and forms the basis for the classic **load–life (P–L)** relationship used in bearing selection.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a heavy steel cabinet across a floor on four small steel ball bearings (rollers).

- **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** is like asking: *"How heavy a cabinet can I push for 1,000,000 cycles of pushing before the bearings fatigue and crack?"* — The bearings are **spinning** continuously, so fatigue becomes the dominant failure mode. The answer depends on **how much** weight is on each ball and **how many cycles** the cabinet moves.

- **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** is like asking: *"If I drop a very heavy box onto the cabinet while the balls are not spinning, how much weight will permanently dent the balls and ruin them?"* — Here, motion is zero, so the failure is **permanent plastic indentation** of the contact patch, not fatigue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Dynamic capacity governs **rotating** bearings (governed by fatigue after millions of revolutions). Static capacity governs **stationary or very slow oscillating** bearings (governed by plastic brinelling). A bearing is **selected on dynamic capacity** when the rotation is continuous, and on **static capacity** when the bearing is stationary under heavy shock loads.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Rating life reference | **1,000,000 revolutions (10⁶)** | ISO 15 / IS 6455 standard |
| Static deformation limit | **0.0001 × d_w** | Permanent brinelling threshold |
| Exponent p (ball bearings) | **3** | Cubic load–life law |
| Exponent p (roller bearings) | **10/3 ≈ 3.33** | Roller load–life law |
| Reliability factor (R) | **90% default (L₁₀)** | Modified to L₅, L₁, L₂ via reliability factors |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – The Two Failure Regimes

A rolling element bearing can fail in one of two distinct physical modes, depending on whether the ring is **rotating** or **stationary**. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats these as two completely separate design equations because the underlying physics differs.

### Regime 1 – Dynamic (Rotating) Operation
- Load is applied on a **moving raceway**, so each ball/roller experiences **cyclic Hertzian contact stress**.
- After millions of cycles, a **fatigue spall (pit)** initiates at the subsurface where the maximum shear stress occurs.
- Failure is **progressive**; governed by cumulative damage → **life equation** is used.
- The **dynamic load capacity C** is a fixed catalogue value (load that gives 1 million revs of life at 90% reliability).

### Regime 2 – Static (Stationary) Operation
- Load is applied on a **stationary raceway**; there is no fatigue cycling.
- If the load exceeds the elastic limit of the material at the Hertzian contact zone, a **permanent plastic indentation (brinell mark)** forms.
- This indentation is **not progressive**, but it causes vibration, noise, and loss of precision → **static load equation** is used.
- The **static load capacity C₀** is the catalogue value at which permanent deformation equals 0.0001 d_w.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Equation

**The Load–Life Equation (Dynamic Capacity):**

The Weibull fatigue theory of bearing steels shows that bearing life is **inversely proportional** to the cube (ball) or 10/3 power (roller) of the equivalent dynamic load. This was experimentally confirmed by **Lundberg & Palmgren** and is codified in **ISO 15:2017** and **IS 6455**.

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$$

where $L_{10}$ is in millions of revolutions, $C$ is the dynamic load capacity, $P$ is the equivalent dynamic load, and $p = 3$ for balls, $10/3$ for rollers.

**The Static Safety Equation:**

There is no life here — only a **load limit**. The check is simply:

$$P_{0} \le \frac{C_{0}}{S_{0}}$$

where $P_0$ is the equivalent static load, $C_0$ is the static load capacity from the catalogue, and $S_0$ is the **static safety factor** (typically 1 to 3 depending on shock severity, smoothness demand, and whether the inner/outer ring rotates).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | Basic rating life in millions of revs (90% reliability) | millions of revs |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | Rating life in operating hours | hours |
| 3 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6} \cdot 60}{n \cdot 2\pi} \cdot \pi \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$  simplified → $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \, n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Same as above (most common form) | hours |
| 4 | $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$ | Required dynamic capacity to achieve a target life | N or kN |
| 5 | $P_{0} = X_{0} F_{r} + Y_{0} F_{a}$ | Equivalent static load on a bearing | N |
| 6 | $P = X F_{r} + Y F_{a}$ | Equivalent dynamic load on a bearing | N |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C_{0}}{P_{0}} \ge S_{0}$ | Static safety check (no failure if satisfied) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $L_{na} = a_1 \, a_{2} \, a_{3} \, L_{10}$ | Modified life with reliability & material factors | millions of revs |
| 9 | $a_1$ | Reliability factor (1.00 for 90%, 0.62 for 95%, etc.) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $a_2$ | Material/lubrication factor (0.5–1.6 typical) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $a_3$ | Operating conditions / contamination factor (0.1–0.8) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This load–life framework is the **universal language** of bearing selection. Every machine tool, automotive gearbox, electric motor, and aircraft turbofan relies on these two equations. The static check protects against brinelling during assembly, handling, or accidental start-up stalls; the dynamic check protects against fatigue over the machine's service life.

## 2.4 Variable Reference Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Dynamic load capacity (catalogue) | 1 kN – 5000 kN |
| $C_0$ | Static load capacity (catalogue) | 0.5 kN – 3000 kN |
| $P$ | Equivalent dynamic load | 0.1 C – 1.0 C |
| $P_0$ | Equivalent static load | ≤ C₀ |
| $F_r$ | Radial load on bearing | varies |
| $F_a$ | Axial (thrust) load on bearing | varies |
| $X, Y$ | Radial & axial dynamic load factors | from catalogue |
| $X_0, Y_0$ | Radial & axial static load factors | from catalogue |
| $n$ | Rotational speed | rpm |
| $S_0$ | Static safety factor | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| $L_{10}$ | Rating life at 90% reliability | 0.1 – 100 (million revs) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Load–Life Equation (Lundberg–Palmgren Concept)

The Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue model is based on the **Weibull weakest-link theory** of brittle fracture. The fatigue life of a bearing is determined by the volume of highly stressed material under the rolling contact. The detailed mathematical derivation proceeds as follows.

### Step 1: Hertzian Contact Stress
For a ball pressed against a raceway with force $Q$, the maximum Hertzian contact pressure is:

$$p_{max} = \dfrac{3 F}{2 \pi a^{2}}$$

where $a$ is the contact radius and $F$ is the contact force. This pressure is **sub-surface**, with the maximum shear stress at depth $\approx 0.5 a$ below the surface.

### Step 2: Subsurface Shear Stress
The orthogonal shear stress at the critical depth is proportional to the applied load:

$$\tau_{0} = k_{1} \cdot \dfrac{Q^{1/3}}{d_{w}^{\,2/3}}$$

where $d_w$ is the ball/roller diameter and $k_1$ is a geometric constant.

### Step 3: Weibull Probability of Survival
For a stressed volume $V$ under cyclic shear stress $\tau_0$, the probability of survival over $N$ cycles is:

$$S(N) = \exp\left[-N \int_V \left(\dfrac{\tau_0}{z_0}\right)^{c} dV \right]$$

where $c \approx 10/3$ for bearing steels and $z_0$ is a Weibull scale parameter.

### Step 4: Integration and Volume of Stressed Material
Integrating over the stressed volume (which scales with ball diameter cubed), the survival probability simplifies to:

$$S(N) = \exp\left[-N \cdot \tau_0^{\,c} \cdot d_w^{\,3} \cdot \text{const}\right]$$

### Step 5: Setting Reliability at 90% ($S = 0.9$)
At the rating life $L_{10}$ (90% survive, 10% fail), we impose $S = 0.9$, giving:

$$0.9 = \exp\left[-\text{const} \cdot \tau_0^{\,c} \cdot d_w^{\,3} \cdot L_{10}\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\tfrac{1}{0.9}\right) = \text{const} \cdot \tau_0^{\,c} \cdot d_w^{\,3} \cdot L_{10}$$

$$L_{10} \propto \dfrac{1}{\tau_0^{\,c} \cdot d_w^{\,3}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute $\tau_0$ from Step 2
$$L_{10} \propto \dfrac{1}{Q^{c/3} \cdot d_w^{\,2c/3} \cdot d_w^{\,3}}$$

$$L_{10} \propto \dfrac{1}{Q^{c/3} \cdot d_w^{\,2c/3 + 3}}$$

### Step 7: Identify the Exponent p
Experimental data on bearing steels show that $c/3 = 3$ for ball bearings, hence $c = 9$ (close to the measured 10/3). Therefore:

$$L_{10} \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{Q}\right)^{3} \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{P}\right)^{3}$$

Defining $C$ as the load that gives $L_{10} = 1$ million revolutions, we obtain the **fundamental load–life equation**:

$$\boxed{\,L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}\,} \quad \text{(ball bearing)}$$

For roller bearings, the corresponding exponent is $p = 10/3$:

$$\boxed{\,L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3}\,} \quad \text{(roller bearing)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Dynamic Load P

A bearing carries both radial ($F_r$) and axial ($F_a$) loads. The catalogue, however, lists capacity for one "equivalent" pure radial load that produces the same life. The equivalent load is defined by the **life-matching condition**:

$$\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \left(\dfrac{C}{F_{r,eq}}\right)^{p}$$

For a single-row deep-groove ball bearing, ISO 15 tabulates values of $X$ and $Y$ such that:

$$P = X F_{r} + Y F_{a}$$

The factors $X$ and $Y$ depend on the ratio $F_a / F_r$ and the bearing geometry (contact angle). They are **not derived from first principles** but fitted from experimental fatigue data. For a typical deep-groove ball bearing with $F_a/F_r \le e$:

$$P = F_{r} \quad (X = 1, Y = 0)$$

For $F_a/F_r > e$:

$$P = 0.56 F_{r} + 1.55 F_{a} \quad \text{(typical values)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Static Load Capacity Criterion

The Hertz contact theory gives the contact area as a function of the load $Q$ on one ball. The maximum pressure $p_{max}$ and the permanent indentation depth $\delta$ are related by:

$$\delta = k \cdot \dfrac{Q^{2/3}}{d_{w}^{\,1/3}}$$

For ball bearings, ISO 15 defines the static load capacity $C_0$ as the load at which $\delta = 0.0001 \cdot d_w$. Substituting:

$$0.0001 \, d_w = k \cdot \dfrac{C_0^{\,2/3}}{d_w^{\,1/3}}$$

$$C_0 = \left(\dfrac{0.0001 \, d_w^{\,4/3}}{k}\right)^{3/2} \propto d_w^{\,2}$$

Thus static capacity scales with the **square of the ball diameter**. Solving for the required equivalent static load on the bearing:

$$\boxed{\,P_{0} = X_{0} F_{r} + Y_{0} F_{a} \le \dfrac{C_0}{S_0}\,}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Demonstration – A KTU-Style Calculation

> **Given (typical KTU numerical):** A deep-groove ball bearing 6208 has $C = 32.5$ kN, $C_0 = 17.8$ kN. It is subjected to $F_r = 4.5$ kN and $F_a = 1.8$ kN at a speed of 1450 rpm. Compute: (a) rating life $L_{10h}$ in hours, and (b) static safety factor.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Rating Life**

For a 6208 bearing, catalogue gives $e = 0.22$, $Y = 1.95$ (for $F_a/F_r > e$). Check the ratio:

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1.8}{4.5} = 0.4 > e = 0.22$$

Hence $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.95$. Therefore:

$$P = X F_{r} + Y F_{a} = 0.56(4.5) + 1.95(1.8)$$

$$P = 2.52 + 3.51 = 6.03 \text{ kN}$$

Rating life in millions of revolutions (ball, p = 3):

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = \left(\dfrac{32.5}{6.03}\right)^{3}$$

$$L_{10} = (5.390)^{3} = 156.6 \text{ million revolutions}$$

Converting to hours at $n = 1450$ rpm:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6} \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \dfrac{10^{6} \cdot 156.6}{60 \cdot 1450}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{1.566 \times 10^{8}}{87000} = 1800 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (b): Static Safety Factor**

For the same bearing, $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 0.5$ (typical catalogue values for static equivalent load).

$$P_0 = X_0 F_{r} + Y_0 F_a = 0.6(4.5) + 0.5(1.8)$$

$$P_0 = 2.7 + 0.9 = 3.6 \text{ kN}$$

$$S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{17.8}{3.6} = 4.94$$

Since $S_0 = 4.94 > 1$ (typical minimum), the bearing is **safe under static loading**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow: Dynamic vs. Static Capacity Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Bearing Application Starts] --> B0{Is the inner ring rotating continuously?}
    B0 -- YES --> C0[Use DYNAMIC Capacity C]
    B0 -- NO or SLOW OSCILLATION --> D0[Use STATIC Capacity C0]
    C0 --> E0{Combined load?}
    E0 -- YES --> F0[Compute equivalent P from X and Y]
    E0 -- NO --> G0[P = radial load or P = axial load]
    F0 --> H0[Apply L10 in millions of revs]
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Convert to L10h using n in rpm]
    I0 --> J0[Compare with desired life]
    J0 --> K0[Check: S0 = C0 divided by P0 is greater than 1]
    D0 --> K0
    K0 -- PASS --> L0[Bearing is ACCEPTABLE]
    K0 -- FAIL --> M0[Select larger bearing or reduce load]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the Load–Life System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Applied Radial Load Fr] --> X1[Radial Factor X]
    B1[Applied Axial Load Fa] --> Y1[Axial Factor Y]
    X1 --> P1[Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    Y1 --> P1
    P1 --> L1[L10 life block]
    C1[Dynamic Capacity C from catalogue] --> L1
    L1 --> LIFE[L10 in million revs]
    LIFE --> HR[Convert to L10h using speed n]
    HR --> OUT[Final rating life in hours]
    P0E[Equivalent Static Load P0] --> SAFE[Static safety block]
    C0E[Static Capacity C0 from catalogue] --> SAFE
    SAFE --> SOUT[S0 greater than required value]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Concept Map of the Two Capacities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Rolling Element Bearing] --> B2{Failure Mode}
    B2 -- ROTATING raceway --> C2[Fatigue Spalling]
    B2 -- STATIONARY raceway --> D2[Plastic Brinelling]
    C2 --> E2[Use DYNAMIC Capacity C]
    D2 --> F2[Use STATIC Capacity C0]
    E2 --> G2[Equation L10 equals C over P raised to p]
    F2 --> H2[Equation S0 equals C0 divided by P0]
    G2 --> I2[Life in millions of revs or hours]
    H2 --> J2[Static safety check only]
    I2 --> K2[Compare with target life]
    J2 --> L2[Compare S0 with catalogue minimum]
    K2 --> M2[Final Design]
    L2 --> M2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define static load capacity and dynamic load capacity of a rolling contact bearing. Mention the standard deformation limit used in defining static capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Static Load Capacity (C₀) (1½ marks):** It is the load acting on a non-rotating or very slowly oscillating bearing that produces a **permanent deformation of 0.0001 times the ball diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact zone, without impairing the bearing's smooth operation.

- **Dynamic Load Capacity (C) (1½ marks):** It is the constant stationary radial (or thrust) load under which a bearing with rotating inner ring can theoretically achieve a rating life of **1,000,000 revolutions (10⁶ revs)** at 90% reliability, before fatigue spalling occurs.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State the basic rating life equation for a ball bearing and a roller bearing. Why are the exponents different?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **rating life equation** for a ball bearing is:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}$$

- For a **roller bearing**, the exponent is $p = 10/3$:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3}$$

- **Reason for the difference (1 mark):** The exponent is derived from the **Weibull fatigue theory of Hertzian contact stresses**. In a ball bearing, the contact is **point**-type, and the load–life exponent is experimentally 3. In a roller bearing, the contact is **line**-type, leading to a different stress distribution and a fatigue exponent of $10/3 \approx 3.33$. The higher exponent means **rollers are more sensitive to overload** than balls.

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) forgetting to convert $L_{10}$ from millions of revolutions to hours, (b) using $p = 3$ for roller bearings (must use $10/3$), (c) using wrong catalogue factors $X, Y, X_0, Y_0$ without checking the $F_a/F_r$ ratio against $e$, and (d) not stating the units explicitly. Always write the final life with units (hours or million revs) and the safety factor as a dimensionless number with a comparison.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define static load capacity ($C_0$) and dynamic load capacity ($C$) of a rolling element bearing. Derive the load–life relationship $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ for a ball bearing, explaining each step of the Lundberg–Palmgren model. **7 marks**

**(b)** A 6205 deep-groove ball bearing carries a radial load of 2.5 kN and an axial thrust of 0.8 kN at a shaft speed of 2880 rpm. The catalogue values are: $C = 14$ kN, $C_0 = 6.95$ kN, $e = 0.24$, $Y = 1.85$ (for $F_a/F_r > e$), $X = 0.56$, and for static: $X_0 = 0.6, Y_0 = 0.5$. Calculate: (i) the equivalent dynamic load $P$, (ii) the rating life $L_{10h}$ in hours, and (iii) the static safety factor $S_0$. **7 marks**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION – Question A

##### Part (a) – 7 marks

- **Definition of $C_0$ (1 mark):** The static load capacity of a rolling element bearing is the load that produces a **permanent deformation of 0.0001 × d_w** at the most heavily loaded rolling element contact, under non-rotating or slowly oscillating conditions. It is the catalogue value above which brinelling becomes unacceptable.

- **Definition of $C$ (1 mark):** The dynamic load capacity is the constant stationary radial or thrust load under which the bearing will theoretically attain a rating life of **1,000,000 revolutions** at 90% reliability without fatigue spalling.

- **Derivation of $L_{10} = (C/P)^3$ (5 marks):**

The Lundberg–Palmgren model assumes that the fatigue life of a bearing is determined by the **maximum orthogonal subsurface shear stress** in the Hertzian contact zone. For a ball of diameter $d_w$ under contact load $Q$:

$$\tau_0 = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{Q^{1/3}}{d_w^{\,2/3}}$$

The Weibull probability of survival over $N$ cycles for a stressed volume $V$ is:

$$S(N) = \exp\left[-N \int_V \left(\dfrac{\tau_0}{z_0}\right)^{c} dV \right]$$

The integral is over the stressed volume, which scales as $d_w^{\,3}$. Hence:

$$S(N) = \exp\left[-N \cdot K \cdot \tau_0^{\,c} \cdot d_w^{\,3}\right]$$

Substituting $\tau_0 \propto Q^{1/3} / d_w^{\,2/3}$:

$$S(N) = \exp\left[-N \cdot K' \cdot Q^{c/3} \cdot d_w^{\,2c/3 - 3}\right]$$

For bearing steel, $c = 9$ (Lundberg–Palmgren), giving $c/3 = 3$ and $2c/3 - 3 = 3$. Therefore:

$$S(N) = \exp\left[-N \cdot K' \cdot Q^{3} \cdot d_w^{\,3}\right]$$

Imposing 90% reliability $S(L_{10}) = 0.9$ and defining $C$ as the load that gives $L_{10} = 1$ million revs:

$$\boxed{\,L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}\,}$$

##### Part (b) – 7 marks

**(i) Equivalent Dynamic Load (2 marks):**

Check load ratio: $F_a / F_r = 0.8 / 2.5 = 0.32$. Since $0.32 > e = 0.24$, use $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.85$.

$$P = X F_r + Y F_a = 0.56 \times 2.5 + 1.85 \times 0.8$$

$$P = 1.4 + 1.48 = 2.88 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Rating Life in Hours (3 marks):**

For a ball bearing, $p = 3$:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = \left(\dfrac{14}{2.88}\right)^{3} = (4.861)^{3}$$

$$L_{10} = 114.9 \text{ million revolutions}$$

Converting to hours:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{60 \times n} = \dfrac{114.9 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 2880}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{1.149 \times 10^{8}}{172800} = 665 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Static Safety Factor (2 marks):**

$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a = 0.6 \times 2.5 + 0.5 \times 0.8 = 1.5 + 0.4 = 1.9 \text{ kN}$$

$$S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{6.95}{1.9} = 3.66$$

Since $S_0 = 3.66 > 1$, the bearing is **safe under static load**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of equivalent dynamic load and equivalent static load on a rolling contact bearing. How is the static load capacity $C_0$ defined, and what is its physical significance? **7 marks**

**(b)** A cylindrical roller bearing 30206 is subjected to a radial load of 8 kN and operates at 1500 rpm. The catalogue gives: $C = 43.5$ kN, $C_0 = 44$ kN, $e = 0.30$, $Y = 1.7$, $X = 0.4$ (radial bearing, $F_a = 0$). For static: $X_0 = 0.6, Y_0 = 0.4$. The required life is 10,000 hours. Verify whether the bearing is suitable. **7 marks**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION – Question B

##### Part (a) – 7 marks

- **Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$ (2 marks):** When a bearing is simultaneously loaded by a radial force $F_r$ and an axial force $F_a$, the combined effect on life is represented by a single hypothetical radial load called the **equivalent dynamic load** $P$. It is defined as the constant stationary radial load which, if applied alone, would give the same bearing life as the actual combined loading. Mathematically:

$$P = X F_{r} + Y F_{a}$$

- **Equivalent Static Load $P_0$ (2 marks):** Similarly, the **equivalent static load** is the hypothetical radial load that produces the same permanent deformation at the contact as the actual combined load. It is defined as:

$$P_0 = X_0 F_{r} + Y_0 F_{a}$$

- **Definition and Significance of $C_0$ (3 marks):** The **static load capacity $C_0$** is defined as the load that produces a permanent indentation of **0.0001 × d_w** at the most stressed contact point under static (non-rotating) conditions. Its **physical significance** is that it represents the **upper limit of non-damaging load** for a stationary bearing. It is checked when the bearing is subjected to heavy shock loads, during assembly, or when the inner ring does not rotate (e.g., in some swing mechanisms). It is **not a life parameter** — it is a **load limit parameter**.

##### Part (b) – 7 marks

**Step 1: Equivalent Load (1 mark)**

Since $F_a = 0$ and the bearing is radial type:

$$P = F_r = 8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Required Dynamic Capacity (2 marks)**

For 10,000 hours at 1500 rpm, required life in million revs:

$$L_{10, \text{req}} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 60 \times 1500}{10^{6}} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{8}}{10^{6}} = 900 \text{ million revs}$$

For a roller bearing, $p = 10/3$:

$$C_{\text{req}} = P \cdot L_{10, \text{req}}^{\,3/10} = 8 \times (900)^{0.3}$$

$$C_{\text{req}} = 8 \times 7.18 = 57.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Compare with Available Capacity (2 marks)**

Since $C_{\text{available}} = 43.5$ kN $< C_{\text{req}} = 57.4$ kN, the bearing **will NOT meet** the required life of 10,000 hours. The bearing is **not suitable**.

**Step 4: Actual Life if Used (1 mark)**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{43.5}{8}\right)^{10/3} = (5.4375)^{3.333}$$

$$L_{10} = 232.0 \text{ million revs}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{232 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1500} = 2577 \text{ hours} \quad (\text{vs. required 10,000 hours})$$

**Step 5: Static Safety Check (1 mark)**

$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a = 0.6 \times 8 + 0.4 \times 0 = 4.8 \text{ kN}$$

$$S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{44}{4.8} = 9.17 \gg 1 \quad \text{(safe under static load)}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The bearing is **statically safe** but **dynamically insufficient** — a larger bearing (e.g., 30208) or a reduced load must be selected.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting units for life:** Always write "$L_{10h} = 1800$ **hours**" or "$L_{10} = 156.6$ **million revolutions**". A bare number loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up p:** Ball = 3, Roller = 10/3. Mixing these loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring $F_a / F_r$ check against $e$:** If you blindly apply $X, Y$ without checking the ratio against $e$, you may get the wrong $P$, and the entire downstream calculation is wrong (–2 to –4 marks).
> 4. **Forgetting the static check:** KTU often gives 2 marks specifically for $S_0 = C_0 / P_0$. Missing this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Using radial-form factors for thrust bearings:** $X = 1$ for pure axial load, and vice versa. Read the catalogue carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Static Load Capacity ($C_0$):** Load limit for **stationary** bearings, defined at **0.0001 d_w** permanent deformation. Checked when $S_0 = C_0 / P_0 \ge \text{required safety}$.
- **Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$):** Load for **1 million revolutions** at 90% reliability for a **rotating** bearing. Governs fatigue life.
- **Load–Life Equation (Ball):** $L_{10} = (C / P)^3$
- **Load–Life Equation (Roller):** $L_{10} = (C / P)^{10/3}$
- **Life in Hours:** $L_{10h} = (10^6 \times L_{10}) / (60 \times n)$
- **Equivalent Dynamic Load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$
- **Equivalent Static Load:** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$
- **Modified Life (advanced):** $L_{na} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10}$ (reliability, material, contamination)
- **Rule of Thumb:** If a bearing rotates, use the **dynamic** equation first; then verify the **static** check. If it does not rotate, only the **static** check applies.
- **Reliability Convention:** $L_{10}$ = 90% reliable. $L_{50}$ (median life) ≈ 5 × $L_{10}$ for ball bearings. KTU questions are 90% based.
- **Safety Factor $S_0$:** Minimum values — 1.0 for no shock, 1.5 for light shock, 2.0–3.0 for heavy shock or precision requirements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axial and Radial Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Axial and Radial Loads # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Axial and Radial Loads In the design of machine elements, the classification of external forces acting on a component is the first step toward analytical modeling. These forces are resolved along the **principal geometric axes** of the element. **Radial Load ($F_r$):** A radial load is a force vector that acts **perpendicular to the axis of rotation (or symmetry)** of a machine element. Its line of action passes through the **shaft centerline**, creating bending and compressive effects on bearings. **Axial Load ($F_a$):** An axial load (also called a *thrust load*) is a force vector that acts **parallel to the axis of rotation** of a machine element. It tends to push or pull the shaft along its longitudinal direction. **Combined Load:** A combined load occurs when both radial and axial components act simultaneously on the same element, producing a resultant inclined force. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT502 Module 4):** *Axial and radial loads* form the foundation for computing the **Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load ($P$)** and **Equivalent Static Bearing Load ($P_0$)** in rolling contact bearings. Without correctly resolving external forces into their radial and axial components, bearing life calculations become mathematically invalid. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are holding a wooden rod (representing a rotating shaft) in your hand: - **Radial Load Analogy:** If you push the rod sideways (horizontally) — for example, pressing a spinning bicycle wheel from the side — the force vector is **perpendicular** to the rod's length. This pushes down on the bearings in a direction normal to the shaft axis. This is a **radial load**. - **Axial Load Analogy:** If you try to push the rod into your palm along its length (like a drill bit being pushed into wood) or pull it outward (like pulling a rod out of a socket), the force vector is **parallel** to the rod's length. This force tries to slide the shaft along its own axis. This is an **axial (thrust) load**. - **Combined Load Analogy:** If you press the spinning bicycle wheel sideways *and* simultaneously push it forward along the axle, both effects combine. The bearing must resist both the side push and the forward push. The two components add up vectorially to form a single resultant load at some angle. > [!IMPORTANT] > The direction of the load is **always defined relative to the axis of rotation**, not relative to gravity. A vertical shaft experiencing vertical weight still carries that weight as a *radial* load because the force is perpendicular to its axis. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bearing Load Analysis The following standard parameters are used throughout KTU design problems: - **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$):** The maximum static load (in **Newtons, N**) a bearing can withstand without permanent deformation exceeding **0.0001 of the ball or roller diameter**. - **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$):** The constant radial load (in **N**) that a bearing can carry for **one million revolutions** with a 90% reliability. - **Load Ratio ($F_a / C_0$):** A dimensionless parameter used to select the thrust factor $Y$ from manufacturer tables. - **Bore Diameter ($d$):** The inner race nominal diameter in **millimeters (mm)**. - **Equivalent Dynamic Load ($P$):** The single hypothetical radial load (in **N**) that, if applied, would give the same bearing life as the actual combined loading. - **Equivalent Static Load ($P_0$):** The single hypothetical static load (in **N**) producing the same permanent deformation as the actual combined load. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Loads Arise in Machine Elements In real machine assemblies, pure radial or pure axial loads are rare. Most elements experience a **combination** of both. The principal sources are: 1. **Transmission of Torque via Gears:** Spur gears transmit purely radial forces to the shaft, while helical, bevel, and worm gears transmit *both* radial and axial (thrust) forces. 2. **Belt and Chain Drives:** Flat and V-belt drives produce predominantly radial loads. Worm and screw mechanisms produce predominantly axial loads. 3. **Weight of Components:** The self-weight of a pulley, gear, or impeller produces a downward radial load. 4. **Working Forces:** Cutting forces in machine tools, press forces in punches, and fluid pressure in turbines contribute to combined loading. 5. **Inertia Effects:** During starting, braking, or speed changes, dynamic axial and radial inertial forces develop. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Equivalent Load Computation The **Why**: Bearings are rated for either pure radial or pure axial standard loads, but actual service loads are combined. To use the rating life equations, we must convert the combined load into a single **equivalent** load $P$ that produces the same fatigue effect. **Step 1 — Identify the Load Type** - For **angular contact ball bearings** and **tapered roller bearings**, the contact angle $\alpha$ inherently allows the bearing to carry a small axial component derived from the radial load through *induced thrust*. **Step 2 — Compute the Load Ratio** - Calculate $\dfrac{F_a}{C_0}$ where $F_a$ is the applied axial load and $C_0$ is the static load rating. **Step 3 — Compare $F_a / F_r$ with the Boundary Ratio $e$** - If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \le e$, then axial load is negligible; $P = F_r$. - If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$, then axial load significantly influences fatigue; use $P = X F_r + Y F_a$. **Step 4 — Determine $X$ and $Y$ from Manufacturer Tables** - Values of $X$, $Y$, and $e$ are provided in bearing catalogues (SKF, FAG, NTN) and are tabulated as functions of $F_a / C_0$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula No. | Equation | Meaning | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | (1) | $P = F_r$ | Equivalent load when axial load is negligible ($\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \le e$) | N | | (2) | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load for combined loading | N | | (3) | $P_0 = F_r$ | Equivalent static load (no axial) | N | | (4) | $P_0 = 0.6 F_r + 0.5 F_a$ | Equivalent static load (combined) | N | | (5) | $P_0 = F_a$ | Equivalent static load (pure axial) | N | | (6) | $F_{ai} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{F_r}{Y}$ | Induced thrust on a single-row angular contact bearing | N | | (7) | $F_{aA} - F_{aB} = F_{a,external}$ | Axial force equilibrium for paired angular contact bearings | N | | (8) | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$ | Load angle with respect to radial direction | rad / deg | | (9) | $C / P = (L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60 / 10^6)^{1/k}$ | Rating life equation ($k = 3$ for ball, $10/3$ for roller) | — | | (10) | $F_r = \sqrt{F_{r1}^2 + F_{r2}^2}$ | Resultant radial load from two perpendicular components | N | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU valuation, the most common mistake is **forgetting the induced thrust $F_{ai}$** when dealing with paired angular contact bearings. Always compute the internal axial reaction first before applying equilibrium. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The ability to resolve and combine axial and radial loads is essential in: - **Automotive wheel hub design:** Combined cornering (radial) and braking (axial) forces. - **Helical gear reducers:** Computing shaft reaction forces for bearing selection. - **Centrifugal pumps:** Handling both radial hydraulic forces and axial thrust from impeller pressure differences. - **Wind turbine main shafts:** Massive combined radial (from rotor weight) and axial (from wind thrust) loads. - **Machine tool spindles:** Tool-cutting forces resolved into axial (feed direction) and radial (depth of cut direction) components. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Equivalent Dynamic Load $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ ### Background and Physical Justification A rolling contact bearing under combined radial and axial loading experiences contact stresses at the raceway-ball interface that are nonlinear functions of the applied forces. To standardize life calculations, the ISO and ABMA standards define the equivalent dynamic load $P$ such that: > *The actual bearing life under the combined loading equals the bearing life under the equivalent radial load $P$.* ### Mathematical Derivation Let $L_{10}$ be the rating life in millions of revolutions. For a ball bearing under a pure radial load $F_r$, life is given by: $$ L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{F_r} \right)^3 $$ Now introduce an axial component $F_a$ that increases the contact stress. The **empirical observation** (from Hertzian contact stress theory and experimental data) is that the additional fatigue damage scales with a weighted sum of the two force components. The general ISO form is: $$ P = X F_r + Y F_a $$ where the coefficients $X$ and $Y$ account for the relative fatigue contribution of radial and axial loading. These are determined from test data such that: $$ L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{X F_r + Y F_a} \right)^3 $$ ### Step-by-Step Algebraic Treatment Starting from the definition that the equivalent load produces the same contact stress: $$ \sigma_{contact}(P) = \sigma_{contact}(F_r) \quad \text{(matched fatigue damage)} $$ For Hertzian line contact (rollers), the maximum contact pressure $p_{max} \propto \sqrt{F / L \cdot R_{eff}}$, where $L$ is the contact length and $R_{eff}$ the effective radius. The fatigue life is empirically: $$ L \propto \dfrac{1}{p_{max}^{\,9}} \quad \text{(line contact, } k = 10/3 \text{)} $$ $$ L \propto \dfrac{1}{p_{max}^{\,9}} \quad \text{→} \quad L \propto \dfrac{1}{F^{\,3}} \quad \text{(point contact, } k = 3 \text{)} $$ Matching the damage from combined loading to pure radial loading $P$: $$ (X F_r + Y F_a)^k = (X F_r)^k + (Y F_a)^k \cdot (\text{correction factor}) $$ In practice, ISO/ABMA simplifies this via empirical constants $X$ and $Y$ which subsume all geometric and material nonlinearities. The standardized result is: $$ P = X F_r + Y F_a $$ When $F_a$ is small relative to $F_r$ (such that $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \le e$), the axial fatigue contribution is dominated by the radial, so the standard reduces to: $$ P = F_r $$ ### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 6308 deep groove ball bearing carries a radial load of $F_r = 3500 \text{ N}$ and an axial load of $F_a = 1200 \text{ N}$. Given $C_0 = 24 \text{ kN}$, and the bearing catalogue values $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.15$, $e = 0.22$. Compute the equivalent dynamic load. **Solution:** Step 1 — Calculate the load ratio: $$ \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1200}{3500} = 0.343 $$ Step 2 — Compare with the boundary value $e$: $$ 0.343 > 0.22 = e $$ Step 3 — Since the load ratio exceeds $e$, the equivalent dynamic load uses both components: $$ P = X F_r + Y F_a $$ $$ P = (0.56)(3500) + (1.15)(1200) $$ $$ P = 1960 + 1380 = 3340 \text{ N} $$ Step 4 — Verification with $C_0$: $$ \dfrac{F_a}{C_0} = \dfrac{1200}{24000} = 0.050 $$ The catalogue $X, Y$ at this $F_a / C_0$ value is consistent with the values given, so the result is valid. **Equivalent dynamic load: $P = 3340 \text{ N}$.** ## 3.2 Derivation of Induced Thrust for Paired Angular Contact Bearings ### Physical Setup Consider two angular contact bearings $A$ and $B$ mounted on a shaft in **face-to-face (X-arrangement)** or **back-to-back (O-arrangement)** configuration. The external axial load $F_{a,ext}$ and the radial loads $F_{rA}$ and $F_{rB}$ interact via the contact angles. ### Derivation The internal axial force generated by a radial load on an angular contact bearing is: $$ F_{ai} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{F_r}{Y} $$ The factor $0.5$ comes from the geometry: only half the radial load is reacted along the contact angle $\alpha$. The axial equilibrium of the shaft gives: $$ F_{aA} - F_{aB} = F_{a,external} $$ where $F_{aA}$ and $F_{aB}$ are the total axial loads on bearings $A$ and $B$. Each is either the induced thrust (if the bearing is unloaded axially) or the maximum of induced and applied thrust. **Algorithm to Solve Paired Bearing Systems:** 1. Compute induced thrusts: $F_{aiA} = 0.5 F_{rA} / Y_A$ and $F_{aiB} = 0.5 F_{rB} / Y_B$. 2. If $F_{aA} + F_{aB} = F_{a,ext}$ is the external direction, identify which bearing is "tight" (carries external load plus induced) and which is "loose" (carries only induced). 3. Apply equilibrium to find the actual axial force on each bearing. 4.
