## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Open Belt Drive Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["DRIVER PULLEY r1 (rotates CW)"] --> B["TANGENT POINT T1A"]
    A --> C["TANGENT POINT T1B"]
    B --> D["TIGHT SIDE T1 - upper straight run"]
    D --> E["TANGENT POINT T2A on DRIVEN PULLEY"]
    E --> F["DRIVEN PULLEY r2 (rotates CW)"]
    F --> G["TANGENT POINT T2B on DRIVEN PULLEY"]
    G --> H["SLACK SIDE T2 - lower straight run"]
    H --> C
    I["CENTRE DISTANCE C"] --- A
    I --- F
    J["WRAP ANGLE theta1 = pi minus 2 alpha"] -.-> A
    K["WRAP ANGLE theta2 = pi plus 2 alpha"] -.-> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Crossed Belt Drive Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["DRIVER PULLEY r1 (rotates CW)"] --> B["TANGENT POINT T1A top"]
    A --> C["TANGENT POINT T1B bottom"]
    C --> D["CROSSED RUN T1 going upward right"]
    D --> E["TANGENT POINT T2A on driven pulley bottom"]
    E --> F["DRIVEN PULLEY r2 (rotates CCW)"]
    F --> G["TANGENT POINT T2B on driven pulley top"]
    G --> H["CROSSED RUN T2 going downward left"]
    H --> B
    I["CENTRE DISTANCE C"] --- A
    I --- F
    J["WRAP ANGLE theta1 = pi plus 2 alpha"] -.-> A
    K["WRAP ANGLE theta2 = pi plus 2 alpha"] -.-> F
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: V-Belt Wedge Action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TIGHT SIDE T1 enters groove"] --> B["CONTACT WITH SIDE FLANK A"]
    B --> C["NORMAL REACTION N1 perpendicular to flank"]
    A --> D["CONTACT WITH SIDE FLANK B"]
    D --> E["NORMAL REACTION N2 perpendicular to flank"]
    C --> F["RESULTANT N pushes belt INTO groove"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["INCREASED NORMAL FORCE equals 2N sin beta"]
    G --> H["MU times N equals HIGH FRICTION FORCE"]
    H --> I["SLACK SIDE T2 leaves groove with reduced tension"]
    J["GROOVE ANGLE 2 beta = 34 to 38 degrees under load"] -.-> F
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Power Transmission Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT ROTATION N1 on DRIVER PULLEY"] --> B["BELT LINEAR VELOCITY v = pi d1 N1"]
    B --> C["FRICTION GRIP generates T1 minus T2"]
    C --> D{"CHECK MAX POWER CONDITION"}
    D -->|T1 = 3Tc| E["OPTIMAL DESIGN"]
    D -->|T1 less than 3Tc| F["UNDERSIZED DRIVE - slip occurs"]
    D -->|T1 greater than 3Tc| G["OVERSIZED - belt may fail"]
    E --> H["OUTPUT POWER P = T1 minus T2 times v"]
    F --> I["INCREASE T1 or USE V-BELT"]
    G --> J["REDUCE BELT TENSION"]
    H --> K["DELIVERED TO DRIVEN PULLEY"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between a flat belt drive and a V-belt drive. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Flat Belt | V-Belt |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rectangular cross-section, runs on flat pulleys | Trapezoidal cross-section, runs in V-grooved sheaves |
| 2 | Power is transmitted by friction only | Power is transmitted by friction **plus wedge action** |
| 3 | Higher tendency to slip, especially at low wrap angles | Negligible slip due to high normal reaction from groove walls |
| 4 | Used for long centre distances (e.g., line shafts) | Used for short centre distances (e.g., automotive drives) |
| 5 | Velocity ratio limited to about 1:6 | Velocity ratio can be up to 1:20 |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: What is the significance of the angle of contact in belt drives? State Euler's belt friction equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **angle of contact ($\theta$)** is the angle (in radians) subtended by the arc of belt in contact with the pulley. It is significant because:

1. It determines the **frictional grip** of the belt on the pulley.
2. The ratio of tensions $T_1/T_2$ increases exponentially with $\theta$.
3. A small $\theta$ (e.g., on a small pulley) leads to slip and power loss.
4. Designers aim for $\theta \ge 150^\circ$ on the smaller pulley.

**Euler's belt friction equation (Eytelwein's equation):**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between belt and pulley, and $\theta$ is the angle of contact in radians.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Two flat pulleys, 300 mm and 600 mm in diameter, are mounted on parallel shafts 2.4 m apart. The belt is an open drive. The coefficient of friction is 0.3, and the maximum permissible tension in the belt is 1000 N. The belt has a mass of 1.2 kg per metre length and runs at 600 m/min.**

**Solve the following:**

**(a) Calculate the angle of contact for each pulley, the length of belt, and the power transmitted.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) If the drive is converted to a crossed belt, find the new angle of contact, new belt length, and the percentage change in power transmitted.** **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Given Data:**
- $d_1 = 600$ mm = 0.6 m, $d_2 = 300$ mm = 0.3 m
- $C = 2.4$ m, $\mu = 0.3$, $T_{\max} = T_1 = 1000$ N
- $m = 1.2$ kg/m, $v = 600$ m/min = **10 m/s**

### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Find $\alpha$ for the open belt**

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{r_1 - r_2}{C} = \frac{0.3 - 0.15}{2.4} = \frac{0.15}{2.4} = 0.0625$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.0625) = 3.584^\circ = 0.0625 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Angles of contact**

Smaller pulley (wrap angle is less):
$$\theta_2 = 180^\circ - 2\alpha = 180^\circ - 7.168^\circ = 172.83^\circ$$

Converting to radians:
$$\theta_2 = \pi - 2\alpha = 3.1416 - 2(0.0625) = 3.0166 \text{ rad}$$

Larger pulley (wrap angle is more):
$$\theta_1 = 180^\circ + 2\alpha = 180^\circ + 7.168^\circ = 187.17^\circ$$

Converting to radians:
$$\theta_1 = \pi + 2\alpha = 3.1416 + 2(0.0625) = 3.2666 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: Length of belt (open drive)**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(2.4) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.6 + 0.3) + \frac{(0.3 - 0.6)^2}{4(2.4)}$$

$$L = 4.8 + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.9) + \frac{0.09}{9.6}$$

$$L = 4.8 + 1.4137 + 0.009375$$

$$\boxed{L = 6.2231 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: Centrifugal tension**

$$T_c = m v^2 = 1.2 \times (10)^2 = 120 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Maximum tension condition**

$$T_1 - T_c = e^{\mu\theta_2}(T_2 - T_c)$$

$$1000 - 120 = e^{0.3 \times 3.0166}(T_2 - 120)$$

$$880 = e^{0.9050}(T_2 - 120)$$

$$880 = 2.4717 (T_2 - 120)$$

$$T_2 - 120 = 356.04$$

$$T_2 = 476.04 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Power transmitted**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (1000 - 476.04) \times 10$$

$$\boxed{P = 5239.6 \text{ W} = 5.24 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Part (b) Solution — Crossed Belt

**Step 1: New $\alpha$ for crossed belt**

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{C} = \frac{0.3 + 0.15}{2.4} = \frac{0.45}{2.4} = 0.1875$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.1875) = 10.81^\circ = 0.1887 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: New angles of contact (equal for both pulleys)**

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 180^\circ + 2\alpha = 180^\circ + 21.62^\circ = 201.62^\circ$$

In radians:
$$\theta = \pi + 2\alpha = 3.1416 + 2(0.1887) = 3.519 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: New belt length (crossed)**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(2.4) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.9) + \frac{(0.9)^2}{9.6}$$

$$L = 4.8 + 1.4137 + 0.084375$$

$$\boxed{L = 6.2981 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: New tension ratio**

$$T_1 - T_c = e^{\mu\theta}(T_2 - T_c)$$

$$880 = e^{0.3 \times 3.519}(T_2 - 120)$$

$$880 = e^{1.0557}(T_2 - 120)$$

$$880 = 2.8733 (T_2 - 120)$$

$$T_2 - 120 = 306.27$$

$$T_2 = 426.27 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: New power transmitted**

$$P_{\text{crossed}} = (1000 - 426.27) \times 10 = 5737.3 \text{ W} = 5.74 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Percentage change in power**

$$\Delta P = 5737.3 - 5239.6 = 497.7 \text{ W}$$

$$\% \text{ Change} = \frac{497.7}{5239.6} \times 100 = 9.5\% \text{ increase}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power increases by } 9.5\% \text{ in crossed drive}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Alternative]**

**Q: A V-belt drive is to transmit 20 kW at 1450 rpm of the driver pulley. The diameters of the driver and driven pulleys are 200 mm and 400 mm respectively, and the centre distance is 600 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.25 and the groove angle is 38°. The belt mass is 0.9 kg/m. The angle of contact on the smaller pulley is 160°.**

**Solve the following:**

**(a) Calculate the belt velocity, centrifugal tension, and effective tensions in the tight and slack sides.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Determine the required maximum belt tension and verify if the design is safe for a belt of breaking strength 1500 N (with a factor of safety of 10).** **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 20$ kW = 20000 W, $N_1 = 1450$ rpm
- $d_1 = 200$ mm = 0.2 m, $d_2 = 400$ mm = 0.4 m
- $C = 600$ mm = 0.6 m, $\mu = 0.25$
- Groove angle $2\beta = 38^\circ$, so $\beta = 19^\circ$
- $m = 0.9$ kg/m, $\theta = 160^\circ = 2.7925$ rad (on smaller pulley)

### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Belt velocity**

$$v = \pi d_1 N_1 / 60 = \pi \times 0.2 \times 1450 / 60$$

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.2 \times 1450}{60} = \frac{911.06}{60}$$

$$\boxed{v = 15.18 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 2: Centrifugal tension**

$$T_c = m v^2 = 0.9 \times (15.18)^2$$

$$T_c = 0.9 \times 230.43$$

$$\boxed{T_c = 207.4 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3: Effective tension ratio for V-belt**

The exponent for V-belt is $\mu\theta/\sin\beta$:

$$\mu\theta/\sin\beta = 0.25 \times 2.7925 / \sin(19^\circ) = 0.6981 / 0.3256 = 2.144$$

$$\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{2.144} = 8.535$$

**Step 4: Power equation**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) v$$

$$20000 = (T_1 - T_2) \times 15.18$$

$$T_1 - T_2 = 1317.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $T_1$ and $T_2$**

From the ratio:
$$T_1 - 207.4 = 8.535 (T_2 - 207.4)$$
$$T_1 = 8.535 T_2 - 8.535(207.4) + 207.4 = 8.535 T_2 - 1562.95$$

Substituting into $T_1 - T_2 = 1317.5$:

$$8.535 T_2 - 1562.95 - T_2 = 1317.5$$

$$7.535 T_2 = 2880.45$$

$$T_2 = 382.27 \text{ N}$$

$$T_1 = 382.27 + 1317.5 = 1699.77 \text{ N}$$

**Rounding:**

$$\boxed{T_1 \approx 1700 \text{ N (tight side)}}$$
$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 382.3 \text{ N (slack side)}}$$

---

### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Maximum belt tension (total) on the tight side**

For belt design, the **maximum stress** occurs on the tight side and includes centrifugal tension:
$$T_{\max} = T_1 + T_c = 1700 + 207.4 = 1907.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Allowable tension with Factor of Safety (FoS = 10)**

$$T_{\text{allow}} = \frac{T_{\text{breaking}}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{1500}{10} = 150 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Verification**

Since $T_{\max} = 1907.4$ N >> $T_{\text{allow}} = 150$ N, the design is **NOT SAFE**.

**Step 4: Recommendation**

The design is unsafe. Either:
- Use a higher section V-belt (e.g., section C or D)
- Increase the number of V-belts in parallel (multiple groove sheave)
- Reduce the transmitted power
- Increase the breaking strength of belt

$$\boxed{\text{Design FAILS safety check. Redesign required.}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Converting rpm to rev/s inconsistently. $v = \pi d N$ requires $N$ in **rev/s** (i.e., rpm/60), not rpm. Marks deducted per occurrence.
> 2. **Angle Units:** Mixing degrees and radians in $e^{\mu\theta}$. The angle $\theta$ **MUST be in radians** for the exponential. Convert carefully: $180^\circ = \pi$ rad.
> 3. **Crossed vs Open Confusion:** Using wrong sign in $r_1 - r_2$ vs $r_1 + r_2$ and the length formula. A single sign error loses 3-4 marks.
> 4. **V-Belt Formula:** Forgetting the $\sin\beta$ factor in the exponent. This is the most-missed concept.
> 5. **Forgetting Centrifugal Tension:** Many students ignore $T_c$ even at $v > 10$ m/s, leading to a wrong $T_1$ value.
> 6. **Sketching:** Not drawing a free-body diagram or showing $\alpha$ clearly. The KTU 2024 scheme rewards labelled diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [x] A **flat belt** has rectangular cross-section; a **V-belt** has trapezoidal cross-section.
- [x] V-belts give a **wedge effect** with factor $1/\sin\beta$, dramatically increasing grip.
- [x] **Open belt** → same direction rotation; **Crossed belt** → opposite rotation.
- [x] **Euler's equation:** $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ (flat belt) and $e^{\mu\theta/\sin\beta}$ (V-belt).
- [x] **Open belt wrap:** $\theta_{\text{small}} = \pi - 2\alpha$; $\theta_{\text{large}} = \pi + 2\alpha$.
- [x] **Crossed belt wrap:** $\theta = \pi + 2\alpha$ for both.
- [x] **$\sin\alpha$ (open)** $= (r_1 - r_2)/C$; **$\sin\alpha$ (crossed)** $= (r_1 + r_2)/C$.
- [x] **Centrifugal tension** $T_c = mv^2$ reduces effective grip; must be included above 10 m/s.
- [x] **Maximum power** condition: $T_1 = 3T_c$.
- [x] **Power** $P = (T_1 - T_2)v$ watts.
- [x] **Open belt length:** $L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \frac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$.
- [x] **Crossed belt length:** $L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \frac{(d_1+d_2)^2}{4C}$.
- [x] Crossed belt drives have **higher power capacity** due to greater wrap angle.
- [x] Belt drives provide **overload protection** and **shock absorption** — unlike gear drives.
- [x] **Slip and creep** are two distinct phenomena: slip = relative motion between belt and pulley; creep = small elastic stretch differences.
- [x] **Creep formula:** $\dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{v} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{E A}$ where $EA$ is the belt's axial stiffness.
- [x] **Initial tension** $T_0 = (T_1 + T_2)/2$ ensures the belt does not slack at rest.
- [x] Always aim for $\theta \ge 150^\circ$ on the smaller pulley to prevent slip.
- [x] V-belt section selection (A, B, C, D, E) is based on power and pulley diameter.
- [x] **Poly-V belts** combine advantages of flat and V-belts — high flexibility, low slip.
- [x] Belt drives should have a **guard** for safety and a **tensioner** for adjusting slack.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials for Belts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Materials for Belts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belt Materials** are the engineering substances (leather, rubber, fabric, synthetics, or composite) selected to manufacture flexible power-transmission belts. The material's **coefficient of friction (μ)**, **flexibility**, **tensile strength**, **resistance to wear**, and **operating temperature range** directly govern the maximum power a flat/V-belt/ toothed belt can transmit without slipping or premature failure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the *material* is the **first design decision** in Module 4 (Design of Flat and V-Belts). Before applying the equation for belt length, angle of contact, or centrifugal tension, the student must justify the **material grade** because every design constant (μ, ρ, permissible stress σ) is **material-dependent**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Choosing the Right Tyre for Your Vehicle**
> Imagine a bicycle tyre, a truck tyre, and a Formula-1 slick — all are "rubber," yet they transmit very different powers, run at very different speeds, and survive very different environments.
> *A belt is the "tyre" of a machine.* It grips the pulley (like a tyre grips the road) and transmits tangential force (like friction force on a road).
> - **Leather belt** → the classic bullock-cart leather strap → strong, durable, but expensive and sensitive to moisture.
> - **Rubber belt** → the modern car tyre → flexible, high friction, absorbs shock, unaffected by dust or water.
> - **Fabric / Canvas / Balata** → the canvas belt used in old washing machines → cheap, quiet, but lower power capacity.
> - **Synthetic / Polyurethane / Kevlar-cord** → the high-performance racing slick → incredible strength-to-weight ratio, but needs matched pulleys and higher cost.

The **material choice is therefore a trade-off** between *power capacity*, *speed*, *environment*, *cost*, and *pulley compatibility*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The key material-property numbers a KTU student must remember:

| Symbol | Property | Typical Range (Belt Materials) |
|--------|----------|-------------------------------|
| **μ** | Coefficient of friction | **0.25 – 0.50** (depends on material pair) |
| **ρ** | Density of belt material | **1000 – 1500 kg/m³** |
| **σ** | Permissible tensile stress | **2 – 6 MPa** |
| **E** | Young's Modulus | **20 – 500 MPa** (low → high flexibility) |
| **v** | Max permissible belt speed | **≤ 30 m/s** (rubber), **≤ 20 m/s** (leather) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In any numerical, the moment the problem says *"rubber belt on cast-iron pulley"* — the student must immediately pick **μ ≈ 0.30** (dry) or **μ ≈ 0.25** (wet). For *"leather on cast iron"* — **μ ≈ 0.40** dry. This single lookup decides the entire solution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Belt Materials

A *flat* or *V-belt* may be made of any one — or a composite — of the following four material families.

### A. Leather (Hide / Tanned Leather)
- **Source:** Cowhide or buffalo hide, tanned with **tannic acid** (vegetable tanning) or chrome salts.
- **Construction:** Multiple plies of leather glued and stitched; a *single-ply* belt is used for small drives, *double-ply* for higher loads.
- **Structure:** Surface is slightly rough → **high coefficient of friction** (μ ≈ 0.4 with cast iron, dry).
- **Merits:**
  - High tensile strength, long life, high μ → transmits more power per unit width.
  - Withstands elevated temperatures better than rubber.
- **Demerits:**
  - Sensitive to moisture, oil, humidity → swells, stretches, loses μ.
  - Expensive; non-uniform quality (natural material).
- **Application:** Old industrial drives, conveyors in dry environments.

### B. Rubber (Friction / Surface Rubber Belt)
- **Construction:** Plies of woven cotton/rayon fabric impregnated with vulcanised rubber; friction surface is the rubber compound.
- **Properties:** μ ≈ 0.30 (cast iron), elastic, oil-resistant grades available, water-resistant.
- **Merits:** Cheaper, flexible, quiet, unaffected by moisture, easily moulded.
- **Demerits:** Lower strength than leather, affected by heat (>80 °C) and oil (unless special grade).
- **Application:** Most common industrial flat belts, V-belts (wrapped and raw-edge).

### C. Fabric / Cotton / Canvas / Balata
- **Cotton / Canvas:** Woven cotton or hemp impregnated with rubber or balata (natural gutta-percha substitute).
- **Balata Belt:** Cotton plies + balata coating; very high μ (≈ 0.48), used in mining.
- **Merits:** Very cheap, very high friction, quiet, no stretch.
- **Demerits:** Low strength, low power capacity, sensitive to humidity, limited life.
- **Application:** Small machine tools, household drives, light conveyors.

### D. Synthetic / Polyurethane / Polyamide / Kevlar-cord
- **Materials:** Polyurethane (PU), PVC, polyamide (Nylon), polyester, with **Kevlar / Aramid / Steel cord** as tension members.
- **Properties:** Extremely high tensile strength per unit mass, low elongation, high μ, dimensionally stable, can run at 50–80 m/s.
- **Merits:** Long life, no maintenance, compact drives, timing accuracy (for toothed/synchronous belts).
- **Demerits:** High cost, requires matching toothed pulleys, not easily field-repaired.
- **Application:** Automotive timing belts, high-speed industrial drives, precision machinery.

## 2.2 Selection Criteria — The "Why" Behind the Choice

A KTU answer must list **all six** selection criteria:

1. **Coefficient of friction (μ):** Higher μ → smaller angle of lap / smaller pulley needed.
2. **Tensile strength:** Decides allowable tension and hence power capacity.
3. **Flexibility:** Lower bending stress → smaller pulley diameter possible.
4. **Resistance to environment:** Moisture, oil, heat, dust, chemicals.
5. **Speed of operation:** Higher v → centrifugal tension rises as ρv² → light synthetic belts preferred.
6. **Cost & life:** Initial cost vs replacement cost (LCC approach).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The numericals in this topic always use the following equations. Memorise them.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta \csc \alpha}$ (V-belt) | $T_1$ = tight, $T_2$ = slack, $\theta$ = wrap angle, $\alpha$ = groove half-angle | Max tension ratio with slip-prevention |
| 2 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ (flat belt) | Same as above, with $\alpha = 90°$ so $\csc\alpha = 1$ | Flat-belt tension ratio |
| 3 | $\mu_{eq} = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha}$ (V-belt) | Effective friction coefficient on V-groove | Material μ gets *amplified* by groove geometry |
| 4 | $T_C = m \, v^2 = \rho \, A \, v^2$ | Centrifugal tension | Independent of material μ; only ρ, A, v |
| 5 | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \, v$ | Power transmitted (W) | Direct design equation |
| 6 | $\theta = \pi + \dfrac{(D-d)}{C}$ (open belt) | Wrap angle on smaller pulley | Depends on geometry, not material |
| 7 | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi (D+d)}{2} + \dfrac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$ | Open belt length | Used to check material stock length |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{T_1}{A} \le \sigma_{per}$ | Permissible tensile stress check | Material property directly enters |
| 9 | $b \cdot t = \dfrac{P}{v \, K}$ | Belt width × thickness for power P (flat belt) | K depends on material & μ |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{v}{\pi D}$ | Pulley speed → belt velocity | Material speed limit check |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Trick:** In markdown tables, **never write** `|T_1/T_2|` — the vertical bar breaks the table. Use `\vert T_1/T_2 \vert` or `\mid T_1/T_2 \mid` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry** → Kevlar-cord rubber synchronous belts have replaced chains in many engines → quieter, lighter, no lubrication.
- **Mining & cement plants** → Balata or rubber-cord fabric belts on conveyors → high μ to handle inclined dusty surfaces.
- **CNC machines & robotics** → Polyurethane timing belts for positional accuracy (no slip → no position error).
- **Textile machinery** → Leather flat belts still survive on old looms → smooth, vibration-free drive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the V-Belt Has a Higher Tension Ratio than a Flat Belt (Material-Independent)

The material property **μ** between belt and pulley is the *same* whether the belt is flat or wedged. But the **normal reaction N** differs.

**For a flat belt:**

The belt sits on the cylindrical surface. The tangential friction force is:

$$
F_f = \mu \, N
$$

**For a V-belt:**

The belt sits inside a V-groove of half-angle α. The two side walls each press on the belt with a normal reaction **N**. Resolve N into components:

- Vertical component pressing the belt radially on the pulley: $N_v = N \sin\alpha$
- Horizontal component (friction direction) = $N \cos\alpha$ (facing the other side wall)

The total radial (wedging) reaction pushing the belt into the groove is:

$$
N_{radial} = 2 N \sin\alpha
$$

Therefore the friction force the two walls can collectively provide is:

$$
F_f = 2 N \mu \cos\alpha
$$

Now express F_f in terms of the *effective* normal force pressing the belt on the pulley:

$$
N_{radial} = 2 N \sin\alpha \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N = \dfrac{N_{radial}}{2 \sin\alpha}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
F_f = 2 \mu \cos\alpha \cdot \dfrac{N_{radial}}{2 \sin\alpha} = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha} \, N_{radial}
$$

Define the **equivalent coefficient of friction**:

$$
\boxed{\;\mu_{eq} = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha}\;}
$$

**Numerical example** — for α = 18° (standard V-belt), sin 18° ≈ 0.309, so:

$$
\mu_{eq} = \dfrac{\mu}{0.309} \approx 3.24 \, \mu
$$

That is, **a leather belt (μ = 0.4) wedged in a 36° V-groove behaves as if its friction coefficient were 1.30** — a 3.24× amplification purely from geometry. This is *why* V-belts transmit 3–4× the power of an equivalent-width flat belt **for the same material**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Centrifugal Tension (Material Property ρ)

When a belt of mass per unit length *m* runs at linear velocity *v* around a pulley, every element is in circular motion → needs centripetal force → supplied by extra tension $T_C$.

Consider an elemental arc of belt of angle dθ on the pulley of radius r. The mass is $dm = m \, r \, d\theta$ (since arc length = r dθ).

The centripetal force required = $dm \cdot v^2 / r$.

This is supplied by the *net* tangential tension $2 T_C \sin(d\theta/2) \approx T_C \, d\theta$ (for small dθ).

Equate:

$$
T_C \, d\theta = m \, r \, d\theta \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r}
$$

Cancel dθ and r:

$$
\boxed{\;T_C = m v^2 = \rho \, A \, v^2\;}
$$

**Material implication:** $T_C$ depends *only* on **density (ρ)**, cross-section (A) and speed (v) — *not* on μ. Therefore:
- A *leather* belt (ρ ≈ 1100 kg/m³) at 20 m/s → $T_C$ is significant.
- A *Kevlar-reinforced polyurethane* belt (ρ ≈ 1200 kg/m³ but very small A) → much smaller $T_C$ → can run at 60 m/s.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Capacity in Terms of Material (μ, σ, ρ)

The maximum power a flat belt can transmit is:

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \, v
$$

From the slip condition:

$$
T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
T_1 - T_2 = T_2 (e^{\mu \theta} - 1)
$$

The *tight-side* tension cannot exceed the material's permissible stress:

$$
T_1 = \sigma_{per} \cdot A
$$

So the maximum effective pull is:

$$
P = \sigma_{per} A \, v \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}} \right)
$$

Subtract the centrifugal loss (effective pull reduces by $T_C$ on both sides equivalently — the *net* transmitted pull is $(T_1 - T_2) - T_C \, (1 - 1/e^{\mu\theta})$ ; but the working form in KTU textbooks is):

$$
\boxed{\;P = (T_1 - T_2 - T_C) \, v = \left[\sigma_{per} A \left(1 - e^{-\mu\theta}\right) - \rho A v^2\right] v\;}
$$

**Reading the equation:**
- **First term** — material strength × grip → the *useful* pull.
- **Second term** — material density × speed² → the *wasted* pull that only bends the belt on the pulley.
- The belt is *self-destructive* if $\rho v^2 \ge \sigma_{per} (1 - e^{-\mu\theta})$ — a design rule many KTU problems test.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material vs Property

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material} \quad &\rightarrow\quad \mu \quad\rightarrow\quad \rho \;(\text{kg/m}^3) \quad\rightarrow\quad \sigma_{per} \;(\text{MPa}) \quad\rightarrow\quad v_{max} \;(\text{m/s}) \\[4pt]
\text{Leather} \quad &\rightarrow\quad 0.4 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1100 \quad\rightarrow\quad 3.0 \quad\rightarrow\quad 25 \\
\text{Rubber (cotton-ply)} \quad &\rightarrow\quad 0.30 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1150 \quad\rightarrow\quad 2.5 \quad\rightarrow\quad 30 \\
\text{Balata} \quad &\rightarrow\quad 0.48 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1050 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1.8 \quad\rightarrow\quad 20 \\
\text{Cotton/Canvas} \quad &\rightarrow\quad 0.35 \quad\rightarrow\quad 950 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1.5 \quad\rightarrow\quad 15 \\
\text{PVC / PU} \quad &\rightarrow\quad 0.25 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1300 \quad\rightarrow\quad 4.0 \quad\rightarrow\quad 40 \\
\text{Kevlar-cord PU} \quad &\rightarrow\quad 0.35 \quad\rightarrow\quad 1250 \quad\rightarrow\quad 6.0 \quad\rightarrow\quad 80
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off revealed:** Highest μ (balata) has the lowest σ → high grip but weak material. Highest σ (Kevlar-cord) has moderate μ but extremely high v → high-performance. **No single material is best for every drive** — the KTU numerical will always tell you the operating conditions and expect you to *justify the material choice*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Belt-Material Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Requirements] --> B{Operating Environment?}
    B -- Dry Clean --> C[Leather or Rubber]
    B -- Wet Oil Hot --> D[Oil-resistant Rubber or PU]
    B -- Dusty Mining --> E[Balata or Rubber-cord Fabric]
    B -- High Speed Precision --> F[Polyurethane or Kevlar PU]
    C --> G{Speed Range?}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- v less than 25 m/s --> H[Permissible Stress Check]
    G -- v between 25 and 60 m/s --> I[Centrifugal Tension Check]
    G -- v greater than 60 m/s --> J[Use Synchronous Toothed Belt]
    H --> K[Compute Power P from Material mu and sigma]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Select PU or Kevlar with matched tooth pulley]
    K --> M[Final Material Selection: Leather or Rubber]
    L --> N[Final Material Selection: PU or Kevlar]
    M --> O[End: Material Grade Chosen]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cross-Section of a Composite Flat Belt (Material Layers)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Friction Surface: Rubber or Leather] --> B[Top Cover: Cotton or PVC]
    B --> C[Tension Carcass: 2 to 6 Plies of Cotton Canvas or Nylon]
    C --> D[Bottom Friction Surface: Same as Top]
    D --> E[Pulley Contact Zone]
```

**Interpretation of diagram:**
- The **outer skins** (A, D) supply the **coefficient of friction** (μ).
- The **cover ply** (B) supplies **wear and weather resistance**.
- The **tension carcass** (C) supplies the **tensile strength** (σ).
- This is why *composite* belts (e.g., rubber-top, nylon-cord, rubber-bottom) dominate modern industry — each material layer is chosen to do *one job* optimally.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — V-Belt Wedge Effect (Material-μ Amplification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulley V-Groove Half Angle alpha] --> B[Normal Reaction N on Each Wall]
    B --> C[Resolve N: Vertical Component N sin alpha and Horizontal N cos alpha]
    C --> D[Friction Force on Each Wall mu times N cos alpha]
    D --> E[Total Friction = 2 mu N cos alpha]
    E --> F[Effective Normal Force on Belt = 2 N sin alpha]
    F --> G[Equivalent Friction Coefficient mu eq = mu divided by sin alpha]
    G --> H[Conclusion: V-Belt Transmits mu by sin alpha Times More Power than Flat Belt for Same Material]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**List any three desirable properties of a material used for flat belts. State the typical coefficient of friction for a leather belt on a cast-iron pulley.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three desirable properties (1½ marks):**
1. **High coefficient of friction** to prevent slip.
2. **High tensile strength** to transmit large tangential force.
3. **Flexibility** to bend over small pulleys without cracking.
*(Other acceptable: wear resistance, oil/moisture resistance, light weight.)*

**Friction coefficient (1½ marks):**
For a leather belt on a cast-iron pulley (dry condition):
$$
\mu \approx 0.40
$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Compare leather and rubber as belt materials in terms of friction, moisture sensitivity, and cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Leather | Rubber |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Coefficient of friction (dry) | **High (μ ≈ 0.40)** | Moderate (μ ≈ 0.30) |
| Moisture sensitivity | **High (swells, loses μ)** | Low (water-resistant) |
| Cost | High | Low |

*(½ mark per correct cell, plus ½ mark for overall comparison statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A 12 kW, 1440 rpm electric motor drives a centrifugal pump at 720 rpm through a flat belt. The pulley diameters are 200 mm (motor) and 400 mm (pump). Centre distance is 1.5 m. The belt is made of rubber-impregnated cotton fabric with μ = 0.30 and permissible tensile stress σ_per = 2.5 MPa. Density of belt ρ = 1150 kg/m³. Determine: (a) the belt speed, wrap angle, and length; (b) the required belt cross-sectional area and width (assume thickness t = 6 mm).**

#### Solution

**Given:** P = 12000 W, n₁ = 1440 rpm, n₂ = 720 rpm, D = 0.4 m, d = 0.2 m, C = 1.5 m, μ = 0.30, σ_per = 2.5 × 10⁶ N/m², ρ = 1150 kg/m³, t = 6 mm.

**Part (a) — Geometry (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Belt speed v**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi d n_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.2 \times 1440}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 288}{60} = 15.08 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(2 marks for v)*

**Step 2: Wrap angle on smaller pulley (open belt)**

$$
\theta = \pi + \dfrac{D - d}{C} = \pi + \dfrac{0.4 - 0.2}{1.5} = \pi + 0.1333 = 3.275 \text{ rad} \approx 187.6°
$$

*(2 marks for θ; also check: ≥ π always — passes.)*

**Step 3: Belt length L**

$$
L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi (D+d)}{2} + \dfrac{(D-d)^2}{4C}
$$

$$
L = 2(1.5) + \dfrac{\pi (0.4 + 0.2)}{2} + \dfrac{(0.4 - 0.2)^2}{4 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
L = 3.0 + \dfrac{\pi \times 0.6}{2} + \dfrac{0.04}{6.0}
$$

$$
L = 3.0 + 0.9425 + 0.0067 = 3.949 \text{ m}
$$

*(3 marks for L)*

**Part (b) — Cross-section (7 marks)**

**Step 4: Centrifugal tension per unit area (ρ v²)**

$$
\rho v^2 = 1150 \times (15.08)^2 = 1150 \times 227.4 = 2.615 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.2615 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Effective permissible stress**

The tight-side stress σ₁ must satisfy:

$$
\sigma_1 \le \sigma_{per} - \rho v^2 = 2.5 - 0.2615 = 2.2385 \text{ MPa}
$$

Wait — re-check the KTU textbook form: the **maximum tight-side tension** is $T_1 = \sigma_{per} \cdot A$ *before* subtracting centrifugal; the **useful tension** is $T_1 - T_C$ where $T_C = \rho A v^2$. The tight-side stress check uses $\sigma_{per}$ directly:

$$
\sigma_1 = \dfrac{T_1}{A} \le \sigma_{per} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6: Maximum tension ratio from slip equation**

$$
\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.30 \times 3.275} = e^{0.9825} = 2.671
$$

**Step 7: Power equation**

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) v
$$

Let $T_1 = x$. Then $T_2 = x / 2.671 = 0.3744 \, x$.

$$
T_1 - T_2 = x(1 - 0.3744) = 0.6256 \, x
$$

$$
12000 = 0.6256 \, x \times 15.08 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x = \dfrac{12000}{0.6256 \times 15.08} = 1272 \text{ N}
$$

So $T_1 = 1272$ N.

**Step 8: Cross-section area**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_1}{\sigma_{per}} = \dfrac{1272}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 5.088 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 508.8 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 9: Belt width**

$$
A = b \times t \quad\Longrightarrow\quad b = \dfrac{A}{t} = \dfrac{508.8}{6} = 84.8 \text{ mm} \approx 85 \text{ mm}
$$

*(Standard width 90 mm may be adopted.)*

**Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\,v = 15.08 \text{ m/s}, \quad \theta = 187.6°, \quad L = 3.95 \text{ m}, \quad A = 509 \text{ mm}^2, \quad b = 85 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**A V-belt drive transmits 20 kW at 1450 rpm driver speed. The diameters are d = 125 mm, D = 300 mm, and the centre distance is 800 mm. The V-belt material has μ = 0.25, density ρ = 1200 kg/m³, groove angle 2α = 38°, permissible stress σ_per = 3 MPa, and belt cross-section is 22 mm × 12 mm (top width × thickness). Determine: (a) the wrap angle, belt length, and equivalent coefficient of friction; (b) the power-transmitting capacity and comment on whether the drive is safe.**

#### Solution

**Part (a) — Geometry and μ_eq (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Belt speed**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi d n_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.125 \times 1450}{60} = 9.49 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Wrap angle on smaller pulley**

$$
\theta = \pi + \dfrac{D - d}{C} = \pi + \dfrac{0.300 - 0.125}{0.800} = \pi + 0.2188 = 3.360 \text{ rad} = 192.5°
$$

**Step 3: Belt length (open belt)**

$$
L = 2(0.8) + \dfrac{\pi (0.3 + 0.125)}{2} + \dfrac{(0.3 - 0.125)^2}{4 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
L = 1.6 + \dfrac{\pi \times 0.425}{2} + \dfrac{0.0306}{3.2}
$$

$$
L = 1.6 + 0.6676 + 0.0096 = 2.277 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Equivalent coefficient of friction**

For α = 19°:

$$
\sin 19° = 0.3256
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mu_{eq} = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.3256} = 0.768
$$

*(2 marks for μ_eq)*

**Part (b) — Power capacity (7 marks)**

**Step 5: Tension ratio**

$$
\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu_{eq} \theta} = e^{0.768 \times 3.360} = e^{2.580} = 13.19
$$

**Step 6: Cross-section area and masses**

$$
A = 22 \times 12 = 264 \text{ mm}^2 = 264 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 7: Centrifugal tension**

$$
T_C = \rho A v^2 = 1200 \times 264 \times 10^{-6} \times (9.49)^2 = 28.52 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 8: Max tight-side tension**

$$
T_{1,max} = \sigma_{per} \cdot A = 3 \times 10^6 \times 264 \times 10^{-6} = 792 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 9: Slack-side tension**

$$
T_2 = T_{1,max} - (T_1 - T_2)
$$

From the ratio: $T_1 = 13.19 \, T_2 \Rightarrow T_1 - T_2 = 12.19 \, T_2$.

Also, the **effective** tight-side tension *available for power* is $T_1 - T_C$:

$$
T_1 - T_C = 13.19 \, T_2 - 28.52
$$

The power equation uses the *net* effective pull $(T_1 - T_2)$:

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) v \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 12.19 \, T_2 \times 9.49 = \text{required power?}
$$

Maximum power capacity:

$$
P_{max} = (T_1 - T_2) v = 12.19 \, T_2 \times 9.49
$$

We need another relation. From the **standard KTU form**, $T_1$ cannot exceed $T_{1,max}$:

$$
T_1 = 13.19 \, T_2 \le 792 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T_2 \le 60.04 \text{ N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{max} = 12.19 \times 60.04 \times 9.49 = 6946 \text{ W} \approx 6.95 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 10: Safety check**

Required P = 20 kW; capacity = 6.95 kW.

$$
\text{Capacity Ratio} = \dfrac{6.95}{20} = 0.348 \quad\ll\quad 1.0
$$

**The drive is grossly under-capacity. Either increase belt cross-section, increase σ_per (use Kevlar-cord), or use multiple V-belts in parallel.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Both Q.A and Q.B):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** — a 1-mark penalty each occurrence.
> 2. **Using μ directly in $e^{\mu\theta}$ for V-belt** — the V-belt demands **μ_eq = μ/sin α**, not μ. This is a 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Forgetting centrifugal tension** $T_C$ when checking $\sigma_1$ — another 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in belt cross-section (mm² vs m²)** — convert all to SI (m²) before substituting into $T_1 = \sigma A$.
> 5. **Not checking wrap angle ≥ 180°** — for crossed belt the formula differs, costing a full sub-part mark.
> 6. **Stopping at the first "wrong" answer without checking the safety factor** — KTU 2024 scheme's *Course Outcome CO4* (design and evaluate) explicitly demands a "safe / not safe" comment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Materials for Belts"**

- [ ] **4 material families:** Leather · Rubber · Fabric/Balata · Synthetic (PU/Kevlar).
- [ ] **6 selection criteria:** μ, σ, flexibility, environment, speed, cost.
- [ ] **Highest μ material** = Balata (0.48); **lowest density** = Cotton/Canvas (950 kg/m³).
- [ ] **Highest speed-rated** = Kevlar-cord PU (up to 80 m/s).
- [ ] **Standard friction lookup (KTU exam favourite):**
  - Leather on cast iron (dry): **μ = 0.40**
  - Rubber on cast iron: **μ = 0.30**
  - Balata on cast iron: **μ = 0.48**
- [ ] **Golden equation** — V-belt: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{(\mu/\sin\alpha)\theta}$.
- [ ] **Centrifugal tension** — independent of μ: $T_C = \rho A v^2$.
- [ ] **Effective friction** — V-belt amplifies μ by $1/\sin\alpha$ ≈ 3.24× for α = 18°.
- [ ] **Speed limit rule** — leather ≤ 25 m/s, rubber ≤ 30 m/s, synthetic ≤ 80 m/s.
- [ ] **Permissible stress range** — leather 3 MPa, rubber 2.5 MPa, PU 4 MPa, Kevlar-PU 6 MPa.
- [ ] **Crossed vs open belt** — wrap angle formula changes; KTU problems will specify.
- [ ] **Always include centrifugal tension** in stress check and final power balance.
- [ ] **Final sentence of every design answer** must say "design is safe" or "design is unsafe — increase width / change material / use multiple belts."

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory aid:**
> *"The material gives μ and σ; the geometry gives θ and α; the speed gives $T_C$ — and power = (T₁ − T₂ − T_C) v."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of V-Belt Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of V-Belt Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **V-Belt Drive** is a friction drive in which a trapezoidal (wedge-shaped) cross-section belt runs in matched V-grooved sheaves (pulleys), transmitting power between two parallel shafts by virtue of the wedging action of the belt flanks against the groove walls. The wedging action generates a **normal reaction** $N$ that has a frictional component $\mu N$ acting tangentially, thereby multiplying the effective frictional grip compared to a flat belt.

The **Selection of a V-Belt Drive** refers to the standard engineering design procedure—based on the manufacturer's catalog (e.g., ISO 4183, IS 3181, RMA standards)—used to determine the *optimum belt cross-section, sheave pitch diameters, center distance, belt length, number of belts, and initial tension* for a given set of operating conditions (power, speeds, service environment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU 2024 scheme (PCRUT502 Module 4), students are expected to understand the *geometric relations*, the *selection procedure as per standard design data books* (V.B. Bhandari / PSG Data Book), and be able to solve complete design problems involving **open V-belt drives** with multiple belts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wedge & the Cylinder

Imagine trying to pull a heavy wooden cylinder (a log) along a flat plank with a simple rope. If you simply drag the rope, the rope slips easily. Now, instead, place a **wedge** underneath the rope and push it against the cylinder — the rope "bites" into the cylinder much harder. This is exactly the principle of a V-belt.

- **Flat belt** → simple rope drag — frictional contact is limited to the bottom surface of the belt.
- **V-belt** → wedge action — the two angled flanks of the belt are jammed against the groove walls of the sheave, generating a *normal force* that is much larger than the belt's own tension.

The deeper the wedge, the stronger the grip — but if the wedge is *too* deep, the belt bottoms out on the sheave and loses its wedging effect (called **wedging-out**). This is why a **groove angle of 34°–38°** is standard (giving a wedge angle of 34°–38°, not the full 40° of the belt itself — there is a small clearance angle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant / Standard Metric:**
> - Standard groove angle: **$\theta = 34°$ to $38°$**
> - Standard belt cross-section designations: **A, B, C, D, E** (classical) and **SPA, SPB, SPC, SPZ** (narrow).
> - Density of belt material (rubber/fabric): **$\rho \approx 1100\text{ to }1250 \text{ kg/m}^3$**.

## 1.3 Why V-Belts? — Advantages over Flat Belts

| Feature | Flat Belt | V-Belt |
|---|---|---|
| Power capacity (per unit width) | Low | **3 to 4 times higher** |
| Slip tendency | Higher | **Much lower (wedging effect)** |
| Center distance | Long | **Short (compact drive)** |
| Speed ratio | Limited | **Up to 7:1 (open) and 6:1 (crossed)** |
| Alignment sensitivity | Low | **High (grooves enforce alignment)** |
| Cost per kW | Higher | **Lower** |

This explains why V-belts are the *de-facto* standard for industrial drives from fractional kW (fans, pumps) up to several hundred kW (compressors, mills).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of an Open V-Belt Drive

```
        Larger Sheave (D)
        ___________
      /             \
     /  belt flanks  \      C = center distance
    /     press into   \    D = pitch diameter (large)
   |    groove walls    |   d = pitch diameter (small)
    \                 /
     \              /
      \___________/
        Smaller Sheave (d)
```

When the two sheaves have unequal diameters, the belt does not wrap the larger sheave through a full 180°. The smaller sheave always has the **smaller angle of wrap** — and that angle is what governs the maximum power transmission.

## 2.2 Angle of Wrap — The Governing Geometric Quantity

Let:
- $D$ = pitch diameter of larger sheave
- $d$ = pitch diameter of smaller sheave
- $C$ = center distance
- $\alpha$ = angle of wrap on the smaller sheave (in radians, often used in radian form for the capstan equation)

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Draw the line of centers connecting the two sheave axes.
2. From the center of the smaller sheave, draw a tangent to the larger sheave on both sides.
3. The angle between the two tangents, measured *on the side of the smaller sheave*, is the wrap angle $\alpha$.

By symmetry, the wrap on the larger sheave is $(360° - \alpha)$.

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a *crossed* V-belt drive:
> $$\alpha = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D + d}{2C}\right)$$

## 2.3 Length of an Open V-Belt

The exact length is the sum of:
- Two straight runs (length $C$ each, projected): $2\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D-d}{2}\right)^2}$
- Two arc segments on the two sheaves: $\dfrac{\pi}{2}(D + d)$
- A small correction for the "shift" between tangent point and pitch diameter.

The **standard, KTU-accepted formula** (Bhandari / Shigley):

$$\boxed{L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \frac{(D - d)^2}{4C}}$$

For a **crossed belt**:

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \frac{(D + d)^2}{4C}$$

## 2.4 Belt Velocity

$$v = \frac{\pi d N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi D N_2}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

where $N_1$ and $N_2$ are the speeds (rpm) of the driver and driven sheaves.

## 2.5 Centrifugal Tension (Critical for V-Belts)

When a belt of mass per unit length $m$ (kg/m) moves with velocity $v$ (m/s), it experiences an outward pull that effectively reduces the available frictional grip:

$$\boxed{T_c = m \cdot v^2 = \rho \cdot A \cdot v^2}$$

where $\rho$ = belt material density (kg/m³), $A$ = cross-sectional area of the belt (m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $T_c$ is **subtracted from both tight-side and slack-side tension** in the capstan equation for V-belts. As speed increases, the net effective tension $\left(T_1 - T_c\right) - \left(T_2 - T_c\right) = T_1 - T_2$ remains the same, but $T_1$ and $T_2$ both grow — leading to higher bearing loads. This is why V-belt drives are **not recommended for $v > 25$–30 m/s**.

## 2.6 Capstan Equation for V-Belt (with wedging)

For a V-belt of groove angle $\theta$ (typically 34°–38°), the effective normal force on the belt flanks is $N = T \sin(\theta/2)$ per flank, and the tangential frictional force is $\mu N$.

Combining both flanks:

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\,\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)}}$$

Compare this with the flat belt capstan equation $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \alpha}$. The term $\dfrac{1}{\sin(\theta/2)}$ acts as a **friction multiplier** — typically 1.8 to 1.95 for $\theta = 34°$ to 38°.

## 2.7 Power Transmitted

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

Substituting the capstan relation:

$$P = T_c\,v\!\left[\,e^{\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)} - 1\,\right]\,\frac{1}{e^{\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)} - 1}\cdot(\text{...})$$

The standard form used in design data books:

$$\boxed{P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v = (T_{\max} - T_c - T_c/e^{\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)})\,v}$$

where $T_{\max} = \sigma_{\max} \cdot A$ is the maximum allowable tension in the belt, limited by material strength.

## 2.8 The KTU / Industry Standard Selection Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact step-by-step procedure** you must reproduce in a KTU exam answer. Memorize the flow.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Design Power**:
$$P_d = P \times K_s$$
where $K_s$ = **service factor** (from standard tables, depends on driven machine + driver type, typically 1.0–2.0).

**Step 2:** Select **belt section** (A, B, C, D, E or SPA, SPB, …) from a *Power vs. RPM of faster shaft* chart (manufacturer's data).

**Step 3:** Choose **standard pitch diameters** $d$ (small sheave) and $D$ (large sheave) from standard tables. Confirm speed ratio $i = D/d = N_1/N_2$.

**Step 4:** Estimate **center distance** $C$:
$$C \approx (D + d) \quad \text{to} \quad 2(D + d)$$
A common starting choice: $C_0 = 1.5(D + d)$.

**Step 5:** Compute **exact belt length** $L$ using the formula in §2.3, then **adjust $C$** so that $L$ matches a *standard belt length* from the catalog.

**Step 6:** Compute **belt velocity** $v$ and **angle of wrap** $\alpha$ on the smaller sheave.

**Step 7:** From the catalog (Bhandari Table 14.4 / PSG 7.18), read the **power rating per belt** $P_{\text{rated}}$ corresponding to the chosen belt section, $d$, $N_1$ and $\alpha$. Apply **length correction factor** $K_L$ and **arc correction factor** $K_\alpha$ (if $\alpha < 180°$):
$$P_{\text{corrected}} = P_{\text{rated}} \times K_L \times K_\alpha$$

**Step 8:** Compute the **number of belts**:
$$n = \frac{P_d}{P_{\text{corrected}}}$$
Round up to the next whole integer.

**Step 9:** Calculate **initial tension** $T_0$ (per belt) and **shaft load** $F_b$:
$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c \quad ; \quad F_b = (T_1 + T_2})\cos\gamma + 2T_c \cos\gamma$$
(For a 90° drive, $\cos\gamma = 1$.) Total shaft load $= n \cdot F_b$.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angle of wrap (open) | $\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right)$ | deg or rad | On smaller pulley |
| 2 | Angle of wrap (crossed) | $\alpha = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D+d}{2C}\right)$ | deg or rad | — |
| 3 | Belt length (open) | $L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D+d) + \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$ | m | Standard form |
| 4 | Belt length (crossed) | $L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D+d) + \frac{(D+d)^2}{4C}$ | m | — |
| 5 | Belt velocity | $v = \pi d N_1/60 = \pi D N_2/60$ | m/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 6 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2 = \rho A v^2$ | N | $m$ = mass/length |
| 7 | Capstan eq. (V-belt) | $\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)}$ | — | $\theta$ = groove angle |
| 8 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2) v$ | W | Per belt |
| 9 | Design power | $P_d = P \times K_s$ | W | $K_s$ = service factor |
| 10 | Number of belts | $n = P_d / P_{\text{corrected}}$ | — | Round up to integer |
| 11 | Speed ratio | $i = D/d = N_1/N_2$ | — | Open belt |
| 12 | Length correction | $P_{\text{rated}} \times K_L$ | W | $K_L < 1$ if $L$ shorter than ref. |

> Real-world utility: This exact selection procedure is used by **Fenner, ContiTech, Gates, and Bando** in their online design calculators for industrial V-belt selection. Every pump, fan, compressor, and CNC spindle manufacturer uses a variant of this flow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Angle of Wrap (Open V-Belt)

**Setup:** Consider two sheaves with centers $O_1$ (small, diameter $d$) and $O_2$ (large, diameter $D$), separated by center distance $C$. A belt wraps the small pulley through an angle $\alpha$. We want an explicit expression for $\alpha$.

**Geometric Construction:**
- Draw the line of centers $O_1 O_2$.
- The common internal tangent of the two circles is the *run of the belt* on the *slack side*; the upper external tangent (for an open belt, both are external) is the *tight side*.
- Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the tangent points on the small sheave. Then $\angle T_1 O_1 T_2 = \alpha$.

**Step 1: Express the geometry in a triangle.**

Drop a perpendicular from $O_1$ to the tangent line of the larger circle. The tangent length between the two tangent points is:

$$\text{Tangent length} = \sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D - d}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2: Relate the half-angle to the geometry.**

Let $\beta$ be the half-angle of wrap on the small sheave, i.e., $\alpha = 2\beta$.

In the right triangle formed by $O_1$, the tangent point, and the foot of the perpendicular on the line of centers:

$$\sin \beta = \frac{(D - d)/2}{C}$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

**Step 3: Combine to obtain the full wrap.**

$$\alpha = 2\beta = 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

But this is the wrap on the **larger** sheave. The wrap on the smaller sheave is the complement (since total wrap on both = 360°):

$$\boxed{\alpha_{\text{small}} = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $D = d$ (equal sheaves), $\alpha = 180° - 0° = 180°$, which is correct — a belt on two equal sheaves wraps each through exactly half a turn. ✔
>
> If $C \to \infty$ (very long center), $\alpha \to 180°$ again. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation of the Belt Length (Open V-Belt)

**Setup:** Belt length $L$ = (length of two straight runs) + (length of arc on small sheave) + (length of arc on large sheave).

**Step 1: Length of the two straight runs.**

Each straight run, projected along the line of centers, has length $\sqrt{C^2 - r^2}$ where $r = (D - d)/2$. So:

$$L_{\text{straight}} = 2\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D - d}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2: Arc on each sheave.**

Total wrap on both sheaves = $2\pi - \alpha_{\text{small}} - \alpha_{\text{large}}$... but a simpler argument: by the wrap formula, small sheave contributes $\alpha_{\text{small}} \cdot d/2$ and large sheave contributes $(2\pi - \alpha_{\text{small}}) \cdot D/2$. After expansion and use of $\alpha = 2\sin^{-1}((D-d)/2C)$:

$$L_{\text{arc}} = \frac{d}{2}\!\left(2\pi - \alpha_{\text{small}}\right) + \frac{D}{2}\alpha_{\text{small}} = \pi \frac{D+d}{2} - (D-d)\beta$$

where $\beta = \sin^{-1}((D-d)/2C)$.

**Step 3: Total length.**

$$L = 2\sqrt{C^2 - r^2} + \pi \frac{D+d}{2} - (D-d)\beta$$

Using the small-angle-like identity that $\sqrt{C^2 - r^2} \approx C - \frac{r^2}{2C}$ for typical $r \ll C$, and that $(D-d)\beta \approx \frac{(D-d)^2}{2C}$ (for small $\beta$):

$$\boxed{L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \frac{(D - d)^2}{4C}}$$

This is the **approximate, KTU-accepted formula** for V-belt length. (The exact form involves an additional small term; in Bhandari's textbook the exact formula is also given, but the above is the one you will use 99% of the time in KTU exams.)

## 3.3 Derivation of the V-Belt Capstan Equation

**Step 1: Differential capstan analysis (like flat belt, but on one flank of the V).**

Consider an infinitesimal element of the belt on the small sheave, subtending angle $d\alpha$ at the center. The flank makes an angle $\theta/2$ with the vertical, where $\theta$ is the groove angle.

The normal force on each flank is $dN$, and the tangential frictional force is $\mu\,dN$.

**Force balance on the element (tangential direction):**

$$dT = \mu \cdot 2\,dN$$

(The factor 2 accounts for *both* flanks.)

**Force balance on the element (radial / normal direction):**

For an infinitesimal element, the centripetal force required is $T\,d\alpha$, supplied by the normal force on the two flanks:

$$2\,dN = T\,d\alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\sin(\theta/2)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dN = \frac{T\,d\alpha}{2\sin(\theta/2)}$$

**Step 2: Combine.**

$$dT = \mu \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{T\,d\alpha}{2\sin(\theta/2)} = \mu T\,d\alpha \cdot \csc(\theta/2)$$

So:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \frac{\mu}{\sin(\theta/2)}\,d\alpha$$

**Step 3: Integrate from $T_2$ (slack) to $T_1$ (tight) over the full wrap angle $\alpha$:**

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \frac{\mu}{\sin(\theta/2)} \int_0^{\alpha} d\alpha$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{\mu \alpha}{\sin(\theta/2)}$$

**Step 4: Include centrifugal tension** (which acts uniformly on both sides because it depends only on belt mass and speed, not on friction):

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The factor $1/\sin(\theta/2)$ is called the **wedging factor** $K_w$. For $\theta = 34°$, $K_w = 1/\sin(17°) \approx 3.42$. For $\theta = 38°$, $K_w \approx 3.05$. This is the *multiplicative gain* in frictional capacity over a flat belt — explaining why V-belts are so compact and powerful.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Full Selection of a V-Belt Drive

**Problem:** Select a V-belt drive to transmit **20 kW** at **1440 rpm** of the driver to a centrifugal pump running at **480 rpm**. The drive is an open one, operates 12 hours/day, has moderate shock, and the center distance is approximately **1000 mm**. Take $\mu = 0.25$ and groove angle $\theta = 38°$.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Speed ratio and design power.**
$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1440}{480} = 3.0$$
For a centrifugal pump with moderate shock, $K_s = 1.4$ (typical Bhandari Table 14.1). Then:
$$P_d = 20 \times 1.4 = 28 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Select belt section.**
From the *Power vs RPM* chart (Bhandari Fig. 14.9) at 28 kW and 1440 rpm, **B-section** belt is selected (B-section range typically 4–22 kW at 1440 rpm, but with $K_s$ corrected, B-section is just adequate). For higher safety margin, **C-section** may be chosen. Let us select **B-section**.

**Step 3 — Standard sheave diameters.**
For B-section, minimum pitch diameter $d = 125$ mm (Bhandari Table 14.2). Choose $d = 125$ mm. Then:
$$D = i \cdot d = 3.0 \times 125 = 375 \text{ mm}$$
Standard sizes: choose $D = 375$ mm. ✔

**Step 4 — Center distance and belt length.**
$C \approx 1000$ mm (given). Use the open-belt length formula:
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \frac{(D - d)^2}{4C}$$
$$L = 2(1000) + \frac{\pi}{2}(375 + 125) + \frac{(375 - 125)^2}{4(1000)}$$
$$L = 2000 + 785.40 + 31.25$$
$$L = 2816.65 \text{ mm} \approx 2817 \text{ mm}$$

Standard belt lengths near this: 2800 mm. Adjust $C$ so that $L = 2800$ mm:
$$2800 = 2C + 785.40 + \frac{250^2}{4C} = 2C + 785.40 + \frac{15625}{C}$$
Multiply by $C$: $2800 C = 2C^2 + 785.40 C + 15625$
$$2C^2 - 2014.6 C + 15625 = 0$$
Using the quadratic formula:
$$C = \frac{2014.6 \pm \sqrt{2014.6^2 - 4(2)(15625)}}{2(2)} = \frac{2014.6 \pm \sqrt{4058500 - 125000}}{4} = \frac{2014.6 \pm \sqrt{3933500}}{4}$$
$$\sqrt{3933500} \approx 1983.3$$
$$C = \frac{2014.6 - 1983.3}{4} = \frac{31.3}{4} = 7.83 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{rejected, too small})$$
$$C = \frac{2014.6 + 1983.3}{4} = \frac{3997.9}{4} \approx 999.5 \text{ mm} \approx 1000 \text{ mm} \;\; ✔$$

So the original $C = 1000$ mm gives $L \approx 2817$ mm. Round $L$ down to the standard $L = 2800$ mm and adjust $C$ very slightly to $999.5$ mm.

**Step 5 — Belt velocity.**
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.125 \times 1440}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 180}{60} = 3\pi = 9.425 \text{ m/s}$$
This is within the safe range (≤ 25 m/s). ✔

**Step 6 — Angle of wrap on small sheave.**
$$\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{375 - 125}{2 \times 1000}\right) = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(0.125)$$
$$\alpha = 180° - 2(7.18°) = 180° - 14.36° = 165.64° = 2.892 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7 — Power rating per belt.**
From Bhandari Table 14.4 for B-section, $d = 125$ mm, $N_1 = 1440$ rpm, $\alpha = 165.64°$:
- Tabulated power $P_0 \approx 6.5$ kW (interpolating for $d = 125$ mm at 1440 rpm).
- Length correction factor $K_L$ at $L = 2800$ mm: $K_L \approx 1.00$ (since this is the reference length range for B).
- Arc correction factor $K_\alpha$ at $\alpha = 165.64°$: from the arc-correction table, $K_\alpha \approx 0.96$.

Therefore:
$$P_{\text{corrected}} = 6.5 \times 1.00 \times 0.96 = 6.24 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Number of belts.**
$$n = \frac{P_d}{P_{\text{corrected}}} = \frac{28}{6.24} = 4.49$$
Round up to **$n = 5$ belts**.

**Step 9 — Initial tension and shaft load.**
From the catalog, for B-section: $m = 0.218$ kg/m (mass per unit length). Then:
$$T_c = m v^2 = 0.218 \times 9.425^2 = 19.37 \text{ N}$$
Using $T_{\max} = 850$ N (typical allowable tension for B-section):
$$\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu\alpha/\sin(\theta/2)} = e^{0.25 \times 2.892 / \sin(19°)} = e^{0.723 / 0.3256} = e^{2.221} = 9.22$$
Also $T_1 + 2T_c = T_{\max} \cdot \text{(per belt, single)}$... we use the standard relation from Bhandari:
$$T_1 = T_{\max} = 850 \text{ N}, \quad T_2 = T_c + (T_1 - T_c)/9.22 = 19.37 + 90.16 = 109.5 \text{ N}$$
$$T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \frac{850 + 109.5}{2} + 19.37 = 479.75 + 19.37 = 499.12 \text{ N per belt}$$
Shaft load per belt: $F_b = (T_1 + T_2)\cos\gamma + 2T_c \cos\gamma = (T_1 + T_2 + 2T_c)\cos 0° = 850 + 109.5 + 2(19.37) = 998.24$ N.
Total shaft load: $F_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 998.24 = 4991$ N ≈ **5.0 kN per shaft.**

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Numerical Magnitudes

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design power | $P_d$ | 28 | kW |
| Belt section | — | B (5 belts) | — |
| Small sheave | $d$ | 125 | mm |
| Large sheave | $D$ | 375 | mm |
| Center distance | $C$ | 1000 | mm |
| Belt length | $L$ | 2800 | mm |
| Belt velocity | $v$ | 9.43 | m/s |
| Wrap on small | $\alpha$ | 165.6 | degrees |
| Centrifugal tension | $T_c$ | 19.4 | N |
| Tight-side tension | $T_1$ | 850 | N |
| Initial tension (per belt) | $T_0$ | 499 | N |
| Total shaft load | $F_b$ | 4991 | N |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — V-Belt Drive Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Power P, Speeds N1, N2, Service Type, Hours/day] --> B[Step 1: Compute Design Power Pd = P x Ks]
    B --> C[Step 2: Select Belt Section from Power vs RPM Chart]
    C --> D[Step 3: Choose Standard Pitch Diameters d and D from Catalog]
    D --> E[Step 4: Estimate Center Distance C0 = 1.5 to 2 x D plus d]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Belt Length L using Open Belt Formula]
    F --> G[Step 6: Adjust C so L matches Standard Catalog Length]
    G --> H[Step 7: Compute Belt Velocity v and Angle of Wrap alpha]
    H --> I[Step 8: Look up Power Rating per Belt from Catalog]
    I --> J[Step 9: Apply Length Correction KL and Arc Correction K alpha]
    J --> K[Step 10: Compute Number of Belts n = Pd / Pcorrected]
    K --> L[Step 11: Compute Initial Tension T0 and Shaft Load Fb]
    L --> M[OUTPUT: Belt Section, Diameters, Length, n, T0, Fb]
    M --> N{Verify: v less than 25 m/s, alpha more than 120 deg, Fb within bearing capacity}
    N -->|All Pass| O[FINAL DESIGN APPROVED]
    N -->|Any Fail| P[Re-iterate: Choose Larger Section or Adjust C or D]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Force and Geometry Schematic of the V-Belt in a Groove

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Belt Cross Section Trapezoidal] --> B[Left Flank]
    A --> C[Right Flank]
    A --> D[Neutral Axis / Belt Top]
    B --> E[Normal Force N on Left Flank]
    C --> F[Normal Force N on Right Flank]
    E --> G[Friction Force mu x N Tangential Up]
    F --> H[Friction Force mu x N Tangential Up]
    G --> I[Resultant Friction Force 2 mu N = 2 mu T sin theta over 2]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Wedging Action Multiplier: 1 over sin theta over 2]
    J --> K[Effective Friction is mu x Effective normal = mu x T x csc theta over 2]
    K --> L[Capstan Result: T1 minus Tc over T2 minus Tc equals exp mu alpha csc theta over 2]
```

## 4.3 V-Belt Cross-Section Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[V Belt Cross Section] --> B[Top Surface: Fabric Jacket]
    A --> C[Compression Rubber Section]
    A --> D[Tension Section with Embedded Cords]
    A --> E[Bottom Cushion Rubber]
    B --> F[Function: Wear Resistance and Heat Dissipation]
    C --> G[Function: Transmits Wedging Pressure]
    D --> H[Function: Carries the Tensile Load T1]
    E --> I[Function: Flex Fatigue Resistance over Sheave]
    H --> J[Material: Polyester or Aramide Fiber Cords]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU exam, you do **not** need to draw the Mermaid diagrams — these are study aids. In your exam, you should be able to (a) draw a labeled V-belt cross-section showing the wedge, (b) draw a labeled open-belt schematic showing $C$, $D$, $d$, and $\alpha$, and (c) write out the **flowchart of the selection procedure** in plain text or as numbered steps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the wedging action of a V-belt. Why is a V-belt preferred over a flat belt for short center distances?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A V-belt has a trapezoidal cross-section that runs in a sheave (pulley) with a matching V-groove. When tension is applied, the belt flanks press against the groove walls, producing a **normal reaction** $N$ on each flank. The tangential component of the friction force on the two flanks is $2\mu N = 2\mu T\sin(\theta/2)$ — yielding a wedging factor of $1/\sin(\theta/2) \approx 3$ for a standard groove angle of $\theta = 38°$.

$$\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\,\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)}$$

V-belts are preferred over flat belts for **short center distances** because (i) the wedging action gives **3–4 times higher power capacity** per unit belt width, (ii) they have a **compact cross-section** and self-aligning grooves, and (iii) they can transmit the same power with **smaller pulleys**, reducing the overall drive footprint. **[3 marks]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define (i) service factor, (ii) design power, and (iii) arc of contact in the context of V-belt selection."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Service factor ($K_s$):** A numerical multiplier applied to the transmitted power to account for the type of driven machine, the driving unit, and the operating conditions (shock load, hours/day). Typical values: 1.0–1.4 (uniform load), 1.4–1.8 (moderate shock), 1.8–2.0 (heavy shock). **[1 mark]**
- **(ii) Design power ($P_d$):** The effective power that the belt must be capable of transmitting, given by $P_d = P \times K_s$. This is the value used to look up the belt section and number of belts. **[1 mark]**
- **(iii) Arc of contact (angle of wrap, $\alpha$):** The angle, in degrees or radians, subtended by the belt at the sheave center. For an open belt on the smaller sheave, $\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}[(D-d)/(2C)]$. It governs the frictional grip — a smaller $\alpha$ means less grip and hence a lower power rating. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **angle of wrap on the smaller pulley** in an open V-belt drive with pitch diameters $D$ and $d$ ($D > d$) and center distance $C$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For an open V-belt drive with $D = 400$ mm, $d = 200$ mm, $C = 1500$ mm, $\mu = 0.25$ and groove angle $\theta = 38°$, compute the **angle of wrap on the smaller sheave** and the **ratio $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c)$**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (Question A)

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]:**

Refer to the derivation in §3.1. Key steps:

1. **Construct the geometry** (sketch the two sheaves, line of centers $O_1O_2$, and the two external tangents representing the belt). **[1 mark]**
2. Identify that the half-wrap angle on the small sheave is $\beta$, with the full wrap being $\alpha = 2\beta$. **[1 mark]**
3. In the right triangle formed by $O_1$, the tangent point, and the foot of perpendicular from $O_1$ onto the line $O_1O_2$:
   $$\sin\beta = \frac{(D - d)/2}{C}$$ **[2 marks]**
4. Therefore $\beta = \sin^{-1}[(D - d)/(2C)]$, and the wrap on the small sheave (which is the complement of the wrap on the large sheave) is:
   $$\boxed{\alpha_{\text{small}} = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)}$$ **[2 marks]**
5. Mention the units (degrees or radians) and the sanity check for $D = d$ gives $\alpha = 180°$. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]:**

Given: $D = 400$ mm, $d = 200$ mm, $C = 1500$ mm, $\mu = 0.25$, $\theta = 38°$.

**Step 1:** Compute the angle inside the inverse sine:
$$\frac{D - d}{2C} = \frac{400 - 200}{2 \times 1500} = \frac{200}{3000} = 0.0667$$

**Step 2:**
$$\sin^{-1}(0.0667) = 3.823°$$

**Step 3:** Angle of wrap on smaller sheave:
$$\alpha = 180° - 2(3.823°) = 180° - 7.65° = \boxed{172.35°}$$
In radians: $\alpha = 172.35 \times \pi/180 = 3.008$ rad. **[3 marks]**

**Step 4:** Compute the capstan ratio. First, $\sin(\theta/2) = \sin(19°) = 0.3256$.

The exponent:
$$\frac{\mu \alpha}{\sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{0.25 \times 3.008}{0.3256} = \frac{0.752}{0.3256} = 2.310$$

**Step 5:**
$$\boxed{\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{2.310} = 10.07}$$ **[4 marks]**

This ratio (~10) is typical for well-designed V-belt drives, indicating a tight-side tension roughly 10× the slack-side tension (after subtracting centrifugal effects).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the **length of an open V-belt drive** in terms of $D$, $d$, and $C$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A V-belt drive is required to transmit **15 kW** from a motor running at **1440 rpm** to a compressor running at **720 rpm**. The center distance should be approximately **1.5 m**. Use a **B-section belt** with the following catalog data: minimum pitch diameter of small sheave = **125 mm**, power rating per belt at $d = 125$ mm, $N_1 = 1440$ rpm, $\alpha = 180°$ is **5.5 kW**, length correction factor $K_L$ is unity, and arc correction factor $K_\alpha = 0.97$. Service factor $K_s = 1.3$. **Determine the number of belts required and the approximate belt length.** **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**(a) Derivation of belt length [7 marks]:**

Refer to the derivation in §3.2. Key steps:

1. Sketch: open V-belt with sheaves of diameters $D$ and $d$, center distance $C$, tangent points labeled. **[1 mark]**
2. The belt length is the sum of:
   - Two straight runs of length $\sqrt{C^2 - r^2}$ each, where $r = (D-d)/2$ **[2 marks]**
   - Two arc segments: arc on small sheave + arc on large sheave
3. Total angular wrap on both sheaves = $2\pi$. Arc length = $\frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) - (D-d)\beta$ where $\beta = \sin^{-1}((D-d)/2C)$ **[2 marks]**
4. After simplification (using the standard small-angle approximations $\sqrt{C^2 - r^2} \approx C - r^2/(2C)$ and $(D-d)\beta \approx (D-d)^2/(2C)$):
   $$\boxed{L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \frac{(D - d)^2}{4C}}$$ **[2 marks]**

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Design power.
$$P_d = P \times K_s = 15 \times 1.3 = 19.5 \text{ kW}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 2:** Speed ratio.
$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1440}{720} = 2.0$$
So $D = 2d = 2 \times 125 = 250$ mm. **[1 mark]**

**Step 3:** Compute belt length with $C = 1500$ mm.
$$L = 2(1500) + \frac{\pi}{2}(250 + 125) + \frac{(250 - 125)^2}{4(1500)}$$
$$L = 3000 + \frac{\pi \times 375}{2} + \frac{125^2}{6000}$$
$$L = 3000 + 589.05 + 2.60$$
$$L = \boxed{3591.65 \text{ mm} \approx 3592 \text{ mm}}$$ **[2 marks]**

(Standard catalog lengths near 3600 mm: choose $L = 3550$ mm or $L = 3600$ mm. We will retain $L = 3592$ mm for further calculation.)

**Step 4:** Compute angle of wrap.
$$\frac{D - d}{2C} = \frac{125}{3000} = 0.04167$$
$$\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(0.04167) = 180° - 2(2.388°) = 175.22°$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 5:** Compute the power rating per belt. With $\alpha = 175.22°$, $K_\alpha = 0.97$ (given), and $K_L = 1.0$:
$$P_{\text{corrected}} = 5.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.97 = 5.335 \text{ kW}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6:** Number of belts.
$$n = \frac{P_d}{P_{\text{corrected}}} = \frac{19.5}{5.335} = 3.655$$
Round up to **$n = 4$ belts**. **[1 mark]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (read carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the service factor** (costing 1–2 marks). Always state $K_s$ explicitly and compute $P_d = P \times K_s$ in the first step.
> 2. **Using flat-belt capstan equation** $e^{\mu\alpha}$ instead of the V-belt form $e^{\mu\alpha/\sin(\theta/2)}$. Examiners *will* deduct 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrapping angle on the wrong pulley.** It must be calculated on the **smaller** sheave, since that has the smaller $\alpha$ and therefore governs the design.
> 4. **Forgetting length and arc correction factors** $K_L$ and $K_\alpha$ in the power rating. Marks are lost on this almost every semester.
> 5. **Not rounding up the number of belts** to the next integer. You cannot have 3.7 belts — the answer must be 4.
> 6. **Mixing up pitch diameter and outside diameter.** V-belt sheaves are always specified by their **pitch diameter** $D$ (or $d$), measured at the belt's neutral axis — not the outside flange diameter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A V-belt is a friction drive with a trapezoidal cross-section that runs in V-grooved sheaves; the wedging action amplifies friction by a factor of $1/\sin(\theta/2)$ (≈ 3 for $\theta = 38°$). ✔
- **Standard sections:** A, B, C, D, E (classical); SPZ, SPA, SPB, SPC (narrow, ISO 4183). ✔
- **Capstan equation (V-belt):** $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\,\mu \alpha / \sin(\theta/2)}$. ✔
- **Centrifugal tension:** $T_c = m v^2 = \rho A v^2$ — depends only on belt mass and speed. ✔
- **Angle of wrap (open, on small sheave):** $\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right)$. ✔
- **Belt length (open):** $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D+d) + \dfrac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$. ✔
- **Selection procedure — 11 steps** in §2.8: $P_d \to$ section $\to$ $d, D \to$ $C_0 \to$ $L \to$ adjust $C \to$ $v, \alpha \to$ $P_{\text{rated}}, K_L, K_\alpha \to$ $n = P_d/P_{\text{corrected}} \to$ $T_0, F_b \to$ verify $v \le 25$ m/s, $\alpha \ge 120°$, $F_b \le$ bearing capacity. ✔
- **Design power:** $P_d = P \times K_s$, where $K_s = 1.0$–$2.0$ depending on shock & duty cycle. ✔
- **Velocity limit:** $v \le 25$ m/s (preferably 10–20 m/s). Beyond 25 m/s, use timing belts. ✔
- **Wrap limit:** $\alpha \ge 120°$ on small sheave. If less, use an **idler pulley** to increase the wrap. ✔
- **Number of belts:** Always an integer; round **up**. Typical industrial drives use 1–8 belts. ✔
- **Initial tension** $T_0$ controls grip; check that $T_{\max} = T_1 \le \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot A$. ✔
- **Shaft load** $F_b$ must be carried by the bearings — do not ignore this in the shaft-design module. ✔
- **Crossed belt wrap:** $\alpha = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D+d}{2C}\right)$ — almost always $> 180°$, but at the cost of higher wear (belt rubs against itself). Not used at high power. ✔
- **Quick-look mental check:** For a 1:1 ratio with $C = 2d$, $\alpha \approx 180° - 2(14.5°) \approx 151°$. ✔
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Limitations of V-Belt Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT502 - KINEMATICS AND DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Limitations of V-Belt Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **V-Belt Drive** is a positive-engagement, friction-based flexible power transmission system that uses a continuous belt with a **trapezoidal (V-shaped) cross-section** running in matched V-grooved sheaves (pulleys) to transmit power and motion between two parallel shafts.

The V-belt is a standardized, endless flexible element with a cross-sectional profile defined by its **top width (b)**, **depth (t)**, and **included angle (typically 40°)**. When seated in the sheave groove, the belt sides make contact at the **groove angle (2α)**, which is normally less than the belt angle. This geometric mismatch produces the celebrated **wedging action**, which is the defining functional characteristic of a V-belt drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The wedge effect increases the normal reaction at the belt sides, thereby multiplying the frictional grip **N times**, where $N$ is the **V-factor** (or slip reduction factor). This is why V-belts can transmit **2 to 3 times more power** than flat belts of equivalent width and tension.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pulling a friend on a **sled using a flat rope** over a smooth, snowy surface. The rope keeps slipping off the hook. Now switch to a **V-shaped wedge groove** on the hook. Suddenly, the rope seats deeper, the sides pinch, and even a modest pull moves the sled without slipping.

That pinch — that **"side grip"** — is exactly what happens in a V-belt. The pulley groove is shaped like a wedge, and the belt's tapered sides are squeezed against the groove walls. The harder the belt tries to slip, the tighter the wedge grips. This is the **self-tightening, self-centering** behavior that makes V-belts a workhorse in industry — from **automotive alternators and air-conditioning compressors** to **industrial conveyors, machine tools, and agricultural pumps**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standards

The following standard metric parameters govern V-belt selection and performance:

- **Belt section designations:** A, B, C, D, E (classical) and SPZ, SPA, SPB, SPC (narrow / metric)
- **Standard pitch width (b_p):** Defined for each belt section per **IS 2494 / ISO 4184**
- **Belt linear density (ρ):** Typically **$900 \text{ to } 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$** for rubber-fabric reinforced belts
- **Allowable belt tension:** Limited by the **cord material** (polyester, aramid, or steel)
- **Coefficient of friction (μ):** **0.25 to 0.40** for rubber against cast iron/polyamide sheaves
- **Service factor (Cs):** **1.0 to 1.6** depending on load type (uniform, light shock, heavy shock)
- **Standard groove angle (2α):** **34° to 38°** (always less than the belt's nominal 40° angle to produce wedging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant to Memorize:** The **V-factor (wedging multiplier)** $N = \sec(\alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the half groove angle. For a 38° groove, $N \approx 1.62$, meaning a V-belt grips **1.62× harder** than an equivalent flat belt.
